Lawful Transport Framework for Proof-Carrying Mathematics, Deterministic Replay, and Obstruction-Driven Discovery

Abstract
This treatise specifies a single executable framework that completes ordinary mathematical practice by making representation change, verification, reuse, and discovery first-class, machine-checkable objects. The framework is a Lawful Transport Calculus for invariants—constants, identities, reductions, and emergent “missing moduli”—implemented as a hash-addressed metro graph whose nodes are invariant objects and whose edges are typed evaluators, transports, reductions, bridges, promotions, and migrations. The central thesis is that an invariant is not a decimal expansion; it is a portable closure object: a value together with a 4-root atlas of lawful evaluators, a complete set of commutation/holonomy/representability witnesses, and a governance record that prevents silent drift in conventions, policies, probes, tolerances, or toolchains. The system is designed so that every accepted claim is replayable and locally verifiable: no proof by narrative, no trust in floating arithmetic, and no untracked convention changes.
A. Core objects: lenses, conventions, corridors, policies, and legality
A lens formalizes a representation as a triple[L := (D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I),]where (D) is a carrier domain (scalar, vector/tensor, function, measure/distribution, operator, algebraic object), (\mathcal A) is an address algebra (canonical indexing, ordering, serialization, hashing, slicing), and (\mathcal I) is an invariant schema (admissibility sets, equivalence relations, observables, and preservation laws defining “same meaning” inside the lens). Every meaning-affecting choice is pinned by a convention fingerprint (\mathrm{ConvFP}) (branch choices, normalization, basis rules, parameterization, continuation rules, ordering/tie-breakers) and bundled into a scope-indexed (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP}) covering all operator families invoked by any route.
Two governance substrates fix admissibility and approximation:
Policy (P) pins admissible operations, budgets, automation permissions, disclosure boundaries, and risk gates (stress/regression requirements).
Corridor (C) pins tolerances and refinement schedules: at minimum the tri-gate thresholds ((\varepsilon_{\text{face}},\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\varepsilon_{\Pi})), a κ ladder (\mathcal K), probe regime requirements, residual functional identifiers, and carrier-typed admissibility constraints.
A transport is a typed morphism between lenses,[T : L_i \to L_j,]accepted only when its legality predicates are satisfied: type correctness; scope coverage under (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP}); policy admissibility; corridor admissibility; invariant preservation (exact or corridor-bounded); projection/canonicalization stability; and deterministic replay constraints. Ordinary mathematical acts (definition, simplification, approximation, reduction, acceleration) are treated as finite programs over a minimal kernel and accepted only with explicit witnesses.
B. WordNF: route identity as a mathematical object
Every computation path is recorded as a canonical normal form WordNF, a deterministic, hashable object that is the route identity. WordNF encodes the segment stream of evaluation steps, gate runs, κ refinements, probe bindings, projection steps, bridge applications, and (when present) tunnel/splice events; it binds method ids, bound ids, rounding ids, projector ids, κ ladder ids, probe regime ids, and context hashes. WordNF is the object that makes “run it again,” “audit it,” and “compare routes” mathematically meaningful. Two routes are identical iff their WordNF hashes match; any rewrite requires witnessed commutation and is itself recorded.
C. UCW and Tri-Lock: commutation + holonomy + representability
The atomic witness is the Unified Commutation Witness (UCW), which enforces Tri-Lock over a κ-indexed probe family:
Face-lock: route disagreement residual curve (r_{\square}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}).
Spin-lock: loop closure residual curve (s_{\square}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) for an explicit loop map derived from WordNF decode/encode/projection.
Projection-lock: representability residual curve (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\Pi}) under the declared projector (\Pi_h).
UCW binds the route word(s), corridor/policy/convention/verifier fingerprints, probe regime and adequacy metadata, residual functional ids, and κ ladder. It defines a deterministic verifier protocol: resolve → validate bindings and adequacy → recompute residual curves → apply gate stack → emit accept/refuse + class + counterexamples. UCW classifies failures into semantic categories (kernel mismatch, holonomy obstruction, uncertainty plateau, drift, convention conflict, policy block, mixed) and produces minimal counterexample objects (minimal κ/probe/subword) that are replay-verifiable and actionable.
D. Certified evaluation: enclosures, tails, and projectors
Certified evaluation is enclosure-based. Evaluator charts produce certified receipts binding:
a conservative enclosure (interval/rectangle/ball/operator enclosure) with outward rounding under a pinned rounding id,
deterministic tail bounds and truncation envelopes under pinned bound ids with explicit assumption regions and composition laws,
projector semantics (\Pi_h) and representability obligations when required,
deterministic truncation selection (N(\kappa)) or discretization rules,
replay sufficiency via pins/witness roots and environment/toolchain pins where required.
No floating value is accepted as decisive equality. Numeric computation is admissible only as part of certified enclosure construction and verified residual comparisons.
E. Receipt algebra: dependency, merge, equivalence
The framework separates:
Dependency: proof consumption with typed dependency edges and membership proofs;
Merge/co-verification: union of indices with no semantic claim; conflicts preserved;
Equivalence/transport: meaning preservation requiring UCW-grade witnesses.
Receipts bind PlanSpec, pins_root, wits_root, and context hashes. Cross-policy reuse requires policy-bridge receipts; cross-convention reuse requires explicit convention bridges. This prevents silent drift and evidence laundering.
F. Metro graph: the certified memory-web
All knowledge is stored as a typed metro graph:
Nodes: invariants (seed/derived/candidate/portal), atlases, bundles.
Edges: baseline evaluators, accel evaluators, transports, reductions, collapses, promotions, bridges, migrations, refusals.
Payloads: chart specs, parameter schemas, bound derivations, projector definitions, probe regimes.
Witnesses: UCWs, Ω*, stress/regression packs, refusal packs, conflict certs, drift certs.
Determinism: canonical serialization, stable hashing, merkle roots for pins/wits, chain hashes for WordNF segment order.
The metro is simultaneously a knowledge base, a compiler target, a verification object, and a provenance ledger.
G. Discovery: Tier-0 obstructions, RQF forcing, portals
Discovery is formalized as failure interpretation. When closure fails repeatedly under lawful repair:
T0-OBSTR extracts stable holonomy-dominant failures into candidate modulus intervals when spin residuals stabilize across κ and are invariant under admissible chart substitutions and adequate probes.
RQF (Root-Quad Forcing) forces any proposed modulus into a 4-root atlas and requires SFCR boundary closure and interior closure certified by Ω*.
If closure cannot be achieved without extending the semantic alphabet (new carrier, new observables, monodromy classes), the system emits a portal report and routes to portal bridge mechanisms rather than inventing a false constant.
H. Basis growth: dimension models and forced generators
A dimension model (d_w) (and refined (d_{w,\delta})) drives systematic growth:
compute deficiency via generating functions,
schedule forced generator slots,
install certified collapse edges (e.g., even-zeta collapses into (\mathbb Q\cdot\pi^{2n}) under declared conventions),
attempt certified reductions into the current basis,
when reductions fail robustly under bounded search and adequate probes, produce non-reduction certificates and promote new primitives under RQF and governance gates.
This yields a deterministic roadmap of what must exist (by model) and what is currently certified (by receipts).
I. Acceleration synthesis: AAS (Apéry and beyond)
AAS synthesizes faster evaluators using deterministic template libraries:
oracle numerics permitted only for candidate discovery,
acceptance requires certified interval verification against baseline receipts with deterministic tail bounds,
promotion requires UCW binding and loop closure; accel charts are additional routes, not additional truths,
activation requires risk-gated stress/regression and rollback readiness.
Template families include Apéry/binomial suppression, hypergeometric, Euler–Maclaurin, continued fractions, kernel semigroups, modular/character sums, each with explicit admissibility and portal boundary triggers.
J. Purpose loop: demand-driven mathematics and enforceable invariants
A UseCase Demand Scheduler ranks tasks by urgency (digits × call volume × latency, plus dependency/deficiency impacts) and triggers baseline evaluation, acceleration, obstruction compilation, and basis growth. Governance enforces as machine predicates:
Replay invariance: promoted objects are reconstructible from hashes, WordNF, pins/wits roots, and pinned environments.
No silent drift: changes in conventions, policies, probes, tolerances change fingerprints; promotion invalid unless bridged explicitly.
Local verifiability: edges/nodes can be independently re-verified without global trust.
Compositionality: receipts and UCWs compose; dependency, merge, equivalence remain distinct.
Budgeted honesty: inability to conclude yields refusal artifacts with classification and counterexamples, not guesses.
Security/tamper-evidence: hashing, merkle proofs, chain hashes; corruption detection; quarantine and rollback; CI enforcement.
Crystal Addressing Scheme (4^4 extraction spine)
Every item is addressed as:[\mathbf{ChXX}.\mathbf{Q}.\mathbf{F}.\mathbf{a}]with ( \mathrm{ChXX}\in{01..21}), ( \mathrm{Q}\in{A,B,C,D}), ( \mathrm{F}\in{1,2,3,4}), ( a\in{a,b,c,d}).Appendices are addressed as ( \mathbf{AppX}.\mathbf{Y}.\mathbf{y} ) with (X\in{A..P}). This makes the manuscript itself a deterministic memory-web: every claim has stable coordinates for extraction, cross-linking, and replay.

Ch01 — Axioms, Scope, and the Completion Problem
A. Square — Objects and Axioms
Ch01.A1 — Spec
Ch01.A1.a — The Completion Problem
Mathematical practice, as commonly executed, interleaves (i) semantic intent, (ii) representational choice, (iii) approximate computation, and (iv) informal equivalence claims. The completion problem addressed by this treatise is to render these four components as explicit, typed, replayable objects so that every accepted claim is locally verifiable and every rejected claim yields a structured refusal artifact.
Completion Objective. Define a universal framework in which any mathematical act is encoded as a finite program over:
Representations (lenses),
Meaning-preserving moves (typed transports),
Execution traces (canonical route words), and
Local witnesses (certificates and refusals),such that:
(O1) Route identity is explicit. A computation is not “a derivation” but a canonical object (W) identifying the exact sequence of representation moves and numeric procedures.
(O2) Acceptance is predicate-based. A claim is accepted iff a fixed verifier can recompute and validate the prescribed witness obligations under pinned policies and corridors.
(O3) Failure is informative. When acceptance cannot be achieved within declared budgets, the framework emits a refusal artifact that is itself replayable and classifies the failure mode.
(O4) Reuse is safe. Accepted objects are compositional; they can be consumed by downstream objects without reintroducing ambiguity or silent drift.
Completion Theorem (informal statement, formal obligations later). Any mathematical claim intended for reuse can be expressed as a finite, typed object whose verification depends only on (i) its pinned inputs, (ii) its route word, and (iii) its witness pack, with no reliance on external narrative or untracked convention.
Ch01.A1.b — Scope Boundaries
The framework is defined for finite, replayable artifacts. The following boundaries are enforced:
Finite object boundary. Every accepted artifact must be representable as a finite serialization with a stable hash identity. Infinite objects may be referenced only through finite generators plus explicit evaluation/approximation obligations.
Algorithmic boundary. A claim is accepted only through a verifier-executable protocol. Human-only arguments are admissible only if they are compiled into a verifier-executable proof object or reduced to checkable obligations (e.g., witness packs with deterministic checks).
Numeric boundary. Approximation is permitted only under corridor discipline. “High precision” is not an argument; only certified enclosures, controlled rounding models, and monotone refinement gates are admissible for promotion.
Semantic boundary. The framework does not assume a single global meaning space. Meaning is defined relative to a representation (lens) plus its invariant schema and convention fingerprint. Cross-lens equivalence is a transport claim and must be witnessed.
Governance boundary. Any action that changes admissible computation (policy) or admissible error tolerance (corridor) must be explicit and hash-addressed; no implicit widening is allowed in promoted artifacts.
Ch01.A1.c — Invariants to Preserve
All promoted artifacts must preserve the following invariants. Each invariant is a predicate that must be satisfiable by a verifier using only the artifact’s pinned hashes and declared obligations.
Type-correctness invariant. Every object has a declared type, including carrier type, lens type, and transport signature. Composition is permitted only when types unify.
Convention integrity invariant. Every representation-dependent semantic choice is pinned by a convention fingerprint (\mathrm{ConvFP}). Any change in semantics must change (\mathrm{ConvFP}) and therefore invalidate reuse unless bridged explicitly.
Policy integrity invariant. The set of admissible operations, budgets, external calls (if any), and security constraints is pinned by a policy hash. Cross-policy reuse requires an explicit compatibility proof or a policy-bridge artifact.
Corridor integrity invariant. All tolerance gates and refinement schedules (κ-ladders) are pinned by a corridor hash. Promoted artifacts may only tighten, never silently widen, corridor obligations.
Replay determinism invariant. Every promoted artifact must be replayable with deterministic outcome under pinned environment assumptions. Non-deterministic choices must be eliminated by canonical ordering, pinned seeds, or explicit nondeterminism declarations that forbid promotion.
Witness sufficiency invariant. Every acceptance claim must include all witnesses required by the verifier protocol: commutation evidence, loop-closure evidence, representability evidence, and any auxiliary certificates (interval bounds, tail bounds, regression/stress evidence when required).
Compositionality invariant. Downstream artifacts may depend on upstream artifacts by hash identity; the dependency semantics must be unambiguous and must not implicitly import unpinned assumptions.
Ch01.A1.d — Extraction Tags
Every artifact is labeled for deterministic memory mapping and extraction. Tags are attached at creation and preserved through promotion.
Address tag. Each paragraph, definition, theorem, algorithm, and schema is addressed as (\mathrm{ChXX}.\mathrm{Q}.\mathrm{F}.\mathrm{a}) with fixed quadrants (Q\in{\mathrm{A,B,C,D}}), facets (F\in{1,2,3,4}), atoms (a\in{\mathrm{a,b,c,d}}).
Object tag. Each formal object is tagged by its category: lens, transport, word, witness, receipt, refusal, node, edge, policy, corridor, scheduler, bridge.
Stability tag. Each artifact is labeled draft, certified, promoted, deprecated, refused. Only promoted is reusable without caveats.
Risk tag. Each artifact carries a risk class: public, bounded-risk, quarantined, blocked. Promotion requires public or an explicit waiver path.
Ch01.A2 — Encode
Ch01.A2.a — Object Taxonomy
The framework is built from a minimal, explicit taxonomy. Every runtime and manuscript element is an instance of one of the following classes.
Lens. A representation object defining a carrier domain, address algebra, and invariant schema.
Transport. A typed morphism between lenses, with legality predicates and execution obligations.
Word. A canonical route identity describing the composition of transports and computation gates.
Witness. A verifiable certificate binding a word to acceptance predicates; includes commutation, loop closure, representability, and auxiliary bounds.
Receipt. A compact dependency and integrity wrapper: hashes of inputs (pins), hashes of witnesses (wits), policy/corridor hashes, and verifier version hash.
Refusal. A structured failure object providing classification, evidence, and replay fields.
Metro Node. A hash-addressed invariant object (e.g., constant, basis element, portal object) that owns an atlas of charts.
Metro Edge. A typed relationship between nodes (baseline evaluation, acceleration, collapse, reduction, transport, promotion, bridge).
Scheduler Job. A governance-controlled task specifying a target, corridor/policy, budgets, and required outputs.
Bridge Pack. A promotion-grade conversion of “successful repair/acceleration” into reusable tissue (typed bridge seeds plus certificate packs).
All objects are serializable under canonical encoding rules and must be stable under hashing.
Ch01.A2.b — Minimal Kernel Assumptions
The treatise assumes a minimal kernel sufficient to support deterministic verification and certified approximation without presupposing any particular mathematical domain.
Canonical encoding. There exists a canonical serialization function (\mathrm{canon}(\cdot)) that is stable under re-serialization and is used for all hash identities.
Cryptographic hashing. There exists a collision-resistant hash (\mathrm{H}(\cdot)) used to bind objects, witness packs, and receipts.
Deterministic arithmetic core. The verifier may assume deterministic bigfloat arithmetic within pinned environments. However, promotion does not rely on raw floating equality; it relies on certified enclosures and explicit rounding models.
Certified enclosure primitives. The kernel supports interval arithmetic and deterministic tail bounds sufficient to certify scalar evaluations and to generalize to higher carriers.
Projector primitive. The kernel defines a representability projector (\Pi_h) (e.g., canonical rounding/quantization/canonical form mapping) which must be explicit and hashed.
Verifier protocol. There exists a deterministic verifier that checks: object typing, hash bindings, probe adequacy, recomputation of residual curves, and acceptance predicates.
Budget enforcement. The kernel enforces computational budgets as part of legality: time/precision caps, search limits, and allowable tunnel steps.
These assumptions are necessary and sufficient to define a verification-complete system for the class of artifacts targeted by this treatise.
Ch01.A2.c — Naming Discipline
Names are not identifiers; identifiers are hashes. Names are maintained as human-readable labels subject to collision and ambiguity controls.
Primary identity. Every promoted object has an identity ( \mathrm{id} := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{object}))).
Stable name fields. Each object includes a name field used for indexing, but never used to assert identity or equivalence.
Disambiguation. Any name collision must be disambiguated by canonical suffixes derived from hashes or typed keys.
No implicit overloading. A name may not refer to different semantic objects under different conventions. Convention differences must appear as distinct objects, bridged explicitly if equivalent.
Lexical determinism. Lists, dictionaries, and basis orders are canonicalized under stable ordering rules. Any non-determinism forbids promotion.
Ch01.A2.d — Address Plan
The manuscript and the executable system share a single addressing plan to support memory mapping and extraction.
Crystal address. (\mathrm{ChXX}.\mathrm{Q}.\mathrm{F}.\mathrm{a}) is the minimal coordinate.
Object address. Every object includes a home_address field pointing to its defining address.
Cross-links. References between objects are expressed as typed edges: REF, DEP, EQUIV, MIGRATE, BRIDGE, with hash pointers.
Extraction invariants. Every addressable element provides: (i) a definition, (ii) a construction, (iii) a witness obligation, (iv) a hook (API or algorithm handle).
Ch01.A3 — Prove
Ch01.A3.a — Soundness Targets
Soundness is defined as a family of local properties rather than an unverifiable global claim. The framework commits to the following soundness targets.
Local verifiability. Every promoted artifact must be verifiable without access to the full graph, using only its pins, witness roots, policy/corridor hashes, and the verifier version hash.
Semantic discipline. Cross-representation equivalence is never assumed. Equivalence exists only when a witness pack certifies commutation/closure/representability under pinned conventions.
Deterministic replay. Re-running the verifier on the same artifact under the same pinned environment yields identical acceptance outcomes.
Non-escalation integrity. A promoted artifact cannot be used to infer stronger claims than those it certifies. In particular, heuristic discovery results cannot be promoted without certified verification.
Scoped trust. Any external component (oracle computations, heuristic searches) is admissible only as a generator for candidates; acceptance depends solely on certified checks.
Ch01.A3.b — Honesty and Refusal Principle
The framework enforces an honesty rule: it is preferable to produce a structured refusal than an ungrounded claim.
Refusal completeness. A refusal must include:
the attempted route word (WordNF hash),
the corridor/policy hashes,
the failure classification,
the best achieved residual curves,
evidence of budget exhaustion,
a finite list of admissible next actions.
No silent fallback. Substituting an unverified method in place of a verified method without changing object identity is forbidden.
No ambiguous acceptance. If equivalence depends on unpinned conventions, acceptance is forbidden; the correct output is a refusal with a required convention-bridge obligation.
No policy laundering. Crossing from strict to lax policies without explicit bridging is forbidden; the correct output is a refusal indicating missing policy-bridge receipts.
Ch01.A3.c — Replay Principle
Replay is a formal contract.
Replay contract. A promoted artifact must contain enough information to replay verification:
canonical object serialization,
pins (hashes of all consumed objects),
witness roots (hashes of all required witness objects),
policy/corridor hashes,
verifier version hash,
deterministic route word hash.
Recompute requirement. The verifier must recompute residual curves from the route word and pinned evaluators, not accept stored residuals as authoritative.
Environment pins. If computation depends on numeric backends, library versions, or machine arithmetic semantics, these are pinned explicitly for promotion.
Replay minimality. Replay is minimized by storing hashes and reconstructible proofs rather than bulk data; however, any omission that prevents recomputation forbids promotion.
Ch01.A3.d — Compositionality
Compositionality is the guarantee that verified artifacts may be assembled without creating new ambiguity.
Typed composition. If objects (A) and (B) compose, there exists a typed rule describing the composition and its witness obligations. Untyped composition is forbidden.
Receipt composition. If (R_A) and (R_B) are receipts for (A) and (B), then the receipt for (A\circ B) includes pins and witness roots of both, plus any new witnesses required by the composite legality predicate.
No merge implies meaning. Union of knowledge stores is not equivalence. Merge is a data operation, not a semantic operation, and must not be interpreted as equality without witnesses.
Transport compositionality. Equivalence and transport edges compose only when the associated UCWs compose and the resulting commutation squares remain within corridor gates.
Ch01.A4 — Extract
Ch01.A4.a — What Is Stored
Promotion-quality storage is restricted to objects that support replay and extraction.
Canonical objects. Lenses, transports, route words, witnesses, receipts, refusals, policies, corridors, and scheduler job records.
Metro graph. Nodes and edges with canonical payload schemas, including atlas charts and activation states.
Witness stores. UCWs, certificate packs, refusal packs, regression and stress results when required by policy.
Provenance. Verifier version hashes, environment pins, and canonical serialization markers.
Index structures. Deterministic indices for lookup: by name, by type, by weight/depth (for constant systems), by policy/corridor, by witness class.
Ch01.A4.b — What Is Derived
Derived content is re-computable and is not authoritative unless tied to a witness.
Numeric approximations. Midpoints, decimal expansions, raw floating results: derived only; authoritative only as part of a certified enclosure.
Heuristic candidates. PSLQ relations, heuristic series, conjectured identities: derived; promotable only after certified verification.
Route suggestions. Planner outputs: derived; must be verified upon execution.
Performance profiles. Latency/complexity models: derived; used for scheduling, not for semantic acceptance.
Ch01.A4.c — What Is Forbidden
The following classes of content are forbidden for promotion and forbidden to serve as semantic evidence.
Unpinned semantics. Any claim dependent on unstated conventions (branch cuts, normalizations, basis choices) without ConvFP binding.
Uncertified numerics. Equality claims based solely on floating-point agreement without certified enclosures and deterministic rounding/tail models.
Implicit policy changes. Any acceptance that relies on relaxing policy constraints without explicit policy-bridge receipts.
Silent corridor widening. Any acceptance that increases tolerance gates or reduces κ requirements without explicit corridor change and re-verification.
Narrative-only proofs. Arguments not compiled into verifier-checkable obligations for reusable claims.
Ch01.A4.d — Audit Checklist
An artifact is promotable only if it passes the following audit checklist.
Identity audit. Canonical serialization exists; hash identity is stable; name is disambiguated.
Pin audit. All dependencies are pinned by hash, and dependency types are explicit.
Policy/corridor audit. Policy hash and corridor hash are present and match admissible constraints.
Route audit. WordNF exists; gate segments and tunnel segments are explicit; route hash matches witness pack.
Witness audit. Required witness objects exist; verifier recomputation succeeds; acceptance predicates pass.
Drift audit. ConvFP matches across claimed equivalences or explicit convention bridges exist.
Refusal audit. If acceptance fails, refusal artifact exists with classification, evidence, and next actions.
B. Flower — Operational View
Ch01.B1 — Spec
Ch01.B1.a — Transports as Programs
A transport is not a slogan (“change variables”); it is an executable program step with a declared signature and legality obligations.
Transport signature. Each transport (T) declares domain lens (L_i), codomain lens (L_j), parameter schema (\theta), and output schema.
Execution semantics. Each transport step is an executable function with deterministic output under pinned environment; if nondeterminism exists it must be pinned or forbid promotion.
Witness obligations. Each transport declares which invariants must be witnessed: invariant preservation, representability stability, commutation obligations with adjacent steps.
Program composition. A computation is a word (W = T_n \circ \cdots \circ T_1). The word is an object, and it is the sole carrier of route identity.
Ch01.B1.b — Legality Predicates
Legality is a predicate that takes a transport step and context and returns legal or a refusal object.
Minimum legality predicates:
Typing predicate. Inputs and outputs match declared domains and codomains.
Convention predicate. ConvFP compatibility holds or a declared bridge is used.
Policy predicate. All operations used are allowed and budgets are respected.
Corridor predicate. All approximations satisfy the corridor gates; any failure triggers Snap/tunnel/refusal protocol.
Replay predicate. Execution is replayable and deterministically reconstructible from the route word and pins.
Ch01.B1.c — Route Identity
Route identity is defined by WordNF.
Uniqueness. Two computations are the same route iff their WordNF hashes are equal.
Canonicalization. Equivalent syntactic expressions must normalize to the same WordNF; non-canonical encodings forbid promotion.
Segment discipline. Route words are segment streams of gate runs and tunnel events. Every computation that changes corridor or method must appear as a segment.
Parameter binding. Every numeric or semantic parameter influencing the computation is bound into the WordNF.
Ch01.B1.d — Operator Vocabulary
The framework uses a fixed vocabulary of operator classes to avoid ambiguous prose.
Encode/Decode. (E_L, D_L) for lens (L).
Normalize. (N_L) canonical form maps within a lens.
Project. (\Pi_h) representability projectors and canonical quantizers.
Evaluate. Certified evaluator charts with interval outputs and tail bounds.
Verify. Verifier protocol operators that recompute residuals and check predicates.
Snap. Corridor-tightening repair operators.
Tunnel. Representation-switch operators that are admissible only when logged and witnessed.
Promote/Refuse. Governance-controlled outcomes producing either reusable tissue or structured refusal.
Ch01.B2 — Encode
Ch01.B2.a — Route Normalization
Route normalization defines canonical forms for transport programs.
Associativity normalization. Parenthesization is erased; only ordered step sequences remain.
Commutation normalization. When commutation is permitted by a declared commutation rule, the rewrite is recorded explicitly or canonicalized under a fixed normalization order.
Redundancy elimination. Identity steps are removed only when their elimination is legal and deterministic under pinned conventions.
Segment normalization. Gate runs and tunnel events have canonical ordering; any reordering must be justified by explicit commutation witnesses.
Ch01.B2.b — Parameter Binding
Parameters influence meaning and must be bound.
Semantic parameters. Branch selection, normalization constants, basis choices, and similar semantic choices are bound via ConvFP.
Numeric parameters. Precision, truncation depth, tail bound strategy, and rounding model are bound via corridor and policy fields and included in segment payloads.
Search parameters. Any candidate discovery search includes explicit bounded parameters (caps, enumerator order, seeds).
Probe parameters. Probe families used for verification are bound and hashed.
Ch01.B2.c — Determinism Pins
Determinism is pinned by explicit fields.
Encoding determinism. Canonical serialization rules are fixed and hashed.
Execution determinism. Numeric libraries, environment assumptions, and seeds are pinned; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Enumeration determinism. Candidate search orders are deterministic; randomness is forbidden in promotable discovery.
Time determinism. If timestamps exist they may not affect semantics; they are metadata only.
Ch01.B2.d — Version Stamps
Every critical component is versioned.
Verifier version. The verifier protocol is identified by a version hash; promotion binds to it.
Schema versions. Object schemas include a version ID; upgrades require migration edges.
Policy versions. Policies are versioned; cross-version reuse requires compatibility proofs.
Corridor versions. Corridors are versioned; narrowing is compatible; widening requires explicit bridging.
Ch01.B3 — Prove
Ch01.B3.a — Commutation as Truth
Equivalence between two routes is defined by commutation within corridor gates.
Square commutation. For a square with two paths (W_1) and (W_2), commutation is the statement that the induced outputs agree within corridor tolerance under certified enclosures.
Witness form. Commutation is not asserted; it is witnessed by a UCW storing κ-indexed residual curves computed on an adequate probe family.
Locality. Commutation witnesses are local: they certify specific routes under specific policy/corridor/ConvFP.
Promotion. A commutation witness is promotable only if it passes verifier recomputation and meets the acceptance predicate.
Ch01.B3.b — Loop Closure as Truth
Loop truth is defined as closure of a route that should return to the same meaning class.
Loop map. A loop is a route word whose semantic intent is identity. The loop map (L) is computed from the route’s encode/decode structure.
Holonomy residual. The deviation from identity is measured on a probe family; the maximal normalized deviation is the spin residual curve.
Interpretation. Persistent loop residuals under refinement indicate either missing moduli (kernel incompleteness) or portal requirements (new semantic alphabet).
Promotion. Loop closure witnesses are mandatory for promoting transports that claim equivalence across cycles.
Ch01.B3.c — Representability as Truth
Representability formalizes the requirement that computed objects remain stable under declared canonicalization.
Projector. A projector (\Pi_h) is declared and hashed.
Residual. Representability residual measures ( |x - \Pi_h(x)| ) normalized under corridor rules.
Stability. Promoted artifacts must show stability under (\Pi_h) across κ refinement; instability forbids promotion.
Meaning. Representability ensures that the accepted object belongs to the intended meaning class and not to an artifact of numeric representation.
Ch01.B3.d — Tri-Gate Logic
Acceptance requires passing a tri-gate predicate:
Face gate: (r_{\square,\max}(\kappa) \le \varepsilon_{\text{face}}) on the declared κ-grid.
Spin gate: (s_{\square,\max}(\kappa) \le \varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) on the declared κ-grid.
Representability gate: (r_{\Pi,\max}(\kappa) \le \varepsilon_{\Pi}) on the declared κ-grid.
Failure of any gate yields either:
a repair obligation (Snap/tunnel admissible), or
a refusal artifact when budgets are exhausted, or
a discovery trigger when failure is stable and holonomy-dominant.
Ch01.B4 — Extract
Ch01.B4.a — Query Primitives
The system exposes minimal, deterministic query primitives.
Lookup by hash. Retrieve exact object by identity.
Lookup by name with disambiguation. Return candidate set with disambiguation keys.
Route query. Given endpoints and constraints, return admissible route words ranked by certified cost under policy.
Witness query. Retrieve UCWs and certificate packs for a given edge, node, or route.
Ch01.B4.b — Minimal Proofs to Export
Exports are minimal sets sufficient for independent verification.
Proof slice. Given a target edge/node, export all pins and witnesses required for the verifier to accept the target.
Policy/corridor slice. Export the relevant policy and corridor objects, and any required bridge receipts.
Verifier slice. Export the verifier version hash and any schema versions required to interpret objects.
Replay slice. Export the route word and deterministic parameters.
Ch01.B4.c — Failure Evidence to Export
Failures are exported as structured evidence packs.
Refusal pack. Classification + best achieved residual curves + budget exhaustion evidence.
Counterexample pack. Minimal probe instances or parameter settings that witness failure.
Drift pack. If failure indicates convention drift, export ConvFP diffs and required bridge obligations.
Uncertainty pack. If plateau indicates non-identifiability, export adequacy failures and recommended probe expansions.
Ch01.B4.d — Glossary Keys
Each chapter defines a key set of terms for extraction.
Object keys. Lens, transport, word, witness, receipt, refusal, node, edge.
Gate keys. Face, spin, representability; corridor; κ-grid; budgets.
Governance keys. Policy, compatibility, bridge, promotion, quarantine.
Discovery keys. Obstruction, modulus, portal, root-quad, atlas.
C. Cloud — Uncertainty Discipline
Ch01.C1 — Spec
Ch01.C1.a — Corridor Meaning
A corridor is the explicit contract governing approximation.
Corridor fields. A corridor declares ε-gates, κ-schedule, admissible approximation methods, and required certificates.
Corridor hash. The corridor is hash-addressed; any change in corridor implies a new corridor identity.
No silent widening. A promoted artifact may not rely on widened tolerances unless the widened corridor is explicitly pinned and bridged.
Corridor locality. Corridors are attached to edges and witnesses; corridor meaning is not global.
Ch01.C1.b — κ-Schedules
A κ-schedule defines refinement checkpoints.
κ-grid. A finite ordered set of refinement levels (precision, truncation depth, mesh size, etc.).
Monotone refinement. At successive κ levels, certified enclosures must tighten or plateau; oscillation indicates nondeterminism or instability.
Schedule adequacy. κ-grid must be sufficient to detect drift and plateau; inadequate schedules forbid promotion.
Schedule binding. κ-grid is hashed as part of the witness obligations.
Ch01.C1.c — Tolerances
Tolerances are expressed as gate thresholds and are interpreted under normalized residual functionals.
Face tolerance. Bounds route disagreement under certified enclosures.
Spin tolerance. Bounds loop closure under the loop map.
Representability tolerance. Bounds deviation under projectors/canonicalization.
Tolerance semantics. Tolerances are not heuristics; they are contractual predicates required for acceptance.
Ch01.C1.d — Admissible Approximations
Approximation methods are admissible only when their error behavior is explicitly certified.
Certified enclosures. Interval arithmetic and deterministic tail bounds.
Controlled rounding. Explicit rounding model; deterministic rounding cushions.
Method identity. Each method is identified by an ID hashed into receipts.
Upgrade path. Heuristic methods may be used for candidate generation but require certified verification for promotion.
Ch01.C2 — Encode
Ch01.C2.a — Interval Types
Intervals provide certified enclosures.
Scalar interval. ([l,u]) with (l\le u), representing a value enclosure.
Composite interval. Product, sum, and function enclosures with certified propagation rules.
Residue interval. Enclosures for route differences and residual functionals.
Canonical interval encoding. Deterministic serialization of endpoints with pinned precision rules.
Ch01.C2.b — Tail Bounds
Tail bounds certify truncation error.
Tail schema. Each evaluator provides a tail bound function or envelope with parameters.
Bound provenance. Tail derivations are pinned and hashed.
Bound composition. Tail bounds compose under sum/product and under declared transforms.
Bound auditability. The verifier can recompute or validate tail bounds under pinned derivation rules.
Ch01.C2.c — Rounding Model
Rounding behavior is a first-class object.
Rounding cushion. A deterministic rounding cushion is attached to enclosures.
Backend pins. Numeric backend identity is pinned.
Quantization. Projectors (\Pi_h) define representability; their semantics are pinned.
Rounding fingerprint. The rounding model is hashed and included in witnesses and receipts.
Ch01.C2.d — Uncertainty Fingerprints
Uncertainty is pinned to prevent silent changes in interpretation.
Corridor fingerprint. Hash of tolerances and κ-grid.
Method fingerprint. Hash of evaluator method IDs and bound derivations.
Probe fingerprint. Hash of probe family generation rules.
Verifier fingerprint. Hash of verifier protocol version.
Ch01.C3 — Prove
Ch01.C3.a — Error Composition Laws
Certified error composition is enforced by explicit laws.
Monotone enclosure law. Enclosures must remain valid under composition.
Propagation law. If inputs are enclosed and operations have certified bounds, outputs are enclosed.
Residual law. Route differences are enclosed by difference of enclosures.
Normalization law. Normalization and projection must not violate enclosures; any such violation forbids promotion.
Ch01.C3.b — Monotone Refinement Laws
Refinement is constrained to monotonicity under the corridor.
Tightening law. As κ increases, enclosure radii must weakly decrease or plateau.
Plateau interpretation law. Plateau below gates is acceptable; plateau above gates yields refusal or discovery triggers.
Budget law. If κ cannot be increased due to budget, refusal must record exhaustion.
Stability law. Non-monotone residuals indicate instability or nondeterminism and require refusal or stronger probes.
Ch01.C3.c — Plateau Detection
Plateau detection prevents false convergence.
Plateau predicate. A residual curve is plateaued if successive κ refinements fail to reduce the residual beyond a fixed threshold.
Classification. Plateau above gates is classified as uncertainty unless evidence indicates holonomy-dominant obstruction.
Adequacy escalation. Plateau triggers probe adequacy escalation; absent adequate probes, promotion is forbidden.
Evidence. Plateau refusal must include the κ-indexed curve and the plateau predicate witness.
Ch01.C3.d — Refusal Forms
Refusals are formal objects with required structure.
Refusal type. Each refusal is typed by failure class: ALIAS_DRIFT, POLICY_MISMATCH, PLATEAU_UNCERTAINTY, HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION, BUDGET_EXHAUSTED, NON_IDENTIFIABLE.
Required fields. Attempted route word hash, corridor hash, policy hash, residual curves, budgets used, and next admissible actions.
Replay fields. Deterministic seeds, method IDs, and environment pins.
No partial promotion. Artifacts with refusals may be stored as evidence but are not promotable as equivalences.
Ch01.C4 — Extract
Ch01.C4.a — Confidence Certificates
Confidence is encoded as checkable certificates.
Gate pass certificate. Explicit record that tri-gate predicates were satisfied on the κ-grid.
Stability certificate. Evidence of monotone refinement or safe plateau below gates.
Robustness certificate. Evidence from perturbation or adversarial probes when policy requires.
Scope certificate. Explicit statement of what is certified and what is not (boundaries).
Ch01.C4.b — Audit Replays
Audit replay is the primary extraction target.
Replay bundle. Pins + route word + witness roots + policy/corridor + verifier version.
Deterministic recomputation. Verifier recomputes residual curves and acceptance predicates.
Trace extraction. Gate run segments and tunnel segments are extracted and inspected.
Outcome extraction. Accept/refuse results are extracted with evidence.
Ch01.C4.c — Regression Hooks
Regression is required to preserve long-term integrity.
Regression triggers. Any promotion, bridge installation, or corridor/policy update triggers regression jobs.
Regression suite. A fixed set of probes and exemplars must be re-verified.
Bisectability. Failures must be bisectable by hash lineage to isolate drift.
Artifact preservation. Regression results are stored as witness packs to support future audits.
Ch01.C4.d — Stress Harness Index
Stress harnesses provide adversarial validation when required.
Stress categories. Perturbation tests, boundary-case tests, convention-variation tests, and adversarial probe tests.
Stress binding. Stress suite IDs are pinned in policy.
Promotion gating. High-risk promotions require stress-pass certificates.
Stress exports. Stress results are exportable as compact witness packs.
D. Fractal — Automation and Growth
Ch01.D1 — Spec
Ch01.D1.a — Discovery as Failure-Interpretation
Discovery is formalized as interpreting structured failure evidence.
Failure evidence. UCW failures contain residual curves and classifications.
Stable obstructions. Persistent holonomy-dominant residuals that stabilize across κ indicate missing moduli or portal requirements.
Interpretation discipline. The system extracts obstruction candidates only when failures satisfy stability and invariance criteria (ConvFP/policy invariance).
No inspirational jumps. New primitives arise from forced obligations, not from aesthetic conjecture.
Ch01.D1.b — Growth Triggers
Growth is triggered by explicit conditions.
Demand trigger. High urgency demand (digits × call volume × latency) triggers baseline evaluation, acceleration, and possibly kernel growth.
Refusal trigger. Budgeted refusal that is stable and holonomy-dominant triggers obstruction compilation.
Deficiency trigger. Dimension-model deficiency triggers forced generator scheduling.
Import trigger. Cross-metro imports may trigger local closure jobs and bridge scheduling.
Ch01.D1.c — Budget Rules
Budgets are contractual.
Search budgets. Candidate discovery is bounded by enumerator caps and method limits.
Verification budgets. κ-grid and enclosure requirements are bounded; exhaustion yields refusal artifacts.
Repair budgets. Snap/tunnel steps are bounded; each tunnel must reduce defect or be refused.
Promotion budgets. Promotion requires sufficient evidence; insufficient evidence cannot be waived without explicit governance changes.
Ch01.D1.d — Meta-Goals
The automation layer exists to enforce and scale correctness.
Maximize reusable truth. Promote only what is locally verifiable and compositional.
Minimize storage without losing replay. Prefer storing hashes and regenerable proofs over bulk text, subject to replay sufficiency.
Maintain integrity under evolution. Ensure upgrades preserve witness obligations or create explicit migration edges.
Support purposeful mathematics. Align development with demand while preserving proof-carrying rigor.
Ch01.D2 — Encode
Ch01.D2.a — Scheduler State
Scheduler state is a first-class object.
Queue objects. Jobs are explicit objects with pinned targets, policies, corridors, budgets, and required outputs.
State hashing. Scheduler decisions are reproducible under pinned decision rules; schedule state is exportable.
Dependency graph. Jobs form a DAG; prerequisites are explicit.
Outcome binding. Job outputs bind to receipts and witness packs; partial outputs are stored as refusals or drafts.
Ch01.D2.b — Demand Signals
Demand signals encode purpose.
Demand record. Each demand includes target object, required digits, expected call volume, latency constraints, and certification requirements.
Aggregation. Demands aggregate across clients; aggregation rules are deterministic.
Signal integrity. Demand signals are metadata and do not affect semantics; they affect scheduling only.
Evidence coupling. Demands can trigger verification/acceleration but cannot override acceptance predicates.
Ch01.D2.c — Priority Metrics
Priority metrics are deterministic functions of demand and risk.
Urgency metric. (U := \text{digits}\times\text{call_volume}\times\text{latency_weight}), with policy-defined weights.
Risk multiplier. High-risk targets require additional stress/regression, modifying effective priority by cost.
Deficiency multiplier. Forced generator obligations modify priority to prevent basis deficiency.
Fairness constraints. Starvation avoidance is enforced by bounded priority decay rules.
Ch01.D2.d — Governance Flags
Governance flags restrict automation.
Auto-tunnel enablement. Tunneling may be enabled only under explicit policy flags.
Promotion gates. Promotion requires explicit gates; automation cannot bypass them.
Quarantine flags. Imported or risky artifacts can be quarantined until re-verified locally.
Audit flags. Certain job classes require audit-grade bundles as mandatory outputs.
Ch01.D3 — Prove
Ch01.D3.a — Safe Automation Constraints
Automation must not compromise soundness.
Verifier primacy. Automation may generate candidates but cannot declare acceptance without verifier-validated witnesses.
Deterministic enumeration. Search and scheduling decisions are deterministic given pinned inputs and policies.
Budget enforcement. Automation must terminate within budgets and record exhaustion evidence.
Isolation. Candidate generation does not contaminate promoted stores unless verified and promoted.
Ch01.D3.b — No Silent Drift Guarantee
Drift prevention is enforced by hashing and compatibility rules.
Fingerprint binding. Any semantic choice alters ConvFP; any corridor/policy alteration alters hashes.
Bridge requirement. Cross-fingerprint reuse requires explicit bridge artifacts with their own witnesses.
Migration discipline. Upgrades occur by migration edges, never by overwriting identities.
Regression discipline. Promotions and migrations trigger regression jobs; failures produce refusal artifacts rather than silent acceptance.
Ch01.D3.c — Reproducibility
Reproducibility is defined as equivalence of verifier outcomes.
Replay determinism. Same pins + same word + same policy/corridor + same verifier ⇒ same acceptance.
Environment pinning. Where backend differences matter, they are pinned; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Auditability. Reproducibility includes the ability to extract minimal replay bundles.
Cross-site verification. Independent verifiers can validate promoted artifacts without privileged access.
Ch01.D3.d — Escalation Logic
Escalation is a deterministic decision tree.
Attempt. Compute witnesses under corridor gates.
Repair. If gates fail and repair is admissible, run Snap/tunnel with defect reduction requirement.
Refuse. If repair budgets exhaust, emit refusal pack with classification.
Discover. If refusal is stable and holonomy-dominant, trigger obstruction compilation; if deficiency is detected, trigger forced-generator scheduling.
Ch01.D4 — Extract
Ch01.D4.a — Pipeline Map
The full pipeline is an ordered set of passes.
Baseline evaluation. Construct certified receipts for demanded targets.
Transport closure. Construct UCWs for chart equivalences and route commutations.
Repair and acceleration. Invoke Snap and Auto-tunnel under policy to improve closure and runtime.
Promotion or refusal. Promote reusable tissue (bridges, charts, nodes) or emit structured refusal packs.
Ch01.D4.b — Metro Map Spine
The metro map is the canonical memory web.
Station spine. Core hubs for representations and invariants (by domain).
Line spine. Functional lines: verification, evaluation, discovery, governance.
Edge spine. Typed edges with witness obligations; no edge is semantic without witnesses.
Atlas spine. Every promoted invariant has a 4-root atlas or a refusal explaining the missing root and the required portal.
Ch01.D4.c — Phase Gating
Work proceeds through gated phases.
Phase 0 (Spec). Define objects, policies, corridors, and route words.
Phase 1 (Baseline). Achieve certified baseline evaluations.
Phase 2 (Closure). Achieve UCW closure across charts and routes.
Phase 3 (Growth). Trigger obstruction compilation and forced generators when closure cannot be achieved under budget.
Ch01.D4.d — Minimal Reproducible Artifacts
Each promoted claim must yield a minimal reproducible bundle.
Pins bundle. Hashes and canonical serializations of all dependencies.
Word bundle. The route word segment stream with all parameters.
Witness bundle. UCWs and certificate packs sufficient for verifier recomputation.
Context bundle. Policy/corridor hashes, verifier version hash, and any required bridge receipts.
Ch01 — Axioms, Scope, and the Completion Problem
Ch02 — Lenses, Carriers, Address Algebras
A. Square
Ch02.A1 — Spec
Ch02.A1.a — The Lens Triple ((D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I))
A lens is the minimal unit of representation. It is a triple[L ;:=; (D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I),]where:
Carrier domain (D). (D) is a typed carrier of mathematical objects. (D) is not assumed to be a set in the naïve sense; it is a carrier equipped with a declared type interface (\mathrm{Ty}(D)) specifying:
constructors and destructors (admissible formation rules),
observables (admissible measurements/extractions),
equality interface (exact or approximate),
admissible evaluation regimes (e.g., symbolic, certified numeric, probabilistic).
Address algebra (\mathcal A). (\mathcal A) is the structure that makes objects in (D) indexable, serializable, and replay-addressable. It is a tuple[\mathcal A ;:=; (\mathrm{Addr},;\mathrm{Enc},;\mathrm{Dec},;\preceq,;\mathrm{NormAddr},;\mathrm{Slice},;\mathrm{Join}),]with:
(\mathrm{Addr}): address tokens (finite strings over a grammar),
(\mathrm{Enc}: D \to \mathrm{Addr}) and (\mathrm{Dec}: \mathrm{Addr}\to D) satisfying the lens’ decode obligations,
(\preceq): a canonical total preorder on addresses,
(\mathrm{NormAddr}): a canonical address normalizer (idempotent),
(\mathrm{Slice},\mathrm{Join}): compositional address operators enabling subobject addressing and recomposition.
Invariant schema (\mathcal I). (\mathcal I) is the formal meaning contract internal to the lens:[\mathcal I ;:=; (\approx_L,;\mathcal P,;\mathcal O,;\mathcal L),]where:
(\approx_L) is the lens equivalence relation (exact or corridor-bounded),
(\mathcal P) is a finite set of invariant predicates (P_k:D\to{\mathrm{true},\mathrm{false}}) and/or (P_k:D\times D\to{\mathrm{true},\mathrm{false}}),
(\mathcal O) is a finite set of observables (o_j:D\to R_j) used to define witness obligations and probes,
(\mathcal L) is a finite set of laws (rewrite rules, normal forms, admissibility constraints) that are treated as obligations rather than prose.
A lens is well-formed iff (\mathcal A) and (\mathcal I) are internally consistent, specifically:
(\mathrm{NormAddr}) is idempotent and preserves decode semantics;
(\mathrm{Dec}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\mathrm{Enc}(x))) \approx_L x) for all (x\in D);
all observables (\mathcal O) are total on the admissible region of (D) declared by (\mathcal P).
Ch02.A1.b — Carrier Types
Carrier types are the internal geometry of meaning. The framework distinguishes carriers by the witness structure they admit and by the form of admissible approximation. The carrier taxonomy below is normative: every carrier instance must declare one of these kinds and satisfy its interface.
Scalar carriers. (D=\mathbb S) where values are scalars (algebraic numbers, transcendental constants, real/complex values). Observables include evaluation, enclosure, and canonical projection. Typical invariants: algebraic relations, functional equations, normalization identities.
Vector and tensor carriers. (D=\mathbb V^n) or (D=\mathbb T^{m\times n \times \cdots}). Observables include coordinate extraction, norms, and structured projections. Typical invariants: linear constraints, orthogonality, rank, symmetry classes.
Function carriers. (D=\mathcal F(\Omega)) where elements are functions with explicit domains (\Omega). Observables include evaluation at points, derivatives, integrals, transforms. Typical invariants: differential equations, boundary conditions, analyticity regions, growth constraints.
Measure/distribution carriers. (D=\mathcal M(\Omega)) or generalized distributions. Observables include integrals against test functions, characteristic functions, moments. Typical invariants: positivity, total mass, support constraints, transform identities.
Operator carriers. (D=\mathcal L(V)) or algebras of operators. Observables include spectra, trace, determinant-like invariants, commutators. Typical invariants: algebraic identities, semigroup properties, functional calculus constraints.
Algebraic structure carriers. Groups, rings, modules, categories; observables include normal forms, generators/relations, homomorphism invariants. Typical invariants: presentation equivalence, subgroup structure, action invariants.
Graph/complex carriers. Combinatorial carriers with addresses as graph paths or simplicial coordinates. Observables include adjacency, homology, canonical labeling. Typical invariants: isomorphism class, homotopy invariants, boundary operators.
Every carrier type must declare: (i) admissible observables (\mathcal O), (ii) a canonicalization target (normal form or projector), and (iii) a probe family generator adequate to distinguish the equivalence relation within corridor gates.
Ch02.A1.c — Invariant Schemas
An invariant schema specifies what it means for two carrier elements to be “the same” and what properties must remain preserved under admissible operations.
Predicate layer. A finite family of invariants (\mathcal P={P_k}) forms the admissible region:[D_{\mathrm{adm}} := {x\in D : \forall k,; P_k(x)=\mathrm{true}}.]If the lens uses pairwise invariants, these are obligations for transports and witnesses:[P_k(x,y)=\mathrm{true}\quad\text{must be witnessed when }x\approx_L y\text{ is claimed.}]
Observable layer. Observables (\mathcal O={o_j}) define witness obligations through computed residuals. Each observable is typed and has declared stability properties under canonicalization and projection.
Law layer. Laws (\mathcal L) are executable obligations: canonical rewrite rules, normalization constraints, admissibility constraints, and required commutation squares. Laws are not “theorems”; they are part of the lens’ semantics and must be enforced by verifiers.
Tolerance layer. When invariants are tolerance-bounded, the schema includes a corridor-binding map:[\mathcal I_C(x,y) := \bigl(\forall j,; \rho_j(o_j(x),o_j(y)) \le \varepsilon_j(C)\bigr),]where (\rho_j) are declared residual functionals and (\varepsilon_j(C)) are corridor gates. This makes approximate meaning a formally checkable object rather than an informal appeal.
Ch02.A1.d — Equivalence Relations
Each lens defines an equivalence relation (\approx_L) capturing “same meaning” within that representation.
Exact equivalence. (\approx_L) is an equivalence relation on (D_{\mathrm{adm}}) with:
reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity,
decidability by canonicalization (when available): (x\approx_L y \iff \mathrm{NF}_L(x)=\mathrm{NF}_L(y)).
Corridor-bounded equivalence. When exact decidability is unavailable or undesired, the lens defines a corridor-indexed family (\approx_{L,C}) such that:[x \approx_{L,C} y \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \mathcal I_C(x,y)=\mathrm{true}.]The equivalence becomes contractually tied to corridor (C); widening or tightening changes the equivalence relation and therefore the meaning class.
Representability closure. The schema must specify the representability projector (\Pi_h) or canonicalizer (\mathrm{NF}_L) and require:[x\approx_L \Pi_h(x),]with explicit residual obligations when corridor-bounded.
Cross-lens equivalence is not assumed. If (x\in D_i) and (y\in D_j), equivalence is not defined until a transport claim (T:L_i\to L_j) and witnesses are produced. Cross-lens equivalence is a theorem of transports, not an axiom of meaning.
Ch02.A2 — Encode
Ch02.A2.a — Canonical Serialization
Canonical serialization is the foundation of replay identity. For every lens (L), define a canonical serialization function[\mathrm{canon}L: D{\mathrm{adm}} \to {0,1}^\ast,]satisfying:
Determinism. (\mathrm{canon}_L(x)) is a pure function of (x) and the pinned semantic parameters of (L). No timestamps, iteration-dependent ordering, or nondeterministic formatting may influence the output.
Idempotence under decode/encode. If (\mathrm{Enc}) and (\mathrm{Dec}) are the lens’ address maps, then[\mathrm{canon}_L!\left(\mathrm{Dec}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\mathrm{Enc}(x)))\right) = \mathrm{canon}_L(x)]on the admissible region, up to explicitly declared tolerance-bounded canonicalization when exact equality is not meaningful.
Schema binding. (\mathrm{canon}_L(x)) includes:
lens identifier (\mathrm{id}(L)),
convention fingerprint (\mathrm{ConvFP}(L)),
carrier-type tag (\mathrm{Ty}(D)),
minimal structural metadata required to reconstruct (x) within the lens.
Witness separation. Serialization of (x) is separated from serialization of witnesses and receipts; semantic identity of (x) is not conflated with the evidence used to assert relations involving (x).
Ch02.A2.b — Address Grammars
The address grammar determines how objects and subobjects are named and extracted. Each lens declares an address grammar (G_L) generating (\mathrm{Addr}) and providing:
Segment structure. Addresses are finite sequences of typed segments:[\alpha = s_1 / s_2 / \cdots / s_m,]where each segment belongs to a finite vocabulary (e.g., node, chart, term, basis, coeff, face, loop, probe).
Parsing and normalization. There exist total functions (\mathrm{Parse}_L) and (\mathrm{NormAddr}_L) such that every syntactically valid address has a unique normal form. Normalization is idempotent.
Subobject semantics. (\mathrm{Slice}) maps ((x,\alpha)) to a subobject or observable, and (\mathrm{Join}) reconstructs when applicable. The slice/join pair is typed and must be declared total on the admissible region intended for extraction.
Linking semantics. Address grammars support typed links between lenses and between objects:REF (reference), DEP (dependency), EQUIV (equivalence claim), BRIDGE (convention/policy bridge), MIGRATE (schema evolution). Each link is a hash pointer with an address anchor.
Ch02.A2.c — Ordering Rules
Ordering rules eliminate hidden nondeterminism and make canonicalization and hashing meaningful.
Canonical ordering on addresses. The preorder (\preceq) is total on (\mathrm{Addr}) and is stable under normalization:[\alpha \preceq \beta ;\Longrightarrow; \mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha) \preceq \mathrm{NormAddr}(\beta).]
Canonical ordering on composite structures. Lists, sets, maps, bases, monomial sets, and graph labels must be encoded under a declared deterministic order:
lexicographic order on normalized address segments,
tie-breakers by hash when semantic order is absent,
explicit basis ordering fields when multiple canonical bases exist.
Stability under refinement. Refinement (κ escalation) may enlarge internal approximants; ordering rules must not change the semantic identity of an object. If ordering changes under refinement, it must be treated as a convention change and therefore alter (\mathrm{ConvFP}).
Forbidden implicit sorting. Any sorting performed by runtime libraries is invalid unless explicitly pinned by the lens and proven stable; otherwise promotion is forbidden for artifacts depending on such ordering.
Ch02.A2.d — Hash Commitments
Hash commitments bind meaning, representation, and admissibility.
Lens identity. Each lens has an identity:[\mathrm{id}(L) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(L_{\mathrm{spec}})),]where (L_{\mathrm{spec}}) includes carrier type, address grammar, invariant schema identifiers, and convention fingerprint.
Object identity. Each object (x\in D) has an identity:[\mathrm{id}(x;L) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}_L(x)).]
Schema commitments. The invariant schema and address grammar are committed by hash inside the lens identity. Any schema evolution requires a migration edge and does not overwrite identities.
Commitment discipline. Identities are immutable. Any change in representation semantics, address grammar, or invariant schema necessarily produces a different identity; bridging and migration are explicit graph edges with their own witnesses.
Ch02.A3 — Prove
Ch02.A3.a — Lens Isomorphism Criteria
Lens isomorphism is the formal condition under which two representations are equivalent as representations, not merely as sets of values.
Two lenses (L_1=(D_1,\mathcal A_1,\mathcal I_1)) and (L_2=(D_2,\mathcal A_2,\mathcal I_2)) are isomorphic iff there exist transports[F: L_1 \to L_2,\qquad G: L_2 \to L_1]such that:
Type bijection. (F) and (G) are type-correct on the admissible region and map admissible objects to admissible objects.
Meaning preservation. For all (x\in D_{1,\mathrm{adm}}),[G(F(x)) \approx_{L_1} x,]and for all (y\in D_{2,\mathrm{adm}}),[F(G(y)) \approx_{L_2} y,]with witnesses specified by the invariant schemas and corridor gates if approximate.
Address compatibility. There exists an address-algebra isomorphism (\Phi:\mathrm{Addr}_1\to \mathrm{Addr}_2) satisfying:[\mathrm{Enc}_2(F(x)) = \Phi(\mathrm{Enc}_1(x)),\qquad \mathrm{Dec}2(\Phi(\alpha)) \approx{L_2} F(\mathrm{Dec}_1(\alpha)),]on the addressable admissible region.
Convention compatibility or bridge. Either (\mathrm{ConvFP}(L_1)=\mathrm{ConvFP}(L_2)) under a declared compatibility predicate, or there exists an explicit bridge artifact with its own witnesses.
Isomorphism is not asserted by analogy; it is promoted only when the verifier can validate the bidirectional conditions under pinned policy/corridor.
Ch02.A3.b — Invariant Preservation Checks
Invariant preservation is the core legality obligation of transports and canonicalizers.
Predicate preservation. For each predicate (P\in\mathcal P), transports must satisfy:[P(x)=\mathrm{true} \Longrightarrow P'(T(x))=\mathrm{true},]where (P') is the corresponding predicate in the codomain schema. When schemas differ, preservation obligations are declared explicitly as part of the transport signature.
Observable coherence. For each observable (o\in\mathcal O), the transport must provide a coherence rule:[o'(T(x)) \equiv \tau_o(o(x),\theta),]where (\tau_o) is a declared transformation of observables under parameters (\theta). Coherence rules define witness residuals and are subject to corridor gates when approximate.
Equivalence respect. Transports must respect the equivalence relation:[x \approx_{L_1} y \Longrightarrow T(x) \approx_{L_2} T(y),]with explicit witness obligations in the approximate case.
Projection stability. If the codomain lens requires representability via projector (\Pi_h), the transport must satisfy:[T(x) \approx_{L_2} \Pi_h(T(x)),]with residual bounded by corridor gates. Failure triggers refusal or repair; it cannot be ignored.
Ch02.A3.c — Address Stability
Address stability ensures that extraction and replay remain meaningful across canonicalization and refinement.
Stable addressing predicate. A lens declares a predicate (\mathrm{StableAddr}_L(x)) indicating that (x) is fully addressable and slice/join semantics are total on the relevant address subset.
Canonicalization stability. For addressable (x), canonicalization may change internal representation but must preserve address semantics:[\mathrm{Slice}(x,\alpha);\approx_L;\mathrm{Slice}(\mathrm{NF}_L(x),\alpha)]for all admissible (\alpha) in the extraction domain.
Refinement stability. Under refinement operator (R_\kappa), addresses must not drift semantically:[\mathrm{Slice}(R_\kappa(x),\alpha) \approx_L \mathrm{Slice}(R_{\kappa'}(x),\alpha)]for (\kappa'\ge\kappa) within the stable region. If drift occurs, it is classified as either representability failure or convention mismatch; promotion is forbidden without explicit bridging.
Address normal form integrity. (\mathrm{NormAddr}) must be idempotent and must preserve denotation:[\mathrm{Dec}(\alpha) \approx_L \mathrm{Dec}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha)).]Violation indicates a malformed address algebra and forbids promotion of any artifact relying on such addresses.
Ch02.A3.d — Identity Tests
Identity tests are the verifier-executable procedures for determining whether two objects are equivalent within a lens or across lenses.
Internal identity (exact). If the lens provides a canonical normal form (\mathrm{NF}_L) and exact equality is meaningful:[x \approx_L y \iff \mathrm{NF}_L(x) = \mathrm{NF}_L(y).]
Internal identity (corridor-bounded). When equivalence is corridor-bounded, identity is decided by corridor obligations:[x \approx_{L,C} y \iff \forall j,;\rho_j(o_j(x),o_j(y)) \le \varepsilon_j(C),]computed via certified enclosures and projector stability requirements.
Cross-lens identity. Given (x\in D_i) and (y\in D_j), identity requires:
a declared transport route word (W: L_i \to L_j),
a commutation witness comparing (W(x)) to (y),
a loop-closure witness ensuring route consistency,
representability witness under the codomain projector.
Refusal determinacy. If identity cannot be decided under the pinned corridor/policy within budgets, the identity test must emit a refusal object with:
the attempted route word hash,
residual curve plateaus and dominance classification,
explicit next admissible actions (refinement, alternative transport, probe expansion, bridge requirement).
Ch02.A4 — Extract
Ch02.A4.a — Lens Registry Queries
The framework maintains a lens registry whose entries are immutable and hash-addressed. The minimal query surface is:
By lens id. Retrieve (L) by (\mathrm{id}(L)) with schema versions and fingerprints.
By carrier type. Retrieve all lenses with declared carrier type (\mathrm{Ty}(D)) matching a target (scalar, operator, function, etc.), optionally filtered by admissibility region tags.
By invariant schema keys. Retrieve lenses declaring required predicates/observables/laws, using schema lookup keys.
By compatibility class. Retrieve lenses compatible under declared convention predicates or connected by bridge artifacts, returning the shortest verified bridge chain.
Ch02.A4.b — Carrier Selection Rules
Carrier selection is a deterministic procedure driven by the target task and witness obligations.
Adequacy-first rule. Choose the minimal carrier type that admits the necessary observables to witness the required invariants. If the required invariants are loop/holonomy-sensitive, select a carrier admitting loop probes and projector stability checks.
Stability rule. Prefer carriers with stable address semantics and stable canonicalization under refinement. If a carrier exhibits non-identifiability plateaus under the intended corridor, escalation to richer carriers is mandatory.
Compatibility rule. Prefer carriers whose ConvFP and schema are compatible with the intended downstream transports. If compatibility fails, either select an alternative carrier or schedule a bridge obligation; silent mixing is forbidden.
Budget rule. Within admissible candidates, select carriers that satisfy policy budgets while preserving certification. Budget constraints may trigger use of acceleration charts, but never at the cost of witness obligations.
Ch02.A4.c — Schema Lookup Keys
Invariant schemas are indexed for deterministic retrieval. Each schema provides keys including:
Predicate keys. Names/identifiers for (P_k) invariants, including required parameterization and admissibility region constraints.
Observable keys. Identifiers for observables (o_j) and residual functionals (\rho_j), including projector requirements and norm choices.
Law keys. Identifiers for laws (\mathcal L): rewrite rules, required commutation squares, canonicalization obligations, admissibility sets.
Tolerance keys. Identifiers linking invariants to corridor gates: which residuals are gated by (\varepsilon_{\text{face}},\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\varepsilon_{\Pi}), and which κ-schedules are required.
Ch02.A4.d — Cross-Lens Links
Cross-lens links are typed edges in the metro memory web, never informal claims.
REF links. Reference relations indicating conceptual similarity without semantic equivalence; no witness obligations.
DEP links. Dependency relations indicating proof consumption; require receipt bindings and membership proofs.
EQUIV links. Equivalence claims between lenses or between objects across lenses; require commutation, holonomy, representability witnesses under pinned policies/corridors.
BRIDGE/MIGRATE links. Bridges reconcile convention or policy differences; migrations reconcile schema versions. Both require explicit witness obligations and preserve immutability of identities.
B. Flower
Ch02.B1 — Spec
Ch02.B1.a — Encode/Decode Operators
Each lens defines encode/decode operators that are part of the semantics, not merely I/O utilities.
Encode. (\mathrm{Enc}_L:D\to\mathrm{Addr}) maps objects to normalized addresses such that addresses can serve as stable extraction handles.
Decode. (\mathrm{Dec}_L:\mathrm{Addr}\to D) reconstructs an object (or an equivalent representative) from an address.
Correctness obligations.
For exact lenses: (\mathrm{Dec}_L(\mathrm{Enc}_L(x))=x).
For corridor-bounded lenses: (\mathrm{Dec}_L(\mathrm{Enc}L(x))\approx{L,C} x) under a declared corridor.
Projection alignment. Encode/decode must commute with the representability projector when required:[\Pi_h(\mathrm{Dec}_L(\alpha)) \approx_L \mathrm{Dec}_L(\alpha),]ensuring decoded objects are stable and canonical within the lens.
Ch02.B1.b — Change-of-Basis Transports
Change-of-basis transports formalize representation changes within compatible carrier classes.
Basis objects. A basis is an addressable object (B) in a lens family, pinned by its own hash identity and convention fingerprint.
Transport form. A change-of-basis transport (T_B) is typed and parameterized by a basis object:[T_B: (L,B)\to L']or within a lens family (L_\theta\to L_{\theta'}).
Witness obligations. Basis change must preserve invariants declared by (\mathcal I) and must provide commutation evidence when composed with other transforms.
Normalization coupling. If multiple bases exist, the lens must define basis-normalization rules; otherwise basis choice is semantic and must appear in ConvFP.
Ch02.B1.c — Normalization Operators
Normalization operators enforce canonicality within a lens.
Canonicalizer. (\mathrm{NF}_L:D\to D) is idempotent and meaning-preserving:[\mathrm{NF}_L(\mathrm{NF}_L(x))=\mathrm{NF}_L(x),\qquad \mathrm{NF}_L(x)\approx_L x.]
Address compatibility. Normalization preserves address semantics for extractable subobjects and stabilizes ordering.
Projection specialization. In corridor-bounded settings, (\mathrm{NF}_L) may be realized as (\Pi_h) plus deterministic simplification steps, with explicit residual obligations.
Normalization as gate. Promotion may require normalization stability checks as part of representability witnesses.
Ch02.B1.d — Composition Rules
Composition rules specify how encode/decode, transports, normalization, and evaluation compose into route words.
Typed composition. Only type-correct composition is admissible; untyped composition is forbidden.
Route words. A computation is a word (W=T_n\circ\cdots\circ T_1) with explicit segment stream encoding.
Commutation obligations. If a route is used to assert equivalence, required commutation squares are declared and must be witnessed.
Loop obligations. If a route forms a semantic loop, loop closure witnesses are mandatory; persistent loop residuals are interpreted by the obstruction machinery rather than ignored.
Ch02.B2 — Encode
Ch02.B2.a — Transport Signatures
A transport signature is the contract of a representation change.
Type signature.[T:;L_i \xrightarrow{\ \theta\ } L_j]with parameter schema (\theta) (finite typed fields) and declared admissibility predicates on inputs.
Operational signature. Each transport declares its operational class: encode, decode, normalize, evaluate, project, reduce, snap, tunnel, bridge, migrate.
Witness signature. Each transport declares which witness obligations it induces:
invariant preservation checks,
commutation checks with specified neighbors,
loop closure checks when part of a loop,
representability checks under projector/canonicalization.
Replay signature. Each transport declares replay requirements: determinism pins, method IDs, bound IDs, and any external dependencies (which, if present, are restricted to candidate generation unless independently certified).
Ch02.B2.b — Parameter Schemas
Parameter schemas are typed and canonicalized.
Typed fields. Each parameter has a declared type and admissible range; invalid parameters produce refusals.
Canonicalization of parameters. Parameters are serialized in canonical order with stable formatting; parameter canonicalization is part of WordNF identity.
Semantic vs numeric parameters. Semantic parameters are incorporated into ConvFP; numeric parameters are incorporated into corridor/policy bindings and segment payloads. Mixing is forbidden.
Budget parameters. Search caps, truncation depths, κ schedules, and tunneling budgets are explicit parameters subject to policy constraints.
Ch02.B2.c — Domain/Range Typing
Typing is enforced at the boundary of every transport.
Carrier typing. Each lens declares a carrier type interface; transports must map compatible carrier types or declare coercion rules with explicit witnesses.
Admissibility typing. Transports require admissibility: if (x\notin D_{\mathrm{adm}}), the transport must refuse rather than silently coerce.
Observable typing. Observables used in witnesses must be well-typed; undefined observables trigger refusal.
Witness typing. Witness objects are typed by the transports they certify; a witness is invalid if used outside its declared transport and corridor/policy scope.
Ch02.B2.d — Gate Catalogs
Gate catalogs are the finite “instruction set” of the transport calculus. Each gate has a signature and a required witness interface.
Evaluation gates. Certified evaluation producing enclosures and tail bounds; binds method IDs and tail-bound IDs.
Projection gates. (\Pi_h) projectors and canonicalizers; binds quantization policy and representability residual obligations.
Commutation gates. Gates producing commutation residual curves and requiring adequate probes; binds κ-grid.
Holonomy gates. Loop map construction and spin residual curves; binds loop word identity and probe generation.
Snap gates. Corridor-tightening repair with defect reduction obligations; binds corridor chain and budgets.
Tunnel gates. Representation-switch actions; recorded as segment events with pre/post corridor hashes and replay fields; promotion requires explicit certificate packs.
Ch02.B3 — Prove
Ch02.B3.a — Legality Predicate Templates
Legality predicates are fixed schemata applied uniformly across transports.
Type legality. Verify domain/range typing and admissibility predicates.
Convention legality. Verify ConvFP compatibility or require explicit bridge artifact; no implicit compatibility is permitted.
Policy legality. Verify all invoked gates are allowed and budgets are respected; disallowed operations force refusal.
Corridor legality. Verify all approximate obligations satisfy corridor gates at the required κ-grid; failure triggers Snap/tunnel/refusal protocol.
Ch02.B3.b — Pre/Post Conditions
Each transport carries a precondition (\mathrm{Pre}_T) and postcondition (\mathrm{Post}_T).
Preconditions. Membership in admissible region, parameter validity, required observables defined, required normalization state satisfied.
Postconditions. Output is in admissible region of codomain, invariants preserved, representability obligations satisfied (or witnessed as within corridor).
Compositional inheritance. For composed transports (T_2\circ T_1), the postconditions of (T_1) must imply the preconditions of (T_2), otherwise composition is illegal and must refuse.
Failure determinacy. If preconditions fail, the refusal class is TYPE/ADMISSIBILITY. If postconditions fail under budget, refusal class is CORRIDOR/UNCERTAINTY/HOLONOMY as determined by witness dominance.
Ch02.B3.c — Invariant Witnesses
Invariant witnesses are the objects that certify preservation and equivalence.
Preservation witnesses. For each invariant (P_k), the witness provides either a direct check or a reduction to observable residuals bounded by corridor.
Commutation witnesses. For routes intended to assert equivalence, witnesses include commutation residual curves over κ and adequate probes.
Holonomy witnesses. For loops, witnesses include loop maps and spin residual curves; persistent holonomy under refinement triggers obstruction analysis.
Representability witnesses. Witnesses certify stability under (\Pi_h) or (\mathrm{NF}_L) across κ; instability forbids promotion.
Ch02.B3.d — Replay Proofs
Replay proofs ensure that transports are not one-off executions but reusable proof-carrying objects.
