Word-based replay. The route word contains sufficient information to deterministically reproduce the computation steps, including gate segments and tunnel events.
Recompute requirement. Verifiers recompute witness residuals; stored residuals are treated as non-authoritative hints.
Environment pinning. Any dependence on external libraries or numeric semantics is pinned; if pinning is incomplete, promotion is forbidden.
Replay minimality. Replay bundles store hashes and reconstructible objects rather than bulk outputs; omission that prevents recomputation is illegal.
Ch02.B4 — Extract
Ch02.B4.a — Route Construction Recipes
Route construction is deterministic assembly under typing and legality constraints.
Target specification. Specify endpoint lens and desired invariant schema; identify source lens and carrier objects.
Transport selection. Select transports whose signatures unify; bind parameters under canonical schemas.
Witness planning. Determine required commutation squares, loop closures, and representability obligations; pre-allocate κ schedules and probes.
Route normalization. Normalize the route into WordNF; ensure segment stream is canonical and complete (including tunnel events if any).
Ch02.B4.b — Transport Lookup
Transport lookup is performed by typed queries over the transport registry.
By signature. Retrieve transports matching (L_i\to L_j) with required invariants and admissibility properties.
By gate class. Retrieve all transports in a gate catalog class (normalize, evaluate, project, tunnel).
By compatibility. Retrieve transports compatible under ConvFP/policy constraints or connected by bridge chains.
By verified status. Retrieve only transports with promoted witnesses for the required corridor/policy scope.
Ch02.B4.c — Nearest Hub Routing Heuristic
Routing is guided by canonical hubs: representations that serve as stable meeting points for many transports.
Hub definition. A hub lens is a lens with high connectivity and stable canonicalization, serving as a common intermediate for routing.
Heuristic rule. Prefer routes (L_s\to H\to L_t) where (H) is a hub that minimizes:
number of transports,
witness cost under required κ,
policy risk and bridge requirements.
Witness-based scoring. Candidate hubs are ranked by availability of promoted commutation/holonomy witnesses in the relevant corridor.
Failure-aware fallback. If a hub route fails, fallback routes are selected by altering the hub or enriching probes; silent substitution is forbidden.
Ch02.B4.d — Failure Localization
When route verification fails, localization identifies the minimal failing obligation.
Edge localization. Determine which transport step violates pre/post conditions or which commutation square violates corridor gates.
Face localization. When a commutation square fails, localize by comparing alternative path segments and isolating the earliest divergence in the segment stream.
Loop localization. When holonomy fails, localize which subloop contributes the dominant spin residual by restricting probes and recomputing loop maps on subwords.
Classification output. Emit a refusal artifact with failure class, minimal failing word segments, residual curves, and admissible remediation actions (refine κ, change carrier, require bridge, schedule tunnel, or escalate to obstruction analysis).
C. Cloud
Ch02.C1 — Spec
Ch02.C1.a — Tolerance-Bounded Invariants
Tolerance-bounded invariants extend exact invariants by binding them to corridor gates.
Bounded predicate. For invariants expressed by observables, define residuals (\rho_j) and gates (\varepsilon_j(C)) such that invariance becomes a checkable inequality.
Corridor dependence. The truth of an invariant claim is indexed by corridor; corridor changes therefore create new semantic regimes.
Witness coupling. Every tolerance-bounded invariant requires a witness demonstrating enclosure correctness and residual evaluation correctness.
No tolerance laundering. Failure under one corridor cannot be “solved” by silently widening; corridor changes require explicit re-verification and new receipts.
Ch02.C1.b — Approximate Equivalence
Approximate equivalence is not informal similarity; it is a corridor-defined equivalence-like relation.
Definition. (x \approx_{L,C} y) is defined by bounded residuals over a declared probe family and projector stability obligations.
Symmetry and reflexivity. These hold when residuals and projection are symmetric and stable; transitivity is permitted only when the schema provides explicit composition laws for residuals.
Scoped claims. Approximate equivalence claims are scoped to the corridor/policy; cross-scope reuse requires compatibility proofs.
Promotion discipline. Only approximate equivalence with explicit composition laws and replayable witnesses may be promoted as reusable equivalence edges.
Ch02.C1.c — Stability Regions
Stability regions define where approximate semantics are reliable.
Parameter region. Many lenses depend on parameters (\theta); stability regions specify subsets (\Theta_{\mathrm{stable}}\subseteq\Theta) where canonicalization, projection, and observables behave predictably.
Boundary behavior. Stability regions must declare boundary behavior; near boundaries, additional probes or alternative carriers may be required.
Refinement stability. Stability regions include monotone refinement conditions: residual curves should decrease or plateau below gates as κ increases.
Witness tagging. Witnesses and receipts include stability region identifiers; using a witness outside its region is illegal.
Ch02.C1.d — Admissibility Sets
Admissibility sets define which operations and approximations are allowed.
Lens admissibility. (D_{\mathrm{adm}}) restricts which objects are meaningful within the lens.
Transport admissibility. Each transport has an admissibility set for parameters and inputs; outside it, refusal is mandatory.
Approximation admissibility. Numerical methods are admissible only when their certification obligations are met under the corridor.
Policy admissibility. Policies restrict admissibility sets further; cross-policy reuse requires explicit bridges.
Ch02.C2 — Encode
Ch02.C2.a — Uncertainty Metadata
Uncertainty metadata binds approximate claims to their certification regime.
Corridor hash. Identifies ε-gates and κ schedule.
Probe hash. Identifies probe family generation rules and adequacy metadata.
Projection hash. Identifies (\Pi_h) or canonicalization regimes and rounding models.
Residual curve storage. Stores κ-indexed residual curves for commutation, holonomy, and representability, in canonical numeric encodings sufficient for verifier recomputation checks.
Ch02.C2.b — Numerical Method IDs
Every approximate computation binds to a method identifier.
Evaluator IDs. Each chart evaluation method has a stable method ID.
Transform IDs. Numerical transforms (integration, summation acceleration, transform inversion) have stable IDs.
Backend IDs. Numeric backend versions are pinned when they influence determinism; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Composition IDs. Composite methods (pipelines) are identified as words over method IDs, enabling replay and audit.
Ch02.C2.c — Tail-Bound IDs
Tail bounds are part of certification and must be indexed.
Bound family ID. Identifies the functional form and assumptions of the tail bound.
Bound parameterization. Tail bound parameters are canonicalized and bound to the route word.
Bound provenance. Derivation or reference to a bound lemma is recorded as an addressable artifact, not informal prose.
Bound audit hooks. The verifier can validate bound structure and recompute it when required.
Ch02.C2.d — Probabilistic vs Certified Tags
The framework distinguishes probabilistic and certified regimes explicitly.
Certified tag. Indicates enclosure-style guarantees: interval + tail + rounding.
Probabilistic tag. Indicates statistical confidence, sampling-based methods, or heuristic evidence; such artifacts cannot be promoted as semantic equivalences without certified confirmation.
Mixed tag. Indicates a pipeline containing probabilistic components used only for candidate generation; promotion requires certified verification.
Upgrade path tag. Records how a probabilistic artifact can be upgraded to certified status (required additional evidence and methods).
Ch02.C3 — Prove
Ch02.C3.a — Interval Composition
Interval composition is the core proof technique for certified numerics.
Closure under operations. If inputs are enclosed and operations are monotone-enclosure-preserving, outputs are enclosed.
Residual enclosure. Differences of routes are enclosed by subtraction of enclosures; residual norms are bounded by endpoint maxima under declared functionals.
Pipeline enclosure. Composite pipelines propagate enclosures; any operation lacking an enclosure law breaks certification and forbids promotion.
Verifier recomputation. The verifier recomputes enclosures and residuals from method IDs, bound IDs, and route words.
Ch02.C3.b — Stability Under Projection
Projection stability ensures representability is not violated by approximation artifacts.
Projection residual. Define representability residual (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)) over adequate probes.
Stability gate. Require (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)\le \varepsilon_{\Pi}) over κ-grid; failure triggers refusal or refinement.
Commutation with routes. Require that projection commutes with admissible transports up to corridor gates when specified by the schema.
No hidden canonicalization drift. If projection changes effective meaning under refinement, the lens’ ConvFP must change; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Ch02.C3.c — Bounded Drift
Bounded drift distinguishes acceptable approximation behavior from semantic drift.
Numeric drift vs semantic drift. Numeric drift is shrinking enclosure under refinement; semantic drift is change in canonical meaning class.
Drift detection. Drift is detected by non-monotone residual behavior, discontinuities across κ, or inconsistency under projection.
Bridge requirement. If drift is due to convention differences, an explicit convention bridge is required; drift cannot be “averaged out.”
Refusal over ambiguity. If drift cannot be classified within budgets, refusal is mandatory with evidence packs.
Ch02.C3.d — Non-Identifiability Triggers
Non-identifiability is the formal condition that available probes and corridors cannot decide equivalence.
Plateau criterion. Residual curves plateau above gates under κ refinement.
Probe inadequacy criterion. Increasing κ does not reduce residual uncertainty because the probe family does not separate equivalence classes.
Admissibility boundary criterion. Objects lie near stability boundaries where observables are ill-conditioned or undefined.
Trigger outcomes. Non-identifiability triggers refusal artifacts or carrier escalation requirements; it does not permit promotion.
Ch02.C4 — Extract
Ch02.C4.a — “Safe to Reuse” Queries
Safety is defined by the existence of promoted witnesses under the intended corridor/policy.
Promoted status. Retrieve only artifacts tagged promoted.
Scope match. Require matching or bridged policy/corridor hashes and ConvFP compatibility.
Witness sufficiency. Ensure commutation, holonomy, and representability witnesses exist and pass verifier recomputation.
Stability region match. Ensure witness stability region includes intended parameter regime.
Ch02.C4.b — “Needs Refinement” Queries
Refinement needs are detected by failing gates or by inadequate κ schedules.
Gate failures below budget. Residuals exceed gates but budgets allow κ escalation or alternative certified methods.
Uncertainty plateau. Residual plateau suggests probe inadequacy or ill-conditioning; refinement requires probe expansion or carrier escalation.
Boundary proximity. Stability region boundaries require additional safeguards or alternative representations.
Action outputs. Queries return admissible next steps: raise κ, switch chart, run Snap, schedule tunnel, expand probes.
Ch02.C4.c — “Blocked by Policy” Queries
Policy blocks are explicit and auditable.
Disallowed operations. Methods or tunnels not allowed by policy.
Budget exhaustion. Required κ refinement or verification cost exceeds policy budgets.
Missing bridges. Required convention/policy bridges are absent.
Quarantine restrictions. Imported or risky artifacts are quarantined and unavailable for reuse until re-verified.
Ch02.C4.d — Certificate Summaries
Certificate summaries provide compact, extraction-optimized views of validity.
Scope. Which lenses, carriers, corridors, and policies the certificate covers.
Gates. Max residuals over κ-grid for commutation, holonomy, representability.
Stability. Whether residuals decrease, plateau below gates, or plateau above gates.
Dependencies. Pins and witness roots required for replay verification.
D. Fractal
Ch02.D1 — Spec
Ch02.D1.a — Lens Families
A lens family is a parametrized collection of lenses ({L_\theta}_{\theta\in\Theta}) sharing a common carrier archetype and address grammar while varying parameters.
Shared schema core. Family members share invariant schema keys and address grammar, enabling structured transports across (\theta).
Parameter semantics. Parameters (\theta) are part of the lens identity if they affect meaning; otherwise they are method parameters and belong in route words.
Family transports. Families admit canonical transports (T_{\theta\to\theta'}) that are meaning-preserving under declared conditions.
Stability stratification. Families declare stability regions (\Theta_{\mathrm{stable}}) and boundary behaviors.
Ch02.D1.b — Lens Streams
A lens stream is a time-ordered or refinement-ordered sequence of lenses and objects:[(L_{\kappa_1},x_{\kappa_1})\to(L_{\kappa_2},x_{\kappa_2})\to\cdots]used to represent refinement, learning, or corridor tightening.
Stream identity. Streams are addressable objects with canonical logs.
Monotone refinement streams. Streams must satisfy monotone tightening obligations when used for certification.
Branching streams. Streams may branch under tunneling; branches are explicit and logged.
Stream witnesses. Stream-level witnesses certify convergence, stability, and absence of drift.
Ch02.D1.c — Refinement Operators
Refinement operators formalize zooming, tightening, and multi-scale structure.
Refinement map. (R_\kappa:D\to D) produces a refined representative under κ (precision, discretization, truncation).
Refinement legality. Refinement is legal only when it preserves admissibility and provides certified enclosure improvements or stable plateaus.
Refinement as program. Refinement steps are gates in route words and are subject to budgets and replay determinism.
Refinement closure. Refinement is meaningful only relative to the lens’ projector/canonicalization; refinement without representability control is forbidden for promotion.
Ch02.D1.d — Fixed-Point and Collapse Operators
Fixed-point and collapse operators formalize stable meaning under repeated refinement and representation changes.
Collapse operator. (\mathrm{Collapse}:D\to Z) maps refined states to a stable summary object (Z) (canonical representative, invariant signature, or equivalence class token).
Expand operator. (\mathrm{Expand}:Z\to D) reconstructs a representative in a chosen representation regime.
Fixed-point invariant. A collapse/expand pair must satisfy a stability invariant:[\mathrm{Collapse}(\mathrm{Expand}(Z)) = Z,]up to declared equivalence in the relevant lens.
Collapse in verification. Collapse operators serve as integrity guards: they enforce that different refinement paths converge to the same collapsed meaning token under promoted witnesses.
Ch02.D2 — Encode
Ch02.D2.a — Lens Stream Logs
Lens streams are encoded as deterministic logs.
Segmented logs. Logs are segment streams containing refinement steps, transport steps, Snap steps, and tunnel events.
Hash chaining. Each segment binds to previous segment hashes, producing a tamper-evident lineage.
Pinned contexts. Each segment includes policy/corridor hashes and ConvFP identifiers to prevent silent drift.
Probe binding. Stream logs bind probe generation rules used to validate convergence or stability.
Ch02.D2.b — Refinement Schedules
Refinement schedules specify κ ladders and escalation rules.
κ-grid encoding. κ grids are encoded canonically and hashed into corridors and witnesses.
Escalation policies. Escalation rules specify when to increase κ, when to switch carriers, and when to schedule tunnels.
Budget encoding. Refinement budgets are explicit and audited.
Schedule provenance. Schedules have provenance fields indicating whether they are default, demand-derived, or failure-derived.
Ch02.D2.c — Checkpointing
Checkpointing provides stable extraction points in streams.
Checkpoint objects. A checkpoint stores a collapsed token (Z), required pins, witness roots, and stream segment hash.
Reconstructability. From a checkpoint, reconstruction of verification context is deterministic.
Checkpoint selection. Checkpoints are created at phase boundaries: after baseline receipts, after closure UCWs, after successful tunnels, after promotions.
Checkpoint immutability. Checkpoints are immutable; updates create new checkpoints connected by migration edges.
Ch02.D2.d — Tunneling Indices
Tunneling indices make representation changes searchable and auditable.
Tunnel catalog. Index of tunnel events keyed by source/target lens ids, corridor hashes, and defect reduction metrics.
Admissibility index. Index of which tunnels are admissible under which policies and stability regions.
Replay index. Index of tunnel replay fields: enumerator states, budgets, and deterministic choices.
Promotion index. Index of promoted tunnel-derived bridges and their certificate packs.
Ch02.D3 — Prove
Ch02.D3.a — Expand/Collapse Invariants
Expand/collapse invariants formalize stability across representations.
Collapse consistency. Different routes that claim equivalence must collapse to the same token (Z) under promoted witnesses.
Expand legality. Expansion from (Z) into a chosen lens must satisfy admissibility predicates and provide representability stability.
Route independence. Collapse results must be independent of the specific refinement path when witnesses certify closure.
Failure handling. If collapse consistency fails, the system classifies failure as drift, non-identifiability, or missing modulus and emits refusal or discovery triggers accordingly.
Ch02.D3.b — Idempotence of Canonicalization
Canonicalization must be stable under repetition and refinement.
Idempotence. (\mathrm{NF}_L(\mathrm{NF}_L(x))=\mathrm{NF}_L(x)).
Meaning preservation. (\mathrm{NF}_L(x)\approx_L x), with explicit residual bounds when approximate.
Address preservation. Canonicalization preserves extraction semantics for addressable subobjects.
Refinement compatibility. Canonicalization commutes with refinement up to corridor gates; if not, it must be treated as a convention change requiring bridging.
Ch02.D3.c — Convergence Guards
Convergence guards prevent premature acceptance.
Monotone residual requirement. Residual curves must decrease or plateau below gates under κ refinement.
Adequacy requirement. Probe families must be adequate to distinguish equivalence classes; inadequacy triggers refusal and probe escalation.
Stability region requirement. Convergence claims are valid only within declared stability regions.
Cross-check requirement. Where available, multiple independent charts or transports must be cross-verified by commutation witnesses to prevent method-specific artifacts.
Ch02.D3.d — Termination Contracts
Termination contracts enforce finite, auditable behavior.
Budgeted termination. All searches, refinements, and tunnel operations terminate within declared budgets or refuse with exhaustion evidence.
Deterministic termination. Enumeration orders and stopping criteria are deterministic; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Refusal completeness. On termination without acceptance, refusal artifacts include minimal failing segments, residual curves, and admissible remediation actions.
Escalation termination. Escalation rules (refine → snap → tunnel → refuse → discover) are finite decision trees under bounded budgets; infinite loops are forbidden in promoted pipelines.
Ch02.D4 — Extract
Ch02.D4.a — Memory-Web Lifts
Memory-web lifts are the extraction mechanism for navigating from local artifacts to global structure.
Lift by lens. From a lens, lift to its family, its compatible lenses, and its bridge/migration neighbors.
Lift by object. From an object, lift to its lens, its canonical address, its witnesses, and its dependent receipts.
Lift by route. From a route word, lift to its segment stream, commutation squares, loop closures, and tunnel indices.
Lift by failure. From a refusal, lift to the minimal failing segment, the responsible invariant/schema key, and the remediation plan.
Ch02.D4.b — Auto-Link Rules
Auto-link rules determine which edges are created automatically and which require explicit proof.
REF auto-links. Conceptual similarity links may be auto-created based on schema key overlap without semantic claims.
DEP auto-links. Dependency links are created only when receipts explicitly reference pins.
EQUIV/BRIDGE links. Never auto-created; require promoted witnesses and compatibility obligations.
MIGRATE links. Created only by explicit schema migration processes that preserve evidence and replay.
Ch02.D4.c — Metro Station Codes
Station codes are deterministic identifiers for navigating the metro.
Lens station codes. Derived from lens id and carrier type, with stable disambiguation suffixes.
Carrier station codes. Derived from carrier taxonomy keys and stability region identifiers.
Schema station codes. Derived from invariant schema keys (predicates, observables, laws) for indexing and retrieval.
Route station codes. Derived from WordNF hashes, enabling route-centric navigation and audit.
Ch02.D4.d — Compaction Strategy
Compaction preserves replay while reducing storage and improving extraction performance.
Store hashes, not bulk. Prefer storing hash-addressed witness packs and regenerable objects over large intermediate numeric traces, subject to replay sufficiency.
Deduplicate by identity. Identical lenses, transports, and witnesses are deduplicated by hash identity; merging does not imply semantic equivalence.
Tiered retention. Keep promoted artifacts and refusal artifacts; allow pruning of non-promoted candidate logs when their derived information is preserved (e.g., by refusal summaries or discovered-chart registries).
Compaction invariants. Compaction must preserve:
the ability to replay verifier outcomes for promoted artifacts,
the ability to audit key failures and their classifications,
the ability to reconstruct the route words and tunnel indices used in promotion.
Ch02 — Lenses, Carriers, Address Algebras
Ch03 — Conventions, Canonicalization, and Fingerprints (ConvFP)
A. Square
Ch03.A1 — Spec
Ch03.A1.a — Meaning of “Convention”
A convention is any representational or procedural choice that is not determined solely by the abstract mathematical object intended, but that changes (i) the denotation of symbols, (ii) the selection of canonical representatives within an equivalence class, or (iii) the semantics of encode/decode and normalization operators. Conventions are therefore part of the semantics of a lens and must be treated as first-class, auditable objects.
Let (L=(D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I)) be a lens. A convention is modeled as a parameter tuple (c\in\mathcal C_L) together with a convention-dependent semantics map (\Sem_{L,c}) that assigns meaning to syntactic representations and defines canonicalization behavior. The pair ((L,c)) is a convention-instantiated lens, written (L[c]). The invariant schema (\mathcal I) is interpreted relative to (c), yielding:
a convention-dependent equivalence relation (\approx_{L[c]}),
convention-dependent canonicalization (\mathrm{NF}{L[c]}) (or projector (\Pi{h[c]})),
convention-dependent admissibility region (D_{\mathrm{adm}}[c]\subseteq D).
A convention is semantic iff there exists (x\in D_{\mathrm{adm}}[c]) such that (\Sem_{L,c}(x)\neq \Sem_{L,c'}(x)) for some (c'\neq c), or such that canonicalization differs in a way that changes equality/ordering/normal forms used in downstream obligations. Non-semantic “formatting preferences” are excluded; any choice that can influence a verifier’s acceptance predicate is semantic and must be included in the convention record.
A convention is scoped: it governs a declared set of operators and transforms (e.g., evaluation of complex logarithms, Fourier transforms, basis orderings, normalization of special functions, regularization schemes). Scope is explicit; a convention cannot be inferred from context.
Ch03.A1.b — Branch and Normalization Choices
Branch and normalization choices constitute the principal family of semantic conventions in analytic and computational mathematics. The framework treats each such choice as a typed component of (c\in\mathcal C_L).
Branch data for multivalued functions. For each multivalued function (f) used within the lens (e.g., (\log, z^\alpha, \sqrt{\cdot}, \operatorname{Li}_s, \Gamma, \zeta) under analytic continuation), the convention specifies:
branch cut geometry (set of excluded rays/curves),
principal argument interval and argument continuity policy,
monodromy conventions (how continuation around singularities is represented),
value selection at cut boundaries (one-sided limits, symmetric principal values, or explicit refusal).
Normalization data for transforms and special functions. For each transform/operator (T) (e.g., Fourier, Laplace, Mellin, z-transform) and each special-function normalization, the convention specifies:
scaling constants (e.g., (2\pi) placement in Fourier),
sign conventions (forward/inverse exponent sign),
unit conventions where dimensionful quantities exist,
normalization of basis elements (orthonormal vs orthogonal; phase conventions).
Basis and ordering conventions. For representations that require a basis (vector spaces, tensor decompositions, basis of constants at a given weight/depth, polynomial bases, monomial sets), the convention specifies:
basis choice (or basis family and selection rule),
basis ordering (deterministic total order),
coefficient normalization (primitive integer content, gcd normalization, sign normalization),
tie-breaking rules for equivalently admissible bases.
Regularization and renormalization conventions. Where divergent objects are assigned finite values (zeta regularization, principal value integrals, dimensional regularization), the convention specifies:
regularization scheme identifier,
subtraction scheme and counterterm conventions,
renormalization scale choices and how they enter meaning,
admissibility constraints delimiting where regularization claims are valid.
All such choices are semantic. Any evaluation or transport that depends on them is a function of (c), and its output is undefined without (c).
Ch03.A1.c — Semantic Drift Hazards
Semantic drift is any change in convention-instantiated meaning that is not captured by explicit identity changes in the artifact graph. Drift is hazardous because it produces false equivalences, invalid replays, and corrupted reuse.
Drift hazards are classified as follows:
Implicit branch drift. A library or algorithm changes branch defaults (e.g., argument interval changes, branch cut changes, continuation path changes). Symptoms include discontinuities across parameter sweeps and inconsistent loop closure under holonomy tests.
Normalization drift. Transform conventions or special-function normalizations change silently. Symptoms include systematic multiplicative factors or sign flips that appear as “mysterious constants” unless explicitly recognized as convention changes.
Ordering drift. Non-deterministic ordering of sets/maps or implicit sorting changes basis order, monomial order, or canonical representation, altering encode/decoding and hash commitments without explicit acknowledgement.
Canonicalization drift. The algorithm implementing (\mathrm{NF}{L[c]}) or (\Pi{h[c]}) changes (e.g., different quantization, different rounding model, different simplifier rules). Symptoms include “equivalence” that holds at one κ and fails at another.
Scope drift. A convention is applied outside its declared scope (e.g., branch-safe rules used in regions crossing cuts; regularization used outside admissible parameter region). Symptoms include plateau above gates and non-identifiability events mislabeled as numeric errors.
Policy-mediated drift. A policy change modifies admissible operations, budgets, or safety constraints, causing different routes to be used without explicitly changing semantic commitments. Symptoms include divergent WordNF selections for the same named target.
The framework forbids drift by requiring that all semantic choices be pinned into a convention fingerprint and bound into route identities and witnesses.
Ch03.A1.d — ConvFP Axioms
A convention fingerprint (\mathrm{ConvFP}) is a cryptographic commitment to a convention record. It is the unique identifier of the semantic regime in which a lens, transport, or evaluator is interpreted. The following axioms are enforced as verifiable obligations.
Completeness Axiom (semantic coverage). Every semantic convention affecting any acceptance predicate must be represented in the convention record. If two executions can produce different semantic denotations while sharing a ConvFP, then the ConvFP schema is incomplete and promotion is forbidden until repaired.
Sensitivity Axiom (change implies fingerprint change). If any semantic convention component differs, then (\mathrm{ConvFP}) must differ. Formally, for convention records (c\neq c') within scope,[\mathrm{canon}(c)\neq \mathrm{canon}(c')\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(c))\neq \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(c')).]
Stability Axiom (same implies same). If the convention record is unchanged under canonical encoding, (\mathrm{ConvFP}) is unchanged. This enforces reproducibility across time and across environments.
Locality Axiom (context independence). (\mathrm{ConvFP}) depends only on declared convention fields and their declared scope. It must not depend on runtime state, timestamps, machine locale, or library-internal nondeterminism.
Scope Axiom (explicit scope binding). A ConvFP is interpreted only on its declared operator scope. Using a ConvFP outside its scope is illegal and must trigger refusal or explicit scope-bridge obligations.
Bridge Explicitness Axiom. If two artifacts with different ConvFPs are treated as semantically equivalent, an explicit ConventionBridge artifact must exist and must be witnessed under the tri-gate acceptance logic. No “close enough” numeric agreement may bypass this axiom.
Auditability Axiom. The convention record committed by ConvFP must be human-auditable and machine-checkable: the system must support deterministic diffing of convention records and deterministic explanation of compatibility predicates.
Ch03.A2 — Encode
Ch03.A2.a — ConvFP Schema
A ConvFP is the hash of a canonical ConventionRecord. The record schema is normative and must be versioned. A minimal schema is:
Header fields.
convfp_version: schema version (major/minor/patch semantics),
lens_id: identity of the lens specification to which the convention applies,
carrier_type: declared carrier taxonomy key,
scope: explicit list of operator families governed by this record.
Branch specification fields.
branchcuts: declarative geometry for each multivalued primitive,
arg_policy: principal argument interval and boundary policy,
continuation_policy: rules for analytic continuation and monodromy representation,
singularity_policy: refusal/limit rules near singularities.
Normalization fields.
transform_norms: constants/sign conventions for each transform family,
special_function_norms: normalization identifiers for special functions,
unit_policy: dimension/units handling when applicable,
basis_norm: normalization rules for basis vectors/monomials.
Canonicalization and ordering fields.
canonical_form: identifiers and parameters for (\mathrm{NF}_{L[c]}),
projector: identifiers and parameters for (\Pi_{h[c]}),
ordering: deterministic ordering rules for address segments and bases,
tie_breakers: deterministic tie-breaking rules for ambiguous canonical choices.
Regularization fields (optional, scoped).
regularization_scheme: scheme ID,
subtraction_scheme: counterterm conventions,
scale_convention: renormalization scale semantics,
admissible_region: parameter region where regularization claims are valid.
Compatibility declarations (optional).
compat_predicates: identifiers for compatibility predicates against other versions or siblings,
bridge_templates: references to declared bridge templates applicable under stated conditions.
The ConvFP is defined as:[\mathrm{ConvFP}(c) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{ConventionRecord}(c))).]No additional hidden fields may influence ConvFP. The schema must be sufficient to reconstruct all semantic choices relevant to verification.
Ch03.A2.b — Stable Byte Encoding
Stable byte encoding is required so that ConvFP is invariant across environments and time.
Canonical encoding function. The framework defines a canonical encoding (\mathrm{canon}(\cdot)) with:
stable key ordering for maps,
stable ordering for lists (which must already be semantically ordered),
numeric fields serialized as normalized decimal strings (no locale dependence),
forbidden NaN/Infinity encodings unless explicitly specified and scoped.
Normalization of textual fields. All identifiers are normalized under a fixed Unicode normalization form and restricted to an allowed character set for security and reproducibility.
Explicit omission rules. Optional fields are either present with explicit null semantics or omitted under a rule that is itself part of the schema version. Ambiguity in omission is forbidden.
Self-description binding. The encoding includes convfp_version and scope so that future verifiers can interpret the record without external inference.
Ch03.A2.c — Version Tags
Versioning separates semantic evolution from implementation evolution.
Schema version. convfp_version increments:
major: incompatible meaning changes or schema field reinterpretation,
minor: additive fields with backward-compatible defaults that do not change semantics when omitted,
patch: clarifications that do not change canonical encoding.
Lens version linkage. A ConvFP binds to a specific lens specification version by lens_id. A change in lens schema or invariant semantics requires a new lens id and therefore cannot reuse old ConvFP unchanged.
Operator catalog version linkage. Conventions reference operator families by stable identifiers. If operator semantics change, either (i) operator IDs change, or (ii) the ConvFP scope explicitly pins operator version tags.
Verifier compatibility. Verifier versions declare which ConvFP schema versions they accept. A verifier must refuse to interpret unsupported ConvFP versions rather than silently approximating semantics.
Ch03.A2.d — Scope Fields
Scope determines where a convention applies and prevents accidental semantic overreach.
Scope declaration. Scope is an explicit set of operator-family identifiers, including:
evaluation primitives (e.g., log, pow, sqrt, polylog),
transform primitives (e.g., fourier, laplace, mellin),
canonicalization primitives (e.g., nf, projector, ordering),
regularization primitives (e.g., zeta_reg, pv_integral).
Scope-limited interpretation. Any artifact that invokes an operator outside scope must:
attach an additional ConvFP record covering that operator, or
refuse due to missing convention binding.
Scope composition. When a route involves multiple scoped convention records, the route binds to a ConvBundleFP computed from the ordered list of ConvFPs and their scopes, with deterministic ordering.
Scope compatibility. Two ConvFP records may be declared compatible on overlapping scope only by an explicit compatibility predicate or bridge artifact. Overlap without compatibility is a conflict.
Ch03.A3 — Prove
Ch03.A3.a — Drift Detection
Drift detection is a verifier-executable procedure that identifies semantic inconsistency.
Fingerprint mismatch detection. If two artifacts claim semantic equivalence while presenting different ConvFP values on overlapping scope, drift is detected unless a valid ConventionBridge exists. The verifier must refuse equivalence in the absence of a bridge.
Cross-κ consistency detection. If ConvFP remains constant but κ-indexed residual curves exhibit non-monotone behavior or discontinuities consistent with branch changes, the system classifies the event as suspected hidden convention drift (implementation drift) and issues a refusal requiring ConvFP schema repair or explicit environment pinning.
Normalization drift detection. If canonicalization outputs differ in a manner that changes address orderings or normal forms while ConvFP is unchanged, the drift is classified as canonicalization drift. Promotion is forbidden until the canonicalization parameters are incorporated into the ConvFP record.
Ordering drift detection. If basis or address ordering differs across replays, then either ordering is not pinned or the ordering rule is not deterministic. This violates ConvFP locality and must be corrected before promotion.
A drift detector must emit a DriftCert object containing: the compared ConvFP records, the conflicting scope intersection, and witness evidence (residual curves, counterexample probes, and route words).
Ch03.A3.b — Compatibility Checks
Compatibility checks determine whether two ConvFPs can be related without semantic contradiction.
Compatibility predicate. Define a predicate (\mathrm{Compat}(c_1,c_2)) that returns one of:
COMPATIBLE: conventions agree on the relevant scope or differ only in non-semantic fields,
BRIDGE_REQUIRED: conventions differ semantically but can be reconciled by a known bridge template,
INCOMPATIBLE: conventions conflict irreconcilably on the relevant scope under declared invariants.
Scope-restricted compatibility. Compatibility is computed on a declared operator scope intersection; compatibility on disjoint scopes is vacuous and does not imply global compatibility.
Witnessed compatibility. If compatibility is not syntactic identity, it must be supported by at least one promoted bridge witness showing that the transformations required by (\mathrm{Compat}) preserve the invariant schema under corridor gates.
Policy-sensitive compatibility. Compatibility depends on policy: a bridge that is admissible under one policy may be forbidden under another. Thus, compatibility results must be indexed by policy hash.
Ch03.A3.c — Equivalence Under Allowed Bridges
Equivalence across conventions is permitted only under an explicit bridging discipline.
ConventionBridge artifact. A ConventionBridge (B) is a typed transport object:[B:; L[c_1] \to L[c_2]]with declared scope, admissibility conditions, and witness obligations.
Bridge correctness. Bridge correctness requires:
invariant preservation in the codomain schema,
commutation witness: the bridge commutes with required canonicalization and evaluation gates on scope,
loop closure witness: applying (B) and returning (if an inverse exists or a round-trip path exists) closes within spin gates,
representability witness: projected canonical forms remain stable.
Bridge promotion. A bridge is promotable only if:
a UCW exists for required commutation squares,
loop closure under ConvFP is certified,
any required stress/regression obligations are satisfied under the policy,
the bridge’s WordNF and parameters are fully pinned and replayable.
Bridge usage in equivalence. For objects (x\in L[c_1]) and (y\in L[c_2]), an equivalence claim must provide:[\mathrm{UCW}\bigl(B(x),y\bigr)\quad\text{under fixed policy/corridor and declared probes,}]and must bind both ConvFPs plus the bridge identity into the receipt. Numeric proximity without the bridge is insufficient.
Ch03.A3.d — Conflict Proofs
A conflict proof establishes that two conventions cannot be reconciled within declared invariants under admissible bridges.
Conflict definition. A conflict exists when:
(\mathrm{Compat}(c_1,c_2)=\mathrm{INCOMPATIBLE}), or
all admissible bridge templates fail verification, or
equivalence claims across (c_1,c_2) lead to contradictory UCW outcomes on an adequate probe family.
Conflict certificate. A conflict certificate must include:
the two ConventionRecords and their ConvFPs,
the conflicting scope intersection,
the minimal set of invariants/observables witnessing the contradiction,
counterexample probes (including boundary probes near branch cuts when relevant),
the route words attempted and their refusal artifacts.
Portal classification. If the conflict arises from intrinsic monodromy or topological obstruction that cannot be bridged by admissible transports, the conflict proof may classify the situation as requiring a portal (new semantic alphabet, new carrier, or new invariant schema) rather than a mere convention bridge.
Downstream safety. Conflict proofs prevent accidental merging: the system must quarantine edges that attempt to equate incompatible conventions without bridges and must block promotions dependent on such edges.
Ch03.A4 — Extract
Ch03.A4.a — Convention Diffs
Convention diffs provide deterministic, auditable explanations of how semantics differ.
Record-level diff. Compute diffs over canonical ConventionRecords, preserving scope structure and showing field-level changes.
Semantic vs non-semantic diff. Diff output is stratified: fields marked semantic by schema are separated from non-semantic fields. Any semantic field change implies ConvFP change.
Impact projection. For each changed field, list impacted operator families and affected witness obligations (commutation, holonomy, representability).
Counterexample hints. When available, include minimal counterexample probes where the two conventions produce distinct results beyond corridor gates.
Ch03.A4.b — Compatibility Reports
A compatibility report summarizes the result of compatibility analysis.
Scope statement. Explicitly state the scope intersection for which compatibility is evaluated.
Outcome. COMPATIBLE, BRIDGE_REQUIRED, or INCOMPATIBLE, each with explicit reasons tied to schema keys.
Bridge plan. If BRIDGE_REQUIRED, list admissible bridge templates, required witnesses, and expected costs under policy budgets.
Policy indexing. Reports are indexed by policy hash and corridor hash; changing either invalidates the report unless bridged.
Ch03.A4.c — Bridge Requirements
Bridge requirement extraction is the mechanism that turns semantic mismatch into actionable obligations.
Required bridge type. Determine whether a convention bridge, policy bridge, or portal is required.
Obligation pack. For a required bridge, extract:
bridge signature,
admissibility conditions,
required UCWs (commutation squares and loop closures),
required regression/stress suites.
Minimal witness set. Produce the minimal witness plan sufficient for promotion, including probe adequacy obligations.
Refusal integration. If no bridge is admissible under current policy, produce a refusal requiring policy change or alternate representation.
Ch03.A4.d — Audit Keys
Audit keys are stable handles enabling verification and forensic reconstruction.
ConvFP audit key. The ConvFP hash and its decoded ConventionRecord (schema-versioned).
Scope audit key. Scope intersection identifiers and operator-family version tags.
Bridge audit key. Bridge identities, WordNF hashes, and witness roots used to reconcile conventions.
Drift audit key. DriftCert or ConflictCert identities plus counterexample probe hashes and residual curve hashes enabling replay localization.
B. Flower
Ch03.B1 — Spec
Ch03.B1.a — Convention-Aware Transports
A transport is convention-aware when its legality and semantics depend on the ConvFP of its domain and codomain.
Transport instantiation. A transport (T) is instantiated as (T[c_i\to c_j]), binding to domain convention (c_i) and codomain convention (c_j). The instantiation is part of the route word identity.
Convention obligations. (T[c_i\to c_j]) declares:
the scope of conventions it respects,
required compatibility predicates or required bridges,
how observables transform under the convention mapping.
Invariant preservation under convention. Preservation obligations are evaluated within (L[c_i]) and (L[c_j]) respectively, and cannot be mixed.
Refusal on ambiguity. If a transport’s semantics is ambiguous under a given convention (e.g., evaluation crosses a branch cut without specified boundary policy), the transport must refuse rather than choose a default.
Ch03.B1.b — Branch-Safe Routes
A route is branch-safe when it stays within declared stability regions and does not cross branch cuts without explicit continuation policy.
Branch safety predicate. Each convention-instantiated lens (L[c]) defines a predicate (\mathrm{BranchSafe}_{L[c]}(x;\theta)) specifying where multivalued primitives are single-valued and continuous along the route.
Path admissibility. For analytic continuation routes, admissibility includes:
a declared continuation path class,
evidence that the path avoids prohibited cut sets or uses explicit monodromy handling,
boundary evaluation policies when approaching singularities.
Witness coupling. Branch safety is not assumed; it is either (i) proven by constraints in the admissibility set, or (ii) tested by adversarial probes near cuts and validated under corridor gates.
Failure classification. Violations of branch safety are classified as convention failures, not numeric uncertainty; they require convention updates or bridges, not increased precision.
Ch03.B1.c — Normalization Gates
Normalization gates are explicit route segments that apply canonicalization within a convention-instantiated lens.
Normalization gate semantics. A normalization gate applies (\mathrm{NF}{L[c]}) or (\Pi{h[c]}) and emits representability residual obligations.
Gate idempotence obligation. Normalization gates must satisfy idempotence:[\mathrm{NF}{L[c]}(\mathrm{NF}{L[c]}(x))=\mathrm{NF}_{L[c]}(x),]up to corridor-bounded equivalence where declared.
Commutation obligations. Normalization gates must commute with declared transports where required by schema; commutation is witnessed through UCWs.
Normalization refusal. If normalization is ill-posed (e.g., multiple canonical candidates under insufficient ordering rules), normalization must refuse unless tie-breakers are explicitly pinned in ConvFP.
Ch03.B1.d — Canonical Order Rules
Canonical order rules determine deterministic encoding and canonical basis selection.
Order rule definition. For each addressable composite structure (basis lists, monomial sets, graph labelings), define a deterministic total order (\preceq_c) scoped by ConvFP.
Order stability. Order must be stable under refinement; otherwise it is a semantic change and requires ConvFP change.
Tie-breakers. When semantic order is underdetermined, tie-breakers must be deterministic and pinned by ConvFP (e.g., hash-based tie-breaking using canonical encodings).
Order witness obligations. Routes relying on ordering for canonicalization must include representability witnesses ensuring that canonical forms are stable under the declared ordering.
Ch03.B2 — Encode
Ch03.B2.a — Route Binds to ConvFP
Every route word binds to the conventions under which it is interpreted.
Conv bundle binding. A route word contains:
ConvFP of each lens instance in scope,
ConvFP of each transport instantiation if transport semantics depends on convention,
ConvBundleFP computed deterministically from the ordered set of ConvFPs and scopes.
Scope coverage check. The verifier checks that every operator invoked in the route is covered by some ConvFP in the bound bundle; uncovered operators force refusal.
No implicit inheritance. Conventions are not inherited implicitly from earlier sections of a route; every segment must state which convention it is executed under.
Convention segregation. If a route transitions between conventions, the transition must be represented as an explicit bridge or migration segment; otherwise the route is ill-typed.
Ch03.B2.b — Gate Parameter Hashes
Gate parameters affecting semantics or verification obligations are hashed and bound into WordNF.
Semantic gate parameters. Parameters that affect semantic choices must be in ConvFP; their hashes appear indirectly via ConvFP hash binding.
Numeric gate parameters. Parameters such as κ-grid, truncation depths, tail-bound methods, and rounding cushions are bound to corridor/policy and included explicitly in gate segment payloads.
Probe parameters. Probe family generation rules are hashed and bound; probe adequacy metadata is part of witness obligations.
Segment-level commitments. Each gate segment includes a segment hash that commits to parameters, scope, and the ConvBundleFP, ensuring that replay cannot substitute parameters silently.
Ch03.B2.c — WordNF Normalization Under Conventions
WordNF normalization must respect convention boundaries.
Normalization invariance. WordNF canonicalization may rewrite routes only under declared commutation rules and only when conventions on the affected scope are compatible or explicitly bridged.
No cross-scope commutation. Reordering segments across operators with different convention bindings is forbidden unless a commutation witness exists that is valid under both conventions and scope.
Segment stream stability. Gate runs and tunnel events form an ordered stream; normalization may simplify identity segments only when identity is semantic identity under the bound ConvFP and corridor.
Normalization proof obligations. Any nontrivial WordNF rewrite that could affect semantics must have an associated witness obligation (commutation or equivalence) and must be replayable.
Ch03.B2.d — Deterministic Sorting Rules
Deterministic sorting is a convention-controlled semantic operation.
Sorting specification. Sorting rules specify comparator semantics, key extraction rules, and tie-breakers, all pinned by ConvFP.
Sorting scope. Sorting is restricted to specific object families (addresses, bases, monomials, graph labels); applying sorting outside declared scope is illegal.
Sorting under refinement. Sorting must not depend on refinement-dependent numeric approximants unless explicitly declared and proven stable; otherwise sorting-induced drift occurs.
Sorting auditability. Sorting decisions must be auditable by storing comparator identifiers and by enabling reconstruction of the sort order from canonical encodings.
Ch03.B3 — Prove
Ch03.B3.a — Route Equivalence vs Semantic Equivalence
Route equivalence and semantic equivalence are distinct.
Route equivalence. Two computations are route-equivalent iff their WordNF hashes match exactly. This is purely syntactic/operational identity.
Semantic equivalence. Two results are semantically equivalent iff there exists a witness pack (UCW + auxiliary certificates) establishing commutation, loop closure, and representability under pinned ConvFP, corridor, and policy.
Non-implication. Route equivalence does not imply semantic equivalence when the route is ill-typed, under-scoped, or violates admissibility; semantic equivalence does not imply route equivalence because different routes may be equivalent under witnessed commutation.
Receipt semantics. Receipts bind semantic equivalence via witness roots; they do not bind route equivalence except through WordNF hash commitments.
Ch03.B3.b — Commutation Under Conventions
Commutation claims are meaningful only relative to conventions.
Convention-scoped commutation. A commutation square is defined by two route words (W_1,W_2) sharing endpoints under the same ConvBundleFP (or bridged bundle). Commutation is witnessed only in that scope.
Branch-aware probe adequacy. Probe families used for commutation must include adversarial probes near branch boundaries when the scope includes multivalued primitives. Otherwise commutation may be illusory.
Commutation under bridges. If routes differ in convention, commutation requires explicit bridge insertion and witness that the bridge commutes with relevant gates.
Failure classification. If commutation fails and ConvFP differs, the failure is classified as convention conflict unless a bridge exists and fails; only then does the failure proceed to holonomy or uncertainty classification.
Ch03.B3.c — Portal Necessity Tests
Portals are required when semantic differences cannot be reconciled by admissible convention bridges.
Portal trigger. A portal is required if:
conventions are incompatible and no admissible bridge exists, or
holonomy evidence indicates intrinsic monodromy or topological obstruction not capturable by scope-limited bridges, or
equivalence would require extending the invariant schema (new observables, new carrier, new meaning class).
Necessity witness. Portal necessity is witnessed by a ConflictCert indicating:
incompatibility on an adequate probe family,
failure of all admissible bridge templates under policy budgets,
stability of the conflict under κ refinement and under projector checks.
Portal semantics. A portal introduces a new semantic alphabet: new carrier, new invariant schema, or new transport class. It is not a “workaround”; it is a structural extension.
Safety invariant. When a portal is necessary, the system must forbid equivalence edges between the conflicting conventions and must quarantine any downstream claims that assumed such equivalence.
Ch03.B3.d — Loop Closure Under ConvFP
Loop closure is convention-dependent and is the principal tool for detecting hidden semantic inconsistencies.
Convention-aware loop map. The loop map (L_{\square}) is defined by the encode/decode and normalization semantics of the conventions bound to the loop’s WordNF. Changing ConvFP changes the loop map.
Spin residual under ConvFP. The spin residual curve (s_{\square}(\kappa)) must be computed using probe families that are adequate under the convention scope. In multivalued scopes, probes must include loops around singularities that test monodromy.
Interpretation of failure. Persistent spin residuals under fixed ConvFP indicate either (i) a missing modulus (kernel incompleteness) or (ii) a portal (intrinsic obstruction). If ConvFP changes across attempts, the failure is convention drift, not kernel incompleteness.
Closure as admissibility criterion. Promoting bridges and transports across conventions requires loop closure witnesses; otherwise the bridge may introduce path-dependent semantics.
Ch03.B4 — Extract
Ch03.B4.a — “Same Value, Different Conventions” Reports
A report of this form is an extraction artifact that explicitly separates numeric agreement from semantic reconciliation.
ConvFP disclosure. Report includes both ConvFP records and their scope intersection.
Agreement evidence. Provide certified enclosure overlap evidence and UCW commutation evidence when available.
Bridge status. Indicate whether a promoted convention bridge exists; if not, the report declares equivalence non-promotable and lists bridge requirements.
Risk classification. Classify whether the situation is low-risk (simple normalization factor), medium-risk (basis reorder), or high-risk (branch/monodromy), and specify required stress/regression obligations.
Ch03.B4.b — Convention Bridge Catalogs
Bridge catalogs store reusable bridges as promoted artifacts.
Bridge indexing. Bridges are indexed by source ConvFP, target ConvFP, scope, policy hash, and corridor class.
Witness indexing. Each bridge entry includes witness roots: UCWs, loop closure certificates, representability certificates, stress/regression certificates as required.
Cost indexing. Store verified cost models (latency/precision) for scheduler selection, without affecting semantic acceptance.
Deprecation flags. Bridges can be deprecated via migration edges; deprecated bridges remain replayable but are not preferred.
Ch03.B4.c — Safe Route Selection
Safe route selection chooses routes that are both admissible and witnessable under conventions.
Convention closure preference. Prefer routes whose ConvBundleFP is stable and requires no bridges for the target scope.
Witness availability. Prefer routes with existing promoted UCWs and existing loop closure certificates.
Branch-safe preference. Prefer routes staying within stability regions away from branch boundaries unless continuation is explicitly planned and witnessed.
Fallback discipline. If the preferred route fails, fallbacks must be selected by explicit rule (change carrier, insert bridge, increase probes), never by silent substitution.
Ch03.B4.d — Change Logs
Change logs provide longitudinal traceability of convention evolution.
ConvFP lineage. For each ConvFP, record predecessor/successor relations via migration edges, with reasons and scope.
Affected artifacts. List artifacts whose witness obligations depend on the changed scope; schedule re-verification jobs deterministically.
Compatibility outcomes. Record compatibility reports and bridge promotions induced by the change.
Reproducibility impact. Record whether old artifacts remain replay-verifiable under preserved verifier versions and whether backward witnesses are maintained.
C. Cloud
Ch03.C1 — Spec
Ch03.C1.a — Uncertainty vs Convention
Uncertainty and convention are orthogonal dimensions.
Uncertainty. Uncertainty quantifies epistemic or numeric enclosure width: it is governed by corridor gates, κ refinement, tail bounds, and rounding models.
Convention. Convention selects semantic meaning among multiple possible denotations or canonical representatives. Convention is discrete, scope-dependent, and not reducible to smaller tolerances.
Non-substitutability. Increasing precision cannot repair a wrong branch choice; tightening corridors cannot reconcile incompatible normalizations; numeric agreement within tolerance does not imply semantic compatibility.
Witness separation. UCW tri-gate logic separates face/spin/projection residuals; convention conflicts are detected via ConvFP mismatch and bridge absence, not via numeric residual magnitudes.
Ch03.C1.b — Tolerances Do Not Excuse Drift
Corridor gates bound numeric discrepancies; they do not license semantic drift.
ConvFP precedence. If ConvFP mismatches on relevant scope and no bridge exists, equivalence is forbidden regardless of enclosure overlap.
No accidental promotion. A candidate discovered under one convention cannot be promoted as a truth artifact under another convention without explicit bridging and re-verification.
κ escalation constraint. Escalating κ cannot change ConvFP; if the implementation changes semantics as κ changes, this is drift and must be refused.
Scope consistency. Corridors may vary across tasks; ConvFP is independent of corridors except for canonicalization/rounding fields explicitly scoped in convention. Mixing is forbidden.
Ch03.C1.c — Stability Constraints
Stable verification requires stable convention behavior across refinement and across admissible parameter regions.
Stability region declarations. Conventions must declare stability regions for multivalued primitives and regularization schemes.
Boundary guarding. Near stability boundaries, additional probes are mandatory; otherwise non-identifiability may masquerade as agreement.
Cross-κ stability. Residual curves are interpreted only under fixed ConvFP; if ConvFP changes, residual curves are not comparable and must be refused.
Canonicalization stability. Canonicalization and ordering under ConvFP must not vary with κ unless κ-dependence is explicitly declared and proven safe; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Ch03.C1.d — Policy Constraints
Policies govern which conventions are admissible and which bridges are permitted.
Allowed convention set. Policies may restrict branch conventions (e.g., principal branch only) or forbid regularization schemes.
Bridge admissibility. Policies may forbid certain bridges due to risk, nondeterminism, or cost; in such cases equivalence across conventions is blocked until policy changes.
Budgeted verification. Policies cap κ schedules, stress/regression suites, and search budgets; refusal artifacts must state when policy budgets prevent decisive verification.
Quarantine. Policies may quarantine imported or untrusted convention regimes until local verification is performed.
Ch03.C2 — Encode
Ch03.C2.a — ConvFP + Corridor Joint Hash
Witnesses bind both convention and uncertainty regimes.
Joint binding. For any witness, define:[\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{joint}} := \mathrm{H}!\left(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},|,\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{Corridor}),|,\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{Policy})\right).]This joint hash commits the verifier to interpret residual curves under fixed semantic and numeric regimes.
No commutation without joint binding. UCW objects must include ConvFP hashes and corridor hashes; omitting either is invalid for promotion.
Scope indexing. Joint hashes are scope-indexed: a witness is valid only for its declared scope and cannot be reused outside that scope.
Compatibility stratification. Compatibility and bridge obligations are indexed by joint hash: a bridge valid under one corridor/policy may not be valid under another.
Ch03.C2.b — Admissible Ranges
Admissible ranges are required to prevent applying conventions outside their stable regions.
Parameter admissibility. For each operator family, conventions declare admissible parameter subsets where branch and normalization semantics are stable.
Runtime checks. Routes must include admissibility checks or must restrict probe families to admissible regions; otherwise refusal is mandatory.
Admissible-range hashing. Admissible ranges are part of the ConvFP record when they affect semantic meaning; if they are purely operational guardrails, they are part of policy.
Boundary probes. Where admissible ranges have boundaries, probes must include boundary-near samples to detect hidden crossing under refinement.
Ch03.C2.c — Drift Alarms
Drift alarms are encoded as structured events.
Alarm triggers. Triggers include ConvFP mismatch under equivalence claims, ordering instability, canonicalization non-idempotence, cross-κ inconsistency, and branch discontinuity signatures.
Alarm payload. Alarm includes: compared ConvFP hashes, scope intersection, route word hashes, residual curves, and counterexample probes.
Alarm severity. Severity is determined by scope and risk class: branch drift is high-severity; ordering drift is medium; non-semantic field drift is low.
Alarm lineage. Alarms are hash-addressed and linked to subsequent remediation artifacts and re-verification jobs.
Ch03.C2.d — Refusal Formats
Convention-related refusals are typed and structured.
Refusal classes. At minimum:
CONV_MISMATCH_NO_BRIDGE,
CONV_SCOPE_GAP,
CONV_ORDERING_NONDETERMINISM,
CONV_BRANCH_UNSAFE,
CONV_CANONICALIZATION_DRIFT,
CONV_INCOMPATIBLE_PORTAL_REQUIRED.
Required fields. Each refusal includes: ConvFP records/hashes, scope, attempted WordNF, policy/corridor hashes, residual curves, and admissible next actions.
κ evidence. When applicable, refusals include cross-κ evidence distinguishing drift from plateau uncertainty.
Remediation obligations. Refusals specify whether remediation requires (i) bridge creation, (ii) ConvFP schema expansion, (iii) carrier escalation, or (iv) portal creation.
Ch03.C3 — Prove
Ch03.C3.a — Plateau vs Drift Distinction
Plateau and drift are distinct failure modes and must be distinguished deterministically.
Plateau (uncertainty). Residual curves plateau above gates under fixed ConvFP and stable implementation semantics; probe adequacy is insufficient or the corridor is too tight for available methods.
Drift (semantic). Residual behavior changes in a manner inconsistent with monotone refinement under fixed ConvFP, or equivalently, the same ConvFP produces different canonicalization/ordering outcomes across replays or environments.
Decision procedure. A verifier distinguishes them by:
checking ConvFP equality and scope coverage,
validating canonicalization idempotence,
comparing residual curves across κ for monotonicity and continuity,
executing adversarial boundary probes for branch-sensitive scopes.
Outcome. Plateau triggers refinement or probe escalation; drift triggers refusal requiring ConvFP repair or explicit environment pinning and migration.
Ch03.C3.b — Cross-κ Consistency Tests
Cross-κ consistency tests enforce that refinement improves certainty without changing semantics.
Monotone tightening test. Under fixed ConvFP, enclosure radii and residual curves must weakly decrease or plateau below gates as κ increases.
Branch continuity test. For branch-sensitive scopes, the route must exhibit continuity across κ at fixed probes unless probes cross declared branch boundaries; otherwise drift is suspected.
Canonicalization stability test. (\mathrm{NF}_{L[c]}) and ordering must be stable across κ; changes imply ConvFP incompleteness or implementation drift.
Probe adequacy test. If residual curves plateau, the system tests whether probe families distinguish the relevant equivalence classes; inadequacy triggers refusal or probe expansion.
Ch03.C3.c — Regression Triggers
Regression ensures that convention evolution does not corrupt promoted knowledge.
Trigger conditions. Any of:
ConvFP schema version changes,
changes to canonicalization implementation,
bridge promotions affecting widely used scopes,
migration edges declaring new “current” conventions.
Regression obligations. Re-verify a fixed suite of UCWs and loop closures spanning:
core commutation squares,
branch-sensitive boundary probes,
ordering/canonicalization stability checks.
Failure handling. Regression failures produce refusal artifacts and can quarantine dependent edges; no silent acceptance of degraded correctness.
Bisectability. Regression artifacts include enough lineage information (hash ancestry, scope, route words) to bisect and localize drift.
Ch03.C3.d — Adversarial Drift Tests
Adversarial drift tests target the boundary cases where convention errors hide.
Branch boundary probes. Probes near branch cuts and singularities to detect inconsistent argument policies and monodromy handling.
Ordering adversaries. Probes designed to create ambiguous orderings (equal keys, near-equal numeric keys) to test tie-breaker determinism.
Normalization adversaries. Inputs whose canonical forms are sensitive to simplifier heuristics, ensuring that canonicalization parameters are properly pinned.
Regularization adversaries. Inputs near divergence thresholds to ensure regularization admissibility constraints are enforced and do not silently widen.
Adversarial tests are mandatory for high-risk scopes and for promoting bridges affecting branch-sensitive semantics.
Ch03.C4 — Extract
Ch03.C4.a — Drift Dashboards
A drift dashboard is an extraction artifact summarizing convention integrity status.
Scope coverage view. Which operator scopes are covered by ConvFP bundles in promoted artifacts.
Mismatch view. List of equivalence attempts blocked by ConvFP mismatch without bridges.
Instability view. Ordering/canonicalization instability events and their lineage.
Resolution view. Status of bridge requirements, migrations, and quarantines.
Ch03.C4.b — Blocked Edges List
Blocked edges are edges forbidden for promotion due to convention/policy constraints.
No-bridge blocks. Edges requiring convention bridges not yet promoted.
Policy blocks. Edges whose required bridges or routes are forbidden by policy.
Scope-gap blocks. Edges invoking operator scopes not covered by convention records.
Drift blocks. Edges blocked due to detected drift or unresolved conflict proofs.
Ch03.C4.c — Remediation Plans
Remediation plans are deterministic action lists derived from refusal artifacts and drift alarms.
Bridge remediation. Specify the required bridge signature, scope, admissibility conditions, and witness obligations.
ConvFP remediation. Specify missing schema fields or incomplete scope declarations that must be added to eliminate drift.
Carrier remediation. Specify carrier escalation when non-identifiability cannot be resolved within current probes/corridors.
Portal remediation. Specify portal creation requirements when incompatibility is intrinsic.
Ch03.C4.d — Reproducibility Checklist
A reproducibility checklist is the minimal set of questions a verifier must answer affirmatively to accept promoted artifacts across environments.
ConvFP availability. All ConvFP records referenced by receipts and witnesses are present, decodable under supported schema versions, and scope-complete.
Environment pinning. All implementation-dependent semantics are pinned (backend versions, canonicalization parameters) or are provably irrelevant under certification.
Witness recomputation. UCWs and auxiliary certificates can be recomputed deterministically and meet tri-gate predicates.
Drift absence. No outstanding drift alarms affecting the artifact’s scope; if alarms exist, corresponding quarantines or bridges are present.
D. Fractal
Ch03.D1 — Spec
Ch03.D1.a — Convention Evolution Without Corruption
Convention evolution is permitted only as explicit, auditable graph evolution.
Immutability of promoted semantics. A promoted artifact’s meaning cannot change; evolution creates new ConvFPs and new artifacts linked by migration and bridge edges.
Explicitness. Any evolution step must declare scope, compatibility intent, and witness obligations.
Audit preservation. Evolution must preserve the ability to audit past results: old ConvFP records remain readable, and old witnesses remain replay-verifiable under pinned verifier versions or via translators that are themselves verified.
No silent retcon. Changing a “default convention” does not rewrite historical artifacts; it changes only “current pointers” used by planners.
Ch03.D1.b — Deprecation Rules
Deprecation is a governance mechanism, not deletion.
Deprecation markers. A deprecated ConvFP remains valid but is not preferred for new constructions; it retains full replay and audit support.
Deprecation reasons. Reasons are explicit: ambiguity, nondeterminism, risk, inefficiency, or supersession by a better canonicalization.
Deprecation obligations. Deprecation may require providing a migration or bridge path to a preferred ConvFP; absence of such a path implies partial deprecation with restricted scope.
Quarantine distinction. Deprecation differs from quarantine: quarantined conventions are blocked from reuse pending resolution; deprecated conventions remain admissible but discouraged.
Ch03.D1.c — Bridge Promotion
Bridge promotion converts ad hoc reconciliations into reusable tissue.
Promotion target. A bridge is promoted when repeated successful uses exist or when the bridge is required by high-urgency demands.
Witness requirements. Promotion requires commutation witnesses, loop closure witnesses, representability witnesses, and required stress/regression certificates under policy.
Stability requirements. Bridge behavior must be stable under κ refinement and under declared stability regions; branch-sensitive bridges require adversarial testing near cuts.
Lifecycle. Promoted bridges are versioned, can be deprecated, and can be migrated; promotion does not erase older bridges.
Ch03.D1.d — Long-Term Stability
Long-term stability is defined as preservation of meaning and verifiability under evolution.
Semantic continuity. New conventions must either be compatible with old ones under promoted bridges or be explicitly declared as introducing new meaning classes (portal events).
Backward verification. The system must retain the ability to verify past promoted artifacts, either by retaining old verifiers or by providing verified translators.
Controlled change. Changes are localized by scope; a change in branch policy does not affect unrelated scopes.
Invariant enforcement. No silent drift is enforced by ConvFP sensitivity and auditability axioms; any violation is detected as drift and blocked.
Ch03.D2 — Encode
Ch03.D2.a — Migration Edges
A migration edge encodes convention evolution as a first-class graph object.
Migration signature. (\mathrm{MIGRATE}:\mathrm{ConvFP}{\mathrm{old}}\to \mathrm{ConvFP}{\mathrm{new}}) with declared scope and admissibility region.
Migration payload. Payload includes:
old/new ConventionRecords,
scope and stability region identifiers,
the transport(s) implementing migration when applicable,
witness obligations and their satisfied status.
Replay binding. Migration is recorded by WordNF segment streams and witness roots; migration is replay-verifiable.
Non-overwriting. Migration does not overwrite old ConvFP or old artifacts; it creates links and possibly updates “current pointers.”
Ch03.D2.b — Equivalence Classes
Equivalence classes organize related conventions without collapsing them.
Class definition. A convention equivalence class is a set of ConvFPs connected by promoted bridges on a specified scope and policy.
Class indexing. Classes are indexed by:
scope identifier,
policy hash,
representative ConvFP chosen by governance rules.
Partial equivalence. Two ConvFPs may be equivalent on one scope and not on another; equivalence classes are therefore scope-indexed and cannot be globalized.
Conflict boundaries. Conflict proofs define boundaries separating equivalence classes; crossings require portals.
Ch03.D2.c — Canonical “Current” Pointers
Current pointers select preferred conventions for planning without rewriting history.
Pointer object. A CURRENT pointer maps a lens family and scope to a preferred ConvFP under a policy.
Pointer constraints. A pointer update requires:
migration/bridge availability from previously-current ConvFP for key scopes, or
explicit declaration that prior artifacts remain supported but new artifacts will use a different convention regime.
Pointer audit. Pointer updates are logged, hashed, and linked to regression runs validating that the new current convention is stable.
Pointer non-authority. Current pointers do not assert equivalence; they only guide construction. Equivalence remains witness-based.
Ch03.D2.d — Tombstones
Tombstones record intentionally retired or invalid convention regimes.
Tombstone semantics. A tombstone marks a ConvFP as invalid for new construction and, optionally, invalid for reuse, with reasons.
Reasons. Reasons include nondeterminism, security risk, semantic ambiguity, or proven incompatibility with core invariants.
Backward preservation. Even tombstoned ConvFPs may remain replayable for audit; tombstoning blocks promotion and route selection.
Remediation links. Tombstones include links to migration edges or portal requirements where available.
Ch03.D3 — Prove
Ch03.D3.a — Safe Migration Criteria
A migration is safe iff it preserves verifiability and does not corrupt semantic commitments.
Witnessed preservation. For the migrated scope, there exists a bridge or migration transport whose correctness is witnessed by UCWs and loop closure certificates within the required corridor gates.
No semantic ambiguity increase. The new convention must not introduce ambiguity on the migrated scope without explicit tie-breakers; otherwise migration is unsafe or requires additional scope constraints.
Stability under refinement. The migration must be stable under κ refinement: residual curves remain below gates or converge, and no drift alarms occur.
Policy compliance. The migration’s operations and bridges are admissible under the governing policy; otherwise migration is unsafe under that policy.
Ch03.D3.b — No-Loss Rewrite
No-loss rewrite ensures that migration does not discard meaning-critical information.
Representability preservation. Migration must preserve representability classes: canonical forms in the old convention must map to canonical forms in the new convention or to explicitly bridged equivalents.
Address preservation where required. For extractable artifacts, address semantics must be preserved or mapped by an explicit address-algebra morphism; otherwise extraction breaks and migration is unsafe.
Scope-limited rewriting. Rewrite applies only to declared scope; non-migrated scopes remain unchanged.
Counterexample exclusion. No-loss rewrite is validated by counterexample probes, including adversarial boundary probes for branch-sensitive scopes.
Ch03.D3.c — Backward Witness Preservation
Backward witness preservation is required for long-lived correctness.
Replay of old artifacts. The system preserves sufficient information to replay verification of old artifacts under their original ConvFP and verifier versions.
Translator correctness. If translators are used to reinterpret old artifacts under new verifiers, translators are themselves promoted artifacts with UCW-grade witnesses.
Bridge closure. Bridge chains connecting old and new conventions must close loops under holonomy gates; otherwise historical equivalences may become path-dependent.
Regression inheritance. Backward witness preservation is enforced by regression suites that include legacy artifacts as mandatory tests.
Ch03.D3.d — Re-Verification Rules
Re-verification ensures that evolution does not silently invalidate prior truth tissue.
Trigger rules. Re-verify any artifact whose scope intersects a changed ConvFP, changed bridge, changed canonicalization rule, or changed ordering rule.
Re-verify obligations. Re-verification recomputes UCW residual curves and loop closure residuals under pinned corridors and policies; it does not accept old stored residuals.
Failure handling. Re-verification failures produce refusal artifacts and may quarantine dependent edges; they do not auto-downgrade or silently widen corridors.
Promotion continuity. Artifacts remain promoted only if re-verification passes or if explicit migration/bridge receipts provide a verified continuity path.
Ch03.D4 — Extract
Ch03.D4.a — Compatibility Map
A compatibility map is a scope-indexed graph over ConvFPs.
Nodes. ConvFPs with decoded ConventionRecords.
Edges. Compatibility edges labeled IDENTICAL, BRIDGEABLE, INCOMPATIBLE, each indexed by scope and policy.
Witness attachments. Bridgeable edges include promoted bridge identities and witness roots; incompatible edges include conflict certificates.
Route planning usage. Planners consult compatibility maps to avoid illegal equivalence claims and to select admissible bridges when necessary.
Ch03.D4.b — Migration Metro
The migration metro is the long-term evolution graph of conventions.
Line structure. Migration edges form lines within lens families and across scopes.
Stations. Key conventions designated as stable hubs; these are used as defaults for new constructions.
Interchanges. Bridges between convention lines where semantic reconciliation is promoted and witnessed.
Quarantines. Regions of the migration metro blocked by unresolved drift alarms or conflict proofs.
Ch03.D4.c — Legacy Support Queries
Legacy support queries determine whether old artifacts remain usable.
Replay support. Can the artifact be replay-verified under preserved verifiers and ConvFP?
Bridge support. Is there a promoted bridge chain connecting legacy ConvFP to current ConvFP on required scope?
Policy support. Is the legacy artifact admissible under current policies, or is a policy-bridge required?
Risk support. Does the artifact pass current stress/regression requirements, or is it quarantined?
Ch03.D4.d — Upgrade Playbooks
Upgrade playbooks are deterministic procedures for evolving conventions safely.
Plan phase. Identify scope, decode ConventionRecord diffs, compute compatibility outcome, and enumerate required bridges/migrations.
Build phase. Implement migration transports or bridges with explicit WordNF routes and bounded budgets.
Verify phase. Produce UCWs, loop closure certificates, representability certificates, and required stress/regression results.
