Deploy phase. Update current pointers, publish compatibility map updates, archive tombstones if needed, and schedule continuous regression to enforce no silent drift.
Ch03 — Conventions, Canonicalization, and Fingerprints (ConvFP)
Ch04 — Corridors, Policies, Budgets, and κ-Schedules
A. Square
Ch04.A1 — Spec
Ch04.A1.a — Corridor Definition
A corridor is a typed, hash-addressed contract that governs approximation, verification, and promotion. It defines the admissible numeric and representational slack within which semantic claims may be asserted, and it specifies the refinement schedule required to justify those claims. A corridor is not a heuristic; it is a juridical object whose fields are interpreted by verifiers as acceptance predicates and by schedulers as obligations.
Formally, a corridor is a tuple[C ;:=; (\mathcal G,;\mathcal K,;\mathcal R,;\mathcal P,;\mathcal B,;\mathcal S),]where:
Gate set (\mathcal G). A finite family of gate thresholds controlling acceptance:[\mathcal G = {\varepsilon_{\text{face}},;\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},;\varepsilon_{\Pi},;\varepsilon_{\text{aux},1},\dots}.]Each (\varepsilon) is typed: it applies to a declared residual functional with explicit normalization.
κ-ladder (\mathcal K). A finite ordered set of refinement levels:[\mathcal K = (\kappa_1 \prec \kappa_2 \prec \cdots \prec \kappa_m),]where (\kappa) denotes a refinement index appropriate to the carrier (precision digits, truncation depth, mesh resolution, timestep, basis size, sample size). Corridors may define multiple κ ladders per carrier class; the ladder is part of the corridor identity.
Residual functionals (\mathcal R). A mapping from verification obligations to computable residual measures:[\mathcal R:{\text{face},\text{spin},\Pi,\text{aux}}\to{\rho_j},]where each (\rho_j) is a declared functional on observable outputs or enclosures that yields a nonnegative scalar.
Probe regime (\mathcal P). A probe family generator and adequacy requirements (deterministic or policy-approved) that specify how residuals are evaluated. Probes are not “test points”; they are part of the semantic witness.
Budgets (\mathcal B). Explicit resource caps: search caps, maximum κ escalation, maximum tunnel depth, time/space caps, and allowed computational methods. Budgets are interpreted as legality constraints.
Scope (\mathcal S). An explicit scope defining to which operator families, carriers, and witness obligations this corridor applies. Corridors are scoped; they cannot be assumed global.
A corridor is satisfied by a witness object (W) when, for every gate (g\in\mathcal G), the verifier recomputes the corresponding residual curve over (\mathcal K) and confirms that all required inequalities hold, and when all probe adequacy conditions are met.
Ch04.A1.b — ε Gates
An ε gate is an acceptance threshold for a specific residual obligation. Gates are not interchangeable; each gate binds to a declared residual functional and a declared normalization. The canonical gates are:
Face gate (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}). Binds commutation claims. For a commutation square with two routes yielding objects (u,v), face residual is computed as:[r_{\square}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathrm{Probes}(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{face}}(u(p,\kappa),v(p,\kappa)),]with the acceptance requirement (r_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{face}}) for all (\kappa\in\mathcal K).
Spin/holonomy gate (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}). Binds loop closure claims. For a loop word with loop map (L), spin residual is:[s_{\square}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathrm{Probes}(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{spin}}(p,L(p,\kappa)),]with acceptance requirement (s_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) for all (\kappa\in\mathcal K).
Representability gate (\varepsilon_{\Pi}). Binds canonicalization/projection stability. For projector (\Pi_h),[r_{\Pi}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathrm{Probes}(\kappa)} \rho_{\Pi}(p,\Pi_h(p,\kappa)),]requiring (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\Pi}) for all (\kappa\in\mathcal K).
Auxiliary gates. Additional gates may bind tail-bound tightness, stability under perturbation, regression invariants, or stress constraints. Each auxiliary gate is typed and must declare:
its residual functional,
its probe regime,
whether it is mandatory for promotion or only for specific risk classes.
Gate semantics obey corridor monotonicity: tightening gates strengthens obligations; widening gates changes the semantic regime and cannot be done silently for promoted artifacts.
Ch04.A1.c — κ Ladders
A κ ladder is the corridor’s refinement schedule, defining when and how evidence strengthens. The ladder is a finite ordered sequence of refinement indices, interpreted by the carrier and methods in use.
Carrier-specific κ meaning.
Scalar carriers: κ often denotes decimal precision digits and/or truncation depth.
Function carriers: κ may denote mesh size, polynomial degree, quadrature nodes, or sample count.
Operator carriers: κ may denote truncation rank, spectral resolution, or Krylov subspace dimension.
Graph carriers: κ may denote combinatorial refinement, canonical labeling depth, or subgraph exploration depth.
Two-tier κ schedules. Corridors may specify a primary κ ladder for numeric refinement and a secondary ladder for probe enrichment. Secondary ladders are treated as separate obligations; they cannot be substituted for numeric refinement unless explicitly declared.
Witness requirement. A UCW stores residual curves indexed by κ. Promotion requires that the curves be computed on the corridor’s κ ladder and that the verifier can recompute them deterministically.
κ-ladder adequacy. A κ ladder is adequate only if it is sufficient to distinguish:
convergence below gates,
plateau above gates,
drift across κ (indicative of hidden semantic issues),
holonomy stability (obstruction vs noise).Inadequate κ ladders forbid promotion; refusal must state inadequacy and propose an admissible ladder upgrade.
Ch04.A1.d — Admissible Target Classes
Corridors are attached to tasks and claims. Each corridor declares the admissible target classes for which it may be used.
Certified equivalence targets. Claims of semantic equivalence across routes, charts, or conventions, requiring tri-gate satisfaction and probe adequacy.
Certified evaluation targets. Computation of invariant values with certified enclosures, requiring tail-bound correctness and rounding correctness.
Certified reduction targets. Reductions of objects to a basis with certified coefficients, requiring coefficient stability and residual checks under κ.
Promotion targets. Promotion of bridges, charts, and nodes, requiring additional stress/regression gates as defined by policy.
Refusal targets. Production of structured refusal artifacts with best-achieved residual evidence under budget. Refusals are admissible outputs of corridor-governed procedures.
The corridor’s scope explicitly specifies which target classes it is valid for. Using a corridor outside its target class scope is illegal and must refuse.
Ch04.A2 — Encode
Ch04.A2.a — Corridor Hash Schema
Corridor identity is a cryptographic commitment to the corridor record. A corridor hash is defined by:[\mathrm{id}(C) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{CorridorRecord}(C))).]The CorridorRecord schema is versioned and includes:
Schema header. corridor_version, scope, carrier_type, and references to residual functional identifiers.
Gate fields. Numeric thresholds stored as canonical strings, each with an explicit residual functional ID and normalization ID.
κ ladder fields. Ordered list(s) of κ values with declared interpretation semantics (precision digits vs truncation depth vs mesh size, etc.). If κ has multiple components, it is encoded as a typed tuple.
Probe regime fields. Probe generator ID, probe determinism pins, adequacy requirements, and optional adversarial probe requirements.
Method admissibility fields. Allowed evaluator IDs, allowed tail-bound IDs, allowed rounding IDs, and any required canonicalization/projection IDs.
Budget fields. Search caps, maximum κ escalation, maximum tunnel events, maximum wall-time, and any memory limits.
Policy binding fields. Either a direct policy hash reference or a compatibility constraint that the corridor may only be used under policies satisfying specified predicates.
Corridor hashing forbids implicit defaults: all defaults must be explicit in the record or explicitly derived from schema version rules that are themselves pinned.
Ch04.A2.b — Budget Fields
Budgets are part of the corridor’s legality. Budget fields are typed and enforceable.
Refinement budgets. Max κ value(s), max number of κ steps, max number of refinement attempts per step.
Search budgets. Limits for candidate discovery (enumeration breadth, PSLQ coefficient bounds, max candidate count).
Verification budgets. Max number of probes per κ, max regression suite size, max stress suite size.
Repair budgets. Max Snap iterations, max tunnel events, max corridor rewrites per job.
Execution budgets. CPU time, memory, and backend restrictions, when enforced by policy.
Budget fields must be sufficient to guarantee termination. If the budget cannot guarantee termination of a declared procedure, the corridor is invalid for that target class.
Ch04.A2.c — Probe Schedule Fields
Probe schedules govern how probe families evolve with κ and with repair/escalation steps.
Probe family ID. Declares the generator for probes at each κ.
κ-indexed probe size. Declares how many probes are used at each κ and how probes are selected or constructed.
Adequacy metadata. Declares the adequacy criteria and the witness obligations needed to claim adequacy.
Adversarial probe flags. For high-risk scopes (branch-sensitive, portal-sensitive), probe schedules include mandatory adversarial probes near boundaries.
Probe schedule encoding must be deterministic. If randomization is used, seeds must be pinned and stored; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Ch04.A2.d — Policy Binding
Policies constrain corridors and corridors constrain policies. Binding is explicit.
Policy hash binding. A corridor may bind directly to a specific policy hash, meaning it is valid only under that policy.
Policy predicate binding. A corridor may bind to a class of policies satisfying a predicate (e.g., “allows these method IDs, allows these tunnel families, enforces these budgets”). The predicate identifier is stored and versioned.
Bridge requirement binding. If the corridor is to be used across policy families, it must specify whether a policy bridge is required, and if so, the bridge template IDs.
Binding verification. The verifier checks policy binding before interpreting corridor gates; mismatch yields refusal class POLICY_MISMATCH, not a numeric failure.
Ch04.A3 — Prove
Ch04.A3.a — Monotone Refinement
Monotone refinement is the central correctness constraint for corridor-governed evidence accumulation.
Monotone enclosure requirement. For certified evaluation, enclosure radii must weakly decrease with κ:[\mathrm{rad}(\kappa_{i+1}) \le \mathrm{rad}(\kappa_i) \quad\text{or}\quad \mathrm{rad}(\kappa_{i+1}) \approx \mathrm{rad}(\kappa_i) \text{ below gates.}]
Monotone residual requirement. For witness residual curves (r(\kappa)), monotonicity is required up to declared tolerances:[r(\kappa_{i+1}) \le r(\kappa_i) + \delta_{\text{mono}},]where (\delta_{\text{mono}}) is a corridor-declared monotonicity slack (default zero unless explicitly permitted).
Plateau interpretation. If (r(\kappa)) plateaus above gates, the claim is not promotable. The procedure must either:
escalate probes or carriers (if admissible),
attempt repair/tunneling (if admissible),
refuse with plateau evidence and classification.
Drift detection. If refinement produces non-monotone behavior inconsistent with numeric error reduction, the failure is classified as drift or instability, not merely “need more precision.” The refusal must distinguish plateau from drift using ConvFP and cross-κ consistency tests.
Ch04.A3.b — Budget Correctness
Budgets must guarantee finite behavior and must be auditable.
Termination guarantee. Every corridor-governed procedure must have a termination proof under the corridor’s budgets. The proof is by bounded iteration over:
κ ladder length,
probes per κ,
candidate counts,
repair/tunnel events.
Budget adherence verification. Execution traces record consumed budget units. The verifier checks that:
iteration counts do not exceed maxima,
κ does not exceed declared maxima,
tunnel events do not exceed tunnel budgets,
search caps are respected.
Budget-respecting refusal. If a procedure terminates due to budget exhaustion without meeting gates, it must emit a refusal artifact containing:
consumed budgets,
best achieved residual curves,
admissible next actions (e.g., widen budget under new policy, change method, change corridor).
No hidden budgets. Any implicit “try until convergence” loop is illegal for promotable procedures. All loops must be bounded by explicit budget fields.
Ch04.A3.c — Corridor Equivalence
Corridor equivalence determines when two corridors define the same acceptance regime.
Two corridors (C) and (C') are equivalent on a scope (\Sigma) iff:
Gate equivalence. For all gates relevant to (\Sigma), thresholds and residual functional IDs match exactly.
κ ladder equivalence. κ ladders match as ordered sequences, with identical interpretation semantics.
Probe regime equivalence. Probe generator IDs and adequacy requirements match, including adversarial probe requirements.
Method admissibility equivalence. Allowed method IDs, bound IDs, and rounding IDs match on (\Sigma).
If equivalence holds, artifacts witnessed under (C) may be reused under (C') without re-verification only if policy bindings are compatible. If corridors differ, reuse requires either re-verification or an explicit corridor-bridge artifact (noting that corridor widening changes semantic regimes for corridor-bounded equivalence).
Ch04.A3.d — Termination Conditions
Termination conditions are the formal criteria determining whether a corridor-governed procedure ends in acceptance, promotion, or refusal.
Acceptance termination. A procedure terminates in acceptance when all relevant residual curves satisfy gate predicates across the κ ladder and probe adequacy is satisfied.
Promotion termination. A procedure terminates in promotion when acceptance holds and additional promotion gates (stress/regression) required by policy are satisfied.
Refusal termination. A procedure terminates in refusal when budgets are exhausted or admissibility fails. Refusal must include evidence sufficient to replay and classify failure.
Escalation termination. When admissible, a procedure may terminate by scheduling an escalation job (e.g., Snap, tunnel, obstruction compilation). Such termination must itself be justified: it must show why local refinement cannot succeed under the current corridor and budgets.
Ch04.A4 — Extract
Ch04.A4.a — Corridor Snapshots
A corridor snapshot is an extractable artifact representing the corridor regime used for a particular witness or job.
Snapshot contents. CorridorRecord, corridor hash, schema version, scope, and links to policy binding and method admissibility.
Usage linkage. Links to all witnesses and receipts that were validated under this corridor.
Refinement outcomes. For each κ, extracted summaries of residual maxima and enclosure radii.
Evolution lineage. Links to predecessor/successor corridors when corridor evolution occurs via Snap/tunnel events.
Ch04.A4.b — Schedule Extractors
Schedule extractors output κ-ladder and probe schedule plans in a deterministic, audit-friendly form.
κ extractor. Outputs ordered κ ladder with interpretation semantics and budget maxima.
Probe extractor. Outputs probe counts per κ and probe generator IDs, including adversarial requirements.
Method extractor. Outputs admissible method IDs and tail-bound/rounding IDs under corridor scope.
Promotion extractor. Outputs additional required gates and suites (stress/regression) as per policy binding.
Ch04.A4.c — Budget Reports
Budget reports summarize budget consumption and constraints.
Declared vs consumed. Show declared maxima and consumed amounts for κ steps, probes, search candidates, repair attempts, and tunnel events.
Bottleneck identification. Identify which budget dimension constrained acceptance (precision, probes, candidate cap, tunnel cap, time).
Remediation suggestions. Provide admissible next actions: increase budget under a new policy, switch methods, schedule tunnel, expand probes, or relax corridor gates explicitly (not silently).
Risk impact. Report whether proposed budget changes would require additional stress/regression gates due to increased risk.
Ch04.A4.d — Gate Summaries
Gate summaries provide compact reports of acceptance predicates.
Gate table. For each gate, list its threshold, residual functional ID, and measured maxima across κ.
Curve shape. Indicate whether residual curves decrease, plateau below gates, plateau above gates, or exhibit drift.
Adequacy status. Indicate whether probe adequacy was satisfied, and if not, specify required probe expansions.
Decision outcome. Summarize whether acceptance, promotion, refusal, or escalation occurred, with hash pointers to relevant artifacts.
B. Flower
Ch04.B1 — Spec
Ch04.B1.a — Corridor-Aware Transports
A transport is corridor-aware when its legality depends on corridor gates and κ schedules.
Corridor parameterization. Transport semantics includes corridor-dependent evaluation choices (e.g., truncation depth chosen as a function of κ and ε).
Witness coupling. Corridor-aware transports must emit sufficient evidence for the verifier to recompute residuals at each κ.
Scope discipline. Corridor awareness applies only to declared operator families; use outside scope is illegal.
No semantic mutation. Corridor changes affect acceptance thresholds and refinement schedules but must not mutate semantic conventions; ConvFP remains separate.
Ch04.B1.b — Legality Under Tolerance
Legality under tolerance is evaluated by corridor gates.
Approximate legality. A step is legal if the induced residuals and enclosures satisfy gates at required κ values.
Local legality. Legality is checked locally at each edge/segment; global success requires all local obligations to be satisfied.
Inadmissibility. If the required residual functional is undefined on the current carrier or probe regime, the step is illegal and must refuse.
Equivalence scope. Any tolerance-bounded equivalence claim is indexed by corridor; switching corridor changes the meaning regime and requires re-verification.
Ch04.B1.c — Refinement Steps
Refinement steps implement κ escalation under corridor control.
Step semantics. A refinement step increases κ and recomputes enclosures and residuals according to admissible methods.
Monotonicity obligation. Each refinement step must demonstrate monotone tightening or provide evidence for plateau classification.
Budget obligation. Refinement steps must respect κ maxima and iteration caps.
Trace obligation. Each refinement step is recorded in the route segment stream with κ, method IDs, and rounding/tail-bound identifiers.
Ch04.B1.d — Escalation Rules
Escalation rules define the legal progression when gates are not met.
Refine-first rule. If refinement is admissible and budgets remain, increase κ.
Snap rule. If refinement stagnates, attempt Snap: tighten corridor or adjust method choices within admissible set, requiring defect reduction.
Tunnel rule. If Snap cannot reduce defect within admissible operations, and policy allows tunneling, invoke tunnel events with explicit logging and defect reduction requirements.
Refusal/discovery rule. If tunnel is not admissible or budgets exhaust, refuse. If refusal is stable and holonomy-dominant, schedule obstruction compilation or portal escalation.
Ch04.B2 — Encode
Ch04.B2.a — Gate Stacks
Gate stacks are canonical sequences of gate evaluations applied during verification.
Gate stack identity. Each stack has an ID and a fixed ordering (e.g., face → spin → representability → aux). Ordering is deterministic and part of the route identity.
Gate parameter binding. Each gate’s parameters (thresholds, residual functional IDs, κ ladder) are bound to corridor hash and recorded in the gate-run segment.
Gate outputs. Gate outputs include κ-indexed residual curves and summary maxima; raw outputs are not authoritative unless recomputed by verifier.
Gate compatibility. Gate stacks must be compatible with carrier type and probe regime; otherwise refusal is mandatory.
Ch04.B2.b — Snap Parameters
Snap parameters encode corridor-tightening and method-selection decisions.
Snap inputs. Current corridor hash, target gates, κ ladder, probe regime, and allowed method set.
Snap state. Iteration count, defect functional values, and selection of method variants.
Snap outputs. New corridor hash candidate (if tightening is performed), updated method choices, and evidence of defect reduction.
Snap trace. A Snap trace is a segment stream and must be sufficient for replay. Snap is illegal if it cannot be replayed deterministically.
Ch04.B2.c — Tunnel Admissibility Fields
Tunnel admissibility fields define when and how tunneling is allowed.
Tunnel eligibility. Which failure classes are eligible (e.g., plateau above gates, high cost, instability), and which are not.
Tunnel budget. Max tunnel events, max candidate charts to try, max coefficient caps, max verification κ levels.
Tunnel scope. Which operator families may be tunneled (evaluation charts, basis changes, transforms), and which may not.
Tunnel witness obligations. Mandatory recording of pre/post corridor hashes, defect measures, witness IDs, and replay fields.
Ch04.B2.d — Replay Seeds
Replay seeds pin any nondeterministic components in candidate generation and scheduling.
Search seeds. Seeds for randomized heuristics when permitted; default is no randomness. If randomness is used, promotion requires explicit determinism guarantees or conversion to deterministic enumeration.
Probe seeds. If probes involve randomness, seeds must be pinned; otherwise probe families must be deterministic.
Scheduler seeds. Any tie-breaking in scheduling must be deterministic or pinned.
Seed scope. Seeds are metadata unless they influence outputs; if they influence outputs, they must be bound into route identity and witness obligations.
Ch04.B3 — Prove
Ch04.B3.a — Defect Reduction Predicate
Defect reduction is required for any repair step (Snap or tunnel) to be admissible as a corridor-changing action.
Defect functional. Define a defect functional (\mathfrak D) that aggregates violations of gate predicates and, optionally, cost metrics under a fixed corridor and κ schedule:[\mathfrak D := w_f\cdot\max(0,r_{\square,\max}-\varepsilon_{\text{face}})+w_s\cdot\max(0,s_{\square,\max}-\varepsilon_{\text{spin}})+w_\Pi\cdot\max(0,r_{\Pi,\max}-\varepsilon_\Pi)+w_c\cdot \mathrm{Cost}.]
Reduction requirement. A repair action from state (S) to (S') is admissible only if:[\mathfrak D(S') < \mathfrak D(S),]with a strict inequality under a declared tolerance for measurement noise. Otherwise the action is rejected or recorded as a failed attempt.
Evidence requirement. Repair actions must record pre/post defect measures and the κ-indexed residual curves used to compute them.
Non-deception. A repair action cannot reduce defect by silently widening gates; any corridor widening changes the corridor identity and requires explicit governance.
Ch04.B3.b — Admissibility Proofs
Admissibility proofs justify that corridor-governed steps remained within declared legal regions.
Carrier admissibility. Inputs and outputs remain in declared admissible sets.
Method admissibility. All invoked method IDs are allowed by corridor/policy binding.
Scope admissibility. Gate stacks and residual functionals are valid for the carrier type and probe regime.
Bridge admissibility. Any cross-policy or cross-convention bridging required is explicitly present and verified; absence blocks admissibility.
Ch04.B3.c — Budgeted Refusal Proofs
Refusal proofs are mandatory when acceptance cannot be achieved under budgets.
Exhaustion evidence. Show that all admissible κ steps were attempted up to κ maxima and that remaining actions would exceed budgets.
Best-achieved evidence. Provide the best residual curves and enclosures achieved within budgets.
Classification evidence. Classify failure as plateau, drift, holonomy obstruction, policy mismatch, or non-identifiability, based on residual dominance and ConvFP/cross-κ checks.
Next-action admissibility. Provide a finite list of admissible next actions, each tied to a policy/corridor change that would enable it.
Ch04.B3.d — Policy Compliance
Policy compliance is enforced by verifier checks.
Operation compliance. All invoked operations are allowed by policy (method IDs, tunnel types, bridge types).
Budget compliance. All consumed budget units are within declared limits.
Security compliance. Any disallowed external dependencies are absent from promotable evidence; oracles are restricted to candidate generation only.
Audit compliance. Required logs (gate segments, tunnel segments) exist and are complete; missing logs cause refusal.
Ch04.B4 — Extract
Ch04.B4.a — Per-Edge Corridor Traces
Per-edge corridor traces extract corridor usage at the granularity of metro edges.
Edge corridor binding. Corridor hash used for each edge’s witnesses.
κ evidence. Residual curves per κ and enclosure radii per κ.
Repair history. Snap/tunnel segments applied, including pre/post corridor hashes and defect reduction evidence.
Promotion status. Whether the edge is promoted, blocked, or refused, with reasons.
Ch04.B4.b — Failure Localization
Failure localization extracts the minimal failing component under corridor discipline.
Gate localization. Identify which gate failed and at which κ values.
Probe localization. Identify probes contributing to maximal residuals and whether those probes are boundary/adversarial probes.
Method localization. Identify which evaluator or bound method is responsible for plateau or instability.
Scope localization. Determine whether failure is numeric (corridor), semantic (ConvFP), or governance (policy). Localize accordingly.
Ch04.B4.c — Suggested Repairs
Suggested repairs are extracted as admissible next steps.
Refinement repairs. Increase κ or expand probe families within budgets.
Method repairs. Switch to alternate certified evaluators or bound methods admissible under corridor/policy.
Snap repairs. Tighten corridor or adjust method selection under defect reduction rules.
Tunnel repairs. Schedule admissible tunnel operations with explicit logging and verification.
Suggested repairs must be sorted deterministically by expected defect reduction per cost and must not include disallowed actions.
Ch04.B4.d — Policy Conflict Extracts
Policy conflict extracts report when corridor obligations cannot be satisfied under current policy.
Disallowed method conflicts. Required evaluators or bounds are not allowed.
Budget conflicts. Required κ schedule exceeds policy budgets.
Tunnel conflicts. Required tunnel operations are forbidden.
Bridge conflicts. Required policy/convention bridges are absent or forbidden.
Policy conflict extracts include required bridge templates or required policy changes, never silent substitutions.
C. Cloud
Ch04.C1 — Spec
Ch04.C1.a — Certified vs Heuristic Regions
The framework stratifies methods and evidence into certified and heuristic regimes.
Certified region. Evidence is based on enclosures, deterministic tail bounds, and explicit rounding models. Certified evidence is admissible for promotion.
Heuristic region. Evidence uses high-precision numerics, oracle computations, or probabilistic checks. Heuristic evidence is admissible only for candidate generation and planning, not for promotion.
Mixed region. Pipelines may include heuristic components provided all promotion-relevant claims are verified using certified evidence. Mixed evidence must carry explicit tags and separation boundaries.
Region transitions. Transitions from heuristic to certified require explicit verification steps and witness packs; no implicit upgrading is allowed.
Ch04.C1.b — Uncertainty Stratification
Uncertainty is stratified into levels tied to corridor gates and witness completeness.
Level 0 (Exact). Equality decided by canonical normal forms.
Level 1 (Certified). Enclosure-based equivalence within corridor gates across κ.
Level 2 (Bounded uncertainty). Evidence indicates near-equivalence but gates not satisfied; admissible repairs exist.
Level 3 (Plateau). Residual curves plateau above gates; probe adequacy escalation required.
Level 4 (Semantic conflict). ConvFP mismatch without bridge or drift detected; corridor tightening cannot resolve.
Stratification is encoded and used for scheduling and for reuse policies.
Ch04.C1.c — Probe Adequacy
Probe adequacy is the requirement that probe families distinguish equivalence classes relevant to the claim.
Adequacy definition. A probe family (\mathcal P) is adequate for an equivalence claim if:
probes cover the stability region for the scope,
probes include boundary probes where semantics is discontinuous or ill-conditioned,
probes produce residual curves whose monotone behavior correlates with κ refinement.
Adequacy is scoped. Adequacy depends on carrier type, operator scope, corridor, and conventions.
Adequacy as obligation. Promotion requires an adequacy certificate; lacking adequacy blocks promotion even if residuals appear small.
Adequacy escalation. When plateau occurs, the system must propose admissible probe expansions or carrier escalations; increasing κ alone may be insufficient.
Ch04.C1.d — Plateau Semantics
Plateau semantics formalize when “more effort” cannot resolve a claim under current regime.
Plateau predicate. A κ-indexed residual curve is plateaued above gates if:
successive κ levels fail to reduce residual beyond a declared tolerance,
no admissible method change within corridor/policy reduces defect,
probes are adequate or escalation is blocked by policy.
Interpretation. Plateau may indicate:
insufficient methods or bounds (repair possible with new charts),
non-identifiability (need richer probes or carriers),
holonomy obstruction (missing modulus; triggers obstruction compilation),
drift (semantic inconsistency; triggers ConvFP remediation).
Action rule. Plateau above gates forbids promotion; procedure must either (i) escalate, (ii) tunnel, (iii) refuse with evidence.
Evidence rule. Plateau refusal includes residual curves, κ schedule, probe adequacy status, and the attempted repair/tunnel history.
Ch04.C2 — Encode
Ch04.C2.a — Interval Precision Rules
Interval precision rules define how enclosures are computed and serialized.
Precision binding. Interval endpoint precision is determined by κ and guard digits; guard policy is explicit.
Serialization binding. Endpoints are serialized as normalized decimal strings under fixed formatting.
Enclosure provenance. Each interval includes method IDs and tail-bound IDs used to produce it.
Verifier recomputation. The verifier must be able to recompute intervals from method/bound IDs and κ; stored intervals are evidence but not authority.
Ch04.C2.b — Tail-Bound IDs
Tail-bound IDs encode certification of truncation error.
Bound identity. Each tail bound has an ID; its functional form and assumptions are recorded and hashed.
Parameterization. Tail bounds include parameters (e.g., N, q, remainder envelope constants); all are bound into route segments.
Composition. Bound IDs specify how they compose under operations; missing composition laws block promotion.
Auditability. Tail bound derivations are addressable and extractable.
Ch04.C2.c — Rounding IDs
Rounding IDs pin rounding models.
Rounding cushion. Explicit rounding cushion functional form.
Backend semantics. Backend identity and numeric semantics pinned when relevant.
Projector coupling. Rounding and (\Pi_h) must be compatible; otherwise representability witnesses fail.
Risk tags. Certain rounding models may be disallowed for high-risk scopes; policy binding enforces this.
Ch04.C2.d — Confidence Certificates
Confidence certificates encode the evidence that corridor obligations were satisfied.
Gate pass record. For each gate, recorded maxima across κ and pass/fail status.
Monotonicity record. Whether residual curves decrease or plateau below gates.
Adequacy record. Probe adequacy status and any adversarial probe results.
Scope record. Conventions, policy, corridor, and stability region identifiers.
Confidence certificates are stored as witness objects and referenced by receipts.
Ch04.C3 — Prove
Ch04.C3.a — Adequacy Theorems
Adequacy theorems establish conditions under which probe families suffice for promotion.
Separation property. Probes must separate equivalence classes for the scope; otherwise small residuals do not imply equivalence.
Boundary coverage. For discontinuous semantics (branch cuts, regularization boundaries), adequacy requires boundary-near probes.
Refinement sensitivity. Adequate probes yield residual curves whose refinement behavior is informative (monotone decrease if correct, plateau if obstructed).
Verifier enforceability. Adequacy conditions must be checkable by the verifier from probe metadata and observed residual behavior; purely informal adequacy claims are invalid.
Ch04.C3.b — Stability Under Perturbation
Perturbation stability guards against fragile equivalences.
Perturbation probes. For each κ, include small perturbations of probe inputs or parameters, within declared admissible ranges.
Stability predicate. Residual maxima remain below gates under perturbations, within a perturbation tolerance declared by corridor.
Risk-based requirement. High-risk edges (tunnel-derived, bridge-derived) require perturbation stability certificates for promotion.
Failure classification. Failure under perturbation indicates ill-conditioning or hidden branch sensitivity; classification must distinguish numeric instability from semantic drift.
Ch04.C3.c — Adversarial Probes
Adversarial probes detect corner-case failures.
Boundary adversaries. Probes near branch cuts or singularities.
Ordering adversaries. Probes that force canonicalization tie-breakers.
Regularization adversaries. Probes near divergence thresholds.
Transport adversaries. Probes designed to maximize commutation defect and reveal hidden non-commutativity.
Adversarial probe requirements are corridor/policy scoped and must be explicitly declared for promotion where needed.
Ch04.C3.d — Refusal Correctness
Refusal correctness ensures that when a corridor-governed procedure refuses, it refuses for a valid reason and with complete evidence.
Completeness. Refusal includes all required hashes, residual curves, budgets, and history.
Soundness. Refusal classification matches witness dominance and ConvFP/policy checks.
Replay. Refusal is replayable; the verifier can confirm the refusal outcome by replaying the attempted steps within budgets.
Actionability. Refusal includes admissible next actions; it is not a dead-end narrative.
Ch04.C4 — Extract
Ch04.C4.a — Adequacy Reports
Adequacy reports summarize probe adequacy status.
Probe generator disclosure. IDs and determinism pins.
Coverage disclosure. Stability region coverage, boundary probe coverage.
Evidence disclosure. Residual curves showing informative refinement behavior.
Escalation suggestions. If inadequate, suggest admissible probe expansions or carrier escalations.
Ch04.C4.b — Regression Harness Links
Regression harness links connect corridors to regression suites required for promotion integrity.
Suite IDs. Regression suite identifiers required by policy for the corridor’s scope.
Trigger conditions. When regression must run (promotion, migration, bridge installation, tunnel activation).
Artifact linkage. Which edges/nodes are covered by which regression suites.
Result extraction. Links to regression result witness packs for audit.
Ch04.C4.c — Stress Suite Keys
Stress suite keys identify required adversarial testing regimes.
Stress suite IDs. Required stress suites for high-risk scopes.
Adversary catalogs. Specific adversarial probe families bound to stress suites.
Promotion gating. Whether stress-pass is required for promotion for the corridor’s target class.
Audit linkage. Links to stress result witness packs and failure evidence.
Ch04.C4.d — Confidence Exports
Confidence exports provide compact evidence suitable for cross-site verification.
Certificate summary. Gate pass maxima, κ ladder, probe adequacy.
Joint binding. ConvFP bundle hash, policy hash, corridor hash.
Replay bundle pointer. Pins, WordNF hash, witness roots.
Risk label. Certified level and any required stress/regression coverage declarations.
D. Fractal
Ch04.D1 — Spec
Ch04.D1.a — Corridor Evolution
Corridors evolve only by explicit operations that preserve auditability.
Tightening evolution. Corridor tightening reduces gates or strengthens κ schedules without changing semantic conventions. Tightening may be treated as compatible strengthening, but still changes corridor identity.
Widening evolution. Widening changes acceptance regimes; it is not compatible with prior promotions without explicit bridging and re-verification.
Method evolution. Corridors may evolve by changing admissible method sets, adding new certified evaluators or bounds. Such evolution requires re-verification of dependent witnesses.
Scope evolution. Corridors may change scope; scope changes require explicit new corridor identities and cannot be inferred.
Ch04.D1.b — Corridor Compaction
Compaction reduces storage while preserving replay.
Evidence compaction. Replace bulky traces with hashes and minimal witness packs, preserving the ability to recompute residuals.
Curve compaction. Store κ-indexed residual curves in canonical compressed representations that preserve numeric values as recomputable evidence, not as authority.
History compaction. Preserve only successful repair/tunnel histories for promoted artifacts while retaining refusal summaries for failed attempts.
Compaction invariants. Compaction must preserve verifier replay outcomes for all promoted artifacts and for designated refusal exemplars.
Ch04.D1.c — Reuse of Corridors
Corridors are reusable if their scope and bindings match.
Scope match. Reuse requires identical scope or an explicit scope-bridge.
Policy match. Reuse requires policy compatibility or explicit policy bridging.
Method match. Reuse requires admissible method overlap sufficient to replay witnesses; otherwise re-verification is required.
Risk match. High-risk corridors require stress/regression suites; reuse is blocked if suites are missing under target policy.
Ch04.D1.d — Corridor Branching
Branching occurs when different corridor regimes are explored for different goals.
Branch causes. Different targets (speed vs tightness), different carrier types, different risk classes.
Branch governance. Branches are explicit corridor identities linked by corridor-lineage edges; branches are not hidden variants.
Branch selection. Schedulers select corridor branches deterministically by demand and risk.
Branch reconciliation. Reconciling branches requires explicit bridging and re-verification if acceptance regimes differ.
Ch04.D2 — Encode
Ch04.D2.a — Corridor Chain Hashes
Corridor evolution is recorded as hash chains.
Lineage hash. Each corridor record includes parent corridor hash (if evolved) and evolution action ID.
Chain integrity. Chains are tamper-evident and are used in audit replays.
Branch markers. Branches are marked explicitly to avoid conflating regimes.
Checkpointing. Corridor checkpoints are created at promotion boundaries and are referenced by receipts.
Ch04.D2.b — Snap/Tunnel Event Logs
Repair and tunneling actions are logged as explicit events.
Snap logs. Pre/post corridor hashes, defect measures, gate residuals, budgets consumed.
Tunnel logs. Pre/post corridor hashes, method changes, chart changes, witness obligations, replay fields.
Segment stream integration. Logs are embedded as route segments in WordNF so that route identity includes repair/tunnel history.
Promotion linkage. Promoted bridges reference these logs via MagicCertPack-style certificate packs.
Ch04.D2.c — Corridor Diffs
Corridor diffs explain how regimes differ.
Gate diffs. Identify changed thresholds and affected residual obligations.
κ schedule diffs. Identify changes in κ ladders, interpretation, and probe schedules.
Method diffs. Identify changes in allowed methods/bounds/rounding models.
Binding diffs. Identify changes in policy binding or scope.
Diffs are deterministic and used in compatibility reports and governance dashboards.
Ch04.D2.d — Governance Hooks
Governance hooks connect corridor evolution to scheduling and audit controls.
Approval gates. Certain corridor changes (widening, adding tunnels) require explicit governance flags.
Regression hooks. Corridor changes trigger regression jobs for dependent witnesses.
Quarantine hooks. High-risk corridor branches may be quarantined until stress/regression is complete.
Publication hooks. Corridors can be marked as recommended defaults only after passing governance checks and regression suites.
Ch04.D3 — Prove
Ch04.D3.a — Corridor-Change Legality
Corridor changes are legal only when they preserve explicit invariants and auditability.
Tightening legality. Tightening must preserve replay of previously promoted artifacts only insofar as tightening does not require stronger evidence than previously stored; otherwise re-verification is required.
Widening legality. Widening creates a new semantic regime for corridor-bounded equivalence; it is legal only as a new corridor identity and cannot retroactively validate prior promotions.
Method-set legality. Changing allowed methods is legal only if affected witnesses are re-verified or are proven method-independent within their scope.
Logging legality. Any corridor change must be logged and hash-chained; missing logs forbid promotion of artifacts relying on the change.
Ch04.D3.b — No Silent Widening
No silent widening is a core integrity rule.
Explicitness. Any gate increase or κ-ladder weakening must produce a new corridor identity.
Receipt binding. Promoted receipts bind corridor hashes; reuse under a widened corridor requires explicit corridor-bridge artifacts or re-verification.
Witness invalidation. UCWs witnessed under tighter corridors do not automatically certify equivalence under widened corridors when the equivalence relation itself depends on corridor; equivalence must be reinterpreted explicitly.
Audit enforcement. Auditors check that corridor hashes used at verification match corridor hashes declared in receipts; mismatches yield refusal.
Ch04.D3.c — Bounded Drift
Bounded drift ensures corridor evolution does not mask semantic drift.
ConvFP separation. Corridors do not encode semantic conventions except for projector/rounding rules explicitly scoped as representability mechanisms; semantic conventions remain in ConvFP.
Cross-κ drift checks. Corridor evolution triggers cross-κ consistency tests; drift indicates implementation or convention problems, not corridor needs.
Bridge discipline. Corridor changes cannot substitute for missing convention bridges; semantic mismatches must be resolved by bridges or portals.
Failure classification. Drift detected under fixed ConvFP and changing corridor is classified as corridor misuse or implementation drift; it triggers refusals and remediation.
Ch04.D3.d — Corridor Rollback Proofs
Rollback proofs justify reverting corridor evolution when it produces worse outcomes or invalidates stability.
Rollback criterion. If a corridor evolution fails to reduce defect functional under admissible repair rules, rollback is admissible.
Rollback evidence. Provide pre/post defect measures and show that rollback restores a regime with better verified status.
Rollback safety. Rollback must not delete evidence; it creates a new corridor lineage edge and preserves all logs.
Rollback auditability. Rollback is recorded as a corridor-chain event and is replay-verifiable.
Ch04.D4 — Extract
Ch04.D4.a — Corridor Lineage Graph
The corridor lineage graph is an extraction map of corridor evolution.
Nodes. Corridor identities with decoded CorridorRecords.
Edges. Evolution edges: tighten, widen, method-add, method-remove, branch, rollback.
Annotations. Defect reduction outcomes, regression/stress status, and scope/policy compatibility.
Queries. Extractors can query shortest lineage path between corridors, identify common ancestors, and find stable default corridors.
Ch04.D4.b — Audit Replay Scripts
Audit replay scripts are minimal deterministic procedures for re-validating corridor-governed claims.
Inputs. Pins, WordNF segment stream, witness roots, policy hash, corridor hash, verifier version hash.
Procedure. Recompute enclosures and residual curves across κ, validate probe adequacy, validate gate predicates, validate budget adherence.
Outputs. Pass/fail plus evidence; failure triggers refusal artifacts with localized causes.
Portability. Scripts are designed to run in independent environments under pinned backend semantics.
Ch04.D4.c — Compaction Receipts
Compaction receipts certify that compaction preserved replay.
Before/after hashes. Commit to pre-compaction artifact identities and post-compaction artifact identities.
Replay equivalence. Provide evidence that verifier outcomes for promoted artifacts are unchanged.
Evidence retention map. Map which traces were removed and which hash-addressed witness packs remain.
Audit guarantee. Guarantee that required minimal replay bundles remain extractable for specified artifact classes.
Ch04.D4.d — Governance Dashboards
Governance dashboards summarize corridor status under policy and risk.
Default corridors. Identify recommended corridors for each scope and carrier type, with regression/stress status.
Quarantined corridors. Identify corridors blocked due to drift alarms, failed stress suites, or policy conflicts.
Upgrade opportunities. Identify where adding certified methods or acceleration charts would reduce defect and cost.
Policy impacts. Identify which policy constraints most frequently block corridor satisfaction and propose explicit policy-bridge requirements.
Ch04 — Corridors, Policies, Budgets, and κ-Schedules
Ch05 — Receipts, Pins, and Proof/Dependency Algebra
A. Square
Ch05.A1 — Spec
Ch05.A1.a — Receipt Definition
A receipt is the minimal, hash-addressed object that makes a claim locally verifiable and safely reusable. A receipt does not itself assert mathematics; it asserts that a specific verifier, operating under specific semantic and numeric regimes, can verify a specific artifact using a finite set of pinned inputs and witness objects.
Formally, a receipt is a tuple[\mathsf{Rcpt} ;:=; (\mathsf{Plan},;\mathsf{Pins},;\mathsf{Wits},;\mathsf{Ctx},;\mathsf{Mode}),]where:
Plan (\mathsf{Plan}). A finite, canonical description of the claim and its verification contract. The plan includes:
the target artifact id(s) being certified (node, edge, transport, evaluation, equivalence),
the intended scope (lens ids, ConvFP bundle scope, carrier type),
the verifier obligations to satisfy (gate stacks, required witness types),
the acceptance predicate (tri-gate and any auxiliary gates),
the minimal export commitments (what must be present for replay).
Pins (\mathsf{Pins}). The immutable dependency commitments. Pins are hashes of all objects whose identity or semantics is required to interpret or replay verification:
object identities (nodes, edges, lenses, transports, charts),
ConvFP bundle hashes and decoded convention records (schema-versioned),
corridor hashes and decoded corridor records (schema-versioned),
policy hashes and decoded policy records (schema-versioned),
environment pins when required (backend ids, deterministic arithmetic semantics).
Wits (\mathsf{Wits}). The witness commitments. Wits are hashes of all witness objects required to satisfy the plan:
UCW objects (commutation/holonomy/representability witnesses),
certified evaluation receipts (interval+tail+rounding packs),
bridge certificate packs (MagicCertPack / BridgeSeed witnesses),
regression/stress result packs when required for promotion.
Context (\mathsf{Ctx}). The verifier binding context:
verifier version hash,
canonical encoding id (canon rules hash),
schema version hashes for all referenced record types,
optional quarantine/visibility constraints.
Mode (\mathsf{Mode}). The export and visibility regime: public, restricted, private, quarantined, controlling what can be exported and under what assumptions.
A receipt is valid iff an independent verifier, given the receipt and the referenced pinned objects and witness objects, can replay verification and reproduce acceptance without external trust.
Ch05.A1.b — Pins vs Witnesses
Pins and witnesses are distinct commitments and must never be conflated.
Pins commit identity and interpretation. Pins answer: “What objects, semantic conventions, corridors, policies, and environments define the meaning of the claim?” Pins are the immutable context needed to interpret any evidence.
Witnesses commit evidence. Witnesses answer: “What verifiable evidence proves the claim within the pinned context?” Witnesses are the computed obligations (residual curves, enclosures, closure certificates) that the verifier recomputes or validates.
Non-substitutability. A witness is meaningless without pins; pins are insufficient without witnesses. Promotion requires both.
Failure semantics. If pins are incomplete, the result is REFUSE:PIN_INCOMPLETE. If witnesses are incomplete or fail gates, the result is REFUSE:WITNESS_FAIL. If pins and witnesses exist but are incompatible (e.g., witness computed under different corridor hash), the result is REFUSE:CTX_MISMATCH.
Ch05.A1.c — Trust Model
The trust model of the framework is minimal and explicit: trust is placed only in verifiers and cryptographic commitments, not in narratives.
Verifier trust boundary. The only trusted procedure is the deterministic verifier protocol specified by a version hash. Trust in the verifier is scoped: an artifact is valid relative to a verifier version.
Cryptographic binding. Object identities, pin sets, and witness sets are bound by collision-resistant hashing over canonical encodings.
No ambient trust. Nothing outside the pinned context influences acceptance. External oracles (high-precision numerics, heuristic searches) may generate candidates but are never trusted for promotion.
Auditability. Any trust placed in a verifier is auditable because the verifier version is pinned and the verification protocol is explicitly specified in the treatise.
Ch05.A1.d — Minimal Receipt Invariants
Receipts must satisfy invariants sufficient to prevent silent drift, hidden assumptions, and non-replayable claims.
Replay sufficiency. The receipt references all pins and witnesses necessary for a verifier to recompute acceptance outcomes.
Context integrity. The receipt binds ConvFP bundle hash, corridor hash, policy hash, verifier hash, and canonical encoding hash.
Scope closure. The receipt’s scope matches the scopes of pinned ConvFPs and corridors; any scope gap is illegal.
Non-ambiguity. The receipt provides deterministic merkle roots over pins and witnesses, ensuring that set membership is unambiguous.
Immutability. A receipt is immutable; updates create new receipts linked by migration or replacement edges, never overwrites.
Ch05.A2 — Encode
Ch05.A2.a — Receipt Schema
A receipt is encoded as a canonical record with versioned schema. The minimal schema fields are:
Header.
rcpt_version,
rcpt_id (hash of the canonical receipt record),
created (metadata; must not influence semantics).
Plan fields.
plan_hash,
plan_type (e.g., node_promote, edge_promote, equiv_claim, eval_claim, bridge_promote),
targets (hash ids),
scope (lens ids, carrier type, operator-family scope),
acceptance_predicate_id (gate stack id).
Context fields.
policy_hash,
corridor_hash,
conv_bundle_hash,
verifier_hash,
canon_hash,
schema_hashes (for all record types referenced).
Pins fields.
pins_root,
pins_list (optional, may be omitted under mode constraints if pins can be reconstructed from roots and referenced indices),
pins_index_ref (reference to an index object that expands the root).
Witness fields.
wits_root,
wits_list (optional; same rules as pins),
wits_index_ref.
Mode and visibility.
mode,
visibility_policy (export restrictions),
quarantine_flags (optional).
Canonical encoding rules for receipts are strict: stable ordering and stable numeric/string encoding; no environment-dependent fields may influence hash commitments.
Ch05.A2.b — Merkle Roots
Merkle roots provide unambiguous set commitments for pins and witnesses.
Deterministic merkle construction. Given a multiset of hash ids (S={h_i}), define:
canonical ordering (h_{(1)}\preceq \cdots \preceq h_{(n)}),
merkle root computed by a fixed, versioned algorithm (\mathsf{Merkle}(h_{(1)},\dots,h_{(n)})).
No hidden multiplicity. If multiplicity matters, merkle construction must incorporate multiplicity (e.g., by hashing pairs ((h_i,\mathrm{count}_i))). Otherwise multiplicity is normalized away.
Root expansion. The receipt must provide a way to expand roots into members:
either include lists directly (when mode allows),
or include an index reference that expands roots deterministically.
Verifier obligation. The verifier recomputes merkle roots from expanded sets and checks equality to pins_root and wits_root; mismatch invalidates the receipt.
Ch05.A2.c — Mode Flags
Mode flags define the visibility and reuse regime and are part of the receipt’s enforceable semantics.
public. Full pins and witnesses are exportable; independent replay is permitted.
restricted. Certain pins (e.g., private datasets, proprietary policies) are hidden; replay may require local access. The receipt must still be internally replayable within the allowed boundary.
private. No export of pins/witnesses beyond local boundary; receipt still binds internal correctness.
quarantined. Artifact is not reusable for promotion in downstream contexts without explicit clearance; used for evidence storage and remediation only.
Mode flags are enforced by verifiers and by export tooling. Mode may restrict disclosure but cannot weaken witness obligations for promotion.
Ch05.A2.d — Visibility Boundaries
Visibility boundaries formalize what an external party can validate.
Disclosure surface. A receipt must declare which pins and witnesses are disclosed and which are withheld.
Withheld dependencies. If dependencies are withheld, the receipt must declare whether external verification is impossible or possible via redacted proofs (if such a mechanism exists and is itself specified).
Boundary consistency. Withholding may not hide semantic assumptions required for equivalence. If equivalence requires hidden ConvFP or corridor fields, the claim cannot be exported as semantically valid; it must be marked as boundary-limited.
Cross-boundary reuse. Downstream reuse across boundaries requires explicit policy and bridge artifacts. Otherwise reuse is blocked by policy constraints.
Ch05.A3 — Prove
Ch05.A3.a — Local Verifiability
Local verifiability is the central property receipts enforce.
Local verifier completeness. Given a receipt and the expanded pins and witnesses, the verifier can reconstruct:
the plan obligations,
the context hashes,
the route words and gate stacks (via witness objects),
the acceptance predicate outcomes.
Recompute requirement. Where possible, residuals and enclosures are recomputed; stored values are treated as non-authoritative hints unless explicitly allowed as cached evidence under a cache-correctness proof.
No global dependencies. Local verification does not require access to the full metro; only to pinned objects and witnesses.
Deterministic outcome. Under pinned environment assumptions, verification yields deterministic acceptance/refusal.
Ch05.A3.b — Dependency Correctness
Dependency correctness ensures that any reuse is valid and cannot smuggle in hidden assumptions.
Explicit dependency graph. Dependencies are edges DEP and are reflected in pins. If an artifact uses an object, that object’s id must be pinned.
Membership proofs. If an artifact claims to depend on a set (e.g., “basis set at weight w”), the dependency includes a membership proof: a hash commitment to the set plus a proof that the referenced element is included.
Typed dependency. Dependencies are typed: uses_lens, uses_transport, uses_corridor, uses_policy, uses_ucw, uses_bridge, uses_eval_receipt. Type mismatch invalidates dependency claims.
Closure of context. Dependency correctness requires that all semantic contexts (ConvFP bundles, corridors, policies) needed to interpret dependencies are also pinned.
Ch05.A3.c — Non-Equivalence of Merge
Merge and co-verification are data operations; they are not semantic equivalence.
Merge definition. A merge is the union of two stores of artifacts and indices. Merge may increase available evidence but does not assert any equality.
No merge implies equivalence. If two metros are merged, the presence of two nodes named similarly does not imply they refer to the same invariant; equivalence requires a witness pack and receipts binding that witness.
Conflict preservation. Merge must preserve conflict proofs and quarantines; it may not “resolve” conflicts by averaging or by selecting one side as default without explicit governance.
Compatibility enforcement. If merged artifacts have incompatible ConvFP or policy bindings, the merged store must maintain the incompatibility and prevent illegal reuse until bridges are promoted.
Ch05.A3.d — No Hidden Assumptions
Receipts forbid hidden assumptions by construction.
Assumption closure. Every assumption used in verification must appear as a pinned object: lens specs, convention records, corridor records, policy records, verifier version.
Method closure. Method IDs and bound IDs used in certified evaluations must be pinned or reconstructible through pinned schemas.
Environment closure. Where environment influences determinism or rounding semantics, environment pins must be explicit; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Scope closure. Scope gaps are illegal: an operator used by a witness must be covered by ConvFP scope and corridor scope, or else the receipt is invalid.
Ch05.A4 — Extract
Ch05.A4.a — Dependency Graphs
Dependency graphs are extracted as typed DAGs over artifact identities.
Node set. Artifacts, receipts, witnesses, policies, corridors, ConvFP records.
Edge set. Typed dependency edges with membership proofs when set dependencies exist.
Closure extraction. Given a target artifact, extract the transitive closure of required dependencies.
Cycle detection. Detect illegal dependency cycles (e.g., an artifact whose proof depends on itself without declared fixed-point semantics). Cycles are legal only when explicitly modeled as fixed-point objects with verification obligations.
Ch05.A4.b — Proof Slices
A proof slice is a minimal subset of the store sufficient to verify a target.
Slice definition. For a target receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}), the slice contains:
the receipt,
all pins expanded from pins_root,
all witnesses expanded from wits_root,
any schemas and verifier records referenced by context.
Minimality criterion. A slice is minimal when removing any member makes verification impossible.
Boundary-aware slicing. Under restricted/private modes, slicing respects visibility boundaries and produces boundary-limited slices with explicit non-exportable requirements.
Slice hashing. A slice is hash-committed as a bundle for export and audit.
Ch05.A4.c — Minimal Export Sets
Minimal export sets are computed deterministically to support cross-site verification.
Export plan. Determine which artifacts must be exported for replay: pins and witnesses, plus schemas and verifier versions.
Redaction rules. If redaction is required by mode, export includes redaction proofs or declarations that independent verification is impossible.
Bridge inclusion. If equivalence depends on bridges (convention/policy), export set includes bridge artifacts and their witness packs.
Index inclusion. Export includes the indices necessary to expand merkle roots into explicit members.
Ch05.A4.d — Audit Packets
Audit packets are pre-packaged proof slices designed for repeated verification and forensic analysis.
Packet contents. Receipt, pins expansion, witness expansion, route words, gate stacks, and refusal artifacts if applicable.
Packet types. promotion_packet, equivalence_packet, bridge_packet, refusal_packet, migration_packet.
Deterministic assembly. Packets are assembled by deterministic rules from receipt roots; no human selection.
Audit execution. Packets include scripts or procedure descriptions enabling independent replay under pinned verifier and environment requirements.
B. Flower
Ch05.B1 — Spec
Ch05.B1.a — ProofDep Rules
Proof dependencies define how proofs consume prior artifacts.
ProofDep relation. (\mathsf{ProofDep}(A\to B)) indicates that (B)’s verification requires (A) as a pinned dependency or as a witness.
Dependency typing. ProofDep is typed: semantic_dep (meaning context), evidence_dep (witness), method_dep (method schema), governance_dep (policy/corridor).
Obligation propagation. If (B) depends on (A), then the scope and context of (A) must be compatible with (B)’s context or bridged explicitly.
Refusal propagation. If (A) is refused or quarantined for a relevant scope, (B) cannot be promoted unless a remediation path is explicitly provided and verified.
Ch05.B1.b — Membership Proofs
Membership proofs ensure correctness when dependencies are sets or structured collections.
Set commitments. A set (S) is committed by a hash root (merkle root). Membership of (x\in S) is proved by a membership proof compatible with the merkle scheme.
Ordered collection commitments. If order matters (bases), the commitment includes ordering rules pinned by ConvFP; membership proofs include position or ordering evidence.
Schema-bound membership. Membership proofs are interpreted under schema versions; schema mismatch invalidates proofs.
Auditability. Membership proofs are replay-verifiable and extracted in audit packets.
Ch05.B1.c — Composition Rules
Receipts and proofs compose by explicit rules.
Receipt composition. If (A) and (B) compose into (C), then (\mathsf{Rcpt}(C)) includes:
(\mathsf{Pins}(A)\cup \mathsf{Pins}(B)\cup) any new pins,
(\mathsf{Wits}(A)\cup \mathsf{Wits}(B)\cup) any new witnesses,
context hashes ensuring compatibility.
Witness composition. Witnesses compose only when their scopes align and when their residual obligations compose under declared laws. Otherwise additional commutation/holonomy witnesses are required.
Scope composition. The scope of composed artifacts is the union of scopes; gaps require additional ConvFP records or bridges.
Budget composition. Composed claims may require stronger budgets; budgets are never silently strengthened or weakened—new corridors/policies must be explicitly bound.
Ch05.B1.d — Reuse Semantics
Reuse is governed by receipt validity and scope compatibility.
Reuse rule. An artifact is reusable iff it has a valid promoted receipt under the consumer’s required context or there exists a verified bridge/migration path to a compatible context.
No implicit reuse. Name matching, numeric agreement, or structural similarity does not authorize reuse.
Selective reuse. Consumers may reuse only subsets of an artifact’s claims (e.g., evaluation receipt but not equivalence) by slicing receipts and witnesses appropriately.
Reuse under evolution. Reuse remains valid under evolution only if migration edges preserve witnesses or if re-verification confirms continued validity.
Ch05.B2 — Encode
Ch05.B2.a — Dependency Edges
Dependency edges encode ProofDep in the metro graph.
Edge schema. DEP(src_id, dst_id, dep_type, scope, ctx_hashes, membership_proof_ref).
Context binding. Dependency edges include policy/corridor/ConvFP bundle hashes relevant to the dependency.
Membership proof integration. When dependencies are set-based, the edge references a membership proof artifact.
Versioning. Dependency edges include schema version tags so that interpretation is stable over time.
Ch05.B2.b — Proof Objects
Proof objects are first-class: UCWs, certified evaluation receipts, bridge packs, refusal packs, regression/stress packs.
Proof object identity. Each proof object is hash-addressed under canonical encoding.
Proof object scope. Each proof object declares scope: which lenses, operators, policies, corridors, and conventions it applies to.
Proof object parameters. Each proof object binds to route words and method IDs; parameter omission is illegal.
Proof object lifecycle. Proof objects can be promoted, deprecated, or quarantined; lifecycle is encoded by migration and status edges.
Ch05.B2.c — Witness Roots
Witness roots are merkle roots over witness object identities.
Root computation. Roots computed by deterministic merkle scheme over witness ids.
Root referencing. Receipts reference witness roots; witness root expansion is provided by an index or explicit list.
Root scope. Witness roots are scope-indexed; a root is meaningful only under the context hashes bound in the receipt.
Root audit. Verifiers recompute roots from expanded lists; mismatches invalidate receipts.
Ch05.B2.d — Canonical Ordering
Canonical ordering eliminates ambiguity in sets and basis structures.
Ordering rules pinned. All orderings used in merkle constructions, basis commitments, or proof lists are pinned by ConvFP or schema rules.
Stable ordering under evolution. If ordering rules evolve, migration edges must update commitments; old commitments remain replayable.
Ordering in proof slicing. Slices preserve ordering commitments and include ordering rules as pins.
Ordering mismatch handling. Ordering mismatches trigger refusal as ORDERING_MISMATCH, not silent re-sorting.
Ch05.B3 — Prove
Ch05.B3.a — Compositionality Lemma
Compositionality states that verified artifacts can be combined without introducing hidden assumptions.
Let (A) and (B) be artifacts with valid receipts (\mathsf{Rcpt}(A)) and (\mathsf{Rcpt}(B)). Suppose there exists a typed composition rule producing (C := A\circ B) with declared additional obligations (\Delta). Then there exists a receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}(C)) such that:
(\mathsf{Pins}(C)=\mathsf{Pins}(A)\cup\mathsf{Pins}(B)\cup\mathsf{Pins}(\Delta)).
(\mathsf{Wits}(C)=\mathsf{Wits}(A)\cup\mathsf{Wits}(B)\cup\mathsf{Wits}(\Delta)).
(\mathsf{Ctx}(C)) binds compatible policy/corridor/ConvFP regimes or includes explicit bridges/migrations.
A verifier can replay verification of (C) using only the slice determined by (\mathsf{Rcpt}(C)).
This lemma fails (and must refuse) if contexts are incompatible and cannot be bridged under admissible policies.
Ch05.B3.b — Substitution Safety
Substitution safety ensures that using a certified artifact in place of another preserves correctness.
Typed substitution. A substitution (A\leadsto A') is legal only when (A) and (A') share the same type and scope, and (A') has a promoted receipt that implies (A)’s obligations under the consumer’s corridor/policy.
Receipt implication. Substitution requires proof that (\mathsf{Rcpt}(A')) satisfies or strengthens the acceptance predicate required for (A). If (A') is certified under a weaker corridor, substitution is illegal.
Convention and policy alignment. ConvFP bundle and policy hashes must match or be bridged; otherwise substitution is illegal.
No silent optimization. Replacing a baseline evaluator with an accel evaluator is legal only when a transport witness certifies equivalence and the substitution is recorded in route identity or in activated chart state under a promoted receipt.
Ch05.B3.c — Scope Safety
Scope safety ensures that proofs are not used outside their declared scope.
Scope inclusion test. A proof object may be consumed only if the consumer scope is a subset of the proof’s declared scope or if explicit bridges extend scope.
Scope conflict refusal. If the consumer uses operators not covered by the pinned ConvFP bundle or corridor scope, consumption is illegal and must refuse.
Scope evolution. When scope definitions evolve, migration edges provide scope mapping; otherwise old proofs remain limited to their original scope.
Boundary enforcement. Visibility boundaries and restricted modes are enforced at consumption time; missing access to pinned objects forbids external verification.
Ch05.B3.d — Replay Determinism
Replay determinism is the property that verification outcomes are stable under replay.
Deterministic encodings. Canonical encoding rules are pinned and versioned.
Deterministic route words. WordNF hashes are pinned; route segment streams include all parameters and method IDs.
Deterministic witness recomputation. Verifiers recompute residual curves and enclosures using pinned method/bound/rounding IDs and κ schedules.
Deterministic dependency expansion. Merkle roots expand deterministically into member lists via pinned indices; expansion mismatches invalidate receipts.
Ch05.B4 — Extract
Ch05.B4.a — Slice-by-Goal
Slice-by-goal extracts minimal evidence for a specified claim goal (e.g., “verify equivalence edge E”).
Goal resolution. Resolve the target receipt for the goal.
Root expansion. Expand pins_root and wits_root deterministically.
Closure completion. Include any schema and verifier records needed to interpret pinned records.
Bundle emission. Emit a goal bundle with deterministic ordering and a bundle hash.
Ch05.B4.b — Slice-by-Node
Slice-by-node extracts all receipts and evidence relevant to a node (invariant object).
Node receipts. Extract promotion receipts for node identity and atlas charts.
Inbound/outbound edges. Extract receipts for edges connected to the node, filtered by scope.
Witness sets. Extract UCWs and certified evaluation receipts referenced by those receipts.
Risk status. Extract quarantines, deprecations, and drift alarms affecting the node.
Ch05.B4.c — Slice-by-Edge
Slice-by-edge extracts all evidence relevant to a specific transport, reduction, or equivalence edge.
Edge receipt. Extract the receipt certifying the edge.
Route words. Extract WordNF segment streams referenced in witnesses.
Witness packs. Extract UCWs and auxiliary certificates certifying commutation/holonomy/projection gates.
Repair/tunnel history. Extract any MagicCertPack or tunnel logs that contributed to edge promotion.
Ch05.B4.d — Slice-by-Policy
Slice-by-policy extracts all evidence admissible under a given policy.
Policy filter. Select receipts whose policy hash matches or is bridged to the requested policy.
Blocked artifacts. List artifacts blocked under policy constraints.
Bridge requirements. Extract missing policy bridges or convention bridges required to reuse blocked artifacts.
Export packages. Emit policy-scoped replay bundles and compatibility reports.
C. Cloud
Ch05.C1 — Spec
Ch05.C1.a — Confidence Levels
Confidence levels are formal strata describing what a receipt certifies.
Exact. Canonical-form equality under exact semantics.
Certified. Tri-gate UCW satisfaction under corridor and adequate probes; certified enclosures.
Near-certified. Residuals near gates with admissible repair options; not promotable.
Heuristic. Candidate-level evidence; not promotable.
Refused. Evidence indicates inability to decide under budgets or policy; refusal pack is authoritative.
Confidence levels are stored as tags in receipts and are interpreted by schedulers and planners.
Ch05.C1.b — Certified vs Heuristic Receipts
Receipts are classified by whether they support promotion.
Certified receipts. Bind to certified witnesses and are eligible for promotion if policy gates are satisfied.
Heuristic receipts. Bind to heuristic evidence; can guide search and scheduling but cannot certify semantic equivalence or promoted evaluation.
Mixed receipts. Bind to both, but only certified witnesses may appear in wits_root for promotable claims; heuristic evidence must be segregated.
Boundary-limited receipts. Under restricted visibility, receipts may be locally certified but not externally verifiable; they must declare boundary limitations.
Ch05.C1.c — Upgrade Paths
Upgrade paths describe how a receipt can move to a higher confidence level.
Heuristic→Certified. Requires certified evaluation, UCW recomputation, tail-bound verification, and projector stability.
Near→Certified. Requires corridor refinement, probe expansion, Snap/tunnel repairs, and defect reduction evidence.
Certified→Promoted. Requires policy-required stress and regression suites, plus any bridge promotions needed for scope.
Refused→Actionable. Requires policy/corridor adjustments or carrier escalation; refusal specifies admissible next actions.
Ch05.C1.d — Refusal Receipts
A refusal receipt is a receipt whose plan certifies a refusal artifact rather than a semantic claim.
Refusal scope. Binds the attempted route, corridor, policy, and ConvFP.
Best-achieved evidence. Includes residual curves, budget consumption, and adequacy status.
Classification. Includes refusal class and dominance evidence (plateau, drift, holonomy obstruction).
Actionability. Includes admissible next actions, which may include scheduling obstruction compilation, bridge creation, or policy changes.
Ch05.C2 — Encode
Ch05.C2.a — Confidence Tags
Confidence tags are canonical fields in receipts and witnesses.
confidence_level and promotion_eligibility.
scope_coverage and visibility_mode.
risk_class and stress_required.
regression_required and suite_ids.
Tags are deterministic and do not alter semantics; they alter governance and scheduling.
Ch05.C2.b — Uncertainty Payloads
Uncertainty payloads encode residual curves, enclosures, and plateau evidence.
κ-indexed residual maxima for face/spin/projection.
enclosure radii and tail bound summaries.
plateau predicates and drift alarms.
probe adequacy metadata.
Uncertainty payloads appear in witness objects and are referenced by receipts.
Ch05.C2.c — Regression References
Regression references bind receipts to regression suites.
regression suite IDs required by policy.
references to regression result witness packs.
triggers and timestamps (metadata only).
bisect lineage links to identify what changed when regressions fail.
Ch05.C2.d — Stress References
Stress references bind receipts to stress suites for adversarial validation.
stress suite IDs and adversary catalogs.
references to stress result witness packs.
required pass/fail status for promotion.
quarantine linkage for failures.
Ch05.C3 — Prove
Ch05.C3.a — Confidence Monotonicity
Confidence monotonicity states that evidence upgrades do not weaken previously certified obligations.
Upgrading a receipt must preserve pins and witness obligations or strengthen them.
Upgrades may tighten corridors or strengthen probe adequacy; they may not silently weaken obligations.
If an upgrade changes semantic context (ConvFP or policy), it must be recorded as migration/bridge and requires new witnesses.
Confidence monotonicity is verified by comparing receipt plans and context hashes.
Ch05.C3.b — Upgrade Soundness
Upgrade soundness ensures that an upgraded receipt truly certifies the claim.
Upgrade requires verifier recomputation under the new corridor/policy.
Any heuristic component must be removed from promotable witness sets.
Any newly required bridge or migration must be present and witnessed.
Upgrade failure produces refusal receipts; it cannot silently remain “certified.”
Ch05.C3.c — Plateau Detection
Plateau detection is the principled stop condition for infinite refinement.
Plateau predicate applied to κ-indexed residual curves.
If plateau above gates, promotion is forbidden; only escalation or refusal remains.
Plateau classification distinguishes uncertainty plateau from drift plateau by ConvFP checks and cross-κ stability tests.
Plateau evidence must be stored in refusal receipts and referenced by future remediation actions.
Ch05.C3.d — Uncertainty Containment
Uncertainty containment ensures that uncertain artifacts do not contaminate promoted knowledge.
Quarantine uncertain receipts and refuse their use in promotion-critical contexts.
Allow uncertain receipts only as planning aids with explicit tags.
Require explicit upgrade paths before reuse in certified pipelines.
Maintain provenance so that when uncertainty is resolved, promotions can be updated via migration edges.
Ch05.C4 — Extract
Ch05.C4.a — Trust Views
Trust views present a filtered view of the metro based on confidence and visibility.
Show only promoted artifacts under specified policy/corridor/ConvFP scope.
Annotate each artifact with confidence and risk tags.
Hide or restrict artifacts per mode flags.
Provide minimal replay bundles for visible artifacts.
Ch05.C4.b — Downgrade Views
Downgrade views show where previously promoted artifacts have become risky due to drift alarms or failed regressions.
Identify affected receipts and their dependent closures.
Provide refusal or quarantine status.
Provide remediation plans and required bridge/migration actions.
Provide bisect links to localize what caused downgrade.
Ch05.C4.c — Risk Reports
Risk reports summarize where trust is limited.
List quarantined artifacts and reasons.
List missing bridges and policy conflicts.
List plateau regions and non-identifiability triggers.
List required stress/regression suites for pending promotions.
Ch05.C4.d — Audit Proofs
Audit proofs are extraction artifacts enabling independent verification.
Proof slices and audit packets for specified targets.
Merkle root expansions for pins and witnesses.
Verifier version and schema records.
Scripts/procedures for replay and validation.
D. Fractal
Ch05.D1 — Spec
Ch05.D1.a — Receipt Compaction
Receipt compaction reduces storage without losing replay.
Compaction replaces redundant or bulky lists with merkle roots and indices.
Compaction preserves the ability to expand roots deterministically.
Compaction preserves all promoted receipts and designated refusal exemplars.
Compaction is itself certified by compaction receipts linking before/after identities.
Ch05.D1.b — Receipt Linking
Receipt linking creates navigable provenance.
Link receipts to targets (nodes/edges), to the witnesses they rely on, and to the pins they bind.
Link upgraded receipts via migration edges; old receipts remain immutable.
Link receipts to regression/stress results and to drift alarms.
Link receipts across metros via import manifests and compatibility reports.
Ch05.D1.c — Long-Term Replay
Long-term replay is the guarantee that receipts remain verifiable across time.
Preserve schema versions and verifiers or provide verified translators.
Preserve ConvFP and corridor records for historical receipts.
Maintain indices allowing merkle root expansion.
Maintain archival formats preserving canonical encodings and hash identities.
Ch05.D1.d — Cross-Metro Exchange
Cross-metro exchange transmits receipts as proof-carrying exports.
Exchange includes receipts plus minimal replay bundles.
Exchange is subject to visibility modes and policy restrictions.
Imported receipts may be quarantined until locally re-verified under local policies/corridors.
Cross-policy reuse requires explicit policy-bridge receipts.
Ch05.D2 — Encode
Ch05.D2.a — Receipt Caches
Receipt caches accelerate verification without weakening soundness.
Caches store expanded pin/witness sets keyed by receipt id.
Cache entries include validity checks and invalidation rules tied to schema/verifier changes.
Cached computations do not replace verifier recomputation; they only accelerate access to required objects.
Cache correctness is proven by cache invariants and verified by periodic cache audits.
Ch05.D2.b — Reference Graphs
Reference graphs provide global navigation over receipts.
Nodes: receipts, targets, witnesses, pins.
