Edges: certifies, depends_on, uses_witness, pins_context, upgrades_to, migrates_to.
Graph is deterministic and hash-addressed by canonical encoding.
Graph supports extraction queries and provenance chaining.
Ch05.D2.c — Checksum Strategy
Checksums ensure integrity of stored and exported bundles.
Bundle root hash commits to all included items.
Item hashes commit to canonical encodings.
Merkle roots commit to sets.
Export manifests commit to mode flags and disclosure policies.
Ch05.D2.d — Archival Formats
Archival formats preserve replay across time.
Archives store canonical encodings and hashes.
Archives store schema versions and verifier versions.
Archives store indices for root expansion.
Archives store migration and deprecation metadata.
Ch05.D3 — Prove
Ch05.D3.a — Compaction Soundness
Compaction soundness ensures compaction does not change verifier outcomes.
For each promoted receipt, verification outcomes before and after compaction are identical under pinned verifiers.
Compaction preserves the ability to expand pins/witness roots.
Compaction preserves necessary schema and verifier records.
Compaction produces compaction receipts proving these properties.
Ch05.D3.b — Link Integrity
Link integrity ensures that reference graphs are correct and unambiguous.
All links reference existing hash ids.
Links are typed and scope-indexed.
Link targets are immutable; link updates create new link artifacts.
Broken links produce refusal artifacts for dependent exports.
Ch05.D3.c — Cross-Policy Safety
Cross-policy safety prevents policy laundering.
Receipts are valid only under pinned policies or under explicit policy bridges.
Imported receipts require compatibility checks with local policy.
If a receipt cannot be verified under local policy, it is quarantined.
Policy bridges must be witnessed and promotable under both source and target policies.
Ch05.D3.d — De-Duplication Safety
De-duplication removes duplicates without collapsing semantics.
De-duplicate only by hash identity, not by name or numeric similarity.
When two objects are numerically close but not identical, de-duplication is forbidden unless an equivalence witness exists.
Preserve conflict proofs and quarantines.
De-duplication must not remove evidence needed for replay; compaction receipts certify preservation.
Ch05.D4 — Extract
Ch05.D4.a — Metro Export Packs
Export packs are proof-carrying bundles for cross-site use.
Include receipts plus minimal replay bundles.
Include schema and verifier records.
Include merkle root expansion indices.
Respect visibility modes and disclosure policies.
Ch05.D4.b — Minimal Replay Bundles
Minimal replay bundles are the smallest sets sufficient for verification.
receipt + plan.
pins expansion.
witness expansion.
schemas + verifier version.
Bundles are hash-committed and deterministic.
Ch05.D4.c — Provenance Chains
Provenance chains trace how an artifact became promoted.
Chain receipts and migrations.
Chain bridge promotions and tunnel logs.
Chain regression and stress results.
Chain drift alarms and remediations.
Ch05.D4.d — Verification Scripts
Verification scripts are deterministic procedures for replay.
Expand roots.
Validate context hashes.
Recompute witness obligations and gate predicates.
Emit accept/refuse plus evidence; record results as audit artifacts.
Ch05 — Receipts, Pins, and Proof/Dependency Algebra
Ch06 — Metro Graph: Nodes, Edges, and the 4-Root Atlas
A. Square
Ch06.A1 — Spec
Ch06.A1.a — Node Types
A metro graph is a typed, hash-addressed, witness-bearing multigraph whose nodes represent invariant objects and whose edges represent typed claims and typed construction moves between those objects. Let[\mathcal M := (\mathcal V,\mathcal E,\mathcal T,\mathcal S)]where (\mathcal V) is a finite set of nodes, (\mathcal E) is a finite multiset of directed edges, (\mathcal T) is the type system for nodes and edges, and (\mathcal S) is the schema system (versioned) that specifies canonical encoding, admissibility fields, and witness obligations.
A node (v\in\mathcal V) has a node type (\tau(v)\in\mathcal T_V). The normative node types are:
Seed node. A minimal generator declared as primitive for a domain and scope. A seed node is not “assumed true” by narrative; it is promotable only if it has a valid atlas and baseline certified evaluators sufficient for downstream verification within declared corridors and policies.
Derived node. An invariant defined by a typed construction from other nodes, supported by a derivation edge set and receipts binding the dependency and witness obligations.
Candidate node. An invariant proposed as necessary by deficiency or obstruction mechanisms (forced generators, Tier-0 obstruction candidates, portal candidates). Candidate nodes may carry refusal or near-evidence but are not promotable without atlas closure obligations.
Alias node. A semantic indirection node representing a name-class or equivalence class pointer. Alias nodes do not assert equivalence; they index known equivalence evidence and route selection defaults, preserving separation between naming and meaning.
Portal node. A node representing a required extension of the semantic alphabet: new carrier class, new invariant schema, or new lens family required to reconcile conflicts or to capture monodromy/topological obstructions. Portal nodes require explicit portal obligations and do not collapse to ordinary seed nodes without additional structure.
Bridge node. A node representing a reusable conversion capability (convention bridge, policy bridge, transport bridge) packaged as “brain tissue.” Bridge nodes exist to make recurring repair/tunnel actions compositional and reusable.
Bundle node. A node representing a packaged extractable proof slice, a minimal replay bundle, or an export pack. Bundle nodes are auxiliary and are governed by receipt algebra and visibility modes.
Each node type declares: (i) admissible inbound/outbound edge types, (ii) required atlas obligations (if promotable), (iii) required witness obligations (if it participates in equivalence or transport), and (iv) a permitted lifecycle (draft, certified, promoted, deprecated, quarantined, refused).
Ch06.A1.b — Invariant Fields
Each node represents an invariant object and carries a normative set of invariant fields. Let (v) be a node; its node specification record (\mathrm{NodeSpec}(v)) includes:
Semantic identity fields.
kind: node type (\tau(v)),
domain_tag: domain identifier (e.g., constant system, operator system, geometry system),
carrier_type: carrier taxonomy key,
lens_family_ids: identifiers of the lens families in which the invariant is represented,
inv_schema_keys: invariant schema keys specifying admissibility and equivalence semantics,
conv_bundle_hash: ConvBundleFP binding conventions relevant to interpreting the node’s representations,
policy_class (optional): the policy class in which the node is intended to be promotable, when promotion is policy-scoped.
Structural invariants.
weight, depth, level (when the domain admits graded structure),
arity and parameters (if the invariant is a family rather than a single object),
admissible_region_id and stability region identifiers,
canonical_form_id or projector_id describing the representability regime required for promotion.
Atlas obligations.
atlas_id: pointer to the atlas object,
required root set: ({\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R}) (Square, Flower, Cloud, Fractal),
closure_targets: explicit edge/face/interior closure obligations relevant to the node.
Evidence pointers.
baseline_receipt_ids: certified evaluation receipts for baseline charts,
promoted_receipt_ids: receipts certifying promotion or equivalence class status,
refusal_ids (optional): refusal receipts providing best-achieved evidence and next actions.
Invariant fields are interpreted by verifiers. Fields that affect meaning are pinned by ConvBundleFP and schema hashes; fields that affect acceptance are pinned by corridor and policy hashes.
Ch06.A1.c — Atlas Requirement
A node is promotable only if it satisfies the atlas requirement. The atlas requirement is the statement that the invariant is represented and certified in four orthogonal representation roots, and that those roots are mutually coherent under witnessed transports.
Atlas definition. The atlas of a node (v) is a finite set of chart objects[\mathrm{Atlas}(v) := { \mathsf{Chart}i }{i=1}^N,]together with a typing map (\mathrm{Root}:\mathrm{Atlas}(v)\to{\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R}) and a set of transport edges between charts and between charts and baseline representations.
Root coverage. For promotion, the atlas must contain at least one certified chart in each root:[\forall r\in{\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R},;\exists,\mathsf{Chart}\in\mathrm{Atlas}(v)\text{ with }\mathrm{Root}(\mathsf{Chart})=r.]
Chart certification. Each chart must provide a certified evaluation receipt: enclosure (interval or carrier-appropriate enclosure), deterministic error bound (tail or truncation bound), and rounding/representability binding. Charts may be slow; speed is not required for baseline atlas certification.
Coherence requirement. The atlas must include sufficient witnessed transports to establish that all root charts represent the same invariant under the tri-gate acceptance logic. Coherence is expressed as UCWs for commutation and as loop witnesses for holonomy, indexed by corridor and policy.
Closure minimality. The atlas must provide a minimal closure skeleton adequate to prevent “chart islands.” Minimality requires that the atlas’ chart graph be connected by promotable transport edges and that the closure obligations declared by the node schema are satisfied (including any required interior coherence certificate for SFCR closure when the domain requires it).
Acceleration separation. Acceleration charts are optional and may be active, but any acceleration chart must be linked back to certified baseline charts by a transport witness. No accelerated chart may introduce a second semantic truth; it is only an alternative route.
Ch06.A1.d — Identity Semantics
Node identity is semantic and immutable. Names do not define identity; hashes do.
Node identity. The identity of node (v) is:[\mathrm{id}(v) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{NodeSpec}(v))),]under the canonical encoding pinned by schema version. Node identity is independent of non-authoritative numeric approximants and independent of human-readable naming.
Equality of nodes. Nodes are strictly equal iff their identities match. Any other claim of equivalence between distinct node identities is an edge-level claim requiring witnesses; it does not merge identities.
Equivalence classes. Equivalence of meaning between distinct node identities is represented by a typed equivalence edge with UCW-grade witnesses and receipts, indexed by conventions, policy, and corridor. Equivalence does not destroy multiplicity; it creates a verified relation.
Immutability. NodeSpec is immutable for a given node id. Any change in meaning-affecting fields (schemas, conventions, admissible regions) yields a different identity. Evolution is expressed by migration edges; selection of “preferred” nodes is expressed by alias and governance pointers, never by overwriting.
Ch06.A2 — Encode
Ch06.A2.a — Node Hashes
Node hashing is the mechanism that binds the metro graph to replay truth.
Canonical encoding. NodeSpec is canonically encoded with stable ordering, stable numeric string forms, explicit schema version fields, and explicit scope fields.
Hash composition discipline. NodeSpec includes hashes of any embedded or referenced records that determine meaning: lens ids, schema hashes, ConvBundleFP, and canonical form ids. It includes only hash pointers for large objects; it does not inline bulk evidence.
Atlas pointer hashing. The node includes an atlas_id pointer; the atlas object is separately hash-addressed. A node can exist without a complete atlas as a candidate, but it cannot be promoted without an atlas that satisfies requirements.
Identity pinning. Receipts that certify a node pin (\mathrm{id}(v)) and the schema hashes necessary to interpret NodeSpec. Any mismatch in node hash or schema hash invalidates the receipt.
Ch06.A2.b — Naming Rules
Naming rules separate human use from semantic truth.
Name fields. Each node carries:
name: human-readable,
synonyms: optional,
tags: indexing labels.These fields are never used to assert identity or equivalence.
Disambiguation. If two distinct nodes share a name, disambiguation is performed by appending typed keys (domain tag, weight/depth, ConvBundleFP prefix) and/or hash prefixes. Disambiguation is deterministic.
Name stability. Names may change without changing identity; such changes are represented as metadata updates attached to alias nodes or indices, not as NodeSpec changes.
Forbidden name-based equivalence. Any planner or extractor must treat name matches as suggestions only. Equivalence requires witnessed edges and receipts.
Ch06.A2.c — Metadata Schema
Metadata supports routing, governance, and extraction without altering semantics.
Lifecycle metadata. status ∈ {draft, certified, promoted, deprecated, quarantined, refused} with timestamps (non-semantic).
Cost metadata. Optional performance models (latency, complexity) used by schedulers; not admissible evidence for semantic claims.
Coverage metadata. Which corridors, policies, and scopes have promoted receipts. Coverage is a map from context hashes to receipt ids.
Risk metadata. Risk class and required stress/regression suites for promotion, indexed by policy.
Metadata is stored as separate index objects or as non-meaning fields that are explicitly excluded from NodeSpec hash commitments.
Ch06.A2.d — Atlas Pointers
Atlas pointers connect nodes to their chart systems and closure obligations.
Atlas object identity. (\mathrm{id}(\mathrm{Atlas}(v)) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{AtlasSpec}(v)))). AtlasSpec includes chart specs, root assignments, chart activation state, and required transport witness pointers.
Chart pointers. Each chart pointer includes:
chart id and type,
root assignment (\mathsf S/\mathsf F/\mathsf C/\mathsf R),
evaluator method ids and bound ids,
ConvFP scope and corridor/policy applicability,
receipt ids certifying its evaluation obligations.
Transport pointers. AtlasSpec references transport edges between charts and their UCWs. These edges are required to certify coherence and to prevent chart islands.
Closure pointers. AtlasSpec references closure obligations (edges, faces, loops, interior certificates when required). Each closure obligation points to the witnesses and receipts that certify it.
Ch06.A3 — Prove
Ch06.A3.a — Node Equality
Node equality is a strict and verifiable relation.
Identity equality. (v=w) iff (\mathrm{id}(v)=\mathrm{id}(w)). This is purely cryptographic identity under canonical encoding.
Semantic equivalence does not imply equality. If (v\neq w) but represent the same invariant under different conventions or schemas, they remain distinct nodes; equivalence is asserted by an equivalence edge, not by identity.
Equality under migration. Migration edges do not preserve identity; they relate identities under declared transformations. A migrated node is not equal to its predecessor; it is linked by a migration edge and may be declared “current” by governance pointers.
Verifier check. Node equality requires only canonical encoding and hashing; it is independent of numeric evaluation.
Ch06.A3.b — Aliasing Rules
Aliasing rules separate naming from meaning while supporting navigation.
Alias nodes as indices. An alias node (a) contains:
a canonical name class,
a set of candidate node ids,
optional governance pointers indicating preferred nodes under given contexts.Alias nodes do not assert that candidates are equivalent.
Equivalence evidence attachment. Alias nodes may reference equivalence edges among candidates, but only as pointers to witness-bearing edges. The alias node itself is not evidence.
Context-indexed preference. Preferences are indexed by ((\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},\mathrm{Policy},\mathrm{Corridor})). A node preferred in one context may not be preferred in another.
Alias safety. Any attempt to treat alias membership as equivalence is illegal. Verifiers refuse claims derived from alias membership without explicit equivalence witnesses.
Ch06.A3.c — Canonical Representatives
Canonical representatives provide deterministic selection without semantic collapse.
Representative selection. Within a context-indexed equivalence class (\mathcal C) (nodes linked by promotable equivalence edges under a fixed context), select a canonical representative by a deterministic rule:
minimal node id under a fixed ordering, or
governance-designated “current” node id, provided governance pointer itself is pinned and auditable.
Representative scope. Canonical representatives are scoped: a representative is canonical only relative to the context in which the equivalence class is defined and witnessed.
Representative stability. If a new node is added or a bridge is promoted, representatives may change. Such changes are recorded as governance pointer updates, not as identity changes.
Representative correctness. Selecting a representative does not modify equivalence obligations; it merely standardizes extraction and route planning.
Ch06.A3.d — Integrity Checks
Metro integrity is a set of verifiable invariants ensuring the graph is sound and auditable.
Schema integrity. Every node and edge record references known schema version hashes; unknown schema versions are refused.
Context integrity. Every promotable edge and promotable atlas chart has pinned ConvBundleFP, policy hash, corridor hash, and verifier hash. Context mismatch invalidates promotion claims.
Witness integrity. Every promotable edge has witness pointers sufficient to satisfy its legality predicates:
UCWs for commutation/holonomy/representability as required,
certified evaluation receipts for evaluation edges,
bridge packs for bridge edges,
regression/stress packs for high-risk promotions.
Atlas integrity. Every promoted node satisfies atlas root coverage and coherence requirements, and atlas closure obligations are witnessed and replayable.
No shortcut integrity. The graph contains no semantic edges without witnesses. Any edge asserting equivalence without witness is invalid and quarantined.
Ch06.A4 — Extract
Ch06.A4.a — Node Queries
Node query primitives are deterministic and scope-aware.
By node id. Retrieve NodeSpec and metadata by (\mathrm{id}(v)).
By structured keys. Retrieve by domain keys such as (domain_tag, weight, depth, level, carrier_type), returning a disambiguated set of nodes.
By scope. Retrieve nodes admissible under a given ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor context, filtered by promotion status.
By evidence coverage. Retrieve nodes that have promoted receipts for specified target classes (evaluation, equivalence, reduction, bridge readiness).
Ch06.A4.b — Atlas Extraction
Atlas extraction returns the full chart system and its closure evidence.
Chart list extraction. Extract all charts with root assignments, activation states, evaluator method ids, bound ids, and receipt ids.
Coherence extraction. Extract transport edges linking charts and the UCWs certifying coherence.
Closure extraction. Extract required closure obligations (edge/face/loop/interior) and their witness packs.
Fallback extraction. Extract at least one certified fallback evaluator per root and the routing rules that select among them under policy and corridor.
Ch06.A4.c — Lineage Extraction
Lineage extraction reconstructs how a node came to exist and how it evolved.
Derivation lineage. Follow derivation edges and dependency receipts to list predecessor nodes, transports, and witnesses.
Promotion lineage. Follow promotion receipts, regression/stress packs, and bridge packs that enabled promotion.
Migration lineage. Follow migration edges and governance pointers showing which convention/policy/corridor regimes led to new versions.
Refusal lineage. Extract refusal artifacts associated with candidate nodes to identify unresolved obligations and next admissible actions.
Ch06.A4.d — Dependency Extraction
Dependency extraction produces proof slices for reuse and audit.
Pins extraction. Expand pins_root and produce the explicit pin set required for replay.
Witness extraction. Expand wits_root and produce witness objects required for verification.
Schema/verifier extraction. Extract schema records and verifier records referenced by the receipt context.
Minimal bundle assembly. Assemble a minimal replay bundle with deterministic ordering and a bundle root hash, respecting visibility mode constraints.
B. Flower
Ch06.B1 — Spec
Ch06.B1.a — Edge Taxonomy
Edges are typed directed relations between nodes, and edge types determine legality predicates and witness obligations. Let (e\in\mathcal E) be an edge with type (\tau(e)\in\mathcal T_E). The normative edge types are:
baseline. Declares a certified baseline evaluator chart for a node (evaluation edge). Requires certified evaluation receipts.
accel. Declares an accelerated evaluator chart. Requires a certified evaluation receipt for the chart and a transport edge linking the accel chart to a baseline chart by UCW witness.
transport. Declares a meaning-preserving mapping between representations (charts or nodes). Requires UCW-grade witnesses and route words.
collapse. Declares a closed-form or collapse identity mapping one node into algebraic combinations of others (e.g., collapse into (\mathbb Q\cdot\pi^{2n}) in graded domains). Requires certified reduction receipts and representability witnesses.
reduce. Declares an expression of a node in a basis (within a graded space). Requires coefficient witness obligations and corridor-gated residual checks.
derive. Declares construction of a derived node from other nodes by a typed program. Requires receipts capturing dependency and witness obligations for each step.
promote. Declares that a candidate becomes a promoted node under a context. Requires promotion receipts binding atlas closure and governance requirements.
bridge. Declares a conversion capability across conventions or policies. Requires bridge packs (MagicCertPack/BridgeSeed) and UCWs showing commutation and loop closure.
migrate. Declares schema or convention evolution linking older and newer nodes or charts. Requires migration receipts and backward witness preservation obligations.
dep. Declares proof dependency; does not assert semantic equivalence. Requires membership proofs when set-based.
refuse. Declares a structured refusal artifact; links attempted targets to refusal receipts and evidence packs.
Edge types are mutually constrained: for example, an accel edge is illegal without an associated transport witness to baseline; a collapse edge is illegal without certified coefficient evidence; a transport edge is illegal without UCW witnesses.
Ch06.B1.b — Transport vs Accel vs Collapse
Transport, acceleration, and collapse express different semantics and therefore require different evidence.
Transport. A transport edge asserts meaning preservation under a typed mapping. It is a semantic edge and requires UCWs (commutation, holonomy, representability). Transports are scoped by ConvBundleFP, policy, and corridor.
Acceleration. An accel edge asserts only a performance improvement route to the same invariant. It is not a semantic claim unless linked by a transport witness to a baseline chart under the same context. Accel edges cannot exist as islands.
Collapse. A collapse edge asserts that one node lies in the subalgebra generated by others under declared laws (e.g., known closed forms or reduction theorems). Collapse edges are semantic and require certified reduction receipts and stability under projection/canonicalization.
Each edge type forbids conflation: numeric agreement does not convert accel to transport; derivation does not convert to collapse; collapse does not eliminate the need for transport witnesses across representations.
Ch06.B1.c — Edge Payload Contracts
Each edge type carries a payload schema that is normative and minimal.
Identity fields. edge_id, etype, src_id, dst_id, schema_version.
Scope fields. ConvBundleFP, policy hash, corridor hash, operator-family scope.
Route fields. WordNF hash pointer for semantic edges; for evaluation edges, chart evaluator spec id and method/bound/rounding ids.
Obligation fields. Required gate stacks, κ ladders, probe regime ids, admissibility conditions, and budget fields relevant to verification.
Evidence pointers. Witness root pointers, receipt ids, and refusal ids as appropriate.
Payload contracts are enforced by verifiers; missing fields are not “optional,” they are failures of legality.
Ch06.B1.d — Composition Laws
Edges compose into routes, and routes are canonicalized into WordNF objects. Composition is typed and governed by legality predicates.
Typed path composition. A path (e_n\circ\cdots\circ e_1) is admissible only if the domain/range types unify and the scope contexts are compatible or bridged.
Associativity under normalization. Path composition is associative at the level of semantic intent, but route identity is given by canonical WordNF. Any algebraic reassociation must preserve WordNF normalization rules and must not violate convention scope.
Commutation law. Two different paths between the same endpoints are semantically equivalent only if a commutation witness exists certifying them within corridor gates and under adequate probes.
Loop law. Any closed path intended to be identity must have loop closure witnesses; persistent loop residuals trigger obstruction or portal mechanisms rather than silent acceptance.
Ch06.B2 — Encode
Ch06.B2.a — Edge Schemas
Edge schemas are versioned records with canonical encoding.
Canonical record. EdgeSpec includes identity fields, payload fields, scope fields, and evidence pointers.
Deterministic encoding. Keys are ordered, lists are canonicalized, numeric fields are normalized. No ambient state influences encoding.
Schema evolution. When edge schema evolves, migration edges relate old to new; old edges remain replayable.
Indexing. Edges are indexed by type, endpoints, scope, and status; indices are non-semantic and can evolve without changing edge identity.
Ch06.B2.b — WordNF Pointers
Semantic edges must bind route identity explicitly.
WordNF binding. Transport, reduction, derivation, migration, and bridge edges reference WordNF hashes that encode the segment stream (gate runs and tunnel events) with bound parameters.
Segment discipline. Any corridor-changing action (Snap, tunnel) must appear as a segment in WordNF; otherwise the edge is illegal.
Route reproducibility. WordNF includes method ids, bound ids, κ schedules, and probe regime ids sufficient for replay.
Normalization constraints. WordNF normalization is scope-aware: segments cannot be reordered across incompatible ConvFP scopes without explicit commutation witnesses.
Ch06.B2.c — Witness Pointers
Witness pointers bind edges to their evidentiary obligations.
UCW pointers. Semantic edges requiring commutation/holonomy/projection evidence reference UCW hashes and their receipt bindings.
Evaluation receipt pointers. Evaluation edges reference certified evaluation receipts (intervals, tails, rounding ids), not raw approximants.
Bridge pack pointers. Bridge edges reference MagicCertPacks and BridgeSeeds with explicit pre/post corridor hashes and defect reduction evidence.
Regression/stress pointers. Promotion-critical edges reference regression and stress suite result packs when required by policy.
Witness pointers are expanded via receipt algebra; merkle roots commit to the witness set.
Ch06.B2.d — Replay Fields
Replay fields provide deterministic reconstruction of verification steps.
Context hashes. Policy hash, corridor hash, ConvBundleFP hash, verifier hash, schema hashes.
Budget fields. Maximum κ levels, probe counts, tunnel caps, search caps; consumed budget evidence recorded in traces.
Environment pins. Backend ids and deterministic arithmetic semantics where required.
Replay indices. References to indices that expand roots into explicit pins/witness sets; replay is impossible without expansion capability.
Ch06.B3 — Prove
Ch06.B3.a — Edge Legality
Edge legality is a verifier-executable predicate. For an edge (e), legality requires:
Schema validity. EdgeSpec conforms to schema version and includes required fields.
Context validity. Scope fields are complete and covered: ConvBundleFP covers invoked operators; corridor and policy bindings match admissibility constraints.
Witness sufficiency. Required witness objects exist, are valid under the pinned context, and pass their verifier recomputation and gate predicates.
Type correctness. Endpoint node types and edge type are compatible; illegal combinations refuse.
No silent drift. Edge legality fails if ConvFP mismatch exists without bridge, corridor mismatch exists without re-verification or corridor bridging, or policy mismatch exists without policy bridging.
Ch06.B3.b — Composition Associativity Conditions
Associativity of composition is conditional: it holds only when the canonicalization of route words respects scope and legality.
Typed associativity. If (e_1,e_2,e_3) are composable and all intermediate contexts match, then ((e_3\circ e_2)\circ e_1) and (e_3\circ(e_2\circ e_1)) denote the same composed intent.
WordNF associativity. The canonical WordNF of the composed path is computed by a deterministic normalization procedure. Associativity is valid only if the normalization procedure yields identical WordNF for both parenthesizations, which requires that any necessary reordering be justified by commutation rules valid under the bound ConvFP scope.
Witness closure under composition. The witness obligations of composed paths must be composable: when they are not, additional commutation and loop closure witnesses are required and must be included in the composed receipt.
Failure and refusal. If associativity fails due to scope conflicts or missing commutation witnesses, the system refuses composition as a promotable semantic object and emits a refusal artifact identifying the minimal conflict.
Ch06.B3.c — Commute Conditions
Commute conditions state when two routes between the same endpoints can be treated as equivalent.
Commutation condition. Given two paths (P,Q) from node (u) to node (v), commuting means:[\mathrm{UCW}(P,Q)\text{ passes all required gates on the corridor κ ladder with adequate probes under pinned context.}]
Holonomy condition. When the commuting claim participates in a loop (e.g., route selection equivalence classes), loop closure must be certified by spin residual gates; otherwise route equivalence may be path-dependent.
Representability condition. Commutation is valid only if both routes respect representability projectors (\Pi_h) and canonicalization obligations, with residuals bounded by (\varepsilon_\Pi).
Scope condition. Commute conditions are scoped: changing ConvBundleFP, corridor, or policy changes the commuting claim; reuse requires explicit bridging or re-verification.
Ch06.B3.d — Forbidden Shortcuts
The metro graph forbids semantic shortcuts that collapse the distinction between evidence and meaning.
Numeric coincidence shortcut. Declaring equivalence because two floating approximations match is forbidden. Equivalence requires UCWs and certified enclosures under pinned context.
Accel-as-truth shortcut. An accel evaluator cannot replace baseline truth without a transport witness linking it to baseline; otherwise it creates an unverified second truth.
Merge-as-equivalence shortcut. Union of stores does not imply equivalence. Any attempt to treat merged nodes as equal without witnesses is invalid.
Policy laundering shortcut. Using evidence produced under a lax policy to certify claims under a strict policy without explicit policy bridges is forbidden.
Corridor laundering shortcut. Widening tolerance gates to pass verification without explicit corridor identity change and re-verification is forbidden.
Convention laundering shortcut. Treating different ConvFP regimes as equivalent without explicit convention bridges is forbidden.
Ch06.B4 — Extract
Ch06.B4.a — Route Planning Queries
Route planning extracts admissible paths under constraints.
Endpoint query. Given source node, target node, and context ((\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},\mathrm{Policy},\mathrm{Corridor})), return admissible path candidates with required witnesses.
Scope query. Filter to paths whose edges’ scopes are covered by the context or bridged by promoted bridges.
Promotion query. Filter to paths whose edges are promoted for the context or whose receipts can be upgraded by admissible jobs.
Refusal-aware query. If no path exists, return refusal-localized blockers: missing bridges, policy conflicts, plateau regions, or missing atlas roots.
Ch06.B4.b — Cheapest Certified Path
Cheapest certified path selection minimizes a cost functional subject to legality.
Cost functional. Cost is computed from metadata (latency, evaluation complexity) and from corridor demands (required κ). Cost is non-semantic and cannot weaken legal obligations.
Constraints. Path must be admissible and have promotable witnesses under the pinned context.
Selection. Choose minimal cost among admissible promoted paths; if none exist, choose minimal cost among upgradable paths and return a job plan rather than a semantic route.
Proof of selection. Output includes the selected path’s WordNF hashes and receipts; selection itself is not semantic but is auditable by deterministic scoring rules.
Ch06.B4.c — Cross-Lens Path
Cross-lens paths connect nodes represented in different lens families.
Lens transition requirement. Any cross-lens transition must be mediated by transport or bridge edges with UCW-grade witnesses.
Conventions and policies. Cross-lens paths must satisfy ConvBundleFP scope coverage and policy admissibility. Missing coverage yields refusal, not fallback.
Carrier escalation. If cross-lens equivalence is non-identifiable under the current carrier, cross-lens planning may propose carrier escalation as an admissible next action.
Portal detection. If conflict proofs indicate intrinsic incompatibility, cross-lens planning yields a portal requirement rather than a path.
Ch06.B4.d — Hub Routing
Hub routing uses stable, witness-rich nodes and lenses as intermediate stations.
Hub definition. A hub is a node or lens family with:
high connectivity via promoted transport edges,
stable atlas coverage and coherence,
robust witnesses across broad corridors and policies.
Routing heuristic. Prefer routes that factor through hubs minimizing bridge count and maximizing witness reuse.
Witness-aware scoring. Hub choice is scored by availability of promoted UCWs and by minimal need for policy/convention bridging.
Failure localization. If hub routing fails, extract which hub constraint failed: missing bridge, policy restriction, corridor insufficiency, or atlas gap.
C. Cloud
Ch06.C1 — Spec
Ch06.C1.a — Corridor Binding per Edge
Every semantic edge is interpreted relative to a corridor; corridor binding is explicit and verifiable.
Edge corridor binding. Each edge includes corridor_hash and either (i) a pointer to the corridor record or (ii) a record reference retrievable by hash.
Edge-specific obligations. Different edge types bind different gate stacks and κ ladders. A transport edge binds tri-gate UCW obligations; an evaluation edge binds enclosure and tail-bound obligations; a collapse edge binds coefficient and representability obligations.
Context indexing. The same edge may have different receipts under different corridors; these are distinct evidence objects, and promotion is indexed by corridor hash.
No silent reinterpretation. An edge witnessed under corridor (C) cannot be treated as witnessed under corridor (C') without re-verification or explicit corridor bridging.
Ch06.C1.b — Uncertainty per Edge
Uncertainty is represented by witness residual curves and enclosure radii.
Residual curves. For semantic edges, κ-indexed residual maxima for commutation, holonomy, and projection are stored and are recomputable.
Enclosure measures. For evaluation edges, enclosure radii and tail-bound summaries are stored and are recomputable.
Confidence stratification. Each edge has a confidence level (exact, certified, near, heuristic, refused) indexed by context.
Uncertainty locality. Uncertainty is localized: an edge may be certified under one corridor and refused under another; this does not change edge identity, only its evidence coverage.
Ch06.C1.c — Admissibility per Edge
Admissibility is the requirement that an edge can be executed and verified under the pinned context.
Operator admissibility. Edge methods and bounds must be allowed by policy and covered by ConvBundleFP scope.
Carrier admissibility. Edge semantics must be defined on the carrier type and admissible region specified by node schemas.
Probe admissibility. Required probe families must be definable and adequate under policy; prohibited probes require refusal.
Budget admissibility. The edge’s verification obligations must be satisfiable within declared budgets. Otherwise the edge can exist but cannot be promoted under that policy.
Ch06.C1.d — Refusal per Edge
Refusal is a first-class outcome at the edge level.
Refusal edges. When an attempt to certify an edge fails, a refusal artifact is linked by a refuse edge from the attempted edge or from the job record to the refusal receipt.
Refusal classification. Refusals classify whether failure arises from plateau, drift, holonomy obstruction, policy mismatch, convention mismatch, or non-identifiability.
Refusal evidence. Refusals include best-achieved residual curves, budgets consumed, probe adequacy status, and admissible next actions.
Refusal containment. Refused edges are quarantined from promotion-critical routing unless explicitly overridden by governance and upgraded by verified actions.
Ch06.C2 — Encode
Ch06.C2.a — Residual Curves Storage
Residual curves are stored as evidence and are recomputable; storage must not replace verification.
Canonical numeric encoding. Residual values are stored as normalized decimal strings with fixed precision sufficient for exact recomputation comparison under the same verifier semantics.
κ indexing. Curves are stored with explicit κ ladder identifiers and with probe bundle hashes that specify how probes were generated.
Curve provenance. Curves reference the WordNF hash and the gate stack id under which they were computed.
Recompute rule. Verifiers recompute curves from WordNF, pinned methods, and probe rules; stored curves are checked for equality to recomputed values and serve as audit evidence.
Ch06.C2.b — Probe Bundles
Probe bundles bind the probe family to the witness.
Bundle identity. Probe bundle hash commits to probe generation rules, adequacy metadata, and any pinned seeds.
Boundary coverage flags. For high-risk scopes, probe bundles declare inclusion of boundary/adversarial probes.
Scope binding. Probe bundles are scope-indexed; using a probe bundle outside its scope is illegal.
Adequacy links. Probe bundles link to adequacy certificates or to refusal evidence showing inadequacy.
Ch06.C2.c — Confidence Tags
Confidence tags are deterministic fields used for routing and governance.
Confidence level. Exact/certified/near/heuristic/refused.
Promotion eligibility. Whether the edge is eligible for promotion under current policy; if not, list missing obligations.
Risk class. Low/medium/high, derived from scope and from whether the edge is tunnel-derived or bridge-derived.
Context coverage map. A map from context hashes to receipt ids, indicating where the edge is certified.
Ch06.C2.d — Regression Tags
Regression tags bind edges and nodes to regression and stress requirements.
Suite identifiers. Regression suite ids and stress suite ids required for the edge type and risk class.
Trigger conditions. When suites must run (promotion, migration, bridge install, chart activation).
Result pointers. Pointers to result witness packs; missing results block promotion.
Bisect lineage. Pointers enabling localization of regression failures by hash ancestry and scope.
Ch06.C3 — Prove
Ch06.C3.a — Stability of Metro Under Refinement
Metro stability ensures that refinement and atlas expansion do not corrupt existing truths.
Monotone evidence strengthening. Adding higher κ receipts or tighter corridors strengthens evidence; it does not invalidate prior promotions unless drift is detected.
Isolation of new evidence. New charts and edges are initially candidates; they become promotable only with new receipts. They cannot retroactively alter existing node/edge identities.
Regression enforcement. When new evidence changes routing defaults or activates accel charts, regression suites ensure that previously certified equivalences remain certified under their pinned contexts.
Drift detection. If refinement produces inconsistent residual behavior under fixed ConvFP and method ids, drift alarms are raised and quarantines prevent contamination.
Ch06.C3.b — Invariant Preservation
Invariant preservation is enforced across nodes and edges.
Node invariants. Node invariant schemas constrain admissibility and equivalence semantics; derived nodes must preserve schema constraints under their derivations.
Edge invariants. Edge legality predicates enforce invariant preservation for transports, reductions, collapses, and bridges.
Atlas invariants. Atlas coherence ensures that different charts preserve the invariant meaning and are mutually transport-equivalent under tri-gate obligations.
Bridge invariants. Bridges preserve meaning across conventions/policies only when witnessed; otherwise they remain candidates or are refused.
Ch06.C3.c — Soundness Under Tolerance
Soundness under tolerance formalizes that approximate proofs remain safe and scope-limited.
Corridor-indexed truth. Equivalence claims are indexed by corridor. The metro records which corridors certify which claims, preventing accidental reuse under incompatible tolerance regimes.
Probe adequacy. Soundness requires adequate probes; insufficient probes cannot be compensated by small residuals.
Representability control. Projection residual gates ensure that acceptance is not an artifact of representation or rounding.
No tolerance laundering. Widening corridor gates changes semantic regimes for corridor-bounded equivalence and requires explicit new receipts and, when required, explicit corridor-bridge artifacts.
Ch06.C3.d — Plateau Semantics
Plateau semantics governs when routes cannot be certified under current methods and constraints.
Plateau detection. A residual curve plateaus above gates under κ refinement and admissible repairs.
Interpretation. Plateau can indicate non-identifiability, holonomy obstruction, or semantic conflict. Classification relies on ConvFP checks, loop residual dominance, and probe adequacy status.
Action. Plateau forbids promotion. The system either escalates (carrier/probes), tunnels (new chart discovery), invokes obstruction compilation (Tier-0), invokes portal mechanisms, or refuses with complete evidence.
Containment. Plateau outcomes are stored as refusal edges and are quarantined from promotion-critical routing unless upgraded.
Ch06.C4 — Extract
Ch06.C4.a — Risk-Aware Routes
Risk-aware routing selects routes that meet safety requirements under policy.
Risk constraints. Exclude quarantined edges, exclude heuristic-only evidence for promotion-critical tasks, and require stress/regression coverage for high-risk edges.
Confidence constraints. Prefer certified edges; allow near edges only when the goal is diagnostic or candidate generation.
Bridge constraints. Require explicit bridges for cross-convention or cross-policy paths; absence yields refusal.
Output. Provide an admissible path plus the required replay bundle pointers; if none exists, provide a minimal blocker set.
Ch06.C4.b — Audit Extracts
Audit extracts produce proof slices for targeted verification.
Edge audit extract. Receipt, pins expansion, witnesses, WordNF segments, and recomputation steps.
Node audit extract. Atlas extraction plus closure witnesses and promotion receipts.
Change audit extract. Migration and bridge audit chains plus regression results.
Refusal audit extract. Plateau/drift evidence packs plus admissible next actions.
Ch06.C4.c — Regression Extracts
Regression extracts provide evidence that promoted structures remain stable.
Suite expansion. Extract regression suite definitions and associated witness obligations.
Result extraction. Extract regression result packs with pass/fail evidence.
Bisect links. Extract lineage information enabling localization of failures.
Quarantine enforcement. Extract quarantines applied due to regression failures and the remediation obligations required to clear them.
Ch06.C4.d — Stress Extracts
Stress extracts provide adversarial validation evidence.
Stress suite definitions. Extract adversarial probe families and scope/risk bindings.
Result packs. Extract stress test witness packs.
Promotion gates. Extract which promotions are gated on stress pass.
Failure remediation. Extract required changes (probe expansion, convention updates, method changes, portal creation) when stress fails.
D. Fractal
Ch06.D1 — Spec
Ch06.D1.a — Atlas as Multi-Chart Object
An atlas is a structured object controlling evaluation, verification, and evolution of an invariant.
Atlas components. Charts, chart roots, chart activation states, transport edges between charts, and closure obligations.
Atlas semantics. The atlas defines the legal ways to evaluate the invariant and the legal ways to assert coherence among evaluations.
Atlas governance. Atlas changes (adding charts, activating accel charts, deprecating charts) are governed by policy and must preserve witness obligations.
Atlas as compilation target. The atlas is the compilation artifact into which discovery and acceleration syntheses deposit new charts, and from which route planning selects evaluators.
Ch06.D1.b — 4-Root Minimality
The 4-root atlas requirement is minimality for semantic robustness and extraction.
Root roles.
Square: discrete/lattice/constructive definitions and canonical addressing.
Flower: spectral/phase/transform representations and commutation via transform identities.
Cloud: analytic/measure/semigroup representations supporting uncertainty control and kernel methods.
Fractal: multiscale/renormalization/holonomy-sensitive representations supporting loop detection and stability under refinement.
Minimality criterion. A node lacks semantic robustness if it cannot be represented in one of the roots; such missing root indicates either incomplete development (needs new charts) or intrinsic portal requirements.
Root interplay. Root diversity is not aesthetic. It is an operational guarantee that no single chart family’s blind spots dominate the meaning claim.
Promotion rule. Promotion requires root coverage unless the node is explicitly scoped to a sub-root regime, in which case the promotion receipt must declare that scope restriction as a semantic limitation and block cross-root reuse.
Ch06.D1.c — Atlas Evolution
Atlas evolution is permitted only through explicit, witnessed operations.
Chart addition. New charts enter as candidate charts with certified evaluation receipts and required transport witness plans.
Chart coherence. A chart becomes promotable only when linked by transport UCWs to the atlas coherence graph and when closure obligations remain satisfied.
Chart activation. Activation of accel charts requires transport witnesses linking accel to baseline, and requires regression/stress obligations when policy demands.
Root completion. Atlas evolution may be driven by root completion obligations: missing roots trigger chart discovery jobs or portal escalation.
Ch06.D1.d — Atlas Compaction
Atlas compaction reduces storage while preserving replay and auditability.
Deduplication by identity. Charts and witnesses are deduplicated by hash identity; no deduplication by numeric similarity.
Evidence compaction. Replace bulky traces with merkle-rooted witness packs and minimal replay bundles.
Lifecycle compaction. Retain promoted and refused evidence; allow pruning of intermediate candidate attempts when refusal summaries preserve necessary evidence.
Compaction receipts. Compaction produces receipts proving that promoted verification outcomes remain replay-identical after compaction.
Ch06.D2 — Encode
Ch06.D2.a — Atlas Registry
The atlas registry is a deterministic index over atlas objects.
Registry entries. Each entry maps node_id to atlas_id and provides pointers to chart ids by root and by status.
Context coverage indices. Registry tracks which charts are certified under which corridor/policy contexts.
Closure indices. Registry stores pointers to closure witnesses: UCWs, loop certificates, and any interior coherence certificates required by domain.
Schema/version indices. Registry records schema versions required to interpret atlas records and chart records.
Ch06.D2.b — Chart Activation
Chart activation changes the route planner’s choices and must be explicit.
Activation state. Charts have states: inactive, candidate, certified, promoted, active, deprecated, quarantined.
Activation conditions. Activation requires:
certified evaluation receipt for the chart under target corridor,
transport UCW linking chart to baseline chart(s),
policy compliance for method ids and budgets,
stress/regression pass when required by risk class.
Activation logging. Activation is recorded as a governance event with pointers to required receipts and witnesses; activation does not alter chart identity.
Activation rollback. If regression fails after activation, chart is quarantined and deactivated; rollback is recorded and does not delete evidence.
Ch06.D2.c — Chart Deactivation
Deactivation prevents unsafe or outdated charts from being selected while preserving auditability.
Deactivation reasons. Drift alarms, failed stress/regression, policy changes, supersession by better charts.
Deactivation records. Deactivation is recorded with reason codes and pointers to evidence packs.
Fallback guarantees. Deactivation must preserve availability of at least one certified chart per root for promoted nodes; otherwise the node’s promotion status is downgraded or quarantined.
Non-destructive deactivation. Deactivated charts remain replayable for audit; they are not deleted.
Ch06.D2.d — Upgrade Markers
Upgrade markers record chart and atlas evolution in a manner compatible with receipts.
Upgrade events. Chart addition, chart activation, chart deprecation, atlas closure completion, portal escalation.
Upgrade receipts. Upgrades that affect promoted status must be accompanied by receipts binding the new witness obligations.
Migration linkage. Schema changes and convention changes are linked by migration edges and require re-verification of affected atlas closure obligations.
Current pointers. Governance pointers may designate preferred charts per root and per context; pointers are auditable and do not alter identities.
Ch06.D3 — Prove
Ch06.D3.a — Atlas Coherence
Atlas coherence is the property that all charts in the atlas represent the same invariant under witnessed transports and closure obligations.
Connectivity. The chart graph must be connected by transport edges whose UCWs are promotable under the node’s declared contexts.
Commutation closure. Required commutation squares among charts and between charts and normalization/projector gates must be witnessed and must pass corridor gates.
Loop closure. The atlas must include loop closure witnesses sufficient to prevent path-dependent semantics: cycles in the chart graph must have spin residuals bounded by (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}).
Representability closure. Each chart must satisfy representability gates and must commute with projection/canonicalization obligations required by the node schema.
Failure of atlas coherence yields refusal or quarantine; it cannot be masked by selecting a single chart and ignoring incoherence.
Ch06.D3.b — Transport Witnesses Among Charts
Transport witnesses are the semantic glue of the atlas.
Witness form. Each transport edge between charts includes a WordNF hash and a UCW hash, binding the exact route and the tri-gate residual curves.
Scope binding. Transport witnesses bind ConvBundleFP scope; different convention regimes require explicit bridge edges and additional witnesses.
Upgrade discipline. Adding a chart requires adding transport witnesses to integrate it into coherence. Without such witnesses the chart remains isolated and cannot be active for promoted semantics.
Refusal discipline. Failed transport witnesses produce refusal artifacts classifying failure and recording best-achieved residual evidence and admissible repair actions.
Ch06.D3.c — Closure Criteria
Closure criteria define when a node’s atlas is sufficient for promotion.
Root coverage. At least one certified chart per root, unless promotion is explicitly scoped to fewer roots with declared semantic limitations.
Coherence. Connectivity and commutation/loop/representability closure as above.
Context coverage. Promotion receipts specify which contexts are covered; coverage is not assumed universal. A node can be promoted under one policy/corridor and only certified or candidate under others.
Robustness gates. For high-risk nodes or nodes with portal sensitivity, closure includes stress and regression gates required by policy.
Ch06.D3.d — Deprecation Correctness
Deprecation is safe only if it preserves replay and does not break promoted obligations.
Deprecation preserves evidence. Deprecated charts and witnesses remain replayable for audit.
Deprecation preserves fallback. Deprecation cannot remove the last certified chart required for a promoted node’s root coverage; otherwise node promotion is downgraded or quarantined.
Deprecation is scoped. Deprecation may apply under specific policies or corridors; it cannot silently remove evidence in other contexts.
Deprecation triggers regression. Deprecation and activation events trigger regression suites; failure produces quarantine and remediation obligations.
Ch06.D4 — Extract
Ch06.D4.a — Fastest Safe Evaluator
Fastest safe evaluator selection is a deterministic query over atlas charts.
Safety constraints. Require chart is active, certified under the target corridor/policy, and linked to baseline by transport witnesses.
Scope constraints. Require ConvBundleFP coverage for all invoked operators and compatibility with node scope.
Cost ranking. Rank admissible charts by cost metadata (latency, complexity) without altering semantic obligations.
Output. Return selected chart id, its certified evaluation receipt id, and the transport witness chain to baseline.
Ch06.D4.b — Fallback Safe Evaluator
Fallback selection ensures robustness when preferred charts are unavailable.
Root fallback. Provide at least one fallback chart per root for promoted nodes.
Policy fallback. When policy forbids a chart, fall back to an admissible chart without weakening gates or scope.
Stability fallback. If drift alarms or regressions quarantine a chart, fall back to a non-quarantined certified chart.
Output. Return fallback charts and the proof obligations that justify their safety.
Ch06.D4.c — Atlas Diff
Atlas diffs provide audit of atlas evolution.
Structural diff. Added/removed charts, changed activation states, changed preferred chart pointers.
Evidence diff. Added/removed transport witnesses, changed closure obligations, changed regression/stress coverage.
Context diff. Changes in corridor/policy coverage and changes in ConvBundleFP scope coverage.
Decision diff. Governance reasons for changes, linked to receipts and witness packs.
Ch06.D4.d — Atlas Provenance
Atlas provenance reconstructs the full lineage of atlas development.
Chart provenance. For each chart, list discovery origin (baseline construction, accel synthesis, tunnel-derived), certified receipts, and integration witnesses.
Transport provenance. For each transport edge, list WordNF, UCW, corridor/policy contexts, and upgrade history.
Governance provenance. Activation/deactivation/deprecation events and their triggers (regression failures, drift alarms, policy changes).
Export provenance. Proof slices and export packs that package the atlas for cross-site verification, with visibility mode constraints.
Ch06 — Metro Graph: Nodes, Edges, and the 4-Root Atlas
Ch07 — Transport Language and Legality Predicates
A. Square
Ch07.A1 — Spec
Ch07.A1.a — Transport Signatures
A transport is a typed, scope-bound, replay-addressable morphism between lenses. It is the primitive unit of representation change and the primitive unit of semantic movement in the metro. A transport is not identified by prose; it is identified by a signature and a canonical route description.
Let (L_i=(D_i,\mathcal A_i,\mathcal I_i)) and (L_j=(D_j,\mathcal A_j,\mathcal I_j)) be lenses. A transport signature is a tuple[\Sigma(T);:=;(\mathrm{op_id},;L_i,;L_j,;\Theta,;\mathrm{Dom},;\mathrm{Cod},;\mathcal S,;\mathcal O_T,;\mathcal W_T),]with the following components:
Operator identity (\mathrm{op_id}). A stable identifier binding the intended semantic action class (encode, decode, normalize, evaluate, project, reduce, snap, tunnel, bridge, migrate) and its verifier interpretation.
Domain and codomain lenses. Explicit lens ids for (L_i) and (L_j). Transport is undefined without lenses.
Parameter schema (\Theta). A finite typed schema for parameters (\theta\in\Theta) (fields, types, admissible ranges, defaults when semantic-neutral, and explicit missing-field behavior).
Domain/codomain typing (\mathrm{Dom},\mathrm{Cod}). Declares the carrier types, admissibility predicates, and output schemas. Transport is required to be total on the declared admissible input region, or to refuse deterministically outside it.
Scope (\mathcal S). Declares operator-family scope (ConvBundleFP coverage requirement), corridor scope (gates and κ schedules required), and policy scope (allowed methods, budgets, and safety constraints).
Operational obligations (\mathcal O_T). Declares which invariants and observables must be preserved or transformed, and how residuals are computed.
Witness obligations (\mathcal W_T). Declares the set of required witness types for promotion and reuse: preservation witnesses, commutation witnesses, loop closure witnesses, representability witnesses, auxiliary certificates (intervals, tail bounds, rounding ids, regression/stress suites where mandated by policy).
A transport instance is a binding of a signature to parameters and context:[T[\theta;,C,P,\mathrm{Conv}];:;D_{i,\mathrm{adm}} \to D_j,]where (C) is a corridor, (P) is a policy, and (\mathrm{Conv}) denotes a ConvBundleFP covering the operator scope. Context is not implicit; it is part of legality.
Ch07.A1.b — Typing Rules
Typing in the transport language is mandatory and first-class. Every transport instance must satisfy a typed judgment of the form[\Gamma \vdash T[\theta] : L_i \to L_j,]where (\Gamma) is the context containing pinned lens ids, schema versions, ConvBundleFP scope coverage, policy admissibility, and corridor obligations.
The typing rules are:
Lens typing. (L_i) and (L_j) are known lenses with valid schema versions and stable identities.
Carrier typing. Input objects (x) satisfy (x\in D_i) and (x\in D_{i,\mathrm{adm}}) under (\mathcal I_i); output objects (y:=T(x)) satisfy (y\in D_j) and (y\in D_{j,\mathrm{adm}}) under (\mathcal I_j).
Parameter typing. (\theta) satisfies the parameter schema (\Theta) and its admissible ranges. Semantic parameters belong to ConvFP scope; numeric parameters belong to corridor/policy scope. Any parameter that can affect meaning or canonicalization must be pinned; otherwise promotion is forbidden.
Scope typing. The operator families invoked by (T) are within the ConvBundleFP scope; the approximation regime invoked by (T) is within the corridor scope; the computational methods invoked by (T) are within the policy scope.
Composition typing. For transports (T_1:L_a\to L_b) and (T_2:L_b\to L_c), composition (T_2\circ T_1) is well-typed only when the codomain of (T_1) matches the domain of (T_2) and the context obligations unify or are bridged explicitly.
Typing failure is not a runtime exception; it is a refusal object with explicit classification.
Ch07.A1.c — Pre/Post Conditions
Each transport signature induces preconditions and postconditions interpreted by the verifier.
Let (T[\theta]) be a transport instance. Define:
Precondition (\mathrm{Pre}_T(x)). A conjunction of:
(x\in D_{i,\mathrm{adm}}) (admissibility under lens schema),
parameter validity (\theta\in\Theta_{\mathrm{adm}}),
scope coverage: ConvBundleFP covers all operators invoked,
policy admissibility: all required methods and budgets are permitted,
corridor admissibility: declared gates and κ schedules can be executed on the carrier and probes.
Postcondition (\mathrm{Post}_T(x,y)). A conjunction of:
(y=T(x)\in D_{j,\mathrm{adm}}),
invariant preservation obligations satisfied (exact or corridor-bounded),
representability obligations satisfied: (y\approx_{L_j}\Pi_{h}(y)) with projection residual gated,
any declared observable coherence obligations satisfied,
replay obligations satisfied: route identity (WordNF) and witness plan exist.
Composition constraint. For composable transports, postconditions must imply downstream preconditions:[\mathrm{Post}{T_1}(x,y);\Longrightarrow;\mathrm{Pre}{T_2}(y),]otherwise the composition is ill-typed and must refuse.
Determinism constraint. Any postcondition dependent on nondeterministic choices is invalid for promotion unless nondeterminism is eliminated or pinned by explicit replay fields.
Pre/post conditions are not optional documentation; they are part of legality predicate evaluation and are audited.
Ch07.A1.d — Invariant Obligations
Invariant obligations bind transports to meaning preservation and to measurable witness requirements.
Predicate preservation. For each predicate (P\in\mathcal P_i) required to be preserved (or to be mapped to a predicate (P'\in\mathcal P_j)), transport must satisfy:[P(x)=\mathrm{true};\Longrightarrow;P'(T(x))=\mathrm{true}.]If preservation is approximate, it is reduced to residual bounds under corridor gates.
Equivalence respect. If (x\approx_{L_i}x'), then (T(x)\approx_{L_j}T(x')) under the codomain schema, with explicit witness obligations when corridor-bounded.
Observable coherence. For each observable (o\in\mathcal O_i) with codomain observable (o'\in\mathcal O_j), transport declares a coherence map (\tau_o) and residual:[o'(T(x));\approx;\tau_o(o(x),\theta),]with explicit residual functionals and gates when approximate.
Commutation obligations. When a transport is used as part of an equivalence claim, it must participate in commutation squares; those squares must be witnessed by UCWs under the corridor κ ladder.
Holonomy obligations. When the transport participates in loops (cycle closures in atlas coherence or route equivalence), loop closure must be witnessed; persistent holonomy triggers obstruction/portal mechanisms rather than silent acceptance.
Representability obligations. Transports must not break canonicalization: (T(x)) must be stable under the codomain projector/canonicalizer with projection residual gated.
Ch07.A2 — Encode
Ch07.A2.a — Operator IDs
Operator IDs identify transport semantics and verifier interpretation.
Stable operator identity. Each operator has an id (\mathrm{op_id}) bound to:
semantic action class,
required input/output typing,
required witness types,
admissible parameter schema version,
admissible scope declarations.
Operator family taxonomy. Operator ids are partitioned into families: enc, dec, nf, proj, eval, trans, reduce, collapse, snap, tunnel, bridge, migrate.
Operator semantics pinning. If an operator’s semantics changes, either the operator id changes or the operator id is versioned and the version is pinned into context; silent semantic updates are forbidden.
Operator catalog invariants. The operator catalog is schema-versioned and hash-addressed; verifiers refuse to interpret unknown operator ids.
Ch07.A2.b — Parameter Schemas
Parameter schemas are canonical records that bind semantic and numeric choices deterministically.
Typed fields. Each field has type and admissible range; invalid parameters yield refusal class PARAM_INVALID.
Semantic partitioning. Parameters affecting semantics (branches, normalizations, basis choices) are required to be part of ConvBundleFP scope and are pinned by ConvFP records. Numeric parameters (κ, truncation, tail bounds) are pinned by corridor/policy.
Canonical parameter encoding. Parameter dictionaries are canonicalized by sorted keys, stable numeric string format, and explicit omission rules; canonicalization is part of WordNF identity for transport instances.
Parameter provenance. Default parameters are permitted only when they are semantically neutral and explicitly declared by schema; implicit runtime defaults are forbidden.
Ch07.A2.c — Version IDs
Version ids pin interpretation across time.
Schema version ids. Each record type (transport signature, WordNF, witness, corridor, policy, ConvFP) has a schema version hash.
Verifier version id. Verifier protocol version is pinned and included in receipts; promotion is relative to a verifier.
Operator version id. Operator catalogs and gate stacks are versioned; route interpretation requires operator version.
Migration semantics. Version changes are represented by migration edges with explicit witness obligations; no overwriting.
Ch07.A2.d — Convention Binds
Transport semantics must bind conventions explicitly.
ConvBundleFP binding. Every transport instance includes ConvBundleFP hash covering all operator families invoked.
Scope coverage check. Verifiers ensure that ConvBundleFP scope includes the operators used; uncovered operators yield refusal class CONV_SCOPE_GAP.
Convention compatibility. When composing transports across convention regimes, explicit convention bridges are required; otherwise composition is illegal.
Convention-sensitive canonicalization. Any canonicalization step dependent on conventions must be covered by ConvFP and included in witness obligations via representability residual gates.
Ch07.A3 — Prove
Ch07.A3.a — Legality Predicate Library
Legality is evaluated by a deterministic library of predicates. A legality decision is a sum-type outcome:[\mathrm{Legal}(T[\theta],x;\Gamma)\in{\mathrm{OK},\mathrm{NEAR},\mathrm{FAIL}},]where NEAR is admissible only as a non-promotable diagnostic state with explicit next-action obligations.
The predicate library contains the following canonical predicates, each returning pass/fail plus evidence:
Type predicate (\mathsf{Ty}). Checks lens existence, carrier typing, admissibility membership, and parameter typing.
Scope predicate (\mathsf{Scope}). Checks ConvBundleFP coverage, corridor scope applicability, and policy admissibility.
Convention predicate (\mathsf{Conv}). Checks ConvFP compatibility across composed steps; requires explicit convention bridge if mismatch on overlapping scope.
Policy predicate (\mathsf{Pol}). Checks method admissibility, budget admissibility, and prohibited operations.
Corridor predicate (\mathsf{Corr}). Checks that required κ ladder exists and that gate stacks can be executed; binds required residual functionals and probe regimes.
Preservation predicate (\mathsf{Pres}). Checks invariant predicates and observable coherence obligations (exact or corridor-bounded).
Representability predicate (\mathsf{Rep}). Checks projection/canonicalization stability and projection residual gates.
Commutation predicate (\mathsf{Comm}). If transport participates in an equivalence claim, verifies UCW commutation obligations.
Holonomy predicate (\mathsf{Hol}). If transport participates in loops, verifies loop closure obligations and spin residual gates.
Replay predicate (\mathsf{Replay}). Checks that a WordNF route exists, is canonical, and pins all required parameters and method ids; checks deterministic replay fields.
Budget predicate (\mathsf{Bud}). Verifies that the transport and its verification obligations terminate within declared budgets; otherwise yields refusal with exhaustion evidence.
The combined legality decision is computed by a deterministic gate stack ordering:[\mathsf{Ty}\rightarrow \mathsf{Scope}\rightarrow \mathsf{Conv}\rightarrow \mathsf{Pol}\rightarrow \mathsf{Corr}\rightarrow \mathsf{Pres}\rightarrow \mathsf{Rep}\rightarrow (\mathsf{Comm},\mathsf{Hol})\rightarrow \mathsf{Replay}\rightarrow \mathsf{Bud},]with early refusal on hard failures.
Ch07.A3.b — Compositional Legality
Compositional legality states conditions under which legal transports compose into a legal route.
Let (T_1:L_a\to L_b) and (T_2:L_b\to L_c) be transport instances. Define the composed transport (T:=T_2\circ T_1). Compositional legality requires:
Type composability. (\mathrm{Cod}(T_1)=\mathrm{Dom}(T_2)) under carrier typing and admissibility constraints.
Context composability. ConvBundleFP, corridor, and policy contexts unify or are bridged explicitly. Unification requires identical hashes; bridging requires promoted bridge artifacts with their own receipts and witness obligations.
Obligation closure. The combined obligation set (\mathcal W_T) is the union of obligations of (T_1,T_2) plus any additional obligations arising from interaction (commutation squares between internal gates, loop closures created by composition). These additional obligations must be explicitly enumerated by the route compiler.
Witness composability. Witnesses for composed transport are formed from witness sets of components plus additional UCWs required to certify commutation or loop closure at the composition boundary.
Replay composability. The composed WordNF is the canonical concatenation and normalization of segment streams, respecting scope; no segment reorderings are permitted without explicit commutation witnesses valid under the bound convention scope.
If any composability condition fails, the route is ill-typed and yields a refusal artifact identifying the minimal failing predicate.
Ch07.A3.c — Partial Legality
Partial legality is a structured non-promotable outcome indicating that legality may be achievable via admissible repair actions.
Definition. A transport instance is NEAR-legal when:
hard predicates (\mathsf{Ty},\mathsf{Scope},\mathsf{Conv},\mathsf{Pol}) pass,
but corridor-bound predicates (\mathsf{Corr},\mathsf{Pres},\mathsf{Rep},\mathsf{Comm},\mathsf{Hol}) fail only by quantitative gate violations, and
admissible repair actions exist under policy and corridor evolution rules.
Repair obligations. A NEAR outcome must include:
the failing gate(s),
best-achieved residual curves,
remaining budgets,
admissible next actions (κ escalation, probe expansion, Snap, tunnel, bridge scheduling) with explicit preconditions.
Non-promotability. NEAR outcomes cannot be consumed as semantic evidence in promoted receipts. They may be consumed only as planning evidence tagged as non-certified.
Termination into refusal. If admissible repair actions are attempted and budgets exhaust without success, the result must be a refusal with complete evidence, not an infinite NEAR state.
Ch07.A3.d — Counterexample Objects
When legality fails, the system produces counterexample objects that witness the failure and enable localization.
A counterexample object is a tuple[\mathsf{CEx} := (\mathrm{pred_id},;\mathrm{ctx},;\mathrm{witness},;\mathrm{minimizer},;\mathrm{next_actions}),]where:
Predicate id. The minimal failing predicate identifier (e.g., CONV_SCOPE_GAP, POLICY_FORBIDS_TUNNEL, FACE_GATE_FAIL).
Context. Pinned hashes: ConvBundleFP, corridor, policy, verifier, schema versions, and route WordNF hash.
Witness. Minimal data that demonstrates failure:
a specific probe (p) and κ level where residual exceeds gate,
a specific parameter value outside admissible range,
a specific operator invocation outside ConvFP scope,
a specific budget exhaustion certificate.
Minimizer. A deterministic minimization record describing how the failure was localized (e.g., smallest subword, smallest κ, smallest probe subset) subject to legality.
Next actions. A finite list of admissible remediation steps, each with explicit preconditions and expected obligations.
Counterexample objects are hash-addressed and stored as refusal evidence; they are used by route auditors and by automated repair planners.
Ch07.A4 — Extract
Ch07.A4.a — Legality Traces
A legality trace is an extraction artifact recording the evaluation of legality predicates for a transport or route.
Trace structure. A trace is an ordered list of predicate evaluations with:
predicate id,
pass/fail outcome,
evidence summary,
references to witness objects or counterexample objects.
Scope binding. Trace binds to context hashes; traces are meaningful only under those hashes.
Segment linkage. Trace references WordNF segments responsible for obligations (gate runs, tunnel events, projection steps).
Audit utility. Traces enable reconstruction of why a route was accepted, rejected, or classified as NEAR.
Ch07.A4.b — Minimal Failing Predicate
Minimal failing predicate extraction produces the most informative failure explanation.
Predicate minimization. Among failing predicates, select the earliest failing hard predicate; among soft failures, select the dominant gate failure under defect functional ordering.
Witness minimization. Produce the smallest counterexample witness (smallest κ, smallest probe set, smallest subword) that still proves failure.
Classification. Classify failure into semantic mismatch (ConvFP), governance mismatch (policy), numeric insufficiency (corridor), or non-identifiability (probe inadequacy), using cross-κ stability checks and scope checks.
Output. Return the minimal failing predicate id, counterexample object id, and remediation obligations list.
Ch07.A4.c — Repair Suggestions
Repair suggestions are extracted from NEAR outcomes and refusals.
κ escalation suggestions. Increase κ along declared ladder; identify required method changes and expected impact on residuals.
Probe expansion suggestions. Add probes required for adequacy; include adversarial boundary probes for high-risk scopes.
Snap suggestions. Tighten corridor or adjust admissible method choices under defect reduction; include required logging and evidence obligations.
Tunnel suggestions. If permitted, schedule tunnel operations (chart discovery, basis change, transform change) with explicit budgets and required transport witnesses back to baseline.
Repair suggestions are admissible actions only; forbidden actions are excluded and replaced by policy-bridge requirements if necessary.
Ch07.A4.d — Proof Obligations List
For any transport or route, extract the explicit proof obligations required for promotion.
Typing obligations. Carrier typing, admissibility membership, parameter validity.
Scope obligations. ConvBundleFP coverage, corridor/policy applicability, method admissibility.
Witness obligations. Required UCWs, evaluation receipts, bridge packs, projection stability checks.
Gate obligations. Required gate stacks, κ ladders, probe regimes, auxiliary regression/stress suites.
Replay obligations. WordNF existence and segment completeness, deterministic encoding, environment pins if required.
This obligations list is the canonical “what must be proven” artifact used by schedulers and by proof slicing.
B. Flower
Ch07.B1 — Spec
Ch07.B1.a — Transport Composition
Transport composition defines the operational language of routes.
Sequential composition. If (T_1:L_a\to L_b) and (T_2:L_b\to L_c), then (T_2\circ T_1:L_a\to L_c), with obligations as specified by compositional legality.
Parallel alternatives. Multiple routes may exist between the same endpoints; semantic equivalence is not assumed, but may be certified by commutation witnesses.
Conditional composition. Certain transports are admissible only on subsets of the admissible region; conditional branches must be explicit and deterministic, and must be represented in WordNF by explicit branch predicates.
Scope composition. Composition collects operator scopes; the ConvBundleFP must cover the union scope.
Ch07.B1.b — Normal Forms
Normal forms remove ambiguity from route identity.
WordNF as route identity. Every route is compiled into a WordNF that uniquely identifies its segment stream.
Rewrite normalization. When rewrite rules (commutations, identity eliminations) are admissible, they are applied deterministically according to a fixed precedence order, and only when scope compatibility holds.
Segment preservation. Corridor-changing actions (Snap, tunnel) are never erased by normalization; they must remain as segments to preserve auditability.
Scope-aware normalization. Normalization never crosses convention boundaries or policy boundaries unless explicit bridge segments are present.
Ch07.B1.c — Route Equivalence
Route equivalence is not semantic equivalence.
Syntactic route equivalence. Two routes are route-equivalent iff their WordNF hashes are identical.
Semantic route equivalence. Two distinct routes are semantically equivalent only when a commutation witness certifies equality of induced semantics under the tri-gate logic.
Scope dependence. Semantic equivalence is indexed by ConvBundleFP, corridor, and policy; changing context changes the claim.
Loop dependence. If route equivalence participates in cycles, loop closure witnesses are required to prevent path-dependent semantics.
Ch07.B1.d — Hub Routing Theorem
Hub routing is a principled method for constructing routes in a large metro graph.
Hub definition. A hub lens (H) is a lens with high connectivity and high witness coverage: many promoted transports connect to (H), and commutation/holonomy witnesses exist for common squares and loops.
Hub routing theorem (operational statement). For a broad class of endpoints (L_s,L_t) within a domain, if there exists at least one admissible path (L_s\to L_t), then there exists an admissible path that factors through a hub (H) and is no less certifiable than the original, provided:
the hub transports are promotable under the same context,
commutation witnesses exist for required re-associations introduced by factoring,
the hub factorization does not introduce new convention or policy scope conflicts.
Witnessed factorization. The theorem is not abstract; it is enforced by the planner: factoring is accepted only when witness obligations for the factorized path are satisfiable under corridor and policy budgets.
Failure localization. When hub routing fails, it localizes to missing hub transport witnesses or scope incompatibility; it does not degrade into heuristic guessing.
Ch07.B2 — Encode
Ch07.B2.a — WordNF Grammar
WordNF is the canonical encoding of a route as a segment stream.
Core fields. src_lens_id, dst_lens_id, conv_bundle_hash, policy_hash, corridor_hash, kappa_ladder_id, probe_regime_id.
Segment stream. An ordered list of segments, each typed:
GateRun segments: evaluation, projection, commutation gate runs, holonomy gate runs, normalization steps,
TunnelEvent segments: representation-switch events with pre/post corridor hashes, defect measures, replay fields,
BridgeEvent segments: explicit convention/policy bridge applications,
RefineEvent segments: κ escalations and probe expansions.
Parameter binding. Each segment includes canonical parameter encodings and method/bound/rounding ids.
Normalization marker. WordNF includes an explicit normalization id indicating which WordNF normalization rules were applied.
Ch07.B2.b — Gate Stack Schema
Gate stacks define predicate ordering and required outputs.
Stack identity. A gate stack id binds the ordered list of predicates and residual functionals.
