Stack parameters. Stack parameters bind ε gates, κ ladder, and probe regime; they are pinned by corridor hash.
Stack outputs. Stack outputs include residual curves (κ-indexed) and summary maxima; they must be recomputable.
Stack scope. Gate stacks are carrier-typed and scope-bound; applying an incompatible stack yields refusal.
Ch07.B2.c — Segment Stream Schema
Segment stream schema is the normative log format for replay and audit.
Deterministic ordering. Segment order is the order of execution; reordering is forbidden unless a commutation witness justifies it and the normalization preserves the justified order.
Hash chaining. Segments may include chain hashes to provide tamper-evident lineage; chain hashes are non-semantic but required for audit integrity where mandated.
Evidence pointers. Segments may reference witness object ids; however, witnesses must be recomputable from pinned context.
Budget accounting. Segments include budget consumption fields where relevant (probes used, κ level used, candidates tried, tunnel events consumed).
Ch07.B2.d — Deterministic Ordering
Deterministic ordering rules apply to route compilation, witness set commitments, and canonicalization.
Operator order. Operator application order is determined by segment stream; normalization rules define deterministic rewrite precedence.
Set order. Pins and witnesses are committed by merkle roots computed over deterministically ordered lists.
Basis and address order. Any basis or address ordering used by transports or canonicalizers is pinned by ConvFP and is included in scope coverage obligations.
Tie-breaking. All ties are broken deterministically by pinned rules; any reliance on ambient iteration order is forbidden.
Ch07.B3 — Prove
Ch07.B3.a — Canonicalization Idempotence
Canonicalization idempotence is required for stable route normalization and for representability obligations.
Idempotence obligation. For canonicalizer (\mathrm{NF}) or projector (\Pi_h),[\mathrm{NF}(\mathrm{NF}(x))=\mathrm{NF}(x)\quad\text{or}\quad \mathrm{NF}(\mathrm{NF}(x))\approx \mathrm{NF}(x)]under corridor-bounded equivalence with explicit residual bounds.
Scope binding. Idempotence is evaluated under the bound ConvBundleFP and corridor; changing either changes the obligation.
Transport commutation. Where required by invariant schemas, canonicalization must commute with transports up to corridor gates; this is witnessed by commutation UCWs.
Refusal on ambiguity. If canonicalization is not deterministic due to under-specified ordering or tie-breaking, the system refuses promotion and requires ConvFP remediation.
Ch07.B3.b — Determinism Lemma
Determinism is required for promotion. Determinism is defined as: under pinned context hashes, verifier outcomes are invariant under replay.
Route determinism. The WordNF segment stream is a pure function of pinned inputs and deterministic planner rules.
Evaluation determinism. Certified evaluation uses deterministic methods, deterministic rounding cushions, and deterministic tail bounds; nondeterministic methods are forbidden for promotion.
Witness determinism. UCW residual curves are deterministically computed from WordNF, corridor, and probes; stored curves are checked against recomputation.
Decision determinism. Legality predicate outcomes are deterministic given the pinned context; any nondeterminism yields refusal or quarantine.
Ch07.B3.c — Replay Lemma
The replay lemma states that promoted transports are replay-verifiable from receipts.
Given a promoted edge or transport with receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}), the verifier can:
Expand pins and witness roots deterministically.
Validate context hashes (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier, schema hashes).
Reconstruct or interpret WordNF segment stream and recompute gate stack outputs.
Re-evaluate legality predicates and reproduce the acceptance decision.
Failure to replay implies the artifact is not promotable and must be downgraded or quarantined; replay is not optional.
Ch07.B3.d — Substitution Lemma
Substitution lemma governs replacing one transport or evaluator with another.
Typed substitution. A substitute (T') may replace (T) only if signatures match and scope obligations are satisfied.
Evidence substitution. A substitute evaluator chart may replace another only if a transport UCW certifies equivalence under the relevant corridor and if the chart activation is recorded and governed.
Context preservation. Substitution must preserve or strengthen corridor gates, not weaken them, unless a corridor change is explicitly declared and witnessed.
Auditability. Substitution changes must be visible in route identity (WordNF segments) or in atlas activation records referenced by receipts; silent substitution is forbidden.
Ch07.B4 — Extract
Ch07.B4.a — Route Builders
Route builders construct well-typed routes under constraints.
Build candidate transport sequences by signature matching and scope coverage.
Bind parameters under canonical schemas and compute WordNF.
Enumerate required witness obligations and job plans to satisfy them.
Emit admissible routes or refusal artifacts with minimal failing predicate localization.
Ch07.B4.b — Route Minimizers
Route minimizers reduce route cost while preserving legality.
Minimize number of edges and witness cost under fixed context.
Apply allowed commutation rewrites only when witnessed and scope-compatible.
Prefer hub factorization when it reduces witness obligations and preserves certifiability.
Produce minimized WordNF and updated proof obligations lists.
Ch07.B4.c — Route Auditors
Route auditors produce audit-grade explanations and evidence packs.
Expand receipts into pins and witnesses; validate hashes and schema versions.
Recompute legality predicates and gate stack outputs; compare to stored evidence.
Extract minimal failing predicate and counterexample objects when failures occur.
Emit audit packets suitable for independent replay verification.
Ch07.B4.d — Route Explainers
Route explainers translate route objects into structured explanation artifacts without altering semantics.
Provide a segment-by-segment account: which gates, which tunnels, which bridges, which κ steps.
Provide a predicate-by-predicate account: which legality predicates were satisfied and which were not.
Provide a witness-by-witness account: which UCWs certify commutation/holonomy/projection and under what context.
Provide a remediation account: if not promotable, list admissible next actions and required bridge/policy/corridor changes.
C. Cloud
Ch07.C1 — Spec
Ch07.C1.a — Approximate Transports
An approximate transport is a transport whose postcondition is corridor-bounded rather than exact. Approximate transports are admissible only when their approximation semantics is explicitly certified and when their use is confined by corridor gates and probe adequacy.
Approximate postcondition. Replace exact equalities by corridor-indexed equivalences (\approx_{C}) on declared observables and carriers.
Witness requirement. Approximate transports require certified enclosures and explicit residual functionals; they cannot rely on raw floating equality.
Scope requirement. Approximate semantics is limited to declared stability regions; outside those regions transport must refuse or require portal escalation.
Promotion requirement. Approximate transports are promotable only when residual curves satisfy gates across κ and when probe adequacy is certified.
Ch07.C1.b — Bounded Error Propagation
Approximate transports must provide bounded error propagation laws.
Error envelope. For each output observable or enclosure, provide a bound:[\mathrm{Err}{\mathrm{out}} \le \Phi(\mathrm{Err}{\mathrm{in}},\theta,\kappa),]where (\Phi) is declared and checkable.
Composition of bounds. When transports compose, propagation bounds compose according to declared rules; missing composition laws forbid promotion.
Monotone refinement. Under κ escalation, error envelopes must tighten or plateau below gates; non-monotone behavior triggers drift/instability classification.
Guard rules. When error propagation becomes ill-conditioned (e.g., near singularities), transports must declare admissibility restrictions or require carrier escalation.
Ch07.C1.c — Admissibility Regions
Admissibility regions constrain where approximate transports are meaningful.
Input admissibility. Declared (D_{\mathrm{adm}}) sets and stability regions.
Parameter admissibility. Parameter domains where numeric methods are stable and where branch/normalization semantics are defined.
Probe admissibility. Probe families must be defined and adequate within admissible regions; inadequate probes block promotion.
Boundary policy. Behavior at boundaries is explicit: refusal, one-sided limits, principal values, or explicit regularization schemes pinned by ConvFP and policy.
Ch07.C1.d — Uncertainty Tags
Uncertainty tags classify evidence quality for transports.
exact: canonical-form decidable.
certified: enclosure and UCW gates satisfied.
near: quantitative gate failures with admissible repairs remaining.
heuristic: candidate-level only; not promotable.
refused: budget exhausted or non-identifiable or semantic conflict; refusal evidence is authoritative.
Uncertainty tags are stored as part of witness and receipt metadata and govern safe routing.
Ch07.C2 — Encode
Ch07.C2.a — Interval Transforms
Interval transforms encode certified propagation of enclosures through transports.
Transform id. Each interval transform has an id and a proof obligation describing how it bounds output enclosures.
Parameter binding. Transform parameters are bound into WordNF and corridor context.
Composition rules. Each transform declares how it composes with others; composition must be deterministic and checkable.
Verifier recomputation. The verifier recomputes interval transforms from pinned method ids and checks enclosure validity.
Ch07.C2.b — Tail Bound Transforms
Tail bound transforms encode truncation error propagation.
Bound id and assumptions. Tail bound transforms are identified and scoped; assumptions are explicit and pinned.
Bound parameters. Truncation depth and envelope constants are bound into route segments.
Propagation. Tail bounds propagate through compositions with declared rules.
Auditability. Tail bound derivations are addressable artifacts; missing derivations block promotion.
Ch07.C2.c — Rounding Transforms
Rounding transforms encode rounding cushions and numerical backend semantics.
Rounding id. Identifies rounding model and cushion functional form.
Backend pins. Backend versions are pinned when they influence determinism or rounding semantics.
Projection coupling. Rounding and representability projectors must be compatible; incompatibility yields representability gate failure.
Policy constraints. Policies may forbid certain rounding models for high-risk scopes; encoding includes admissibility fields.
Ch07.C2.d — Corridor Transforms
Corridor transforms encode legal changes in corridor regime during repair.
Tightening transforms. Reduce ε gates or strengthen κ ladder; creates new corridor identity.
Widening transforms. Create new corridor regime; forbidden as silent repair in promotable evidence.
Transform logging. Any corridor transform is logged in WordNF segment stream as a Snap or tunnel event with pre/post corridor hashes.
Transform admissibility. Policy controls whether corridor transforms are permitted and what additional regression/stress obligations are required.
Ch07.C3 — Prove
Ch07.C3.a — Error Envelopes
Error envelopes are the theorems that make approximate transports promotable.
Enclosure correctness. Output enclosures contain true values given input enclosures and declared transforms.
Residual correctness. Residual functionals computed from enclosures upper-bound semantic discrepancies.
Envelope composability. Composition of transports yields composed envelopes consistent with declared rules.
Verifier enforceability. All envelope computations must be recomputable by the verifier from pinned ids and parameters; otherwise they are heuristic and non-promotable.
Ch07.C3.b — Monotone Refinement
Monotone refinement ensures evidence improves with κ.
Residual monotonicity. Residual curves (r(\kappa)) and (s(\kappa)) decrease or plateau below gates under κ.
Enclosure monotonicity. Enclosure radii decrease or plateau below required thresholds.
Plateau vs drift. Plateau above gates under stable ConvFP indicates non-identifiability or obstruction; non-monotone behavior indicates drift or instability and triggers refusal or remediation.
Repair admissibility. When monotone refinement fails to reduce defect, admissible repairs are Snap/tunnel actions with defect reduction requirements; otherwise refusal is mandatory.
Ch07.C3.c — Perturbation Robustness
Perturbation robustness ensures transports are not brittle.
Perturbation probes. Probe families include small perturbations within admissible regions.
Robustness gate. Residual maxima remain bounded under perturbations, within corridor-declared perturbation tolerances.
Risk gating. High-risk transports (tunnel-derived, bridge-derived) require perturbation robustness certificates for promotion.
Failure classification. Perturbation failure indicates ill-conditioning, hidden boundary crossing, or semantic ambiguity; classification and refusal obligations are explicit.
Ch07.C3.d — Plateau Classification
Plateau classification determines when a transport cannot be certified under the current regime.
Plateau detection. Residual curves plateau above gates across κ, despite admissible method changes and repairs.
Classification. Plateau is classified as:
UNCERTAINTY_PLATEAU (probe inadequacy or insufficient methods),
HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION (stable loop residual suggests missing modulus),
SEMANTIC_CONFLICT (ConvFP mismatch or branch instability),
POLICY_BLOCK (required actions forbidden or budgets too small).
Action outcomes. Plateau forbids promotion and triggers:
probe escalation or carrier escalation,
tunnel synthesis jobs,
obstruction compilation (Tier-0),
portal escalation,
refusal with complete evidence if blocked.
Containment. Plateau outcomes are stored as refusal artifacts linked to the transport; they do not contaminate promoted routing.
Ch07.C4 — Extract
Ch07.C4.a — Certified Path Selection
Certified path selection extracts admissible routes meeting certification requirements.
Filter edges by promoted receipts under the requested context.
Require UCW tri-gate satisfaction for semantic edges and certified enclosures for evaluation edges.
Require required bridges for cross-convention/policy transitions.
Return path plus minimal replay bundle pointers; if none exists, return blocker set.
Ch07.C4.b — “Needs More Digits” Detection
Detection is corridor-driven.
Identify which κ levels fail gates and whether κ escalation remains admissible under budgets.
Determine whether residual decline is monotone (suggesting more κ may help) or plateaued (suggesting it will not).
Identify whether failure is numeric (corridor) or semantic (ConvFP/policy).
Output admissible κ escalation plan or refusal with evidence; never output informal advice.
Ch07.C4.c — Refusal Explanation
Refusal explanation is extracted from refusal artifacts.
Provide minimal failing predicate and counterexample object id.
Provide dominant residual curves and plateau/drift classification.
Provide consumed budget evidence and blocked actions (policy constraints).
Provide admissible next actions with explicit requirements (bridges, new corridors, probe expansions, carrier escalations).
Ch07.C4.d — Reliability Ranking
Reliability ranking orders transports and routes by certified robustness.
Rank by confidence level (exact > certified > near > heuristic > refused).
Within certified, rank by margin to gates and by perturbation robustness.
Penalize high-risk routes requiring many bridges or tunnels unless they have strong stress/regression coverage.
Output ranking with evidence pointers (receipts, witness roots) and context scope; ranking is non-semantic and cannot replace legality.
D. Fractal
Ch07.D1 — Spec
Ch07.D1.a — Transport Search
Transport search is the controlled exploration of possible routes satisfying typing and legality.
Search space. The space of candidate routes is the directed multigraph of edges filtered by type and scope.
Constraints. Search is constrained by policy budgets, corridor target classes, and ConvBundleFP scope coverage.
Witness-aware search. Search prefers routes with existing promoted witnesses and minimal missing obligations.
Failure-aware search. Search returns refusal-localized blockers when no admissible route exists, enabling targeted remediation.
Ch07.D1.b — Transport Synthesis
Transport synthesis constructs new transports or bridge candidates when search fails.
Synthesis triggers. Repeated refusal with stable failure patterns (e.g., plateau under existing transports) triggers synthesis of:
new evaluator charts (accel synthesis),
new bridges (convention/policy bridges),
new carriers or portals (when conflicts are intrinsic).
Synthesis discipline. Synthesis outputs candidates only; promotion requires certified verification and witnessed integration into the metro.
Synthesis templates. Synthesis uses typed templates: chart templates, bridge templates, and migration templates, each with explicit witness obligations.
No inspirational output. Synthesis is a finite, budgeted compiler pass producing candidate artifacts and explicit verification plans.
Ch07.D1.c — Transport Caching
Caching accelerates routing and verification without altering semantics.
Cache stores previously validated route fragments, legality traces, and witness roots, keyed by context hashes.
Cache entries are non-authoritative; verifiers still recompute witness obligations as required.
Cache entries are invalidated by changes in schema versions, verifier versions, or relevant ConvFP/policy/corridor contexts.
Cache correctness is itself subject to audit and regression checks.
Ch07.D1.d — Transport Pruning
Pruning reduces the search space while preserving completeness relative to admissible scopes.
Dominance pruning. If transport (T_1) is strictly dominated by (T_2) in all relevant contexts (higher cost, weaker evidence, more bridges), (T_1) may be deprioritized but not deleted.
Inadmissibility pruning. Transports forbidden by current policy are pruned from admissible search results but remain in the graph for other policies.
Quarantine pruning. Quarantined transports are excluded from certified routing until cleared.
Evidence pruning. Candidate transports without verification evidence may be pruned from certified routing but remain available for synthesis/verification jobs.
Ch07.D2 — Encode
Ch07.D2.a — Operator Indices
Operator indices accelerate lookup and synthesis.
Index operators by signature: domain lens, codomain lens, carrier type, scope, and action class.
Index operators by witness coverage: which corridors/policies have promoted receipts.
Index operators by risk class and required stress/regression suites.
Index operator versions and migration paths.
Ch07.D2.b — Memo Tables
Memo tables store intermediate routing and legality results.
Memo key includes: endpoints, required scope, policy hash, corridor hash, ConvBundleFP hash, and target class.
Memo value includes: selected route WordNF hashes, legality traces, missing obligations list, and suggested jobs.
Memo entries include invalidation dependencies: schema hashes, verifier hash, operator catalog hash.
Memo entries are reproducible and audit-friendly; nondeterministic memoization is forbidden.
Ch07.D2.c — Equivalence Caches
Equivalence caches store frequently used commutation and transport witnesses.
Cache UCWs by endpoints and WordNF hashes, indexed by context hashes.
Cache minimal witness sets for common commutation squares and loops.
Cache bridge chains connecting convention/policy regimes, with their witness roots.
Cache invalidation on drift alarms, regression failures, or migration updates.
Ch07.D2.d — Invalidation Rules
Invalidation prevents stale reuse.
Invalidate on schema version changes affecting interpretation.
Invalidate on verifier version changes affecting acceptance predicates.
Invalidate on ConvFP or corridor changes affecting scope or gates.
Invalidate on policy changes affecting admissibility or budgets.
Invalidation produces explicit invalidation artifacts and triggers re-verification jobs when required.
Ch07.D3 — Prove
Ch07.D3.a — Cache Correctness
Cache correctness ensures caches do not create false acceptance.
Caches may not assert acceptance; they may only store references to receipts and witnesses that the verifier can replay.
Cache outputs are checked against receipt and witness roots; mismatches are refused.
Cache entries are invalidated deterministically under declared invalidation rules.
Cache correctness is enforced by periodic audit replays on a regression schedule.
Ch07.D3.b — No Stale Reuse
No stale reuse ensures that routing does not silently depend on outdated evidence.
Any cached route must be revalidated for context hash match (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP/verifier/schema).
Any mismatched context requires re-search or explicit bridging/migration; otherwise routing must refuse.
Any quarantined or deprecated artifact is excluded unless explicitly allowed by governance and accompanied by up-to-date receipts.
Any evidence invalidated by drift alarms or regression failures is excluded until cleared.
Ch07.D3.c — Pruning Safety
Pruning safety ensures that pruning does not remove necessary paths.
Certified routing prunes only inadmissible or uncertified edges for the target class; it does not delete them from the graph.
Pruning preserves completeness relative to admissible evidence: if a certified path exists under context, pruning must not remove it.
Pruning decisions are deterministic and auditable; reasons are recorded.
Pruning interacts with synthesis: pruned candidates remain available as synthesis targets for verification jobs.
Ch07.D3.d — Synthesis Soundness Under Verification
Synthesis is sound only when it is coupled to verification.
Synthesized transports are candidates until verified by legality predicates and witnessed by receipts.
No synthesized artifact can be activated as semantic evidence without certified verification.
Synthesis correctness is judged by its ability to produce promotable witnesses under corridor and policy constraints, not by heuristic numeric agreement.
Failed synthesis attempts produce refusal artifacts and counterexample objects to guide future synthesis.
Ch07.D4 — Extract
Ch07.D4.a — Reusable Transport Kits
Transport kits are packaged collections of operators, witnesses, and receipts enabling reuse.
Include canonical transport signatures, parameter schemas, and operator ids.
Include minimal witness packs (UCWs, evaluation receipts, bridge packs) for common routes.
Include policy-safe corridor presets and κ ladders required for verification.
Include a kit receipt committing pins and witness roots for independent replay.
Ch07.D4.b — Fast Route Templates
Fast route templates are admissible route skeletons optimized for cost while preserving certification.
Templates specify endpoint classes, hub intermediate stations, and preferred chart selections.
Templates specify required bridges and scope coverage.
Templates specify fallback strategies and admissible repair actions.
Templates include proof obligations lists and expected budgets for schedulers.
Ch07.D4.c — Audit Macro Words
Audit macro words are canonical WordNF templates for repeated audit patterns.
Templates for commutation audits (two-route comparisons).
Templates for loop closure audits (cycle tests in atlas coherence).
Templates for bridge audits (pre/post convention/policy reconciliation).
Templates for corridor audit replays (κ ladder gate revalidation).
Each macro word is a reusable, addressable artifact with explicit scope and context binding.
Ch07.D4.d — Policy-Safe Templates
Policy-safe templates are route and verification plans guaranteed to respect policy constraints.
Templates enumerate only admissible methods, tunnels, and bridges.
Templates include explicit budget fields and termination guarantees.
Templates include required regression and stress suite obligations for promotion.
Templates produce refusal artifacts deterministically when policy blocks decisive verification, preventing unsafe fallbacks.
Ch07 — Transport Language and Legality Predicates
Ch08 — UCW Theory: Commutation, Holonomy, Representability
A. Square
Ch08.A1 — Spec
Ch08.A1.a — UCW Definition
A Unified Commutation Witness (UCW) is the minimal, proof-carrying object that certifies that two computational routes represent the same semantic claim under a pinned context. UCW is the atomic unit of truth in the metro: it binds a route identity, a corridor and policy regime, a convention regime, a probe regime, and three orthogonal verification obligations:
Commutation (face) obligation: two route evaluations agree on an adequate probe family within corridor gates.
Holonomy (spin) obligation: loop closure holds on an adequate probe family within corridor gates.
Representability obligation: projected/canonical forms are stable and consistent with the declared representability regime within corridor gates.
UCW is defined relative to:
a pair of routes (W_A,W_B) with common semantic intent and compatible endpoints,
a pinned context (X := (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V)), where (P) is policy, (C) is corridor, and (V) is verifier version,
a probe regime (\mathcal P) and κ ladder (\mathcal K) specified by the corridor.
Let (x) denote a carrier object or an abstract input, and let (\mathcal P(\kappa)) be the probe set at κ. The UCW stores κ-indexed residual maxima:[r_{\square}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{face}}(W_A(p,\kappa),W_B(p,\kappa)),][s_{\square}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{spin}}(p,L_{\square}(p,\kappa)),][r_{\Pi}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_{\Pi}(p,\Pi_h(p,\kappa)),]where:
(\rho_{\text{face}},\rho_{\text{spin}},\rho_{\Pi}) are declared residual functionals pinned by corridor schema,
(L_{\square}) is the loop map associated with the route pairing and its encode/decode semantics,
(\Pi_h) is the representability projector pinned by conventions, corridor, and schema.
UCW is not a narrative explanation. It is a finite object whose verifier recomputes all residuals deterministically and decides acceptance via explicit gate predicates.
Ch08.A1.b — Residual Curves
Residual curves are the evidence functions that measure how a claim behaves under refinement. A UCW contains at least three κ-indexed curves:
Face residual curve (r_{\square}(\kappa)). Measures commutation defect (route disagreement). For scalar carriers, this may be relative interval disagreement; for higher carriers it may be normed differences of observables, distributional discrepancies, or operator norm differences. The residual functional is pinned.
Spin residual curve (s_{\square}(\kappa)). Measures holonomy (failure to close a semantic loop). Spin residual is evaluated through a loop map (L_{\square}) derived from route semantics. For scalar systems, a loop map may be instantiated as a round-trip encode/decode/projection shadow; for geometric or analytic continuation systems, it is a monodromy-sensitive map.
Projection residual curve (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)). Measures representability stability: whether the computed object remains stable under canonicalization/projection, and whether projection commutes with declared transforms as required by schema.
Optional auxiliary curves may include: tail-bound tightness, perturbation sensitivity, regression deltas, stress-suite maxima, and cost residuals (used only for scheduling, never for semantic acceptance).
Residual curves are scoped: they are meaningful only under the pinned context and probe regime, and they are not transferable across contexts without explicit bridging.
Ch08.A1.c — κ-Grid Meaning
The κ grid (\mathcal K) is the ordered refinement schedule at which residual evidence is required. κ is interpreted by the carrier and the methods in use, but UCW imposes a uniform discipline:
κ-indexed evidence. UCW must provide residual maxima for each κ in (\mathcal K). Evidence at a single precision is insufficient for promotion.
Refinement interpretation. κ is chosen to expose:
convergence toward gates when the claim is true and methods are adequate,
plateau above gates when the claim is undecidable under current regime,
drift signatures when semantics changes or numerical methods are unstable,
holonomy persistence when the loop map is obstructed.
Monotone expectations. Under stable semantics and adequate probes, residual curves are expected to decrease or plateau below gates. Deviations are classified and may trigger refusal or remediation.
κ adequacy. The κ grid itself is subject to adequacy: it must be sufficient to detect plateau/drift. Inadequate κ grids block promotion and are recorded as refusal reasons.
Ch08.A1.d — Class Taxonomy
UCW classifies outcomes to separate semantic failures from numeric failures and to drive deterministic escalation. The canonical UCW classes are:
ALIAS. Residual curves satisfy gates; route disagreement is below face gate and representability is stable. The routes are equivalent under pinned context.
HOLONOMY. Face residual is within gate (routes agree locally) but spin residual violates the spin gate stably, indicating loop non-closure. This is a candidate signal for missing moduli or portal requirements, depending on stability and bridge admissibility.
KERNEL. Face residual violates gates while representability and spin may be stable; indicates route disagreement not attributable to loop issues. Often indicates missing transport witness, incompatible methods, or failure of claimed equivalence.
UNCERTAINTY. Residual curves plateau above gates under κ refinement, or probe adequacy fails. Indicates that the claim cannot be decided under current corridor/probes/budgets.
MIXED. Multiple residual obligations fail without a dominant signature; requires localized failure analysis and may trigger probe escalation, carrier escalation, or tunnel attempts.
POLICY_BLOCK. Required verification steps are forbidden or budgets are insufficient under policy; the semantic truth may exist but is inaccessible under current governance.
CONVENTION_CONFLICT. ConvFP mismatch on relevant scope without a promoted bridge; equivalence cannot be asserted regardless of numeric proximity.
Class taxonomy is used by the scheduler and by repair logic to select admissible next actions. Class assignment is itself a verifier output, not a human label.
Ch08.A2 — Encode
Ch08.A2.a — UCW Payload Schema
UCW is encoded as a canonical, versioned record with required fields sufficient for replay verification. The minimal UCW schema includes:
Header.
ucw_version,
ucw_id (hash of the canonical UCW record),
created (metadata; non-semantic).
Route binding.
word_hash: hash of the canonical WordNF for the route pairing,
pathA_id, pathB_id (optional explicit path identifiers when WordNF includes multiple segments),
segment_commitments: hashes of segment stream and gate stack ids.
Context binding.
conv_bundle_hash,
policy_hash,
corridor_hash,
verifier_hash,
canon_hash (canonical encoding id),
schema_hashes required to interpret residual functionals, probes, projection, and loop map signatures.
Probe binding.
probe_hash: hash commitment to probe generator and adequacy metadata,
kappa_grid: explicit κ ladder list,
probe_counts: counts per κ (optional, but mandatory if policy requires bounded verification).
Residual bindings.
eps_face, eps_spin, eps_pi (gate thresholds as canonical numeric strings),
r_face_curve: κ-indexed residual maxima list,
s_spin_curve: κ-indexed residual maxima list,
r_pi_curve: κ-indexed residual maxima list,
optional auxiliary curves and their ids.
Classification and refusal.
class: UCW class taxonomy label,
refusal (optional): structured refusal payload when class is not promotable, including minimal failing predicate ids, counterexample probe ids, and admissible next actions.
UCW payload fields are exhaustive; omission of any required binding makes UCW non-promotable.
Ch08.A2.b — Hash Bindings
UCW binds all meaning-critical and verification-critical objects by cryptographic hash.
Word binding. UCW binds word_hash; if WordNF changes, UCW is invalid. This prevents reusing witnesses across altered routes.
Context binding. UCW binds policy, corridor, and ConvBundleFP; mismatch invalidates UCW, preventing policy laundering and corridor laundering.
Verifier binding. UCW binds a verifier version hash; acceptance is relative to a verifier protocol. Verifier changes require re-verification or translation via verified migration.
Schema binding. UCW binds schema hashes defining residual functionals, projection semantics, and probe generators. Schema mismatch yields refusal rather than silent reinterpretation.
Ch08.A2.c — Probe Hashes
Probe hashes bind the probe regime used for verification.
Probe generator ID. Identifies the deterministic generator for probes at each κ.
Adequacy metadata. Declares adequacy criteria, stability region coverage, and adversarial probe requirements where relevant.
Seed pins. If any randomness is allowed by policy, seed values are pinned and included in probe hash; otherwise probe generation must be deterministic.
Scope binding. Probes are scope-indexed; using a probe regime outside scope invalidates UCW.
Probe hashes are essential to prevent false commutation arising from an unrepresentative probe set.
Ch08.A2.d — Corridor Hashes
Corridor hashes bind the acceptance regime.
Gate binding. ε thresholds are interpreted relative to corridor record; UCW includes explicit copies of ε thresholds as well, but corridor hash is authoritative.
κ ladder binding. κ grid must match the corridor κ ladder or be declared as an admissible equivalent; mismatch invalidates UCW.
Residual functional binding. Corridor defines which residual functionals and normalizations are used; UCW binds these by schema hashes and corridor hash.
Budget binding. Corridor encodes budgets; UCW evidence must respect those budgets. Budget violations invalidate UCW or require refusal classification.
Ch08.A3 — Prove
Ch08.A3.a — Verifier Protocol
UCW validity is determined by a deterministic verifier protocol. For UCW record (U), WordNF (W), and context (X=(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V)), the verifier executes:
Resolve. Resolve all pinned records: lens specs, operator catalogs, corridor record, policy record, ConvFP records, and schema records referenced by hashes. Failure yields refusal CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL.
Validate bindings. Confirm that word_hash, conv_bundle_hash, policy_hash, corridor_hash, verifier_hash, and schema hashes match the resolved records. Any mismatch yields refusal BINDING_MISMATCH.
Validate probe regime. Reconstruct probe generator from probe_hash and validate adequacy requirements for the scope. If adequacy cannot be certified, classify as UNCERTAINTY with refusal PROBE_INADEQUATE.
Recompute residual curves. For each κ in κ grid, recompute:
commutation residual maxima (r_{\square}(\kappa)),
spin residual maxima (s_{\square}(\kappa)),
projection residual maxima (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)),using pinned residual functionals, projection signatures, and loop map signatures.
Check monotone behavior (optional but recommended). Determine whether curves decrease or plateau; use for classification and refusal evidence, not as a separate acceptance predicate unless corridor requires it.
Apply acceptance predicate. Evaluate tri-gate and any auxiliary gates:[\forall \kappa\in\mathcal K:; r_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{face}},\quad s_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\quad r_{\Pi}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\Pi}.]
Emit decision. Output promotable status, UCW class, and if not promotable, a refusal payload with minimal failing predicate localization and admissible next actions.
This protocol is fully mechanical. Stored residuals do not substitute for recomputation; they are evidence for audit and for debugging.
Ch08.A3.b — Acceptance Predicate
The acceptance predicate is the tri-gate condition plus any corridor/policy-required auxiliary gates.
Tri-gate acceptance. A UCW is promotable only if all three curves satisfy gates across the κ ladder. Failure at any κ fails acceptance.
Auxiliary gate acceptance. For high-risk edges, policy may require:
perturbation stability gates,
adversarial probe gates,
regression pass gates,
stress pass gates.These are treated as additional required witness obligations and must be satisfied for promotion.
Scope completeness. Acceptance implies scope completeness: all operators used are covered by ConvBundleFP and corridor scope. Missing scope coverage blocks acceptance irrespective of numeric residuals.
Context integrity. Acceptance implies that all bindings match; a UCW cannot be promotable if it is computed under different corridor/policy/ConvFP than the one being claimed.
Ch08.A3.c — Refusal Predicate
Refusal predicates define structured failure outcomes.
Hard refusal. Any failure of type/scope/policy/convention bindings yields immediate refusal with class CONVENTION_CONFLICT or POLICY_BLOCK or CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL, as appropriate.
Soft refusal. Failures of tri-gate conditions yield refusal classes KERNEL, HOLONOMY, or UNCERTAINTY based on dominance and stability signatures.
Plateau refusal. If residual curves plateau above gates under κ refinement, refusal must encode plateau evidence and recommend probe escalation, method change, or tunnel jobs.
Budget refusal. If verification cannot be completed within corridor budgets, refusal must encode budget exhaustion evidence and list admissible next actions that require policy/corridor changes.
Refusals are not errors; they are certified outcomes that preserve evidence and guide deterministic remediation.
Ch08.A3.d — Witness Compositionality
Witness compositionality is the property that UCWs can be composed to certify larger route equivalences.
Commutation composition. If UCWs certify commutation of adjacent squares in a grid of routes, then a higher-level commutation claim can be certified by composing the squares, provided:
shared boundaries are consistent under pinned context,
intermediate nodes satisfy representability gates,
loop closures formed by the composed square are also certified.
Transport chain composition. If transport edges (T_1,\dots,T_n) each have UCWs certifying their correctness, then the composed transport may be certified by:
concatenating WordNF segments under normalization,
composing residual obligations, and
adding UCWs for any new commutation squares introduced by composition boundaries.
Context stability requirement. UCWs compose only when context hashes match or when explicit bridge UCWs exist to reconcile contexts. Composition across contexts without bridges is illegal.
Receipt binding. A composed claim is promotable only when its receipt includes pins and witness roots for all constituent UCWs and any additional required closure witnesses. Composition never permits dropping evidence.
Ch08.A4 — Extract
Ch08.A4.a — UCW Queries
UCW queries retrieve witnesses by route, context, and class.
By UCW id. Retrieve UCW record and decoded bindings by hash identity.
By WordNF hash. Retrieve all UCWs certifying a given route pairing, indexed by corridor/policy/ConvBundleFP contexts.
By endpoints. Retrieve UCWs linking specified charts or nodes, returning candidate witnesses and their promotable status.
By class. Retrieve UCWs by class taxonomy to locate holonomy obstructions, uncertainty plateaus, or convention conflicts.
Ch08.A4.b — Class Dashboards
Class dashboards summarize verification health.
Promotable coverage dashboard. Counts and lists of promotable UCWs per scope and per corridor.
Holonomy dashboard. UCWs classified as HOLONOMY with stable loop residuals, grouped by loop families and candidate obstruction intervals.
Uncertainty dashboard. UCWs classified as UNCERTAINTY, grouped by probe inadequacy and by plateau signatures.
Conflict dashboard. UCWs blocked by convention or policy conflicts, grouped by required bridge or policy changes.
Dashboards are extraction artifacts and do not alter semantics; they guide scheduling and remediation.
Ch08.A4.c — Failure Localization
Failure localization extracts minimal failing components.
Minimal failing κ. Identify smallest κ where a gate fails; provide residual magnitude and margin to gate.
Minimal failing probe. Identify probe(s) achieving the maximum residual; provide probe ids and adequacy context.
Minimal failing subword. If failure is localized to a subroute, compute minimal subword where residual increases beyond gates, producing counterexample objects.
Classification evidence. Provide dominance evidence for class taxonomy (e.g., face fails but spin passes → kernel; face passes but spin fails → holonomy; plateau signatures → uncertainty).
Ch08.A4.d — Replay Scripts
Replay scripts are deterministic procedures for re-verifying UCWs.
Input. UCW id (or WordNF hash plus context), plus access to pinned pins and witness sets via receipt expansions.
Procedure. Resolve records, validate bindings, regenerate probes, recompute residual curves at each κ, apply acceptance predicate.
Output. Promotable/not, class, maximal residuals, and refusal payload if not promotable.
Portability. Scripts are designed to run across environments under pinned backend semantics; any mismatch yields refusal, not silent acceptance.
B. Flower
Ch08.B1 — Spec
Ch08.B1.a — Commutation Squares
A commutation square is the basic geometric object UCW certifies. It consists of two distinct paths between the same endpoints in the metro.
Let (u) and (v) be endpoints (charts or nodes). A commutation square is defined by two paths:[P: u \to v,\qquad Q: u \to v,]each a WordNF-defined route (possibly with multiple segments). The commutation claim is that (P) and (Q) induce equivalent results under the pinned context.
Commutation squares are typed: they specify which carrier observables are compared and which residual functional (\rho_{\text{face}}) applies.
Ch08.B1.b — Face Paths
Face paths are the two alternative routes across the square.
Path formation. Each path is a composition of transports and evaluations that is well-typed and admissible under policy/corridor.
Path normalization. Each path is normalized into WordNF; normalization must preserve scope and must not erase tunnel events.
Path observables. Each path produces outputs that can be compared via declared observables; undefined observables yield refusal.
Path costs. Costs are non-semantic metadata used by schedulers; costs do not affect acceptance.
Ch08.B1.c — Route Pairing
UCW certifies a specific pairing of routes.
Pair identity. The pairing is identified by WordNF hash of the paired route object; changing either route changes the pairing.
Context binding. Pairing is meaningful only under pinned context; different corridor/policy/ConvBundleFP regimes define different claims.
Probe binding. Pairing binds probe regime and κ ladder.
Loop implications. Pairings may induce loops when composed; loop closure must be witnessed for robust equivalence across multiple pairings.
Ch08.B1.d — WordNF Binding
WordNF binds route identity and enables replay.
WordNF includes segment stream encoding of gate runs, tunnel events, bridge events, and refinement events.
WordNF binds method IDs, bound IDs, rounding IDs, κ schedules, and probe regime IDs.
WordNF normalization is scope-aware; commutation rewrites require explicit witnesses.
WordNF hash is the anchor for UCW binding; UCW is invalid without a matching WordNF hash.
Ch08.B2 — Encode
Ch08.B2.a — Square IDs
Each commutation square has a deterministic identifier.
Square signature. A square id is computed from:
endpoint ids,
path WordNF hashes,
context hashes (ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor),
scope ids for observables and residual functionals.
Square id immutability. A change in any component yields a different square id; squares are not mutable.
Square indexing. Squares are indexed by endpoints and by scope to support rapid retrieval and audit.
Square lifecycle. Squares can be certified, promoted, refused, or quarantined; lifecycle is determined by UCW outcomes and receipts.
Ch08.B2.b — Path Word IDs
Paths are identified by their WordNF hashes.
WordNF pointer. UCW stores WordNF hash pointer(s) for both paths.
Segment pointers. UCW may store hash pointers to segment stream and to gate stack ids used.
Parameter binds. WordNF binds parameters; UCW does not duplicate them except as context hashes.
Replay binds. WordNF includes replay fields; UCW relies on them for deterministic recomputation.
Ch08.B2.c — Gate Segment IDs
Gate segment ids identify which gate stack runs produced residual curves.
Gate stack id. Identifies the ordered predicate evaluation and residual functional set.
Gate parameter commit. Gate parameters are committed by corridor hash and segment payload hashes.
Gate output references. Stored residual curves in UCW reference recomputed outputs; their equality is checked by verifier.
Compatibility enforcement. Gate stacks are carrier-typed; using a gate stack incompatible with carrier yields refusal.
Ch08.B2.d — Parameter Binds
Parameter binds ensure that the same UCW cannot be reused under altered conditions.
κ binds. κ ladder is explicitly stored; mismatch invalidates UCW.
Probe binds. Probe hash is stored; mismatch invalidates UCW.
Projection binds. Projection signature hash is stored; mismatch invalidates UCW.
Loop map binds. Loop map signature hash is stored; mismatch invalidates UCW.
Ch08.B3 — Prove
Ch08.B3.a — Commutation ⇒ Equivalence Theorem (Bounded)
Bounded commutation yields scoped semantic equivalence.
Let (P,Q:u\to v) be two admissible paths with a UCW (U) under context (X=(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P_o,C,V)). If (U) is promotable (tri-gate and auxiliary gates satisfied), then the outputs of (P) and (Q) are equivalent within the equivalence relation induced by the corridor and lens schema on the declared observables. This equivalence is:
Scoped. Valid only under the pinned ConvBundleFP scope, corridor, policy, and verifier version.
Witnessed. Supported by UCW residual curves and probe adequacy evidence.
Composable. May be consumed by downstream receipts as a semantic equivalence edge provided downstream contexts match or are bridged.
Non-global. Does not imply global equality independent of corridor or conventions; it certifies the bounded claim only.
Ch08.B3.b — Transport Witness Usage
UCWs are the witness objects used to justify transport edges.
Transport edge certification. A transport edge is promotable only if it references UCWs certifying commutation and holonomy obligations required by the transport’s legality predicates.
Atlas coherence. Atlas transport edges among charts require UCWs; activation of accel charts requires a transport UCW back to baseline.
Bridge correctness. Convention and policy bridges require UCWs showing that bridge application commutes with required gates and closes loops.
Refusal containment. Transport edges with non-promotable UCWs remain candidates; they cannot be used in certified routing.
Ch08.B3.c — Stability Under Refinement
Stability under refinement is required for promotable UCWs.
Residual monotonicity. Residual curves should decrease or plateau below gates across κ in stable regimes; the corridor may explicitly require monotonicity checks.
Plateau classification. Plateau above gates is classified as UNCERTAINTY unless additional evidence indicates holonomy obstruction or semantic conflict.
Cross-κ drift detection. Non-monotone changes inconsistent with numeric tightening indicate drift or instability; such UCWs are refused and may trigger ConvFP remediation or method migration.
Budgeted refinement. Refinement proceeds only within corridor budgets; if budgets exhaust, refusal records exhaustion evidence and recommended next actions.
Ch08.B3.d — Ambiguity Handling
Ambiguity is handled by multi-hypothesis discipline rather than by silent selection.
Ambiguity detection. If probes are insufficient to distinguish classes or if multiple canonicalizations are possible under under-specified conventions, UCW is classified as UNCERTAINTY or CONVENTION_CONFLICT.
No forced choice. Verifier does not choose between ambiguous alternatives; it refuses promotion and records ambiguity evidence.
Escalation. Ambiguity triggers admissible escalation: probe expansion, carrier escalation, convention bridging, or portal creation.
Auditability. Ambiguity evidence includes counterexample objects and minimal failing predicates; downstream claims cannot ignore ambiguity.
Ch08.B4 — Extract
Ch08.B4.a — “Why Equivalent” Explanation
The explanation artifact for equivalence is a structured extraction from UCW and receipts.
Identify the two routes and their WordNF hashes.
Identify the context hashes (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier).
Report tri-gate maxima across κ and margins to gates.
Provide probe adequacy summary and any auxiliary stress/regression status.
The explanation does not provide informal reasoning; it provides witness-based evidence pointers.
Ch08.B4.b — “Where Failed” Explanation
Failure explanation extracts minimal failing information.
Identify which gate failed (face/spin/projection/aux).
Identify minimal κ where failure occurs.
Identify probe(s) achieving maximal residual and their adequacy context.
Identify minimal failing subword when available, and return counterexample object ids.
Ch08.B4.c — Minimal Counterexample
Minimal counterexamples are extracted as counterexample objects.
Minimal failing predicate id.
Minimal κ and probe set.
Minimal route segment subset (subword) responsible for failure.
Admissible remediation actions list.
Counterexamples are replay-verifiable and are stored as refusal evidence.
Ch08.B4.d — Witness Export
Witness export provides portable proof objects.
Export UCW record and referenced WordNF.
Export pinned context records (ConvFP, corridor, policy, schema, verifier).
Export probe regime definition and adequacy metadata.
Export minimal replay script specification (procedure) sufficient to re-verify UCW externally, respecting visibility modes.
C. Cloud
Ch08.C1 — Spec
Ch08.C1.a — Loop Maps
A loop map (L_{\square}) is the semantic closure operator associated with a loop in the metro. It is defined by the encode/decode/normalize semantics of the loop word and the conventions in scope.
Loop word. A loop is a WordNF whose endpoints coincide in semantic intent.
Loop map signature. The loop map is a typed function on probe carriers:[L_{\square}(\cdot;\kappa): \mathcal P(\kappa)\to \mathcal P(\kappa),]constructed by executing the loop word and applying required projection/canonicalization steps.
Monodromy sensitivity. For branch-sensitive scopes, loop maps include continuation semantics and capture monodromy by design; probes must include loop-around-singularity probes when required.
Stability. Loop maps must be stable under κ refinement in promotable regimes; instability indicates drift or non-identifiability.
Ch08.C1.b — Spin Residual Definition
Spin residual quantifies loop non-closure.
Residual functional. (\rho_{\text{spin}}) is declared and may be a normed difference, a distance in distribution space, or a discrepancy in invariants.
κ-indexed maxima. For each κ:[s_{\square}(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{spin}}(p,L_{\square}(p,\kappa)).]
Spin gate. Promotion requires (s_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) for all κ in the ladder.
Interpretation. Persistent spin residuals under stable face and projection residuals indicate holonomy obstructions; these trigger Tier-0 obstruction compilation or portal analysis.
Ch08.C1.c — Probe Adequacy
Probe adequacy is required to interpret residual curves.
Probes must cover stability regions relevant to the scope.
Probes must include adversarial boundary probes for discontinuous semantics.
Probes must be deterministic or seed-pinned.
Probe adequacy must be certified or else UCW is not promotable.
Ch08.C1.d — Representability Projector (\Pi_h)
Representability projector (\Pi_h) enforces canonical meaning class stability.
Projector signature. (\Pi_h) is a typed operator pinned by conventions, corridor, and schema.
Projection residual. (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)) measures deviation of probes from their projected forms.
Commutation requirements. Where required by schema, projection must commute with transports; commutation is witnessed by UCWs.
Projection as guard. Projection is the stability guard that prevents accidental acceptance due to representation artifacts or numeric rounding idiosyncrasies.
Ch08.C2 — Encode
Ch08.C2.a — Probe Set Schema
Probe sets are encoded as canonical records.
probe_version, probe_id, probe_hash.
generator id and parameters, κ binding, determinism pins.
adequacy metadata and stability region coverage.
adversarial flags and boundary probe descriptions.
Probe sets are referenced by UCWs and by receipts.
Ch08.C2.b — Adequacy Metadata
Adequacy metadata encodes why probes are sufficient.
coverage of stability regions and scope.
boundary probe inclusion for discontinuous semantics.
probe cardinalities and κ-indexed growth rules.
any empirical adequacy evidence required by policy (e.g., regression tests confirming probe informativeness).
Ch08.C2.c — Loop Map Signature
Loop maps are encoded by signature records.
loop word hash and scope.
required projection/canonicalization steps.
carrier type and residual functional id.
determinism pins and method ids used to instantiate loop map execution.
Ch08.C2.d — Projection Signature
Projection signatures encode projector semantics.
projector id and parameters.
rounding model id and canonicalization id.
scope and stability region applicability.
commutation obligations with transports (when declared by schema).
Ch08.C3 — Prove
Ch08.C3.a — Spin Gate Theorem
If a UCW is promotable, then loop closure is certified within corridor gates.
For each κ in ladder, (s_{\square}(\kappa)\le\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}).
Under adequate probes and stable projection, repeated traversal of the loop does not accumulate semantic discrepancy beyond gates within the corridor regime.
If face residual is small but spin residual violates gates stably, the system classifies as holonomy obstruction and forbids promotion.
Spin gate satisfaction is necessary for safe reuse of equivalence classes and bridge chains.
Ch08.C3.b — Adequacy Lemmas
Adequacy lemmas specify conditions under which probe families can be trusted.
Separation lemma. Adequate probes separate equivalence classes relevant to the scope.
Boundary lemma. Adequate probes include boundary probes for discontinuities; otherwise false equivalence is possible.
Refinement lemma. Under adequate probes, κ refinement yields informative residual behavior (decrease or plateau reflecting truth or obstruction).
Compositional lemma. Probe adequacy composes when composing UCWs only if probe regimes are compatible or explicitly bridged.
Ch08.C3.c — Regression Requirements
Regression requirements ensure UCW validity remains stable under evolution.
UCWs used in promoted edges must be part of regression suites.
Regression reruns recompute residual curves and re-evaluate gates.
Regression failures quarantine dependent edges and require remediation.
Regression records include bisect lineage to localize cause of failure.
Ch08.C3.d — Adversarial Probe Design
Adversarial probes target failure modes that ordinary probes miss.
branch boundary probes for multivalued primitives.
near-singularity probes for analytic continuation and regularization.
ordering tie probes for canonicalization.
worst-case commutation probes designed to maximize defect.
Adversarial probe design is scoped and policy-controlled; required for high-risk promotions.
Ch08.C4 — Extract
Ch08.C4.a — Holonomy Summaries
Holonomy summaries extract loop closure evidence.
Loop word hash and loop map signature.
κ-indexed spin residual maxima and margins to gate.
dominant probes contributing to spin maxima.
classification and recommended next actions if spin gates fail.
Ch08.C4.b — Plateau Alarms
Plateau alarms identify persistent inability to satisfy gates.
plateau predicate evidence over κ.
adequacy status and whether probe expansion is admissible.
classification of plateau (uncertainty vs obstruction vs conflict).
escalation job suggestions (tunnel, carrier escalation, obstruction compilation, portal).
Ch08.C4.c — Non-Identifiability Alarms
Non-identifiability alarms indicate that current probes/corridor cannot decide the claim.
evidence of probe inadequacy.
stability region boundary proximity evidence.
recommended probe expansions or carrier escalations.
refusal receipts and counterexample object ids.
Ch08.C4.d — Uncertainty Reports
Uncertainty reports provide structured summaries of what is not known.
which gates fail and by how much.
which κ levels and probes are responsible.
which admissible repairs remain.
which governance constraints (policy/corridor) block resolution.
D. Fractal
Ch08.D1 — Spec
Ch08.D1.a — UCW Reuse
UCW reuse is permitted only under strict context matching or explicit bridging.
A UCW is reusable if its context hashes match the consumer context hashes.
If contexts differ, reuse requires explicit bridges (policy bridges, convention bridges, corridor bridging when applicable) with their own witnesses.
Reuse preserves scope: using UCW outside its scope is illegal.
Reuse is mediated by receipts; UCW ids appear in witness roots of receipts.
Ch08.D1.b — UCW Compaction
Compaction reduces UCW storage while preserving replay.
Store hashes and minimal curve data required for audit; preserve ability to recompute curves.
Deduplicate identical UCWs by hash identity.
Preserve refusal UCWs with plateau/obstruction evidence as they guide future discovery.
Produce compaction receipts proving replay equivalence for promoted claims.
Ch08.D1.c — UCW Indexing
Indexing supports retrieval and memory mapping.
Index by WordNF hash and endpoints.
Index by class taxonomy and scope.
Index by corridor/policy contexts.
Index by failure signatures (plateau patterns, holonomy patterns) to support obstruction compilation and scheduling.
Ch08.D1.d — UCW Cross-Metro Exchange
Cross-metro exchange transmits UCWs as proof-carrying objects.
Export UCWs with their pinned contexts and required pins.
Import UCWs into quarantine until local verification under local policy/corridor is performed.
If local policy differs, require policy bridge receipts.
Exchange must preserve visibility modes; restricted UCWs may be unusable externally without access to withheld pins.
Ch08.D2 — Encode
Ch08.D2.a — Witness Roots
Witness roots are merkle roots over UCW ids and auxiliary witness ids.
Receipts commit witness sets by witness roots.
Witness roots expand deterministically via indices.
Witness roots are scope-indexed and context-indexed.
Root expansion mismatches invalidate receipts.
Ch08.D2.b — Witness References
Witness references provide addressable pointers.
UCW id references and decoded record pointers.
WordNF references.
probe regime references.
corridor/policy/convention record references.
Witness references are stored in audit packets and minimal replay bundles.
Ch08.D2.c — Witness Caches
Witness caches accelerate verification.
Cache resolved pins and recomputed residual curves keyed by UCW id and context.
Cache is non-authoritative; verifier still checks bindings and can recompute as needed.
Cache invalidates on schema/verifier updates, drift alarms, or context changes.
Cache correctness is audited by regression suites.
Ch08.D2.d — Invalidation
Invalidation prevents stale UCW reuse.
Invalidate UCWs when their pinned contexts become unsupported (schema version unsupported, verifier unsupported).
Invalidate when drift alarms indicate ConvFP schema incompleteness affecting scope.
Invalidate when corridor or policy changes require stronger gates or different κ ladders.
Invalidate via explicit invalidation artifacts linked to remediation tasks.
Ch08.D3 — Prove
Ch08.D3.a — Reuse Soundness
Reuse soundness states that consuming UCWs via receipts preserves correctness.
UCW reuse is sound only when context hashes match or are bridged by promoted bridges with witnessed correctness.
Consuming a UCW outside scope is illegal and refused.
Witness compositionality ensures that higher-level claims remain verifiable when built from UCW components.
Regression enforces ongoing soundness; failures quarantine dependent artifacts.
Ch08.D3.b — Compaction Soundness
Compaction soundness ensures UCW compaction does not change verification outcomes.
For promoted receipts, recomputation of residual curves yields same gate outcomes before and after compaction.
Compaction preserves required pins and witness references.
Compaction produces receipts proving replay equivalence.
Compaction never removes refusal evidence required for future remediation or discovery.
Ch08.D3.c — Exchange Safety
Exchange safety prevents imported UCWs from corrupting local truth.
Imported UCWs are quarantined until verified locally.
Compatibility checks ensure ConvFP scope and policy admissibility.
If incompatibilities exist, require bridges or treat as conflict evidence.
No imported UCW may be used for promotion-critical routing without local promotion receipts.
Ch08.D3.d — Policy-Bridge Requirements
Cross-policy UCW reuse requires explicit policy bridging.
If a UCW was certified under policy (P_1) and is to be used under policy (P_2), either (P_2) must accept (P_1) as compatible or a policy bridge must exist.
Policy bridge artifacts must be witnessed and promotable; they cannot be informal permissions.
Policy bridging preserves budgets and prohibits policy laundering.
Absent bridging, UCW reuse is refused and remains quarantined.
Ch08.D4 — Extract
Ch08.D4.a — Witness Packs
Witness packs are minimal bundles for UCW replay.
UCW record.
WordNF record.
probe regime record.
corridor/policy/convention records and schema/verifier records.
Witness packs are hash-committed and mode-respecting.
Ch08.D4.b — Minimal Witness Sets
Minimal witness sets are computed for composite claims.
Determine which UCWs and auxiliary certificates are required.
Deduplicate by identity.
Ensure context compatibility or include bridges.
Emit a witness root and an expansion index.
Ch08.D4.c — Audit-Grade Bundles
Audit-grade bundles include full evidence for independent validation.
receipts with pins and witness roots.
expanded pins and expanded witnesses.
replay scripts and verifier catalogs.
regression/stress suite results as required.
Ch08.D4.d — Verifier Catalogs
Verifier catalogs index verifier versions and supported schemas.
supported UCW schema versions.
supported corridor/policy/convention schema versions.
supported operator catalogs and gate stacks.
migration and translator artifacts enabling backward verification.
Ch08 — UCW Theory: Commutation, Holonomy, Representability
Ch09 — Snap Operator: Corridor Tightening and Deterministic Repair
A. Square
Ch09.A1 — Spec
Ch09.A1.a — Snap Input/Output Types
Snap is a deterministic repair operator that transforms a verification attempt from a failing or near state toward a promotable state by applying a bounded sequence of admissible refinements and local rewrites, without altering semantic conventions and without silently widening acceptance regimes. Snap is defined as a typed partial operator on a verification state.
A Snap state is a tuple[S ;:=; (\mathsf{Goal},;\mathsf{Route},;\mathsf{Ctx},;\mathsf{Obs},;\mathsf{Defect},;\mathsf{Budget},;\mathsf{History}),]where:
Goal (\mathsf{Goal}). A typed target claim to certify (equivalence/transport edge, atlas closure obligation, evaluator certification, bridge promotion step). Goal includes the acceptance predicate id (gate stack) and required witness types.
Route (\mathsf{Route}). A route object: endpoints, WordNF segment stream, selected evaluators/charts, and any required intermediate squares/loops. Route is the object whose commutation/holonomy/projection behavior Snap attempts to repair.
Context (\mathsf{Ctx}). Pinned hashes: ConvBundleFP, policy hash, corridor hash, verifier hash, schema hashes. Snap is illegal if context is ill-typed or incomplete.
Observations (\mathsf{Obs}). Recomputable evidence: κ-indexed residual curves, enclosure radii, probe adequacy status, and any auxiliary gate outcomes.
Defect (\mathsf{Defect}). A scalar functional (\mathfrak D(S)) aggregating gate violations and (optionally) cost metrics, used only to enforce monotone repair.
Budget (\mathsf{Budget}). A finite resource cap for repair: maximum κ steps, maximum probe expansions, maximum method substitutions, maximum Snap iterations, and maximum allowed tunnel escalation triggers.
History (\mathsf{History}). An ordered log of prior Snap actions, corridor transformations, and legality checks, recorded as segment events for replay.
Snap consumes an input state (S) and produces an output in the sum type:[\mathrm{Snap}(S)\in {\mathrm{SnapOK}(S'),;\mathrm{SnapNEAR}(S'),;\mathrm{SnapFAIL}(R)},]where:
(\mathrm{SnapOK}(S')) indicates that all required gate predicates are satisfied across the corridor κ ladder and probe adequacy is satisfied, yielding a promotable witness plan under the existing semantic context.
(\mathrm{SnapNEAR}(S')) indicates that hard legality predicates pass, but quantitative gates fail and admissible repairs remain. SnapNEAR is non-promotable evidence and must include admissible next actions.
(\mathrm{SnapFAIL}(R)) yields a refusal object (R) certifying that repair cannot succeed under current budgets and admissible transforms, with classification and evidence sufficient for deterministic escalation (tunnel, obstruction compilation, portal, or policy change).
Snap is scoped: it may tighten corridors, refine κ, expand probes, and substitute admissible certified methods. It may not change ConvBundleFP semantics or policy constraints; it may only expose missing bridge requirements.
Ch09.A1.b — SnapOK / SnapNEAR / SnapFAIL
Snap outcomes are defined by precise predicates.
SnapOK predicate. SnapOK holds iff:
all hard legality predicates pass (typing, scope, policy admissibility, convention coverage),
for every required UCW on the goal, tri-gate residual curves satisfy corridor gates across κ ladder,
probe adequacy requirements are satisfied and recorded,
representability obligations are satisfied across κ,
any required auxiliary gates (regression/stress) mandated by policy for the goal’s risk class are satisfied or explicitly delegated to subsequent jobs with pinned obligations.
SnapOK produces an updated state whose witness set is promotable. SnapOK does not itself promote; it delivers a promotable evidence state suitable for receipt binding and promotion.
SnapNEAR predicate. SnapNEAR holds iff:
hard legality predicates pass,
at least one quantitative gate fails (face/spin/projection/aux), but
there exists at least one admissible repair action within remaining budgets that is predicted to reduce defect, and
no semantic conflicts (ConvFP mismatch without bridge, policy forbiddance) block further progress.
SnapNEAR must include: minimal failing gate(s), best-achieved residual curves, remaining budgets, and an admissible action plan. SnapNEAR is never promotable and cannot be used as semantic evidence.
SnapFAIL predicate. SnapFAIL holds iff:
hard legality predicates fail (and are not repairable within Snap scope), or
all admissible quantitative repair actions within budgets fail to produce defect reduction, or
plateau or non-identifiability is detected under adequate probes and admissible refinements, or
progress is blocked by policy constraints or missing bridges that are not admissible within current policy.
SnapFAIL emits a refusal object with classification:POLICY_BLOCK, CONVENTION_CONFLICT, UNCERTAINTY_PLATEAU, HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION, KERNEL_MISMATCH, BUDGET_EXHAUSTED, or ADMISSIBILITY_FAIL.
Ch09.A1.c — Monotone Defect Rule
Snap is governed by a strict monotone defect rule: repair steps must strictly decrease a defect functional without altering semantic commitments.
Define the defect functional as:[\mathfrak D(S) ;:=; w_f\cdot \Delta_f(S) ;+; w_s\cdot \Delta_s(S) ;+; w_\Pi\cdot \Delta_\Pi(S) ;+; w_a\cdot \Delta_a(S) ;+; w_c\cdot \Delta_c(S),]where:
(\Delta_f(S)=\max(0,\max_{\kappa} r_{\square}(\kappa)-\varepsilon_{\text{face}})),
(\Delta_s(S)=\max(0,\max_{\kappa} s_{\square}(\kappa)-\varepsilon_{\text{spin}})),
(\Delta_\Pi(S)=\max(0,\max_{\kappa} r_{\Pi}(\kappa)-\varepsilon_{\Pi})),
(\Delta_a(S)) aggregates auxiliary gate violations (stress/regression if required),
(\Delta_c(S)) is an optional cost term (latency, complexity) used only for scheduler guidance; it must not alter acceptance semantics.
A Snap action (S\to S') is admissible only if:[\mathfrak D(S') < \mathfrak D(S),]under a deterministic measurement regime and within fixed context hashes. Any action that reduces defect by widening gates or relaxing policies is forbidden; such changes produce new corridor/policy identities and must be explicit outside Snap scope.
The defect rule is the mechanism that prevents infinite loops and enforces progress or refusal.
Ch09.A1.d — Budgets
Snap budgets ensure termination and auditability. Snap consumes a Snap budget record:[\mathcal B_{\mathrm{snap}} := (\mathrm{max_iters},;\mathrm{max_kappa_steps},;\mathrm{max_probe_expansions},;\mathrm{max_method_swaps},;\mathrm{max_rewrite_ops},;\mathrm{max_tunnel_escalations}),]together with corridor and policy budgets. Budgets are interpreted as hard constraints:
Iteration bound. Snap performs at most max_iters repair iterations.
κ escalation bound. Snap escalates κ at most max_kappa_steps times and never beyond corridor maxima.
Probe expansion bound. Snap expands probes at most max_probe_expansions times, subject to policy and corridor admissibility.
Method swap bound. Snap swaps certified methods at most max_method_swaps times, restricted to admissible method ids.
Rewrite bound. Snap performs at most max_rewrite_ops canonical route rewrites (scope-safe and legality-preserving).
Escalation bound. Snap may suggest or trigger at most max_tunnel_escalations tunnel escalation jobs; it cannot perform tunnel synthesis unless policy explicitly includes AUTO_TUNNEL as an admissible in-Snap action, in which case tunnel events must be logged and defect reduction must still hold.
Budget consumption is recorded in Snap traces and audited by verifiers. Budget exhaustion yields SnapFAIL with BUDGET_EXHAUSTED and complete evidence.
Ch09.A2 — Encode
Ch09.A2.a — Snap Trace Schema
A Snap trace is a canonical, replay-addressable record capturing every decision and evidence update. A Snap trace record includes:
Header.
snap_version,
snap_id (hash of trace record),
goal_id,
start_ctx_hashes (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier, schema hashes).
Iteration records. For each iteration (t=1,\dots,T):
action_id (refine κ, expand probes, swap method, rewrite route, adjust corridor within tightening-only rules),
pre_state_hash and post_state_hash,
pre_defect and post_defect,
pre_residual_summaries and post_residual_summaries,
pre_budget and post_budget,
evidence_refs (UCW ids, evaluation receipts, probe bundle ids),
decision_reason referencing minimal failing predicate(s).
Outcome record.
outcome ∈ {SnapOK,SnapNEAR,SnapFAIL},
final_residual_curves (hash pointers; raw curves optionally cached but recomputable),
final_classification and refusal_id if failure,
next_actions list with explicit admissibility constraints.
Snap traces must be deterministic: given the same input state and context hashes, the trace must be reproducible, or else Snap is not promotable as a repair mechanism.
Ch09.A2.b — Corridor Chain Schema
Snap may produce corridor tightening steps; these are recorded as corridor chain events.
Corridor chain event. Each corridor change event includes:
corr_pre_hash, corr_post_hash,
change_kind (tighten gates, strengthen κ ladder, restrict methods),
justification (defect reduction evidence),
policy_bind_check result (policy still admits new corridor).
No widening representation. Any corridor widening attempt is illegal within Snap and must be represented only as an explicit new corridor identity outside Snap scope, requiring separate governance and re-verification.
Chain integrity. Corridor chain is hash-chained and included as segments in WordNF (or linked from Snap trace), ensuring that route identity includes corridor evolution where it affects verification.
Chain replay. Verifier can replay corridor chain validity: it checks that each corridor transition is legal and that it does not change semantics or violate policy binding.
Ch09.A2.c — Decision Logs
Decision logs record predicate evaluation outcomes and why specific repairs were chosen.
Minimal failing predicate record. For each iteration, record the earliest failing hard predicate or the dominant failing gate.
Repair selection rule record. Record the deterministic selection rule used (e.g., choose the action with maximal predicted defect decrease per cost, subject to admissibility).
Admissibility record. Record which actions were admissible and which were blocked by policy, corridor, or convention scope.
Counterexample record. Record counterexample objects (minimal failing κ/probe/subword) used to justify decisions.
Decision logs are mandatory for auditability; Snap is invalid if it makes unlogged decisions affecting outcomes.
Ch09.A2.d — Proof Obligations
Snap produces explicit proof obligations for promotion or for escalation.
Obligation list. Enumerate remaining obligations required to reach SnapOK:
which UCWs fail and on which gates,
which probes are inadequate,
which κ steps remain,
which auxiliary gates remain.
Obligation provenance. Each obligation links to the relevant schema keys and gate stack ids.
Obligation admissibility. Each obligation includes an admissibility check: whether it is achievable under current policy and corridor.
Obligation export. Obligations are encoded as a job plan artifact that can be scheduled deterministically.
Ch09.A3 — Prove
Ch09.A3.a — Snap Soundness
Snap soundness is the property that Snap does not create false promotions and does not silently alter semantics.
Context preservation. Snap does not modify ConvBundleFP or semantic conventions; if convention mismatch is detected, Snap returns SnapFAIL with bridge requirement, not a silent adjustment.
No silent widening. Snap never widens corridor gates. Any action that would make a failing gate pass by increasing ε is forbidden. Tightening is permitted only when it does not alter semantic meaning and remains admissible under policy.
Witness recomputation. Snap relies on recomputable evidence. Any claimed defect reduction must be supported by recomputed residual curves under pinned contexts and adequate probes.
Promotion safety. If Snap returns SnapOK, then the resulting state satisfies the acceptance predicates required for promotion under the pinned context. SnapOK is therefore sufficient for subsequent receipt binding and promotion, subject to any required stress/regression gates.
Ch09.A3.b — NEAR Semantics
SnapNEAR is a certified diagnostic state with precise meaning.
Hard legality passed. SnapNEAR implies that typing, scope coverage, policy admissibility, and convention coverage are satisfied; the failure is quantitative or evidentiary (gates, probes).
Progress possible. SnapNEAR implies that admissible repair actions remain within budgets and are expected to reduce defect.
Non-promotability. SnapNEAR cannot be bound into a promoted receipt as evidence of equivalence or correctness; it may only be stored as candidate evidence and used for scheduling repairs.
Deterministic next actions. SnapNEAR must provide a finite deterministic set of next actions with explicit obligations, enabling reproducible scheduling.
Ch09.A3.c — FAIL Semantics
SnapFAIL is a certified refusal state.
Definitive under budgets. SnapFAIL means that, under the pinned policy/corridor and within declared Snap budgets, there is no admissible sequence of Snap actions that can produce SnapOK.
Classification completeness. SnapFAIL includes a refusal class that distinguishes:
plateau uncertainty (non-identifiability),
drift (semantic inconsistency or canonicalization instability),
holonomy obstruction (stable spin residual),
kernel mismatch (face residual),
policy block (forbidden operations or insufficient budgets),
convention conflict (missing bridge),
admissibility failure (invalid inputs/parameters).
Evidence completeness. SnapFAIL includes best-achieved residual curves, plateau/drift evidence, and minimal counterexample objects.
Escalation readiness. SnapFAIL includes admissible next actions for escalation: schedule tunnel synthesis, schedule obstruction compilation, schedule portal analysis, or request policy/corridor changes.
Ch09.A3.d — Termination Guarantees
Snap must terminate within finite time and finite steps, producing SnapOK, SnapNEAR, or SnapFAIL.
Budgeted termination. Because all actions are bounded by explicit budgets (κ steps, probe expansions, method swaps, rewrites, iterations), Snap terminates.
Monotone defect termination. Because defect must strictly decrease on admissible actions, Snap cannot cycle. Once no admissible action decreases defect, Snap must terminate with SnapNEAR (if further actions exist) or SnapFAIL (if blocked).
Deterministic choice termination. Action selection is deterministic among admissible options; nondeterministic tie-breaking is forbidden for promotable Snap.
Exhaustion termination. When any relevant budget dimension is exhausted, Snap terminates and records exhaustion evidence.
Ch09.A4 — Extract
Ch09.A4.a — Snap Summaries
Snap summaries are compact extraction artifacts.
Outcome summary. SnapOK/NEAR/FAIL, with classification and promotable status.
Gate summary. Max residuals and margins to gates across κ for face/spin/projection.
Budget summary. Declared vs consumed budgets for κ steps, probes, method swaps, rewrites.
Context summary. ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier hashes.
Ch09.A4.b — Repair Deltas
Repair deltas record what changed during Snap.
Route delta. WordNF segment differences: rewrites applied, method swaps recorded, κ steps added.
Corridor delta. Corridor chain changes (tightening only), with pre/post hashes and justification.
Probe delta. Probe schedule expansions and adequacy improvements, with probe bundle ids.
Evidence delta. Which UCWs moved from failing to passing, with residual curve comparisons.
Ch09.A4.c — Failure Reasons
Failure reasons are extracted from SnapFAIL refusal artifacts.
Minimal failing predicate id(s).
Minimal counterexample probes and κ level.
Plateau/drift/holonomy dominance evidence.
Policy and convention blocks and missing bridge requirements.
Ch09.A4.d — Next-Step Suggestions
Next-step suggestions are admissible action plans extracted deterministically.
Refinement plan. Increase κ or strengthen probe regime within corridor/policy.
Method plan. Swap to alternate certified evaluators or bounds admissible under policy.
Tunnel plan. If allowed, schedule tunnel synthesis/discovery with explicit budgets and required logging.
Discovery plan. If holonomy-dominant, schedule obstruction compilation; if conflict-dominant, schedule portal analysis; if policy-dominant, request policy bridge or policy change.
B. Flower
Ch09.B1 — Spec
Ch09.B1.a — Repair Order
Snap follows a fixed repair order to prevent arbitrary hacking.
Context validation first. Validate typing, scope coverage, ConvBundleFP coverage, policy admissibility. If any fail, Snap refuses immediately with missing bridge or policy block; no numeric repair is attempted.
Refinement second. Attempt κ escalation along corridor ladder and recompute residual curves.
