Probe adequacy third. If plateau or ambiguity is detected, expand probes according to admissible probe schedule.
Method substitution fourth. Swap to alternate certified evaluators/bounds within admissible method set, prioritizing those with existing transport witnesses.
Route rewrite fifth. Apply scope-safe WordNF rewrites justified by existing commutation witnesses.
Escalation sixth. If repairs cannot succeed, emit SnapNEAR or SnapFAIL with explicit escalation plan to tunnel or discovery passes.
Repair order is deterministic and is recorded in Snap traces.
Ch09.B1.b — Legal Transforms
Snap is limited to a bounded set of legal transforms.
κ escalation within corridor ladder.
probe expansion within probe regime rules.
certified method swaps within admissible method ids.
route rewrites under existing commutation rules and scope compatibility.
corridor tightening (never widening), only when permitted by policy and when it preserves semantic conventions.
Any transform outside this set is illegal within Snap and must be scheduled as a separate governed job (tunnel synthesis, bridge creation, policy change).
Ch09.B1.c — Route Rewrites
Route rewrites are changes to the route WordNF that preserve semantics under witnessed commutations.
Rewrites must be justified by commutation witnesses or declared equivalence laws in the lens schema.
Rewrites cannot cross convention boundaries or policy boundaries without explicit bridges.
Rewrites cannot erase corridor-changing events; they preserve auditability.
Rewrites produce new WordNF hashes and must be recorded as rewrite records; reuse of old UCWs requires re-verification unless witnesses are explicitly reusable via compositionality.
Ch09.B1.d — Escalation to Tunnel
Escalation to tunnel is the boundary between repair and discovery.
Tunnel admissibility. Tunneling is admissible only when policy allows it and when failure is not due to hard context mismatch.
Trigger conditions. Plateau above gates under adequate probes, repeated method swap failure, or cost defect domination may trigger tunnel scheduling.
Logging requirement. Tunnel events must be logged as segments with pre/post corridor hashes and defect measures.
Transport witness requirement. Any tunneled chart must be linked back to baseline by a transport UCW; otherwise the tunnel result remains a candidate and cannot be activated as truth.
Ch09.B2 — Encode
Ch09.B2.a — Gate-Run Segments
Gate-run segments record verification evaluations used by Snap.
Gate stack id.
κ ladder id and explicit κ list used.
probe bundle id and adequacy metadata pointer.
residual curve outputs (as evidence pointers) and summary maxima.
Gate-run segments are part of WordNF segment streams and are replayable.
Ch09.B2.b — Rewrite Records
Rewrite records capture route changes.
Pre-WordNF hash and post-WordNF hash.
rewrite rule id and justification witness id.
scope compatibility evidence (ConvBundleFP scope).
affected obligations list (which UCWs must be recomputed).
Rewrite records are immutable and used by auditors to reconstruct repair sequences.
Ch09.B2.c — Parameter Deltas
Parameter deltas record changes in κ, probes, method parameters, and budgets across iterations.
κ delta: which κ values were added.
probe delta: which probes were added and why.
method delta: which evaluator/bound method ids changed.
budget delta: which budget units were consumed.
Parameter deltas are required for deterministic replay and for evidence comparison.
Ch09.B2.d — Replay Seeds
Replay seeds pin any nondeterministic components of repair.
If probe generation uses randomness, seeds are pinned and included in probe bundle ids.
If method selection uses tie-breaking, tie-breaking is deterministic or seed-pinned.
If search for candidates is invoked under AUTO_TUNNEL, enumerator order and seeds are pinned and logged.
Seeds are included in segment commitments and therefore affect WordNF identity when they influence outcomes.
Ch09.B3 — Prove
Ch09.B3.a — Defect Decrease Certificate
A defect decrease certificate is required for each successful Snap action.
It records pre/post defect values (\mathfrak D(S),\mathfrak D(S')).
It records the residual curves and gates used to compute defect components.
It records admissibility evidence: action was legal under policy/corridor and within scope.
It records that defect decrease did not result from silent widening, which is verified by corridor hash checks and explicit gate threshold checks.
Defect decrease certificates prevent Snap from “fixing” failures by changing semantics or acceptance regimes.
Ch09.B3.b — Legality Preservation
Snap must preserve legality of the route and context.
Hard legality predicates remain satisfied; if a repair action introduces hard failure, the action is rejected.
Route rewrites preserve typing and scope coverage; otherwise they are illegal.
Method swaps preserve policy admissibility and corridor admissibility.
Corridor tightening preserves policy binding; if tightening yields a corridor that policy cannot support, the action is illegal.
Ch09.B3.c — No Silent Widening
No silent widening is enforced as a formal proof obligation.
Corridor gates cannot increase during Snap.
κ ladder cannot be weakened; it can be strengthened by adding higher κ requirements.
Probe adequacy cannot be downgraded; added probes can strengthen adequacy.
Any attempt to relax acceptance criteria yields refusal ILLEGAL_WIDENING_ATTEMPT and is recorded in the trace.
Ch09.B3.d — Rewrite Equivalence
Rewrite equivalence ensures that route rewrites preserve semantic intent.
A rewrite is legal only if there exists a commutation witness or a declared equivalence law certifying that the pre-route and post-route are semantically equivalent under pinned context.
If rewrite introduces new commutation squares, those squares require new UCWs before promotion.
Rewrite does not allow dropping loop closure obligations; loops introduced by rewrites must be closed or classified.
If rewrite equivalence cannot be witnessed under budgets, Snap must refuse or return NEAR with explicit obligation to generate needed UCWs.
C. Cloud
Ch09.C1 — Spec
Ch09.C1.a — Defect Functionals
Defect functionals are the quantitative drivers of Snap progress.
Defect aggregates gate violations and optionally cost metrics.
Defect uses maxima across κ, not single κ values, unless corridor explicitly permits weighted aggregation.
Defect is computed under fixed context; changing context changes defect meaning and is not permitted within Snap.
Defect is used for repair selection and termination; it is not semantic evidence.
Ch09.C1.b — Thresholds
Thresholds are corridor gates and auxiliary gates.
Face/spin/projection gates are mandatory for semantic promotions.
Auxiliary thresholds may be required for high-risk promotions (stress/regression).
Threshold histories are tracked to prevent silent changes.
Threshold mismatch across iterations is forbidden unless corridor tightening is explicitly logged and hash-chained.
Ch09.C1.c — Plateau Detection
Plateau detection determines when additional κ refinement or probe expansion will not improve outcomes.
Plateau predicate applies to residual curves; plateau above gates indicates non-identifiability or obstruction.
Plateau detection requires adequate probes or must be classified as probe inadequacy.
Plateau under stable face but failing spin indicates holonomy obstruction.
Plateau detection drives escalation: tunnel, obstruction compilation, portal.
Ch09.C1.d — Refusal Categories
Snap refusals are categorized to preserve evidence and guide escalation.
UNCERTAINTY_PLATEAU: inadequate probes or method insufficiency.
ALIAS_DRIFT: drift detected under fixed ConvFP or canonicalization instability.
HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION: stable spin residual fails gates.
KERNEL_MISMATCH: face residual fails gates without holonomy dominance.
POLICY_BLOCK: required actions forbidden or budgets insufficient.
CONVENTION_CONFLICT: ConvFP mismatch without admissible bridge.
ADMISSIBILITY_FAIL: invalid inputs or parameters outside admissible regions.
Refusal categories are not subjective; they are derived from evidence and legality predicates.
Ch09.C2 — Encode
Ch09.C2.a — Residual Curves
Residual curves are stored as evidence and recomputable.
κ-indexed maxima for face/spin/projection.
curve provenance: WordNF hash, gate stack id, probe bundle id.
curve comparisons across iterations for defect decrease certificates.
storage as canonical numeric strings sufficient for deterministic comparison.
Ch09.C2.b — Threshold Histories
Threshold histories track corridor gates across Snap.
record corridor hash per iteration.
record explicit gate values per iteration and verify consistency.
record whether any tightening occurred (never widening).
record justification and defect reduction evidence for tightening events.
Ch09.C2.c — Adequacy Histories
Adequacy histories track probe adequacy evolution.
probe bundle ids per iteration.
adequacy metadata per iteration (coverage, boundary probes).
adequacy pass/fail decisions.
adequacy-driven escalations and their outcomes.
Ch09.C2.d — Refusal Payloads
Refusal payloads are structured and replayable.
minimal failing predicate ids and counterexample objects.
best-achieved residual curves and plateau/drift evidence.
budgets consumed and blocked admissible actions.
admissible next actions with explicit requirements (tunnel scheduling, obstruction compilation, portal, policy bridge).
Ch09.C3 — Prove
Ch09.C3.a — Plateau ⇒ Uncertainty
If residual curves plateau above gates under stable semantics and adequate probes, Snap must classify as uncertainty and refuse promotion.
Plateau evidence is stored and replayable.
Snap may propose probe escalation or carrier escalation if admissible.
If escalation is blocked by policy, classify as policy block with plateau evidence.
Plateau classification cannot be overridden by increasing ε gates within Snap.
Ch09.C3.b — Drift ⇒ Alias
If cross-κ behavior or replay behavior indicates semantic drift under fixed ConvFP, Snap classifies as drift (alias drift) and refuses.
Drift includes canonicalization instability, ordering nondeterminism, or scope gaps.
Drift triggers ConvFP remediation or migration, not numeric refinement.
Drift evidence is stored and linked to affected routes and operators.
Drift quarantines affected edges until resolved.
Ch09.C3.c — Holonomy ⇒ Kernel/Portal
If face residual is controlled but spin residual fails stably, Snap classifies as holonomy obstruction.
Holonomy obstruction triggers obstruction compilation for Tier-0 candidates if the domain admits missing moduli.
If conflict proofs indicate intrinsic obstruction beyond moduli, Snap triggers portal escalation.
Holonomy evidence includes loop maps, spin residual curves, and boundary probes when required.
Holonomy failures are not repaired by method swaps alone unless a tunneling step changes representation to one that closes the loop, which must be logged and witnessed.
Ch09.C3.d — Confidence Progression
Confidence progression is the governance rule that Snap must obey.
heuristic evidence may guide repair but cannot be promoted.
near evidence is diagnostic and requires explicit repair plans.
certified evidence requires tri-gate satisfaction and adequacy.
promoted evidence additionally requires stress/regression gates where mandated.
Snap can move evidence upward only by adding certified witnesses; it cannot promote by relaxing obligations.
Ch09.C4 — Extract
Ch09.C4.a — Refusal Packs
Refusal packs provide minimal, replayable evidence for failures.
refusal receipt and refusal payload.
associated WordNF and segment stream.
best-achieved residual curves and probe bundles.
budget consumption logs and blocked action list.
Refusal packs are stored for future remediation and discovery triggers.
Ch09.C4.b — Evidence Bundles
Evidence bundles package SnapOK or SnapNEAR results for downstream use.
pins and witness roots for achieved UCWs.
corridor/policy/ConvBundleFP context hashes.
Snap trace and defect decrease certificates.
explicit remaining obligations (if NEAR).
Ch09.C4.c — Regression Triggers
Regression triggers are extracted from Snap outcomes.
any corridor tightening or method swaps trigger regression.
any route rewrites that change WordNF trigger commutation regression for affected squares.
any tunnel escalation triggers regression on integrated charts.
regression suites are listed with required coverage and scheduling priority.
Ch09.C4.d — Stress Triggers
Stress triggers are extracted for high-risk repairs.
tunnel-derived repairs require adversarial stress near boundaries.
bridge-related repairs require stress on bridge commutation and loop closure.
portal-related escalations require stress on new carrier or schema boundaries.
stress suite keys and required evidence are included in next-action suggestions.
D. Fractal
Ch09.D1 — Spec
Ch09.D1.a — Snap as Compiler Pass
Snap is a compiler pass that transforms a failing proof attempt into a certified proof attempt or into a structured refusal.
Inputs: goal + route + context + evidence + budgets.
Outputs: SnapOK/SnapNEAR/SnapFAIL and associated trace and obligation plans.
Determinism: pass behavior is deterministic under pinned context and tie-breaking rules.
Compositionality: Snap outputs are consumable by receipt algebra; Snap traces are addressable evidence objects.
Ch09.D1.b — Snap Caching
Snap caching stores previous repair outcomes to avoid repeated work.
Cache keys include: goal id, WordNF hash, ConvBundleFP hash, corridor hash, policy hash, verifier hash.
Cache values include: Snap outcome, trace summary, and witness/obligation pointers.
Cache is non-authoritative; verifier re-validates before reuse.
Cache invalidates on schema changes, drift alarms, corridor/policy updates, or verifier upgrades.
Ch09.D1.c — Snap Scheduling
Snap scheduling integrates Snap with demand-driven governance.
High-urgency failures schedule Snap jobs before expensive tunnel synthesis.
Snap jobs are prioritized by defect severity and by closeness to promotability.
Snap scheduling respects policy budgets and fairness constraints.
Snap scheduling emits escalation jobs when SnapFAIL indicates obstruction or portal necessity.
Ch09.D1.d — Snap Meta-Learning Hooks
Meta-learning hooks permit improvement of repair policies without compromising determinism.
Collect telemetry on which repair actions reduce defect most effectively under which contexts.
Learn deterministic heuristics for action selection; learned heuristics are pinned by version and become part of the planner policy, not silent runtime behavior.
Enforce that learned heuristics cannot bypass legality predicates or widen corridors; they only order admissible actions.
Learned updates trigger regression suites to ensure they do not change semantic outcomes.
Ch09.D2 — Encode
Ch09.D2.a — Snap Memo Keys
Snap memo keys uniquely identify a Snap job class.
goal id.
WordNF hash.
context hashes (ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor/verifier/schema).
budget record hash.
Memo keys are used for caching and for audit.
Ch09.D2.b — Invalidation Keys
Invalidation keys specify when cached Snap outcomes become unsafe.
schema version changes affecting any predicate or gate.
verifier version changes affecting UCW acceptance.
ConvFP/corridor/policy changes affecting scope or admissibility.
drift alarms, regression failures, or quarantines affecting any pinned dependency.
Invalidation emits explicit invalidation artifacts.
Ch09.D2.c — Scheduling Metrics
Scheduling metrics guide prioritization without affecting semantics.
defect magnitude and margin to gates.
estimated cost of repair actions.
urgency weights from demand scheduler.
risk class requiring stress/regression.
Metrics are metadata; they do not alter acceptance predicates.
Ch09.D2.d — Telemetry Tags
Telemetry tags record repair outcomes for meta-learning and governance.
action types used.
defect reduction achieved.
budgets consumed.
failure classifications encountered.
Telemetry is scoped and does not affect semantics; it is used to improve deterministic heuristics by governance-controlled updates.
Ch09.D3 — Prove
Ch09.D3.a — Cache Correctness
Snap cache correctness ensures caching cannot create false promotions.
cached SnapOK outcomes are reusable only if context hashes match and witnesses are still valid.
verifier recomputes required evidence before accepting cached results.
cache entries are invalidated deterministically.
cache correctness is audited by regression runs that replay cached outcomes.
Ch09.D3.b — Schedule Fairness
Fairness prevents starvation and uncontrolled focus.
scheduling uses deterministic priority plus fairness decay.
high-urgency tasks are prioritized but do not starve lower urgency tasks indefinitely.
repeated SnapFAIL tasks are deprioritized unless new admissible actions appear (new bridges, new methods, policy changes).
fairness is auditable and does not affect semantic acceptance.
Ch09.D3.c — Priority Correctness
Priority correctness ensures scheduling aligns with declared objectives.
prioritize repairs that unlock promotion of high-demand nodes/edges.
prioritize repairs that reduce repeated failures across many dependent tasks.
prioritize repairs that resolve drift alarms or remove quarantines.
prioritize repairs that clarify whether an obstruction indicates Tier-0 modulus or portal.
Ch09.D3.d — Safe Automation Constraints
Automation constraints prevent Snap from acting as an unbounded heuristic.
Snap cannot call unrestricted search or oracle methods unless explicitly permitted by policy and fully logged.
Snap cannot widen corridors or relax policies.
Snap must terminate under budgets and record all actions.
Snap outcomes cannot be promoted without verifying tri-gate and auxiliary gates under pinned context.
Ch09.D4 — Extract
Ch09.D4.a — Macro Repair Templates
Macro repair templates are reusable Snap plans for common failure patterns.
face-gate failures with stable spin: focus on method swaps and commutation witness completion.
spin-gate failures with stable face: focus on loop map analysis and obstruction triggers.
projection failures: focus on representability projector adjustments or ConvFP remediation.
policy blocks: focus on bridge requirements and policy changes.
Templates include explicit budgets and admissible action ordering.
Ch09.D4.b — Best-Known Repairs
Best-known repairs are extracted from successful Snap traces.
minimal action sequences yielding SnapOK.
context patterns where the sequence applies.
witness packs produced by the sequence.
regression/stress obligations satisfied or required.
Ch09.D4.c — Blocked Regions
Blocked regions are extracted from repeated SnapFAIL outcomes.
list of edges/nodes failing under policy constraints.
list of missing bridges or forbidden tunnels.
list of plateau regions requiring new carriers or probes.
list of holonomy obstructions requiring Tier-0 or portals.
Ch09.D4.d — Roadmap Lists
Roadmap lists convert Snap failure evidence into prioritized development tasks.
add missing evaluators/charts to satisfy atlas roots.
promote required bridges.
increase certified method coverage for plateau regions.
extend probe regimes for adequacy in branch-sensitive scopes.
Roadmap lists are deterministic extraction artifacts linked to refusal packs and demand signals.
Ch09 — Snap Operator: Corridor Tightening and Deterministic Repair
Ch10 — Tunneling and Portal Bridges (Repair + Acceleration)
A. Square
Ch10.A1 — Spec
Ch10.A1.a — Tunnel Definition
A tunnel is a controlled, explicit, and witness-bearing change of representation or method that is invoked to (i) reduce verification defect under a fixed semantic intent, or (ii) accelerate certified evaluation under fixed semantic commitments. Tunneling is not a heuristic side effect; it is a first-class operation that appears in route identity and that is governed by policy, corridor, and convention scope.
Formally, let a verification attempt be represented by a state (S) with pinned context (X=(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V)) and route word (W). A tunnel is a typed operator[\tau:; S \to S']whose defining property is that it changes the representation regime used by the route while preserving the claim’s semantic intent. In the metro graph, tunneling typically introduces or activates a new chart or transport fragment and splices it into the route.
A tunnel has the following mandatory characteristics:
Explicit representation change. The tunnel modifies one or more of:
lens selection (move to a different lens family member),
chart selection (switch evaluator chart),
basis selection (change-of-basis),
method selection (switch certified method and tail-bound regime),
transport fragment selection (use a different route segment).
Context binding. The tunnel is interpreted under fixed ConvBundleFP and policy; a tunnel cannot silently change semantic conventions. If a convention change is required, that is a portal bridge action, not a tunnel.
Auditability. The tunnel produces a tunnel event record that commits pre/post corridor hashes and defect measures, and is embedded in WordNF as a segment.
Witness plan. The tunnel introduces witness obligations: transport UCWs linking new charts to baseline, loop closure obligations for any new cycles, representability obligations for new projectors, and any required stress/regression obligations for high-risk tunnel actions.
A tunnel may be invoked for repair (to close a commutation square or loop) or for acceleration (to reduce cost of certified evaluation), but in all cases it must respect the verification discipline: verified-only promotion, no silent drift, no hidden semantics.
Ch10.A1.b — Portal vs Tunnel
Tunnels and portals are distinct operations and are never conflated.
Tunnel. A tunnel changes representation or method while preserving semantic conventions and invariant schemas. It is an internal movement inside a fixed semantic regime.
Portal. A portal is required when the semantic regime itself must change to represent the intended object coherently. Portals introduce:
new carrier type,
new invariant schema (new observables or equivalence relation),
new semantic alphabet (e.g., monodromy class, genus change),
new convention regime requiring a bridge.
Bridge distinction. A portal bridge is an explicit bridge artifact connecting two incompatible semantic regimes. It is not a tunnel, because it changes semantic conventions or schema meaning. Portal bridges require their own UCWs and conflict/compatibility proofs.
Decision criterion. If failure arises from ConvFP mismatch or conflict proofs, the correct action is a portal bridge or refusal—not a tunnel. If failure arises from plateau or cost but remains within fixed conventions and schema, a tunnel may be admissible.
Safety invariant. A tunnel may not resolve a semantic conflict by implicitly changing conventions. Any such behavior is classified as drift and is forbidden.
Ch10.A1.c — Admissibility
Tunnel admissibility is a legality predicate evaluated before any tunnel execution.
A tunnel (\tau) is admissible in context (X) iff:
Policy admissibility. Policy (P) allows the tunnel operator family (\tau) and its search budgets, method families, and required probes. If policy forbids tunnel actions, the system must refuse or require a policy bridge.
Corridor admissibility. Corridor (C) declares that tunneling is allowed for the target class, and provides required κ schedule and gate obligations for verifying tunnel outcomes.
Convention admissibility. The ConvBundleFP scope covers all operators used by tunnel search and verification. If tunnel requires operators outside scope, a scope-expansion ConvFP record must be provided; otherwise tunnel is refused.
Carrier admissibility. The tunnel’s target representation must be defined for the carrier type and admissible region of the node. If not, tunnel requires portal escalation.
Budget admissibility. Search and verification budgets are sufficient to ensure termination and to produce a verified outcome or a refusal artifact; unbounded search is forbidden.
Admissibility failure yields a refusal with explicit missing requirements (policy permission, corridor allowances, scope coverage, or carrier limitations).
Ch10.A1.d — Corridor Impact
A tunnel affects corridor obligations in a controlled way.
No silent widening. Tunnels cannot widen corridor gates. Any use of a laxer corridor is an explicit corridor change and requires a new corridor identity and re-verification.
Tightening via better representation. Tunnels may reduce defect by moving to a representation with better convergence, enabling existing corridor gates to be satisfied at lower κ or with smaller residuals.
Evidence impact. After tunneling, residual curves and enclosure radii must be recomputed under the same corridor gates; defect reduction is evaluated under the same acceptance regime.
Risk impact. Tunneling increases risk class because it introduces new methods and representations. Therefore, high-risk tunnels require additional stress/regression gates for promotion, as mandated by policy.
Coverage impact. A tunnel may improve evidence under one corridor/policy but not another; coverage is context-indexed and must be recorded.
Ch10.A2 — Encode
Ch10.A2.a — Tunnel Event Schema
A tunnel event is a canonical, hash-addressed record embedded in WordNF segment streams. The minimal tunnel event schema includes:
Header.
tunnel_version,
tunnel_id (hash),
tau_id (tunnel operator family id),
scope (operator-family scope affected),
node_or_edge_target_id (what the tunnel is applied to).
Pre-state commitments.
word_pre_hash,
corr_pre_hash,
policy_hash,
conv_bundle_hash,
probe_hash,
defect_pre summary (dominant failing gate, residual maxima).
Action commitments.
enumerator_state (if search is involved),
candidate_id (chart candidate id or bridge candidate id),
method_ids, bound_ids, rounding_ids,
parameter_deltas (N, κ, basis, chart selection).
Post-state commitments.
word_post_hash,
corr_post_hash (should match pre unless corridor tightening is explicitly performed by Snap logic; corridor identity changes must be explicit),
defect_post summary,
new_chart_or_transport_ids.
Witness hooks.
required_witnesses list (transport UCW ids, loop UCW ids, projection stability witnesses),
verification_receipts produced (if any),
stress_regression_requirements (suite ids required before promotion).
Replay fields.
search_budget and consumed counters,
deterministic enumeration order id,
seed pins (only if policy permits randomness; otherwise absent),
environment pins if required.
Tunnel events are immutable; any change produces a different tunnel id and therefore a different route identity.
Ch10.A2.b — Pre/Post Hashes
Pre/post hashes bind tunnel actions to concrete contexts and prevent silent substitution.
Route hashes. word_pre_hash and word_post_hash bind route identity changes. A tunnel always changes route word unless it is a no-op, in which case it is rejected for failing defect reduction.
Context hashes. policy_hash and conv_bundle_hash must be unchanged by tunneling; if they differ, the action is not a tunnel but a portal or policy bridge action and must be classified accordingly.
Corridor hashes. Corridor hash should remain constant during tunneling, except when corridor tightening is performed explicitly within a Snap-controlled corridor evolution step. Any widening is forbidden.
Artifact hashes. New chart ids and transport ids are hash-addressed; activation of these artifacts is recorded separately by atlas registry updates and requires transport witness links.
Ch10.A2.c — Replay Fields
Replay fields ensure the tunnel is reproducible and auditable.
Deterministic enumeration id. Identifies the canonical ordering used for candidate exploration.
Enumerator state. Records the index of the candidate stream and the parameter search frontier. It must be sufficient to reproduce the same candidate selection.
Budget counters. Records how many candidates were tried, what caps were used (PSLQ coefficient caps, truncation depths), and which verification κ levels were executed.
Seed pins. If any nondeterminism exists and is permitted, seed pins are recorded and bound into the route identity. Otherwise, nondeterminism is forbidden.
Environment pins. If search or verification depends on numeric backend semantics, environment pins are recorded and bound.
Ch10.A2.d — Witness Hooks
Witness hooks connect tunnels to the verification system.
Transport witness hooks. Every tunnel that introduces a new chart must create or schedule a transport UCW linking the new chart to baseline charts.
Coherence hooks. If tunneling introduces new cycles in the chart graph, loop closure UCWs must be created to prevent path-dependent semantics.
Projection hooks. If tunneling uses a different canonicalization or projector regime, projection stability witnesses and ConvFP scope coverage checks are required.
Promotion hooks. Tunnels that yield useful charts produce promotion candidates and bind the required stress/regression suite obligations for those candidates to become active.
Witness hooks are mandatory; missing witness hooks means the tunnel result is non-integrable and remains quarantined.
Ch10.A3 — Prove
Ch10.A3.a — Tunnel Legality
Tunnel legality is a deterministic predicate. A tunnel event is legal iff:
Admissibility satisfied. Policy, corridor, and convention scope admit the tunnel operator family and its operations.
No semantic mutation. ConvBundleFP remains unchanged; any required convention change is represented as a portal bridge and is not permitted as a tunnel.
Replay determinism. The tunnel event contains sufficient replay fields to reproduce selection and verification; missing replay fields yields refusal.
Verification compliance. Any claims produced by the tunnel (e.g., chart equivalence, improved evaluation) are backed by certified verification steps or are explicitly labeled as candidates pending verification. A tunnel may not directly promote without verification evidence.
Logging completeness. Pre/post hashes, budget consumption, and witness hooks are complete and consistent.
Illegal tunnels are refused and recorded as refusal logs, preventing silent corruption.
Ch10.A3.b — Defect Reduction Requirement
A tunnel is admissible and successful only if it produces strict defect reduction under fixed gates.
Define defect functional (\mathfrak D) as in Snap theory. A tunnel from (S) to (S') is successful only if:[\mathfrak D(S') < \mathfrak D(S),]computed under the same corridor gates and under the same ConvBundleFP and policy.
Defect reduction must be supported by recomputed residual curves after integration of the tunneled representation.
Reducing defect by switching to heuristic evidence is forbidden for promotable outcomes; any heuristic evidence remains candidate-only.
No-op or worsening tunnels are recorded as failed candidates and count against budgets; repeated failures contribute to plateau detection and future pruning.
Ch10.A3.c — No Hidden Semantics
Tunneling must not introduce hidden semantic changes.
ConvFP invariance. The tunnel must not change ConvFP regime. If the tunnel requires a different branch policy or normalization, this must be declared as a portal bridge requirement.
Scope coverage. Any new operators introduced by the tunneled chart must be covered by the existing ConvBundleFP scope; otherwise tunnel is illegal or requires explicit scope expansion, which changes ConvBundleFP and therefore is not a tunnel.
Canonicalization invariance. If a new chart changes canonicalization behavior, this must be reflected in projection signatures pinned by ConvFP or corridor; otherwise it is drift and must be refused.
Bridge transparency. Any reconciliation between semantic regimes must be explicit and witnessed; tunnels cannot masquerade as bridges.
Ch10.A3.d — Policy Compliance
Policy compliance is mandatory and verifiable.
Allowed operator families. Tunnel operator family ids must be allowed by policy.
Budget compliance. Search and verification budgets must not exceed declared caps; consumed budgets are recorded.
Allowed methods/bounds. All method ids, tail-bound ids, and rounding ids used by tunnel verification must be allowed by policy and corridor.
Risk compliance. High-risk tunnels require stress/regression suite completion before promotion and activation; policy must declare these requirements.
Policy noncompliance yields refusal POLICY_BLOCK and blocks promotion.
Ch10.A4 — Extract
Ch10.A4.a — Tunnel Catalogs
Tunnel catalogs are indices of available tunnels and their outcomes.
Catalog entries. Each entry includes tunnel id, tunnel operator family id, scope, and context hashes.
Outcome summaries. Defect reduction achieved, residual maxima before/after, costs, and promotable status.
Integration status. Whether resulting charts are integrated into atlases with transport witnesses, whether active or quarantined.
Applicability. Which nodes/edges and which contexts the tunnel applies to; tunnel outcomes are context-indexed.
Ch10.A4.b — Admissibility Reports
Admissibility reports explain whether a tunnel is admissible and why.
Policy admissibility results (allowed/forbidden tunnel families, budgets).
Corridor admissibility results (allowed target classes, required κ schedules).
Convention scope coverage results (ConvBundleFP coverage).
Carrier admissibility results and stability region checks.
Reports are deterministic and used by schedulers to decide whether to attempt tunneling.
Ch10.A4.c — Tunnel Provenance
Tunnel provenance reconstructs how a tunnel result was discovered and verified.
Enumerator states and candidate logs.
Verification logs and residual curve comparisons.
Witness hooks and created transport UCWs.
Regression/stress results required for promotion and their statuses.
Provenance is essential for audit and for reproducible discovery.
Ch10.A4.d — Tunnel Diffs
Tunnel diffs compare tunnel outcomes or tunnel variants.
Pre/post route diffs: WordNF segment stream differences.
Method diffs: evaluator ids, bound ids, rounding ids.
Evidence diffs: residual curve changes, probe regime changes, classification changes.
Budget diffs: candidates tried, κ steps used, failure reasons for rejected candidates.
Tunnel diffs support pruning and selection of best tunnels.
B. Flower
Ch10.B1 — Spec
Ch10.B1.a — Tunnel Operator Family
Tunnel operators form a typed family of representation-change operators.
Chart tunnel operators. Switch evaluator charts or discover new charts within the same semantic regime (e.g., Apéry/binomial acceleration charts).
Basis tunnel operators. Change bases to improve stability or reduce complexity (e.g., switch to orthonormal basis, or to a basis with better conditioning).
Transform tunnel operators. Switch representation by applying transforms (Fourier/Mellin/Laplace) to move to a chart with faster convergence.
Carrier tunnel operators. Move to a different carrier type within the same schema when allowed (e.g., from pointwise function to distribution representation) without changing semantic conventions; this is admissible only if the schema declares carrier polymorphism.
Each tunnel operator family declares its admissibility constraints, expected defect reduction mechanism, and required witness hooks.
Ch10.B1.b — Route Splicing
Route splicing inserts a tunnel outcome into an existing route.
Splice location. A splice point is a segment boundary in WordNF where representation substitution is legal and where required commutation witnesses can be established.
Splice typing. The spliced fragment must be type-compatible with adjacent fragments; otherwise splicing is illegal and must be refused.
Splice scope. Splicing must not introduce operators outside scope; otherwise the route becomes ill-scoped.
Splice obligations. Splicing introduces new commutation squares and possibly new loops; witness obligations are enumerated explicitly and must be satisfied before promotion.
Ch10.B1.c — Chart Switching
Chart switching is the primary acceleration tunnel action.
Baseline anchor. Chart switching is meaningful only relative to baseline charts; accel charts must be linked to baseline by transport witnesses.
Switch conditions. A chart may be selected for activation only if it provides certified evaluation receipts and transport UCWs linking it to baseline.
Fallback discipline. If an accel chart is unavailable or quarantined, the planner must fall back to baseline; no silent switching to heuristic charts is permitted.
Activation governance. Activation of charts is governed by atlas registry and requires regression/stress obligations for high-risk charts.
Ch10.B1.d — Canonical Tunnel Placement
Canonical tunnel placement prevents non-reproducible ad hoc tunneling.
Placement rule. A tunnel is placed only at a canonical splice point determined by deterministic heuristics: earliest segment where defect is dominated and where substitution reduces defect without adding scope conflicts.
Witness feasibility rule. A tunnel placement is permitted only if witness hooks can be satisfied within budgets; otherwise placement is rejected.
Scope rule. Tunnels are not placed across convention boundaries; bridges handle convention transitions.
Minimality rule. Prefer minimal tunnel placements that reduce defect while introducing the fewest new witness obligations.
Ch10.B2 — Encode
Ch10.B2.a — WordNF Segment Stream
Tunnel operations are encoded directly in WordNF.
Tunnel events are segments with the tunnel event schema, including pre/post hashes and witness hooks.
Splice records and route rewrite records are segments or references linked to tunnel events.
Chart switching is represented by explicit chart id substitution in subsequent evaluation segments.
Tunnel-induced corridor tightening (if any) is represented as Snap-controlled corridor chain segments and is never implicit.
Ch10.B2.b — Splice Records
Splice records encode how a route was modified.
splice_id (hash), word_pre_hash, word_post_hash.
splice point identifier (segment index and scope).
inserted fragment identifier (new chart id or route fragment WordNF hash).
justification evidence: admissibility check results and expected witness obligations.
Splice records ensure auditability and replay.
Ch10.B2.c — Chart Activation
Chart activation is recorded as an atlas governance event.
activation event references chart id, node id, root assignment, and context coverage.
activation requires transport witness ids linking chart to baseline.
activation requires certified evaluation receipts and policy compliance checks.
activation requires stress/regression suites when mandated; activation is blocked until suites pass.
Ch10.B2.d — Transport Link Creation
Every tunnel chart must be linked into atlas coherence by transport edges.
Create transport edge baseline↔new_chart with UCW witness and WordNF binding.
If multiple baselines exist, establish sufficient transport links to avoid chart islands.
Create loop closure witnesses for new cycles introduced by the link graph.
Bind links into receipts and atlas registry; links remain candidates until verified.
Ch10.B3 — Prove
Ch10.B3.a — Splice Equivalence
Splice equivalence is the theorem that route splicing preserves semantic intent when witnessed.
If a splice replaces a subroute fragment with an alternative fragment and if commutation witnesses certify equivalence of the substituted fragment under pinned context, then the overall route semantics is preserved.
Any new commutation squares introduced by the splice must be witnessed; absence blocks promotion.
Splice equivalence is scoped; it holds only under pinned corridor and conventions.
Splice equivalence is auditable: it is recorded in WordNF and witness packs.
Ch10.B3.b — Transport Witness After Tunnel
Transport witnesses are mandatory after tunnel.
After introducing a new chart, transport UCWs must certify equivalence between new chart evaluation and baseline evaluation.
Transport witnesses must satisfy tri-gate and any auxiliary gates required by policy.
Without transport witnesses, the new chart cannot be activated for promoted semantics and remains candidate-only.
Transport witnesses enable safe substitution and route minimization.
Ch10.B3.c — Coherence Preservation
Tunneling must preserve atlas and metro coherence.
Tunneling must not break existing commutation closures; any affected squares are re-verified by regression.
New charts must integrate into the coherence graph; chart islands are forbidden for promoted status.
Loop closure must be re-checked for cycles; new cycles must not introduce holonomy failure beyond gates.
If coherence cannot be preserved under budgets, tunnel results are quarantined and recorded as failed candidates.
Ch10.B3.d — Rollback Proof
Rollback proof ensures that unsuccessful or harmful tunnels can be undone without corrupting history.
Rollback is recorded as a governance event: deactivation/quarantine of chart and reversion of route planner pointers.
Rollback does not delete evidence; it preserves tunnel event logs and refusal logs for audit.
Rollback preserves fallback availability: baseline charts remain active, ensuring continued evaluability.
Rollback is replay-verifiable: receipts and witness packs confirm that post-rollback routing uses only certified and non-quarantined charts.
Ch10.B4 — Extract
Ch10.B4.a — Before/After Route
Before/after route extraction provides the concrete impact of tunneling.
WordNF segment stream before tunneling.
WordNF segment stream after tunneling, highlighting tunnel and splice segments.
list of substituted charts/methods and any new witness obligations.
context hashes confirming no semantic regime change occurred.
Ch10.B4.b — Speedup Metrics
Speedup metrics are extracted as non-semantic metadata.
certified cost before and after (latency model, digits-per-term).
κ level required to meet gates before and after.
number of terms/iterations in certified evaluation before and after.
policy compliance costs (stress/regression overhead) for activation.
Ch10.B4.c — Defect Metrics
Defect metrics quantify repair success.
defect functional pre/post.
gate margin improvements for face/spin/projection.
probe adequacy improvements if probes were expanded.
classification changes (e.g., from UNCERTAINTY to ALIAS).
Ch10.B4.d — Audit Trail
Audit trail extraction provides all evidence required to validate tunnel actions.
tunnel event record and splice record.
candidate logs and verification logs.
transport UCWs and evaluation receipts.
regression/stress results and activation/deactivation governance events.
C. Cloud
Ch10.C1 — Spec
Ch10.C1.a — Tunnel Risks
Tunneling introduces risks that must be controlled.
Semantic drift risk. New charts may rely on hidden conventions; prohibited by ConvBundleFP scope checks and ConvFP invariance.
False relation risk. Discovery methods (PSLQ, heuristic fits) may produce spurious identities; controlled by verified-only promotion and interval/tail verification.
Non-identifiability risk. Improved performance may hide non-identifiability; controlled by probe adequacy and adversarial probes.
Ill-conditioning risk. Certain representations may be numerically ill-conditioned; controlled by perturbation robustness tests and stability region constraints.
Risk is classified and tagged; high-risk tunnels require stress/regression coverage.
Ch10.C1.b — Non-Identifiability
Non-identifiability occurs when evidence cannot decide equivalence under current probes/corridor.
Tunneling may reduce cost but not resolve non-identifiability; plateau detection distinguishes these cases.
Non-identifiability triggers probe expansion or carrier escalation rather than further tunneling unless tunneling changes the observable regime in a way that increases identifiability while remaining within the same schema.
If non-identifiability persists after admissible tunnels, the correct outcome is refusal with evidence or portal escalation.
Non-identifiability evidence is preserved in refusal logs to guide future development.
Ch10.C1.c — Adversarial Tests
Adversarial tests are required for high-risk tunnels.
boundary probes near branch cuts and singularities.
ordering tie probes for canonicalization.
perturbation probes to test stability under small changes.
worst-case probes to test commutation and loop closure sensitivity.
Adversarial tests are policy-scoped and must be passed before promotion of high-risk tunnel outcomes.
Ch10.C1.d — Refusal Triggers
Tunneling must refuse rather than silently proceed when conditions are violated.
policy forbids tunnel operator family.
ConvBundleFP scope gap for new operators.
failure to verify candidate under certified evidence (interval/tail mismatch).
defect reduction failure: no tunnel candidate reduces defect.
drift detection: inconsistent behavior across κ or across replay under fixed ConvFP.
Refusal triggers yield structured refusal logs and block activation.
Ch10.C2 — Encode
Ch10.C2.a — Stress Suite Tags
Tunnel outcomes carry stress suite tags.
risk class determines required stress suite ids.
stress suite ids are pinned by policy and referenced by tunnel event record.
stress results are stored as witness packs and required for promotion gating.
missing stress results block activation and promotion.
Ch10.C2.b — Regression Probe Tags
Regression probe tags bind tunnel outcomes to regression suites.
regression suite ids required by policy.
probes covering boundary and adversarial cases relevant to tunnel scope.
regression results stored and linked to chart activation.
regression failures trigger quarantine and rollback.
Ch10.C2.c — Risk Tags
Risk tags classify tunnels and tunnel outcomes.
low-risk: chart switch among already certified and witnessed charts.
medium-risk: new chart discovered but verified under strong certified evidence and stable probes.
high-risk: chart discovered via heavy search or near stability boundaries, or introduces new operators; requires stronger stress/regression.
Risk tags govern promotion gating and routing selection.
Ch10.C2.d — Refusal Logs
Refusal logs record failed tunnel attempts.
candidate id, enumerator state, and budget consumption.
verification evidence showing failure (residual curves, enclosure mismatch).
refusal classification and minimal counterexample objects.
admissible next actions (increase budgets, change carrier, portal escalation, policy bridge).
Refusal logs are preserved to prevent repeated futile searches and to guide pruning.
Ch10.C3 — Prove
Ch10.C3.a — Safety Under Perturbation
Perturbation safety is required for promotable tunnels.
residual curves remain within gates under small perturbations of probes and parameters.
stability region constraints are respected; outside region tunnel is inadmissible.
perturbation failures classify as ill-conditioning or hidden boundary crossing, triggering refusal or scope restriction.
perturbation evidence is stored as stress witness packs.
Ch10.C3.b — Stress-Pass ⇒ Promotable
Stress-pass is a necessary condition for promotion of high-risk tunnels.
if policy requires stress suites for tunnel type, then tunnel outcome is promotable only if stress witness packs pass.
stress-pass implies that tunnel does not fail under adversarial boundary probes and perturbations within declared admissible region.
stress-pass does not substitute for transport UCWs; both are required.
failure to stress-pass yields quarantine and rollback without deleting evidence.
Ch10.C3.c — Drift Detection
Drift detection prevents tunnels from introducing hidden semantic changes.
cross-κ consistency checks compare residual behavior under fixed ConvBundleFP.
canonicalization stability checks ensure ordering and projection behavior is stable.
if new chart introduces operators not covered by ConvBundleFP, drift is detected as scope gap and tunnel is refused.
drift evidence triggers ConvFP remediation or portal bridge requirements.
Ch10.C3.d — Plateau Detection
Plateau detection governs when tunneling is exhausted.
if enumerated tunnel candidates cannot reduce defect within budgets, plateau is detected in the tunnel search space.
plateau yields refusal logs with failure evidence and future search regions.
plateau may trigger obstruction compilation or portal escalation depending on failure class dominance.
plateau results are used to prune candidate search in future attempts.
Ch10.C4 — Extract
Ch10.C4.a — “Safe Tunnels” List
Safe tunnels are extracted as tunnel outcomes with promoted status.
tunnel operator family id and scope.
contexts (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP) under which tunnels are promotable.
transport UCWs linking new charts to baseline.
regression/stress suite coverage and pass status.
Ch10.C4.b — “Unsafe Tunnels” List
Unsafe tunnels are extracted as quarantined or refused tunnel outcomes.
refusal classification (policy block, scope gap, failed verification, drift, plateau).
minimal counterexample objects.
failure evidence and budgets consumed.
admissible remediation plans.
Ch10.C4.c — Risk Reports
Risk reports summarize tunnel risk exposure.
counts of tunnel outcomes by risk class.
missing stress/regression coverage.
drift alarms associated with tunnels.
high-risk nodes dependent on tunneled charts and their mitigation status.
Ch10.C4.d — Remediation Plans
Remediation plans specify how to convert unsafe tunnel regions into safe ones.
promote missing transport witnesses.
expand ConvFP scope or create explicit convention bridges when needed.
add certified tail bounds or rounding models for new charts.
adjust policies to allow necessary verification or to impose stricter stress/regression gates.
D. Fractal
Ch10.D1 — Spec
Ch10.D1.a — AUTO_TUNNEL Policy
AUTO_TUNNEL is a policy-governed automation mode enabling tunnel search and discovery.
AUTO_TUNNEL may generate candidate charts using deterministic enumeration and bounded search.
AUTO_TUNNEL cannot promote candidates without verified-only promotion steps; it produces candidates plus verification logs and required witness hooks.
AUTO_TUNNEL is scoped to specific operator families and node domains; global auto-tunneling is forbidden without explicit governance.
AUTO_TUNNEL actions are logged as tunnel events and are replay-addressable.
Ch10.D1.b — Search Budgets
Search budgets bound candidate generation and verification.
caps on number of candidates explored.
caps on parameter ranges (e.g., truncation depth, coefficient bounds).
caps on verification κ levels and probe counts.
caps on wall-time and memory.
Search budgets are enforced; exhaustion yields refusal logs rather than silent continuation.
Ch10.D1.c — Deterministic Enumeration
Candidate generation is deterministic.
candidate parameter spaces are ordered canonically.
enumeration order is pinned and recorded.
any stochastic search is forbidden for promotable outcomes unless policy explicitly permits it and seeds are pinned; even then, verification remains deterministic and certified.
deterministic enumeration ensures reproducible discovery and prevents irreproducible “lucky finds.”
Ch10.D1.d — Promotion Gating
Promotion gating enforces verified-only acceptance.
candidate discovered by search oracle is not evidence.
verification must be performed against certified baseline receipts using interval/tail proofs.
transport UCWs must link new charts to baseline.
stress/regression gates must pass for high-risk tunnels before chart activation.
Promotion gating is enforced by the verifier and by atlas governance; it cannot be bypassed by scheduler urgency.
Ch10.D2 — Encode
Ch10.D2.a — Enumerator State
Enumerator state encodes the frontier of candidate search.
current candidate index and parameter tuple.
caps and ranges in effect.
deterministic ordering id.
seeds if applicable.
Enumerator state makes search reproducible and enables resumption without ambiguity.
Ch10.D2.b — Candidate Logs
Candidate logs record discovered candidates and reasons for rejection.
candidate id and parameters.
oracle fit evidence (non-authoritative), stored as candidate-only.
verification attempt summary and outcome.
failure classification and counterexample objects.
Candidate logs are retained for pruning and future guidance.
Ch10.D2.c — Verification Logs
Verification logs record certified verification steps.
baseline receipt ids used.
interval evaluation results and tail bound ids.
residual curves and UCW outcomes for transport witnesses.
probe adequacy metadata and adversarial test outcomes.
Verification logs are part of the evidence for promotion and are replayable.
Ch10.D2.d — Promotion Logs
Promotion logs record when a tunnel outcome becomes promotable or active.
required witness hooks satisfied.
receipts and witness roots bound.
regression/stress suites completed and pass status.
atlas registry updates and activation events.
Promotion logs are auditable and do not alter node identities; they alter activation states under governance.
Ch10.D3 — Prove
Ch10.D3.a — No Search-Oracle Acceptance
Search oracles cannot certify truth.
oracle computations may guide candidate discovery but are never used as promotion evidence.
any identity derived from oracle fitting must be verified by certified evidence.
refusal logs record when oracle-derived candidates fail certified verification.
this rule prevents PSLQ hallucinations and ensures proof-carrying correctness.
Ch10.D3.b — Verified-Only Promotion
Promotion requires certified verification.
candidates must pass certified verification against baseline receipts.
transport UCWs must pass tri-gate acceptance.
representability must be stable under (\Pi_h).
stress/regression requirements must pass if mandated.
Verified-only promotion is the defining safety property of the tunneling system.
Ch10.D3.c — Determinism of Search Order
Deterministic search order is required for reproducibility.
candidate enumeration order is fixed and recorded.
tie-breaking is deterministic.
budgets enforce termination.
any change in enumeration order or caps changes tunnel event identity and must be logged as a new event.
Ch10.D3.d — Reproducible Discovery
Reproducible discovery is the property that the same inputs and budgets yield the same candidate outcomes.
deterministic enumeration and pinned contexts guarantee the same candidate stream.
certified verification produces deterministic accept/refuse outcomes under pinned verifiers.
discovered chart registry and failed candidate ledger preserve search outcomes for audit and pruning.
reproducible discovery enables cross-site replication of tunnel results without narrative.
Ch10.D4 — Extract
Ch10.D4.a — Discovered Chart Registry
The discovered chart registry is an extraction artifact listing verified and candidate charts.
chart ids, root assignments, evaluator method/bound ids.
contexts under which charts are certified and promotable.
transport witness ids linking charts to baseline.
activation status and regression/stress coverage.
Ch10.D4.b — Failed Candidate Ledger
Failed candidate ledger records rejected candidates to prevent repetition.
candidate ids and parameter tuples.
failure reasons and minimal counterexamples.
budgets consumed and search frontier identifiers.
remediation recommendations and future search region hints.
Ch10.D4.c — Learnable Heuristics
Learnable heuristics are extracted from search and verification logs, but they are governed.
identify parameter patterns associated with successful candidates.
identify common failure modes and prune regions accordingly.
heuristics are versioned and become part of deterministic enumerators only after governance approval and regression validation.
heuristics do not alter semantics; they alter search ordering and prioritization only.
Ch10.D4.d — Future Search Regions
Future search regions are extracted as prioritized parameter spaces for next exploration.
identify unsearched or undersearched candidate parameter ranges.
identify candidate families suggested by deficiency and obstruction evidence.
identify which new probes or carriers would improve identifiability.
bind future search plans to explicit budgets and required verification obligations.
Ch10 — Tunneling and Portal Bridges (Repair + Acceleration)
Ch11 — Certified Evaluation: Intervals, Tails, and Projectors
A. Square
Ch11.A1 — Spec
Ch11.A1.a — Interval Semantics
Certified evaluation is the production of sound enclosures of values or carrier objects together with sufficient metadata to replay and verify enclosure correctness under pinned contexts. The foundational certified object is an interval enclosure (or its carrier-appropriate generalization), which asserts containment of the true value within a closed set determined by explicit computation and explicit error accounting.
Scalar interval. For scalar carriers, an interval is a closed set[I := [\ell,u] \subset \mathbb R \quad(\text{or }\mathbb C\text{ as a product of real intervals})]such that the semantic assertion is:[x^\star \in I,]where (x^\star) is the intended mathematical value under pinned conventions. The interval representation is a data type; the containment claim is the certified semantic content.
Carrier-general enclosures. For non-scalar carriers, the interval generalizes to a certified enclosure set (\mathcal E) (e.g., ball in normed space, spectral enclosure, distributional band) with a declared containment predicate (\mathrm{Contains}(\mathcal E, x^\star)) checkable under pinned schemas.
Interval operations. Enclosures must support operations used in evaluator charts:
addition/subtraction and scalar multiplication,
multiplication and division where defined,
functional transforms under certified transform laws,
norm and observable extraction with certified upper bounds.
Interpretation under context. Interval semantics are indexed by:
ConvBundleFP (semantic conventions),
policy (admissible methods and budgets),
corridor (gates and κ ladder),
verifier version and schema versions.Intervals are meaningless without these pins because the intended value (x^\star) depends on conventions and admissibility.
Conservativeness. Certified intervals must be conservative: if uncertainty exists, intervals widen, never shrink incorrectly. Any under-approximation is an integrity violation and must be treated as drift or implementation fault.
Auditable rounding. Interval endpoints must include explicit rounding cushions consistent with pinned rounding IDs; raw floating endpoints are never trusted as enclosure claims without rounding accounting.
Ch11.A1.b — Tail Bounds
Most evaluator charts are infinite series, integrals, limits, or iterative schemes. Certified evaluation requires bounding the truncation error by a deterministic tail bound.
Let an evaluator represent a target (x^\star) as[x^\star = S_N + R_N,]where (S_N) is a computable approximant (partial sum, quadrature sum, truncated expansion) and (R_N) is the remainder. A tail bound is a computable function (B(N,\theta,\kappa)\ge 0) such that:[|R_N| \le B(N,\theta,\kappa)]under declared assumptions (parameter region, monotonicity, positivity, analyticity region). Tail bounds are not optional; they are part of the certificate.
Tail bounds must satisfy:
Explicit assumptions. Each bound declares its validity conditions (admissibility region) and fails or refuses outside them.
Deterministic computability. Bound evaluation must be deterministic under pinned backend semantics and must be reproducible.
Compositionality. Bounds must compose under operations used by evaluator charts: sums, products, transforms, and substitutions. Missing composition laws block certification of composite evaluators.
Conservativeness and verification. Bounds must be conservative; any use of non-conservative bounds invalidates certification. Bounds are audited by verifiers via bound provenance and recomputation checks.
κ coupling. Tail bounds are coupled to κ because κ determines required digits, truncation thresholds, or mesh sizes. The evaluator must choose (N=N(\kappa)) deterministically to satisfy corridor tolerance targets.
Ch11.A1.c — Projector (\Pi_h)
Certified evaluation is not only about numeric containment; it is about meaning-class containment. The projector (\Pi_h) enforces representability: it maps raw computed objects to canonical forms inside the lens, controlling how representation artifacts are eliminated.
Projector definition. (\Pi_h) is a typed, hash-addressed operator:[\Pi_h : D \to D,]with scope, admissibility region, and parameters pinned by ConvBundleFP and corridor/policy.
Representability requirement. Certified evaluation must guarantee that the evaluated object is stable under projection:[x^\star \approx \Pi_h(x^\star),]where equivalence is exact or corridor-bounded as declared. In practice, this requires bounding the projection residual on adequate probes:[r_\Pi(\kappa) := \max_{p\in\mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_\Pi(p,\Pi_h(p,\kappa)) \le \varepsilon_\Pi.]
Projector semantics. (\Pi_h) may include:
canonical rounding/quantization rules,
canonical basis selection,
canonical simplification rules,
canonical ordering constraints.All projector semantics that influence meaning are pinned by ConvFP; numeric rounding and stability obligations are pinned by corridor/policy.
Projector commutation obligations. Where required by schema, (\Pi_h) must commute with transports and evaluator charts up to corridor gates; such commutation is witnessed by UCWs.
Projector as safety guard. (\Pi_h) prevents “certificate of a representation artifact” by forcing all promoted claims into stable canonical form classes.
Ch11.A1.d — Certified Receipt Definition
A certified evaluation receipt is the minimal proof-carrying artifact asserting that a particular evaluator chart encloses the intended value within a declared corridor and under pinned semantic and governance contexts.
A certified evaluation receipt (\mathsf{EvalRcpt}) binds:
Target and chart. The node id (invariant) and chart id (evaluation method) including root assignment (\mathsf S/\mathsf F/\mathsf C/\mathsf R).
Context. ConvBundleFP hash, policy hash, corridor hash, verifier hash, schema hashes.
Parameters. Chart parameters (e.g., truncation depth (N), step size (h), transform parameters), encoded canonically.
Enclosure. The enclosure object (\mathcal E) (interval or carrier enclosure) with:
midpoint/representative,
radius/bound,
method-specific computed approximant (S_N),
deterministic rounding cushion.
Tail bound evidence. Bound id and provenance plus computed (B(N,\theta,\kappa)) sufficient to justify enclosure.
Projection binding. Projection signature id and representability residual obligations (or references to UCWs that certify projection stability when relevant).
Replay fields. Method IDs, tail-bound IDs, rounding IDs, κ ladder used, probe regime id (when representability is part of the certificate), and environment pins when required.
An evaluation receipt is promotable only when it is replay-verifiable and satisfies all corridor/policy-required obligations, including projection stability when required for downstream semantic use.
Ch11.A2 — Encode
Ch11.A2.a — Receipt Schema
Certified evaluation receipts use a strict, versioned schema. The minimal schema includes:
Header.
eval_rcpt_version,
eval_rcpt_id,
node_id, chart_id, root,
created metadata.
Context binding.
conv_bundle_hash,
policy_hash,
corridor_hash,
verifier_hash,
canon_hash,
schema_hashes.
Method binding.
method_id,
bound_id,
rounding_id,
projector_id (if applicable).
Parameters.
params (canonical map: (N), (h), truncation thresholds, transform params),
kappa (or κ ladder reference and the specific κ used for the receipt when receipts are κ-scoped).
Enclosure payload.
representation of enclosure (\mathcal E) (for scalar: lo, hi; for complex: rectangle; for vector: center + radius in declared norm),
approx representative (S_N),
tail_bound_value,
rounding_cushion_value.
Provenance.
bound_provenance_ref,
assumption_region_id,
admissibility_checks outcomes.
Audit pointers.
route WordNF hash if evaluation is part of a larger route word,
optional links to UCWs validating chart equivalence or projector commutation.
Receipt schemas enforce that certified enclosures are fully interpretable and replayable.
Ch11.A2.b — Bound Provenance
Bound provenance is addressable and auditable.
Bound derivation record. Each bound has a derivation artifact that includes:
bound functional form,
assumptions and admissibility region,
parameterization,
composition rules,
proof obligations for use.
Bound ID. Bound provenance is committed by a bound id:[\mathrm{bound_id} := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{BoundSpec})).]
Assumption enforcement. Receipts must record whether bound assumptions were checked and satisfied. If assumptions cannot be checked mechanically, the receipt must be marked non-promotable or boundary-limited under policy.
Bound upgrade. Improved bounds are introduced by new bound ids and migration edges; old bounds remain replayable.
Ch11.A2.c — Method IDs
Method IDs pin the evaluation algorithm.
Evaluator method id. Each chart has a method id identifying the algorithmic family (series, integral, recurrence, transform, product) and its certified evaluation semantics.
Algorithm version pinning. If algorithm behavior changes, method id changes or is versioned and the version is pinned in receipts.
Composition semantics. Composite methods (pipelines) have method ids derived from the canonical concatenation of component method ids, ensuring determinism.
Method admissibility. Policies and corridors restrict admissible method ids; receipts include admissibility checks.
Ch11.A2.d — Rounding IDs
Rounding IDs pin numerical rounding semantics.
Rounding model. Rounding id identifies:
backend arithmetic semantics,
rounding mode assumptions,
rounding cushion function used to ensure enclosure validity.
Deterministic rounding cushion. The rounding cushion is deterministic and conservative and is recorded in the receipt.
Backend pins. If backend differences can affect rounding semantics, backend version ids are pinned in the rounding id or as environment pins.
Policy constraints. Policies may restrict rounding models; receipts include policy admissibility checks.
Ch11.A3 — Prove
Ch11.A3.a — Enclosure Theorems
Enclosure theorems establish that certified receipts provide true containment.
Series enclosure theorem. If (x^\star=\sum_{n\ge 0} a_n) and (S_N=\sum_{n=0}^N a_n), and if a tail bound (B(N)) satisfies (|\sum_{n>N} a_n|\le B(N)) under declared assumptions, then:[x^\star \in [S_N - B(N) - \delta_{\mathrm{rnd}},; S_N + B(N) + \delta_{\mathrm{rnd}}],]where (\delta_{\mathrm{rnd}}) is the rounding cushion pinned by rounding id.
Integral enclosure theorem. If (x^\star=\int_\Omega f) and (Q_N) is a quadrature rule with certified error bound (B(N)), then the analogous enclosure holds with (S_N:=Q_N).
Iterative enclosure theorem. If (x^\star) is a fixed point of an operator and the iteration has a certified contraction or error envelope, then an enclosure can be certified provided the envelope is conservative and assumptions are satisfied.
Carrier enclosure theorem. For non-scalar carriers, enclosures generalize to balls or sets with certified containment predicates; enclosure theorems specify conditions under which the computed enclosure contains the true object in the carrier metric.
Enclosure proofs are not narrative; they are realized by bound provenance and by verifier recomputation of the enclosure formula and assumptions.
Ch11.A3.b — Composition Theorems
Certified evaluation must compose.
Sum composition. If (x^\star\in \mathcal E_x) and (y^\star\in \mathcal E_y), then (x^\star+y^\star\in \mathcal E_x\oplus\mathcal E_y) where (\oplus) is the enclosure addition law.
Product composition. If enclosures support multiplication and division, then (x^\star\cdot y^\star) is enclosed by the enclosure multiplication law, provided zero-division constraints are satisfied. If constraints are violated, certification must refuse or widen conservatively by declared rules.
Transform composition. If a transform (T) has a certified enclosure law, then (T(x^\star)) is enclosed by applying (T) to the enclosure with a transform-bound correction. Transform composition requires bound ids for transform error.
Pipeline composition. Composite charts (pipelines of operations) are certifiable only if every step has certified enclosure laws and bound provenance. Any missing step breaks certification and yields non-promotable evidence.
Composition theorems imply that derived nodes can be evaluated by composing certified receipts, and the resulting receipts remain replay-verifiable.
Ch11.A3.c — Stability Under (\Pi_h)
Certification is insufficient if the value is not stable under representability projection. Stability under (\Pi_h) is enforced as follows:
Projection stability theorem. If (x^\star\in \mathcal E) and (\Pi_h) is a projector with a certified stability property on the admissible region, then:[\Pi_h(x^\star)\in \Pi_h(\mathcal E)\oplus \mathcal E_{\Pi},]where (\mathcal E_{\Pi}) is an enclosure for projection residual. In scalar settings, (\mathcal E_{\Pi}) is bounded by rounding residuals; in higher carriers, it is bounded by projector-specific error envelopes.
Gate satisfaction. For promotion, the projection residual curve must satisfy (\varepsilon_\Pi) across κ and adequate probes.
Commutation requirements. If schema requires (\Pi_h) to commute with transports, the commutation is witnessed by UCWs; failure blocks promotion.
Drift detection. If (\Pi_h) behavior changes under κ or across replays while ConvFP and rounding ids are fixed, drift is detected and certification is refused until remediation.
Ch11.A3.d — Certified Upgrade Paths
Certified upgrade paths specify how to strengthen or extend certified evaluation evidence.
Precision upgrade. Increase κ (digits, truncation depth) deterministically until enclosure width satisfies target tolerances. Upgrade is legal only within corridor budgets.
Bound upgrade. Replace a conservative but loose bound by a tighter bound id under verified provenance; requires migration edges and re-verification of dependent receipts.
Method upgrade. Introduce a new chart (often via tunneling/AAS) with certified verification linking it to baseline via UCWs; method upgrades cannot bypass baseline truth.
Projector upgrade. If representability requires improved projector semantics, upgrade projector id via migration and re-verify all affected projection residual obligations.
Upgrade paths are structured: they produce new receipts and preserve old receipts as immutable evidence, linked by migration edges.
Ch11.A4 — Extract
Ch11.A4.a — Receipt Queries
Receipt queries retrieve certified evaluation receipts by multiple indices.
By receipt id.
By node id and chart id, filtered by context hashes (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP).
By κ level or κ ladder coverage.
By method id/bound id/rounding id for audit and migration planning.
Ch11.A4.b — Minimal Receipts
Minimal receipts are the smallest receipts sufficient for a specified verification goal.
For evaluation-only usage: enclosure + bound provenance + rounding id + context hashes.
For semantic equivalence usage: evaluation receipts plus UCWs linking charts and proving projection stability.
For promotion usage: above plus regression/stress witness packs required by policy.
Under restricted visibility: minimal receipts may be boundary-limited; export includes disclosure limitations and local-only verification statements.
Ch11.A4.c — Bound Audit
Bound audits verify the correctness and applicability of tail bounds.
Check that bound assumptions are satisfied for the parameter regime.
Recompute bound value (B(N,\theta,\kappa)) and compare to receipt record.
Verify bound composition rules used in pipelines.
Detect stale or deprecated bounds and propose migration to new bound ids.
Bound audits produce audit artifacts and may quarantine receipts if bounds are invalid.
Ch11.A4.d — Replay Steps
Replay steps for certified evaluation are deterministic.
Resolve pinned contexts and schemas.
Recompute approximant (S_N) under method id and parameters.
Recompute tail bound (B(N)) under bound id and assumptions.
Recompute rounding cushion under rounding id.
Construct enclosure (\mathcal E) and compare to receipt.
If required, evaluate projection stability obligations and UCWs for chart equivalence.
B. Flower
Ch11.B1 — Spec
Ch11.B1.a — Evaluator Charts
An evaluator chart is a typed, parameterized method for computing certified enclosures of a node’s invariant. Charts belong to atlas roots (\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R) and are interpreted under pinned conventions.
A chart includes:
a method id,
bound ids for truncation and transform error,
rounding id,
admissibility region and stability region,
parameter schema (e.g., truncation depth, step size),
output enclosure schema,
required projection regime.
Charts are objects in the metro and are hash-addressed. Charts may be baseline or acceleration, but both must satisfy certified evaluation semantics for promotion.
Ch11.B1.b — Baseline vs Accel Charts
Baseline charts provide the canonical anchor for truth; accel charts provide faster routes.
Baseline charts. Must be present for root coverage and must be certified even if slow.
Accel charts. May be added to improve performance; must be certified and must be linked to baseline charts by transport UCWs.
No second truth. Accel charts are not independent semantic claims; they are alternative evaluators whose equivalence to baseline is witnessed.
Activation governed. Accel charts are activated only under atlas governance after satisfying transport witness and stress/regression gates.
Ch11.B1.c — Chart Switching
Chart switching is the selection of which chart to use under a context and demand.
Chart switching is allowed only between charts certified under the same corridor/policy context or under explicitly bridged contexts.
Switching to an accel chart requires transport UCWs to baseline and must preserve representability obligations.
Switching is recorded: either in WordNF route segments or in atlas activation state referenced by receipts.
Switching is forbidden when it introduces scope gaps, policy violations, or unverified equivalence.
Ch11.B1.d — Transport Binding
Transport binding is the evidence linking charts.
Baseline-to-accel binding requires transport UCWs certifying commutation, holonomy, and projection stability.
Binding is context-indexed: a UCW under one corridor/policy does not necessarily certify binding under another.
Binding must be maintained under chart evolution: migration edges and regression enforce continued validity.
Without binding, a chart remains a candidate and cannot be activated for promoted routing.
Ch11.B2 — Encode
Ch11.B2.a — Chart Specs
Chart specs are canonical records.
chart id, root assignment, method id, bound ids, rounding id, projector id.
parameter schema and admissible ranges.
stability region ids and scope coverage.
required witness hooks for equivalence binding.
Chart specs are hash-addressed; changes create new chart ids and migration edges.
Ch11.B2.b — Parameter Schemas
Chart parameters are typed and canonicalized.
truncation depth (N), mesh size (h), sample count, transform parameters.
guard digits and precision controls as corridor-coupled numeric parameters.
admissibility region checks for parameters.
canonical serialization and omission rules.
Ch11.B2.c — Route Words
Evaluation routes are WordNF objects.
evaluation steps, projection steps, bound evaluation steps, rounding steps as segments.
chart selection and parameters bound in segment payloads.
any tunnel events recorded explicitly.
gate-run segments and probe regime ids recorded when evaluation is used in UCWs.
Ch11.B2.d — Activation Rules
Activation rules govern which charts can be selected.
baseline charts must be available for promoted nodes.
accel charts can be active only when transport UCWs to baseline exist and pass gates.
high-risk charts require stress/regression pass before activation.
deactivation/quarantine is triggered by drift alarms or failed regressions, preserving fallback availability.
Ch11.B3 — Prove
Ch11.B3.a — Equivalence via UCW
Equivalence between charts is certified by UCWs.
If UCW between chart outputs passes tri-gate across κ, then charts are equivalent within corridor-bounded equivalence.
Holonomy gates ensure that equivalence is not path-dependent when charts participate in cycles.
Projection gates ensure that equivalence is stable under canonicalization.
Equivalence is scoped by context hashes and cannot be globalized without bridging.
Ch11.B3.b — Accel Correctness
Accel correctness is the claim that accel charts produce correct certified enclosures of the same invariant.
accel chart must be certified by enclosure and tail bounds.
accel chart must be linked to baseline by transport UCW; this prevents spurious identities.
accel chart must satisfy projection stability obligations.
accel chart must pass required stress/regression suites when policy requires, due to higher risk.
Ch11.B3.c — Fallback Correctness
Fallback correctness ensures that disabling accel charts preserves evaluability and correctness.
baseline charts provide certified evaluation under all promotable contexts.
fallback selection must respect policy and scope; fallback cannot use forbidden methods.
fallback must preserve representability obligations and must be certified.
regression ensures that fallback remains correct under evolution.
Ch11.B3.d — Switch Safety
Switch safety ensures chart switching does not change meaning or violate constraints.
switching is safe only when contexts match or are bridged.
switching is safe only when equivalence binding exists (transport UCW) and is promotable under target context.
switching is safe only when projection stability remains satisfied under target corridor.
switching is auditable: it is visible in WordNF or atlas activation state bound to receipts.
Ch11.B4 — Extract
Ch11.B4.a — Fastest Certified
Fastest certified selection is an extraction query.
filter charts certified and active under target context.
rank by cost metadata (non-semantic) and required κ to meet gates.
return selected chart and its certified evaluation receipt and binding transport UCWs.
include fallback set and reason codes.
Ch11.B4.b — Cheapest Certified
Cheapest certified selection minimizes cost subject to certification constraints.
cost includes evaluation cost plus verification overhead.
exclude charts requiring missing stress/regression results.
return minimal replay bundle for selected chart.
include policy compliance evidence.
Ch11.B4.c — Most Stable
Most stable selection prioritizes robustness over speed.
rank by margins to gates and perturbation robustness evidence.
prefer baseline charts with wide stability regions.
penalize charts near boundaries or requiring complex tunnels.
output stability report and risk classification.
Ch11.B4.d — Recommended Default
Recommended default selection is governance-controlled.
default chart per root for baseline.
default accel chart per root when safe and beneficial.
defaults indexed by policy and corridor contexts.
default changes recorded as governance pointer updates and trigger regression.
C. Cloud
Ch11.C1 — Spec
Ch11.C1.a — Error Budgets
Error budgets are corridor obligations for evaluation.
budgets specify target enclosure widths and residual margins.
budgets are κ-indexed; higher κ yields stronger budgets.
budgets include rounding and tail bound contributions explicitly.
budget exhaustion yields refusal receipts; it does not permit silent weakening.
Ch11.C1.b — κ Ladders
κ ladders drive certified tightening.
for each κ, evaluator selects parameters (N(\kappa)) or (h(\kappa)) to satisfy error budgets.
κ ladder is pinned by corridor and recorded in receipts.
monotone tightening expected; non-monotone indicates instability or drift.
κ ladder adequacy is required for promotion of evaluators used in UCWs.
Ch11.C1.c — Plateau vs Convergence
Plateau and convergence are distinguished by residual behavior.
