convergence: enclosure radii and residuals decrease with κ.
plateau: residuals stop decreasing above gates.
plateau triggers method change, bound upgrade, probe escalation, or tunnel synthesis.
plateau classification is stored as refusal evidence; it is not a vague diagnosis.
Ch11.C1.d — Uncertainty Stratification
Uncertainty is stratified for evaluation artifacts.
certified: enclosure and bound verified.
near: enclosure exists but does not meet required budgets.
heuristic: numeric approximant without certified bound; candidate-only.
refused: assumptions violated, bounds invalid, or budgets insufficient.
Stratification governs safe reuse and routing.
Ch11.C2 — Encode
Ch11.C2.a — Residual Curves
Residual curves for evaluation are stored when evaluation participates in UCWs or when evaluation budgets require monitoring.
enclosure radius curves across κ.
projection residual curves when projection is part of evaluation.
perturbation residual curves when required.
curves are recomputable and stored as evidence with provenance pointers.
Ch11.C2.b — Confidence Levels
Confidence levels are encoded as tags.
certified / near / heuristic / refused.
promotable status under contexts.
required upgrades listed.
risk class and required stress/regression.
Ch11.C2.c — Regression Probes
Regression probes for evaluators ensure stability across evolution.
fixed set of targets and κ levels.
boundary probes for stability regions.
probes for projection stability.
bisect hooks to localize failures.
Ch11.C2.d — Stress Probes
Stress probes for evaluators test adversarial conditions.
near singularity probes.
near branch cut probes.
ill-conditioned parameter probes.
extreme κ probes to detect implementation drift.
Stress probe requirements are policy-scoped.
Ch11.C3 — Prove
Ch11.C3.a — Monotone Tightening
Monotone tightening is required for certified evaluation across κ.
enclosure radii decrease or plateau below required budgets.
tail bound values decrease with increasing (N(\kappa)) or decreasing (h(\kappa)).
rounding cushions remain conservative and do not break monotonicity.
violation triggers drift alarms or method instability classification.
Ch11.C3.b — Plateau Classification
Plateau classification for evaluation is explicit.
bound insufficiency: bound too loose; fix by bound upgrade.
method insufficiency: chart converges too slowly; fix by tunnel to accel chart.
instability: non-monotone behavior; fix by method migration or backend pinning.
non-identifiability: evaluation cannot decide equivalence; fix by probe or carrier escalation.
Plateau classification yields refusal receipts with admissible next actions.
Ch11.C3.c — Adversarial Robustness
Robustness is required for high-risk evaluator activation.
evaluate under adversarial probes and perturbations.
enforce that certified enclosure remains valid and stable.
enforce that projection stability holds.
failure yields quarantine and blocks promotion.
Ch11.C3.d — Refusal Correctness
Refusal correctness ensures evaluation refusals are honest and actionable.
refusals include violated assumptions, failed checks, and minimal counterexamples.
refusals include budgets consumed and remaining admissible actions.
refusals are replayable and verifier-checkable.
refusals block promotion and are quarantined from certified routing.
Ch11.C4 — Extract
Ch11.C4.a — Confidence Exports
Confidence exports package evaluation status for cross-site use.
enclosure summaries and margins.
bound provenance ids and assumption region ids.
context hashes (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP/verifier).
minimal replay bundles and visibility constraints.
Ch11.C4.b — Risk Exports
Risk exports summarize evaluator risk.
risk class and required stress/regression suites.
boundary sensitivity reports.
known drift alarms and migration status.
quarantine status and remediation plans.
Ch11.C4.c — Failure Exports
Failure exports package refusal evidence.
refusal receipts and counterexamples.
plateau classification evidence.
blocked admissible actions due to policy.
recommended upgrade paths.
Ch11.C4.d — Diagnostic Exports
Diagnostics provide actionable insight for repair and synthesis.
which bound is limiting.
which method is limiting.
which κ levels are problematic.
suggested tunnels or bound upgrades and their expected obligations.
D. Fractal
Ch11.D1 — Spec
Ch11.D1.a — Evaluator Synthesis
Evaluator synthesis constructs new evaluator charts when existing ones plateau or are too costly.
synthesis outputs candidate charts and verification plans.
synthesis uses deterministic enumeration of template families (series, transforms, Apéry-like charts, kernel methods).
synthesis produces certified evidence only after verified-only promotion steps.
synthesis integrates charts into atlases via transport UCWs and activation governance.
Ch11.D1.b — Bound Library Reuse
Bound library reuse prevents duplicated proof work.
bounds are stored as bound ids with provenance.
evaluator charts reference bound ids rather than rewriting bound proofs.
bound composition rules enable reuse in pipelines.
bound upgrades propagate via migration edges and re-verification jobs.
Ch11.D1.c — Auto-Tightening
Auto-tightening is the automated selection of (N(\kappa)) or other parameters to satisfy error budgets.
selection rules are deterministic and pinned.
selection rules are verified by recomputation and bound checks.
selection respects corridor budgets and terminates.
failures yield refusal receipts and suggest method or bound upgrades.
Ch11.D1.d — Performance Models
Performance models guide scheduling and chart selection.
model cost as function of κ and parameters.
model digits-per-term or error decrease rate for charts.
model verification overhead (UCW costs, probe costs).
models are non-semantic metadata and cannot affect acceptance predicates.
Ch11.D2 — Encode
Ch11.D2.a — Bound Catalogs
Bound catalogs index available bounds.
bound ids by method family.
validity regions and assumptions.
composition rules.
migration links and deprecation status.
Ch11.D2.b — Memo Keys
Memo keys enable caching of evaluation results safely.
keyed by node id, chart id, context hashes, κ, and parameter tuple.
memoization stores evidence pointers, not semantic claims.
memoization invalidates on method/bound/rounding id changes.
memo soundness is maintained by replay verification.
Ch11.D2.c — Workload Stats
Workload stats encode demand signals.
call volumes, required digits, latency constraints.
context distribution (policies/corridors) of demands.
benefit of acceleration vs baseline.
workload stats guide scheduler but do not affect semantics.
Ch11.D2.d — Scheduling Metrics
Scheduling metrics guide job selection.
defect severity and plateau incidence.
expected cost reduction from accel.
evidence coverage gaps (missing roots, missing transports).
risk overhead (required stress/regression).
Metrics are deterministic and audit-friendly.
Ch11.D3 — Prove
Ch11.D3.a — Memo Soundness
Memo soundness ensures caching cannot corrupt certification.
cached receipts are valid only if context hashes match.
verifiers can recompute enclosures and bounds from pinned ids.
caches do not assert acceptance; they store replayable evidence.
caches invalidate on schema/verifier drift.
Ch11.D3.b — Bound-Reuse Soundness
Bound reuse is sound only if bounds are valid under the parameter regime.
bound assumptions must be checked.
composition rules must apply.
migration of bounds must preserve correctness and trigger re-verification.
invalid bounds quarantine dependent receipts.
Ch11.D3.c — Schedule Correctness
Scheduler correctness ensures evaluator synthesis and upgrades are pursued appropriately.
high-demand nodes receive accel synthesis when baseline is too costly.
plateau regions trigger method/bound upgrades rather than infinite refinement.
risk gates ensure high-risk acceleration is stress/regression tested.
scheduling is deterministic and auditable.
Ch11.D3.d — Performance Prediction Sanity
Performance models must be validated.
compare predicted costs to measured costs under pinned contexts.
ensure models do not change semantic outcomes.
update models via governance-controlled migration.
regression ensures model updates do not alter routing legality.
Ch11.D4 — Extract
Ch11.D4.a — Recommended Bound Recipes
Recommended bound recipes are extracted as best practices.
bound selection per method family and stability region.
parameter selection for (N(\kappa)) to satisfy budgets.
conservative rounding cushion rules.
composition recipes for pipelines.
Recipes are context-indexed and do not alter semantics.
Ch11.D4.b — Method Selection Rules
Method selection rules choose charts under constraints.
prefer certified baseline for robustness.
use accel when linked by transport UCW and stress/regression coverage exists.
select most stable chart near boundaries.
respect policy constraints and corridor budgets.
Ch11.D4.c — Autopruning Rules
Autopruning removes unhelpful evaluator candidates from active consideration.
prune candidates failing certified verification.
prune candidates requiring forbidden operators or scope expansions.
prune candidates with repeated defect non-reduction.
preserve refusal logs and candidate ledgers for audit.
Ch11.D4.d — Optimization Playbooks
Optimization playbooks guide systematic acceleration without violating certification.
identify high-demand nodes and cost bottlenecks.
schedule AAS to discover accel charts.
verify accel charts via interval/tail and transport UCWs.
activate charts under atlas governance; run regression/stress; rollback on failure.
Ch11 — Certified Evaluation: Intervals, Tails, and Projectors
Ch12 — Tier-0 Obstruction Compiler (T0-OBSTR)
A. Square
Ch12.A1 — Spec
Ch12.A1.a — Tier-0 Definition (Loop-Closure Modulus)
A Tier-0 constant is a loop-closure modulus: the minimal scalar (or minimal element of a declared modulus space) required to make a family of semantic loops commute within a fixed semantic regime. Tier-0 is not defined by aesthetic primitivity or by decimal behavior; it is defined operationally by stable holonomy that persists under refinement and that is invariant to admissible representational changes.
Let (\mathcal M) be a metro restricted to a fixed context (X=(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V)). Consider a family of loops (\mathcal L={\ell}), each loop (\ell) represented by a WordNF route word whose semantic intent is identity (or an identity up to a known subgroup). Let the loop map induced by (\ell) be (L_\ell(\cdot;\kappa)) on probe carriers (\mathcal P(\kappa)).
Define the κ-indexed spin residual maxima:[s_\ell(\kappa) := \max_{p\in \mathcal P(\kappa)} \rho_{\text{spin}}(p,L_\ell(p,\kappa)).]If loop closure were exact under the semantics of the context, the spin residual would be within gate (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}). Tier-0 arises precisely when:
the system has exhausted admissible repairs (κ escalation, probe expansion, admissible method changes, admissible tunnels that preserve semantic regime), yet
the holonomy defect remains stable and structural: it does not vanish as κ increases, and it exhibits invariance under admissible changes of chart within the same semantic regime, and
the holonomy defect compresses to a scalar obstruction or a low-dimensional obstruction parameter in a declared modulus space (\mathcal Z) such that adding this parameter as a kernel seed closes the loop family under the tri-gate acceptance predicate.
Tier-0 thus denotes a missing kernel parameter that is forced by the attempt to make the transport calculus commute. In the scalar setting, Tier-0 is an interval enclosure (I=[\underline z,\overline z]) that represents the admissible values of the modulus consistent with observed holonomy evidence under the corridor.
Ch12.A1.b — Obstruction Interval
An obstruction interval is the certified enclosure of the modulus required to close a loop family. It is extracted from holonomy-dominant UCWs as a κ-stable interval intersection.
Let each loop (\ell) yield, after admissible repair attempts, a residual-derived scalar enclosure (I_\ell(\kappa)) interpreted as the interval of modulus values that would close the loop at κ within corridor gates. The obstruction interval for loop (\ell) is defined as:[I_\ell^\star := \bigcap_{\kappa\in\mathcal K} I_\ell(\kappa),]provided the intersection is nonempty and stable under admissible probe and method variations within the fixed context.
For a loop family (\mathcal L), the family obstruction interval is extracted by clustering consistent loop obstructions:[I_{\mathcal L}^\star := \mathrm{Cluster}\bigl({I_\ell^\star : \ell\in\mathcal L}\bigr),]where clustering is a deterministic overlap-based partition that groups intervals that share nontrivial overlap and yields candidate intervals with support sets (which loops support each candidate).
Obstruction intervals are candidate Tier-0 moduli; they are not promoted as constants until Root-Quad Forcing (Ch13) closes atlas coherence with the new seed.
Ch12.A1.c — Stability Criteria
An obstruction is a valid Tier-0 candidate only if it satisfies stability criteria that rule out numeric artifacts, probe inadequacy, and semantic drift.
A candidate obstruction interval (I^\star) is stable if:
κ-stability. Across the corridor κ ladder (\mathcal K), the extracted interval intersections are nonempty and tighten to a bounded width:[\mathrm{width}(I_\ell^\star)\le \delta_{\mathrm{stab}},]for a corridor/policy-declared stability tolerance (\delta_{\mathrm{stab}}), and the center estimate does not drift beyond tolerance across κ.
Probe stability. Under admissible probe expansions that preserve adequacy, the interval remains consistent (overlaps persist).
Method stability. Under admissible chart substitutions within the same atlas root (or across roots when transport witnesses exist), the interval remains consistent. This excludes method-specific artifacts.
Context stability. ConvBundleFP, policy, and corridor hashes remain fixed. Any change in these hashes invalidates stability; the result becomes a different claim or requires explicit bridging.
Holonomy dominance. The failure signature is holonomy-dominant: face residuals are at or below gates (or are controlled by existing transport witnesses), but spin residuals remain above gates stably, indicating loop non-closure rather than mere route disagreement.
Stability criteria are strict; any violation yields refusal or escalation rather than candidate promotion.
Ch12.A1.d — Rejection Criteria
T0-OBSTR must reject candidates that are artifacts or misclassified failures. Rejection criteria are:
Convention dependence. If the candidate interval changes when ConvFP changes on relevant scope, then it is not a Tier-0 modulus but a convention artifact. Required output: CONVENTION_CONFLICT with bridge requirements.
Policy dependence. If the candidate depends on forbidden operations or cannot be reproduced under fixed policy, it is not promotable; classify as POLICY_BLOCK or require policy bridge.
Probe inadequacy. If probe adequacy cannot be certified, any extracted interval is non-authoritative. Required output: UNCERTAINTY_PLATEAU with probe escalation plan.
Non-stability. If (I_\ell^\star) is empty or unstable across κ, reject as numeric instability or method artifact.
Face dominance. If face residual failures dominate (routes disagree), this indicates missing transport evidence or incorrect equivalence claim; reject as Tier-0 and route to transport completion or atlas coherence tasks.
Portal necessity. If conflict proofs or topological obstructions indicate that no scalar modulus can close the loop within the current schema, reject Tier-0 and emit portal candidate requirements.
Rejection is encoded as structured refusal artifacts; rejection does not discard evidence but classifies it for future use.
Ch12.A2 — Encode
Ch12.A2.a — Obstruction Record Schema
An obstruction record is a canonical object representing a Tier-0 candidate extraction result. Minimal schema:
Header.
obstr_version,
obstr_id,
context_hashes (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier).
Loop family binding.
loop_family_id and support loop ids,
minimal_loop_suite_id (subset that suffices to reproduce extraction),
face_set_id (related commutation squares considered).
Extraction results.
per-loop (I_\ell^\star) intervals,
clustered candidate intervals (I_k^\star) with support sets,
stability metrics (widths, κ variation, probe variation).
Evidence pointers.
supporting UCW ids (holonomy-dominant) and their WordNF hashes,
probe bundle ids and adequacy metadata pointers,
method/chart ids used for extraction.
Decision fields.
classification: candidate, reject, portal_required, policy_block, probe_inadequate,
refusal payload if rejected, including minimal counterexamples and next actions.
Next-step plan.
atlas-forcing plan pointers (Ch13),
verification job plan pointers,
governance requirements (stress/regression suites).
Obstruction records are immutable; updates create new records linked by migration edges.
Ch12.A2.b — Loop Family IDs
Loop family ids identify the semantic loop structures relevant to a domain.
Loop family definition. A loop family is a finite set of loop words ({\ell}) with a shared semantic intent class (e.g., discrete↔continuous normalization loops, transform inversion loops, branch/monodromy loops, basis change loops).
Loop id computation. Each loop word has id:[\mathrm{id}(\ell) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{WordNF}(\ell));|;\mathrm{context_hashes}).]Loop ids are context-indexed to prevent cross-context confusion.
Family id. A loop family id is a hash commitment to the ordered set of loop ids and their semantic intent metadata.
Minimal loop suite. A minimal loop suite is a deterministically selected subset that suffices to reproduce the obstruction interval extraction within declared stability tolerances.
Loop family ids support extraction, audit, and reproducible discovery.
Ch12.A2.c — κ-Intersection Logic
κ-intersection logic defines how per-κ evidence is collapsed into a stable interval.
Per-κ interval extraction. For each κ, derive (I_\ell(\kappa)) from the spin residual evidence and the declared mapping from holonomy defect to modulus parameter. This mapping is a schema artifact and must be pinned.
Intersection operator. Compute:[I_\ell^\star := \bigcap_{\kappa\in\mathcal K} I_\ell(\kappa),]with deterministic handling of empty intersections (reject) and deterministic rounding of endpoints (to preserve stable hashing).
Width criterion. Require (\mathrm{width}(I_\ell^\star)\le \delta_{\mathrm{stab}}) when stability is required; otherwise classify as uncertain.
Robustness overlay. Optionally compute intersections across multiple probe bundles or chart variants; stable candidates persist across overlays.
κ-intersection is deterministic and replayable; any nondeterminism yields refusal.
Ch12.A2.d — Clustering Logic
Clustering groups compatible obstruction intervals into candidate moduli.
Overlap predicate. Two intervals overlap if their intersection is nonempty.
Deterministic clustering. Sort intervals by endpoints and apply a fixed greedy clustering algorithm producing clusters with merged envelopes and support sets.
Cluster minimality. Produce maximal overlap clusters; each cluster yields a candidate interval (I_k^\star) and a support loop set.
Cluster rejection. Clusters supported by too few loops (below a policy-declared support threshold) are marked weak and require additional loop evidence before proceeding to atlas forcing.
Clustering output is a finite set of candidate moduli with provenance.
Ch12.A3 — Prove
Ch12.A3.a — Convention-Invariance Test
Tier-0 candidates must be invariant to admissible convention choices within the fixed scope. The convention-invariance test is:
Fix the intended semantic scope and ConvBundleFP. If ConvFP changes within relevant scope, the candidate is not Tier-0; it is a convention difference requiring a bridge or a portal.
Under fixed ConvBundleFP, vary only non-semantic metadata (e.g., formatting) and confirm that extracted intervals are identical.
Where the domain admits multiple charts within the same root under fixed ConvBundleFP, verify that substitution of charts does not change the extracted interval beyond stability tolerance.
If invariance fails, classify as drift or convention artifact and emit refusal with remediation: expand ConvFP schema or add explicit bridge artifacts.
This test prevents mislabeling convention artifacts as new constants.
Ch12.A3.b — Policy-Invariance Test
Tier-0 candidates must not be artifacts of policy relaxation.
Candidates must be reproducible under the pinned policy. If extraction requires forbidden operations, the result is policy-blocked rather than promotable.
If the candidate depends on widening budgets or using heuristic oracles for acceptance, it is rejected. Heuristics may guide candidate search but cannot certify Tier-0.
Policy changes that would enable extraction are represented explicitly as policy-bridge requirements; candidates remain quarantined until verified under admissible policies.
If the candidate appears only under lax policies and disappears under strict policies, it is treated as non-promotable under strict policy; this is not Tier-0.
Policy invariance enforces governance honesty.
Ch12.A3.c — Stability Under Refinement
Tier-0 candidates must be stable under κ refinement and under admissible probe expansions.
κ stability. Extracted (I_\ell^\star) intervals must tighten or remain stable as κ grid strengthens; they must not drift.
Probe stability. Under probe expansions improving adequacy, interval overlap must persist; otherwise the candidate is reclassified as uncertainty.
Method stability. Under admissible chart substitutions and route rewrites justified by commutation witnesses, interval overlap must persist.
Termination. Stability evaluation must terminate under budgets; if stability cannot be decided due to budget constraints, classify as policy block or uncertainty.
Stability under refinement is the distinguishing mark of structural obstructions.
Ch12.A3.d — Non-Artifact Proof
A non-artifact proof is the argument, realized as verifier-checkable obligations, that the extracted obstruction is not a numerical illusion.
Certified evidence basis. All residual curves used are certified and replayable, derived from UCWs that pass all hard legality predicates and have adequate probes.
Cross-chart consistency. Candidates persist across multiple independent charts and roots when available, linked by transport witnesses. This prevents method artifacts.
Adversarial checks. Candidates are tested under adversarial probes relevant to the loop family (boundary probes, ordering probes, perturbation probes). Failure under adversaries rejects candidate.
Drift exclusion. Cross-κ consistency tests ensure no semantic drift or canonicalization instability. Any drift evidence rejects candidate.
Refusal completeness. When non-artifact proof cannot be established, the output is refusal with evidence, not “maybe Tier-0.”
Non-artifact proof is encoded as a refusal-free obstruction record with stability metrics and witness pointers.
Ch12.A4 — Extract
Ch12.A4.a — Candidate List
Candidate list extraction produces Tier-0 candidates suitable for atlas forcing.
list of candidate intervals (I_k^\star),
support loop sets and minimal loop suite ids,
supporting UCW ids and their contexts,
stability metrics and risk class tags,
required next-step obligations (Ch13 atlas forcing).
Candidates are ordered deterministically by support size and by stability strength.
Ch12.A4.b — Supporting UCWs
Supporting UCW extraction provides evidence for candidates.
all holonomy-dominant UCWs used in extraction,
their WordNF hashes and loop map signatures,
κ residual curves and margins to gates,
probe bundle ids and adequacy metadata.
This is the audit-grade evidence basis of Tier-0 proposals.
Ch12.A4.c — Minimal Loop Suite
Minimal loop suite extraction provides a reproducible subset sufficient to reconstruct the candidate.
minimal set of loop ids,
required probes and κ grid,
required charts and transport witnesses,
deterministic replay plan to recompute intervals and clustering.
Minimal loop suites enable cross-site replication of Tier-0 discovery.
Ch12.A4.d — Next Actions
Next actions are deterministically derived from classification.
Atlas forcing plan. If candidate stable: schedule Root-Quad Forcing (Ch13) to integrate modulus as a seed and close SFCR closure obligations.
Repair completion plan. If face-dominant or missing transport evidence: schedule completion of commutation squares or transport witnesses.
Probe escalation plan. If probe inadequate: schedule probe expansions and adversarial tests.
Policy/bridge plan. If policy blocked or convention conflict: schedule policy bridges or convention bridges.
Portal plan. If portal required: schedule portal node creation and semantic extension obligations.
B. Flower
Ch12.B1 — Spec
Ch12.B1.a — Loop Suite Selection
Loop suite selection chooses the loop family (\mathcal L) used to extract obstructions.
Semantic relevance. Loops are selected to target the semantic mismatch suspected: normalization loops, transform inversion loops, branch/monodromy loops, basis change loops, or representation collapse loops.
Minimal generator loops. Prefer primitive loops (cannot be decomposed into smaller loops within the loop family) to reduce redundancy.
Diversity of carriers/roots. When possible, include loops realized in different atlas roots to avoid method artifacts.
Deterministic selection. Loop suite selection is deterministic given the failure signature (UCW class distribution) and domain configuration.
Loop selection is part of the obstruction record and is replayable.
Ch12.B1.b — Face Set Selection
Face set selection chooses commutation squares relevant to the loop family.
Identify squares whose commutation residuals are within gates (to isolate holonomy), or whose failures localize where loop closure breaks.
Include faces that define the boundary of higher-order closure (edge vs face vs interior attribution).
Face selection must respect scope and policy; forbidden faces are excluded and noted as blockers.
Face selection yields explicit witness obligations: which UCWs must be recomputed and which are already promotable.
Face sets support obstruction localization logic.
Ch12.B1.c — Canonical Carrier Choice
Obstruction extraction depends on carrier choice. Canonical carriers are selected to maximize identifiability and stability.
Choose carriers where loop maps and projection are well-defined and stable.
Prefer carriers with existing adequacy-proven probe regimes.
Avoid carriers near ill-conditioned stability boundaries unless boundary probes are mandatory and budgets allow robust checks.
Carrier choice is deterministic and recorded; changing carrier yields a different candidate extraction and requires explicit comparison.
Canonical carrier selection prevents spurious obstructions.
Ch12.B1.d — Route Pinning
Route pinning fixes the specific WordNF loops and associated evaluation routes used for obstruction extraction.
Pin loop WordNF hashes and all segment parameters (methods, bounds, κ grid, probes).
Pin chart selections and transport witnesses that connect charts.
Pin projection signatures and loop map signatures.
Ensure that pinned routes are admissible under policy and scope; otherwise extraction refuses.
Pinned routes are the mechanism that makes Tier-0 discovery reproducible.
Ch12.B2 — Encode
Ch12.B2.a — WordNF Templates
T0-OBSTR uses canonical WordNF templates for loop families.
Each loop family defines a template structure: encode/decode steps, projection gates, evaluation gates, and loop closure checks.
Templates specify where κ refinement occurs and how probes are generated.
Templates specify admissible tunnel insertion points if tunnels are allowed for repair.
Templates include segment stream schemas ensuring that tunnel events and corridor changes are logged.
Templates are schema artifacts and versioned.
Ch12.B2.b — Loop Word Templates
Loop word templates define the canonical structure of loop words.
identification of start/end lens and carrier,
specification of intermediate representations and transforms,
specification of projection steps to enforce representability,
specification of loop closure evaluation and spin residual computation.
Loop word templates bind loop map signatures and residual functionals.
Ch12.B2.c — Probe Templates
Probe templates define how probes are generated for loop suites.
base probe family for stable interior region,
boundary probes for branch/monodromy loops,
perturbation probes to test robustness,
adversarial probes targeting worst-case commutation and loop closure.
Probe templates are deterministic; any randomness is seed-pinned and typically forbidden for promotion-critical extraction.
Ch12.B2.d — Carrier Templates
Carrier templates define the canonical carriers used for loop extraction.
scalar carriers for Tier-0 moduli,
kernel carriers for semigroup normalization loops,
loop carriers for monodromy/phase loops,
ladder carriers for refinement stability loops,
split-flow carriers for noncommuting generator loops.
Carrier templates determine observables and residual functionals.
Ch12.B3 — Prove
Ch12.B3.a — Obstruction Localization (Edge vs Face vs Interior)
Obstruction localization identifies whether the mismatch is dyadic (edge), triadic (face), or tetrahedral (interior) in the 4-root closure skeleton.
Dyadic localization. If obstruction appears when comparing two roots or two charts (edge), then it is an edge-level mismatch: missing transport witness, missing bridge, or a local modulus.
Triadic localization. If dyadic edges close but a 3-way face fails, then the obstruction is triadic: a face-level coherence issue requiring promotion of a higher-order bridge or completion of missing face witnesses.
Interior localization. If all edges and faces close but the 4-way interior coherence fails (SFCR interior), then the obstruction is a true Tier-0 modulus candidate: a missing kernel parameter required to fill the interior.
Localization is performed by analyzing the UCW failure distribution across the closure skeleton and by Möbius attribution logic (below).
Ch12.B3.b — Möbius / Attribution Logic
Attribution logic assigns the observed defect to minimal structural components.
Define a defect functional (F(S)) on subsets of roots (or on subgraphs) measuring best-achieved residual after admissible repair.
Apply a deterministic inclusion–exclusion (Möbius inversion) to separate dyadic, triadic, and tetra contributions.
If the tetra contribution is nonzero and stable under refinement, it is candidate Tier-0. If dyadic contributions dominate, focus on edge witnesses; if triadic dominate, focus on face witnesses.
Attribution produces a localization report linking specific loops and faces to obstruction evidence, minimizing the risk of inventing a constant to fix a missing witness.
Attribution logic is a formal method for failure interpretation.
Ch12.B3.c — Refusal Logic
Refusal logic enforces that obstruction extraction is honest and bounded.
If probe adequacy cannot be established, refuse extraction as uncertainty rather than producing a candidate.
If context hashes differ across evidence, refuse as convention/policy conflict.
If stability under κ fails, refuse as numeric instability or method artifact.
If budgets exhaust, refuse with exhaustion evidence and list admissible next actions (increase budgets under new policy, change carrier, schedule tunnel).
Refusal logic produces structured refusal packs rather than weak candidates.
Ch12.B3.d — Portal Necessity Tests
Portal necessity tests determine when no Tier-0 scalar can close the loop under current schema.
If conflict proofs show intrinsic incompatibility under fixed semantic regime, portal required.
If loop map evidence indicates topological obstruction (monodromy) not representable as scalar modulus in the existing schema, portal required.
If closing the loop requires new observables or a new equivalence relation, portal required.
Portal necessity yields a portal candidate record with required semantic extensions; it rejects Tier-0 promotion.
Ch12.B4 — Extract
Ch12.B4.a — Obstruction Localization Report
Localization report extraction outputs:
which structural level dominates (edge/face/interior),
which loops and faces provide evidence,
which UCWs support the classification,
which missing witnesses or bridges must be built before proceeding.
Reports are deterministic and replayable.
Ch12.B4.b — Minimal Failing Words
Minimal failing words extract the smallest loop subwords responsible for obstruction.
identify the smallest subloop whose spin residual remains above gate.
identify the minimal κ where failure occurs.
identify the minimal probe(s) realizing failure.
produce counterexample objects for audit.
Ch12.B4.c — Repair Candidates
Repair candidates extraction lists admissible actions short of Tier-0 promotion.
missing transport UCWs (edge completion),
missing face UCWs (face completion),
admissible tunnels that may close loops without new moduli,
probe expansions and adversarial checks.
Repair candidates ensure Tier-0 is not used to patch incomplete verification.
Ch12.B4.d — Portal Candidates
Portal candidates extraction lists semantic extensions required.
required new carrier type or lens family,
required new observables or invariant schema extensions,
required convention bridge or new ConvFP regime,
required verification obligations to validate portal.
Portal candidates are scheduled by governance and treated as major semantic expansions.
C. Cloud
Ch12.C1 — Spec
Ch12.C1.a — Plateau Detection
Plateau detection distinguishes unresolved uncertainty from structural obstruction.
plateau is detected when κ refinement and admissible repairs fail to reduce spin residuals below gates.
plateau must be evaluated under adequate probes; otherwise classify as probe inadequacy.
plateau is stable if residual curves remain within a narrow band across κ.
plateau detection results drive obstruction extraction only when holonomy dominance holds and drift is excluded.
Ch12.C1.b — Uncertainty vs Holonomy Separation
Separation prevents false Tier-0 discovery.
uncertainty: residual curves plateau due to inadequate probes or insufficient methods, not because of a missing modulus.
holonomy: residual curves plateau due to loop non-closure, with face residuals controlled and projection stable.
separation relies on tri-gate decomposition: face controlled + projection controlled + spin failing → holonomy.
if face fails, the system focuses on transport completion, not Tier-0.
Ch12.C1.c — Robustness Tests
Robustness tests ensure candidates are not artifacts.
perturbation probes: small perturbations of probes and parameters must not destroy interval overlap.
alternative chart probes: substitute charts within same root; candidate must persist.
boundary probes: include adversarial probes relevant to loop family.
regression probes: re-run extraction under pinned contexts to detect drift.
Ch12.C1.d — Adversarial Checks
Adversarial checks are mandatory for high-risk Tier-0 candidates.
probes near branch cuts or singularities for analytic continuation loops.
ordering tie probes for canonicalization stability.
worst-case commutation probes to ensure face residual control is genuine.
multi-root cross-check probes to ensure candidate persists across independent representations.
Adversarial checks reduce risk of promoting spurious constants.
Ch12.C2 — Encode
Ch12.C2.a — Plateau Certificates
Plateau certificates record plateau evidence.
κ-indexed spin residual curve and plateau predicate witness.
adequacy metadata and probe regime id.
budgets consumed and remaining admissible actions.
classification linking plateau to holonomy dominance or uncertainty.
Plateau certificates are evidence objects stored and used by the scheduler.
Ch12.C2.b — Perturbation Probes
Perturbation probe records include:
perturbation model id and parameters,
probe sets per κ,
residual results under perturbations,
stability outcomes and failure counterexamples.
Ch12.C2.c — Regression Probes
Regression probe records include:
fixed set of loop suites and faces,
periodic re-extraction runs,
drift detection outcomes,
bisect lineage for failures.
Regression probes ensure candidate stability over time.
Ch12.C2.d — Refusal Packs
Refusal packs for T0-OBSTR include:
refusal class,
minimal failing loop suite and counterexamples,
best-achieved intervals and why unstable/invalid,
admissible next actions and required governance changes.
Refusal packs preserve evidence and guide future work.
Ch12.C3 — Prove
Ch12.C3.a — Plateau ⇒ Not Promotable
Plateau above gates implies non-promotability of closure claims; therefore:
obstruction candidates may be proposed only as candidates, not as promoted constants, until atlas forcing closes coherence.
plateau evidence blocks use of the claim in promotion-critical routing.
plateau evidence triggers escalation to probe expansion, method synthesis, or portal analysis.
refusal is mandatory when budgets exhaust; no indefinite searching.
Ch12.C3.b — Stable Obstruction ⇒ Candidate
If an obstruction interval is stable under κ refinement, probe adequacy, method substitution, and context invariance, then it is a valid Tier-0 candidate.
candidate is recorded with supporting UCWs and stability metrics.
candidate is scheduled for Root-Quad Forcing to confirm it closes atlas interior.
candidate is quarantined from promotion until atlas forcing succeeds.
candidate includes risk and required stress/regression obligations.
Ch12.C3.c — Drift ⇒ Reject
If evidence indicates drift, reject Tier-0 extraction.
drift includes ConvFP mismatch, canonicalization instability, ordering nondeterminism, or environment-dependent behavior.
drift evidence yields refusal with remediation: ConvFP schema expansion, method migration, or environment pinning.
rejected candidates are not scheduled for atlas forcing.
drift triggers quarantines on affected loops and UCWs.
Ch12.C3.d — Mixed ⇒ Escalate
Mixed failures require escalation rather than premature Tier-0.
if both face and spin failures occur, first complete transport witnesses and commutation squares.
if probe adequacy uncertain, escalate probes.
if policy forbids required actions, escalate to policy bridging.
only after isolating holonomy dominance is Tier-0 candidate extraction admissible.
Escalation prevents misuse of Tier-0 to patch incomplete proof work.
Ch12.C4 — Extract
Ch12.C4.a — Plateau Dashboards
Plateau dashboards summarize where loops fail to close.
list loops with plateaued spin residuals above gates.
classify plateau types (uncertainty vs holonomy).
list missing probes, missing methods, missing witnesses.
prioritize by demand and by downstream dependency impact.
Ch12.C4.b — Robustness Dashboards
Robustness dashboards summarize candidate stability.
interval widths and overlap metrics across probe/method variations.
adversarial check outcomes.
regression stability outcomes.
risk classifications and required stress/regression.
Ch12.C4.c — Refusal Evidence
Refusal evidence extraction provides audit-ready failure artifacts.
refusal packs and counterexamples.
supporting UCW ids and their contexts.
budget exhaustion evidence.
remediation obligations list.
Ch12.C4.d — Escalations Queue
Escalations queue lists deterministic next jobs.
probe escalation jobs,
method synthesis/tunnel jobs,
transport completion jobs (face closure),
policy bridge jobs,
portal analysis jobs,
atlas forcing jobs for stable candidates.
Queue ordering is deterministic by urgency and by dependency impact.
D. Fractal
Ch12.D1 — Spec
Ch12.D1.a — Integration with Scheduler
T0-OBSTR is integrated as a compiler pass triggered by stable failures.
T0-OBSTR runs only after Snap and admissible tunnels fail to close loops within budgets.
T0-OBSTR consumes refusal packs, plateau certificates, and holonomy-dominant UCWs.
T0-OBSTR outputs candidate moduli and atlas forcing plans, not promotions.
Scheduler prioritizes T0-OBSTR by demand and by how many downstream routes are blocked by holonomy.
Ch12.D1.b — Auto-Trigger Rules
Auto-trigger rules ensure T0-OBSTR is invoked only when justified.
Trigger when spin residuals fail gates stably across κ and face residuals are controlled.
Require probe adequacy certified; otherwise trigger probe escalation instead.
Require convention and policy invariance; otherwise trigger bridges or policy changes.
Require repeated failure under admissible repairs and tunnel attempts within budgets.
Auto-trigger rules prevent runaway discovery and false constants.
Ch12.D1.c — Bounded Search
T0-OBSTR is bounded.
bounded loop family size,
bounded κ grid,
bounded probe expansions,
bounded method substitutions,
bounded clustering and candidate count.
Bounded search ensures termination and auditability; budget exhaustion yields refusal.
Ch12.D1.d — Compaction of Evidence
Evidence compaction preserves essential obstruction evidence without storing excessive traces.
store loop ids, supporting UCW ids, clustered intervals, and stability metrics.
store minimal loop suite sufficient to reproduce extraction.
store refusal summaries for rejected candidates and plateau evidence.
produce compaction receipts proving replay equivalence of evidence for promoted claims.
Ch12.D2 — Encode
Ch12.D2.a — Job Queue Schema
Obstruction jobs are encoded as scheduler job objects.
job type T0_OBSTR.
loop family id, face set id, carrier template id.
context hashes and budgets.
required output: obstruction record, candidate list, atlas forcing plan.
Jobs are deterministic and replayable.
Ch12.D2.b — Priority Model
Priority model ranks obstruction jobs.
urgency derived from demand: digits × call volume × latency impact.
dependency impact: number of blocked downstream routes.
stability strength: candidates with strong stability metrics prioritized for atlas forcing.
risk overhead: high-risk candidates require stress/regression; cost included in scheduling.
Priority is non-semantic but deterministic and auditable.
Ch12.D2.c — Evidence Cache
Evidence cache stores intermediate results.
cache per loop suite and context.
cache per UCW and probe regime.
cache clustered interval sets.
cache invalidates on schema/verifier changes, drift alarms, or context changes.
Caching accelerates repeated obstruction attempts without changing semantics.
Ch12.D2.d — Invalidation Rules
Invalidation prevents stale obstruction evidence.
invalidate on ConvFP changes affecting loop semantics.
invalidate on corridor changes affecting gates/κ.
invalidate on policy changes affecting admissibility.
invalidate on verifier or schema changes affecting residual functionals, probe generation, loop maps, or projection.
Invalidation yields explicit invalidation artifacts and triggers re-extraction if needed.
Ch12.D3 — Prove
Ch12.D3.a — Safe Automation
Safe automation constraints prevent T0-OBSTR from generating unverified truth.
candidates are not promoted constants; they are proposals.
promotion requires atlas forcing and UCW closure in Ch13.
automation must log all decisions and evidence.
automation must refuse on inadequate probes or drift.
Ch12.D3.b — No Runaway Discovery
Runaway discovery is prevented by strict triggers and budgets.
T0-OBSTR runs only when holonomy dominance and stability criteria hold.
candidate count is bounded; weak candidates are rejected or deferred.
repeated failed attempts are pruned deterministically and recorded.
policy blocks stop T0-OBSTR; it cannot bypass policy.
Ch12.D3.c — Deterministic Ordering
Deterministic ordering is required for reproducibility.
loop suite ordering is canonical.
κ ordering is corridor-defined.
probe ordering and clustering ordering are deterministic.
candidate ordering is deterministic by support size and interval stability.
Any nondeterminism forbids promotion of candidates and yields refusal.
Ch12.D3.d — Replay of Discovery
Discovery is replayable.
obstruction record contains minimal loop suite, probe regime, charts, and context.
re-running extraction reproduces interval intersections and clustering.
replay includes budget evidence; if budgets differ, output is a different claim and must be recorded.
replay supports cross-site validation and cross-metro exchange under policy compatibility.
Ch12.D4 — Extract
Ch12.D4.a — New-Seed Proposals
New-seed proposals are extracted candidates suitable for atlas forcing.
candidate interval (I_k^\star) and support loop set.
required verification plan to instantiate candidate as a node seed.
required atlas forcing plan: root coverage and closure obligations.
required stress/regression obligations and risk class.
Ch12.D4.b — Proof Obligations
Proof obligations list for each candidate.
required transport UCWs among roots.
required loop closure UCWs for new cycles.
required representability witnesses.
required adversarial checks.
Proof obligations are explicit and become job plans for Ch13.
Ch12.D4.c — Verification Plan
Verification plan describes how to test the candidate.
instantiate candidate modulus as a seed node candidate.
build baseline charts across roots sufficient for evaluation.
attempt closure of loops in minimal loop suite by adding candidate modulus.
verify closure by UCWs and tri-gate acceptance; if closure fails, reject candidate or escalate to portal.
Ch12.D4.d — Atlas-Forcing Plan
Atlas-forcing plan is the direct bridge to Ch13.
required root receipts (Square, Flower, Cloud, Fractal) for the candidate modulus.
required edge and face closure obligations and which UCWs certify them.
required interior coherence certificate when applicable (SFCR closure).
activation and promotion conditions, including required stress/regression.
Ch12 — Tier-0 Obstruction Compiler (T0-OBSTR)
Ch13 — Root-Quad Forcing (RQF) and Ω* Coherence
A. Square
Ch13.A1 — Spec
Ch13.A1.a — 4-Root Requirement
Root-Quad Forcing (RQF) is the verification procedure that turns a candidate invariant (often a Tier-0 obstruction candidate) into a promotable node by forcing it to admit a certified four-root atlas and by forcing closure coherence across those roots. RQF is not a heuristic “add more formulas” step; it is a structured closure program with explicit obligations.
Let (v) be a candidate node representing an invariant (I) (scalar constant, modulus, or higher carrier invariant) within a fixed context (X=(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V)). The 4-root requirement is:
Root coverage. There exist certified evaluator charts (\mathsf{Chart}_S,\mathsf{Chart}_F,\mathsf{Chart}_C,\mathsf{Chart}_R) such that:[\mathrm{Root}(\mathsf{Chart}_S)=\mathsf S,\quad\mathrm{Root}(\mathsf{Chart}_F)=\mathsf F,\quad\mathrm{Root}(\mathsf{Chart}_C)=\mathsf C,\quad\mathrm{Root}(\mathsf{Chart}_R)=\mathsf R,]and each chart produces certified evaluation receipts under corridor (C) (or under an explicitly pinned compatible corridor) satisfying the evaluation obligations of Ch11.
Root semantics. Each root is not merely a different formula; it is a different representation lens regime with different failure modes and different observables. Root diversity is required to prevent method artifacts and to force robustness.
Root admissibility. Each root chart must be admissible under the same ConvBundleFP and policy scope, unless explicit bridges are introduced and witnessed. RQF does not permit hidden semantic changes.
Root integration. Root charts must be connected by transport witnesses that certify equivalence under tri-gate logic (Ch08) and that ensure loop closure in the chart graph (Ch08.C).
A candidate node lacking any root is not promotable; it is either incomplete (missing chart discovery) or portal-requiring (semantic extension needed). RQF is the procedure that distinguishes these cases by constructive obligations.
Ch13.A1.b — SFCR Closure Target
RQF targets the closure of the canonical 4-root coherence skeleton. Let the four roots define vertices of a tetrahedral closure object with:
Vertices: (S,F,C,R).
Edges: (SF,SC,SR,FC,FR,CR).
Faces: (SFC,SFR,SCR,FCR).
Interior: (SFCR).
The closure target is:
Edge closure. Each pairwise relation between root charts is certified by a transport witness (UCW) under corridor gates. Edge closure ensures pairwise consistency.
Face closure. Each triple interaction is certified by commutation witnesses ensuring that different orderings of transports and evaluations agree within gates. Face closure ensures coherence of composing transports across three roots.
Interior closure (Ω).* The tetrahedral interior closure certifies that boundary closures are globally consistent: the entire root quad closes without residual holonomy beyond gates. Interior closure is required to prevent “pairwise consistency but globally inconsistent” failure modes.
SFCR closure is the canonical target because it is the minimal structure that detects higher-order inconsistencies not visible in pairwise checks. RQF thus forces the candidate invariant to become a stable kernel object in the transport calculus.
Ch13.A1.c — Ω* Certificate Meaning
An Ω* certificate is the interior coherence witness produced by successful RQF. It is a hash-addressed witness object asserting that the 4-root atlas closes as a single semantic object under the pinned context.
Ω* certifies:
Global coherence. All six edges and four faces admit promotable UCWs under corridor gates and under adequate probes; these UCWs compose without inducing residual holonomy above gates.
Interior loop closure. Every cycle in the chart transport graph induced by the tetrahedral structure closes within the spin gate. In particular, the boundary-of-boundary constraint is satisfied: the composition of face closures yields a null interior residual under the declared modulus.
Representability consistency. Projection/canonicalization regimes across roots are consistent and stable: the candidate invariant belongs to a single representability class across the atlas.
Promotion readiness. Given Ω*, the candidate node may be promoted as a seed or as a derived invariant under the pinned context, subject to policy-required stress/regression obligations.
Ω* is not a numerical coincidence certificate. It is a structured, replay-verifiable, compositional witness that allows the invariant to serve as a kernel hub in the metro.
Ch13.A1.d — Acceptance Predicate
RQF acceptance is a deterministic predicate producing one of:[\mathrm{RQF}(v)\in{\mathrm{RQF_OK},\mathrm{RQF_NEED_PORTAL},\mathrm{RQF_REFUSE}}.]
RQF_OK. Holds iff:
root coverage is satisfied (four certified root receipts exist),
all six edges have promotable UCWs under corridor gates,
all four faces have promotable commutation UCWs under corridor gates,
Ω* interior certificate exists and passes its verifier checks,
no ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor drift is present,
required stress/regression suites (if mandated) pass.
RQF_NEED_PORTAL. Holds iff:
root coverage cannot be achieved under fixed semantic regime because at least one root requires extending the invariant schema, carrier type, or semantic conventions beyond admissible bridges; or
edges/faces cannot be closed because incompatibilities are intrinsic (conflict proofs persist under adequate probes and refinement).
RQF_NEED_PORTAL emits a portal report specifying required semantic extensions.
RQF_REFUSE. Holds iff:
admissibility is violated (policy forbids required operations, scope gaps exist),
probe adequacy cannot be established,
plateau above gates occurs without admissible escalation paths,
budgets exhaust without closure.
RQF_REFUSE emits a refusal pack with minimal failing obligations and admissible next actions.
Ch13.A2 — Encode
Ch13.A2.a — Atlas Node Schema
RQF produces or updates an AtlasNode object linked to the candidate node. The AtlasNode schema includes:
Header.
atlas_version,
atlas_id,
node_id,
context_hashes (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier).
Root chart pointers.
one or more chart ids per root,
certified evaluation receipt ids per chart,
chart activation states (candidate/certified/promoted/active),
chart scope and admissibility fields.
Edge witness pointers.
transport edges between root charts,
UCW ids certifying each edge,
WordNF hashes for each edge witness.
Face witness pointers.
commutation squares for each face,
UCW ids certifying face commutation,
WordNF hashes for face paths.
Interior witness pointers.
Ω* certificate id,
interior WordNF hash and loop suite id,
minimal witness roots needed to replay the interior proof.
Governance.
regression/stress suite ids required,
promotion status under context,
quarantine/deprecation markers if needed.
AtlasNode is hash-addressed and immutable; changes create new atlas ids linked by migration edges.
Ch13.A2.b — Root Receipts Schema
Root receipts are certified evaluation receipts (Ch11) with additional root metadata.
Each root receipt includes:
root ∈ {S,F,C,R}.
chart_id, method_id, bound_id, rounding_id, projector_id.
context_hashes.
enclosure_payload and bound_provenance_ref.
stability_region_id and admissibility checks.
Root receipts are the baseline truth anchors for RQF; they are required even when acceleration charts exist.
Ch13.A2.c — Edge/Face Witness Schema
Edge and face witnesses are UCWs with additional structural metadata.
Edge witness record. Includes:
endpoint chart ids (root pair),
transport WordNF hash,
UCW id and tri-gate residual curves,
loop implications (cycles introduced) and required loop UCWs.
Face witness record. Includes:
three roots defining face,
two (or more) path WordNF hashes defining commutation comparison,
UCW id certifying commutation and representability,
any required auxiliary probes.
All edge/face witnesses are context-indexed; mismatched context invalidates RQF.
Ch13.A2.d — Interior Witness Schema
The interior witness schema defines Ω* certificate structure.
Interior loop suite. A deterministic set of interior loops derived from the boundary closures (minimal set sufficient to test global coherence). Each loop is identified by WordNF hash.
Ω* UCW set. A set of UCWs certifying that interior loops close within spin gates and that interior commutation constraints hold.
Boundary-to-interior commitments. The interior witness must commit to the exact edge and face witnesses used; otherwise it is not a genuine closure certificate.
Replay bundle pointers. Minimal witness roots and pin roots required for replay verification.
Ω* is a dedicated witness object with its own verifier protocol and version hash.
Ch13.A3 — Prove
Ch13.A3.a — Edge Closure Implies Face Preconditions
Face closure requires edge closure preconditions. RQF enforces:
Edge-first discipline. A face commutation witness is well-posed only when the constituent edges are admissible and witnessed. Without edge transport witnesses, face commutation comparisons are ill-defined or ambiguous.
Shared context precondition. All edges in a face must share compatible context hashes. If contexts differ, explicit bridges are required before face closure is attempted.
Projection precondition. Projection regimes (\Pi_h) must be consistent across the face’s routes, or projection commutation witnesses must exist. Otherwise representability comparisons are invalid.
Adequacy precondition. Probe regimes must be adequate for each edge before face commutation is meaningful; inadequate probes yield refusal or probe escalation.
Thus, edge closure is a necessary precondition for meaningful face closure; attempting face closure without edges yields illegality, not mere failure.
Ch13.A3.b — Face Closure Implies Interior Well-Posedness
Interior closure is well-posed only when face closures exist and are consistent.
Boundary completeness. The boundary of the tetrahedron consists of the four faces. Without promoted face witnesses, interior closure is undefined as a global claim because boundary constraints are incomplete.
Compatibility of faces. Face witnesses must be compatible on shared edges. If two faces disagree on shared edge semantics, interior closure is blocked and the conflict must be resolved at edge/face level before interior forcing.
Interior loop generation. Interior loops are generated deterministically from boundary faces; if faces are not closed, interior loop suite cannot be generated consistently.
Interior proof obligation. Given closed faces, interior closure reduces to verifying that the composed boundary constraints induce no residual holonomy beyond gates. This is a well-posed UCW problem and yields Ω* if successful.
Face closure thus establishes the conditions under which Ω* is meaningful and verifiable.
Ch13.A3.c — Rejection Correctness
RQF rejection correctness ensures that RQF does not falsely promote candidates and does not misclassify portal necessity.
Reject on missing root. If any root receipt cannot be constructed under admissible operations and scopes, RQF must refuse or classify as portal-required rather than silently proceeding with fewer roots.
Reject on inadequacy. If probe adequacy cannot be established, RQF must refuse; it cannot accept based on residual curves computed on inadequate probes.
Reject on instability/plateau. If edge/face residual curves plateau above gates under κ refinement with adequate probes, RQF must refuse; it cannot accept via hidden corridor widening.
Reject on drift. If any evidence indicates ConvFP drift or canonicalization instability under fixed ConvBundleFP, RQF must refuse and require remediation.
Reject on incompatibility. If conflict proofs persist under admissible bridges and stable contexts, RQF must classify as portal-required rather than propose a scalar modulus.
Rejection correctness is realized by refusal packs and portal reports; it is verifiable by replay.
Ch13.A3.d — Portal Classification
Portal classification is the deterministic decision that closure cannot be achieved within the current semantic regime.
A portal is required when any of the following hold:
Schema insufficiency. Required root charts cannot be defined without adding new observables, new equivalence relations, or new admissibility sets.
Carrier insufficiency. Required closure obligations cannot be expressed or witnessed in the current carrier type (e.g., monodromy requires a richer carrier).
Intrinsic topological obstruction. Loop evidence indicates monodromy/topological nontriviality that cannot be closed by adding a scalar modulus within the existing schema.
Convention non-bridgeability. Conventions required by different roots are incompatible and no admissible convention bridge exists under policy.
Portal classification produces a portal report detailing the required semantic extension and the verification obligations that would certify the portal.
Ch13.A4 — Extract
Ch13.A4.a — Atlas Summary
Atlas summary extraction produces:
list of root charts and their certified receipts,
edge transport witnesses (UCWs) and their gate margins,
face commutation witnesses and their gate margins,
Ω* certificate status,
promotion status and required governance suites.
Atlas summaries are context-indexed and audit-friendly.
Ch13.A4.b — Missing Root Report
Missing root report identifies which root(s) lack certified charts and why.
scope gaps (ConvBundleFP missing operator coverage),
policy blocks (forbidden methods or insufficient budgets),
carrier inadequacy (cannot define required observables),
stability region issues (chart exists but not certified under required κ).
Each missing root report includes admissible next actions: chart discovery jobs, policy bridges, probe escalation, or portal escalation.
Ch13.A4.c — Closure Proof Pack
Closure proof pack is the minimal replay bundle for RQF_OK.
pins: node spec, atlas spec, chart specs, ConvFP/corridor/policy records,
witnesses: root receipts, edge UCWs, face UCWs, Ω* certificate witnesses,
WordNF records: edge and face path words, interior loop suite words,
regression/stress evidence required by policy.
Proof packs are hash-committed and exportable under visibility modes.
Ch13.A4.d — Portal Report
Portal report is the structured output for RQF_NEED_PORTAL.
minimal failing closure obligations,
conflict proofs and counterexamples,
required semantic extensions (carrier/schema/convention),
verification plan for portal creation,
governance impact and required bridges.
Portal reports are actionable and replayable.
B. Flower
Ch13.B1 — Spec
Ch13.B1.a — 6 Edges Closure
RQF edge closure certifies pairwise coherence among roots.
Each edge (XY\in{SF,SC,SR,FC,FR,CR}) is certified by a transport UCW linking chart (X) to chart (Y).
Edge UCWs must satisfy tri-gate obligations under corridor κ ladder and adequate probes.
Edge closure requires that both charts satisfy representability obligations and share compatible projection regimes (or have projection commutation witnesses).
Edge closure introduces potential cycles; cycles must be tracked for subsequent loop closure certification.
Ch13.B1.b — 4 Faces Closure
RQF face closure certifies that compositions across three roots commute.
Each face (e.g., (SFC)) is defined by at least two distinct path orderings across the three roots.
Face UCWs certify commutation of these path orderings under corridor gates.
Face closure relies on edge UCWs for the edges on the face; missing edges block face closure.
Face closure ensures that local coherence is not order-dependent.
Ch13.B1.c — Interior Forcing
Interior forcing is the step that produces Ω*.
Derive a deterministic interior loop suite from the boundary faces: loops that traverse edges and faces and return to the starting chart.
For each interior loop, compute loop map signatures and spin residual curves under adequate probes.
Certify that all interior loops close within (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) and that associated commutation constraints remain within (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}).
Produce Ω* certificate binding boundary witnesses and interior loop witnesses.
Interior forcing is the decisive step: it confirms that the candidate invariant is globally coherent across roots.
Ch13.B1.d — Canonical Path Ordering
Canonical path ordering prevents ambiguity in face and interior definitions.
For each face, define canonical path orderings (e.g., (S\to F\to C) vs (S\to C\to F)) and fix them deterministically.
Path ordering is scope-aware; it cannot cross convention boundaries without bridges.
Path ordering is recorded in WordNF hashes for face witnesses.
Interior loop orderings are derived deterministically from face orderings, ensuring reproducibility.
Ch13.B2 — Encode
Ch13.B2.a — Edge WordNFs
Edge WordNFs encode the exact transport routes between root charts.
Edge WordNF includes segment stream: evaluation, projection, transport gates, and any tunnel events.
Edge WordNF binds method ids, bound ids, κ ladder, probe regime, and contexts.
Edge WordNF is referenced by edge UCWs; mismatch invalidates witness.
Edge WordNF is replayable and audit-ready.
Ch13.B2.b — Face WordNFs
Face WordNFs encode the two alternative face paths.
For each face, store two WordNF hashes representing the two canonical path orderings.
Bind intermediate chart ids and transport edges used.
Bind gate stacks and probe regimes used to certify face commutation.
Include splice records if face paths were constructed by splicing tunnel-derived charts.
Ch13.B2.c — Interior WordNF
Interior WordNF encodes the interior loop suite.
list of loop WordNF hashes derived deterministically,
loop map signature ids for each loop,
gate stacks and probe regimes used for spin residual evaluation,
link to Ω* certificate id.
Interior WordNF ensures that Ω* is replay-addressable.
Ch13.B2.d — Splice Records
Splice records encode any integration steps that inserted new charts into closure paths.
pre/post WordNF hashes,
inserted chart ids and transport edge ids,
justification witness ids for splice equivalence,
new obligations introduced and their witness ids.
Splice records preserve auditability and prevent hidden rewriting.
Ch13.B3 — Prove
Ch13.B3.a — Commutation Witnesses per Cell
Each cell in the closure skeleton is certified by a UCW.
edge cells: transport UCWs,
face cells: commutation UCWs comparing two face paths,
interior cells: loop closure UCWs and interior commutation UCWs,
projection cells: representability UCWs or projection stability obligations.
Cell-level witnesses are scoped and compositional; they are bound into receipts and merkle-rooted witness sets.
Ch13.B3.b — Boundary-to-Interior Constraint
Interior coherence is constrained by boundary coherence.
The interior witness must commit to boundary witnesses.
Interior loops are generated from boundary paths; their correctness depends on boundary commutation.
Any inconsistency on boundary edges or faces propagates into interior failure; therefore interior failure is localized by tracing which boundary witness first violates gates.
This constraint ensures that Ω* failure yields actionable localization: repair edges/faces rather than inventing new semantics.
Ch13.B3.c — Uniqueness of Filler (When Exists)
When a Tier-0 modulus is forced, RQF yields a uniqueness principle: within the declared modulus space (\mathcal Z) and within the corridor gate regime, there is at most one stable candidate interval that closes the interior coherently.
If two distinct modulus candidates would close the same minimal loop suite under the same corridor gates, then either:
probe adequacy is insufficient (non-identifiability), or
the modulus space is under-specified (needs schema refinement), or
the corridor is too wide to identify uniqueness.
Therefore, RQF requires adequacy strong enough that interior closure pins a unique stable modulus candidate up to corridor tolerance.
If uniqueness cannot be established, RQF refuses promotion and yields a non-identifiability report and probe escalation obligations.
Uniqueness is operational: it is defined by the failure of alternative candidates under adequate probes and refinement, not by informal arguments.
Ch13.B3.d — Repair Order Correctness
RQF obeys a fixed repair order to avoid nondeterministic closure.
Construct root receipts first (existence in each root).
Close edges second (pairwise transport witnesses).
Close faces third (triple commutation witnesses).
Attempt interior forcing last (Ω*).
If any step fails, apply Snap repair within budgets; if repair fails, classify as refusal or portal-required.
Repair order correctness ensures determinism and minimality of added obligations.
Ch13.B4 — Extract
Ch13.B4.a — Closure Routes
Closure route extraction returns all WordNF objects used in closure.
edge WordNFs and their UCW ids,
face WordNFs and their UCW ids,
interior WordNFs and Ω* certificate id,
splice records and tunnel event logs if applicable.
Closure routes are the executable proof of coherence.
Ch13.B4.b — Fastest Closure Route
Fastest closure route selection minimizes verification cost while preserving certification.
select edge and face proofs with minimal κ cost while still satisfying gates,
prefer already-promoted UCWs and receipts,
avoid high-risk tunnels unless required,
output minimal replay bundle for selected proof.
Fastest route selection is non-semantic metadata guiding scheduling; the semantic claim remains the same.
Ch13.B4.c — Fallback Closure Route
Fallback closure route ensures robustness.
provide alternative charts per root,
provide alternative transport paths between roots (if multiple exist),
provide alternative probe regimes where admissible,
ensure fallback remains within policy constraints.
Fallback closure is critical when drift alarms or regressions quarantine certain charts.
Ch13.B4.d — Audit Trail
Audit trail extraction produces a complete provenance chain.
root receipt provenance (bounds, rounding ids, method ids),
transport witness provenance (UCWs, WordNFs),
tunnel and splice provenance (if used),
regression/stress evidence for promotion,
governance events (activation/deactivation, promotions).
Audit trails make Ω* claims independently verifiable.
C. Cloud
Ch13.C1 — Spec
Ch13.C1.a — Corridor Gates for Closure
RQF closure is evaluated under corridor gates.
Edge and face UCWs must satisfy tri-gate predicates across κ ladder.
Interior Ω* must satisfy spin gate across κ ladder for interior loop suite.
Auxiliary gates (stress/regression) may be required for high-risk closure, especially when closure relied on tunnels or bridges.
No corridor widening is permitted during closure; if widening is considered, it is a new corridor identity requiring explicit governance and re-verification.
Ch13.C1.b — Probe Adequacy for Each Root
Adequacy must hold across roots.
Each root’s probe regime must be adequate for its carrier semantics.
Cross-root probes must be compatible or bridged: commutation comparisons require that probes represent comparable semantic inputs across roots.
Boundary probes are mandatory when roots involve branch-sensitive or regularization-sensitive operators.
Inadequate probes yield refusal or probe escalation; RQF does not accept closure under inadequate probes.
Ch13.C1.c — Sensitivity to Perturbation
Closure must be robust under perturbation when required by policy.
Perturbation probes perturb parameters and probe inputs within admissible ranges.
Edge and face residuals must remain within gates under perturbations, within corridor perturbation tolerances.
Interior loop closure must remain stable under perturbations; otherwise the closure is brittle and non-promotable.
Perturbation failures classify as ill-conditioning, hidden boundary crossing, or non-identifiability; RQF refuses and provides remediation.
Ch13.C1.d — Refusal Semantics
RQF refusal semantics are structured and actionable.
Refusal due to missing root: produces missing root report and chart discovery obligations.
Refusal due to inadequate probes: produces adequacy report and probe escalation plan.
Refusal due to plateau: produces plateau certificate and suggests tunnels or carrier escalation.
Refusal due to drift: produces drift evidence and requires ConvFP or method migration remediation.
Refusals are replayable and become evidence for subsequent passes.
Ch13.C2 — Encode
Ch13.C2.a — Adequacy Packs
Adequacy packs are witness objects asserting probe adequacy.
probe generator id and scope.
stability region coverage and boundary probes.
adequacy proofs or check results required by policy.
links to UCWs that depend on the probe regime.
Adequacy packs are prerequisites for promotion.
Ch13.C2.b — Regression Packs
Regression packs record repeated verification of closure.
regression suite ids and results,
rerun of edge and face UCWs at specified κ,
rerun of interior Ω* loop suite,
bisect lineage for failures.
Regression packs are required before declaring long-term promotion stability.
Ch13.C2.c — Stress Packs
Stress packs record adversarial validation.
stress suite ids and adversary definitions,
stress outcomes on boundary probes and perturbations,
failures and counterexample objects,
quarantine triggers and rollback actions.
Stress packs gate high-risk closures.
Ch13.C2.d — Refusal Packs
Refusal packs for RQF include:
minimal failing closure obligation (edge/face/interior),
UCW ids and residual evidence,
probe adequacy status,
budgets consumed and blocked actions,
admissible next actions.
Refusal packs are used by scheduler and by atlas evolution logic.
Ch13.C3 — Prove
Ch13.C3.a — Closure Robustness Theorem
If RQF_OK holds under adequate probes and required stress/regression suites, then closure is robust within the declared corridor regime.
Edge and face UCWs remain within gates under perturbations specified by corridor/policy.
Interior loop closure remains within spin gates under perturbations and κ refinement.
Projection stability ensures that closure is not representation-dependent.
Regression ensures that closure persists under evolution, and drift detection prevents silent corruption.
Closure robustness is scoped: it holds only under pinned contexts and admissible regions.
Ch13.C3.b — Non-Identifiability Detection
Non-identifiability detection prevents false closure.
If multiple distinct modulus candidates satisfy closure under weak probes but diverge under stronger probes, then the earlier closure was non-identifiable.
If closure passes at low κ but fails at higher κ consistently, non-identifiability or drift is detected; RQF refuses.
Non-identifiability yields probe escalation obligations and may require richer carriers or portal escalation.
Non-identifiability is recorded as refusal evidence; it blocks promotion.
Ch13.C3.c — Plateau Logic
Plateau logic governs when closure cannot be achieved.
plateau on edges suggests missing transport witnesses or need for tunnels.
plateau on faces suggests higher-order coherence problems and may require new bridges or completion of missing face witnesses.
plateau on interior suggests that candidate modulus does not close the system or that portal is required.
plateau classification yields refusal or portal report with actionable remediation.
Ch13.C3.d — Confidence Stratification
Closure produces confidence stratification for the candidate node.
promoted if RQF_OK and governance gates pass.
certified if edge/face closure exists but interior closure or stress/regression incomplete; node is usable in limited contexts but not as kernel hub.
candidate if partial evidence exists but missing roots or missing coherence.
refused if closure fails under adequate probes and admissible actions exhausted.
Confidence stratification is recorded and governs reuse.
Ch13.C4 — Extract
Ch13.C4.a — Robustness Reports
Robustness reports extract perturbation and stress results.
margins to gates under perturbations,
boundary probe outcomes,
stability region coverage,
regression status.
Robustness reports are required for high-risk promotions.
Ch13.C4.b — Stress Results
Stress results extraction returns stress pack contents.
stress suite definitions,
adversarial probes,
pass/fail outcomes,
counterexamples and quarantines.
Ch13.C4.c — Regression Results
Regression results extraction returns regression pack contents.
suite ids and schedules,
rerun UCW outcomes,
drift alarms if any,
bisect lineage for failures.
Ch13.C4.d — Confidence Exports
Confidence exports package RQF outcomes for cross-site use.
Ω* certificate id and evidence pack,
root receipts and edge/face UCWs,
context hashes and scope limitations,
visibility mode constraints and minimal replay bundle pointers.
D. Fractal
Ch13.D1 — Spec
Ch13.D1.a — Atlas Evolution
Atlas evolution following RQF is structured and auditable.
add missing root charts via chart discovery jobs,
integrate new charts via transport witnesses,
update closure obligations and interior loop suite,
activate charts under governance and run regression/stress suites.
Atlas evolution never overwrites past evidence; it adds new artifacts and links them.
Ch13.D1.b — Root Upgrades
Root upgrades improve chart quality without changing meaning.
replace slow baseline charts with improved certified baseline charts,
add accel charts linked by transport UCWs,
improve bounds and rounding models,
maintain equivalence witnesses and rerun regression.
Root upgrades require migration edges and re-verification; they cannot be silent.
Ch13.D1.c — Acceleration Hooks
Acceleration hooks integrate AAS results into RQF.
AAS-produced charts enter as candidate charts in a root.
they are verified by certified evaluation and linked to baseline by transport UCWs.
if they improve defect or cost and pass governance gates, they can be activated.
activation triggers regression and stress as needed.
Acceleration hooks ensure performance improvements do not compromise coherence.
Ch13.D1.d — Compaction Rules
Compaction preserves replay while reducing storage.
store hashes and minimal replay bundles,
deduplicate identical UCWs and receipts,
preserve Ω* proof packs for promoted nodes,
preserve refusal evidence for portal classification and future remediation.
Compaction produces compaction receipts proving replay equivalence.
Ch13.D2 — Encode
Ch13.D2.a — Chart Activation States
Chart activation is encoded as a finite state machine:
inactive → candidate → certified → promoted → active → (deprecated | quarantined).
State transitions are governed by receipts and witness packs.
Ch13.D2.b — Chart Deprecation States
Deprecation encodes that a chart is no longer preferred but remains replayable.
deprecated charts remain available for audit and fallback unless explicitly quarantined.
deprecation reasons are recorded and linked to drift alarms or superseding charts.
deprecation triggers regression to ensure closure remains valid under remaining charts.
deprecation is scope-indexed; a chart may be deprecated under one policy but still usable under another.
Ch13.D2.c — Migration Edges
Migration edges relate atlas versions and chart versions.
migration edges record what changed: added charts, updated bounds, changed activation pointers.
migrations include witness obligations and receipts proving closure is preserved or improved.
migrations preserve backward witness sets for replay.
migrations are audit-linked and trigger regression.
Ch13.D2.d — Reference Pointers
Reference pointers enable extraction and routing.
pointers to preferred charts per root per context,
pointers to Ω* certificate packs for promoted nodes,
pointers to fallback charts and fallback closure routes,
pointers to portal reports and refusal packs where closure failed.
Pointers are governance metadata and do not alter semantic identity.
Ch13.D3 — Prove
Ch13.D3.a — Safe Upgrades
Upgrades are safe only if they preserve closure.
any upgrade that changes a chart or transport must preserve edge and face UCWs or produce new UCWs.
any upgrade that changes bounds or rounding ids must preserve certified enclosure correctness.
any upgrade that activates a new chart must preserve atlas coherence and interior Ω* closure.
regression must pass; failures quarantine upgraded components until resolved.
Ch13.D3.b — Safe Deprecations
Deprecations are safe only if closure remains satisfiable.
do not deprecate all certified charts of a root; preserve at least one baseline chart per root for promoted nodes.
do not remove transport witnesses required for coherence; if removal is necessary, downgrade promotion status and record reasons.
deprecations are logged and do not delete evidence.
deprecations trigger regression and update fallback closure routes.
Ch13.D3.c — Link Integrity
Link integrity ensures atlas references remain valid.
all chart ids and witness ids referenced exist and are decodable under schema versions.
witness roots expand deterministically and match merkle commitments.
context hashes match across linked artifacts.
broken links cause refusal and quarantine; they do not silently degrade closure.
Ch13.D3.d — Re-Verification Requirements
Re-verification is required under evolution.
any change to charts, bounds, rounding ids, projectors, transports, corridors, or policies that affect scope triggers re-verification of edge and face UCWs.
any change that affects interior loops triggers re-verification of Ω*.
re-verification failures quarantine affected nodes and produce remediation plans.
backward verification must remain possible via preserved verifiers or verified translators.
Ch13.D4 — Extract
Ch13.D4.a — Atlas Diffs
Atlas diffs compare atlas versions.
chart additions/removals and state transitions,
edge/face witness changes,
Ω* certificate changes,
context coverage changes (corridor/policy scopes),
reasons and governance events.
Ch13.D4.b — Upgrade Playbooks
Upgrade playbooks describe how to improve a promoted node safely.
identify performance bottlenecks and missing coverage,
schedule AAS for accel chart discovery,
verify charts and link by transport UCWs,
activate charts under governance and run regression/stress,
update pointers and preserve fallback.
Ch13.D4.c — Deprecation Playbooks
Deprecation playbooks describe safe retirement of charts or transports.
ensure alternate certified charts exist for affected roots,
ensure coherence and Ω* remain satisfiable under remaining charts,
