quarantine or deprecate with reasons and evidence,
rerun regression and update fallback closure routes.
Ch13.D4.d — Long-Term Roadmap
Long-term roadmap extraction converts RQF outcomes into structured future tasks.
missing roots → chart discovery programs,
interior failures → obstruction refinement or portal creation,
weak probe adequacy → probe regime upgrades and adversarial suites,
high-risk dependence → stress/regression expansion,
performance gaps → acceleration synthesis and activation.
Roadmap items are prioritized by demand and by dependency impact and are linked to refusal evidence and closure diffs.
Ch13 — Root-Quad Forcing (RQF) and Ω* Coherence
Ch14 — Atlas Acceleration Synthesis (AAS): Apéry and Beyond
A. Square
Ch14.A1 — Spec
Ch14.A1.a — Accel Chart Definition
An accel chart is a certified evaluator chart whose primary purpose is to reduce the computational cost of certified evaluation without altering semantic meaning. Accel charts are part of a node’s atlas (Ch06, Ch13) and are governed by the same certification discipline as baseline charts, with additional binding obligations to ensure they are not treated as independent truths.
Formally, for a node (v) representing an invariant (I), an accel chart (\mathsf{Chart}_{\mathrm{acc}}) is a chart object with:
Certified evaluation semantics. It produces certified enclosures (\mathcal E) of (I) under corridor gates via interval/tail/rounding methods (Ch11).
Accelerative convergence profile. For a target precision κ, its cost (\mathrm{Cost}_{\mathrm{acc}}(\kappa)) is strictly better than baseline under a policy-defined cost functional, or it reduces κ requirements to meet the same gates.
Transport binding. It is linked to at least one baseline chart (\mathsf{Chart}_{\mathrm{base}}) by a transport witness (UCW) under the relevant context, proving that accel and baseline represent the same invariant.
Admissible scope. It operates under the same ConvBundleFP semantic regime and within policy/corridor admissibility; any required convention change is not an acceleration chart but a portal/bridge event.
Accel charts are therefore “faster routes to the same truth,” and their legitimacy is wholly derived from certified evaluation receipts plus transport UCW binding to baseline.
Ch14.A1.b — Search vs Verify Separation
AAS enforces a strict separation between search (candidate generation) and verify (certified acceptance).
Search phase. Generates candidate chart specifications using heuristic or oracle computations (high-precision numeric approximants, PSLQ fits, experimental parameter sweeps). Search outputs are never promotion evidence.
Verify phase. Validates a candidate chart using certified receipts:
construct certified evaluation of candidate chart (interval + tail + rounding),
compare candidate enclosure to baseline certified enclosure under corridor gates,
compute UCW transport binding candidate ↔ baseline (commutation, holonomy, representability) under the same pinned context.
Separation invariant. No object produced purely in search may appear in witness roots of promotable receipts. Search artifacts may be stored only as candidate logs, never as certification evidence.
Deterministic boundary. Search may be nondeterministic only if explicitly permitted and seed-pinned; verification must be deterministic and replay-verifiable. Any nondeterminism that influences a promotable decision is forbidden.
This separation is the core mechanism that prevents spurious identities from becoming “truth tissue.”
Ch14.A1.c — Acceptance Predicate
AAS acceptance determines whether a candidate chart is certified as a valid evaluator chart for the invariant.
A candidate chart (\mathsf{Chart}) is accepted (as certified, not necessarily active) iff:
Certified evaluation. Under the pinned corridor (C), policy (P), and ConvBundleFP, the candidate chart yields a certified enclosure (\mathcal E_{\mathrm{cand}}(\kappa)) for each required κ in the corridor evaluation ladder (or under declared equivalence mapping).
Baseline compatibility. There exists a baseline certified enclosure (\mathcal E_{\mathrm{base}}(\kappa)) such that for each κ:[\mathcal E_{\mathrm{cand}}(\kappa)\cap \mathcal E_{\mathrm{base}}(\kappa) \neq \varnothing,]and the resulting difference residual (defined by corridor residual functional) satisfies the face gate. In scalar settings, this is equivalently: the interval residual contains 0 and the normalized discrepancy is within (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}).
Transport binding. A UCW exists certifying candidate↔baseline equivalence under tri-gate logic (commutation, holonomy, projection) on an adequate probe regime.
Assumption satisfaction. Tail bound assumptions and stability region constraints for the candidate are satisfied under the parameter regime.
Policy compliance. All method ids, bound ids, rounding ids used are permitted under policy; budgets are respected.
Acceptance yields a certified chart candidate that can be integrated into the atlas coherence graph but remains inactive until promotion gating completes.
Ch14.A1.d — Promotion Predicate
Promotion determines whether a certified accel chart becomes promoted and optionally active.
Promotion requires:
Acceptance holds as above.
Risk gates satisfied. If the chart is high-risk (new operator families, near stability boundaries, tunnel-derived), policy requires stress and regression suites. These suites must pass and produce witness packs.
Coherence preservation. Adding the chart does not break atlas coherence. Any newly introduced cycles in the chart graph must have loop closure witnesses (spin gates).
Activation governance. Activation as default or preferred evaluator requires governance pointers and is reversible; activation is recorded and triggers regression.
Promotion yields:
a promoted chart state in the atlas registry,
promotion receipts binding evaluation receipts, UCWs, and stress/regression packs,
optional activation pointers for route planning.
Ch14.A2 — Encode
Ch14.A2.a — Chart Template Schema
AAS operates over chart template families. A chart template is a typed parameterized schema specifying a family of candidate series/integrals/recurrences.
Minimal chart template schema includes:
Template id and family. E.g., Apéry-binomial, hypergeometric, Mellin transform, Euler-Maclaurin accelerated series, kernel semigroup chart, continued fraction chart.
Target invariant signature. Which node or class of nodes the template targets (e.g., odd zeta ladder, beta-even ladder, depth-two MZV candidates).
Parameter schema. Parameters describing the family:
basis features (harmonic monomials, polynomial degrees),
coefficient representation (integers, rationals),
suppression factors (binomial, factorial),
sign characters (alternating, Dirichlet characters),
truncation strategies (N).
Admissibility conditions. Stability regions and tail bound assumptions required for certification.
Witness hooks. Required transport binding to baseline and required probes for UCW.
Templates are versioned and hash-addressed. Template evolution occurs by migration.
Ch14.A2.b — Coefficient Bounds
Search is bounded by explicit coefficient bounds to ensure termination and reproducibility.
Max coefficient. For integer relation search (PSLQ), a maximum coefficient magnitude (B) is specified.
Denominator bounds. For rational coefficients, denominators are bounded.
Feature bounds. The number of basis features (monomials) is bounded.
Deterministic escalation schedule. Bounds are increased in a fixed sequence (e.g., (10^4,10^5,10^6)), recorded as enumerator states. Any deviation yields a different tunnel event identity.
Coefficient bounds are part of replay fields; they are not optional metadata.
Ch14.A2.c — Truncation Bounds
Certified evaluation requires deterministic selection of truncation depth (N) or analogous discretization controls.
Search truncation (N_{\mathrm{fit}}). Candidate discovery may use a fixed (N_{\mathrm{fit}}) to compute approximate basis values used by PSLQ. This is oracle-level and non-authoritative.
Verify truncation (N_{\mathrm{ver}}). Certified verification uses (N_{\mathrm{ver}}(\kappa)) selected deterministically to satisfy error budgets under tail bounds and rounding. (N_{\mathrm{ver}}) is part of certified receipts.
Termination guarantee. Truncation selection must terminate under budgets; infinite “increase N until it works” loops are forbidden.
Parameter binding. (N) choices are bound into chart evaluation receipts and into WordNF segments.
Ch14.A2.d — Tail Bound Schema
Tail bound schema is the certification core for AAS.
Bound id. Each template family declares a tail bound id with provenance and assumptions.
Bound evaluation rules. Bound evaluation must be deterministic and must produce conservative error envelopes.
Bound composition rules. If the template includes products or nested sums, bound composition rules must be declared; missing rules block certification.
Bound audit hooks. Bound derivations are addressable and are included in audit packs. Any failure of assumptions yields refusal.
Ch14.A3 — Prove
Ch14.A3.a — Verify-Only Promotion Theorem
AAS promotion is sound only if promotion is based solely on certified verification evidence.
Theorem (Verify-Only Promotion). If a chart is promoted by AAS, then there exists a proof slice consisting of:
baseline certified receipts,
candidate certified receipts,
transport UCWs candidate↔baseline,
any required stress/regression packs,such that an independent verifier can replay all acceptance predicates and reproduce the promotion decision under pinned context hashes.
Corollary: no oracle computation, PSLQ fit, or heuristic similarity is sufficient for promotion; such artifacts may appear only as non-authoritative candidate logs.
Ch14.A3.b — No Oracle Trust Theorem
Oracle computations are permitted only for candidate generation.
Theorem (No Oracle Trust). Any equivalence claim between candidate chart and baseline must be certified using interval/tail/rounding receipts and UCW tri-gate witnesses. If the claim depends on oracle value evaluation, it is non-promotable and must be tagged heuristic.
Oracle-derived candidates that fail certified verification are rejected and recorded as failures; repeated oracle success does not substitute for certification.
Ch14.A3.c — Tail Bound Correctness
Tail bound correctness is required for certified evaluation.
Assumption correctness. Tail bound assumptions must be checkable or explicitly asserted under policy. Uncheckable assumptions restrict promotability or require stronger policies.
Conservativeness. Bound must dominate the true truncation error. Any evidence of underestimation yields drift alarms and quarantine of the bound id.
κ coupling correctness. For each κ, truncation depth (N(\kappa)) is chosen so that bound (B(N)) plus rounding cushion is below target tolerance. Incorrect coupling yields failure to meet corridor gates, not silent acceptance.
Composition correctness. For composite templates, bound composition must be correct; missing composition rules forbid promotion.
Tail bound correctness is enforced by bound audits and replay verification.
Ch14.A3.d — Transport Binding Requirement
Every accel chart must be transport-bound to baseline.
Binding UCW. A UCW must certify candidate↔baseline equivalence under tri-gate logic on adequate probes. This prevents accel charts from becoming independent truths.
Loop closure. Adding accel charts introduces cycles; loop closure must be certified within spin gates, preventing path-dependent semantics.
Projection stability. Candidate and baseline must be stable under projection, and projection commutation must hold where required.
Context indexing. Binding is indexed by ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor; accel charts certified under one context cannot be used under another without bridging.
Transport binding is mandatory for chart activation and for safe substitution.
Ch14.A4 — Extract
Ch14.A4.a — Discovered Charts List
Discovered charts list includes all charts accepted and/or promoted.
chart ids and template family ids.
root assignments.
method/bound/rounding ids.
contexts where charts are certified or promoted.
transport UCWs linking charts to baseline.
Ch14.A4.b — Rejected Charts List
Rejected charts list includes all failed candidates.
candidate template parameters and coefficient bounds.
failure reasons: failed certified verification, invalid bounds, scope gaps, policy blocks.
minimal counterexamples and residual evidence.
budgets consumed and pruning hints.
Rejected charts are preserved as evidence for pruning and for future synthesis.
Ch14.A4.c — Proof Packs
Proof packs provide minimal replay bundles for accepted/promoted charts.
baseline receipts.
candidate receipts.
transport UCWs and loop closure witnesses.
stress/regression packs if required.
WordNF segment streams including tunnel events.
Proof packs are hash-committed and exportable under visibility modes.
Ch14.A4.d — Performance Packs
Performance packs provide non-semantic evaluation performance data.
cost model as function of κ.
digits-per-term or convergence rate estimates.
verification overhead estimates (probe costs, UCW costs).
recommended activation contexts and fallback plans.
Performance packs guide scheduling and routing but do not affect semantic acceptance.
B. Flower
Ch14.B1 — Spec
Ch14.B1.a — Deterministic Enumeration Order
AAS candidate exploration is deterministic.
candidate template parameter spaces are ordered canonically.
coefficient bounds are escalated in a fixed schedule.
feature sets (monomial bases) are expanded in a fixed order.
truncation parameters (N_{\mathrm{fit}}) and (N_{\mathrm{ver}}) are selected deterministically.
Enumeration order is recorded in enumerator state and is part of tunnel event replay fields. Determinism ensures reproducible discovery.
Ch14.B1.b — Integer Relation Search
Integer relation search (e.g., PSLQ) is used to discover rational combinations of basis series that match a target numeric oracle.
PSLQ is used only in search phase to propose coefficient vectors.
PSLQ output is candidate-only evidence and cannot be promoted without certified verification.
PSLQ parameters (maxcoeff, precision, basis dimension) are bound and logged.
PSLQ failure yields candidate log entries and influences pruning.
Integer relation search is treated as a generator, not a proof mechanism.
Ch14.B1.c — Route Binding
AAS binds discovered charts into the metro by creating explicit route objects.
chart evaluation route WordNFs record evaluation steps, bounds, rounding, and projection steps.
candidate→baseline transport WordNF records commutation witness evaluation steps.
tunnel events record the discovery and integration step.
route binding ensures that chart selection is auditable and replayable.
Ch14.B1.d — Chart Switching Rules
Chart switching rules govern safe use of accel charts.
switch only to charts certified and active under the target context.
require transport binding to baseline and preserve projection stability.
record switching via WordNF segments or atlas activation state.
provide fallback to baseline if accel chart is unavailable or quarantined.
Chart switching never introduces semantic changes; it changes only evaluation routes.
Ch14.B2 — Encode
Ch14.B2.a — Candidate Logs
Candidate logs record each enumerated candidate.
template id and parameters.
enumerator state id and budgets consumed.
oracle evidence used (tagged heuristic and non-authoritative).
candidate status: rejected, pending verification, verified, promoted.
Candidate logs enable reproducible discovery and pruning.
Ch14.B2.b — PSLQ/Fit Logs
PSLQ logs record the integer relation search process.
basis values computed at (N_{\mathrm{fit}}) and oracle precision.
PSLQ parameters and results (relation vector).
normalization of coefficients (gcd, sign normalization).
reasons for failure (no relation found within bounds).
PSLQ logs are not promotion evidence but are stored for audit and reproduction.
Ch14.B2.c — Verification Logs
Verification logs record certified verification of candidates.
candidate certified evaluation receipts (intervals, tails, rounding).
baseline receipts used for comparison.
residual interval containment evidence and tri-gate outcomes.
transport UCWs and loop closure witnesses.
Verification logs are promotion evidence and are replayable.
Ch14.B2.d — Promotion Logs
Promotion logs record chart promotion/activation events.
promotion receipts and witness roots.
stress/regression suite results and pass status.
atlas registry updates and activation state transitions.
rollback events if later regression fails.
Promotion logs are governance artifacts and are auditable.
Ch14.B3 — Prove
Ch14.B3.a — Determinism of Search Order
Determinism is required for reproducible AAS.
enumeration order is fixed and recorded.
tie-breaking is deterministic.
any randomness must be forbidden or seed-pinned; seed affects tunnel event identity.
the same inputs and budgets yield the same candidate stream and the same verification outcomes, under pinned backends.
Determinism ensures discovery can be replayed and audited.
Ch14.B3.b — Reproducibility of Discovery
Reproducibility means the same candidate can be found and verified again.
candidate is defined by template parameters and coefficient vector.
certified verification uses deterministic evaluation and bounds.
transport UCWs recompute residual curves deterministically.
thus a discovery is a reproducible proof-carrying artifact, not a lucky numeric coincidence.
Ch14.B3.c — Splice Equivalence
Integrating an accel chart into an existing atlas requires splice equivalence.
replacing a subroute with accel evaluator must preserve semantic intent under witnessed equivalence.
splice equivalence is certified by transport UCWs and commutation closure.
any new cycles introduced require loop closure witnesses.
without splice equivalence, accel chart remains candidate-only and cannot be activated.
Ch14.B3.d — Rollback Correctness
Rollback correctness ensures unsafe accel charts can be deactivated without corrupting truth.
rollback is recorded as governance event; evidence preserved.
fallback routes revert to baseline charts; evaluability preserved.
regression failures quarantine chart and invalidate activation pointers.
rollback does not delete receipts; it changes activation state and blocks promotion-critical routing.
Rollback correctness is essential for safe evolution.
Ch14.B4 — Extract
Ch14.B4.a — Auto-Tunnel Routes
Auto-tunnel route extraction provides the explicit WordNF sequences used for discovery and integration.
candidate generation segments (search), tagged heuristic.
certified verification segments (interval/tail), tagged certified.
transport binding UCW segments.
activation and governance segments.
Routes are replay-addressable and audit-ready.
Ch14.B4.b — Best Charts by Weight
For graded domains, charts can be indexed by weight/depth.
list charts per invariant and per weight.
list convergence rates and costs.
list contexts where charts are certified/promoted.
list transport bindings to baseline.
This supports systematic ladder building (odd zeta ladder, beta-even ladder, etc.).
Ch14.B4.c — Best Charts by Latency
Latency-based selection uses performance packs.
filter by certified and active charts under context.
rank by cost model at target κ.
include verification overhead and risk overhead.
provide fallback to baseline.
Latency ranking is non-semantic and auditable.
Ch14.B4.d — Fallback Charts
Fallback charts extraction provides safe alternatives.
baseline per root.
alternate certified charts per root.
conditions that trigger fallback (quarantine, policy forbiddance).
witness packs required for fallback selection.
Fallback charts ensure reliability under evolution.
C. Cloud
Ch14.C1 — Spec
Ch14.C1.a — Verification with Certified Receipts
AAS verification uses certified receipts exclusively.
candidate evaluation is certified (interval + tail + rounding).
baseline evaluation is certified.
residual containment checks are performed using certified enclosures.
transport binding UCWs are computed using certified evaluation and adequate probes.
No oracle evidence is used for acceptance.
Ch14.C1.b — Perturbation Tests
Perturbation tests ensure candidate charts are stable.
small perturbations of template parameters and probe inputs.
verify enclosure containment and gate satisfaction under perturbations.
detect ill-conditioning and boundary sensitivity.
required for high-risk charts per policy.
Perturbation failures trigger rejection or quarantine.
Ch14.C1.c — Regression Tests
Regression tests ensure accel charts remain correct over time.
rerun certified verification for standard κ levels.
rerun transport UCWs to baseline.
rerun loop closure witnesses for cycles introduced.
bisect failures by hash lineage.
Regression failures trigger deactivation and remediation.
Ch14.C1.d — Stress Tests
Stress tests target adversarial regimes.
branch boundary probes if template uses multivalued functions.
near-singularity probes for analytic continuation.
extreme parameter probes for ill-conditioning.
worst-case commutation probes.
Stress tests are required by policy for high-risk templates and gate promotion.
Ch14.C2 — Encode
Ch14.C2.a — Interval Residual Packs
Interval residual packs record certified residual evidence.
baseline interval and candidate interval.
residual interval and containment test result.
normalized residual measure and margin to gates.
provenance pointers: method/bound/rounding ids.
Residual packs are replayable.
Ch14.C2.b — Tail Derivation Packs
Tail derivation packs record tail bound provenance.
bound id and derivation reference.
bound parameters and evaluated bound values.
assumption region checks and outcomes.
composition rule references.
Tail packs support audit and reuse.
Ch14.C2.c — Regression Packs
Regression packs record regression suite outcomes.
suite ids and context.
rerun evidence and pass/fail.
drift alarms detected.
bisect lineage.
Regression packs gate continued activation.
Ch14.C2.d — Stress Packs
Stress packs record stress test outcomes.
suite ids and adversarial probes.
pass/fail outcomes.
counterexample objects for failures.
quarantine and rollback actions.
Stress packs support safe promotion.
Ch14.C3 — Prove
Ch14.C3.a — Residual Containment ⇒ Acceptance
Acceptance requires residual containment.
candidate enclosure must overlap baseline enclosure at required κ.
normalized discrepancy must satisfy (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}).
projection stability must satisfy (\varepsilon_\Pi).
loop closure must satisfy (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) when chart integration introduces cycles.
Residual containment is necessary but not sufficient; transport binding is also required.
Ch14.C3.b — Stress-Pass ⇒ Promotable
For high-risk charts, stress-pass is necessary for promotion.
stress-pass indicates chart does not fail under adversarial probes.
stress-pass complements, not replaces, certified verification.
stress-pass does not imply equivalence; UCW binding still required.
failure yields quarantine and rollback.
Ch14.C3.c — Plateau ⇒ Reject
Candidate charts are rejected when plateaued.
if certified residuals cannot meet gates as κ increases within budgets, reject or keep as candidate-only.
plateau indicates either insufficient convergence or bound looseness; bound upgrades or template changes are required.
plateaued candidates are logged and pruned from active consideration.
repeated plateau across templates may indicate need for new representation family or portal.
Ch14.C3.d — Drift ⇒ Reject
Drift indicates hidden semantic or implementation instability.
if candidate verification behavior changes across replays under fixed context, drift is detected.
if projection stability fails inconsistently, drift is detected.
drift yields rejection and remediation (ConvFP schema repair, method migration, backend pinning).
drift evidence is stored and triggers regression updates.
Ch14.C4 — Extract
Ch14.C4.a — Confidence Levels
Confidence levels for accel charts are extracted.
candidate-only (search success, not verified).
certified (verified under certified receipts, transport binding exists).
promoted (certified + stress/regression pass).
active (promoted + activated by governance pointer).
Confidence levels guide routing and scheduling.
Ch14.C4.b — Risk Levels
Risk levels are extracted per chart.
low risk: known templates, wide stability region, simple operators.
medium risk: new coefficients or moderate boundary sensitivity.
high risk: near singularities, new operator families, deep search required.
Risk levels determine stress/regression requirements.
Ch14.C4.c — Refusal Evidence
Refusal evidence extraction includes:
rejected candidate logs and reasons.
minimal counterexamples.
budget exhaustion evidence.
remediation suggestions.
Refusal evidence guides future synthesis and pruning.
Ch14.C4.d — Remediation Suggestions
Remediation suggestions specify admissible next actions.
change template family.
increase coefficient bounds or basis features (search).
tighten tail bounds or use improved bound ids.
expand probes or use adversarial probes.
escalate to new carrier or portal if repeated failures occur.
Suggestions are deterministic and audit-linked.
D. Fractal
Ch14.D1 — Spec
Ch14.D1.a — Chart Lifecycle
Accel charts follow a governed lifecycle.
candidate (discovered by search).
verified/certified (passes certified verification and transport binding).
promoted (passes governance gates including stress/regression).
active (selected by default pointers for route planning).
deprecated/quarantined (due to drift alarms, regression failures, supersession).
Lifecycle transitions are logged and replayable.
Ch14.D1.b — Activation Policies
Activation policies specify when a promoted chart becomes selectable.
activate only if it improves cost under demand signals and remains within risk tolerances.
activate only if baseline fallback exists.
activate only if transport binding to baseline is promotable and loop closure remains satisfied.
activation triggers regression.
Ch14.D1.c — Deactivation Policies
Deactivation policies ensure safety.
deactivate if regression fails.
deactivate if drift alarms occur.
deactivate if policy changes forbid required operators.
deactivate if superseded by better chart with equal or better risk profile.
Deactivation preserves auditability and fallback.
Ch14.D1.d — Scheduler Integration
AAS integrates with scheduler via demand signals.
high-demand nodes trigger AAS.
plateaued nodes trigger AAS to discover faster converging charts.
risk overhead and stress/regression cost are included in scheduling.
AAS outputs are fed into atlas evolution and RQF closure checks where needed.
Ch14.D2 — Encode
Ch14.D2.a — Lifecycle States
Lifecycle states are encoded explicitly and context-indexed.
state transitions recorded as governance events.
per-context certification coverage maps.
quarantine flags and reasons.
deprecation markers and migration links.
Ch14.D2.b — Usage Stats
Usage stats support scheduling.
call volume and κ distribution.
latency measurements and predicted costs.
failure incidence and regression failures.
stats are non-semantic metadata and do not affect acceptance.
Ch14.D2.c — Invalidation Rules
Invalidation prevents stale accel chart reuse.
invalidate on method/bound/rounding id changes.
invalidate on corridor/policy changes.
invalidate on ConvFP scope changes.
invalidate on regression or stress failures.
Invalidation triggers deactivation/quarantine and reruns verification as needed.
Ch14.D2.d — Cache Keys
Cache keys support memoization.
keyed by node id, chart id, context hashes, κ, and parameters.
memoization stores receipts and evidence pointers, not semantic claims.
caches invalidate under invalidation rules.
caches are audited by regression.
Ch14.D3 — Prove
Ch14.D3.a — No Stale Accel Reuse
No stale reuse ensures charts remain safe.
route planner selects only charts active under current context with current receipts.
cached evidence is revalidated against context hashes.
quarantined charts excluded from certified routing.
fallback to baseline ensures continuity.
Ch14.D3.b — Activation Safety
Activation safety ensures charts do not break atlas coherence.
activation requires transport binding and loop closure witnesses.
activation triggers regression; pass required.
activation is reversible by rollback.
activation cannot occur if it removes last safe fallback.
Ch14.D3.c — Deactivation Safety
Deactivation safety ensures removing charts does not break promoted claims.
ensure baseline charts remain.
ensure RQF closure remains satisfiable.
ensure fallback routes exist.
preserve evidence and audit trails.
Ch14.D3.d — Performance Monotonicity Under Policy
Performance monotonicity states that performance improvements do not compromise policy safety.
charts activated must not require forbidden operators.
performance improvements cannot be achieved by relaxing certification requirements.
charts must maintain or strengthen evidence coverage.
scheduler uses performance models but does not alter acceptance predicates.
Ch14.D4 — Extract
Ch14.D4.a — Chart Dashboards
Chart dashboards summarize chart health.
certified/promoted/active charts per node and per root.
context coverage maps.
regression/stress status.
drift alarms and quarantines.
Ch14.D4.b — Performance Dashboards
Performance dashboards summarize cost.
latency vs κ curves.
speedups over baseline.
verification overhead.
recommendations for activation and tuning.
Ch14.D4.c — Cache Dashboards
Cache dashboards summarize memoization.
cache hit rates.
invalidation incidence.
stale reuse prevented.
audit status.
Ch14.D4.d — Maintenance Dashboards
Maintenance dashboards summarize required work.
missing transport bindings.
charts pending stress/regression.
plateaued nodes needing new template families.
portal candidates triggered by repeated failures.
Ch14 — Atlas Acceleration Synthesis (AAS): Apéry and Beyond
Ch15 — Seed Promotion Pipeline (SPP): Dimension Models and Forced Generators
A. Square
Ch15.A1 — Spec
Ch15.A1.a — Dimension Model (d_w)
The Seed Promotion Pipeline (SPP) formalizes growth of the metro’s invariant basis as a governed, verifiable process driven by a dimension model. The dimension model is a domain-specific prediction or axiom-set specifying the expected dimension of the invariant space at each weight (w) (and, when applicable, depth (d)). SPP uses the model to detect deficiency, to schedule forced generators, and to convert repeated non-reduction evidence into promotable primitives.
Let (\mathcal V_w) denote the vector space (or module) of invariants at weight (w) under a fixed semantic regime (ConvBundleFP, invariant schema, policy class). The dimension model is a function:[d_w := \dim(\mathcal V_w),]or, in refined form,[d_{w,\delta} := \dim(\mathcal V_{w,\delta}),]where (\delta) indexes depth, level, or additional grading.
A dimension model is not a claim of truth by narrative; it is a governance contract used by SPP to decide which objects must exist for completeness under the declared model. The model must be pinned by:
a model id and schema hash,
declared scope (which invariant family it applies to),
declared admissibility region.
SPP treats (d_w) as an external specification and derives internal obligations to match it by constructing a generator set and proving relations or non-relations via certified evidence.
Ch15.A1.b — Forced Degree Multiset
Given a dimension model, SPP constructs a forced degree multiset (G^\star), the minimal multiset of generator degrees (weights) required such that the graded monomial count matches the dimension model at each weight.
Let a generator multiset be (G={g_1,g_2,\dots,g_m}). Define the generating function:[F_G(x) := \prod_{g\in G} (1-x^g)^{-1} = \sum_{w\ge 0} f_w(G),x^w,]where (f_w(G)) counts the number of monomials in the generators of total weight (w) (with multiplicity).
The forced degree multiset (G^\star) is defined by the minimality condition:[\forall w\le W_{\max}:\quad f_w(G^\star);\ge; d_w,]with a deterministic selection rule that adds a generator of degree (w) whenever (f_w(G)\lt d_w) at that weight. The rule is:
start from a specified seed set (G_0) (domain-specific, often including degree 2 collapse generator where appropriate),
for (w=1) to (W_{\max}), if current (f_w(G)\lt d_w), append (w) to (G) until the inequality holds,
canonicalize (G) as an ordered multiset.
This produces a deterministic “required generator schedule” driven by deficiency. The multiset is an obligation object: it specifies weights at which new primitives must be introduced unless the dimension model itself is revised.
Ch15.A1.c — Generator Scheduling
Generator scheduling is the mapping from forced degrees and deficiency signals to concrete candidate node proposals and verification jobs.
Forced generation times. For each weight (w) such that adding generators at (w) is required by the forced degree multiset, SPP schedules a generator introduction. This introduction is not automatic promotion; it is a candidate creation and a verification plan.
Canonical candidate families. For each required generator at weight (w), SPP selects canonical families of candidates (domain-dependent). For example, in zeta/MZV-like domains, candidates may include:
depth-1 odd zeta generators at specific weights,
depth-2 MZV candidates at specific weights,
other structured candidates that satisfy known collapse relations.
Verification tasks. Scheduling creates:
reduction tasks (try to express candidate in current basis),
non-reduction tasks (try to certify independence under corridor),
atlas forcing tasks (build root receipts and coherence witnesses),
regression/stress tasks (if promotion is high-risk).
Deterministic scheduling. Scheduling is deterministic given the model id, existing promoted basis, and policy/corridor constraints. Any nondeterministic selection is forbidden for promotable scheduling decisions.
Scheduling is therefore a compiler stage: it transforms model deficiency into explicit jobs and candidate artifacts.
Ch15.A1.d — Depth Notion
Depth is a second grading used when invariants are not fully characterized by weight alone. Depth is defined by a domain-specific decomposition rule pinned in schema.
Depth interface. Each invariant node includes a depth field (d\in\mathbb N), interpreted as the minimal depth of representation under a pinned algebra of generators. Examples:
depth-1: single primitive (e.g., (\zeta(k))),
depth-2: nested structures (e.g., (\zeta(a,b))),
higher depth: nested iterated integrals or multiple sums.
Depth stratified dimension model. If available, (d_{w,\delta}) refines (d_w) and provides stronger scheduling guidance.
Depth constraints and collapse laws. Depth interacts with collapse edges (closed forms that reduce depth) and with known relations (e.g., depth-reduction theorems). Such laws are pinned by schema and must be witnessed.
Promotion by depth. Promotion obligations differ by depth: higher depth often requires richer probe regimes, stronger regression/stress suites, and stronger non-reduction evidence due to increased risk of spurious relations.
Depth notion is a structural lens for “what remains undeveloped,” and SPP uses it to systematically fill undeveloped regions.
Ch15.A2 — Encode
Ch15.A2.a — Dimension Tables
Dimension tables encode (d_w) (and (d_{w,\delta}) when available) as canonical records.
Table schema. Includes model id, scope, admissibility region, and the sequence ({d_w}{w\le W{\max}}) (and optionally (d_{w,\delta}) matrices).
Versioning. Tables are versioned; updates create migration edges and require re-evaluation of deficiency and forced generator schedules.
Compatibility. Dimension tables are scoped by invariant family and semantic regime; mixing tables across regimes is forbidden without explicit bridges.
Auditability. Table provenance is recorded (source assumptions, model derivation, or accepted axioms), but promotion depends on verification evidence, not on table provenance.
Ch15.A2.b — Generator Multiset Encoding
The forced degree multiset (G^\star) is encoded as a canonical multiset record.
ordered list of degrees with multiplicities,
computed monomial counts (f_w(G^\star)) for (w\le W_{\max}),
deficiency witnesses showing where generator insertions were required,
deterministic construction trace (seed set (G_0), insertion steps).
Generator multiset records are used by schedulers to generate “must exist” lists.
Ch15.A2.c — Schedule Encoding
Schedules are encoded as job graphs.
job nodes: candidate creation, reduction attempt, non-reduction attempt, atlas forcing, stress/regression.
dependency edges: a candidate promotion depends on reduction outcomes and on atlas forcing completion.
context hashes: each job binds policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP context.
budgets: each job has explicit budgets and termination guarantees.
Schedules are replayable and auditable.
Ch15.A2.d — Provenance Encoding
Provenance records why a generator was scheduled.
deficiency at weight (w): (f_w(G)\lt d_w),
relation evidence: inability to express candidate in existing basis within certified tolerances,
obstruction evidence: stable holonomy indicates missing modulus/seed,
cross-metro evidence: imported candidates and their compatibility reports,
governance decisions: policy limitations and risk gating.
Provenance is pinned and used for audit; it does not substitute for verification.
Ch15.A3 — Prove
Ch15.A3.a — Deficiency Criterion
Deficiency is the formal condition that the current promoted generator set cannot span the model-predicted space.
Let (G) be the multiset of promoted generator degrees. Compute (f_w(G)) from the generating function. Deficiency at weight (w) is:[\mathrm{Def}(w) := \max(0,d_w - f_w(G)).]If (\mathrm{Def}(w)>0), then at least (\mathrm{Def}(w)) new independent generators are required at weights (\le w), and by the canonical construction rule, they are scheduled at weight (w) unless earlier weights are also deficient.
Deficiency criterion is purely combinatorial under the model. Its correctness is the correctness of the generating function computation and the deterministic rule, both verifiable.
Ch15.A3.b — Forced-Generator Criterion
A forced generator at weight (w) is a candidate whose promotion is required to remove deficiency, provided no existing relations collapse it into the current basis.
Forced generator criterion is satisfied when:
deficiency requires at least one new generator at (w) (via (G^\star)),
no certified reduction exists expressing the candidate in the current basis,
certified evidence supports independence (non-reduction certificate) within corridor tolerances,
atlas forcing (Ch13) yields a coherent 4-root atlas and, when required, Ω* closure.
Forced generator criterion does not assume which candidate is correct; it enforces that some promotable candidate must exist to satisfy the model.
Ch15.A3.c — Non-Redundancy Criterion
Non-redundancy ensures that new promoted generators are not redundant with existing ones.
Let (B_w) be the current promoted basis at weight (w). A candidate (c) at weight (w) is non-redundant if:
No certified reduction: there does not exist a coefficient vector (\alpha) such that:[c \approx \sum_{b\in B_w}\alpha_b,b]under corridor gates, with certified coefficient evidence and representability stability.
Certified non-reduction: there exists a certified non-reduction witness showing that all attempted reductions within bounded coefficient complexity fail under certified residual checks and adequate probes, and that failure is stable under κ refinement.
Robustness: non-reduction is robust under perturbation probes and under alternative chart evaluations, preventing spurious independence due to method artifacts.
Non-redundancy is the operational meaning of “new primitive,” and it is certified by refusal of all reductions under bounded search plus stability evidence.
Ch15.A3.d — Promotion Obligations
Promotion obligations for a new generator candidate include:
Certified evaluation receipts in at least one root sufficient to support verification, and ultimately root-quad coverage for promotion as a hub.
Transport coherence witnesses linking charts and roots via UCWs, ensuring candidate is not chart-dependent.
Reduction attempt obligations: attempt to reduce candidate into existing basis using bounded search and certified residual checks; record success or non-reduction evidence.
Non-reduction certificate when reduction fails: plateau under bounded coefficient complexity with certified evidence.
Stress/regression obligations for high-risk promotions, especially for depth>1 candidates.
Receipt binding: pins and witness roots that enable independent replay of the promotion decision.
Promotion obligations are explicit and become job plans; they are not implied.
Ch15.A4 — Extract
Ch15.A4.a — “What Must Exist” List
This list is computed deterministically from the forced degree multiset and current basis coverage.
required generator counts per weight (w) up to a target (W_{\max}),
canonical target types per weight (depth-1 vs depth-2 candidates),
explicit job plans for each required generator,
contexts (policy/corridor) under which promotion is sought.
The “must exist” list is a model-relative obligation list, not a claim that specific candidates are correct.
Ch15.A4.b — “What May Exist” List
This list enumerates candidate families not forced by deficiency but admissible as optional additions.
candidates that might reduce cost or simplify coherence, even if not required by dimension model,
candidates suggested by obstruction evidence or by cross-metro imports,
candidates that might be redundant but useful for acceleration or as alternate basis.
Each “may exist” item includes a verification plan and non-redundancy checks if promotion is attempted.
Ch15.A4.c — Obligations List
Obligations list per weight and candidate includes:
required reductions to attempt,
required non-reduction evidence,
required root receipts and closure witnesses,
required stress/regression suites.
Obligations lists are used by schedulers and by audit tooling.
Ch15.A4.d — Test Plan List
Test plan list encodes the verification experiments to validate basis completeness.
relation search tasks at each weight,
independence tests using certified non-reduction certificates,
atlas coherence tests for promoted nodes,
regression/stress suites ensuring stability over time.
Test plans are deterministic and replay-addressable.
B. Flower
Ch15.B1 — Spec
Ch15.B1.a — Collapse Edges (Closed Forms)
Collapse edges encode known closed forms that reduce generator requirements.
collapse edges are semantic edges requiring certified coefficient evidence and representability stability.
collapse edges reduce the effective dimension of generator needs by expressing certain invariants as combinations of lower-level hubs.
collapse edges must be scoped by ConvBundleFP and policy; different conventions may yield different normalizations and require bridges.
collapse edges are used by basis construction to reduce redundancy and to define derived nodes.
Ch15.B1.b — Reduction Edges (Basis Expressions)
Reduction edges express a node as a linear combination of basis elements at the same weight.
reductions are certified by coefficient proofs, residual checks, and projection stability.
reductions are scoped to corridor; tighter corridors may reject a reduction accepted under wider corridors.
reductions must be replayable: coefficient derivations and residual computations are pinned.
reductions contribute to “relations” in the space and reduce the need for new generators.
Ch15.B1.c — Promote Edges (New Primitives)
Promote edges represent the act of promoting a candidate to a new primitive generator.
promotion requires non-redundancy evidence and atlas coherence (Ch13).
promotion creates new basis elements and updates generator multiset coverage.
promotion is governed by policy and requires stress/regression gates when high-risk.
promotion is auditable: all evidence is pinned in receipts.
Ch15.B1.d — Dependency Edges
Dependency edges encode how reductions and promotions depend on prior artifacts.
dependencies include basis definitions, coefficient proofs, UCWs, evaluation receipts, and policies/corridors.
dependencies are typed and require membership proofs for set-based dependencies.
dependency closure ensures no hidden assumptions.
dependency graphs are used for proof slicing and export.
Ch15.B2 — Encode
Ch15.B2.a — Reduction Scripts
Reduction scripts are route words that implement reduction attempts.
specify basis set (B_w),
specify coefficient search strategy (bounded complexity),
specify certified evaluation and residual checks,
specify projection and canonicalization steps.
Reduction scripts are WordNF-encoded and replayable.
Ch15.B2.b — Coefficient Proofs
Coefficient proofs certify the coefficients in a reduction.
coefficients are represented as rationals or algebraic numbers, with canonical normalization.
coefficient discovery may use heuristic methods in search phase but must be verified by certified residual checks.
coefficient proofs include bounds on coefficient complexity and residual containment.
coefficient proofs are replayable and are part of reduction receipts.
Ch15.B2.c — Interval Checks
Interval checks certify reductions.
compute certified enclosure for candidate and for linear combination of basis.
compute residual interval and verify containment within corridor gates.
verify stability across κ and adequate probes.
record residual curves and margins to gates.
Interval checks are the core verification mechanism for reductions.
Ch15.B2.d — Refusal Scripts
Refusal scripts encode non-reduction evidence.
bounded relation search attempts fail under certified checks,
failure persists under κ refinement,
failure persists across multiple charts and probes,
record plateau and counterexamples.
Refusal scripts produce non-reduction certificates when conditions are met.
Ch15.B3 — Prove
Ch15.B3.a — Reduction Correctness
A reduction is correct iff there exists a reduction receipt certifying that candidate equals the basis combination under corridor gates.
certified evaluation receipts exist for both sides,
residual interval contains 0 across κ ladder,
projection stability holds,
probes adequate, and commutation/holonomy obligations satisfied when reductions participate in loops.
Reduction correctness is scoped and replayable.
Ch15.B3.b — Reduction Failure ⇒ Candidate
Reduction failure does not automatically imply independence. It yields a candidate status with required further evidence.
if reduction fails due to policy block or inadequate probes, it is not evidence of independence.
if reduction fails due to plateau above gates under adequate probes and stable contexts, it is evidence supporting candidate independence.
reduction failure must be stable under κ refinement and method substitution to support candidate promotion.
candidate status triggers atlas forcing and further robustness tests.
Reduction failure is interpreted under strict rules to avoid spurious primitives.
Ch15.B3.c — Bounded Search Honesty
Bounded search honesty prevents claiming independence without exploring sufficient relation space.
relation search is bounded by coefficient complexity caps and basis feature caps.
caps are explicit and recorded; they are part of the refusal evidence.
bounded search cannot conclude absolute independence; it concludes “no relation found within caps” plus stability evidence.
promotion requires that bounded non-reduction evidence be sufficiently robust and consistent with the dimension model and with cross-root atlases.
Honesty is enforced by refusal scripts and by explicit caps.
Ch15.B3.d — Promotion Gating
Promotion gating enforces that new primitives are promoted only with strong evidence.
satisfy forced-generator criterion where applicable.
satisfy non-redundancy criterion (non-reduction certificate).
satisfy atlas coherence and Ω* closure when required.
satisfy policy-required stress/regression suites.
bind promotion receipt with pins and witness roots enabling independent replay.
Promotion gating prevents contaminating the basis with spurious candidates.
Ch15.B4 — Extract
Ch15.B4.a — Basis at Weight (w)
Extract current basis (B_w).
list basis node ids and their promotion receipts,
list collapse/reduction relations among them,
list certified evaluation receipts per basis element,
list contexts where basis is certified/promoted.
Ch15.B4.b — Independent Set at Weight (w)
Extract the independent set under current evidence.
list promoted primitives at weight (w),
list non-reduction certificates supporting their independence,
list minimal loop suites and UCWs used,
list risk status and required stress/regression.
Ch15.B4.c — Relations at Weight (w)
Extract relations among invariants at weight (w).
list reduction edges and coefficient proofs,
list certified residual evidence,
list contexts where relations hold,
list conflicts or convention dependencies requiring bridges.
Ch15.B4.d — Unresolved Regions
Extract unresolved regions where deficiency remains or where candidates are pending.
list weights where (\mathrm{Def}(w)>0),
list candidate nodes pending promotion,
list blocked actions due to policy or scope gaps,
list required next tasks (AAS, RQF, bridge promotions).
C. Cloud
Ch15.C1 — Spec
Ch15.C1.a — Certified Reductions
Certified reductions are reductions proven under certified evaluation and corridor gates.
reduction receipts include certified evaluation on both sides and residual containment evidence.
reductions must be stable across κ and under projection.
reductions must be robust to perturbation when required.
reductions are context-indexed; they cannot be globalized without bridging.
Ch15.C1.b — Certified Non-Reductions
Certified non-reductions are evidence that no relation exists within bounded search caps, robustly.
non-reduction certificates include bounded search caps.
include residual plateau evidence above 0 across κ.
include probe adequacy and cross-chart consistency evidence.
include adversarial checks to rule out method artifacts.
Non-reductions are required for promotion of new primitives.
Ch15.C1.c — Plateau Criteria
Plateau criteria determine when relation search is exhausted.
residuals plateau above gates despite κ refinement.
additional coefficient cap increases do not improve residuals.
method substitutions do not yield relation.
probes adequate; otherwise plateau is probe inadequacy.
Plateau criteria govern refusal and escalation.
Ch15.C1.d — Uncertainty Handling
Uncertainty is handled explicitly.
uncertain relations remain candidate-only; cannot be used in promotions.
uncertainty triggers probe expansion, carrier escalation, or policy changes.
uncertainty is quarantined from certified routing.
uncertainty evidence is stored as refusal packs to guide future work.
Ch15.C2 — Encode
Ch15.C2.a — Relation Residual Packs
Relation residual packs store residual evidence for reduction attempts.
residual intervals and normalized residual values.
κ-indexed residual curves.
probe bundle ids and adequacy metadata.
method/bound/rounding ids used.
Residual packs are replayable.
Ch15.C2.b — Perturbation Packs
Perturbation packs test robustness of relations.
perturbation probe definitions.
residual evidence under perturbations.
pass/fail outcomes.
counterexample objects for failures.
Perturbation packs are required for high-risk promotions.
Ch15.C2.c — Regression Packs
Regression packs ensure basis relations remain stable.
rerun relation checks at fixed κ.
rerun non-reduction certificates on key candidates.
detect drift or newly discovered relations due to method upgrades.
bisect lineage for failures.
Regression packs prevent silent corruption of basis.
Ch15.C2.d — Refusal Packs
Refusal packs record failed relation searches and blocked promotions.
refusal class: policy block, probe inadequacy, plateau, drift.
bounded search caps and evidence.
minimal counterexamples.
admissible next actions.
Refusal packs guide scheduling.
Ch15.C3 — Prove
Ch15.C3.a — Non-Reduction Certificates
A non-reduction certificate supports independence of a candidate under bounded search.
all reductions within bounded coefficient complexity fail under certified residual checks,
failure persists under κ refinement,
failure persists under method substitutions and cross-root evaluations,
probes adequate and include adversarial checks,
no policy conflicts invalidate the search.
Non-reduction certificates are promotable evidence but remain scoped by caps; promotion uses them as sufficient evidence under governance, not as absolute independence proof.
Ch15.C3.b — Independence Under Corridor
Independence is corridor-indexed.
a candidate is independent if no certified reduction exists under corridor gates and under bounded search caps.
independence must be stable under κ; if tightening corridor breaks an independence claim, then independence was not robust.
independence cannot be asserted under widened corridor unless explicitly re-verified.
independence is bound to ConvBundleFP; convention changes may alter relations and require bridges.
Independence is thus a certified, scoped property.
Ch15.C3.c — Adversarial Robustness
Adversarial robustness prevents spurious independence.
test relation residuals on boundary probes and perturbation probes.
test across multiple charts and roots.
test against worst-case probes designed to maximize residual sensitivity.
failures yield uncertainty or drift classification, not promotion.
Robustness is required for high-risk depth>1 promotions.
Ch15.C3.d — Policy-Safe Promotion
Promotion must be policy-safe.
all evidence must be admissible under policy.
stress/regression suites required by policy must pass.
policy bridges required for cross-policy reuse must be explicit.
if policy blocks decisive evidence, promotion is refused and recorded as policy block with admissible next actions.
Policy-safe promotion prevents laundering.
Ch15.C4 — Extract
Ch15.C4.a — Independence Dashboards
Independence dashboards summarize promoted primitives and their evidence.
list primitives per weight/depth.
list non-reduction certificates and caps.
list robustness status and stress/regression coverage.
list pending candidates and missing evidence.
Ch15.C4.b — Relation Dashboards
Relation dashboards summarize known relations and reductions.
list reductions per weight.
list coefficient proofs and contexts.
list convention dependencies and required bridges.
list drift alarms affecting relations.
Ch15.C4.c — Uncertainty Dashboards
Uncertainty dashboards summarize unresolved claims.
plateaued relation searches,
probe inadequacy regions,
policy blocks,
portal candidates.
Dashboards guide prioritization.
Ch15.C4.d — Risk Reports
Risk reports summarize risk in basis evolution.
high-risk depth>1 candidates and their evidence gaps,
missing stress/regression coverage,
drift alarms and quarantines,
potential contamination paths and mitigations.
Risk reports ensure safe growth.
D. Fractal
Ch15.D1 — Spec
Ch15.D1.a — Automated Basis Growth
Automated basis growth is the scheduler-driven execution of SPP jobs.
compute deficiency and forced generator schedule.
create candidate nodes and job graphs.
run reduction/non-reduction tasks with certified verification.
run atlas forcing and coherence checks.
promote or refuse with evidence.
Automation is bounded, deterministic, and audited.
Ch15.D1.b — Automated Relation Search
Automated relation search discovers reductions and relations.
search is bounded by coefficient caps and basis feature caps.
search uses oracle methods for candidate generation only.
acceptance requires certified residual containment and UCW stability.
failures are recorded as refusal packs.
Automated relation search supports systematic basis completion.
Ch15.D1.c — Automated Compaction
Compaction reduces storage while preserving replay.
deduplicate receipts and UCWs by identity.
compress relation logs into refusal summaries and minimal evidence packs.
preserve promotion-critical evidence and refusal exemplars.
produce compaction receipts proving replay equivalence.
Compaction is safe and auditable.
Ch15.D1.d — Cross-Metro Import Integration
Imports integrate external evidence.
imported candidates and relations are quarantined until verified locally.
policy and convention compatibility checks required.
imported evidence may reduce deficiency if promoted under local context.
imports may introduce new candidate families or suggest portal expansions.
Integration is governed by ICIB discipline.
Ch15.D2 — Encode
Ch15.D2.a — Job Graphs
Job graphs encode the SPP pipeline.
nodes: deficiency computation, candidate creation, reduction, non-reduction, atlas forcing, promotion, regression/stress.
edges: dependency and prerequisites.
context hashes and budgets per job.
deterministic job ordering and priorities.
Job graphs are replayable.
Ch15.D2.b — Queue Priorities
Queue priorities rank tasks deterministically.
deficiency severity.
demand urgency.
dependency impact.
risk overhead.
Priorities are metadata for scheduling and are auditable.
Ch15.D2.c — Caches
Caches store intermediate results safely.
cache reduction attempts and residual packs.
cache UCW outcomes for chart equivalence.
cache non-reduction certificates.
cache invalidates on schema/verifier drift.
Caches accelerate without altering semantics.
Ch15.D2.d — Invalidation
Invalidation prevents stale basis evidence.
invalidate on ConvFP/corridor/policy changes affecting relations.
invalidate on method/bound upgrades affecting certified residual outcomes.
invalidate on drift alarms.
invalidation triggers re-verification jobs or quarantines.
Invalidation is explicit and logged.
Ch15.D3 — Prove
Ch15.D3.a — Safe Automation
Safe automation constraints apply.
bounded search and bounded refinement.
verified-only promotion.
deterministic enumeration and scheduling.
refusal instead of guessing.
Safe automation prevents runaway and spurious basis growth.
Ch15.D3.b — Bounded Resource Use
Resource bounds ensure termination.
relation search caps.
κ ladder caps.
probe expansion caps.
tunnel caps.
Exhaustion yields refusal packs with evidence.
Ch15.D3.c — Determinism
Determinism ensures reproducibility.
deterministic forced generator schedule.
deterministic candidate selection rules.
deterministic search order and tie-breaking.
deterministic verification.
Nondeterminism forbids promotion and is logged as drift risk.
Ch15.D3.d — Reproducibility
Reproducibility ensures cross-site consistency.
exported proof packs allow independent replay.
imported evidence is validated locally.
regression ensures long-term reproducibility under evolution.
migration edges preserve historical proof validity.
Reproducibility is the basis for trustworthy expansion.
Ch15.D4 — Extract
Ch15.D4.a — Roadmap to Weight (N)
Roadmap extraction produces a schedule of tasks to reach target weight (N).
deficiency and required generator counts per weight.
candidate families per weight/depth.
required relation searches and atlas forcing tasks.
required budgets and risk gates.
Roadmaps are deterministic and linked to evidence.
Ch15.D4.b — Compute Budgets
Compute budgets extraction provides required resources.
κ ladder and probe counts per weight.
relation search caps per weight.
stress/regression suites required.
expected costs and scheduling priorities.
Budgets guide planning.
Ch15.D4.c — Blocked Bottlenecks
Blocked bottlenecks extraction identifies constraints.
policy forbiddance of needed tunnels/bridges.
probe inadequacy requiring new probe regimes.
missing chart families requiring new AAS templates.
portal requirements blocking closure.
Bottlenecks are linked to refusal evidence and remediation plans.
Ch15.D4.d — Next Forced Targets
Next forced targets extraction lists immediate obligations.
next weights where deficiency is positive.
next candidate primitives required.
next relation searches to attempt.
next atlas forcing jobs.
Targets are prioritized by demand and dependency impact.
Ch15 — Seed Promotion Pipeline (SPP): Dimension Models and Forced Generators
Ch16 — UseCase Demand Scheduler and Governance (Purpose Loop)
A. Square
Ch16.A1 — Spec
Ch16.A1.a — Demand Model
The UseCase Demand Scheduler is the governance-controlled mechanism that converts external and internal needs into deterministic job plans over the metro graph. The scheduler does not decide mathematical truth; it decides which verification and synthesis actions are pursued, in what order, under which budgets and policies, while preserving the proof-carrying integrity of the system.
A demand is a typed record:[D := (\mathsf{Target},;\mathsf{Class},;\mathsf{Req},;\mathsf{Ctx},;\mathsf{SLA},;\mathsf{Risk},;\mathsf{Mode}),]where:
Target (\mathsf{Target}). A node id, edge id, basis weight/depth region, atlas obligation, or verifier task.
Class (\mathsf{Class}). The demanded outcome class:
eval: certified evaluation receipt at specified κ,
equiv: certified UCW equivalence between charts/routes,
promote: promotion of node/edge/chart,
accel: addition or activation of accel chart,
repair: Snap repair to reach certification,
obstr: Tier-0 obstruction extraction,
rqf: Root-Quad Forcing closure,
spp: basis growth or relation search.
Requirements (\mathsf{Req}). The requested accuracy, scope, and admissibility:
digits or error tolerance target,
required corridor and policy class, or constraints that induce corridor/policy selection,
required scope (operator families), stability region constraints,
required coverage (which contexts must be supported).
Context (\mathsf{Ctx}). Required ConvBundleFP, policy and corridor constraints (possibly as predicates), and visibility mode constraints.
SLA (\mathsf{SLA}). Service-level objectives:
latency requirements (for evaluation),
freshness requirements (for recently updated evidence or regression),
durability requirements (promotion vs temporary).
Risk (\mathsf{Risk}). Risk class of the demand and required governance gates:
low/medium/high, with stress/regression suite requirements,
whether negatify/failure-space mapping is required before promotion.
Mode (\mathsf{Mode}). Visibility boundary: public/restricted/private/quarantined.
Demands may originate externally (consumer requests) or internally (deficiency triggers from SPP, drift alarms from ConvFP governance, regression failures, atlas missing roots). The scheduler unifies all demands into a single priority queue while enforcing policy compliance and refusing unsafe shortcuts.
Ch16.A1.b — Urgency Metric
The scheduler ranks demands by a deterministic urgency metric. Urgency affects scheduling order but never alters acceptance predicates.
A canonical urgency functional is:[U(D) := w_1\cdot \mathrm{Digits}(D)\cdot \mathrm{Calls}(D)\cdot \mathrm{LatencyWeight}(D);+;w_2\cdot \mathrm{DependencyImpact}(D);+;w_3\cdot \mathrm{DeficiencyImpact}(D);-;w_4\cdot \mathrm{RiskCost}(D),]where:
(\mathrm{Digits}(D)): required κ level or target error budget.
(\mathrm{Calls}(D)): observed or declared invocation rate.
(\mathrm{LatencyWeight}(D)): weight emphasizing runtime requirements.
(\mathrm{DependencyImpact}(D)): number and importance of downstream tasks blocked by this demand (computed from dependency graphs).
(\mathrm{DeficiencyImpact}(D)): impact on basis completeness or forced generator schedule (from SPP).
(\mathrm{RiskCost}(D)): expected governance overhead (stress/regression suites, adversarial probes, quarantine handling).
All weights (w_i) and metric definitions are pinned by a scheduler policy version. Urgency must be deterministic; nondeterministic scheduling is forbidden for promotion-critical tasks.
Ch16.A1.c — Service Levels
Service levels define what “done” means for demands.
Evaluation SLA. Specifies:
maximum allowed enclosure width or target digits,
maximum allowed latency for evaluation route selection,
fallback requirements (baseline always available).
Equivalence SLA. Specifies:
required UCW tri-gate margins,
required probe adequacy level,
required regression coverage.
Promotion SLA. Specifies:
required RQF closure status (Ω* where required),
required stress/regression pass,
required scope coverage.
Acceleration SLA. Specifies:
required speedup factor at target κ,
required transport binding to baseline,
activation policy constraints and rollback plan.
Repair SLA. Specifies:
maximum Snap iterations and budgets,
success criterion (SnapOK vs SnapNEAR vs refusal).
Discovery/Obstruction SLA. Specifies:
stability criteria for candidate extraction,
bounded search budgets and reproducibility requirements.
Service levels are interpreted as scheduling constraints and job completion criteria.
Ch16.A1.d — Governance Constraints
Governance constraints ensure safety, reproducibility, and integrity.
Policy primacy. Scheduler cannot bypass policy forbiddances; it can only schedule policy-bridge requests or produce refusals.
No silent widening. Scheduler cannot “solve” failures by relaxing corridor gates without explicit corridor identity changes and re-verification; corridor changes are governed events.
Verified-only promotion. Scheduler cannot promote based on heuristic evidence; promotion requires certified receipts, UCWs, and required suites.
Auditability. Every scheduling decision is logged with deterministic reasoning and context hashes.
Quarantine discipline. Imported evidence and high-risk candidates remain quarantined until local verification and required suites pass.
Determinism. Scheduling order and tie-breaking are deterministic under pinned scheduler policy.
Governance constraints are enforceable predicates; violating them yields refusal artifacts.
Ch16.A2 — Encode
Ch16.A2.a — Demand Records
Demand records are canonical, hash-addressed objects.
Record identity. (\mathrm{id}(D):=\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{canon}(D))).
Fields. Target id(s), class, requirements, context constraints, SLA, risk class, mode.
Aggregation. Multiple demands for the same target are aggregated by deterministic rules producing an aggregate demand record (D^\Sigma) with merged requirements (e.g., max digits, max call rate).
Provenance. Demand provenance includes origin (external request id, internal alarm id, deficiency id), but provenance is metadata and does not change semantics.
Ch16.A2.b — Metric Computation
Metric computation is deterministic and auditable.
record the scheduler policy version and weights used.
compute urgency (U(D)) from demand record and current telemetry.
compute dependency impact from current dependency graphs.
compute deficiency impact from SPP tables and basis status.
compute risk cost from required suites and current evidence coverage.
Metric outputs are stored in run logs; they can be recomputed for audit.
Ch16.A2.c — Budget Binding
Budgets bind how much computation may be spent per demand and per job.
Policy budgets. Define global and per-class caps.
Corridor budgets. Define κ ladders and verification caps.
Demand budgets. Demand may include explicit budget ceilings; otherwise budgets are derived from policy and SLA.
Budget objects. Budgets are canonical objects linked to jobs; job execution must record budget consumption.
Budget binding ensures finite behavior and prevents runaway discovery.
Ch16.A2.d — Audit Binding
Audit binding records why each scheduling decision occurred.
demand id and aggregate id.
computed urgency and its components.
context hashes (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP).
selected job plan and dependencies.
any blocked actions and their reasons (policy forbids, scope gap, missing bridge).
Audit binding produces a schedule log object with hash identity.
Ch16.A3 — Prove
Ch16.A3.a — Priority Correctness
Priority correctness states that the scheduler’s ordering respects the urgency metric and governance rules.
For any two demands (D_1,D_2) in the same admissible class and context regime, if (U(D_1) > U(D_2)), then (D_1) is scheduled no later than (D_2) unless constrained by dependencies or risk gates.
If dependencies require that (D_2) must execute before (D_1), scheduler records dependency justification and schedules prerequisites first.
Risk gates may delay high-risk promotions until stress/regression suites are available; scheduler records risk justification rather than silently deferring.
Priority correctness is auditable by replaying metric computation from demand records and scheduler policy.
Ch16.A3.b — Starvation Avoidance
Starvation avoidance prevents low-urgency tasks from never executing.
scheduler applies a deterministic aging mechanism: urgency increases slowly with time for pending demands, bounded to prevent inversion of high-urgency tasks.
periodic fairness windows allocate compute to pending demands in each class.
demands blocked by hard constraints (policy forbids) do not consume compute repeatedly; they are converted to policy-bridge demands or to refusal governance records.
fairness logic is deterministic and auditable; it does not alter semantic acceptance rules.
Ch16.A3.c — Budget Compliance
Budget compliance ensures scheduling and execution stay within declared caps.
job plans specify budgets; execution logs record consumption.
if a job hits budget limits, it must terminate with SnapNEAR/SnapFAIL or refusal packs, not silent continuation.
scheduler may requeue unfinished demands with adjusted priorities only if policy allows and budgets remain; otherwise it records refusal and blocks repeated waste.
budget compliance is verified by auditors by replaying job logs against budget objects.
Ch16.A3.d — Policy Compliance
Policy compliance is strict.
scheduler selects only jobs and methods admissible under policy.
if required actions are forbidden, scheduler emits a policy-block refusal or schedules a policy bridge request as a new demand.
scheduler cannot change policy; it can only request governance-approved policy changes.
policy compliance is audited by checking job specs against policy hashes and admissibility predicates.
Ch16.A4 — Extract
Ch16.A4.a — Demand Dashboards
Demand dashboards summarize demand state.
active demands by class and target.
urgency rankings with component breakdowns.
evidence coverage status (which demands are already satisfied by existing receipts).
blocked demands and their blockers (policy, scope, missing bridges, plateau).
Dashboards are context-indexed.
Ch16.A4.b — Backlog Extracts
Backlog extracts list pending job plans.
queued jobs per demand.
dependency DAGs and critical paths.
budgets and expected costs.
predicted completion status under current resource envelope.
Backlogs are deterministic and auditable.
Ch16.A4.c — SLA Extracts
SLA extracts show satisfaction status.
evaluation SLA compliance (latency, digits).
equivalence SLA compliance (gate margins, adequacy).
promotion SLA compliance (Ω*, stress/regression).
acceleration SLA compliance (speedup, binding, rollback readiness).
SLA extracts are evidence-based.
Ch16.A4.d — Governance Reports
Governance reports summarize governance-relevant events.
policy-block refusals and requested policy bridges.
corridor change requests and their justifications.
quarantines due to drift alarms or regression failures.
portal candidate requirements.
Reports are linked to refusal artifacts and job plans.
B. Flower
Ch16.B1 — Spec
Ch16.B1.a — Job Types
Scheduler operates over a finite set of job types, each with explicit inputs/outputs and witness obligations.
baseline_eval. Produce certified evaluation receipts in required roots.
equiv_ucw. Produce UCWs certifying transport or chart equivalence.
snap_repair. Run Snap to attempt deterministic repair.
accel_aas. Run AAS to discover and verify accel charts.
tunnel. Execute admissible tunnel integration and witness hooks (Ch10).
obstr_t0. Run T0-OBSTR to extract Tier-0 candidates (Ch12).
rqf. Run Root-Quad Forcing to close atlas and produce Ω* (Ch13).
spp. Run seed promotion pipeline tasks: deficiency computation, relation search, non-reduction certificates, promotion gating (Ch15).
regression. Run required regression suites and produce packs.
stress. Run required stress suites and produce packs.
Each job type is deterministic and has a schema version and verifier obligations.
Ch16.B1.b — Job Dependencies
Job dependencies form a DAG.
promotion depends on certified evaluation and UCWs.
activation of accel charts depends on AAS verification and transport binding UCWs.
RQF depends on root receipts and edge/face witnesses.
T0-OBSTR depends on SnapFAIL or stable holonomy evidence.
stress/regression depend on the artifacts they test and gate promotion.
Dependencies are explicit edges in the job graph and are included in audit logs.
Ch16.B1.c — Scheduling Rules
Scheduling rules map demands to job graphs.
for eval demands: schedule baseline_eval jobs; if too costly, schedule accel_aas jobs.
for equiv demands: schedule equiv_ucw jobs; if fails, schedule snap_repair; if holonomy-dominant, schedule obstr_t0.
for promote demands: schedule rqf; if missing roots, schedule baseline_eval and accel_aas; if portal required, schedule portal creation demands.
for spp demands: schedule deficiency computation and relation search; schedule promotion gating tasks.
Scheduling rules are deterministic and recorded.
Ch16.B1.d — Escalation Rules
Escalation rules are the decision tree for failure.
if hard legality fails: escalate to bridge demands or policy change demands.
if quantitative gates fail: escalate to Snap repair; if fails and tunnel admissible, escalate to tunnel; if still fails and holonomy-dominant, escalate to T0-OBSTR; if portal indicated, escalate to portal creation.
if plateau persists: escalate probes/carriers or revise templates; do not loop indefinitely.
if budgets exhaust: emit refusal governance record and requeue only if new admissible actions exist.
Escalation is deterministic and audited.
Ch16.B2 — Encode
Ch16.B2.a — Job Graph Schema
Job graph schema encodes tasks and dependencies.
job nodes with type, inputs, outputs, context hashes, budgets.
dependency edges with required completion predicates.
run-state fields: queued/running/succeeded/failed/refused/quarantined.
job ids are hash-addressed; changes create new job ids.
Job graphs are replayable and auditable.
Ch16.B2.b — Run Logs
Run logs record job execution.
job id, start/end, consumed budgets, outputs produced.
references to produced receipts, UCWs, refusal packs.
failure classifications and minimal counterexamples when failures occur.
deterministic decision records and tie-breakers.
Run logs are the audit trail of scheduler actions.
Ch16.B2.c — Route Bindings
Route bindings link job outputs to WordNFs and atlas states.
evaluation jobs produce WordNFs for evaluation routes.
UCW jobs produce WordNFs for compared routes.
tunnel jobs produce tunnel events embedded in WordNF.
promotion jobs update atlas registry and pointers, referenced by receipts.
Route bindings ensure outputs are replay-addressable.
Ch16.B2.d — Result Bindings
Result bindings attach outputs to demands.
link demand id to job outputs that satisfy it.
link to receipts and witness roots.
record which SLA elements are satisfied.
record any remaining obligations and next actions.
Bindings are immutable records and enable “best-effort explanations.”
Ch16.B3 — Prove
Ch16.B3.a — Correct Triggering Conditions
Triggering conditions ensure correct job creation.
AAS is triggered only when baseline evaluation is costly relative to SLA and accel is permitted.
T0-OBSTR is triggered only on stable holonomy-dominant failures after admissible repairs.
RQF is triggered only when candidate node exists and root receipts are available or schedulable.
SPP tasks are triggered by deficiency criterion and/or demand for basis completeness.
Triggers are deterministic and recorded.
Ch16.B3.b — Safe Escalation
Safe escalation ensures failure does not cause unsafe actions.
escalation cannot bypass policy constraints.
escalation cannot widen corridors silently.
escalation cannot promote heuristic evidence.
escalation must terminate in either a successful certified outcome or a refusal governance record.
Escalation safety is audited by run logs.
Ch16.B3.c — Correct Cancellation
Cancellation prevents wasted compute.
cancel jobs made redundant by newly satisfied demands or updated evidence coverage.
cancel jobs invalidated by context changes (policy/corridor/ConvFP updates) and reschedule under new contexts.
cancellation is recorded and auditable; cancellation never deletes evidence.
cancellation respects dependencies; do not cancel prerequisite jobs needed by other demands.
Ch16.B3.d — Replay of Schedules
Schedules are replayable.
given demand records, scheduler policy version, and telemetry snapshot, the job graph is deterministically reconstructed.
run logs verify that execution followed the plan and obeyed budgets.
auditors can replay schedule decisions and validate priority ordering and fairness rules.
schedule replay is part of governance audit.
Ch16.B4 — Extract
Ch16.B4.a — Per-Constant Job History
For each invariant node, extract:
evaluation history (baseline and accel).
UCW equivalence history.
tunnel history and chart activation history.
regression/stress history and quarantines.
Histories are context-indexed.
Ch16.B4.b — Per-Weight Job History
For graded domains, extract:
SPP deficiency history and forced generator scheduling.
relation search history and non-reduction certificates.
promoted basis evolution and migration history.
unresolved region history and next forced targets.
Ch16.B4.c — Per-Policy Job History
Extract per policy:
which demands are satisfiable.
which jobs are blocked and why.
required policy bridges and governance changes.
evidence coverage maps under policy.
Per-policy histories support multi-policy deployment.
Ch16.B4.d — Best-Effort Explanations
Best-effort explanations summarize why a demand is or is not satisfied.
list evidence coverage: which receipts and UCWs satisfy parts of the demand.
list blockers: missing roots, missing bridges, policy blocks, plateau evidence.
list scheduled next actions and expected obligations.
list refusal artifacts when blocked, with minimal counterexamples.
Best-effort explanations are audit artifacts, not informal narratives.
C. Cloud
Ch16.C1 — Spec
Ch16.C1.a — Risk Scoring
Risk scoring quantifies governance overhead and safety requirements.
risk depends on operator scope (branch-sensitive, regularization, portal-sensitive).
risk depends on method novelty (new charts, tunnel-derived charts).
risk depends on evidence stability (regression history, drift alarms).
risk determines required stress/regression suites and quarantine policies.
Risk scoring is deterministic given risk policy version and context.
Ch16.C1.b — Negatify Protocol (Failure-Space Mapping)
Negatify is a structured protocol that maps the failure space of a domain, producing “inverse-phi” maps of worst-case regions to accelerate learning and avoid hidden pitfalls.
identify failure modes by UCW classes and SnapFAIL categories.
for each failure mode, construct adversarial probe families and boundary cases.
record negative maps: regions where commutation fails, loops fail, projection fails, or policies block.
use negative maps to prioritize development: missing probes, missing bounds, missing bridges, portal needs.
Negatify outputs are evidence artifacts that guide scheduling and risk mitigation.
Ch16.C1.c — Adversarial Tests
Adversarial tests are scheduled based on risk.
branch boundary tests for analytic functions.
singularity proximity tests.
ordering tie tests for canonicalization.
worst-case commutation and loop closure tests.
Adversarial tests produce stress packs required for high-risk promotion and activation.
Ch16.C1.d — Refusal Governance
Refusal governance ensures refusals are used constructively.
refusals are stored as first-class artifacts with replay evidence.
refusals are classified and indexed for scheduling.
repeated refusals trigger escalation (probe expansion, method synthesis, policy bridges, portal analysis).
