refusals do not silently decay into heuristics; they remain authoritative evidence of limitation under current context.
Ch16.C2 — Encode
Ch16.C2.a — Stress Suites
Stress suites are canonical records.
suite id, scope, adversarial probe definitions.
required gates and pass criteria.
linkage to risk classes and job types.
results stored as stress packs with counterexamples.
Ch16.C2.b — Regression Suites
Regression suites are canonical records.
suite id, scope, fixed κ levels and probes.
rerun obligations for UCWs, evaluation receipts, and Ω* closures.
bisect hooks and failure classification rules.
results stored as regression packs.
Ch16.C2.c — Failure Maps
Failure maps encode negatify outputs.
mapping from domain regions and operator scopes to failure classes.
minimal counterexamples and refusal evidence pointers.
remediation obligations and required new artifacts.
priority ranks and scheduling hints.
Failure maps guide systematic improvement.
Ch16.C2.d — Refusal Artifacts
Refusal artifacts are canonical records.
refusal class and context hashes.
minimal failing predicate and counterexample objects.
best-achieved evidence and budgets.
admissible next actions.
Refusal artifacts are used in governance and scheduling.
Ch16.C3 — Prove
Ch16.C3.a — Risk Monotonicity
Risk monotonicity states that promoting more powerful or more complex capabilities cannot reduce required safety obligations.
adding new tunnels or new charts increases or maintains risk; it cannot reduce required stress/regression unless justified by evidence and governance updates.
activating accel charts does not reduce baseline requirements.
extending scope coverage increases verification obligations.
risk scoring updates are versioned and require regression validation.
Ch16.C3.b — Safety Invariants
Safety invariants constrain scheduling.
no heuristic promotion.
no policy laundering.
no corridor widening without explicit governance and re-verification.
no convention drift without explicit ConvFP migration/bridging.
Schedulers must obey these invariants; violations are refused.
Ch16.C3.c — Adversarial Robustness
Adversarial robustness is required for high-risk actions.
stress suites must pass before promotion/activation.
failures trigger quarantine and rollback.
adversarial results are replayable and auditable.
robustness evidence is integrated into risk scoring and scheduling.
Ch16.C3.d — Refusal Correctness
Refusal correctness ensures refusals are honest.
refusal produced only after exhausting admissible actions within budgets.
refusal includes evidence and classification.
refusal includes admissible next actions.
refusal is replayable and verifiable.
Refusal correctness prevents silent degradation.
Ch16.C4 — Extract
Ch16.C4.a — Risk Dashboards
Risk dashboards summarize:
risk class distribution across nodes/edges/charts.
missing stress/regression coverage.
drift alarms and quarantines.
upcoming high-risk promotions.
Dashboards guide governance.
Ch16.C4.b — Failure Maps
Failure maps extraction returns negatify maps.
failure classes and affected scopes.
counterexamples and refusal evidence.
remediation obligations and priorities.
links to scheduled jobs.
Ch16.C4.c — Stress Results
Stress results extraction provides stress packs.
suite definitions and contexts.
pass/fail outcomes.
counterexample objects for failures.
quarantine/rollback actions.
Ch16.C4.d — Regression Results
Regression results extraction provides regression packs.
suite definitions and contexts.
pass/fail outcomes.
bisect lineage for failures.
evidence for stability of promoted artifacts.
D. Fractal
Ch16.D1 — Spec
Ch16.D1.a — Long-Term Learning
Long-term learning improves scheduling and synthesis ordering while preserving determinism and safety.
telemetry accumulates evidence of which actions reduce defect most effectively.
learned heuristics may reorder admissible actions but cannot change legality predicates or acceptance gates.
learned heuristics are versioned and governed; updates require regression validation.
learning must preserve reproducibility: given pinned heuristic version, scheduler decisions are replayable.
Ch16.D1.b — Caching Strategy
Caching accelerates scheduling and verification.
cache evidence coverage maps and route plans.
cache expanded witness sets and frequently used UCWs.
caches are non-authoritative; verifiers still check hashes and recompute as required.
caches invalidate on context changes, drift alarms, or schema/verifier updates.
Ch16.D1.c — Compaction Strategy
Compaction reduces storage while preserving replay and audit.
keep promoted artifacts and refusal exemplars.
deduplicate identical receipts and UCWs.
compact candidate logs into summarized ledgers while preserving failure evidence.
produce compaction receipts proving replay equivalence.
Ch16.D1.d — Cross-Project Routing
Cross-project routing maps demands across different domains or metros.
route demand to the correct domain metro via domain tags and scope.
enforce compatibility constraints; cross-domain reuse requires bridges or portals.
quarantine imported evidence until verified locally.
unify scheduling priorities using deterministic mapping from domain urgency and dependency impact.
Cross-project routing enables integrated growth of the whole memory web.
Ch16.D2 — Encode
Ch16.D2.a — Telemetry Schema
Telemetry schema records scheduler-relevant events.
demand arrivals and aggregations.
job executions and outcomes.
defect reductions achieved by repairs and tunnels.
costs and latencies observed.
failure classifications and counterexamples.
Telemetry is metadata and does not alter semantics; it is used for deterministic learning updates.
Ch16.D2.b — Cache Schema
Cache schema defines stored accelerants.
evidence coverage cache keyed by context.
route plan cache keyed by endpoints and target class.
witness expansion cache keyed by receipt ids.
invalidation dependencies and timestamps.
Caches are audit-aware.
Ch16.D2.c — Compaction Schema
Compaction schema defines what is retained.
promotion-critical proofs.
refusal evidence for blocked regions.
summarized candidate logs.
indices for merkle root expansion.
Compaction is governed and produces compaction receipts.
Ch16.D2.d — Routing Schema
Routing schema maps demands to job graphs.
mapping rules per demand class.
escalation decision trees.
policy/corridor selection rules.
risk gating rules.
Routing schema is versioned and replayable.
Ch16.D3 — Prove
Ch16.D3.a — Cache Soundness
Cache soundness ensures caches cannot produce false results.
caches only store pointers to receipts and witnesses.
caches do not assert acceptance; verifiers still verify.
caches validate context hashes before reuse.
invalidated caches are not used.
Ch16.D3.b — Compaction Soundness
Compaction soundness ensures compaction does not change verifiable outcomes.
promoted receipts remain replay-verifiable.
minimal replay bundles remain constructible.
refusal exemplars remain audit-ready.
compaction receipts prove equivalence before/after.
Ch16.D3.c — Telemetry Integrity
Telemetry integrity ensures telemetry is not used as semantic evidence.
telemetry is metadata and cannot influence acceptance predicates.
telemetry is used only to reorder admissible jobs or to allocate resources.
telemetry is hash-addressed for audit but is not pinned into semantic proofs.
telemetry tampering does not alter semantic correctness because semantic proofs are pinned to receipts and UCWs.
Ch16.D3.d — Routing Safety
Routing safety ensures scheduler does not create unsafe actions.
all job plans respect policy and corridor admissibility.
routing never promotes heuristic evidence.
routing never bypasses missing bridges or scope gaps.
routing terminates and produces refusal governance records when blocked.
Routing safety is enforced by verifiers and audit.
Ch16.D4 — Extract
Ch16.D4.a — Performance Reports
Performance reports summarize system service performance.
achieved latencies and cost models.
demand satisfaction rates.
backlog health and bottlenecks.
improvement impact of accel charts and tunnels.
Reports are non-semantic and audit-linked.
Ch16.D4.b — Compaction Diffs
Compaction diffs describe what changed.
which artifacts were deduplicated or compacted.
which evidence was retained or summarized.
compaction receipt ids proving replay equivalence.
impact on caching and routing performance.
Ch16.D4.c — Route Selection Heuristics
Route selection heuristics are extracted and versioned.
which hubs are preferred per domain.
which charts are preferred per root.
which repair actions tend to succeed for given failure classes.
heuristics are deterministic under pinned versions and audited.
Ch16.D4.d — Roadmap Updates
Roadmap updates convert demand and failure evidence into development tasks.
missing roots and missing charts.
missing bridges and policy conflicts.
plateau regions requiring new AAS templates or new carriers.
portal candidates requiring semantic extension.
Roadmap updates are linked to refusal evidence and prioritized by urgency and dependency impact.
Ch16 — UseCase Demand Scheduler and Governance (Purpose Loop)
Ch17 — BridgeRegistry, MagicCertPack, and BridgeSeed (“brain tissue”)
A. Square
Ch17.A1 — Spec
Ch17.A1.a — MagicCertPack Definition
A MagicCertPack is the minimal, replay-verifiable certificate bundle that converts a successful repair or acceleration episode into a reusable, audit-grade evidence object. “Magic” denotes a corridor-changing or defect-reducing event that was not guaranteed by baseline routes yet became reliable through verification. A MagicCertPack is not mysticism; it is a strict structure that captures the before/after state, the route identity, and the witness obligations that justify reuse.
A MagicCertPack (\mathsf{MK}) binds:
Context binding. ConvBundleFP hash, policy hash, corridor hash (pre and post, if tightening occurred), verifier hash, schema hashes.
Route binding. WordNF segment stream hash for the repair/accel route, including tunnel events, Snap steps, and any bridges applied.
Defect evidence. A defect decrease certificate:[\mathfrak D_{\mathrm{pre}} > \mathfrak D_{\mathrm{post}},]computed under fixed acceptance gates, with explicit residual maxima and margins.
Witness evidence. The set of witness objects required to justify that the outcome is not accidental:
UCWs for commutation squares affected by the repair,
loop closure UCWs for cycles introduced or affected,
projection stability witnesses under (\Pi_h),
certified evaluation receipts involved in verification,
any required stress/regression results for high-risk events.
Replay evidence. Deterministic replay fields: enumerator states, budgets, pinned seeds if permitted, environment pins when required.
The MagicCertPack is thus the “receipt of magic”: it is a proof-carrying artifact that preserves the event as reusable knowledge, making it safe to treat it as an ingredient in future proofs.
Ch17.A1.b — BridgeSeed Definition
A BridgeSeed is a typed, promotable bridge artifact derived from one or more MagicCertPacks that provides a reusable transport capability between contexts, charts, or policies. If MagicCertPack is the certificate of a successful episode, BridgeSeed is the extractable capability that can be applied repeatedly and composed.
Formally, a BridgeSeed is a tuple[\mathsf{BS} := (\mathrm{kind},;\mathrm{src},;\mathrm{dst},;\mathrm{scope},;\mathrm{ctx},;\mathrm{word_template},;\mathsf{Wits},;\mathsf{Replay},;\mathsf{Guards}),]where:
kind. Typed bridge kind (see below).
src/dst. Source and target objects (lens ids, ConvFP regimes, policy ids, chart ids, node ids).
scope. Operator-family scope and carrier scope to which the bridge applies.
ctx. Context constraints: which policies/corridors/ConvBundleFP regimes it is valid under, or predicates describing admissible contexts.
word_template. A canonical WordNF template describing how to apply the bridge: a segment stream skeleton with parameter slots.
Wits. Witness roots required to justify bridge correctness, including UCWs and any auxiliary certificates.
Replay. Deterministic replay requirements: budgets, enumerator rules (if any), environment pins.
Guards. Safety guards: representability guards, drift detection guards, stability region guards, and refusal categories.
BridgeSeed is “brain tissue”: it stores the invariant conversion move learned from successful episodes and makes it reusable under strict governance.
Ch17.A1.c — Typed Bridge Kinds
Bridge kinds classify the semantic role of a BridgeSeed. The normative kinds are:
CONV_BRIDGE. Bridges between convention regimes (ConvFP(_1)(\to)ConvFP(_2)) on a declared scope. Requires explicit convention compatibility or conflict proofs and UCW evidence showing semantic preservation.
POLICY_BRIDGE. Bridges between policy regimes (Policy(_1)(\to)Policy(_2)) enabling reuse of evidence across governance constraints. Requires explicit admissibility mapping and verified safety constraints.
CORRIDOR_BRIDGE. Bridges between corridors when permissible. Because corridor changes alter acceptance regimes for corridor-bounded equivalence, corridor bridges are restricted and must be explicit and witnessed. Silent widening is forbidden.
CHART_BRIDGE. Bridges between evaluator charts (baseline ↔ accel) that certify equivalence and allow safe chart switching.
LENS_BRIDGE. Bridges between lens representations within a family or across compatible families, providing stable encode/decode/normalize transports.
PORTAL_BRIDGE. Bridges that connect distinct semantic regimes requiring portal semantics. This is the highest-risk kind and requires portal reports, new schema obligations, and heavy stress/regression gating.
META_BRIDGE. Bridges that represent higher-order repair templates: multi-step composites combining several bridges and tunings, treated as a single reusable capability, with explicit compositional witnesses.
Bridge kinds determine required witness obligations and promotion gates.
Ch17.A1.d — Promotion Criteria
Promotion criteria define when MagicCertPacks and BridgeSeeds become reusable, promotable truth tissue.
MagicCertPack promotion. A MagicCertPack is promotable iff:
it is replay-verifiable (all bindings resolvable, all evidence recomputable),
defect reduction is strict and measured under fixed gates,
all required witness objects are present and promotable,
no drift alarms exist in scope,
required stress/regression suites pass (if mandated by risk class).
BridgeSeed promotion. A BridgeSeed is promotable iff:
it is derived from promotable MagicCertPacks or from already-promoted bridge components,
it has a correct word_template whose application is deterministic and scoped,
it has transport UCWs certifying semantic preservation under tri-gate logic,
it composes legally with existing transports (typed compositionality),
it satisfies governance gates (stress/regression as required),
it has refusal semantics and guard predicates ensuring safe failure modes.
Promotion does not mean “always works.” It means “when applicable and within scope, it is a certified, replayable, compositional capability.”
Ch17.A2 — Encode
Ch17.A2.a — Pack Schema
MagicCertPack schema is versioned and canonical. Minimal fields:
Header. mk_version, mk_id, kind=MagicCertPack.
Context. conv_bundle_hash, policy_hash, corr_pre_hash, corr_post_hash, verifier_hash, schema_hashes.
Route. word_hash and segment references; includes tunnel and Snap event ids.
Defect. defect_pre, defect_post, gate_margins_pre, gate_margins_post, and the defect functional id.
Evidence roots. pins_root, wits_root, plus index references to expand them deterministically.
Proof artifacts. UCW ids, evaluation receipt ids, stress/regression pack ids, and any refusal ids related to attempted alternatives.
Replay. budgets used, enumerator state, deterministic ordering id, seeds if permitted, environment pins if required.
Guards. drift alarms checked, stability region ids, admissibility checks.
Ch17.A2.b — Seed Schema
BridgeSeed schema is versioned and canonical. Minimal fields:
Header. bs_version, bs_id, bridge_kind.
Endpoints. src_ref, dst_ref (typed references: ConvFP ids, policy ids, corridor ids, chart ids, lens ids).
Scope. operator-family scope, carrier type scope, admissibility region ids.
Context constraints. allowed policy class predicates, corridor predicates, ConvFP compatibility predicates.
Word template. canonical segment stream with parameter slots and fixed operator ids.
Witness roots. required UCW ids and auxiliary certificate ids; merkle-rooted witness set commitments.
Promotion status. promoted/certified/candidate/quarantined/deprecated with context coverage map.
Guards and refusal semantics. guard predicates and refusal categories with required evidence outputs.
BridgeSeed encoding ensures that bridges can be applied safely and audited.
Ch17.A2.c — Verifier Bindings
Verifier bindings ensure that bridge evidence is interpreted correctly.
each pack and seed binds verifier_hash and schema hashes of UCW, WordNF, corridor, policy, ConvFP records.
verifiers refuse to interpret unsupported schema versions or mismatched bindings.
verifier bindings are part of receipt context; changing verifier requires migration edges or verified translators.
bridge verification is replay-based: verifiers recompute UCW obligations and gate predicates.
Ch17.A2.d — Replay Bindings
Replay bindings ensure that a bridge is reproducible.
word_template includes parameter binding rules and canonical ordering rules.
replay budgets constrain application and verification.
any nondeterminism is forbidden or seed-pinned; seeds are part of bridge identity if they influence outcomes.
environment pins ensure numeric backend determinism where required.
Replay bindings are mandatory for promotable bridges.
Ch17.A3 — Prove
Ch17.A3.a — Promotability Theorem
Theorem (Bridge Promotability). If a BridgeSeed (\mathsf{BS}) is promoted under context (X), then for any admissible application instance (T_{\mathsf{BS}}[\theta]) within the declared scope, there exists a proof slice containing:
the BridgeSeed record,
required pins (ConvFP/policy/corridor/lens/chart records),
required witness roots (UCWs and auxiliary certificates),
the instantiated WordNF application route,such that an independent verifier can replay:
admissibility checks,
transport UCW checks (commutation/holonomy/projection),
gate predicate checks,and conclude that the bridge preserves meaning under (X), or else return a structured refusal under the bridge’s refusal semantics.
Thus promoted bridges are proof-carrying operators, not informal shortcuts.
Ch17.A3.b — Stability Under Replay
Bridge stability under replay requires that repeated verification yields identical outcomes under pinned contexts.
re-running the verifier on the bridge application under same pins yields the same UCW residual curves and the same acceptance decision.
any deviation indicates drift: ConvFP incompleteness, method instability, or environment differences; such drift triggers quarantine and requires remediation.
stability is enforced by regression suites that re-test promoted bridges.
stability under replay is required for bridge chaining; unstable bridges cannot be composed reliably.
Ch17.A3.c — Compositional Bridge Chaining
Bridge chaining is the composition of multiple bridges into a composite capability.
Typed chaining. Two bridges (B_1: A\to B) and (B_2: B\to C) can be chained iff endpoint types unify and scopes are compatible.
Context chaining. ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor contexts must unify or be bridged explicitly; chain cannot silently change contexts.
Witness chaining. The chain’s witness obligations include:
witness roots of each bridge,
any additional UCWs required to certify commutation at chain boundaries,
loop closure witnesses for new cycles introduced by chaining.
Receipt composition. A chain is promotable only if a receipt binds the full pin set and witness set and is replay-verifiable.
Chaining yields reusable macro bridges (META_BRIDGE) and supports modular proof construction.
Ch17.A3.d — De-Duplication Correctness
De-duplication in BridgeRegistry removes duplicates without collapsing meaning.
de-duplicate only by hash identity of BridgeSeed or MagicCertPack.
do not de-duplicate by name, numeric similarity, or perceived equivalence; equivalence requires UCW witnesses and is represented by explicit edges.
preserve multiple bridges with different scope or context constraints even if they perform similar actions.
de-duplication must not remove evidence required for replay; compaction receipts prove preservation.
Ch17.A4 — Extract
Ch17.A4.a — Bridge Lookup
Bridge lookup is deterministic and context-aware.
lookup by bridge kind and endpoints (src_ref, dst_ref).
filter by scope coverage and admissibility region.
filter by policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP compatibility.
rank by promoted status, safety (stress/regression coverage), and cost metadata.
Lookup returns bridge id plus required witness packs and refusal semantics.
Ch17.A4.b — Pack Lookup
Pack lookup retrieves MagicCertPacks by target and by effect.
lookup by affected node/edge/chart.
lookup by defect reduction type (face, spin, projection, cost).
lookup by tunnel family id or Snap trace id.
lookup by context hashes.
Pack lookup supports provenance and audit.
Ch17.A4.c — Provenance Chain
Provenance chain extraction reconstructs how a bridge was created and promoted.
originating Snap traces and tunnel events.
MagicCertPacks certifying defect reduction episodes.
UCWs and evaluation receipts used in verification.
stress/regression packs and governance events.
Provenance chains are replay-addressable and support forensic audit.
Ch17.A4.d — Audit Pack Export
Audit pack export produces minimal replay bundles for bridges.
BridgeSeed record and required pins.
witness roots expanded (UCWs, certificates).
WordNF template and instantiated examples (when policy allows).
stress/regression evidence and drift checks.
Audit packs enable cross-site verification and safe exchange.
B. Flower
Ch17.B1 — Spec
Ch17.B1.a — Bridge Creation from Snap/Tunnel
Bridges arise from repeated successful repair/accel episodes.
Snap and tunnel produce MagicCertPacks when defect reduction is achieved under verified evidence.
Bridge extraction identifies the reusable core: the stable WordNF segment pattern that causes defect reduction.
Bridge creation binds the pattern to scope and admissibility constraints; outside scope the bridge must refuse.
Bridge creation produces candidate BridgeSeeds which are promoted only after sufficient evidence and governance gates.
Bridge creation is a compiler pass converting episodic success into reusable capability.
Ch17.B1.b — Bridge Application
Bridge application instantiates a BridgeSeed’s word_template with parameters and applies it to a target context.
check admissibility (scope, policy, corridor, ConvBundleFP compatibility).
instantiate WordNF template with parameters and bind into a route word.
execute verification: recompute UCWs required by bridge; check tri-gate predicates.
output either acceptance (bridge works and preserves meaning) or refusal (bridge not applicable or insufficient evidence), with structured evidence.
Bridge application is auditable and deterministic.
Ch17.B1.c — Bridge Chaining
Bridge chaining composes bridges into higher-level conversions.
chain is typed and context-checked.
chain defines a macro WordNF template.
chain inherits witness obligations of components plus boundary commutation and loop closures.
chain is promotable only when composed evidence is replay-verifiable.
Chaining enables building complex conversions from atomic “brain tissue.”
Ch17.B1.d — Bridge Rollback
Rollback ensures safe removal or deactivation of bridges without corrupting history.
rollback changes governance pointers and activation state; does not delete evidence.
rollback is triggered by regression failures, drift alarms, or policy changes.
rollback preserves fallback routes (use baseline or alternate bridges).
rollback produces audit logs and may quarantine the bridge until remediation.
Rollback is essential for safe evolution.
Ch17.B2 — Encode
Ch17.B2.a — Application Traces
Application traces record bridge application attempts.
instantiated WordNF hash and parameters.
context hashes and admissibility checks.
UCW ids computed and gate outcomes.
accept/refuse outcome and refusal evidence when applicable.
Application traces are stored and used for regression and audit.
Ch17.B2.b — Chaining Traces
Chaining traces record composition of bridges.
list of component bridge ids.
composed WordNF template hash.
additional witness obligations introduced and their ids.
context constraints for the composite.
Chaining traces enable reproducible macro bridge formation.
Ch17.B2.c — Rollback Traces
Rollback traces record deactivation/quarantine events.
bridge id and affected scopes.
reason codes (drift, regression fail, policy change).
replacement bridges or fallback routes selected.
evidence pointers and required remediation actions.
Rollback traces preserve auditability.
Ch17.B2.d — Parameter Deltas
Parameter deltas record what changed between bridge versions.
updated scope constraints.
updated WordNF templates.
updated witness roots.
updated policy/corridor admissibility.
Parameter deltas are used in migration and compatibility checks.
Ch17.B3 — Prove
Ch17.B3.a — Bridge Preserves Meaning Under UCW
Meaning preservation is certified by UCWs.
bridge application must produce UCWs showing commutation of relevant squares between src and dst representations.
bridge must satisfy loop closure obligations to prevent path dependence, especially for convention/policy bridges.
bridge must satisfy projection stability obligations under (\Pi_h).
failure yields refusal with evidence; no partial acceptance.
Thus, bridges preserve meaning exactly in the sense defined by the transport calculus: witnessed commutation and closure under pinned contexts.
Ch17.B3.b — Chain Legality
Chain legality ensures composed bridges remain admissible and safe.
component endpoints unify; no missing intermediate types.
contexts unify or are bridged explicitly; no silent context switching.
composed witness obligations are complete: all component UCWs plus new boundary UCWs.
composed chain passes tri-gate obligations and governance gates.
Chain legality failures produce structured refusals, not silent fallbacks.
Ch17.B3.c — No Shortcut Equivalence
Bridges must not be used as informal shortcuts.
a bridge cannot assert equivalence without UCWs.
a bridge cannot bypass missing scope coverage or policy constraints.
a bridge cannot replace atlas coherence obligations; it must integrate into atlas via transport edges.
any attempt to treat bridge presence as “proof” is refused unless witness obligations are satisfied.
No shortcut equivalence preserves integrity.
Ch17.B3.d — Rollback Safety
Rollback safety guarantees that deactivating a bridge does not invalidate unrelated promoted claims.
rollback does not alter existing receipts; it only changes selection pointers and activation states.
fallback routes ensure evaluability remains.
regression ensures that remaining bridges and baseline routes still satisfy required evidence.
rollback preserves evidence and creates audit artifacts for the reason and consequences.
Rollback safety is essential for governance.
C. Cloud
Ch17.C1 — Spec
Ch17.C1.a — Stress Before Promotion
Stress is required before promoting high-risk bridges.
stress suites include adversarial probes near branch boundaries, singularities, and ordering ties relevant to the bridge scope.
stress suite requirements are policy-defined and depend on bridge kind.
stress outcomes must be stored as stress packs and pass criteria must be met.
failing stress yields quarantine and remediation plans.
Stress prevents fragile bridges from becoming trusted tissue.
Ch17.C1.b — Regression Before Promotion
Regression ensures stability.
regression reruns bridge application traces and UCW computations at fixed κ and probe regimes.
regression checks for drift and stability under replay.
regression failures block promotion and trigger rollback/quarantine.
regression suites are required for promoted bridges and must run periodically.
Regression enforces long-term integrity.
Ch17.C1.c — Drift Alarms
Drift alarms detect semantic instability.
drift arises from ConvFP changes, canonicalization instability, ordering nondeterminism, or environment-dependent behavior.
drift alarms are raised when bridge UCWs change outcomes under fixed context.
drift alarms quarantine affected bridges and trigger remediation (ConvFP schema repair, method migration, environment pinning).
drift evidence is stored as drift logs and linked to affected artifacts.
Ch17.C1.d — Refusal Semantics
Bridge refusals are structured and predictable.
non-applicable due to scope mismatch or admissibility failure.
blocked due to policy forbiddance or missing bridges.
fails due to tri-gate violations (face/spin/projection).
fails due to inadequate probes or plateau above gates.
Refusal semantics ensure safe failure and prevent misuse.
Ch17.C2 — Encode
Ch17.C2.a — Stress Logs
Stress logs record stress suite execution.
suite ids, contexts, and probes.
pass/fail and margins.
counterexample objects for failures.
quarantine actions and remediation requirements.
Ch17.C2.b — Regression Logs
Regression logs record periodic verification.
suite ids, contexts, κ levels.
rerun UCWs and outcomes.
drift detection outcomes.
bisect lineage for failures.
Regression logs gate continued trust.
Ch17.C2.c — Drift Logs
Drift logs record detected drift.
affected bridge ids and scopes.
evidence: changed residual curves, changed UCW outcomes.
suspected causes: ConvFP incompleteness, method changes, environment changes.
remediation plans and migration requirements.
Drift logs prevent silent corruption.
Ch17.C2.d — Refusal Logs
Refusal logs record refused bridge applications.
refusal class and context.
minimal failing predicate and counterexample.
best-achieved residual evidence.
admissible next actions.
Refusal logs guide improvements and pruning.
Ch17.C3 — Prove
Ch17.C3.a — Stress-Pass ⇒ Safe Promotion
For high-risk bridges, stress-pass is necessary for safe promotion.
stress-pass indicates robustness under adversarial conditions.
stress-pass complements certified UCW evidence; both required.
stress-pass failure blocks promotion and triggers quarantine.
stress evidence is replayable and auditable.
Ch17.C3.b — Regression-Pass ⇒ Stability
Regression-pass indicates stability under replay.
repeated verification yields consistent UCW outcomes.
no drift alarms under fixed context.
regression ensures bridge remains safe over time.
regression failure yields deactivation and remediation.
Regression is a stability gate.
Ch17.C3.c — Drift ⇒ Reject
Drift implies bridge cannot be promoted or must be quarantined.
drift indicates hidden semantic change or nondeterminism.
drift triggers remediation and migration; it is not solved by widening corridors.
drift invalidates activation pointers.
drift evidence is preserved and used to update policies and ConvFP schemas.
Ch17.C3.d — Plateau ⇒ Reject
Plateau above gates implies bridge is not achieving its purpose.
if bridge application yields residual curves plateaued above gates, it is not promotable.
plateau may indicate insufficient probes or missing supporting charts.
plateau triggers probe expansion or chart synthesis, not promotion.
plateau evidence is stored as refusal packs.
Ch17.C4 — Extract
Ch17.C4.a — Promotion Dashboards
Promotion dashboards summarize bridge readiness.
candidate bridges pending stress/regression.
promoted bridges and their context coverage.
missing witness obligations.
quarantined bridges and reasons.
Dashboards guide governance decisions.
Ch17.C4.b — Failure Dashboards
Failure dashboards summarize bridge failures.
refusal classes and frequencies.
minimal counterexamples and failure hotspots.
drift alarms and instability regions.
recommended remediation tasks.
Ch17.C4.c — Risk Reports
Risk reports summarize exposure from bridges.
high-risk bridges and scopes.
missing stress/regression coverage.
policy conflicts affecting bridges.
risk mitigation plans and rollback readiness.
Ch17.C4.d — Remediation Plans
Remediation plans specify how to promote or fix bridges.
add missing UCWs and closure witnesses.
improve probe adequacy and add adversarial probes.
fix ConvFP scope gaps or create explicit convention bridges.
adjust policies or create policy bridges.
Plans are actionable and audit-linked.
D. Fractal
Ch17.D1 — Spec
Ch17.D1.a — Bridge Lifecycle
Bridges evolve through lifecycle states:
candidate → certified → promoted → active → (deprecated | quarantined).
candidate: derived from MagicCertPacks or discovered as useful pattern.
certified: passes UCW tri-gate in limited contexts.
promoted: passes governance gates and is reusable.
active: selected by governance pointers for default use.
deprecated: superseded but replayable.
quarantined: unsafe due to drift or failures.
Lifecycle transitions are logged and auditable.
Ch17.D1.b — Deprecation
Deprecation is safe retirement.
deprecate when superseded or when risk profile is unacceptable.
preserve fallback bridges and baseline routes.
preserve replay evidence and audit trails.
trigger regression to ensure closure remains.
Ch17.D1.c — Migration
Migration upgrades bridges and packs.
new versions of BridgeSeed created when scope expands or word_template changes.
migration edges link old to new.
backward witness preservation maintained or translators provided.
migration triggers regression and updates to compatibility maps.
Ch17.D1.d — Cross-Metro Packaging
Bridge packaging supports exchange across metros.
export BridgeSeeds and required MagicCertPacks as proof packs.
respect visibility modes and policy constraints.
quarantine imported bridges until local verification.
include compatibility reports and required policy bridges.
Cross-metro packaging enables shared brain tissue under safe governance.
Ch17.D2 — Encode
Ch17.D2.a — Lifecycle States
Lifecycle states are encoded in governance records and atlas registry pointers.
state transitions include reason codes and evidence pointers.
context coverage maps indicate where states apply.
activation pointers are context-indexed and reversible.
state changes trigger regression/stress as required.
Ch17.D2.b — Migration Edges
Migration edges encode bridge evolution.
MIGRATE(old_bs_id → new_bs_id) with scope and context.
include changes: word_template deltas, witness root deltas, scope deltas.
include re-verification receipts and regression/stress results.
preserve backward compatibility or declare breaks explicitly.
Ch17.D2.c — Alias Classes
Alias classes provide naming and grouping without semantic collapse.
group bridges by endpoint types and scope.
group bridges by function (chart switching, convention mapping, policy mapping).
do not imply equivalence; only provide lookup and selection hints.
selection requires promoted evidence and context compatibility.
Ch17.D2.d — Export Formats
Export formats define proof-carrying bundles.
bridge pack: BridgeSeed + required pins + witness roots expansion.
magic pack: MagicCertPack + required evidence.
chain pack: composite bridge with constituent packs.
manifests specify context, visibility mode, and replay requirements.
Exports are hash-committed and audit-ready.
Ch17.D3 — Prove
Ch17.D3.a — Safe Migration
Safe migration preserves correctness.
new bridge version must be verified and produce equivalent or stronger witness obligations.
migration cannot weaken gates silently; it can tighten.
migration preserves replay of old versions or provides verified translators.
migration triggers regression and quarantine on failure.
Ch17.D3.b — Safe Deprecation
Safe deprecation preserves functionality.
ensure alternate bridges or baseline routes exist.
ensure dependent promotions remain replay-verifiable.
deprecation is logged and reversible.
deprecation triggers regression.
Ch17.D3.c — Cross-Policy Safety
Cross-policy safety prevents policy laundering.
policy bridges must be explicit and witnessed.
imported bridges are verified under local policy before activation.
policy constraints on methods and budgets are enforced.
any mismatch yields refusal or quarantine.
Ch17.D3.d — Integrity Under Exchange
Integrity under exchange ensures imported brain tissue does not corrupt local truth.
imported BridgeSeeds and MagicCertPacks are quarantined until verified.
compatibility checks performed on ConvFP and corridor/policy contexts.
missing pins or hidden evidence yields boundary-limited status or refusal.
exchange preserves conflict proofs and drift alarms.
Ch17.D4 — Extract
Ch17.D4.a — Bridge Maps
Bridge maps summarize connectivity.
graph of bridges by endpoint types and scopes.
promoted chains and their contexts.
blocked regions and missing bridges.
risk overlays and required stress/regression.
Bridge maps support routing.
Ch17.D4.b — Migration Playbooks
Migration playbooks guide safe upgrades.
identify scope expansion needs.
generate new BridgeSeed version with updated word_template.
re-verify UCWs and run regression/stress.
update pointers and preserve old evidence.
Ch17.D4.c — Deprecation Playbooks
Deprecation playbooks guide safe retirement.
identify replacement bridges.
ensure fallback routes.
deprecate and log reasons.
rerun regression and monitor drift.
Ch17.D4.d — Exchange Packs
Exchange packs are proof-carrying exports.
include BridgeSeeds, MagicCertPacks, and required pins/witnesses.
include compatibility and policy-bridge requirements.
include quarantine instructions and verification scripts.
include provenance chain and risk classification.
Ch17 — BridgeRegistry, MagicCertPack, and BridgeSeed (“brain tissue”)
Ch18 — Cross-Metro ICIB: Imports, Compatibility, and Policy Bridges
A. Square
Ch18.A1 — Spec
Ch18.A1.a — ICIB Objective
ICIB (Inter-Context Integrity Bridge) is the cross-metro protocol for importing proof-carrying artifacts from an external metro into a local metro while preserving semantic integrity, governance constraints, and replay verifiability. ICIB is explicitly not a “share knowledge and assume it’s true” mechanism; it is a compatibility-and-closure protocol that treats external artifacts as candidates until they are locally validated or bridged.
The objectives are:
Integrity preservation. Imported artifacts must not corrupt local truth tissue. Local promotions remain valid; imports cannot override existing identities or acceptance predicates.
Local verifiability. Any imported artifact used in local promotion-critical paths must become locally replay-verifiable under local policies/corridors or under explicit policy bridges.
Explicit compatibility. Semantic compatibility (conventions, scope, schemas) and governance compatibility (policies, budgets) must be checked and witnessed; mismatches yield quarantines, bridges, or refusals.
Conflict containment. Conflicts are preserved, not erased. ICIB must detect and record conflicts and block unsafe merges.
Determinism and auditability. The import process must be deterministic given an import manifest and local context. All mapping and bridging decisions are logged and replayable.
ICIB converts “foreign evidence” into “local candidates,” then into “local promoted tissue” only when requirements are satisfied.
Ch18.A1.b — Import Object Types
ICIB imports objects that are proof-carrying or that can become proof-carrying under local verification. The normative import types are:
Node specs. Candidate or promoted nodes representing invariants, including atlas pointers and metadata. Imported nodes remain distinct identities unless equivalence is witnessed.
Chart specs and evaluation receipts. Certified evaluation charts (baseline/accel) with bound provenance, method ids, rounding ids.
UCWs and witness packs. Commutation/holonomy/projection witnesses, including probe regime definitions and corridor bindings.
Receipts. Proof receipts binding pins and witness roots; visibility modes and boundary limitations are honored.
Bridge artifacts. BridgeSeeds and MagicCertPacks, potentially enabling compatibility bridging across conventions or policies.
Policies and corridors. Policy and corridor records are importable as references; they are not automatically adopted. Local policy primacy holds; imported policies and corridors may be used only as constraints or for compatibility analysis.
Regression/stress packs. Evidence of robustness; used as candidate evidence and may be translated into local governance constraints.
Refusal artifacts. Refusal packs, plateau evidence, drift alarms, conflict proofs—importable as negative evidence guiding local search and preventing repeated failure.
All import types are interpreted through schema version binding and verifier catalog compatibility.
Ch18.A1.c — Compatibility Definition
Compatibility is a typed predicate determining whether a foreign artifact can be interpreted under local semantics and governance.
Compatibility has three layers:
Semantic compatibility. Compatibility of ConvBundleFP scope and ConvFP conventions, lens schemas, invariant schemas, and projection semantics. Semantic compatibility may be:
identical (same ConvFP records and scope),
bridgeable (compatible under promoted convention bridges),
incompatible (requires portal or schema extension).
Governance compatibility. Compatibility of policy constraints and corridor regimes. Governance compatibility may be:
identical (same policy/corridor),
bridgeable (policy bridges exist, corridor regimes are compatible or re-verifiable),
blocked (policy forbids required operations; corridor budgets incompatible).
Verifier/schema compatibility. Compatibility of schema versions and verifier versions. If local verifiers cannot interpret imported schemas, imported artifacts remain quarantined until translators (verified migrations) are available.
A foreign artifact is import-admissible only if it is schema-decodable and boundary-respectable. It is reuse-admissible only if semantic and governance compatibility conditions are satisfied or bridged.
Ch18.A1.d — Policy Bridge Definition
A policy bridge is a BridgeSeed (Ch17) whose function is to safely translate or re-validate evidence produced under one policy regime to become admissible under another policy regime.
Let (P_s) be source policy and (P_t) be target policy. A policy bridge (\mathsf{PB}: P_s\to P_t) specifies:
Admissibility mapping. Which operations and evidence types are permitted under (P_t) and how evidence from (P_s) must be transformed or re-verified to comply.
Budget mapping. How budgets and κ ladders translate or require strengthening. Policy bridges never weaken evidence requirements; they may require additional checks.
Visibility mapping. How visibility boundaries map (restricted/private evidence may not become public evidence without explicit disclosure mechanisms).
Witness obligations. Required UCWs, receipts, stress/regression suites to ensure that evidence transferred does not violate (P_t)’s safety invariants.
Policy bridges are promotable only under verified-only promotion, and they are required for cross-policy reuse of imported proof tissue.
Ch18.A2 — Encode
Ch18.A2.a — Compat Receipts
Compatibility results are recorded as compatibility receipts that are replay-verifiable.
A compat receipt includes:
foreign artifact ids and local context hashes,
decoded schema versions and verifier compatibility checks,
semantic compatibility outcome (identical/bridgeable/incompatible) and required bridge ids,
governance compatibility outcome (identical/bridgeable/blocked) and required policy bridge ids,
scope coverage checks and missing scope reports,
decision: reuse_allowed, reuse_quarantined, or reuse_refused, with explicit reasons.
Compat receipts are evidence objects used by ICIB to make deterministic decisions.
Ch18.A2.b — Policy-Bridge Receipts
Policy-bridge receipts certify that evidence has been upgraded or translated to meet local policy constraints.
source policy hash and target policy hash,
mapping procedure and word_template ids,
additional verification performed (recomputed UCWs, rerun evaluations under local corridor),
stress/regression packs required and satisfied,
resulting local receipts binding upgraded evidence.
Policy-bridge receipts are required before imported evidence becomes promotion-critical under local policy.
Ch18.A2.c — Import Manifests
An import manifest is the canonical input to ICIB.
list of imported object ids and their types,
exported proof packs: pins and witness roots expansion indices, subject to visibility mode,
schema and verifier catalog info,
claimed contexts (policies/corridors/ConvFP) under which artifacts were valid at source,
provenance fields (source metro id, export timestamp; metadata only).
Import manifests are hash-addressed. ICIB execution is deterministic given manifest and local context.
Ch18.A2.d — Provenance Fields
Provenance fields record where imported artifacts came from and what was done to them.
source metro id and source artifact ids,
mapping decisions and compatibility receipts,
local quarantine actions, bridge applications, and re-verification actions,
migration/translator usage.
Provenance is required for audit and for conflict evolution analysis.
Ch18.A3 — Prove
Ch18.A3.a — Safe Import Theorem
Theorem (Safe Import). If an imported artifact (a) is used in a locally promoted claim under local context (X_t), then there exists a local proof slice containing:
the import manifest and provenance,
compat receipts showing semantic and governance compatibility,
required policy bridges and convention bridges (if any),
locally replay-verifiable receipts and UCWs (either re-verified locally or translated via verified bridges),such that a local verifier can replay acceptance under (X_t) without trusting the source metro.
Therefore, imports can contribute to local truth only through locally verifiable evidence, preserving integrity.
Ch18.A3.b — No Merge ⇒ Meaning Theorem
Theorem (No Merge ⇒ Meaning). Merging data stores or importing artifacts does not imply semantic equivalence of nodes, charts, or claims. Any equivalence is represented only by explicit semantic edges witnessed by UCWs and bound by receipts.
Corollaries:
identical names across metros do not imply identity.
numeric proximity of approximations does not imply equivalence.
shared receipts do not imply shared context; context hashes must match or be bridged.
This theorem is enforced by ICIB by quarantining imports until compatibility and closure are verified.
Ch18.A3.c — Conflict Detection
Conflict detection determines when imported evidence contradicts local evidence under comparable contexts.
Context alignment. Conflicts are meaningful only when contexts are comparable (same or bridged ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor). If contexts differ and cannot be bridged, conflict is not asserted; instead, incompatibility is recorded.
Witness comparison. When comparable, compare UCW outcomes:
if one metro certifies equivalence and the other refuses under same context, conflict is detected,
if both certify but with incompatible ConvFP scope, convention conflict is detected.
Counterexample extraction. Conflict detection produces minimal counterexamples: probes and κ levels where witness outcomes diverge.
Conflict classification. Conflicts are classified as:
semantic conflict (convention/schema mismatch),
governance conflict (policy/corridor mismatch),
drift conflict (implementation instability),
non-identifiability (probe inadequacy).
Conflict detection outputs conflict proofs and triggers quarantines.
Ch18.A3.d — Quarantine Rules
Quarantine is the default import state for any artifact not yet locally verified.
Default quarantine. Imported artifacts enter as quarantined unless compat receipts certify reuse admissibility.
Quarantine propagation. Artifacts dependent on quarantined artifacts cannot be promoted; they may be evaluated as candidate-only.
Quarantine clearance. Clearance requires:
local verification (recompute receipts/UCWs) under local context, or
verified bridges/translators proving compatibility and preservation of evidence.
Quarantine permanence. Some artifacts may remain permanently quarantined due to incompatibility or policy restrictions; such status is recorded and indexed.
Quarantine rules enforce safe import and prevent contamination.
Ch18.A4 — Extract
Ch18.A4.a — Import Reports
Import reports summarize import outcomes.
list of objects imported and their types,
initial quarantine status per object,
compat outcomes and required bridges,
jobs scheduled for local verification or bridging,
refusals due to missing visibility or schema incompatibility.
Ch18.A4.b — Compat Reports
Compat reports summarize compatibility per imported artifact.
semantic compatibility outcomes and required convention bridges,
policy/corridor compatibility outcomes and required policy bridges,
schema/verifier compatibility outcomes and required translators,
scope coverage gaps and missing root/closure obligations.
Compat reports are used for scheduling and governance.
Ch18.A4.c — Conflict Reports
Conflict reports extract detected conflicts.
conflicting claims and contexts,
minimal counterexamples and divergent witness outcomes,
classification and root cause suspects,
required remediation actions: bridges, portal, probe expansion, policy changes.
Ch18.A4.d — Quarantine Reports
Quarantine reports list quarantined artifacts and clearance requirements.
reason for quarantine (incompatibility, missing pins, policy block, drift alarm),
required local jobs for clearance,
visibility limitations preventing external verification,
timeline and priorities under scheduler.
B. Flower
Ch18.B1 — Spec
Ch18.B1.a — Import Routing
Import routing maps foreign artifacts into local namespaces without collapsing identity.
foreign node ids are preserved; local alias nodes may reference them without asserting equivalence.
imported artifacts are placed into quarantine lanes by domain tag and scope.
mapping of names is handled by alias classes; meaning is handled by UCW-based edges.
routing includes compatibility checks and schedules bridging/verification jobs.
Ch18.B1.b — Hub Alignment
Hub alignment is the attempt to map foreign hubs to local hubs.
identify hub nodes/lenses in foreign metro.
propose alignments to local hubs based on schema keys and known bridge capabilities.
alignment is candidate-only until verified by transport UCWs and compatibility receipts.
alignment failure yields conflict or portal requirements; no forced alignment is allowed.
Hub alignment accelerates cross-metro import by leveraging stable meeting points.
Ch18.B1.c — Bridge Application
Bridge application is how imported evidence becomes locally admissible.
apply convention bridges when ConvFP differs but is bridgeable.
apply policy bridges when evidence must be upgraded to local policy constraints.
apply corridor re-verification when corridors differ and claims are corridor-indexed.
all bridge applications are logged as WordNF segments and produce local receipts.
Bridge application is never implicit and never modifies foreign evidence; it creates local upgraded evidence.
Ch18.B1.d — Local Closure After Import
Local closure is the process of integrating imported artifacts into local atlases and routes.
re-verify imported charts with local certified evaluation where required.
compute local UCWs linking imported charts/nodes to local baselines.
run RQF closure for imported candidate seeds if they are to be promoted locally.
run regression/stress suites required by local policy.
Local closure turns foreign evidence into local truth tissue.
Ch18.B2 — Encode
Ch18.B2.a — Import WordNFs
Import operations are encoded as WordNF segment streams.
mapping segments (namespace placement, aliasing).
bridge segments (convention/policy bridges applied).
verification segments (recompute evaluation receipts, UCWs).
closure segments (RQF, SPP updates).
Import WordNFs provide replay and audit.
Ch18.B2.b — Mapping Tables
Mapping tables encode how foreign ids map to local ids.
id mapping for nodes, charts, policies, corridors, ConvFP records.
mapping is typed: mapping does not assert equivalence; it asserts reference and provenance.
mapping includes disambiguation and alias pointers.
mapping is versioned; updates produce migration edges.
Mapping tables support deterministic import routing.
Ch18.B2.c — Bridge Chains
Bridge chains encode sequences of bridges needed for compatibility.
chain ids, component bridge ids, endpoint types.
context constraints and admissibility regions.
required witness roots for chain legality.
chain application logs and outcomes.
Bridge chains enable systematic cross-metro reconciliation.
Ch18.B2.d — Closure Jobs
Closure jobs are job graph nodes created after import.
local verification jobs for imported receipts/UCWs.
RQF jobs for imported candidate seeds.
SPP jobs for updating basis completeness with imported relations.
regression/stress jobs required by local governance.
Closure jobs are scheduled deterministically and are auditable.
Ch18.B3 — Prove
Ch18.B3.a — Mapping Soundness
Mapping soundness ensures that mapping tables do not corrupt semantics.
mapping preserves foreign identities and provenance.
mapping does not assert equivalence; it only provides references.
any equivalence must be proven by local UCWs and receipts.
mapping is replayable and auditable by importing the manifest and replaying mapping WordNF.
Ch18.B3.b — Bridge Soundness
Bridge soundness ensures cross-context upgrades preserve meaning.
convention bridges preserve semantic meaning under UCWs.
policy bridges preserve governance invariants and require re-verification where necessary.
corridor bridging is restricted; corridor-indexed claims require explicit re-verification under local corridors.
bridge soundness is replayable and verified by receipts.
Ch18.B3.c — Closure Soundness
Closure soundness ensures local integration is correct.
local verification reproduces acceptance outcomes under local contexts.
integrated charts are linked to local baseline by transport UCWs.
RQF closure yields Ω* certificates before promotion of imported seeds.
regression/stress suites enforce robustness.
Closure soundness guarantees imported evidence cannot bypass local verification.
Ch18.B3.d — Rollback Soundness
Rollback soundness ensures unsafe imports can be undone.
rollback preserves foreign evidence but revokes local activation pointers and quarantines unsafe artifacts.
rollback preserves audit trails and refusal evidence.
rollback preserves local baseline functionality; it does not corrupt existing promoted artifacts.
rollback is replayable and documented via governance logs.
Rollback soundness is essential for safe cross-metro exchange.
Ch18.B4 — Extract
Ch18.B4.a — What Was Imported
Extract list of imported artifacts by type.
node ids, chart ids, UCW ids, receipts.
policies/corridors/ConvFP records imported as references.
refusal artifacts and conflict proofs imported.
visibility modes and boundary limitations.
Ch18.B4.b — What Was Mapped
Extract mapping tables and alias pointers.
foreign→local reference mappings.
hub alignment proposals and their status.
bridge chain requirements and their status.
migration edges applied.
Ch18.B4.c — What Was Rejected
Extract rejected imports and reasons.
schema incompatibility.
missing pins or visibility restrictions preventing verification.
policy forbiddance.
conflicts and portal requirements.
Rejected imports are preserved as evidence but remain quarantined.
Ch18.B4.d — What Was Promoted
Extract locally promoted outcomes derived from imports.
local receipts and witness roots enabling promotion.
activated charts and bridge seeds.
updated basis elements and relations via SPP.
regression/stress coverage and governance pointer updates.
Promotion outputs are local truth tissue and are replay-verifiable.
C. Cloud
Ch18.C1 — Spec
Ch18.C1.a — Uncertainty Across Metros
Uncertainty differs across metros due to differing corridors, policies, probes, and conventions. ICIB must translate uncertainty explicitly rather than assuming equality.
uncertainty is context-indexed; imported “certified” status may correspond to different corridor gates than local.
probe regimes may differ; adequacy may differ.
policy may restrict methods used to verify uncertainty.
therefore imported uncertainty must be reinterpreted or downgraded until local verification completes.
Ch18.C1.b — Confidence Translation
Confidence translation maps foreign confidence levels to local ones conservatively.
foreign promoted → local quarantined until verified.
foreign certified → local candidate until verified under local corridor/policy.
foreign heuristic remains heuristic; cannot upgrade without certified verification.
foreign refused remains refused evidence; may guide local remediation.
Confidence translation is conservative: never overstate confidence.
Ch18.C1.c — Risk Translation
Risk translation maps foreign risk to local risk.
local policy may treat certain operators as higher risk.
local stress/regression requirements may be stricter.
imported artifacts inherit at least the max of foreign and local risk classifications.
risk translation determines required suites for local promotion.
Ch18.C1.d — Refusal Translation
Refusal translation preserves negative evidence.
refusal classes are mapped to local refusal taxonomy; unmapped refusals are preserved as opaque classes with evidence.
refusal evidence (counterexamples, plateau evidence) is preserved and used to avoid repeating failures.
refusal translation informs scheduling by indicating which actions are likely unproductive under similar contexts.
refusal translation may trigger portal analysis or policy bridge demands.
Ch18.C2 — Encode
Ch18.C2.a — Confidence Mapping
Confidence mapping records the translation.
mapping tables from foreign confidence tags to local tags.
required local verification tasks to upgrade confidence.
visibility constraints affecting confidence (missing pins).
mapping is versioned and auditable.
Ch18.C2.b — Risk Mapping
Risk mapping records translated risk and required suites.
risk class and scope.
required stress/regression suite ids.
quarantine requirements.
expected governance overhead.
Ch18.C2.c — Refusal Mapping
Refusal mapping records translated refusal classes and evidence links.
foreign refusal class and local mapped class.
counterexample objects and probes preserved.
blocked actions and policy constraints.
remediation suggestions.
Ch18.C2.d — Stress Requirements
Stress requirements encode what must be tested before local promotion.
required stress suites per imported artifact type.
adversarial probe regimes required.
pass/fail criteria.
linkage to activation and promotion gating.
Ch18.C3 — Prove
Ch18.C3.a — Translation Correctness
Translation correctness ensures conservative mapping.
translated confidence never exceeds what local evidence supports.
translated risk never underestimates local risk requirements.
translated refusals preserve evidence and do not weaken negative claims.
translation is replayable and auditable via mapping receipts.
Ch18.C3.b — No Overstated Confidence
No overstated confidence is enforced.
imported promoted artifacts cannot be treated as locally promoted without local verification.
imported certified evidence cannot satisfy local certified demands unless contexts match or are bridged and verified.
missing pins or restricted visibility forces downgrade or quarantine.
any attempt to bypass these rules yields refusal.
Ch18.C3.c — Adversarial Import Tests
High-risk imports require adversarial tests.
rerun UCWs on adversarial probes under local contexts.
test boundary conditions and stability regions.
test for drift under local numeric backends.
failure yields quarantine and conflict reports.
Adversarial tests prevent subtle incompatibilities.
Ch18.C3.d — Safe Downgrade Rules
Safe downgrades prevent unsafe reuse.
if local policy cannot verify imported evidence, downgrade to candidate-only.
if contexts are incompatible, quarantine indefinitely until bridges exist.
downgrades are recorded and do not delete evidence.
downgrades guide scheduler to prioritize bridge creation or portal analysis if beneficial.
Ch18.C4 — Extract
Ch18.C4.a — Cross-Metro Risk Report
Risk report summarizes risk introduced by imports.
counts by risk class.
missing suite coverage.
quarantined artifacts and reasons.
recommended mitigation tasks.
Ch18.C4.b — Confidence Report
Confidence report summarizes translated confidence.
imported promoted/certified artifacts and local translated statuses.
required verification tasks for upgrades.
blocked upgrades due to visibility or policy constraints.
audit links.
Ch18.C4.c — Stress Report
Stress report summarizes required and completed stress tests.
required suite ids and completion status.
pass/fail outcomes.
counterexamples for failures.
quarantine actions and remediation.
Ch18.C4.d — Regression Report
Regression report summarizes required and completed regression tasks.
required suite ids and completion status.
pass/fail outcomes.
drift alarms detected.
bisect lineage and remediation tasks.
D. Fractal
Ch18.D1 — Spec
Ch18.D1.a — Periodic Sync
Periodic sync is the governed process of repeating imports and maintaining alignment.
sync schedules are deterministic and policy-controlled.
sync does not auto-promote; it imports and quarantines until verified.
sync updates compatibility maps and conflict ledgers.
sync triggers regression and stress checks on affected promoted artifacts.
Periodic sync supports collaboration without compromising integrity.
Ch18.D1.b — Conflict Evolution
Conflicts evolve as evidence grows.
new bridges may resolve previously incompatible artifacts.
new drift alarms may invalidate previous compatibility.
conflicts are tracked as ledgers and are not erased by merges.
conflict evolution triggers portal analysis when repeated incompatibilities persist.
Conflict evolution ensures long-term clarity.
Ch18.D1.c — Compaction Across Metros
Compaction reduces cross-metro storage while preserving replay.
store minimal replay bundles for imported promoted artifacts once locally verified.
deduplicate identical objects by identity across metros when possible.
preserve refusal exemplars and conflict proofs.
compaction produces receipts proving replay equivalence.
Compaction enables scalable exchange.
Ch18.D1.d — Long-Term Governance
Long-term governance defines how ICIB affects system evolution.
policies define which external metros are trusted for candidate import.
governance defines quarantine durations and clearance requirements.
governance defines how cross-metro bridges are promoted and shared.
governance defines portal escalation thresholds when conflicts persist.
Governance ensures ICIB remains safe and purposeful.
Ch18.D2 — Encode
Ch18.D2.a — Sync Schedules
Sync schedules are canonical objects.
periodicity and triggers.
domains and artifact types to sync.
budgets and policy constraints.
audit binding: schedule logs and run logs.
Ch18.D2.b — Diff Formats
Diff formats encode changes between sync rounds.
new artifacts, removed artifacts, updated artifacts.
changed activation states and promotions.
changed compatibility outcomes and conflict outcomes.
diffs are deterministic and replayable.
Ch18.D2.c — Compaction Formats
Compaction formats encode compacted import stores.
minimal replay bundles stored.
indices for root expansion.
deduplication by identity.
compaction receipts.
Ch18.D2.d — Governance Logs
Governance logs record import decisions.
quarantine actions and clearances.
bridge promotions and policy bridge requirements.
conflict report issuance and portal escalations.
policy changes affecting imports.
Governance logs are auditable.
Ch18.D3 — Prove
Ch18.D3.a — Sync Determinism
Sync determinism requires that given the same manifest and local context, the same import results occur.
deterministic mapping rules.
deterministic compatibility checks.
deterministic quarantine and scheduling actions.
deterministic logs.
Determinism enables audit.
Ch18.D3.b — Diff Correctness
Diff correctness ensures diffs reflect true changes.
diffs computed from hash identities.
diffs do not collapse semantics; they report changes only.
diffs preserve conflict and refusal evidence.
diffs are replayable from sync logs.
Ch18.D3.c — Compaction Soundness
Compaction soundness ensures compaction does not change verifiable outcomes.
locally promoted artifacts remain replay-verifiable.
imported candidate evidence remains accessible for audit.
conflict proofs remain accessible.
compaction receipts prove equivalence.
Ch18.D3.d — Governance Compliance
Governance compliance ensures imports obey policies.
policies control allowed sources, scopes, and artifact types.
policies control quarantine requirements and clearance rules.
policies control bridge admissibility and required suites.
violations produce refusals and are logged.
Ch18.D4 — Extract
Ch18.D4.a — Sync Dashboards
Sync dashboards summarize sync status.
last sync time and scope.
imported artifacts counts and statuses.
quarantines and clearance progress.
planned next sync tasks.
Ch18.D4.b — Drift Dashboards
Drift dashboards summarize drift detected due to imports.
drift alarms and affected scopes.
quarantined artifacts and bridges.
required remediation.
regression impacts.
Ch18.D4.c — Conflict Ledgers
Conflict ledgers record unresolved conflicts.
conflict classes and evidence pointers.
minimal counterexamples.
attempted bridges and failures.
portal escalation status.
Conflict ledgers guide long-term resolution.
Ch18.D4.d — Governance Summaries
Governance summaries report:
policy bridge demands.
convention bridge demands.
portal candidates.
risk and confidence translation outcomes.
Summaries are linked to evidence and are audit-ready.
Ch18 — Cross-Metro ICIB: Imports, Compatibility, and Policy Bridges
Ch19 — Deterministic Replay, Verification CI, and Security
A. Square
Ch19.A1 — Spec
Ch19.A1.a — Determinism Definition
Determinism is the property that, under pinned contexts and pinned execution environments, verification outcomes and certified artifacts are invariant across replays. Determinism is not an implementation convenience; it is a semantic safety invariant enabling proof-carrying reuse, audit, and cross-site verification.
A computation or verification procedure (\mathcal P) is deterministic relative to a context (X) and environment (E) iff:[(\mathrm{Inputs},X,E);\mapsto;(\mathrm{Outputs},\mathrm{Decisions},\mathrm{Artifacts})]is a pure function such that repeated executions yield identical:
Canonical encodings. Serialized artifacts are identical under canonical encoding rules.
Hash identities. Object hashes are identical.
Verifier decisions. Accept/refuse outcomes and classification outcomes are identical.
Witness curves. Recomputed residual curves and enclosure values match as canonical numeric strings under pinned rounding rules.
Determinism is scoped: differences in policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP hashes define different contexts and therefore different admissible outcomes. Determinism prohibits hidden dependence on:
uncontrolled randomness,
nondeterministic iteration orders,
time-dependent behavior affecting semantics,
uncontrolled floating backend differences that influence certified outcomes.
Determinism is mandatory for promotion-critical artifacts (receipts, UCWs, bridges, Ω* certificates). Candidate-only artifacts may include nondeterministic search as long as it is segregated and does not influence promotion evidence.
Ch19.A1.b — Replay Contract
The replay contract defines the minimal evidence required to reproduce verification and certification decisions.
A promoted artifact (A) with receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}(A)) satisfies replay contract iff:
Pin completeness. The receipt’s pins root expands to all objects required to interpret and execute verification:
lens specs, schema versions,
ConvFP records and ConvBundleFP,
corridor and policy records,
method/bound/rounding/projection records,
any required environment/toolchain pins.
Witness completeness. The receipt’s witness root expands to all witness objects required:
UCWs, evaluation receipts,
MagicCertPacks/BridgeSeeds where required,
stress/regression result packs when required.
Route completeness. All relevant WordNF route objects (segment streams) are present and decodeable.
Verifier completeness. Verifier version hash is present and supported; if not supported, a verified translator exists and is pinned.
Replay termination. Replay procedure terminates under pinned budgets and yields a definitive accept/refuse outcome with evidence; no unbounded loops are allowed.
Replay contract is enforced by verifiers: failure to replay is a correctness failure leading to quarantine or de-promotion.
Ch19.A1.c — Verifier Contract
The verifier contract specifies obligations of verifiers and what artifacts may rely on them.
Input/Output contract. Given a receipt and access to pins and witness expansions, the verifier must:
validate hash bindings and schema versions,
recompute required evidence (residual curves, enclosures) where required,
apply acceptance predicates deterministically,
output acceptance/refusal and classification with evidence.
No trust in stored outcomes. Stored residual values are evidence for audit, not authority. Verifier either recomputes or validates by a checkable certificate; it never “trusts” uncheckable stored numbers for promotion decisions.
Deterministic semantics. Verifier behavior must be deterministic given pinned environment and toolchain pins. If determinism cannot be guaranteed, verifier must refuse promotion-critical decisions.
Schema discipline. Verifier interprets artifacts only under supported schema versions. Unsupported schemas require verified migration or refusal.
Safety discipline. Verifier enforces policy and corridor constraints strictly; it cannot bypass policy forbiddances or widen corridors.
The verifier contract defines the only trusted computation boundary in the system; all other computations are candidate generators.
Ch19.A1.d — Threat Model
Security is defined by an explicit threat model. The framework assumes adversaries may attempt to:
Corrupt artifacts. Modify stored objects, receipts, or witness packs to induce false acceptances.
Substitute contexts. Swap policies/corridors/ConvFP records to launder evidence.
Exploit nondeterminism. Use randomness or backend differences to produce inconsistent or opportunistic results.
Insert spurious proofs. Inject UCWs or evaluation receipts that appear valid but are not replay-verifiable.
Hide semantics. Use incomplete ConvFP scope or hidden defaults to create semantic drift.
Privacy leakage. Exfiltrate restricted data through receipts or proofs, or produce exports that reveal hidden pins.
Denial of verification. Trigger runaway verification cost or non-termination.
The defense posture is:
cryptographic commitments (hashing, merkle roots),
canonical encodings,
deterministic verifiers,
strict scope and policy enforcement,
explicit visibility modes and disclosure boundaries,
quarantine of unverified imports and high-risk artifacts,
budgeted verification and refusal semantics.
Ch19.A2 — Encode
Ch19.A2.a — Canonical JSON Rules
Canonical encoding rules are pinned and versioned. Canonical JSON (or canonical serialization) must satisfy:
Key ordering. Map keys sorted lexicographically under a fixed normalization.
List ordering. Lists reflect semantic ordering; no implicit sorting. Any ordering must be pinned by schema or ConvFP.
Numeric encoding. Numbers are encoded as normalized decimal strings under fixed rules:
no locale dependence,
fixed exponent representation rules,
disallow NaN/Infinity unless explicitly scoped.
Unicode normalization. Text fields normalized under fixed Unicode form.
Omission rules. Optional fields have deterministic inclusion/omission behavior pinned by schema version.
Schema id inclusion. Records include schema version identifiers; decoders refuse unknown schema versions.
Canonical encoding is itself a pinned object (canon_hash) referenced by receipts and verifiers.
Ch19.A2.b — Hash Rules
Hash rules define how identities are computed.
Primary hash. Use collision-resistant hash (\mathrm{H}) over canonical encoding bytes.
Domain separation. Hash inputs include type tags to prevent cross-type collisions (e.g., UCW|..., Rcpt|..., WordNF|...).
Merkle roots. Pins and witnesses are committed via merkle roots over deterministically ordered lists. Expansion indices must be present for replay.
Hash chaining. For segment streams, optional chain hashes provide tamper-evident ordering; chain hashes are non-semantic but audit-relevant.
Hash upgrades. Hash algorithm upgrades require migration edges and re-verification; old hashes remain supported for backward replay via verifier catalogs.
Ch19.A2.c — Environment Pins
Environment pins capture any dependency on execution environment that can influence deterministic outcomes.
Numeric backend pins. Bigfloat backend version, rounding behavior, integer arithmetic semantics when relevant.
Library pins. Versions of libraries implementing evaluation, interval arithmetic, PSLQ, and transforms when they influence certified outcomes.
CPU/OS pins. Only when required to guarantee determinism (generally avoided; prefer pure algorithmic determinism).
Seed pins. Random seeds when randomness is permitted (usually for candidate search only). Seeds must be included in route identities if they influence outputs.
Locale/timezone pins. Must not affect semantics; if they do, system is non-deterministic and must refuse promotion.
Environment pins are treated as pins in receipts; missing pins yield boundary-limited or refused replay.
Ch19.A2.d — Toolchain Pins
Toolchain pins capture the verifier and build pipeline environment.
Verifier pin. Verifier version hash and supported schema catalog.
Compiler/build pin. Build system version and any code generation settings that influence serialized artifacts.
CI runner pin. CI environment versions when they influence determinism.
Artifact tooling pins. Tool versions for canonicalization, compaction, export, and migration.
Toolchain pins support reproducible CI and forensic audit.
Ch19.A3 — Prove
Ch19.A3.a — Replay Invariance
Replay invariance is the theorem that promoted artifacts reproduce verification outcomes.
Given a promoted receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) and access to all pinned objects and witness objects, a verifier can replay verification and obtain the same accept/refuse decision.
Replay invariance is conditioned on environment pins: the replay environment must satisfy pinned requirements.
Replay invariance implies that stored artifacts are sufficient; missing pins or schema mismatch breaks replay and yields quarantine.
Replay invariance is maintained over time by preserving schema versions or providing verified translators.
Ch19.A3.b — Verifier Invariance
Verifier invariance ensures that verifiers behave consistently.
For a fixed verifier version, inputs determine outputs deterministically.
If verifier upgrades, old verifier outcomes remain replayable via:
running old verifier versions, or
applying verified translators/migrations that preserve semantics and produce equivalent acceptance under new verifier.
Verifier invariance is enforced by CI regression suites that rerun verification on canonical bundles.
Verifier invariance is a governance requirement; unverifiable verifier changes are forbidden.
Ch19.A3.c — Corruption Detection
Corruption detection identifies tampering or accidental corruption.
Hash mismatch detection. Any mismatch between stored object bytes and stored hash id is corruption.
Merkle root mismatch detection. Expansion of pins/witnesses not matching merkle roots indicates corruption or index mismatch.
Replay mismatch detection. If recomputed residual curves differ from stored evidence under pinned context and environment, either drift occurred or corruption occurred; classification requires additional checks.
Schema mismatch detection. Unknown schema versions or inconsistent schema hashes indicate corruption or incompatible migration.
Corruption detection yields refusal and quarantines affected artifacts.
Ch19.A3.d — Tamper-Evidence
Tamper-evidence ensures that corruption cannot be hidden.
All artifacts are hash-addressed; modification changes hash.
Receipts bind pins and witness roots; swapping dependencies breaks roots.
Route segment streams may include chain hashes; reordering breaks chain.
CI manifests record expected root hashes for canonical bundles; mismatch indicates tampering.
Tamper-evidence is the basis for trust in stored proofs.
Ch19.A4 — Extract
Ch19.A4.a — Replay Scripts
Replay scripts are deterministic procedures derived from receipts.
expand pins and witness roots deterministically via indices.
validate all hashes and schema versions.
reconstruct WordNF segment streams and recompute evaluation receipts and UCWs.
apply acceptance predicates and output decisions.
Scripts are versioned and pinned; their semantics are verifier-defined.
Ch19.A4.b — CI Manifests
CI manifests define what is verified continuously.
list of canonical proof bundles to verify.
required verifier versions and schema catalogs.
required environment/toolchain pins.
expected root hashes and acceptance outcomes.
Manifests are hash-addressed and support tamper-evident CI.
Ch19.A4.c — Environment Snapshots
Environment snapshots package environment pins.
numeric backend versions.
library versions.
toolchain versions.
hardware/OS identifiers when required.
Snapshots are included in audit packets and external exports when permitted.
Ch19.A4.d — Integrity Proofs
Integrity proofs are compact certificates proving that an artifact set is untampered.
root hash commitments for bundles.
merkle roots and expansion indices.
chain-hash proofs for segment streams.
CI verification results and signatures (where applicable).
Integrity proofs are used in cross-site exchange and archival.
B. Flower
Ch19.B1 — Spec
Ch19.B1.a — CI Pipelines
CI pipelines enforce continuous verification of promoted artifacts.
run verifier on canonical proof bundles (promotion bundles, Ω* bundles, key bridges, key basis relations).
run regression suites on representative sets of nodes/edges/charts.
run stress suites for high-risk scopes and for newly promoted tunnels/bridges.
enforce policy compliance: CI runs under strict policies and records policy hashes.
CI pipelines are deterministic under pinned environments; nondeterminism is treated as failure.
Ch19.B1.b — Job Replays
Job replays reproduce scheduler jobs.
replay uses job graphs and run logs; given same inputs and contexts it reconstructs job decisions and outputs.
replay verifies that budgets were respected and that outputs match recorded hashes.
replay supports audit of scheduling and governance.
replay ensures that candidate generation remains segregated from promotable evidence.
Ch19.B1.c — Regression Execution
Regression execution reruns verification on a fixed suite.
re-verify UCWs and evaluation receipts at fixed κ.
re-verify RQF closures and Ω* certificates for promoted nodes.
re-verify bridges and policy bridges.
detect drift and quarantine affected artifacts.
Regression execution is part of stability guarantee.
Ch19.B1.d — Stress Execution
Stress execution performs adversarial testing.
execute adversarial probe suites near branch cuts, singularities, and ordering ties.
execute perturbation tests for robustness.
execute worst-case commutation and loop closure tests.
stress-pass is required for high-risk promotions and activations.
Stress execution supports safety.
Ch19.B2 — Encode
Ch19.B2.a — CI Logs
CI logs record pipeline runs.
manifest id, verifier version, environment snapshot.
bundles tested and their root hashes.
pass/fail outcomes and failure classifications.
links to failure artifacts and remediation tasks.
CI logs are tamper-evident and auditable.
