Ch19.B2.b — Replay Logs
Replay logs record job replays.
job ids replayed and contexts.
recomputed outputs and hash comparisons.
discrepancies and classifications (drift vs corruption vs environment mismatch).
rollback or quarantine actions.
Replay logs support audit and forensics.
Ch19.B2.c — Failure Artifacts
Failure artifacts include:
minimal failing predicate ids and counterexamples.
diffs of residual curves and enclosures.
context mismatch evidence.
schema mismatch or environment mismatch evidence.
Failure artifacts are used to drive remediation.
Ch19.B2.d — Remediation Artifacts
Remediation artifacts are structured plans.
migration edges required.
bridge promotions required.
probe expansions required.
policy changes required.
Remediation artifacts are tracked by governance and scheduler.
Ch19.B3 — Prove
Ch19.B3.a — CI Correctness
CI correctness ensures CI results reflect true verification outcomes.
CI uses pinned verifier versions and environment snapshots.
CI replays verification deterministically.
CI failures are reproducible by replay scripts.
CI never accepts heuristic evidence as promotion evidence.
CI correctness is enforced by independent replay checks.
Ch19.B3.b — Regression Correctness
Regression correctness ensures regressions detect drift reliably.
regression suite is fixed and versioned.
regression reruns recomputation, not stored outcomes.
regression failures produce quarantines and refusal evidence.
regression suite changes require governance and migration.
Regression correctness maintains stability.
Ch19.B3.c — Stress Correctness
Stress correctness ensures stress tests are meaningful.
adversarial probes are adequate and scoped.
stress results are replayable and deterministic.
stress failures produce counterexample artifacts.
stress suite changes require governance and versioning.
Stress correctness prevents false safety.
Ch19.B3.d — False-Positive Control
False-positive control prevents spurious CI failures.
strict determinism ensures that failures are real; however, acceptable nondeterminism is permitted only for candidate search and is excluded from CI promotion checks.
tolerance gates are fixed by corridor; CI does not arbitrarily tighten gates without corridor migrations.
environment mismatches are detected and classified separately from semantic failures.
CI uses stable numeric encodings for comparison; rounding ids prevent trivial formatting differences from causing failures.
False-positive control ensures CI remains actionable.
Ch19.B4 — Extract
Ch19.B4.a — CI Dashboards
CI dashboards summarize CI health.
pass/fail rates by bundle class.
drift alarms and quarantines.
failures by scope (which operator family).
backlog of remediation tasks.
Dashboards support governance.
Ch19.B4.b — Failure Diff Packs
Failure diff packs provide minimal evidence for debugging.
before/after hashes.
residual curve diffs.
enclosure diffs.
context and environment diffs.
Diff packs enable bisect and remediation.
Ch19.B4.c — Bisect Tools
Bisect tools localize failures by hash lineage.
bisect over migration edges.
bisect over chart activation events.
bisect over corridor/policy changes.
bisect over verifier version changes.
Bisect tools produce minimal failing changesets.
Ch19.B4.d — Rollback Plans
Rollback plans specify safe rollback procedures.
rollback chart activations.
rollback bridge activations.
rollback policy pointer changes.
rollback corridor pointer changes.
Rollbacks preserve audit trails and maintain baseline functionality.
C. Cloud
Ch19.C1 — Spec
Ch19.C1.a — Adversarial Failure Modes
Adversarial failure modes are threats to correctness and safety.
semantic drift masked by numeric agreement.
spurious PSLQ relations.
branch cut boundary failures.
ill-conditioning near singularities.
ordering nondeterminism causing canonicalization drift.
policy laundering via imported evidence.
Adversarial failure modes are addressed by stress suites and negatify maps.
Ch19.C1.b — Negatify Maps
Negatify maps encode failure-space regions.
map operator scopes and parameter regions to failure classes.
store minimal counterexamples.
store remediation obligations.
use maps to schedule stress and to prune unsafe regions.
Negatify maps are part of the security posture: they prevent repeating known dangerous patterns.
Ch19.C1.c — Uncertainty Containment
Uncertainty containment prevents uncertain artifacts from contaminating promoted truth.
quarantined artifacts cannot be used in promoted routing.
heuristic evidence cannot enter witness roots for promotion.
refusals are authoritative evidence of limitation under contexts.
upgrades are explicit and require certified verification.
Uncertainty containment is a safety invariant.
Ch19.C1.d — Refusal Guarantees
Refusal guarantees ensure honesty.
any inability to verify yields a structured refusal artifact, not guesswork.
refusals include minimal failing predicates and counterexamples.
refusals are replayable under pinned contexts.
refusals guide remediation and policy change demands.
Refusal guarantees are essential for security and trust.
Ch19.C2 — Encode
Ch19.C2.a — Adversarial Test Suites
Adversarial suites are versioned and scoped.
suite ids, operator scopes, parameter regions.
probe definitions and adequacy metadata.
required acceptance gates.
result pack schema.
Ch19.C2.b — Threat Proofs
Threat proofs are evidence objects.
corruption detection proofs (hash mismatch evidence).
drift detection proofs (cross-κ inconsistency under fixed context).
policy laundering detection proofs (context mismatch evidence).
disclosure boundary violation proofs.
Threat proofs trigger quarantines and remediation.
Ch19.C2.c — Refusal Logs
Refusal logs store failure evidence.
refusal class and context.
minimal counterexample objects.
budgets consumed and blocked actions.
remediation suggestions.
Refusal logs are used in governance and CI.
Ch19.C2.d — Risk Badges
Risk badges classify artifacts.
risk class and scope.
required stress/regression coverage.
quarantine status.
compliance status.
Risk badges guide routing and scheduling.
Ch19.C3 — Prove
Ch19.C3.a — Robustness Claims
Robustness claims are valid only if stress suites pass.
claims must be scoped and context-indexed.
stress pass evidence is replayable.
robustness claims do not override corridor gates; they complement them.
failures yield quarantine and rollback.
Ch19.C3.b — Downgrade Safety
Downgrade safety ensures failures lead to safe system behavior.
failed artifacts are quarantined and deactivated.
fallback to baseline preserved.
downgrade is recorded and auditable.
dependent promotions may be suspended until remediation.
Downgrade safety prevents cascading corruption.
Ch19.C3.c — Quarantine Correctness
Quarantine correctness ensures quarantines are justified and enforceable.
quarantines are based on verifiable evidence: drift alarms, stress/regression failures, corruption evidence.
quarantines block promotion-critical routing.
quarantines include clearance requirements and remediation tasks.
quarantines preserve evidence and audit trail.
Ch19.C3.d — Disclosure Boundaries
Disclosure boundaries prevent leakage.
receipts and proof packs respect visibility modes.
restricted pins are not exported unless policy permits.
boundary-limited proofs are labeled and cannot be treated as externally verifiable.
attempts to export private evidence yield refusal.
Disclosure boundaries are enforced by export tooling and verified by CI compliance checks.
Ch19.C4 — Extract
Ch19.C4.a — Security Reports
Security reports summarize threats and mitigations.
corruption incidents and integrity proofs.
drift alarms and quarantines.
policy laundering blocks and bridge requirements.
disclosure boundary violations and mitigations.
Reports are audit-ready.
Ch19.C4.b — Risk Reports
Risk reports summarize system risk.
risk class distribution.
missing stress/regression coverage.
high-risk active charts/bridges.
recommended deactivations and remediation.
Ch19.C4.c — Quarantine Reports
Quarantine reports summarize quarantined artifacts.
reasons and evidence.
affected scopes and dependencies.
clearance requirements.
scheduled remediation jobs.
Ch19.C4.d — Compliance Summaries
Compliance summaries ensure governance adherence.
policy compliance of CI runs.
corridor compliance of verification.
ConvFP scope coverage compliance.
export compliance with visibility modes.
Compliance is evidence-based and auditable.
D. Fractal
Ch19.D1 — Spec
Ch19.D1.a — Long-Term Integrity
Long-term integrity is preserving replay and meaning across time.
preserve canonical encoding rules and schema versions.
preserve verifier versions or provide verified translators.
preserve promoted receipts and witness packs and refusal exemplars.
maintain regression/stress and drift detection as continuous enforcement.
Long-term integrity ensures the metro remains a trustworthy memory web.
Ch19.D1.b — Archival Replay
Archival replay enables verification from archives.
archives store proof packs and minimal replay bundles.
archives store schema/verifier catalogs needed to interpret artifacts.
archives store environment snapshots when required.
archival replay is deterministic and auditable.
Archival replay supports long-term reproducibility.
Ch19.D1.c — Compaction with Verification
Compaction must preserve verifiability.
compaction deduplicates by identity only.
compaction retains replay-critical pins/witness indices.
compaction produces compaction receipts proving replay equivalence.
compaction does not delete conflict and refusal evidence required for governance.
Compaction is governed and audited.
Ch19.D1.d — Cross-Version Migration
Cross-version migration updates schemas/verifiers safely.
migrations are explicit artifacts.
migrations include verification evidence that meaning and acceptance outcomes are preserved or correctly changed.
migrations preserve backward verification or provide verified translators.
migrations trigger CI regression and quarantines on failure.
Migration is safety-critical.
Ch19.D2 — Encode
Ch19.D2.a — Archival Formats
Archival formats are canonical bundles.
store canonical encodings and hashes.
store indices for merkle root expansion.
store schema/verifier catalogs.
store environment snapshots and toolchain pins where required.
Archives are tamper-evident.
Ch19.D2.b — Compaction Receipts
Compaction receipts certify that compaction preserved replay.
before/after bundle hashes.
mapping of retained artifacts.
replay equivalence evidence for promoted receipts.
retained refusal exemplars and conflict proofs list.
Compaction receipts are audit-ready.
Ch19.D2.c — Migration Receipts
Migration receipts certify schema/verifier upgrades.
old/new schema hashes and verifier hashes.
translation procedure ids.
re-verification results demonstrating preserved outcomes.
rollback plan for failed migrations.
Migration receipts are required before adopting new versions.
Ch19.D2.d — Reference Pointers
Reference pointers preserve navigation across archives and versions.
pointers to current verifier catalog.
pointers to current schema catalog.
pointers to archived proof packs for promoted artifacts.
pointers to migration chains and translators.
Pointers are governed metadata.
Ch19.D3 — Prove
Ch19.D3.a — Archival Soundness
Archival soundness ensures archives preserve replay.
archived bundles contain all pins and witness roots expansions needed for promoted receipts.
archived bundles contain schema/verifier catalogs to interpret artifacts.
archived bundles can be replay-verified under pinned environments or via translators.
archival corruption is detected by hash mismatches and merkle root checks.
Ch19.D3.b — Compaction Soundness
Compaction soundness ensures compaction does not alter outcomes.
promoted receipts remain replay-verifiable.
witnesses and pins expansions remain available.
compaction receipts prove equivalence.
compaction preserves refusal evidence required for remediation.
Ch19.D3.c — Migration Soundness
Migration soundness ensures upgrades preserve meaning.
translators are verified and replayable.
migrated artifacts preserve acceptance outcomes or change them only when explicitly declared and justified.
migration is scoped; changes do not leak across unrelated scopes.
migration failures trigger rollback and quarantines.
Ch19.D3.d — Backward Witness Preservation
Backward witness preservation ensures old proofs remain verifiable.
preserve old verifier versions or provide verified translation.
preserve old ConvFP, corridor, and policy records referenced by old receipts.
preserve old UCWs and evaluation receipts.
regression suites include legacy artifacts to enforce backward verification.
Backward witness preservation maintains trust over time.
Ch19.D4 — Extract
Ch19.D4.a — Archive Indices
Archive indices support retrieval.
index by node/edge/chart ids.
index by receipt ids and witness ids.
index by schema/verifier versions.
index by time and governance events (metadata).
Ch19.D4.b — Compaction Diffs
Compaction diffs explain what changed.
deduplicated artifacts list.
compacted candidate logs summary.
retained vs removed evidence map.
compaction receipts and replay equivalence evidence.
Ch19.D4.c — Migration Playbooks
Migration playbooks guide safe upgrades.
identify impacted schemas and artifacts.
run translators and re-verify canonical bundles.
run CI regression and stress suites.
update catalogs and pointers; rollback on failures.
Ch19.D4.d — Integrity Dashboards
Integrity dashboards summarize system integrity.
CI health and regression/stress pass rates.
drift alarms and quarantines.
corruption incidents and integrity proofs.
migration status and backward verification coverage.
Dashboards are audit-ready and support governance.
Ch19 — Deterministic Replay, Verification CI, and Security
Ch20 — Reference Architecture and Implementation Blueprint
A. Square
Ch20.A1 — Spec
Ch20.A1.a — Module Boundaries
The reference architecture is modular by design to enforce determinism, auditability, and separation of concerns. Module boundaries are normative; cross-module coupling is limited to typed interfaces with explicit schemas and version pins.
The system is partitioned into the following modules:
Core Kernel (K). Minimal primitives required for canonical encoding, hashing, deterministic arithmetic interface, interval/enclosure primitives, and verifier execution scaffolding. Kernel contains no domain-specific mathematics; it contains only the infrastructure for proof-carrying artifacts.
Schema Registry (SR). Versioned schema definitions and validators for all record types: LensSpec, ConvFP records, CorridorRecord, PolicyRecord, WordNF, UCW, Receipts, Refusals, ChartSpec, BoundSpec, BridgeSeed/MagicCertPack, SchedulerJob, ImportManifest.
Object Store (OS). Content-addressed storage keyed by hash identities. Supports retrieval by hash, bundle storage, and expansion indices for merkle roots. Storage may be local or remote but must preserve canonical bytes.
Metro Graph Store (MG). Graph layer storing nodes and edges plus indices for lookup, routing, and dashboards. MG references OS for payloads by hash; MG does not inline large content.
Evaluator Library (EL). Certified evaluation charts and associated bound libraries. EL provides deterministic evaluators that return receipts (Ch11). EL is domain-pluggable: different domains provide different chart families.
Transport Library (TL). Typed transport operators with legality predicate hooks, WordNF compilation, and commutation/holonomy obligations (Ch07–Ch08).
Verification Engine (VE). The deterministic verifier implementing UCW verification, receipt verification, RQF verification, bridge verification, and policy/corridor checks.
Repair Engine (RE). Snap implementation and deterministic repair planning (Ch09), including trace generation and defect decrease certification.
Tunnel Engine (TE). Tunneling execution and logging (Ch10), including candidate chart integration and witness hooks.
Discovery Engines (DE). Structured discovery passes:
AAS (acceleration synthesis) (Ch14),
T0-OBSTR (Tier-0 obstruction compilation) (Ch12),
SPP (basis growth and relation search) (Ch15),
RQF (atlas forcing and Ω* coherence) (Ch13).
Scheduler & Governance (SG). Demand model, job graphs, prioritization, fairness, risk gating, and governance logs (Ch16).
CI & Security (CS). Deterministic replay CI, regression/stress suites, integrity proofs, archive/migration tooling (Ch19).
Cross-Metro ICIB (XM). Import manifests, compatibility receipts, policy bridges, quarantine rules, and sync tools (Ch18).
All modules communicate via hash-addressed objects and schema-validated records. Modules may be replaced or extended only via versioned interfaces.
Ch20.A1.b — Object Schemas
Object schemas define the executable data model. The architecture mandates that every persisted object belongs to a schema class and is hash-addressed.
Key schema classes:
Specification objects. LensSpec, InvariantSchema, OperatorCatalog, GateStackSpec, BoundSpec, ChartTemplateSpec, RiskPolicySpec, SchedulerPolicySpec.
Context objects. ConvFPRecord, ConvBundleRecord, CorridorRecord, PolicyRecord, EnvironmentSnapshot, ToolchainSnapshot, VerifierCatalog.
Execution identity objects. WordNF, SegmentStream, TunnelEvent, SnapTrace, SpliceRecord.
Witness objects. UCW, Ω* certificate, EvaluationReceipt, NonReductionCertificate, StressPack, RegressionPack, ConflictCert, DriftCert, RefusalPack.
Receipt objects. Rcpt (pins_root, wits_root), CompatReceipt, PolicyBridgeReceipt, CompactionReceipt, MigrationReceipt.
Metro objects. NodeSpec, EdgeSpec, AtlasSpec, ChartSpec registry entries, ActivationEvent, DeprecationEvent, QuarantineEvent.
Scheduler objects. DemandRecord, JobSpec, JobGraph, RunLog, GovernanceLog, FailureMap.
Exchange objects. ImportManifest, MappingTable, SyncSchedule, DiffRecord, ExchangePack.
Schemas are versioned and enforced by VE. Unsupported schemas must be migrated via explicit migration artifacts.
Ch20.A1.c — Core APIs
Core APIs are typed functions that operate exclusively on hash-addressed schema objects. No API accepts “raw” untyped data for promotion-critical paths.
Normative API groups:
Storage API.
put(bytes)->hash,
get(hash)->bytes,
exists(hash)->bool,
put_bundle(manifest)->bundle_hash,
get_bundle(bundle_hash)->manifest,
expand_root(root_hash,index_ref)->list[hash].
Schema API.
validate(schema_id, obj_bytes)->obj,
encode(obj)->bytes,
decode(bytes)->obj,
migrate(obj_hash, target_schema)->new_hash.
Evaluation API.
eval(chart_id, params, context)->EvaluationReceipt,
bound_eval(bound_id, params, context)->BoundValue,
project(projector_id, x, context)->Projected.
Transport/Word API.
compile_route(path)->WordNF,
normalize_word(word)->WordNF,
apply_transport(transport_id, x, params, context)->y.
Witness API.
compute_ucw(word_pair, context)->UCW,
verify_ucw(ucw, context)->Decision,
compute_omega_star(atlas, context)->OmegaStar,
verify_receipt(rcpt)->Decision.
Repair/Tunnel API.
snap(goal_state)->SnapOutcome,
tunnel(goal_state, tunnel_policy)->TunnelOutcome,
apply_bridge(bridge_id, instance)->Decision.
Discovery API.
aas_run(node_id, demand)->ChartCandidates,
t0_obstr_run(loop_suite, context)->ObstructionRecord,
rqf_run(node_id, context)->RQFOutcome,
spp_run(target_weight, context)->SPPOutcome.
Scheduler API.
submit_demand(demand)->demand_id,
plan_jobs(demand_id)->JobGraph,
run_job(job_id)->RunLog,
status(demand_id)->StatusReport.
ICIB API.
import_manifest(manifest)->ImportReport,
compat_check(imported_obj)->CompatReceipt,
apply_policy_bridge(pb)->PolicyBridgeReceipt,
local_closure(imported_candidate)->ClosureReport.
All APIs produce schema objects and store outputs by hash identity. Promotion is done only through receipts generated by VE.
Ch20.A1.d — Minimal Kernel
The minimal kernel is the smallest trusted substrate.
Kernel responsibilities:
Canonical encoding and hashing. Deterministic canonical serialization and hash computation.
Content-addressed storage interface. Immutable storage by hash identity and merkle root expansion.
Deterministic arithmetic interface. A pinned interface to bigfloat arithmetic sufficient for verifier recomputation; raw float equality is never used for acceptance.
Enclosure primitives. Interval/enclosure data types and operations with conservative rounding cushions.
Verifier scaffolding. Execution engine for verifying predicates and recomputing evidence under pinned contexts.
Budget enforcement primitives. Deterministic budget counters and termination checks.
The kernel must remain minimal and auditable; adding domain-specific logic to kernel is forbidden. Domain plugins live above the kernel and are verified by the kernel.
Ch20.A2 — Encode
Ch20.A2.a — Datamodel Layout
The datamodel is a typed hierarchy.
spec/: schema specs and catalogs (LensSpec, OperatorCatalog, BoundSpec, ChartTemplateSpec).
ctx/: ConvFP, Policy, Corridor, VerifierCatalog, environment/toolchain snapshots.
obj/: NodeSpec, EdgeSpec, AtlasSpec, ChartSpec, BridgeSeed, MagicCertPack.
word/: WordNF and segment streams (GateRun, TunnelEvent, SpliceRecord, SnapTrace).
wit/: UCW, EvalReceipt, OmegaStar, StressPack, RegressionPack, RefusalPack, ConflictCert, DriftCert.
rcpt/: receipts (Rcpt, CompatReceipt, PolicyBridgeReceipt, MigrationReceipt, CompactionReceipt).
sched/: DemandRecord, JobSpec, JobGraph, RunLog, GovernanceLog, FailureMap.
xmetro/: ImportManifest, MappingTable, SyncSchedule, DiffRecord, ExchangePack.
Each object is stored by hash in OS; MG stores graph indices and references.
Ch20.A2.b — Serialization Layout
Serialization layout is canonical and schema-driven.
canonical bytes. Each object encodes to canonical bytes under schema version.
type tags. Every serialized object includes a type tag and schema id.
numeric strings. Numeric fields encoded as normalized decimal strings.
segment streams. WordNF includes an ordered list of segments, each with its own schema id.
root expansions. Merkle roots are expanded via explicit index objects stored by hash.
Serialization layout is pinned by canon_hash and enforced by VE.
Ch20.A2.c — Hash Layout
Hash layout defines identity computation.
id(obj)=H(type_tag || schema_id || canonical_bytes).
merkle roots commit to deterministic lists of ids.
bundle hashes commit to manifests listing included ids.
chain hashes optionally commit to segment stream order.
Hash layout supports tamper-evidence and integrity proofs.
Ch20.A2.d — Storage Layout
Storage layout is content-addressed.
OS stores canonical bytes keyed by hash.
MG stores indices: by name, by type, by weight/depth, by context coverage, by promotion status.
cache stores computed expansions and computed evidence pointers, keyed by memo keys; caches are non-authoritative.
archives store bundles plus schema/verifier catalogs and environment snapshots.
Storage preserves immutability; updates create new hashes.
Ch20.A3 — Prove
Ch20.A3.a — Schema Invariants
Schema invariants must hold for all stored objects.
decodability. Each object is decodable under its schema id; unsupported schema yields refusal or requires migration.
canonicalization idempotence. Encoding then decoding yields identical canonical encoding.
type separation. Objects of different types cannot collide in identity due to type-tag domain separation.
reference integrity. All referenced ids exist or are declared external with explicit boundary limitations.
Schema invariants are enforced by SR and audited by CI.
Ch20.A3.b — API Invariants
API invariants define correctness constraints for core APIs.
APIs never accept untyped data for promotion-critical outcomes.
APIs must return schema-validated objects with hash identities.
APIs must respect policy and corridor constraints; policy/corridor mismatches yield refusal objects.
APIs must log decisions deterministically when decisions affect outcomes (scheduler, repair, tunnel).
API invariants ensure correctness is enforceable.
Ch20.A3.c — Upgrade Invariants
Upgrades must preserve replay and integrity.
schema upgrades occur by migration edges and migration receipts.
verifier upgrades occur by verifiers catalogs and migration playbooks.
upgrades do not overwrite identities; they create new identities and preserve backward witnesses.
upgrades trigger regression and stress suites.
Upgrade invariants ensure long-term stability.
Ch20.A3.d — Test Invariants
Test invariants define the verification obligations of CI.
promoted receipts must replay-verify.
UCWs and Ω* certificates must recompute and pass gates.
bridges must pass regression and stress requirements.
imports must remain quarantined until verified; no CI test may treat imported evidence as local truth without local receipts.
Test invariants ensure integrity.
Ch20.A4 — Extract
Ch20.A4.a — API Index
API index lists all core APIs, grouped by module, with schema and version ids.
Storage API.
Schema API.
Evaluation API.
Transport/Word API.
Witness API.
Repair/Tunnel API.
Discovery API.
Scheduler API.
ICIB API.
Index includes input/output schemas and error/refusal classes.
Ch20.A4.b — Schema Index
Schema index lists schema ids, versions, and decoders.
list of schema types and their versions.
migration paths between versions.
verifier support coverage.
deprecation/tombstone status.
Schema index supports backward verification.
Ch20.A4.c — Version Index
Version index lists:
verifier versions.
operator catalog versions.
scheduler policy versions.
risk policy versions.
canon hash versions.
Version index supports determinism and audit.
Ch20.A4.d — Tool Index
Tool index lists all internal tools and workflows.
CI tools and manifests.
compaction tools.
migration tools.
import/export tools.
audit tools and replay scripts.
Tool index supports operations and governance.
B. Flower
Ch20.B1 — Spec
Ch20.B1.a — Evaluator Library
Evaluator Library (EL) is a plugin architecture for certified evaluators.
provides baseline charts per root.
provides accel charts verified by AAS and bound to baseline.
provides bound library and rounding models.
provides stability region metadata and admissibility checks.
EL outputs EvaluationReceipts and supports bound audits.
Ch20.B1.b — Transport Library
Transport Library (TL) provides typed transports and legality predicate hooks.
transport signatures and operator ids.
WordNF compilation and normalization.
commutation/holonomy witness planning.
scope and admissibility checking.
TL integrates with VE to compute UCWs.
Ch20.B1.c — Snap/Tunnel Engine
Repair and tunneling are engines with strict logging.
Snap applies deterministic repair actions under budgets.
Tunnel applies representation changes and logs tunnel events.
both require defect reduction certificates and no silent widening.
both produce MagicCertPacks and bridge candidates for BridgeRegistry.
Ch20.B1.d — Discovery Engine
Discovery engines produce candidates and verified promotions.
AAS for accel charts.
T0-OBSTR for Tier-0 candidates.
RQF for atlas closure and Ω* certificates.
SPP for basis growth and relation search.
Discovery engines are governed by scheduler; no engine promotes without verification.
Ch20.B2 — Encode
Ch20.B2.a — Evaluator Specs
Evaluator specs encode chart definitions.
chart id, method id, bound id, rounding id, projector id.
parameter schema and admissible ranges.
root assignment and scope coverage.
required witness hooks and regression/stress suites.
Evaluator specs are hash-addressed.
Ch20.B2.b — Transport Specs
Transport specs encode transport operators.
operator id, signature, parameter schema.
scope and admissibility constraints.
required witness obligations.
WordNF templates for application.

Ch20.B2.c — Job Specs
Job specs encode scheduler tasks.
job type and inputs.
context hashes and budgets.
expected outputs and acceptance predicates.
dependency edges.

Ch20.B2.d — Pipeline Specs
Pipeline specs encode standard workflows.
baseline→UCW closure→Snap/Tunnel→AAS→RQF→promotion.
SPP basis growth pipeline.
ICIB import pipeline.
CI regression/stress pipeline.
Pipeline specs are versioned and used to build job graphs deterministically.
Ch20.B3 — Prove
Ch20.B3.a — Unit Verification
Unit verification tests module correctness.
schema validation tests for each record type.
canonical encoding idempotence tests.
hash identity determinism tests.
evaluator enclosure and bound tests.
UCW computation and verification tests.
Unit verification is part of CI.
Ch20.B3.b — Integration Verification
Integration verification tests whole workflows.
baseline evaluation produces receipts.
UCW closure works across charts.
Snap repairs reduce defect or refuse.
AAS produces verified accel charts.
RQF produces Ω* certificates.
BridgeRegistry produces promotable bridge seeds.
Integration verification includes replay tests from receipts.
Ch20.B3.c — Property Tests
Property tests enforce invariants.
no silent widening property.
no heuristic promotion property.
deterministic scheduling property.
cross-context mismatch detection property.
Property tests detect integrity violations.
Ch20.B3.d — Replay Tests
Replay tests validate that promoted artifacts replay.
expand pins/wits roots.
recompute evaluations and UCWs.
verify Ω* certificates.
verify bridge applications.
Replay tests are the core long-term guarantee.
Ch20.B4 — Extract
Ch20.B4.a — Performance Profiles
Performance profiles provide non-semantic cost data.
evaluation cost vs κ per chart.
UCW computation cost vs probes.
Snap and tunnel cost distributions.
scheduler throughput.

Ch20.B4.b — Correctness Profiles
Correctness profiles summarize verification outcomes.
coverage of promoted artifacts.
regression pass rates.
stress pass rates.
drift alarms and quarantines.
Profiles guide governance.
Ch20.B4.c — Coverage Reports

Ch20.B4.d — Benchmark Packs
Benchmark packs provide standardized evaluation suites.
sets of canonical nodes and demands.
expected receipts and witness packs.
expected performance bounds.
used in CI and in performance testing.
Benchmarks are hash-addressed and replayable.
C. Cloud
Ch20.C1 — Spec
Ch20.C1.a — Numeric Backends
Numeric backends provide arithmetic and special function evaluation for certified evaluation.
backend must be pinned for determinism.
backend must support required precision and deterministic rounding.
backend may be used as oracle only in search; certified evaluation relies on enclosures and bounds.
backend differences are treated as environment pins and can trigger refusal for promotion-critical outcomes.
Ch20.C1.b — Interval Kernels
Interval kernels implement enclosure operations.
scalar intervals and complex rectangles.
vector/tensor balls in declared norms.
operator enclosures (spectral bounds, operator norms).
kernels must be conservative and deterministic.
Interval kernels are part of the trusted evaluation stack and must be audited.
Ch20.C1.c — Tail Bound Library
Tail bound library stores bound specs and evaluators.
bound ids and provenance.
assumption region checks.
composition rules.
bound audits and upgrades.
Tail bounds are essential for certified evaluation.
Ch20.C1.d — Projector Library
Projector library stores (\Pi_h) and canonicalization operators.
projector ids and parameters.
scope and admissibility regions.
projection residual functionals.
commutation obligations with transports.
Projectors guard meaning-class membership.
Ch20.C2 — Encode
Ch20.C2.a — Backend IDs
Backend IDs identify numeric backends.
backend name and version.
deterministic behavior pins.
supported precision ranges.
restrictions and known issues.
Backend ids are included in environment snapshots.
Ch20.C2.b — Method IDs
Method IDs identify evaluation algorithms.
method family and version.
required bound ids.
required rounding id.
stability region.
Method ids are pinned in evaluation receipts.
Ch20.C2.c — Bound IDs
Bound IDs identify tail bound functions.
bound functional form and assumptions.
composition rules.
derivation references.
migration links.
Bound ids are pinned in evaluation receipts.
Ch20.C2.d — Rounding IDs
Rounding IDs identify rounding models and cushions.
rounding mode assumptions.
cushion function.
backend dependencies.
policy admissibility.
Rounding ids are pinned in evaluation receipts and UCWs.
Ch20.C3 — Prove
Ch20.C3.a — Enclosure Tests
Enclosure tests verify interval kernels and evaluation correctness.
known identities and enclosure containment.
monotone tightening under κ.
cross-method agreement via UCWs.
adversarial numeric cases.
Enclosure tests are required in CI.
Ch20.C3.b — Bound Audits
Bound audits verify tail bounds.
check assumptions.
recompute bound values.
check conservativeness via stress/regression where possible.
quarantine invalid bounds and migrate.
Bound audits maintain certified evaluation integrity.
Ch20.C3.c — Regression Suite
Regression suite ensures stability.
rerun key evaluation receipts.
rerun key UCWs.
rerun key Ω* certificates.
rerun bridges.
Regression suite is pinned and versioned.
Ch20.C3.d — Stress Suite
Stress suite ensures robustness.
adversarial probes.
perturbation tests.
branch boundary tests.
ill-conditioned cases.
Stress suite is required for high-risk promotions.
Ch20.C4 — Extract
Ch20.C4.a — Method Selection Guides
Guides show how to select evaluators under constraints.
choose baseline vs accel.
choose most stable near boundaries.
choose cheapest under SLA.
fallback rules.
Guides are context-indexed.
Ch20.C4.b — Reliability Ranking
Reliability ranks charts and transports.
by confidence and gate margins.
by perturbation robustness.
by regression stability.
by risk class.
Ranking is audit-friendly.
Ch20.C4.c — Fallback Ladder
Fallback ladder lists safe fallbacks.
baseline per root.
alternate charts.
alternate transports.
refusal-driven next steps.

Ch20.C4.d — Audit Recipes
Audit recipes are standard replay procedures.
replay evaluation receipt.
replay UCW.
replay Ω* closure.
replay bridge application.
Recipes are versioned and used in CI.
D. Fractal
Ch20.D1 — Spec
Ch20.D1.a — Scheduler Integration
Scheduler integrates modules into workflows.
demands map to job graphs.
job graphs reference module APIs.
scheduling respects risk gates and budgets.
outcomes produce receipts and evidence for promotion or refusal.
Scheduler integration is deterministic.
Ch20.D1.b — Caching Integration
Caching accelerates evaluation and verification.
caches store expanded roots and computed evidence pointers.
caches store frequently used evaluation receipts and UCWs.
caches invalidate on context/schema/verifier changes.
caches are non-authoritative and audited.
Ch20.D1.c — Compaction Integration
Compaction reduces storage without losing replay.
deduplicate by identity.
compact candidate logs.
preserve promoted proofs and refusal exemplars.
produce compaction receipts.
Compaction is governed and auditable.
Ch20.D1.d — Cross-Metro Integration
Cross-metro integration integrates ICIB.
import manifests and mapping tables.
compatibility receipts and bridges.
local closure and quarantines.
periodic sync and conflict ledgers.
Cross-metro integration preserves integrity.
Ch20.D2 — Encode
Ch20.D2.a — Telemetry Fields
Telemetry fields capture non-semantic metrics.
demand stats.
evaluation and verification costs.
defect reductions.
failure classifications.
Telemetry is metadata and cannot influence semantic acceptance.
Ch20.D2.b — Cache Keys
Cache keys uniquely identify cached entries.
node/chart/context/κ/params.
UCW id and context.
job id and context.
invalidation dependencies.
Cache keys ensure correctness.
Ch20.D2.c — Compaction Keys
Compaction keys identify compaction operations.
bundle ids compacted.
before/after root hashes.
preserved evidence sets.
compaction receipts.
Compaction keys support audit.
Ch20.D2.d — Sync Keys
Sync keys identify sync operations.
sync schedule id.
import manifest ids.
mapping table ids.
compatibility receipts and quarantine logs.
Sync keys support deterministic sync.
Ch20.D3 — Prove
Ch20.D3.a — Cache Correctness
Cache correctness ensures caches cannot assert false results.
caches store pointers to receipts and witnesses.
verifiers revalidate context hashes.
invalidation prevents stale reuse.
regression audits caches.
Ch20.D3.b — Scheduler Correctness
Scheduler correctness ensures scheduling respects governance.
deterministic urgency computation.
fairness and starvation avoidance.
policy and corridor compliance.
no heuristic promotion.
Scheduler correctness is audited by schedule replay.
Ch20.D3.c — Compaction Correctness
Compaction correctness ensures compaction preserves replay.
promoted receipts remain replay-verifiable.
merkle root expansions remain possible.
compaction receipts prove equivalence.
refusal exemplars retained.
Ch20.D3.d — Sync Correctness
Sync correctness ensures imports do not corrupt local truth.
quarantines enforce local verification.
compatibility receipts guide reuse.
bridges and policy bridges are required before promotion-critical reuse.
sync logs are deterministic and auditable.
Ch20.D4 — Extract
Ch20.D4.a — Ops Playbooks
Ops playbooks guide operation.
deploy verifier catalogs and schema registries.
run CI and regression/stress.
handle drift alarms and quarantines.
manage corridor/policy changes.
Playbooks are audit-oriented.
Ch20.D4.b — Maintenance Playbooks
Maintenance playbooks guide upkeep.
rotate and archive bundles.
compact stores with compaction receipts.
migrate schemas and verify.
prune candidate logs safely.
Ch20.D4.c — Upgrade Playbooks
Upgrade playbooks guide safe evolution.
upgrade verifiers and schemas via migrations.
rerun regression and stress suites.
update catalogs and pointers.
rollback on failures.
Upgrades preserve backward verification.
Ch20.D4.d — Roadmap Tasks
Roadmap tasks convert coverage gaps into work.
missing root charts.
missing bridges and policy bridges.
plateaued regions requiring new templates.
portal candidates requiring semantic extension.
Tasks are prioritized by demand and dependency impact.
Ch20 — Reference Architecture and Implementation Blueprint
Ch21 — Worked Exemplars, Deep Implications, and Roadmap
A. Square
Ch21.A1 — Spec
Ch21.A1.a — Exemplar Selection Criteria
Exemplars are not illustrations; they are proof-carrying reference instances that demonstrate end-to-end execution of the framework under real constraints. Each exemplar must be selected so that it exercises distinct failure modes and demonstrates the corresponding governance and verification responses.
Selection criteria:
Coverage of core passes. Each exemplar must invoke:
certified evaluation (Ch11),
UCW commutation/holonomy/projection witnessing (Ch08),
transport legality (Ch07),
Snap repair and refusal semantics (Ch09),
tunneling and/or AAS where applicable (Ch10, Ch14),
atlas forcing and Ω* coherence where applicable (Ch13),
receipt binding and proof slicing (Ch05),
regression/stress gating where risk requires (Ch19).
Root diversity. Each exemplar must implement at least two distinct atlas roots, and the exemplar set collectively must cover all four roots across exemplars.
Defect diversity. Exemplars must include:
a successful closure case with strong margins,
an acceleration case showing verified-only promotion,
a case where tunneling is refused or rolled back,
a case where portal necessity is detected and proved,
a case where policy blocks decisive verification.
Reproducibility. Each exemplar must be replay-verifiable under pinned contexts. The exemplar must produce minimal replay bundles and audit packs.
Compositional reuse. Each exemplar must yield reusable templates: chart templates, bridge templates, macro repair templates, and CI harness bundles.
Exemplar selection is governed: each exemplar has a ledger entry defining scope, risk class, required suites, and acceptance outcomes.
Ch21.A1.b — Baseline Atlas Requirements
Every exemplar must begin from baseline truth anchors.
Baseline charts. Each exemplar requires at least one certified baseline chart in each root that it uses. Baselines must be independent of acceleration and must be certified by interval/tail/rounding receipts.
Transport binding. If multiple charts exist for the same invariant, baseline charts must be bound by UCWs to establish coherence or must remain isolated as candidates until coherence is proven.
Projection stability. Baseline charts must satisfy projection stability requirements under (\Pi_h) where required, and must include projection residual evidence.
Fallback existence. Exemplar kits must include fallback charts and fallback routes; no exemplar is permitted to depend on a single fragile chart.
Baseline atlas requirements ensure that exemplars are grounded in certified evaluation rather than heuristic numerics.
Ch21.A1.c — Closure Targets
Each exemplar declares explicit closure targets.
Edge closure targets. Specific transport edges (baseline↔baseline, baseline↔accel) must have promotable UCWs.
Face closure targets. Where composition matters, specified commutation squares must be closed.
Loop closure targets. Any cycle introduced by chart integration must have spin residual gates satisfied.
Ω* targets. For exemplars whose purpose is atlas forcing, the SFCR interior closure target is declared, and Ω* certificate must be produced for promotion.
Closure targets are explicit and determine which evidence must be produced and which jobs are scheduled.
Ch21.A1.d — Reusable Templates
Exemplars must produce reusable templates that are extracted as “kits.”
Chart templates. Parameterized chart specs usable for other weights or related invariants.
Repair templates. Snap action sequences that reliably reduce defect for common failure classes.
Bridge templates. BridgeSeeds and MagicCertPacks converting episodic success into reusable capability.
CI templates. Regression/stress suite subsets adequate to maintain long-term integrity for the exemplar domain.
Reusable templates are versioned and exported as template libraries.
Ch21.A2 — Encode
Ch21.A2.a — Exemplar Ledgers
An exemplar ledger is a canonical record describing the exemplar instance.
Fields include:
exemplar id, scope, and domain tag.
target nodes/edges/charts and required contexts (ConvBundleFP/policy/corridor).
declared closure targets and acceptance predicates.
risk class and required stress/regression suites.
produced artifacts: receipts, UCWs, tunnel logs, Snap traces, bridge packs, Ω* certificates.
minimal replay bundle ids and audit pack ids.
Ledger is hash-addressed and used by CI manifests.
Ch21.A2.b — Route Words
Route words are WordNF objects encoding:
evaluation routes for baseline charts,
transport routes linking charts,
tunnel events and splice records for acceleration integration,
closure routes for face and interior witnesses,
repair routes for Snap iterations.
Route words are pinned in exemplar kits and are replay-addressable.
Ch21.A2.c — Witness Packs
Witness packs collect:
UCWs for commutation and loop closure,
certified evaluation receipts and bound provenance,
adequacy packs for probes,
stress/regression packs required by risk class,
refusal artifacts where applicable.
Witness packs are committed by merkle roots and expanded via indices.
Ch21.A2.d — Receipt Packs
Receipt packs are minimal replay bundles:
pins_root expansion (lenses, policies, corridors, ConvFP records, method/bound/rounding/projection ids),
wits_root expansion (UCWs, evaluation receipts, stress/regression packs),
verifier catalog and schema records,
replay scripts and CI manifest entries.
Receipt packs are exportable according to visibility mode and are the core deliverable for independent verification.
Ch21.A3 — Prove
Ch21.A3.a — Exemplar Proofs
Each exemplar proof is a replay-verifiable chain of receipts and witnesses certifying the declared closure targets.
Evaluation proofs. Each baseline and accel chart used must have certified evaluation receipts.
Transport proofs. Each claimed chart equivalence or transport step must have promotable UCWs under the pinned context.
Composition proofs. Face commutation squares must be closed where declared.
Atlas proofs. Where declared, Ω* certificate is produced and verified by replay.
The exemplar proof is the composition of these proof objects under receipt algebra, not an informal explanation.
Ch21.A3.b — Robustness Proofs
Robustness proofs demonstrate stability under adversarial and perturbation regimes when required.
run perturbation tests on probes and parameters,
run stress suites for branch boundaries or ill-conditioned regions,
verify that gates remain satisfied under perturbations,
record counterexamples and quarantine if robustness fails.
Robustness proofs are required for high-risk exemplars and produce stress packs.
Ch21.A3.c — Regression Proofs
Regression proofs demonstrate that exemplar correctness persists over time.
define regression suite of UCWs and evaluation receipts,
rerun under pinned contexts periodically,
detect drift and produce drift logs and quarantines,
maintain backward verification via schema/verifier catalogs.
Regression proofs are recorded as regression packs and are included in CI manifests.
Ch21.A3.d — Stress Proofs
Stress proofs are required for high-risk bridges, tunnels, and portal-sensitive scopes.
define adversarial probe suites,
execute deterministically under pinned environments,
require pass criteria for promotion,
failures produce refusal packs and remediation plans.
Stress proofs prevent fragile accelerations from becoming trusted defaults.
Ch21.A4 — Extract
Ch21.A4.a — Exemplar “Kits”
Exemplar kits are exportable bundles.
minimal replay bundle,
proof slice for each closure target,
template extraction outputs (chart templates, bridge seeds),
CI harness fragments (regression/stress suite definitions).
Kits are designed to be executed independently and to serve as reference implementations.
Ch21.A4.b — Template Libraries
Template libraries collect reusable components:
chart templates per domain and root,
bound templates and projector recipes,
bridge templates and magic packs,
repair templates (Snap macro words),
scheduling templates (job graph patterns).
Templates are versioned and indexed by scope.
Ch21.A4.c — Quickstart Routes
Quickstart routes provide minimal paths to reproduce core outcomes.
evaluate baseline charts,
compute UCW equivalences,
run AAS for accel charts,
integrate accel charts and verify binding,
run RQF and obtain Ω* when applicable.
Quickstart routes are WordNF sequences with required contexts and budgets.
Ch21.A4.d — Audit Routes
Audit routes provide procedures for independent verification.
validate receipt pins and witness roots,
replay evaluation receipts,
replay UCWs and Ω* certificates,
rerun regression/stress suites.
Audit routes are deterministic and part of the CI toolchain.
B. Flower
Ch21.B1 — Spec
Ch21.B1.a — (\zeta(3)) Multi-Chart Closure
This exemplar demonstrates baseline and acceleration coherence for a single invariant using multiple charts.
baseline chart (e.g., alternating eta-based evaluation) yields certified receipt,
accel chart (e.g., Apéry/binomial-suppressed series) yields certified receipt,
transport UCW certifies equivalence baseline↔accel under tri-gate,
chart activation governed: accel becomes active only after UCW and required suites pass.
This exemplar demonstrates the CHART_BRIDGE and transport binding requirement.
Ch21.B1.b — (\zeta(5)) Apéry Acceleration
This exemplar demonstrates AAS search→verify separation and deterministic tunnel integration.
search: PSLQ-driven coefficient discovery in Apéry-like template family (candidate-only),
verify: certified evaluation of candidate chart with tail bounds,
transport binding: UCW to baseline (\zeta(5)) evaluation,
promotion: chart promoted and optionally activated with regression/stress gating.
This exemplar demonstrates AAS and tunnel logging plus rollback safety.
Ch21.B1.c — (\beta(4)) (\chi_4) Acceleration
This exemplar demonstrates acceleration for Dirichlet beta values with character-modulated series.
baseline: alternating odd series certified by tail bounds,
candidate: binomial-suppressed, (\chi_4)-projected template family candidate from deterministic enumeration,
verify: certified residual containment and transport UCW binding,
promotion and activation under governance.
This exemplar demonstrates template generalization beyond pure zeta ladders.
Ch21.B1.d — (\varphi / i / \log A) / Elliptic Portals
This exemplar category demonstrates portal detection and semantic extension.
show where standard root charts cannot close under existing schema (e.g., monodromy or genus change),
produce conflict proofs and portal necessity reports,
define portal nodes requiring new carrier/schema or convention bridges,
verify portal bridge obligations when possible.
This exemplar demonstrates the boundary between tunneling and portal creation and the correctness of portal classification.
Ch21.B2 — Encode
Ch21.B2.a — Chart Specs
Chart specs for exemplars are recorded with:
chart ids, root assignments,
method/bound/rounding/projector ids,
parameter schemas and selected parameters,
certified receipts and stability region metadata.
Ch21.B2.b — Tunnel Event Logs
Tunnel event logs record AAS integration:
enumerator states and candidate logs,
verification logs and residual packs,
splice records for chart integration,
promotion logs and activation events.
Tunnel logs are part of audit trails.
Ch21.B2.c — Transport UCWs
Transport UCWs certify equivalences:
WordNF hashes for compared routes,
tri-gate residual curves across κ,
probe adequacy packs and projection signatures,
classification and promotable status.
UCWs are the core semantic evidence for exemplar closures.
Ch21.B2.d — Atlas Diffs
Atlas diffs record evolution:
baseline charts added,
accel charts added and activated,
witness obligations added,
coherence closures achieved,
deprecations/quarantines recorded.
Atlas diffs enable audit and maintainability.
Ch21.B3 — Prove
Ch21.B3.a — Closure UCWs
Closure UCWs prove commutation and equivalence.
baseline↔baseline equivalences where multiple baselines exist,
baseline↔accel transport binding UCWs,
loop closure UCWs for new cycles,
projection stability UCWs when required.
Closure UCWs are replayable and certify semantic coherence.
Ch21.B3.b — Acceleration UCWs
Acceleration UCWs certify that accel charts preserve meaning.
certified evaluation receipts exist for accel chart,
residual containment holds against baseline receipts,
transport binding UCW passes tri-gate,
robustness evidence exists if required.
Acceleration UCWs are necessary for activation and safe substitution.
Ch21.B3.c — Portal Necessity Proofs
Portal necessity proofs show that closure cannot be achieved in the current regime.
repeated failures under admissible tunnels and repairs,
conflict evidence under adequate probes and κ refinement,
incompatibility of conventions or required new schema fields,
portal report specifying required extensions.
Portal necessity proofs prevent inventing wrong constants.
Ch21.B3.d — Rejection/Refusal Proofs
Rejection proofs demonstrate that certain candidates must not be promoted.
failed certified verification of candidate charts,
drift detection or instability under replay,
plateau evidence and probe inadequacy evidence,
policy block evidence.
Refusal proofs preserve honesty and guide remediation.
Ch21.B4 — Extract
Ch21.B4.a — Reusable Accelerators
Reusable accelerators are extracted as promoted accel charts and BridgeSeeds.
chart templates generalized to other weights.
bound and projector recipes.
transport binding bridges baseline↔accel.
activation policies and fallback ladders.
Accelerators become reusable library components.
Ch21.B4.b — Reusable Portal Bridges
Reusable portal bridges are extracted when portal requirements are satisfied.
portal node specs and schema extensions.
portal bridge seeds with explicit word templates.
conflict proofs and compatibility receipts.
stress/regression suites required.
Portal bridges are high-risk and heavily governed.
Ch21.B4.c — Regression Harnesses
Regression harnesses are extracted per exemplar.
suites of evaluation receipts and UCWs to rerun.
fixed κ ladders and probes.
bisect tools for failures.
CI manifest entries.
Harnesses maintain long-term integrity.
Ch21.B4.d — Stress Harnesses
Stress harnesses are extracted per exemplar.
adversarial probes for branch boundaries and singularities.
perturbation suites.
worst-case commutation and loop closure probes.
pass criteria and remediation procedures.
Harnesses support safe promotion.
C. Cloud
Ch21.C1 — Spec
Ch21.C1.a — “What Uncertainty Remains” Taxonomy
Uncertainty remains in structured categories:
numeric uncertainty: insufficient κ or loose bounds for desired tolerance.
probe uncertainty: inadequate probes causing non-identifiability.
method uncertainty: no certified charts available in required roots or stability regions.
holonomy uncertainty: loop closure failing stably, suggesting missing moduli or portal needs.
semantic uncertainty: convention conflicts or scope gaps.
governance uncertainty: policy forbids needed actions.
Taxonomy is used to classify refusals and schedule remediation.
Ch21.C1.b — Confidence Stratification
Confidence is stratified for exemplar outcomes.
promoted and active (best).
promoted but inactive (safe but not default).
certified but not promoted (missing governance suites).
candidate (incomplete evidence).
refused/quarantined (blocked or unsafe).
Stratification guides reuse.
Ch21.C1.c — Open Bottlenecks
Open bottlenecks are classified:
missing root charts,
missing bounds or projector stability,
missing bridges (convention/policy),
plateaued relation searches requiring new templates,
portal requirements needing schema extensions.
Bottlenecks are linked to refusal evidence and are actionable.
Ch21.C1.d — Safe Claims Boundary
Safe claims boundary defines what the system may assert now.
only claims with promotable receipts and UCWs are asserted as semantic equivalences.
candidate charts found by oracle search are not asserted as truth.
portal claims require portal reports and conflict proofs.
anything outside boundary is recorded as candidate or refusal with evidence.
Safe boundary preserves integrity.
Ch21.C2 — Encode
Ch21.C2.a — Open-Problem Ledgers
Open-problem ledgers record unresolved tasks.
target node/edge/weight.
missing obligations.
refusal evidence and counterexamples.
proposed remediation and required budgets.
Ledgers are scheduled by demand.
Ch21.C2.b — Plateau Ledgers
Plateau ledgers record plateau regions.
which residual curves plateau.
adequacy status.
attempted repairs and tunnels.
proposed next methods or carriers.
Plateau ledgers guide AAS and portal work.
Ch21.C2.c — Risk Ledgers
Risk ledgers record high-risk areas.
high-risk charts and bridges.
missing stress/regression coverage.
drift alarms and quarantines.
mitigation plans.
Risk ledgers guide governance.
Ch21.C2.d — Governance Ledgers
Governance ledgers record policy and corridor constraints.
policy blocks and required policy bridges.
corridor changes requested and required evidence.
deprecations and migrations.
compliance status.
Ledgers ensure controlled evolution.
Ch21.C3 — Prove
Ch21.C3.a — What Can Be Concluded Now
Only claims with replay-verifiable receipts and witnesses can be concluded.
baseline certified evaluations and their enclosures.
chart equivalences certified by UCWs.
accelerated evaluations certified and bound to baseline by transport UCWs.
promoted basis elements and relations under SPP evidence.
Ω* closures for promoted nodes where required.
These conclusions are scoped by context hashes and do not generalize beyond them.
Ch21.C3.b — What Cannot Be Concluded
Claims cannot be concluded when:
evidence is heuristic only,
probe adequacy insufficient,
residuals plateau above gates,
policy forbids decisive verification,
conventions conflict without bridges,
portal necessity is unresolved.
In these cases the system must refuse or mark candidate status.
Ch21.C3.c — Why
Reasons for non-conclusion are encoded.
minimal failing predicates and counterexamples.
plateau evidence and dominance classification.
blocked actions due to policy.
missing root charts or missing bridges.
The system never offers narrative excuses; it offers evidence.
Ch21.C3.d — What Evidence Is Needed
Evidence needed is given as explicit obligations.
stronger bounds or higher κ ladders.
probe expansions and adversarial probes.
new chart templates (AAS) and transport binding UCWs.
bridge promotions (policy/convention) and regression/stress suites.
portal schema extensions with conflict proofs.
Obligations become scheduled tasks.
Ch21.C4 — Extract
Ch21.C4.a — Research Queue
Research queue lists tasks requiring new mathematics or new template families.
new tail bounds or transform bounds.
new chart families for plateaued invariants.
new carriers for portal regions.
new probe adequacy designs.
Queue items are prioritized by deficiency and demand.
Ch21.C4.b — Compute Queue
Compute queue lists tasks requiring computational resources.
high κ verification jobs.
AAS candidate search with bounded caps.
SPP relation searches with bounded coefficient caps.
stress/regression suite runs.
Compute queue is governed by budgets and fairness.
Ch21.C4.c — Policy Queue
Policy queue lists governance changes needed.
policy bridge needs for importing evidence.
policy expansions to permit certain tunnels or probes.
stricter policies for safety in high-risk regions.
visibility mode decisions for exports.
Policy queue is governed and auditable.
Ch21.C4.d — Verification Queue
Verification queue lists tasks needed to upgrade candidates to certified or promoted.
UCW computations for missing edges/faces.
RQF closure tasks for candidates.
stress/regression tasks for high-risk promotions.
migration re-verification tasks after upgrades.
Verification queue maintains integrity.
D. Fractal
Ch21.D1 — Spec
Ch21.D1.a — Emergent Implication: Representation as First-Class Reality Model
The framework’s emergent implication is that representation change is not peripheral; it is the missing object class that makes mathematics and computation coherent at scale. By elevating lenses, transports, and witnesses, the framework turns “ways of describing” into typed, lawful objects. This yields a reality-model implication: complex systems are best understood as graphs of representations connected by lawful transports, where invariants are those objects that survive transport under verified constraints.
This implication is operational: “truth” becomes “closure under transport,” and “new constants” become “missing moduli required for closure.”
Ch21.D1.b — Discovery as Obstruction Extraction
Discovery is formalized as extracting stable obstructions from failed closure attempts. Instead of treating failure as inability, the framework treats failure as evidence revealing missing kernel parameters, missing bridges, or missing semantic alphabets. Tier-0 candidates and portal candidates arise from stable holonomy signatures and conflict proofs, turning discovery into a deterministic compiler stage rather than a subjective act.
Ch21.D1.c — Metro as Memory-Web
The metro graph is a memory-web: a hash-addressed, witness-bearing network that stores not only results but the legality and replay context of results. This converts knowledge into a navigable structure where extraction and reuse are safe and compositional. The metro is simultaneously:
a proof store,
a planner graph,
a scheduling substrate,
an audit log.
Ch21.D1.d — Roadmap Beyond Weight 12
Beyond weight 12, the roadmap continues by SPP deficiency schedules, AAS template generalizations, and portal expansions where required. The roadmap is not a list of constants; it is a job graph of obligations:
expand basis completeness to higher weights under dimension models,
add depth-lift candidates where forced,
discover accel charts for high-demand invariants,
expand probe adequacy and adversarial suites,
extend portal semantics for new geometric/topological regimes.
Ch21.D2 — Encode
Ch21.D2.a — Future Chapter Stubs
Future stubs are reserved modules for:
deeper portal semantics (genus transitions, monodromy classes),
higher-dimensional carriers and operator algebras,
extended bound libraries for advanced transforms,
extended ICIB policies for multi-domain collaboration.
Stubs are schema-indexed and preserve extensibility.
Ch21.D2.b — Future Appendix Stubs
Future appendices include:
expanded bound catalogs,
expanded probe and adversarial suite catalogs,
portal schema catalogs,
verified translator catalogs for schema upgrades.
Appendix stubs are designed for memory mapping and extraction.
Ch21.D2.c — Cross-Domain Imports
Cross-domain imports define how different invariant systems interoperate.
define mapping of lens families and hubs across domains,
define policy bridges for cross-domain evidence reuse,
define portal bridges for semantic extensions,
define quarantines and conflict ledgers.
Cross-domain imports extend the metro beyond a single domain safely.
Ch21.D2.d — Meta-Scheduler Extensions
Meta-scheduler extensions include:
multi-domain prioritization,
learning heuristics under governance,
failure-space mapping integration,
resource allocation strategies.
All extensions are deterministic, versioned, and audited.
Ch21.D3 — Prove
Ch21.D3.a — Safety of Scaling
Scaling is safe only if:
promotion remains verified-only,
determinism and replay invariance hold,
compaction preserves replay,
regression/stress suites maintain integrity,
quarantines isolate uncertain imports and candidates.
Safety of scaling is enforced by CI and governance.
Ch21.D3.b — Safety of Cross-Domain Expansion
Cross-domain expansion is safe only if:
compatibility is explicit and witnessed,
policy bridges exist for evidence reuse,
portal semantics are explicit where required,
conflicts are preserved and quarantined until resolved.
Cross-domain safety prevents semantic contamination.
Ch21.D3.c — Integrity Under Compaction
Integrity under compaction requires:
promoted receipts remain replay-verifiable,
minimal replay bundles remain reconstructible,
refusal evidence remains for blocked regions,
compaction receipts prove equivalence.
Compaction cannot erase evidence required for audit.
Ch21.D3.d — Integrity Under Migration
Integrity under migration requires:
schema upgrades via migration receipts,
verifier upgrades via verified translators,
backward witness preservation,
regression and stress suites pass after migration.
Migration is safe only when audit and replay remain possible.
Ch21.D4 — Extract
Ch21.D4.a — Next Milestones
Next milestones are deterministic tasks.
complete missing roots for key hubs,
promote key bridges and policy bridges,
expand AAS template families beyond current ladders,
extend probe adequacy and adversarial suites,
produce more Ω* closures for promoted nodes.
Milestones are prioritized by demand and dependency impact.
Ch21.D4.b — Next Forced Targets
Next forced targets come from SPP deficiency schedules.
weights where deficiency is positive,
depth-lift candidates forced by model,
relations to search and certify,
RQF closures to produce.
Targets are encoded as job graphs and are auditable.
Ch21.D4.c — Next Risk Mitigations
Risk mitigations are:
stress suites for high-risk charts/bridges,
regression expansion for stability,
drift detection improvements,
quarantine improvements and clearance playbooks.
Risk mitigations prevent future corruption.
Ch21.D4.d — Next Verification Upgrades
Verification upgrades are:
stronger probe regimes and adequacy proofs,
improved bound libraries and audits,
improved Ω* interior loop suite generation and verification,
improved policy bridge and compatibility receipts,
improved CI manifests and replay tooling.
Upgrades are versioned, governed, and replay-verifiable.
Ch21 — Worked Exemplars, Deep Implications, and Roadmap
Appendix A — Notation, Addressing, and Canonical Serialization
A.1 Spec
A.1.a Symbol Table
This section fixes the global symbol table for the treatise. Every symbol is typed by an object class and an admissibility scope. Symbols are descriptive; identities are hash-addressed objects.
A.1.a.1 Domains and Grading
( \mathsf{Dom} ): a domain tag identifying an invariant family (e.g., constant system, operator system, geometric system).
( w \in \mathbb{N} ): weight (domain-specific grading).
( d \in \mathbb{N} ): depth (domain-specific grading).
( \ell \in \mathbb{N} ): level (optional grading, domain-specific).
A.1.a.2 Carriers, Lenses, and Address Algebras
( D ): a carrier domain (typed object carrier), with admissible region (D_{\mathrm{adm}}\subseteq D).
( \mathrm{Ty}(D) ): carrier type interface (scalar, vector/tensor, function, measure/distribution, operator, algebraic structure, graph/complex).
( \mathcal{A} ): address algebra of a lens; includes address grammar, encode/decode, normalization, ordering, slice/join.
( L ): a lens, (L=(D,\mathcal{A},\mathcal{I})).
( \mathcal{I} ): invariant schema of a lens; defines equivalence (\approx_L), predicates (\mathcal{P}), observables (\mathcal{O}), and laws (\mathcal{L}).
A.1.a.3 Conventions, Corridors, Policies
( c ): a convention record (semantic regime parameters).
( \mathrm{ConvFP}(c) ): convention fingerprint, a hash commitment to the convention record.
( \mathrm{ConvBundleFP} ): a scope-indexed bundle hash of one or more ConvFP records covering all operator families used by a route.
( C ): a corridor record; contractual tolerance and refinement regime.
( \varepsilon_{\text{face}},\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\varepsilon_{\Pi} ): canonical corridor gates (commutation/holonomy/representability).
( \mathcal{K} = (\kappa_1 \prec \cdots \prec \kappa_m) ): κ ladder (refinement schedule).
( P ): policy record; admissible operation set, budgets, risk gates, disclosure boundaries.
A.1.a.4 Transports, Words, and Segments
( T ): a transport operator; typed morphism (T:L_i\to L_j), instantiated with parameters.
( W ): a route word; canonical route identity object (WordNF).
( \mathrm{WordNF} ): canonical normal form of a route; segment stream with bound parameters and context hashes.
( \mathsf{Seg} ): a segment in WordNF; typed record, including at minimum:
GateRun (evaluation/projection/commutation/holonomy gates),
RefineEvent (κ escalation/probe expansion),
SnapEvent (corridor tightening action, never widening),
TunnelEvent (representation switch, logged),
BridgeEvent (convention/policy bridge application),
SpliceRecord (route splice, explicitly justified).
A.1.a.5 Witnesses, Receipts, Refusals
( U ): a Unified Commutation Witness (UCW).
( \Omega^* ): the interior coherence certificate for Root-Quad closure.
( \mathsf{Rcpt} ): a receipt binding pins and witness roots under a verifier and context.
( \mathsf{Refuse} ): a refusal artifact (structured failure object).
( \mathsf{CEx} ): counterexample object (minimal failing predicate + minimal witness instance).
A.1.a.6 Probes and Projectors
( \mathcal{P}(\kappa) ): probe set at κ; deterministic or seed-pinned if permitted by policy.
( \mathrm{ProbeFP} ): hash commitment to probe generator and adequacy metadata.
( \Pi_h ): representability projector/canonicalizer; typed operator enforcing canonical meaning class stability.
( \rho_{\text{face}},\rho_{\text{spin}},\rho_{\Pi} ): residual functionals (typed, corridor-pinned).
A.1.a.7 Atlases and Closure Objects
( \mathrm{Atlas}(v) ): chart set for node (v), with root assignment ({\mathsf{S},\mathsf{F},\mathsf{C},\mathsf{R}}).
Edges: (SF,SC,SR,FC,FR,CR) between roots.
Faces: (SFC,SFR,SCR,FCR).
Interior: (SFCR).
( \mathrm{RQF} ): Root-Quad Forcing procedure; yields ( \Omega^* ) on success.
A.1.b Address Grammar
All objects are accessible through stable addresses. Addresses are distinct from hash identities: addresses are human-usable handles; identities are cryptographic commitments.
A.1.b.1 Global Address Form
The manuscript address space uses the crystal form:[\mathrm{ChXX}.\mathrm{Q}.\mathrm{F}.\mathrm{a}\quad\text{with}\quad\mathrm{Q}\in{A,B,C,D},; \mathrm{F}\in{1,2,3,4},; \mathrm{a}\in{a,b,c,d}.]Appendices use:[\mathrm{AppX}.\mathrm{Y}.\mathrm{y}\quad\text{where}\quad\mathrm{X}\in{A,\dots,P},; \mathrm{Y}\in{1,2,3,4},; \mathrm{y}\in{a,b,c,d}.]
A.1.b.2 Typed Object Address Space
Every stored object also has a typed object address. The typed prefix identifies the schema class. The canonical typed address forms are:
lens:<lens_id>
conv:<convfp> and convbundle:<convbundlefp>
corr:<corridor_id>
pol:<policy_id>
node:<node_id>
edge:<edge_id>
atlas:<atlas_id>
chart:<chart_id>
bound:<bound_id>
method:<method_id>
round:<rounding_id>
proj:<projector_id>
word:<word_hash>
ucw:<ucw_id>
omega:<omega_id>
rcpt:<receipt_id>
refuse:<refusal_id>
job:<job_id>
manifest:<bundle_id>
migrate:<migration_id>
Each suffix is the object’s hash identity (or a stable id field that is itself hash-addressed).
A.1.b.3 Segment Vocabulary
Addresses are composed of segments. Segment vocabulary is finite and versioned; new segments require schema migration. The canonical segment alphabet includes:
ch, app, sec, def, thm, alg, ex, note (manuscript anchors)
node, edge, atlas, chart, word, ucw, omega, rcpt, refuse
pin, wit, probe, kappa, gate, scope, policy, corridor, conv
link, dep, equiv, bridge, migrate, quarantine, deprecate
A.1.b.4 Parsing Rules
Addresses parse into a typed AST. Parsing is total on syntactically valid addresses and produces an explicit failure object otherwise.
Parsing obligations:
Type determinacy: the prefix determines the object class; ambiguous prefixes are forbidden.
Segment determinacy: each segment has a known grammar; unknown segments are refused.
Normalization determinacy: parsing returns a normalized address form.
A.1.b.5 Normalization Rules (NormAddr)
An address normalizer ( \mathrm{NormAddr} ) maps any syntactically valid address into a canonical normal form:
normalize Unicode under the canonical text normalization policy (A.2.a),
normalize separators (single : for typed prefix; single / for segment path),
normalize case rules (all prefixes and segment keys are lowercase ASCII; schema ids and hash ids remain case-preserving if specified),
remove redundant identity segments (e.g., node:<id>/node:<id> is forbidden; normalization refuses),
canonicalize relative anchors (./, ../) are forbidden in stable addresses; normalization refuses rather than rewriting.
NormAddr must be idempotent:[\mathrm{NormAddr}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha))=\mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha).]
A.1.b.6 Subobject Addressing
Subobjects are addressed by appending a slice path to a typed object address:
node:<id>/field:<fieldname>
ucw:<id>/curve:face/kappa:<k>
chart:<id>/param:<name>
word:<id>/seg:<n>/field:<fieldname>
Subobject addressing must satisfy:
Total slice on declared extractable fields: if a field is declared extractable in schema, slicing it is total.
Schema-typed slices: slice selectors are typed and validated; unknown fields are refused.
Join semantics (when declared): for reconstructible composite objects, join reconstructs an object from slices; otherwise join is undefined and refused.
A.1.b.7 Link-Edge Addressing
Typed links are addressable as first-class objects:
link:<link_type>@src:<src_id>#dst:<dst_id>where link_type ∈ {REF,DEP,EQUIV,BRIDGE,MIGRATE,QUARANTINE,DEPRECATE}.
Link addresses commit to:
link type,
endpoint ids,
scope and context hashes (when semantic),
witness pointers (when semantic).
Links never imply equivalence unless link_type is EQUIV and a receipt binds the required witnesses.
A.1.c Naming Rules
Names exist for human use; identities exist for verification. Naming is strict and non-authoritative.
A.1.c.1 Name vs Identity
Name: a mutable label, may collide, may have synonyms.
Identity: immutable hash id derived from canonical encoding.
A name must never be used as a proof key. All semantic references in receipts and witnesses are by identity.
A.1.c.2 Disambiguation Keys
When a name collides or is ambiguous, disambiguation keys are appended in a deterministic order:
domain tag,
carrier type,
weight/depth (if present),
ConvBundleFP prefix (first (k) hex chars, with fixed (k)),
policy/corridor context class (optional),
identity prefix (first (k') hex chars, with fixed (k')).
Disambiguation keys are indexing metadata; they do not change semantic identity.
A.1.c.3 Canonical Suffix Policy
A canonical suffix policy defines how identities are rendered in names:
use a fixed-length hex prefix for display,
never truncate to fewer than the policy minimum,
never include ambiguous characters or locale-specific formatting,
never include non-semantic timestamps.
A.1.c.4 Alias Classes
Alias classes are explicit index objects:
alias node: alias:<alias_id> with members node:<id> or chart:<id>,
alias membership is non-semantic,
preferences are context-indexed pointers and are not semantic evidence.
Alias classes improve navigation and extraction without collapsing meaning.
A.1.c.5 Scope-Indexed Naming
Names may be scope-indexed for clarity, but scope indexing is metadata. If the same name is used under different scopes, those are distinct alias entries, not distinct identities.
A.1.c.6 Forbidden Overloads
The following are forbidden:
using the same name to refer to two distinct semantic objects without disambiguation keys,
treating a name match as equivalence,
using naming as a fallback for missing receipts or missing witnesses,
embedding mutable data (timestamps, environment paths) into canonical naming.
A.1.d Reserved Words
Reserved words are prohibited in user-defined identifiers, except where explicitly allowed by schema.
A.1.d.1 Schema Keywords
Reserved: schema, version, id, hash, pins_root, wits_root, scope, policy, corridor, conv, verifier, canon, migrate, deprecated, quarantined, refuse, promoted, certified, candidate, active, inactive.
A.1.d.2 Operator-Family Identifiers
Reserved: enc, dec, nf, proj, eval, trans, reduce, collapse, snap, tunnel, bridge, migrate, import, export, sync.
A.1.d.3 Gate Identifiers
Reserved: face, spin, pi, aux, stress, regression, budget, scope_check, policy_check, corridor_check.
A.1.d.4 Refusal Classes
Reserved refusal tokens: CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL, BINDING_MISMATCH, TYPE_FAIL, SCOPE_GAP, CONV_CONFLICT, POLICY_BLOCK, CORRIDOR_FAIL, PROBE_INADEQUATE, PLATEAU, DRIFT, HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION, KERNEL_MISMATCH, BUDGET_EXHAUSTED, ILLEGAL_WIDENING_ATTEMPT, ORDERING_MISMATCH.
A.1.d.5 Lifecycle States
Reserved: draft, certified, promoted, active, inactive, deprecated, quarantined, refused, tombstoned.
A.1.d.6 Visibility Modes
Reserved: public, restricted, private, boundary_limited, quarantined_export.
A.1.d.7 Migration Verbs
Reserved: upgrade, downgrade, translate, rewrite, rollback, deprecate, tombstone, invalidate, reverify.
A.2 Encode
A.2.a Canonical JSON
Canonical JSON is the authoritative encoding for all hash-addressed records unless a schema explicitly declares an alternative canonical encoding. Canonical JSON is schema-aware.
A.2.a.1 Canonical Map Encoding
keys are UTF-8 strings normalized under the canonical Unicode normalization policy (NFC),
keys are sorted lexicographically by Unicode codepoint order after normalization,
duplicate keys are forbidden; canonicalization refuses,
map iteration order in runtime is irrelevant; only canonical order is used.
A.2.a.2 Canonical List Encoding
Lists are encoded in the order provided by schema semantics. Lists are not implicitly sorted. Any list that is semantically a set must be represented as:
either a map from element id to multiplicity,
or a list with an explicit pinned ordering rule and evidence that ordering is stable.
Canonicalization refuses a “set as list” without an ordering declaration.
A.2.a.3 Canonical Numeric Encoding
All numeric fields are encoded as strings in one of the following canonical forms, as declared by schema:
Integer: optional leading - followed by digits, no leading zeros except 0.
Rational: p/q with gcd(p,q)=1, q>0, p integer string.
Decimal: [-]d.ddd... with:
at least one digit before the decimal point,
no trailing zeros after decimal point unless schema requires fixed scale,
-0 forbidden; canonical is 0,
scientific notation permitted only if schema explicitly allows; if allowed, exponent is e±k with integer k and mantissa in normalized form.
Interval endpoints: decimal strings with explicit precision policy pinned by rounding id and corridor. Endpoints must be representable under the declared numeric backend; otherwise canonicalization refuses.
All floating runtime values must be converted to canonical numeric strings via the schema’s numeric encoder; raw float serialization is forbidden.
A.2.a.4 Canonical Boolean and Null
Booleans and null are encoded as JSON true, false, null. Optional field omission rules are schema-defined; if a field is optional and omitted, it must be omitted under deterministic rule (not runtime convenience).
A.2.a.5 Unicode Normalization and Character Policy
normalize all text fields under NFC,
restrict identifier fields (schema keys, operator ids, refusal class ids) to lowercase ASCII plus _ and digits, unless schema explicitly allows additional characters,
disallow control characters and bidirectional override characters in identifiers; canonicalization refuses.
A.2.a.6 Schema Id Inclusion
Every canonical JSON record includes:
type_tag (record type),
schema_id (version hash or semantic version plus hash),
any required schema_hashes for referenced sub-schemas.
Omitting schema identifiers is forbidden for promotable artifacts.
A.2.b Hashing Rules
Hashing is the identity mechanism. Hashing is domain-separated and canonical-encoding based.
A.2.b.1 Domain Separation Tags
Define a domain separation tag string for each schema type:
LENS, CONV, CORR, POL, WORD, UCW, RCPT, REFUSE, CHART, BOUND, BRIDGESEED, MAGICPACK, JOB, MANIFEST, MIGRATE, OMEGA, etc.
A.2.b.2 Object Identity Hash
Object id is computed as:[\mathrm{id}(O) := \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{tag}(O);|,0x00;|,\mathrm{schema_id};|,0x00;|,\mathrm{canon}(O)),]where (\mathrm{canon}(O)) is canonical JSON bytes and H is a pinned collision-resistant hash.
A.2.b.3 Merkle Roots (Pins/Wits)
Given an ordered list of leaf ids ([h_1,\dots,h_n]) (already deterministically ordered), define the merkle root:
leaf hash: (L_i := \mathrm{H}(\texttt{"LEAF"}|0x00|h_i)),
internal hash: (N := \mathrm{H}(\texttt{"NODE"}|0x00|L_{\mathrm{left}}|0x00|L_{\mathrm{right}})),
if odd leaf count at a level, duplicate last leaf deterministically.
The merkle root is:[\mathrm{MerkleRoot}(h_1,\dots,h_n) := \text{root node hash}.]
The ordering of ([h_i]) is part of the commitment; therefore ordering rules must be pinned (A.2.c).
A.2.b.4 Chain Hashes for Segment Streams
For a WordNF segment stream (\mathsf{Seg}_1,\dots,\mathsf{Seg}_m), define:
segment hash: (s_i := \mathrm{id}(\mathsf{Seg}_i)),
chain hash: (c_0 := \mathrm{H}(\texttt{"CHAIN0"})),[c_i := \mathrm{H}(\texttt{"CHAIN"}|0x00|c_{i-1}|0x00|s_i).]The final chain hash (c_m) is committed in WordNF. Any reordering changes chain hash and is detected.
A.2.b.5 Bundle Manifests
A bundle manifest is a canonical list of object ids plus required indices for merkle expansion, plus schema/verifier catalogs and environment snapshots when needed. Bundle hash is computed by the object identity hash of the manifest record.
A.2.b.6 Hash Upgrade Rules
Hash algorithm upgrades are permitted only through migration:
keep old ids immutable,
compute new ids using new hash algorithm under new schema,
create MIGRATE artifacts mapping old→new,
preserve backward verification by storing both hashes or providing verified translators,
CI verifies that migrated artifacts replay equivalently.
A.2.c Ordering Rules
Ordering rules eliminate nondeterminism. Any object containing an order-sensitive collection must declare its ordering rule explicitly.
A.2.c.1 Address Preorder
Define a total preorder (\preceq_{\mathrm{addr}}) on normalized addresses:
compare typed prefix lexicographically,
compare primary id lexicographically (hex string),
compare segment paths lexicographically by segment key then segment value,
ties broken deterministically by full canonical bytes of the addressed object if needed (only when available; otherwise ties are refused).
A.2.c.2 Basis Order
