Basis sets must be ordered by a pinned rule:
primary: weight, depth, level (ascending),
secondary: canonical name disambiguation keys,
tertiary: identity hash lexicographic,
optional domain-specific ordering keys permitted only if declared and stable under refinement.
If basis ordering depends on numeric approximants, it is forbidden unless proven refinement-stable and pinned by ConvFP and rounding id.
A.2.c.3 Probe Order
Probe sets are ordered by:
probe generator id,
κ level,
probe address or canonical probe encoding hash,
tie-breaking by identity.
Probe ordering must not depend on runtime enumeration; it is constructed deterministically.
A.2.c.4 Tie-Breakers
Tie-breaking rules are pinned and deterministic:
if semantic order exists, use it;
otherwise use lexicographic order on identity hash;
if identity hashes collide (theoretical), invoke collision handling procedure (A.3.b).
A.2.c.5 Deterministic Sorting Constraints
Any use of sorting must:
use a pinned comparator id,
use pinned key extraction rule,
apply pinned tie-breakers,
be audited by determinism tests (A.3.d).
A.2.c.6 Refinement-Stable Ordering
Ordering used in promotable artifacts must be stable under κ refinement. Stability means:
ordering does not change as κ increases, or
ordering changes are treated as convention changes requiring new ConvFP and migration, never silent changes.
A.2.d Environment Pins
Environment pins capture any execution dependence that could alter canonical bytes or verifier outcomes.
A.2.d.1 Backend IDs
Pins include:
numeric backend identifier and version,
special function library identifier and version (when relevant),
interval arithmetic kernel id and version.
A.2.d.2 Library IDs
Pins include versions for:
canonical encoding library,
hashing library,
PSLQ/integer relation library (search-only unless verified),
transform libraries used in certified evaluation.
A.2.d.3 Toolchain IDs
Pins include:
verifier implementation version hash,
schema registry version hash,
CI runner version id (if it influences determinism),
compaction/migration tooling ids.
A.2.d.4 Seed Pins
Randomness is forbidden for promotable verification. If policy allows randomness for candidate search, seed pins must be included and bound into the candidate log identity; they must not influence promoted evidence.
A.2.d.5 Locale/Time Isolation
Locale and time must not influence canonical encodings or verifier decisions. Any dependency on timezone, locale collation, or timestamp in semantic fields is forbidden and triggers determinism audit failure.
A.2.d.6 Determinism Boundary Conditions
Promotion-critical outcomes require:
pinned canon_hash,
pinned verifier_hash and schema hashes,
pinned backend/toolchain ids where necessary,
deterministic ordering rules,
budgeted termination.
If these conditions are not met, the artifact is boundary-limited or refused.
A.3 Prove
A.3.a Idempotence Checks
Idempotence checks are mandatory verification obligations. They ensure that canonicalization and normalization are stable operations.
A.3.a.1 Canonical Encoding Idempotence
For every schema type (T), require:[\mathrm{canon}(\mathrm{decode}_T(\mathrm{canon}(x))) = \mathrm{canon}(x)]for all valid instances (x) of schema (T). Canonical encoding idempotence is checked in CI and is a prerequisite for stable hashing.
A.3.a.2 NormAddr Idempotence
For every syntactically valid address (\alpha), require:[\mathrm{NormAddr}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha)) = \mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha).]Additionally, for any addressable object (x), require denotation preservation:[\mathrm{DecAddr}(\alpha) \approx \mathrm{DecAddr}(\mathrm{NormAddr}(\alpha)),]where (\approx) is the relevant equivalence for the addressed object class.
A.3.a.3 WordNF Normalization Idempotence
Let (\mathrm{NormWord}) be the WordNF normalization function. Require:[\mathrm{NormWord}(\mathrm{NormWord}(W)) = \mathrm{NormWord}(W),]and require that normalization preserves segment semantics and does not erase corridor-changing events (SnapEvent, TunnelEvent, BridgeEvent).
A.3.a.4 Projector Idempotence Obligations
For each projector (\Pi_h), require:[\Pi_h(\Pi_h(x)) \approx \Pi_h(x)]under the projector’s declared equivalence regime. When approximate, projection residual must satisfy corridor (\varepsilon_{\Pi}) gates. Projector idempotence is verified by dedicated projection UCWs or by direct enclosure-based projection audits where available.
A.3.b Collision Handling
Hash collisions are treated as catastrophic integrity events. The system must not attempt semantic resolution of collisions.
A.3.b.1 Collision Policy
Detect: collision is detected when two distinct canonical byte strings yield the same hash id under the pinned hash algorithm.
Quarantine: immediately quarantine both artifacts and all dependent artifacts.
Escalate: emit a collision incident artifact requiring hash algorithm upgrade and migration.
Forbid reuse: no promoted artifact may depend on collision-tainted ids.
A.3.b.2 Type-Tag Separation
Domain separation tags prevent cross-type collisions. If a collision occurs across distinct type tags, it indicates a broken hash function or broken domain separation; treat as immediate system-level quarantine.
A.3.b.3 Conflict Resolution Without Semantic Collapse
The system never merges colliding objects. Resolution consists only of:
migrating to a stronger hash algorithm,
rehashing canonical bytes under new algorithm,
producing migration receipts mapping old ids to new ids,
rerunning CI replay verification for all promoted artifacts.
A.3.b.4 Quarantine-on-Collision Rule
Collision automatically triggers:
quarantine of affected nodes/edges/charts,
invalidation of caches and indices,
rollback of activation pointers relying on collision-tainted artifacts,
refusal artifacts for any attempt to use collision-tainted ids.
A.3.c Scope Correctness
Scope correctness ensures that every operator invoked by any proof or computation is covered by explicit semantic and governance regimes.
A.3.c.1 ConvFP Scope Coverage Obligations
For any WordNF (W) and associated ConvBundleFP (B), require:
every operator-family id invoked in (W) is included in (B)’s scope,
every convention-dependent operator has a ConvFP record covering it,
scope intersection conflicts require explicit convention bridges, otherwise refuse.
A.3.c.2 Corridor Scope Coverage Obligations
For any witness or receipt requiring corridor gates, require:
the corridor record includes required gate ids and residual functional ids,
the κ ladder is defined for the carrier,
probe regime is declared and admissible,
required auxiliary suites (if any) are declared and admissible.
A.3.c.3 Policy Scope Coverage Obligations
For any operation, require:
method ids used are allowed,
budgets are within limits,
tunnel/bridge families are allowed where used,
visibility mode constraints are satisfied.
A.3.c.4 Scope-Gap Refusal Rules
If scope coverage fails, the system must emit a refusal with:
missing scope identifiers (operator families),
the minimal segment(s) invoking missing scope,
required ConvFP additions or bridges,
policy/corridor implications of extending scope.
No attempt is made to guess semantics for uncovered scope.
A.3.d Determinism Audits
Determinism audits are mandatory for promotion-critical systems and are enforced by CI.
A.3.d.1 Stable Serialization Tests
encode→decode→encode idempotence for each schema type,
canonical numeric encoding stability across environments,
canonical omission rule stability.
A.3.d.2 Stable Ordering Tests
address ordering stability,
basis ordering stability under κ refinement and under projection,
probe ordering stability,
tie-breaker determinism.
A.3.d.3 Replay Invariance Tests
Given a canonical proof bundle:
expand pins/witness roots deterministically,
recompute UCWs and evaluation receipts under pinned environments,
verify that recomputed outputs match stored canonical evidence,
verify that acceptance decisions match.
Replay mismatches are classified as drift vs corruption vs environment mismatch; promotion is blocked on any mismatch.
A.3.d.4 Environment Pin Sufficiency Tests
run replay tests across approved environment variants; if outcomes differ, require additional environment pins or forbid promotion in that regime,
verify that candidate search randomness never influences promotable evidence,
verify that CI runner differences do not influence canonical bytes.
Environment pin sufficiency tests ensure the determinism boundary is respected.
A.4 Extract
A.4.a Quick Lookup Tables
Extraction produces deterministic lookup tables for rapid navigation and audit.
Symbol index: mapping from symbol to section address and schema types.
Operator id index: operator family ids, signatures, scope requirements, version ids.
Gate stack index: gate stack ids, predicate ordering, residual functional ids, required κ ladders.
Refusal class index: refusal class tokens, required evidence fields, remediation templates.
Lifecycle state index: allowed transitions and governance requirements.
Lookup tables are schema objects and are hash-addressed.
A.4.b Schema Index
Schema index is a canonical registry:
schema id → decoder availability,
schema id → migration paths,
schema id → deprecation status,
schema id → required verifier versions,
schema id → compatibility predicates.
Schema index is required for replay and archival verification.
A.4.c Version Index
Version index is the authoritative map of versioned components:
verifier versions and supported schema sets,
canon_hash versions and canonical encoding policies,
operator catalog versions and gate stack versions,
scheduler policy versions and risk policy versions,
toolchain versions (CI, compaction, migration).
Version index is used in CI manifests and migration playbooks.
A.4.d Audit Recipes
Audit recipes are deterministic procedures for verification and forensic reconstruction.
A.4.d.1 Hash-Check Recipe
retrieve canonical bytes for object id,
recompute (\mathrm{H}) with domain separation tag and schema id,
confirm equality to stored id,
on mismatch: emit corruption evidence and quarantine.
A.4.d.2 Merkle Expansion Recipe
retrieve merkle root and expansion index object,
expand to ordered list of leaf ids,
recompute merkle root using pinned merkle algorithm,
confirm equality,
on mismatch: emit index corruption evidence and quarantine.
A.4.d.3 Segment-Chain Audit Recipe
retrieve WordNF and segment list,
compute segment ids and chain hash (c_m),
confirm equality to committed chain hash in WordNF,
confirm that segment ordering is canonical and scope-safe,
on mismatch: classify as tamper or normalization error and quarantine.
A.4.d.4 Cross-Context Mismatch Audit Recipe
extract context hashes from receipt (ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, verifier, schema hashes),
verify that all referenced witnesses carry identical context hashes,
verify that any differences are bridged by explicit bridge receipts,
if mismatch unbridged: emit policy laundering or convention laundering refusal, quarantine dependent artifacts.
Appendix A — Notation, Addressing, and Canonical Serialization
Appendix B — Certificate Schemas (UCW, Receipts, Refusals)
B.1 Spec
B.1.a UCW Schema
A Unified Commutation Witness (UCW) is a certificate object that binds a pair of routes and asserts, under a pinned context, that commutation, holonomy, and representability obligations are satisfied (or that they fail with structured refusal evidence). UCW is the atomic witness class for semantic equivalence and transport correctness.
B.1.a.1 UCW Object Class and Scope
A UCW is a schema-typed record (U) whose validity is defined only relative to a context:[X := (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},;P,;C,;V,;\Sigma),]where (P) is policy hash, (C) corridor hash, (V) verifier hash, and (\Sigma) is the schema-hash set required to interpret all ids referenced by UCW. UCW scope is the operator-family scope of its routes and the carrier scope of its probes and residual functionals.
A UCW certifies bounded equivalence, not global equality. The certification is indexed by the corridor gates and κ ladder.
B.1.a.2 UCW Bindings
UCW binds the following identities, each by hash commitment:
Route identity binding.
word_hash: the canonical WordNF hash of the route pairing object (or a composite record including both paths).
optionally pathA_word_hash and pathB_word_hash when the pairing is represented as two WordNFs.
Context identity binding.
conv_bundle_hash,
policy_hash,
corridor_hash,
verifier_hash,
canon_hash,
schema_hashes sufficient to interpret residual functionals, probes, projectors, loop maps.
Operator scope binding.
explicit operator-family scope list or scope id; must be covered by ConvBundleFP.
Carrier binding.
carrier type id and admissibility region id for the probe carrier domain.
Any mismatch between UCW bindings and the referenced objects invalidates the UCW for promotion and must yield refusal upon replay.
B.1.a.3 κ-Grid and Probe Regime
UCW contains:
κ-grid. An ordered list of κ values matching corridor’s κ ladder (or a declared equivalent κ ladder id). UCW evidence is κ-indexed; a UCW that lacks required κ points is incomplete and non-promotable.
Probe regime. A probe set commitment:
probe_hash (ProbeFP),
adequacy metadata id,
optional probe cardinalities per κ,
determinism pins (seed pins only if permitted and bound).
Probe adequacy is a witness obligation. A UCW with inadequate probes is classified as uncertainty and is non-promotable regardless of small residuals.
B.1.a.4 Residual Curves
UCW must store κ-indexed residual maxima curves:
Face residual curve (r_{\square}(\kappa)): commutation defect.
Spin residual curve (s_{\square}(\kappa)): loop non-closure defect, computed via loop map (L_{\square}).
Projection residual curve (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)): representability defect under (\Pi_h).
Each curve is a list of canonical numeric strings aligned to the κ-grid.
Additionally, UCW binds the residual functional ids:
face_residual_id,
spin_residual_id,
proj_residual_id,and gate thresholds:
eps_face,
eps_spin,
eps_pi,all of which must match corridor interpretation.
Optional auxiliary curves may be included only if they are declared by corridor/policy and are bound by schema ids.
B.1.a.5 Class Taxonomy
UCW class is an enumerated tag, determined by verifier from evidence:
ALIAS (tri-gate satisfied),
HOLONOMY (spin fails stably; face controlled),
KERNEL (face fails; holonomy not dominant),
UNCERTAINTY (plateau or inadequate probes),
MIXED (multiple failures without dominance),
POLICY_BLOCK,
CONVENTION_CONFLICT.
Class is non-authoritative unless produced by verifier; stored class must match verifier recomputation outcome or else indicates drift/corruption.
B.1.a.6 Refusal Payloads
If UCW is not promotable, it must include a refusal payload or a pointer to a refusal object containing:
minimal failing predicate id(s),
minimal κ at failure and margin to gate,
minimal counterexample probes (ProbeFP subaddresses),
minimal failing subword or segment subset if localization is available,
admissible next actions consistent with policy and corridor,
budget exhaustion evidence when applicable.
Refusal payload must be replay-verifiable and deterministic.
B.1.b Receipt Schema
A receipt is a proof-carrying wrapper that binds pins and witnesses to a plan under a verifier and a context. Receipts enforce local verifiability and safe reuse.
B.1.b.1 Receipt Object Class and Plan Binding
A receipt (\mathsf{Rcpt}) binds:
Plan binding.
plan_hash: commitment to a PlanSpec describing the claim type and acceptance predicate,
plan_type: one of eval, equiv, promote, bridge, rqf, import, migrate, compaction, refuse.
Targets.
list of target ids (node id, edge id, chart id, UCW id, Ω* id).
Acceptance predicate binding.
gate stack id,
corridor id,
any auxiliary suite ids required.
Receipt validity is defined relative to the plan; receipts without plan binding are invalid.
B.1.b.2 Pins Root and Witness Root
A receipt commits to two merkle roots:
Pins root (pins_root): all objects required to interpret the claim (lenses, policies, corridors, ConvFP records, schema/verifier catalogs, method/bound/rounding/projection ids, environment/toolchain pins where required).
Witness root (wits_root): all witness objects required to verify the claim (UCWs, evaluation receipts, Ω* certificates, stress/regression packs, bridge packs, refusal packs where applicable).
Receipts must provide expansion indices or explicit lists under visibility constraints. If expansion is impossible, receipt is boundary-limited and cannot be used for external verification.
B.1.b.3 Context Hashes
Receipt binds:
conv_bundle_hash,
policy_hash,
corridor_hash,
verifier_hash,
canon_hash,
schema_hashes.
Receipt reuse is allowed only when context hashes match or are bridged by explicit bridge receipts. Context mismatch without bridge is illegal.
B.1.b.4 Visibility Modes and Export Constraints
Receipt carries a visibility mode:
public, restricted, private, boundary_limited, quarantined_export.
Export constraints define:
which pins can be exported,
which witnesses can be exported,
whether external verification is possible.
If a receipt is restricted/private, it may still be locally verifiable; however, it must declare that external verification is not possible without access to withheld pins/witnesses.
B.1.b.5 Replay Sufficiency
Replay sufficiency is an invariant: receipt must contain enough information to replay verification under the pinned environment and toolchain.
Receipt includes:
references to expansion indices for merkle roots,
pointers to WordNF objects used,
environment/toolchain pins when required.
Failure to satisfy replay sufficiency invalidates the receipt for promotion-critical use.
B.1.c Refusal Schema
Refusals are first-class proof objects certifying the inability to verify or promote a claim within constraints. Refusals preserve evidence and provide admissible remediation.
B.1.c.1 Refusal Classes
Refusals are classified by enumerated refusal class tokens, at minimum:
CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL,
BINDING_MISMATCH,
TYPE_FAIL,
SCOPE_GAP,
CONV_CONFLICT,
POLICY_BLOCK,
CORRIDOR_FAIL,
PROBE_INADEQUATE,
PLATEAU,
DRIFT,
HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION,
KERNEL_MISMATCH,
BUDGET_EXHAUSTED,
ILLEGAL_WIDENING_ATTEMPT,
ORDERING_MISMATCH.
Refusal class determines governance actions and allowed next steps. Refusal classes are deterministic outputs of verifier.
B.1.c.2 Minimal Failing Predicate
Every refusal must record:
the minimal failing predicate id,
the predicate stack ordering,
evidence for minimality (first failing hard predicate, or dominant failing gate).
Minimal failing predicate enables localized remediation and prevents diffuse failure narratives.
B.1.c.3 Counterexample Objects
Refusals must include or reference counterexample objects (\mathsf{CEx}) containing:
minimal κ level where failure occurs,
minimal probe instance(s) responsible,
minimal subword or segment subset if applicable,
evidence pointers (residual values, enclosure diffs),
admissible next actions.
Counterexamples are replay-verifiable and are essential for audit and debugging.
B.1.c.4 Budgets Consumed
Refusal must include budget consumption:
κ steps attempted,
probe expansions attempted,
method swaps attempted,
tunnel candidates tried,
wall-time/memory caps consumed where tracked.
Budget evidence is necessary for refusal correctness: refusal is valid only when admissible actions were exhausted under budgets or were blocked by policy.
B.1.c.5 Admissible Next Actions
Refusal must list admissible next actions, each annotated with:
required policy/corridor/convention changes (if any),
required evidence to attempt,
expected job type (Snap, tunnel, AAS, RQF, T0-OBSTR, portal),
required stress/regression suites if promotion is intended.
Next actions must be finite and deterministic; forbidden actions are excluded.
B.1.c.6 Quarantine Semantics
Refusal may attach quarantine semantics:
whether the target is quarantined from certified routing,
whether dependent artifacts are quarantined,
clearance requirements (bridges, upgrades, new probes),
rollback actions (deactivate charts/bridges).
Quarantine is enforced by governance and routing; refusals define quarantine conditions.
B.1.d Bridge Pack Schema
Bridge packs package reusable repair/acceleration tissue. A bridge pack consists of MagicCertPack(s) and a BridgeSeed.
B.1.d.1 MagicCertPack
MagicCertPack binds:
pre/post context hashes (corridor pre/post; policy and ConvBundleFP fixed),
pre/post defect measures with defect functional id,
WordNF segment stream including tunnel and Snap events,
required witness hooks and fulfilled witness pointers,
replay fields (enumerator state, budgets, environment pins),
guards (stability region guards, drift checks performed).
MagicCertPack is promotable only if replay-verifiable and defect reduction is strict under fixed gates.
B.1.d.2 BridgeSeed
BridgeSeed binds:
bridge kind (CONV_BRIDGE, POLICY_BRIDGE, CORRIDOR_BRIDGE, CHART_BRIDGE, LENS_BRIDGE, PORTAL_BRIDGE, META_BRIDGE),
endpoints and scope constraints,
WordNF template for application (segment skeleton with parameter slots),
witness roots required for application correctness,
refusal semantics and guard predicates,
lifecycle state and context coverage map.
BridgeSeed is promoted only after stress/regression gating when risk requires and after demonstrating stable replay.
B.1.d.3 Defect Decrease Certificate
Bridge packs must include a defect decrease certificate demonstrating:[\mathfrak D_{\mathrm{post}} < \mathfrak D_{\mathrm{pre}}]under pinned acceptance gates and without silent widening. This ensures the bridge is functionally meaningful as a repair or acceleration.
B.1.d.4 Pre/Post Corridor Hashes
Bridge packs must record corr_pre_hash and corr_post_hash. Corridor widening is forbidden; corridor tightening must be explicit and logged. Any corridor change must be consistent with policy binding and must be replay-verifiable.
B.1.d.5 Witness Hooks, Replay Bindings, Guards
Bridge pack schema must include:
witness hooks required (UCWs, loop closure, projection stability),
replay bindings (budgets, enumerator, environment pins),
guards (scope guards, representability guards, drift detection guards),
clearance requirements for activation.
B.2 Encode
B.2.a Field Types
All certificate schema fields must use canonical field types.
Scalar strings: ASCII identifiers for enumerations; normalized Unicode strings for descriptive fields.
Typed ids: hex strings representing hash identities, prefixed by schema type when displayed; internal encodings use raw hex ids plus type tag via schema.
Lists: ordered sequences whose order is semantic or schema-pinned; sets must be encoded as ordered lists with pinned ordering or as maps.
Maps: canonical key-ordered maps under canonical JSON rules.
Optional fields: explicit omission rules pinned by schema version. Optional fields cannot change semantics; if a field affects semantics, it is required.
Canonical numeric encodings: integers, rationals, decimals, interval endpoints as strings using Appendix A numeric rules.
Scope fields: explicit operator-family scope lists or scope ids; never inferred.
Field types are enforced by schema validators; invalid types yield refusal.
B.2.b Required Hashes
All promotion-critical certificate objects must include required hashes:
word_hash (or word_pre_hash/word_post_hash where applicable),
conv_bundle_hash,
corridor_hash (and pre/post when applicable),
policy_hash,
verifier_hash,
schema_hashes required to interpret the object,
pins_root and wits_root for receipts and packs where applicable.
Any omission yields non-promotable status and must be refused in certified routing.
B.2.c Optional Fields
Optional fields may be included only if they do not affect semantics and are excluded from identity or are included under explicit schema rules.
Permitted optional fields:
cached residual curves duplicated from recomputable sources (non-authoritative),
performance metadata (cost models, latencies),
trace references (pointers to logs stored elsewhere by hash),
provenance references (import source ids),
disclosure boundaries and redaction markers (visibility-mode metadata).
Optional fields are never used for acceptance; they are used for audit, dashboards, and scheduling.
B.2.d Compression Rules
Certificates must support store-in-hashes compaction while preserving replay.
Store-in-hashes strategy. Large objects are referenced by hash; receipts store roots and indices.
Curve encoding. Residual curves may be stored compactly as canonical numeric strings; recomputation remains authoritative.
Witness pack bundling. Witness objects are packaged into bundles with manifests; manifests are hash-addressed and include merkle expansion indices.
Minimal replay bundle construction. For any promotable certificate, a minimal replay bundle exists consisting of:
the certificate object,
expanded pins and witnesses (or indices),
schema/verifier catalogs,
environment/toolchain snapshots where required.
Compression is validated by compaction receipts proving replay equivalence.
B.3 Prove
B.3.a Verifier Checklist
Verifier checklist is the canonical procedure applied to certificate objects.
Resolve: load all referenced records by hash; fail with CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL if missing.
Validate bindings: compare stored hashes to resolved objects; fail with BINDING_MISMATCH on mismatch.
Validate scope: check ConvBundleFP scope covers invoked operators; fail with SCOPE_GAP if uncovered.
Validate policy/corridor admissibility: check allowed methods, budgets, κ ladder; fail with POLICY_BLOCK or CORRIDOR_FAIL as appropriate.
Recompute evidence: recompute residual curves and enclosures; do not trust cached values.
Apply gates: evaluate tri-gate and auxiliary gates; determine promotability.
Classify: assign UCW class or refusal class deterministically.
Emit decision: output accept/promotable or refuse/quarantine and required next actions.
Log deterministically: produce a verifier log artifact with minimal failing predicate and counterexamples when failing.
B.3.b Promotion Checklist
Promotion checklist ensures safety before promoting any artifact.
tri-gate pass for all relevant UCWs,
probe adequacy pass (adequacy pack present and valid),
stress/regression pass if required by risk class,
no drift alarms in scope; determinism audits passed,
no policy laundering and no corridor laundering; context hashes match or are bridged by explicit bridge receipts,
replay success: minimal replay bundle reconstructible and replay-verifiable.
Failing any item yields non-promotion with refusal or quarantine, not partial promotion.
B.3.c Refusal Checklist
Refusal correctness checklist ensures refusals are honest and actionable.
classification correctness derived from minimal failing predicate and evidence,
counterexample minimality: smallest κ/probe/subword sufficient to witness failure,
evidence completeness: residual curves, budgets, scope/corridor/policy context,
budget exhaustion proof when refusal is exhaustion-based,
admissible next actions list is finite, deterministic, and policy-respecting,
quarantine semantics declared where necessary.
Refusals are replay-verifiable; verifier can confirm refusal outcome.
B.3.d Replay Checklist
Replay checklist ensures that a certificate can be independently replayed.
expand merkle roots to explicit id lists using indices; verify roots,
decode schemas and confirm schema compatibility; migrate if permitted and witnessed,
validate environment pins and toolchain pins if required,
deterministically recompute evaluations and UCWs,
confirm outcome matches stored certificate decision,
produce integrity proofs (hash checks, chain checks) and CI manifest compatibility.
Replay failures yield quarantine and remediation requirements.
B.4 Extract
B.4.a Minimal Witness Sets
Minimal witness sets are computed deterministically by target class.
Edge equivalence (transport).
WordNF,
UCW for the transport,
evaluation receipts for any charts used in UCW,
probe adequacy pack,
projection signature records.
Face closure.
two path WordNFs,
face UCW,
supporting edge UCWs if required,
adequacy pack.
Loop closure.
loop WordNF,
loop UCW(s) with spin residual curves,
loop map signature records,
boundary probes if required.
Ω* interior.
atlas spec,
root receipts,
six edge UCWs,
four face UCWs,
interior loop suite WordNFs,
Ω* certificate.
Bridge minimal set.
BridgeSeed,
MagicCertPack(s),
required UCWs,
stress/regression packs if required.
Regression/stress minimal sets.
suite definition,
required probes,
result packs,
counterexample objects on failure.
Minimality is defined by replay sufficiency: removing any required item breaks verification.
B.4.b Minimal Receipt Sets
Minimal receipt packs by target class:
eval: evaluation receipt + pins for method/bound/rounding/projector + corridor/policy/ConvBundleFP + verifier catalog.
equiv: UCW + WordNF(s) + pins + evaluation receipts required by UCW + adequacy pack.
promote: RQF evidence: root receipts + edge/face UCWs + Ω* + stress/regression packs if required + governance receipts.
bridge: BridgeSeed + MagicCertPack + UCWs + stress/regression packs as required + policy bridge receipts if cross-policy.
import: import manifest + compat receipts + quarantine logs + local verification receipts for clearance + policy bridge receipts.
B.4.c Audit Bundles
Audit bundles are deterministic packaging formats:
Promotion packet: target receipt + expanded pins/wits + replay scripts + integrity proofs.
Refusal packet: refusal object + counterexamples + budgets consumed + blocked actions + remediation plan.
Drift packet: drift certs + cross-κ mismatch evidence + environment diffs + quarantine actions.
Conflict packet: conflict certs + counterexamples + compat receipts + portal reports.
Migration packet: migration receipts + old/new schema catalogs + re-verification packs + rollback plans.
Audit bundles are hash-addressed and can be archived and exchanged under visibility constraints.
B.4.d Exchange Bundles
Exchange bundles are cross-metro export formats:
Export manifest: list of object ids; pins/wits expansion indices; schema/verifier catalogs; environment snapshots where needed.
Boundary-limited bundles: redacted pin sets; explicit disclosure boundaries; no claims of external verifiability beyond boundary.
Compat receipts: semantic/governance/schema compatibility outcomes; required bridges; quarantine rules.
Policy-bridge receipts: evidence upgrade artifacts allowing reuse under different policies.
Exchange bundles enforce ICIB quarantine-by-default and verified-only clearance.
Appendix B — Certificate Schemas (UCW, Receipts, Refusals)
Appendix C — Corridor & Policy Catalog
C.1 Spec
C.1.a Default Corridors
This section defines the normative corridor families used throughout the system. A corridor is a contractual acceptance regime for approximation and verification; default corridors are canonical presets selected by target class, risk class, and service level requirements.
C.1.a.1 Corridor Taxonomy by Target Class
Default corridors are organized by target class:
EVAL corridors. Certified evaluation corridors governing interval/tail/rounding obligations for evaluator receipts. These corridors specify enclosure-width targets and κ ladders for evaluation, and bind method admissibility for certified evaluation.
EQUIV corridors. UCW corridors governing commutation, holonomy, and projection gates. These corridors specify tri-gate thresholds and probe adequacy requirements.
PROMOTE corridors. Promotion corridors governing atlas closure and Ω* coherence. These corridors include auxiliary gates required by governance (stress/regression) and stronger adequacy obligations.
BRIDGE corridors. Bridge corridors governing convention/policy bridge correctness. These corridors require robust loop closure and often stricter perturbation gates due to higher risk.
IMPORT corridors. Import corridors governing ICIB quarantine clearance. These corridors are conservative by default, requiring local re-verification and strict context matching.
DISCOVERY corridors. Discovery corridors governing candidate extraction (AAS, T0-OBSTR, SPP). Discovery corridors allow candidate-only evidence in search phases but strictly separate it from verification corridors; discovery verification remains certified.
Each corridor family specifies which witness obligations are mandatory, which are optional, and which are forbidden.
C.1.a.2 Standard Gate Presets
Every corridor defines at minimum:
face gate (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}),
spin gate (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}),
projection gate (\varepsilon_{\Pi}),each bound to residual functional identifiers and normalization rules.
Default gate presets are defined as named corridor variants (e.g., C_EVAL_1, C_UCW_STRICT, C_RQF_PROMOTE), each with:
explicit numeric thresholds encoded canonically,
explicit κ ladder id,
explicit probe regime id,
explicit admissibility of auxiliary gates (stress/regression) when required.
Gate presets are never inferred; they are referenced by corridor id and committed by corridor hashes.
C.1.a.3 Stability Region Presets
Default corridors include stability region presets that constrain admissible parameter regimes and mandate boundary probes where necessary.
Interior stability preset. Excludes known discontinuities and ill-conditioned regions; uses standard probe families.
Boundary-aware preset. Includes boundary probes near branch cuts/singularities; imposes stricter adequacy and perturbation requirements.
Regularization preset. Enforces admissibility regions for regularization schemes and requires explicit scheme ids and stress suites.
Stability region presets are corridor fields and are part of corridor identity.
C.1.a.4 Per-Domain Corridor Families
Each domain defines corridor families tailored to its carrier types and typical failure modes. Domain corridor families must provide:
at least one certified evaluation corridor for baseline charts,
at least one UCW corridor for equivalence checking,
at least one promotion corridor for atlas closure,
a conservative import clearance corridor.
Domain corridor families are registered in the corridor catalog with schema hashes and compatibility predicates.
C.1.b κ Schedules
κ schedules define refinement ladders for certified evaluation and for UCW verification. A κ schedule is a corridor object; it is not a runtime preference.
C.1.b.1 Scalar κ Ladders
Scalar κ ladders are used for scalar carriers and include:
a finite ordered sequence of precision levels (digits or bits),
rules mapping κ to truncation depth (N(\kappa)) or analogous discretization,
guard-digit policy and rounding cushion policy.
Scalar κ ladders must be sufficient to detect:
monotone tightening,
plateau above gates,
drift signatures.
C.1.b.2 Probe κ Ladders
Probe κ ladders specify how probe sets evolve with refinement.
probe count per κ,
adversarial probe inclusion rules,
deterministic probe generation or seed-pinning rules,
adequacy triggers.
Probe κ ladders are distinct from numeric κ ladders; corridors may require both.
C.1.b.3 Multi-Component κ
Some carriers require κ as a tuple:[\kappa := (\kappa_{\mathrm{prec}},\kappa_{\mathrm{mesh}},\kappa_{\mathrm{rank}},\kappa_{\mathrm{probe}})]with a deterministic ordering and a monotone escalation rule. Multi-component κ schedules must define:
partial order to total order linearization (deterministic),
budget caps per component,
evidence recomputation requirements per component.
C.1.b.4 Monotone Refinement Requirements
Default corridors specify monotone refinement requirements:
enclosures must tighten or plateau below required thresholds,
UCW residual curves must decrease or plateau below gates,
non-monotone behavior must be classified as drift/instability unless explicitly permitted by corridor as a tolerance-bounded non-monotonicity with strict slack bounds.
Monotone refinement requirements are part of corridor acceptance predicates when declared.
C.1.b.5 Adequacy Triggers
κ schedules must include triggers for probe adequacy escalation:
if residual curves plateau above gates,
if boundary sensitivity is detected,
if multiple candidate moduli appear in Tier-0 extraction under weak probes.
Adequacy triggers are deterministic and produce specific next-action obligations.
C.1.c Budget Presets
Budget presets define termination guarantees for each pass and target class. Budgets are enforced by verifiers and schedulers.
C.1.c.1 Evaluation Budgets
Evaluation budgets include:
maximum κ escalation steps,
maximum truncation depth (N) or mesh resolution,
maximum time and memory budgets,
maximum number of fallback charts to attempt.
Evaluation budgets must ensure that either:
a certified receipt is produced meeting corridor targets, or
a refusal is produced with exhaustion evidence.
C.1.c.2 UCW Budgets
UCW budgets include:
maximum probes per κ,
maximum κ levels to evaluate,
maximum route segments to compare (for localization),
maximum loop suite size for holonomy.
UCW budgets produce either promotable UCWs or refusal objects; they never permit silent acceptance.
C.1.c.3 Snap Budgets
Snap budgets include:
max iterations,
max κ steps,
max probe expansions,
max method swaps,
max route rewrites,
max tunnel escalation triggers.
Snap budgets guarantee termination and enforce strict defect decrease for admissible actions.
C.1.c.4 Tunnel Budgets
Tunnel budgets include:
max candidate charts to explore,
max parameter cap escalations,
max verification κ levels per candidate,
max tunnel events per goal,
max witness hooks to schedule.
Tunnel budgets prevent runaway discovery and enforce determinism of search order.
C.1.c.5 AAS Budgets
AAS budgets include:
max template families explored,
max coefficient cap schedule depth,
max basis feature dimension,
max oracle precision used in search (candidate-only),
max certified verification attempts.
AAS budgets enforce search vs verify separation and ensure reproducible discovery.
C.1.c.6 RQF Budgets
RQF budgets include:
max root chart candidates per root,
max edge UCW attempts,
max face UCW attempts,
max interior loop suite size,
required stress/regression suites for high-risk closure.
RQF budgets ensure that atlas forcing terminates with closure or refusal/portal report.
C.1.c.7 SPP Budgets
SPP budgets include:
max coefficient complexity caps for relation search,
max number of candidate generators per weight,
max κ refinement levels for non-reduction certificates,
max number of relations to attempt per weight,
max cross-metro imports to consider per cycle.
SPP budgets enforce bounded search honesty and prevent spurious basis growth.
C.1.d Policy Presets
Policy presets define admissibility, governance gates, and disclosure boundaries. Policies are hash-addressed and immutable.
C.1.d.1 Strict/Certified Policy
The strict policy is the default for promotion-critical work.
forbids heuristic evidence in witness roots,
forbids randomness in verification,
requires certified evaluation receipts and UCWs for semantic claims,
enforces stress/regression gates for high-risk actions,
enforces strict visibility constraints.
C.1.d.2 Permissive Search Policy
Permissive search policy enables candidate generation.
allows oracle computations and heuristic fits for candidate logs,
may allow seed-pinned randomness for search,
forbids promotion under search evidence; verification must still occur under strict policy,
requires deterministic enumeration order for reproducibility.
C.1.d.3 Import Quarantine Policy
Import quarantine policy applies to ICIB.
imported artifacts enter quarantine by default,
requires compat receipts and local re-verification before reuse,
restricts adoption of foreign policies and corridors as authoritative,
enforces conservative confidence translation.
C.1.d.4 High-Risk Stress-Required Policy
High-risk policy is applied to portal-sensitive scopes, tunnel-derived charts, and cross-policy bridges.
requires stress suites and perturbation robustness,
requires expanded probe adequacy and boundary probes,
enforces stronger regression cadence,
enforces stricter rollback triggers.
C.1.d.5 Visibility Mode Policies
Visibility policies define export constraints:
public: full replay bundles exportable,
restricted: pins/wits partially withheld; boundary-limited verification,
private: no external replay; local-only verification,
quarantined export: candidate-only evidence exportable with explicit non-assertion of truth.
Visibility policy is enforced by export tooling and audited by CI.
C.2 Encode
C.2.a Corridor Fingerprints
A corridor fingerprint is the corridor identity plus a structured digest of its acceptance regime.
Corridor fingerprint includes:
corridor_hash,
gate ids and thresholds with residual functional ids,
κ ladder ids and explicit κ lists,
probe regime ids and adequacy requirements,
method admissibility ids (allowed method/bound/rounding/projector ids),
budget preset ids,
scope ids and target class ids.
Fingerprints are used for compatibility checks and for selection via the corridor chooser. Fingerprints are not advisory; they are commitments.
C.2.b Policy Fingerprints
A policy fingerprint is the policy identity plus a structured digest of admissibility.
Policy fingerprint includes:
policy_hash,
allowed operator families and disallowed operations,
allowed method families and forbidden methods,
budget ceilings and escalation permissions,
governance flags (AUTO_TUNNEL allowed, import quarantine rules, bridge permissions),
disclosure boundaries and visibility modes,
required stress/regression suites by risk class.
Policy fingerprints are used by policy chooser and by compat receipts.
C.2.c Compat Predicates
Compatibility predicates are deterministic functions producing outcomes used by ICIB and internal reuse.
C.2.c.1 Corridor Compatibility
Corridor compatibility predicate returns:
IDENTICAL if fingerprints match,
TIGHTENING_COMPATIBLE if one corridor tightens another without changing semantics for the target class and re-verification requirements are satisfied,
REVERIFY_REQUIRED if corridors differ in gates or κ schedules for the target class,
INCOMPATIBLE if residual functionals or scope definitions differ irreconcilably.
C.2.c.2 Policy Compatibility
Policy compatibility predicate returns:
IDENTICAL,
BRIDGE_REQUIRED,
BLOCKED (policy forbids required operations),
INCOMPATIBLE (visibility boundaries prevent required verification).
C.2.c.3 Scope Compatibility
Scope compatibility predicate checks:
ConvBundleFP coverage,
operator-family scope overlap,
required bridge presence for mismatched conventions.
Outputs: COVERED, SCOPE_GAP, BRIDGE_REQUIRED, CONFLICT.
C.2.c.4 Bridge-Required Conditions
Bridge-required conditions specify when:
a convention bridge is required (ConvFP mismatch),
a policy bridge is required (policy mismatch),
a corridor bridge or re-verification is required (corridor mismatch).
C.2.c.5 Refusal Conditions
Refusal conditions specify when compatibility cannot be established:
missing schema decoders,
missing pins due to visibility restrictions,
policy forbiddance without admissible policy bridge,
scope conflicts without admissible bridges.
Compatibility predicates are pinned by schema id and versioned.
C.2.d Policy-Bridge Templates
Policy-bridge templates define admissible upgrade procedures.
A policy-bridge template includes:
source policy class predicate and target policy class predicate,
admissibility mapping (what evidence must be re-verified),
corridor mapping (required κ ladders and gates),
stress/regression obligations required for upgrade,
visibility translation rules (what remains boundary-limited),
refusal rules for blocked upgrades.
Policy-bridge templates are implemented as BridgeSeeds with proof obligations and are promoted only via verified evidence.
C.3 Prove
C.3.a Safe Widening Rules
Corridor widening is safety-critical because corridor-bounded equivalence semantics change when gates widen.
Safe widening rules:
Explicit new identity. Any widening produces a new corridor record with new corridor hash.
No retroactive validation. Artifacts certified under a tighter corridor are not automatically valid under a wider corridor if equivalence relation is corridor-indexed; any reuse must be explicit and context-indexed.
Re-verification required. If widened corridor is used for acceptance, verification must be replayed and receipts must bind the new corridor hash.
Quarantine transitions. Any attempt to “fix” failures by widening inside repair pipelines is refused; widening is a governance event and triggers quarantine until re-verification under the new corridor completes.
Disclosure. Widening must be reported in governance logs and corridor diffs; silent widening is forbidden.
C.3.b Safe Tightening Rules
Tightening strengthens obligations and may preserve or strengthen trust when applied correctly.
Safe tightening rules:
Explicit new identity. Tightening produces a new corridor hash. Tightening is not a mutation of the old corridor.
Evidence strengthening. Tightening requires either:
existing evidence already satisfies tighter gates, or
re-verification under the tighter corridor produces new receipts.
Regression triggers. Tightening that changes default corridors triggers regression suites to ensure existing promoted artifacts remain valid under their original corridors and to establish coverage under tightened corridors if required.
No semantic drift. Tightening cannot change semantic conventions; any drift indicates ConvFP or method instability and is refused.
Transfer discipline. Tightening does not automatically transfer UCWs unless corridor compatibility predicate declares it identical or tightening-compatible under explicit rules; otherwise re-verification is required.
C.3.c Rollback Rules
Rollback rules define safe reversal of corridor/policy pointer changes and activation choices.
Rollback evidence. Rollback is justified by:
regression failures,
drift alarms,
defect non-reduction evidence,
policy compliance failures.
Rollback receipts. Rollback produces rollback receipts/logs binding the rollback action to specific pointers, contexts, and reasons.
Fallback guarantees. Rollback must preserve baseline charts and baseline corridors for service continuity; if fallback is impossible, affected nodes are quarantined and demand scheduler emits service-level degradation records.
Audit linkage. Rollback logs link to failure artifacts and to the activation/deprecation events being rolled back. No evidence is deleted.
C.3.d Conflict Rules
Conflicts arise when policies/corridors/scopes are incompatible and cannot be bridged safely.
Policy conflicts. If policy forbids required operations, classify as POLICY_BLOCK. Remedy is policy bridge demand or policy change request; not corridor widening.
Corridor conflicts. If corridor functionals differ, classify as CORRIDOR_INCOMPATIBLE and require re-verification under a locally selected corridor; if re-verification cannot proceed, classify as policy block or probe inadequacy.
Scope gaps. If ConvBundleFP does not cover required operators, classify as SCOPE_GAP and require ConvFP expansion or convention bridges.
Incompatibility classification. If conflicts are intrinsic (e.g., convention regimes incompatible without portal), emit portal candidate requirements rather than repeated bridging attempts.
Escalation paths. Conflict escalation follows: compat receipt → bridge requirement → policy bridge or convention bridge → portal report → quarantine with remediation plan.
Conflict rules preserve integrity and prevent silent misuse of evidence.
C.4 Extract
C.4.a Corridor Chooser
The corridor chooser is a deterministic selection procedure that outputs a corridor id, κ plan, and budgets, given target class, risk class, and SLA.
Inputs:
demand target class (eval/equiv/promote/bridge/import/discovery),
risk class,
SLA requirements (digits/latency/freshness),
operator scope and carrier type,
policy constraints.
Outputs:
selected corridor hash,
κ ladder id and explicit κ list,
probe regime id and adequacy requirements,
budget preset id,
required auxiliary suites (stress/regression) if any,
a compat report if multiple corridors are eligible, showing selection rationale.
Chooser is auditable; it records decision logs and ties to scheduler policy version.
C.4.b Policy Chooser
Policy chooser selects policy by visibility, risk, and operator scope.
Inputs:
visibility mode (public/restricted/private),
risk class,
operator-family scope,
whether search vs verify phases are needed (candidate generation).
Outputs:
selected policy hash,
admissibility map (allowed operator families and methods),
budget ceilings,
required stress/regression suites,
required bridges if target evidence is produced under different policy.
Policy chooser produces policy selection logs and is deterministic.
C.4.c Compat Report Templates
Compat report templates standardize compatibility evidence.
Corridor compat report. Compares corridor fingerprints; outputs compatibility outcome and re-verification requirements.
Policy compat report. Compares policy fingerprints; outputs bridge-required or blocked conditions and required policy bridges.
Combined compat receipt. Binds corridor compat and policy compat and scope compat into a single receipt with required next actions.
Remediation plans. Derived deterministic action lists: re-verify under selected corridor, apply policy bridge, expand ConvFP scope, run stress/regression.
Compat templates are used by ICIB and internal reuse.
C.4.d Governance Dashboards
Governance dashboards are extraction artifacts summarizing corridor/policy state.
Corridor coverage maps. Which corridors certify which edges/nodes/UCWs; missing coverage gaps.
Policy blocks. List of blocked demands due to policy forbiddance; required policy bridges.
Widening/tightening events. Corridor lineage graphs, diffs, rollback events, and their reasons.
Quarantine statuses. Quarantined artifacts due to drift, stress/regression failures, policy conflicts; clearance requirements.
Dashboards are context-indexed and audit-linked to receipts and failure artifacts.
Appendix C — Corridor & Policy Catalog
Appendix D — Deterministic Numerical Library (Intervals, Tails, Projectors)
D.1 Spec
D.1.a Interval Ops
This section specifies the deterministic enclosure calculus used for certified evaluation and UCW verification. Enclosures are carriers of soundness: they must be conservative, compositional, and replay-verifiable under pinned rounding policies.
D.1.a.1 Enclosure Kinds
The library supports the following canonical enclosure kinds, each with a containment semantics and an algebra of operations.
Scalar interval (real).An interval (I=[\ell,u]\subset\mathbb{R}) with (\ell\le u). Semantics: (x^\star\in I).Canonical form: endpoints encoded as canonical decimals under rounding id; endpoints must be outward-rounded.
Complex rectangle (axis-aligned).A complex enclosure (R=[\ell_r,u_r]\times[\ell_i,u_i]\subset\mathbb{R}^2). Semantics: (z^\star\in R).Canonical form: four endpoints with outward rounding. This is the default complex enclosure unless a schema declares a different complex enclosure.
Vector ball.A ball enclosure (B(c,r)) in a normed vector space ((\mathbb{V},|\cdot|)). Semantics: (x^\star\in {x:|x-c|\le r}).Canonical form: center (c) encoded as a vector of canonical numeric strings; radius (r) canonical; norm id pinned.
Tensor ball.A tensor enclosure in an induced norm; treated as vector ball on flattened representation with pinned reshape and norm semantics.
Operator enclosure.An enclosure for linear operators, specified by a carrier-dependent predicate, e.g.:
spectral enclosure (bounds on spectrum),
operator norm ball (|A-A_0|\le r),
interval bounds on matrix entries plus induced norm bound.Operator enclosure type is pinned by schema; semantics must be checkable or conservatively approximable.
Distribution/measure enclosure (band).A distribution enclosure specified by bounds on moments or characteristic functions over a probe family. Semantics is defined by containment of all probed observables within bounds. This enclosure is used only when schema declares it and requires adequacy metadata.
Each enclosure kind includes:
a containment predicate id describing how membership is interpreted,
a canonical representation for encoding,
a set of admissible operations with conservative semantics.
D.1.a.2 Enclosure Algebra
Enclosure algebra provides deterministic operations that preserve sound containment. For each enclosure kind, the library defines:
Addition/subtraction.If (x^\star\in \mathcal E_x) and (y^\star\in \mathcal E_y), define (\mathcal E_x \oplus \mathcal E_y) and (\mathcal E_x \ominus \mathcal E_y) such that:[x^\star \pm y^\star \in \mathcal E_x \oplus/\ominus \mathcal E_y.]For scalar intervals: ([a,b]+[c,d]=[a+c,b+d]) with outward rounding applied to each endpoint. Similar for subtraction.
Multiplication.Define (\mathcal E_x \otimes \mathcal E_y) conservatively. For scalar intervals, interval multiplication is performed by evaluating the set of endpoint products and taking min/max, with outward rounding. For complex rectangles, conservative bounds are obtained via bounding real and imaginary parts, or via norm enclosures if schema prefers.
Division and inversion.Division is admissible only when zero is excluded from denominator enclosure under a certified predicate. If the denominator enclosure includes 0, division must:
refuse, or
produce a schema-declared conservative enclosure that explicitly includes singularity behavior and is treated as boundary-limited and non-promotable for semantic equality claims.
Scalar multiplication.Multiply enclosure by scalar with outward rounding.
Norm and observable extraction.For vector/operator enclosures, compute conservative upper bounds on norms or observables using pinned norm semantics.
Transform application.Transforms (Fourier, Laplace, Mellin, etc.) may be applied only when transform enclosure laws are provided with corresponding bound ids (D.1.b and D.2.b). Otherwise transform application is candidate-only.
All operations are deterministic: they must not depend on runtime iteration order and must use pinned rounding policies.
D.1.a.3 Conservativeness Axioms
Conservativeness is a required invariant.
Sound containment axiom. Every enclosure operation produces an enclosure that contains the true result, under stated admissibility conditions and pinned contexts.
Outward rounding axiom. Every numeric endpoint produced is outward-rounded according to rounding id so that rounding error is included. Under no circumstance may inward rounding occur in promotable certified evaluation.
Monotone tightening axiom. Refinement of κ and truncation depth should not shrink enclosures unsafely; it may shrink conservatively or remain stable. If enclosures shrink while becoming unsound, drift is detected and quarantined.
Admissibility axiom. Operations outside admissible regions must refuse rather than produce unsound enclosures.
Conservativeness is enforced by audits and regression suites.
D.1.b Tail Bounds
Tail bounds certify truncation errors for series, integrals, transforms, and iterative schemes.
D.1.b.1 Bound Families
Bound families are schema-defined and versioned. Each bound family specifies:
functional form (B(\cdot)),
assumption region (\mathcal R),
parameter schema,
composition rules.
Canonical bound families include:
Series remainder bounds (monotone decreasing terms, alternating series, ratio test envelopes),
Integral quadrature bounds (error envelopes for quadrature rules),
Transform truncation bounds (finite window approximations),
Recurrence and solver bounds (error propagation envelopes),
Kernel approximation bounds (semigroup truncation and tail envelopes).
Each bound family must include a conservative bound evaluation method that is deterministic and auditable.
D.1.b.2 Assumption Regions
Every bound family declares an assumption region (\mathcal R) and admissibility checks:
parameter constraints (e.g., (q<1), monotonicity, positivity, analytic region),
stability constraints (avoid singularities),
operator scope constraints (branch-safe requirements).
Bound use is promotable only when assumptions are checkable and satisfied; otherwise the receipt must be boundary-limited or refused under strict policy.
D.1.b.3 Deterministic Selection of Truncation
For each κ, certified evaluation must deterministically select truncation depth (N(\kappa)) (or analogous discretization controls) such that:[B(N(\kappa)) + \delta_{\mathrm{rnd}} \le \tau(\kappa),]where (\tau(\kappa)) is the error budget implied by corridor gates and (\delta_{\mathrm{rnd}}) is rounding cushion.
Selection rules:
are monotone in κ (higher κ implies equal or stronger truncation),
terminate under budgets,
are replayable and recorded in receipts.
If selection cannot satisfy budgets within constraints, evaluation refuses with exhaustion evidence.
D.1.b.4 Composition Laws
Bound composition laws are required for composite evaluators. For operations (\oplus,\otimes) and transforms (T), bounds must provide:
how to bound sum remainders,
how to bound product remainders (including cross-terms),
how to bound transform-induced error,
how to propagate bounds through projection/canonicalization when required.
If composition laws are missing, certification halts and yields refusal or boundary-limited evidence.
D.1.b.5 Auditability Requirements
Each bound must be:
hash-addressed (bound id),
recomputable by verifiers,
accompanied by provenance references (derivation artifacts),
regression-tested in bound audit suites,
quarantined if found invalid or non-conservative.
D.1.c Projector Families
Projectors enforce representability and canonical meaning class stability.
D.1.c.1 Rounding Projectors
Rounding projectors map computed objects into canonical numeric forms consistent with enclosures.
Interval endpoint rounding. Apply outward rounding by rounding id to any computed endpoints.
Canonical numeric encoding projector. Ensure numeric encodings follow Appendix A numeric rules; reject ill-formed encodings.
Precision guard projector. Apply guard digits and rounding cushion policies deterministically.
Rounding projectors must be deterministic and pinned by rounding id.
D.1.c.2 Basis Canonicalizers
Basis canonicalizers enforce canonical basis representations in algebraic carriers.
canonical basis ordering pinned by ConvFP,
canonical coefficient normalization (gcd, sign normalization),
canonical representation of zero and small coefficients under corridor.
Basis canonicalizers must be stable under refinement; instability triggers drift alarms.
D.1.c.3 Normalization Projectors
Normalization projectors implement canonical forms for structural objects.
canonicalization of expression trees,
canonicalization of route words (WordNF),
canonicalization of addresses (NormAddr),
canonicalization of charts and bound parameter records.
Normalization must be idempotent and scope-aware; it must not erase corridor-changing events.
D.1.c.4 Representability Residuals
For promotion-critical claims, projectors require representability residual evidence:[r_{\Pi}(\kappa) \le \varepsilon_{\Pi}.]Residual functionals and probe regimes are pinned; representability checks are performed by UCWs or projector audits.
D.1.c.5 Commutation Obligations
Projectors must commute with transports where schema demands. Commutation is certified by UCWs. If projector commutation fails, the system refuses equivalence and triggers remediation (projector migration, ConvFP remediation, or portal escalation).
D.1.d Error Composition
Error composition governs how uncertainties propagate through computations.
D.1.d.1 Propagation Laws
Propagation laws define conservative envelopes for composite computations. If (x^\star\in \mathcal E_x) and (y^\star\in\mathcal E_y), error envelopes define (\mathcal E_{f(x,y)}) such that:
for sums, radii add,
for products, radii propagate with cross-term bounds,
for transforms, include transform-bound corrections,
for projections, include projection residual corrections.
Propagation laws are pinned by envelope ids and must be checkable by verifiers.
D.1.d.2 Envelope Algebra
Envelope algebra defines the combination of:
truncation bounds,
rounding cushions,
transform bounds,
projection residual bounds,into a single conservative enclosure.
Envelopes are represented as compositional bound objects; missing envelope rules block certification.
D.1.d.3 Monotone Tightening
As κ increases, envelopes must tighten or plateau conservatively. Non-monotone tightening is treated as drift unless explicitly permitted and bounded by corridor slack with proof obligations.
D.1.d.4 Perturbation Envelopes
Perturbation envelopes bound sensitivity to small perturbations and are required for high-risk claims. They are computed by perturbation probes and must be conservative and deterministic.
D.1.d.5 Failure Semantics
Failures are classified:
Insufficient κ or bounds (numeric insufficiency): refusal with admissible κ escalation or bound upgrade plan.
Ill-conditioning (perturbation failures): refusal with carrier escalation or stability region restriction.
Scope or policy block: refusal with bridge/policy change plan.
Drift: quarantine and migration remediation.
Failure semantics are expressed as refusal objects with counterexamples and remediation.
D.2 Encode
D.2.a Method IDs
Method ids identify certified evaluator algorithms and their transform/solver components.
Evaluator method ids: series evaluation, quadrature evaluation, recurrence evaluation, kernel evaluation, continued fraction evaluation.
Transform ids: Fourier, Laplace, Mellin, z-transform families, each with normalization scope pinned by ConvFP.
Solver ids: root-finding, fixed-point iteration, linear system solvers, each allowed only if certified envelope laws exist.
Composition ids: pipeline composition ids derived from ordered lists of component method ids, with deterministic normalization.
Migration ids: method upgrades are represented by migration edges; new method id must be used in new receipts.
Method ids are hash-addressed and referenced in receipts.
D.2.b Bound IDs
Bound ids identify tail and envelope bounds.
Tail-bound ids: remainder bounds for series/integrals/recurrences.
Transform-bound ids: bounds for truncated transforms and inversion approximations.
Envelope ids: compositional error envelopes for pipelines.
Provenance references: derivation artifacts and assumption region ids.
Composition references: explicit rule ids describing how to combine bounds.
Bound ids are mandatory for certified evaluation; missing bound ids yield refusal.
D.2.c Rounding IDs
Rounding ids pin rounding semantics.
rounding cushion function id and parameters,
backend dependencies and pinned numeric semantics,
deterministic numeric encoding policies (Appendix A numeric rules),
admissible rounding modes by policy.
Rounding ids are required for all certified enclosures.
D.2.d Proof Tags
Proof tags classify evidence.
certified: interval/tail/rounding proof is present and replay-verifiable.
heuristic: evidence derived from oracle computations; candidate-only.
boundary-limited: certified within local visibility boundary; external replay not possible.
admissibility tags: stable region ids and assumption region ids satisfied.
risk tags: risk class; required stress/regression suite ids.
Proof tags are metadata but are enforced by governance; they determine whether evidence may enter witness roots for promotion.
D.3 Prove
D.3.a Enclosure Proofs
Enclosure proofs certify containment.
Containment theorems: series/integral/iterative enclosure formulas validated by bound ids.
Rounding conservativeness: rounding cushions ensure endpoints are outward-rounded and include numeric backend error.
Interval arithmetic correctness: enclosure algebra operations preserve containment under pinned rounding ids.
Transform enclosure correctness: transform application yields conservative enclosures only when transform bounds exist and assumptions are satisfied.
Enclosure proofs are replay-verifiable by recomputation under pinned contexts.
D.3.b Bound Audits
Bound audits are mandatory to ensure conservativeness.
Assumption checks: verify that bound assumptions hold for parameter regime.
Recomputation checks: recompute bound values and compare to stored evidence.
Conservativeness checks: regression tests detect underestimation; any evidence of underestimation quarantines the bound id and all dependent receipts.
Regression of bounds: bounds are included in regression suites; changes require migration.
Quarantine invalid bounds: invalid bounds trigger refusal for dependent promotions; remediation requires bound upgrade and re-verification.
D.3.c Stability Proofs
Stability proofs ensure representability and commutation under projection.
Projection stability: projection residual curves satisfy (\varepsilon_{\Pi}) across κ under adequate probes.
Commutation with transports: UCWs certify that projection and transports commute where schema requires.
κ monotonicity: enclosures and residuals tighten or plateau below gates; non-monotone behavior indicates drift/instability.
Perturbation stability: perturbation envelopes and stress suites certify robustness where required.
Stability failures yield refusals or quarantines, never silent acceptance.
D.3.d Regression Proofs
Regression proofs ensure long-term correctness.
Fixed suites: a fixed set of evaluators, bounds, and projectors are rerun periodically at specified κ.
Drift detection: cross-κ and cross-replay mismatches under pinned contexts trigger drift alarms.
Bisect lineage: failures include lineage pointers enabling localization to a method/bound/rounding migration.
Replay invariance: regression verifies that replay scripts reproduce outcomes exactly; failures quarantine affected artifacts.
Regression proofs are packaged as regression packs and referenced by receipts.
D.4 Extract
D.4.a Method Selection Table
Method selection is context-indexed and risk-aware.
for each target class (eval/equiv/promote), list recommended baseline methods,
list admissible accelerators under AAS with required bindings,
list fallback methods when policies forbid preferred methods,
list which method/bound/rounding ids are required and which corridors support them,
list stability regions and boundary constraints.
The method selection table is deterministic and used by scheduler and route planners.
D.4.b Fallback Ladder
Fallback ladder defines a safe hierarchy:
baseline-first certified evaluators per root,
cross-root fallbacks when a root is missing or quarantined,
policy fallbacks when methods are forbidden,
quarantine fallbacks: revert to last known good certified method set.
Fallback ladder must preserve certified service continuity and is audited by CI.
D.4.c Audit Recipes
Audit recipes are deterministic replay procedures.
Bound replay: recompute bounds and assumption checks; verify bound id integrity.
Enclosure replay: recompute approximant, bound, rounding cushion; reconstruct enclosure; verify identity.
Projector replay: apply (\Pi_h) deterministically; recompute projection residuals; verify idempotence and commutation obligations.
Cross-backend determinism checks: compare results across permitted environments; require additional environment pins or refuse promotion if outcomes differ.
Audit recipes produce audit artifacts and integrity proofs.
D.4.d Stress Recipes
Stress recipes define adversarial testing regimes for numerical library components.
Adversarial probes: boundary probes near branch cuts and singularities; worst-case commutation probes.
Boundary probes: stability region boundary sweeps; regularization boundary tests.
Ill-conditioned probes: parameter regimes with poor conditioning; perturbation sensitivity tests.
Worst-case commutation probes: probes designed to maximize commutation and holonomy residuals, ensuring robustness of UCWs and projectors.
Stress recipes are policy-scoped and produce stress packs used for promotion gating.
Appendix D — Deterministic Numerical Library (Intervals, Tails, Projectors)
Appendix E — Algorithms (Snap, Tunnel, T0-OBSTR, RQF, AAS, SPP)
E.1 Spec
E.1.a Pass Interfaces
This appendix specifies the algorithmic passes as executable, deterministic interfaces. Each pass is a total function over a typed input record and returns a sum-type output record, together with mandatory logs and optional caches. All passes are context-indexed and policy-governed.
E.1.a.1 Common Input Record
Every pass consumes a PassInput record:
goal_id: typed goal (edge equivalence, chart verification, atlas closure, basis update, import clearance).
context_hashes: conv_bundle_hash, policy_hash, corridor_hash, verifier_hash, canon_hash, schema_hashes.
scope: operator-family scope and carrier scope; must be covered by ConvBundleFP.
pins_root_ref: reference to pins set (or derivable pins from goal).
wits_root_ref: reference to any existing witness set available as starting evidence.
budget: pass-specific budget object.
state_refs: references to current metro objects (nodes, edges, charts, atlas spec, basis sets), as required by goal type.
Admissibility checks are performed before any computational work. If admissibility fails, the pass returns a refusal output with classification and missing requirements.
E.1.a.2 Common Output Sum Type
Every pass returns PassOutcome:
OK(output_payload, evidence_roots, logs_ref),
NEAR(output_payload, evidence_roots, logs_ref, next_actions),
FAIL(refusal_id, evidence_roots, logs_ref, next_actions).
NEAR is permitted only when hard legality predicates pass and repair paths remain within admissible budgets. FAIL is returned when hard legality fails, budgets exhaust, plateau is stable, or policy blocks decisive verification.
E.1.a.3 Context Bindings
All passes must:
validate that all referenced objects match input context hashes,
refuse on any mismatch unless explicit bridges are present and verified,
record any bridge applications as explicit WordNF segments or bridge application traces.
Context binding is a first-class legality predicate. No pass is permitted to reinterpret evidence under different corridor/policy/convention silently.
E.1.a.4 Witness Hooks
Passes must declare witness hooks: outputs must either include required witness objects or include explicit job plans to create them.
Witness hooks include:
UCWs required for transport/equivalence edges,
evaluation receipts required for charts,
Ω* certificates required for RQF promotion,
stress/regression packs required for high-risk actions,
BridgeSeeds/MagicCertPacks produced by Snap/Tunnel/AAS integrations.
Witness hooks are logged and bound into receipts when promotion is attempted.
E.1.a.5 Refusal Semantics
Refusals are structured and replayable. Every pass must produce a refusal object when it cannot proceed, containing:
minimal failing predicate id,
minimal counterexample objects (κ/probe/subword),
budgets consumed and blocked actions,
admissible next actions,
quarantine semantics if needed.
E.1.b Inputs/Outputs
This section defines each pass’s specific input/output schema extensions.
E.1.b.1 Snap Pass (SNAP)
Input: PassInput + snap_state including current defect curves, candidate actions set, and Snap budget.Output:
SnapOK(state'): tri-gate satisfied for all required UCWs; evidence ready for receipt binding.
SnapNEAR(state'): hard legality passes; quantitative gates fail; admissible repairs remain.
SnapFAIL(refusal): no admissible defect-decreasing actions remain under budgets or hard legality/policy blocks.
Mandatory artifacts: Snap trace, defect decrease certificates per accepted action, refusal pack on failure.
E.1.b.2 Tunnel Pass (TUNNEL)
Input: PassInput + tunnel_policy and enumerator state (deterministic), target splice point, candidate chart families allowed.Output:
TunnelOK(event, new_chart_candidates, witness_hooks): tunnel event logged; candidate chart produced; verification hooks created.
TunnelNEAR(event, candidate_set, next_actions): candidates exist but require additional verification or bridging; not promotable.
TunnelFAIL(refusal): search exhausted under budgets or forbidden by policy/scope.
Mandatory artifacts: tunnel event log, candidate ledger entries, splice records, rollback plan fields.
E.1.b.3 Tier-0 Obstruction Compiler (T0-OBSTR)
Input: PassInput + loop family id, minimal loop suite id (or rule), face set id, carrier template id.Output:
ObstrOK(record): stable obstruction candidates produced, with supporting UCWs and κ-intersection evidence.
ObstrNEAR(record): evidence suggestive but probe adequacy insufficient or stability weak; requires probe/method upgrades.
ObstrFAIL(refusal): not holonomy-dominant, drift detected, policy blocks, or budgets exhausted.
Mandatory artifacts: obstruction record, clustering record, stability metrics, refusal pack when rejected.
E.1.b.4 Root-Quad Forcing (RQF)
Input: PassInput + candidate node id, atlas spec ref, required roots, closure targets.Output:
RQF_OK(omega_star, atlas_update): root receipts, edge and face UCWs, interior Ω* produced; promotion-ready evidence.
RQF_NEAR(atlas_update, missing_obligations): partial closure; missing roots or missing faces; repair jobs required.
RQF_FAIL(refusal_or_portal_report): closure impossible under current schema; portal required or budgets exhausted/policy blocks.
Mandatory artifacts: atlas diff, closure proof pack pointers, portal report if classified.
E.1.b.5 Atlas Acceleration Synthesis (AAS)
Input: PassInput + target node id, template family set, coefficient caps schedule, search budgets, verification corridor/policy.Output:
AAS_OK(chart, verification_receipts, binding_ucw): accel chart verified and bound to baseline; promotable candidate.
AAS_NEAR(candidate_chart, missing_bounds_or_ucws): candidate exists but lacks certified bound/projection or binding UCW.
AAS_FAIL(refusal): no candidate survives certified verification or search exhausted.
Mandatory artifacts: candidate logs (search), verification logs (certified), promotion logs if activated, rollback plan.
E.1.b.6 Seed Promotion Pipeline (SPP)
Input: PassInput + dimension model id, target weight/depth region, current basis id, relation search caps.Output:
SPP_OK(basis_update, relations_update, promotion_candidates): basis expanded or relations certified; deficiency reduced.
SPP_NEAR(partial_updates, outstanding_obligations): partial results; missing non-reduction certificates or missing RQF closures.
SPP_FAIL(refusal): policy blocks, probe inadequacy, or bounded search exhausted with uncertainty.
Mandatory artifacts: updated basis records, reduction receipts, non-reduction certificates (or refusal packs), scheduling plan for forced generators.
E.1.c Budgets
Budgets are explicit, pass-specific, and enforce termination. Each pass has a BudgetSpec with mandatory fields and consumption counters in logs.
E.1.c.1 Per-Pass Caps
SNAP: max iterations; max κ steps; max probe expansions; max method swaps; max route rewrites; max tunnel escalations.
TUNNEL: max candidate charts; max cap escalations; max verification attempts; max witness-hook jobs scheduled; max tunnel events.
T0-OBSTR: max loop suite size; max κ levels; max probe expansions; max clustering candidates; max stability checks.
RQF: max charts per root; max edge UCW attempts; max face UCW attempts; max interior loop size; max repair iterations per cell.
AAS: max template families; max coefficient cap levels; max feature dimension; max oracle precision (search-only); max certified verification attempts.
SPP: max relation search caps; max candidate primitives per weight; max κ levels for non-reduction; max relations per weight; max import comparisons.
E.1.c.2 Termination Guarantees
Termination is ensured by:
bounded iteration counts,
deterministic action selection,
monotone defect decrease requirement for repair actions,
bounded enumeration in search,
bounded κ ladders.
Any non-terminating behavior is a violation and must be refused.
E.1.c.3 Budget Exhaustion Refusal Forms
If a budget cap is reached before success, the pass must emit FAIL with:
consumed budget counters,
best-achieved evidence (residual curves, enclosure widths),
minimal failing predicate and counterexamples,
admissible next actions that require changed budgets via policy change or new corridor.
E.1.c.4 Deterministic Escalation Rules
Escalation rules are fixed:
attempt admissible refinement (κ/probes),
attempt admissible method swaps,
attempt admissible rewrites,
attempt tunnels when policy allows and when defect signatures justify,
attempt T0-OBSTR when holonomy-dominant and stable,
attempt portal classification when conflicts persist or closure cannot be expressed.
Escalation decisions are logged and replayable.
E.1.d Determinism Constraints
Determinism is mandatory for promotion-critical outputs and is enforced by strict constraints.
E.1.d.1 Deterministic Enumeration
All search spaces must be enumerated in deterministic order:
canonical ordering of template families,
canonical ordering of parameter tuples,
canonical escalation schedule for caps,
canonical selection of splice points and candidate priorities.
Any deviation changes event identity and must be logged as a new event.
E.1.d.2 Seed Pinning Rules
Randomness is forbidden for promotable evidence. If policy permits randomness in candidate search:
seeds are pinned in candidate logs,
randomness cannot influence verification outcomes,
any candidate promoted must be re-verified under deterministic certified verification regardless of how it was discovered.
E.1.d.3 No Silent Widening
Corridor widening is forbidden inside passes. Any widening:
produces a new corridor identity,
requires governance event and re-verification,
is recorded in corridor lineage logs and is never implicit.
E.1.d.4 No Heuristic Promotion
Heuristic evidence is confined to candidate logs. Promotion requires:
certified evaluation receipts,
UCW recomputation and tri-gate pass,
stress/regression pass if required,
replay success.
E.2 Encode
E.2.a Job Graphs
Each pass emits a job graph specification describing:
job nodes (pass executions, verification tasks, stress/regression tasks),
dependency edges (e.g., RQF depends on root receipts and edge UCWs),
context hashes per node,
budgets per node,
expected output artifacts per node,
witness hooks per node.
Job graphs are schema-validated and hash-addressed. Job graphs are the canonical executable scheduling artifacts.
E.2.b Logs
Logs are required for every pass and are immutable.
Run logs: start/end; budgets consumed; outputs produced; outcome class (OK/NEAR/FAIL).
Decision logs: why an action chosen (minimal failing predicate, defect dominance, risk gates).
Candidate logs: search-phase artifacts (PSLQ/fit results, candidate parameter tuples), tagged heuristic.
Verification logs: certified verification results (receipts, UCWs, residual packs).
Promotion logs: activation events, promotion receipts, governance pointer updates.
Rollback logs: deactivation/quarantine events, reasons, fallback selection, impact analysis.
All logs bind to context hashes and include integrity checks.
E.2.c Caches
Caches accelerate execution but are non-authoritative.
Memo keys: include goal id, WordNF hash, policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP hashes, verifier hash, schema hashes, budget id.
Cache values: pointers to previously computed receipts/UCWs, expansion indices, trace summaries; never raw unpinned outcomes.
Invalidation dependencies: schema versions, verifier versions, corridor/policy versions, drift alarms, regression failures.
Audit hooks: cache use is logged; cache must be bypassable; cache correctness is verified by periodic replay audits.
E.2.d Invalidation
Invalidation is a first-class event.
Invalidation events: record what was invalidated (cache entries, bridge activations, chart activations, receipts), why, and required remediation.
Triggers: schema migration; verifier upgrades; ConvFP updates; corridor/policy changes; drift alarms; regression failures; stress failures; import conflicts.
