Quarantine propagation: invalidation may propagate quarantines through dependency graph; dependent promotions may be suspended.
Rescheduling rules: invalidated tasks may be requeued with updated contexts and budgets; repeated failures are pruned deterministically unless new admissible actions exist.
Invalidation is deterministic and auditable.
E.3 Prove
E.3.a Soundness Obligations
All passes must satisfy core soundness obligations.
Verify-only acceptance. Promotion-critical decisions depend only on certified evidence; search oracles are never accepted as proof.
UCW recomputation. UCW-based acceptance requires recomputation of residual curves under pinned context; cached curves are evidence only.
Enclosure correctness. Certified evaluation uses intervals/tails/rounding under pinned ids; enclosures are conservative.
No policy laundering. Evidence produced under one policy cannot certify claims under another without explicit policy bridge receipts and re-verification where required.
No scope gaps. All operators used must be covered by ConvBundleFP scope; scope gaps are refused, not guessed.
Soundness obligations are enforced by verifiers and CI.
E.3.b Promotion Obligations
Promotion obligations are pass-dependent but share a common structure.
RQF closure. For promoting new seeds or kernel hubs, RQF must produce edge/face closure and Ω* when required.
Ω.* Interior coherence certificate must be replay-verifiable and bound to the exact boundary witnesses.
Stress/regression. High-risk promotions require stress and regression packs; pass must not activate defaults without them.
Bridge correctness. Bridges must be witnessed by UCWs and must have replay-stable application traces.
Receipt binding. Promotion decisions must be captured in receipts binding pins and witness roots, enabling independent replay verification.
E.3.c Refusal Obligations
Refusals must be complete, minimal, and actionable.
Completeness. Include context hashes, budgets consumed, best-achieved evidence, and required missing artifacts.
Counterexample minimality. Provide minimal failing κ/probe/subword sufficient to witness failure.
Plateau/drift classification. Distinguish plateau from drift and from holonomy obstruction via deterministic checks.
Admissible next actions. Provide a finite list of admissible next actions consistent with policy and corridor; forbidden actions are excluded.
Refusal obligations ensure honesty and accelerate remediation.
E.3.d Replay Obligations
Replay obligations ensure outputs remain verifiable over time and across environments.
Pins/wits expansion. Provide expansion indices or explicit lists; verify merkle roots.
Deterministic recomputation. Recompute evidence under pinned environment/toolchain pins; accept only if outcomes match.
Bundle integrity proofs. Produce integrity proofs for proof packs and archives (hash checks, chain checks, manifests).
Archival replay rules. Store verifier catalogs and schema registries needed for backward verification; migrations must include verified translators.
Replay obligations are enforced by CI and archival policies.
E.4 Extract
E.4.a Pseudocode Index
The system provides extraction-optimized pseudocode references. Each pseudocode item is a schema-addressed artifact; pseudocode is descriptive of deterministic behavior, not an unpinned implementation.
ALG:SNAP — defect-driven deterministic repair pass.
ALG:TUNNEL — bounded deterministic tunneling and integration.
ALG:AAS — search/verify acceleration synthesis and chart promotion gating.
ALG:T0_OBSTR — holonomy-dominant obstruction extraction and clustering.
ALG:RQF — root-quad forcing and Ω* interior coherence verification.
ALG:SPP — deficiency detection, forced generator scheduling, certified relations/non-relations.
ALG:ICIB_HOOKS — import manifests, compat receipts, quarantine clearance.
ALG:CI_HOOKS — deterministic replay, regression/stress pipelines, integrity proofs.
Each pseudocode reference includes: inputs, outputs, budgets, determinism constraints, refusal outcomes.
E.4.b Complexity Notes
Complexity notes are non-semantic but necessary for budgeting.
cost models for evaluation receipts by κ and truncation depth,
UCW cost models by probe count and route length,
Snap cost models by iteration caps and action sets,
tunnel and AAS search costs by candidate caps and coefficient bounds,
RQF closure costs by number of roots and closure cells,
SPP costs by relation search caps and basis size.
Worst-case caps are explicit; exceeding caps yields refusals, not silent retries.
E.4.c Regression Suite Links
Regression suite links specify required suites per pass and per risk class.
evaluator regression suites (baseline and accel charts),
UCW regression suites (core edges/faces/loops),
bridge regression suites (BridgeSeeds and policy bridges),
RQF regression suites (Ω* certificates and atlas coherence),
ICIB regression suites (compat receipts and quarantine correctness),
portal regression suites (portal bridges and conflict ledgers).
Regression suites are versioned and referenced by receipts.
E.4.d Stress Suite Links
Stress suite links specify adversarial suites per scope.
branch boundary suites for analytic scopes,
singularity proximity suites for continuation and transforms,
ordering tie suites for canonicalization,
perturbation suites for robustness,
worst-case commutation suites for transport and atlas closure,
drift suites for determinism and replay stability.
Stress suites gate high-risk promotions and are referenced by policy and risk tags.
Appendix E — Algorithms (Snap, Tunnel, T0-OBSTR, RQF, AAS, SPP)
Appendix F — Worked Ledgers and Regression Suites
F.1 Spec
F.1.a Ledger Format
Ledgers are the extraction-optimized, proof-carrying records that define and preserve worked exemplars, their full execution traces, and their long-term maintenance obligations. A ledger is not narrative; it is a canonical object whose contents are sufficient to reconstruct and replay the exemplar’s verification outcomes under pinned contexts.
F.1.a.1 Ledger Object Classes
A worked ledger is composed of schema-typed records:
ExemplarLedger: declares the exemplar identity, target objects, required contexts, closure targets, and acceptance criteria.
RunLogBundle: binds the executed job graph, run logs, decision logs, and budget consumption.
EvidencePackBundle: binds all witness and receipt objects required for replay.
PromotionDecisionRecord: records which artifacts were promoted/activated and why (with receipts).
RefusalDecisionRecord: records refusals/quarantines and their minimal counterexamples and remediation plans.
RegressionSuiteRecord: defines the long-term tests required to preserve exemplar correctness.
StressSuiteRecord: defines adversarial tests required by exemplar risk class.
Each record is hash-addressed and linked by typed dependencies and merkle roots. All ledger records are immutable; updates create new ledger versions linked by migration edges.
F.1.a.2 Ledger Minimal Required Fields
Every ExemplarLedger must include:
exemplar_id, domain_tag, risk_class, visibility_mode.
targets: node ids, chart ids, edge ids, and closure objects (faces/loops/interior) that define the exemplar.
contexts: ConvBundleFP, policy hash, corridor hash, verifier hash, schema hashes; any context predicates must be explicit and pinned by policy.
closure_targets: explicit list of edge/face/loop/Ω* obligations required for success.
acceptance_predicates: gate stack ids and auxiliary suite requirements (stress/regression) for promotion.
budgets: budget presets for each job type and pass.
outputs: receipt pack ids, witness pack ids, run log ids, performance pack ids.
maintenance: regression suite id, stress suite id, cadence metadata (non-semantic), and rollback rules.
Ledgers that omit any of these fields are non-executable and must be refused for CI inclusion.
F.1.a.3 Run Logs and Evidence Pack Binding
A worked ledger must bind:
the job graph executed,
the deterministic run logs for each job,
the decision logs that justify route selections and escalations,
the evidence packs (UCWs, evaluation receipts, Ω*, bridge packs) sufficient for replay.
Run logs cannot be replaced by summaries. Evidence pack binding must be via pins_root and wits_root commitments with deterministic expansion indices.
F.1.a.4 Promotion and Refusal Decisions
Ledgers record decisions explicitly:
Promotion decisions must reference:
promotion receipts binding pins and witness roots,
stress/regression pass packs when required,
governance pointer updates and activation events,
rollback plans.
Refusal decisions must reference:
refusal objects with minimal failing predicates and counterexamples,
quarantines and clearance requirements,
scheduled remediation jobs or policy bridge requirements.
Promotion and refusal decisions are part of the ledger’s proof-carrying content and are replay-verifiable.
F.1.b Exemplar List
The exemplar list defines the canonical reference instances used to validate and demonstrate the framework end-to-end. Each exemplar is specified by required roots, required suites, and intended demonstration goals.
F.1.b.1 Canonical Exemplars
Exemplar ζ(3): multi-chart closure.Purpose: demonstrate baseline evaluation, accel evaluation, UCW binding, chart activation, and rollback readiness.Required roots: at least two roots; recommended full four-root coverage when feasible.Required suites: baseline regression; UCW regression; stress if branch-sensitive operators are used.
Exemplar ζ(5): Apéry acceleration (AAS).Purpose: demonstrate search/verify separation, deterministic enumeration, certified verification, transport binding to baseline, and activation gates.Required roots: baseline root + accel chart root; ideally two distinct roots plus cross-root binding.Required suites: AAS regression; stress suites if near stability boundaries.
Exemplar β(4): χ₄ acceleration.Purpose: demonstrate character-modulated template families and certified verification beyond ζ-ladders.Required roots: at least two roots; required projector stability due to normalization conventions.Required suites: regression and stress tuned to character and transform scopes.
Exemplar RQF Ω: root-quad forcing for a candidate seed.*Purpose: demonstrate atlas forcing, six edges closure, four faces closure, interior Ω* certificate, and promotion gating.Required roots: full four-root coverage.Required suites: RQF regression, interior loop stress, drift checks.
Exemplar Portal Case(s): φ / i / log A / elliptic portals.Purpose: demonstrate portal necessity proofs, conflict detection, schema extension requirements, and quarantine governance.Required roots: at least two roots sufficient to establish incompatibility; portal work may require specialized carriers.Required suites: adversarial scope stress; ICIB conflict ledgers if imported evidence participates.
Each exemplar is declared with a precise target set and closure obligations; the exemplar list is versioned and governs CI reference bundles.
F.1.b.2 Required Roots
Exemplar root requirements are explicit:
for Ω* exemplars: (S,F,C,R) required,
for acceleration exemplars: baseline root required; accel root required; binding UCWs required,
for portal exemplars: roots sufficient to show incompatibility; missing root is not permitted unless justified by portal report.
F.1.b.3 Required Suites
Each exemplar declares required suites:
regression suite id(s),
stress suite id(s),
cadence metadata (non-semantic),
required environment/toolchain pins.
Suites are part of acceptance criteria for promotion.
F.1.c Acceptance Criteria
Acceptance criteria define when an exemplar is considered correct and promotable as reference truth tissue.
F.1.c.1 Tri-Gate Margins
For each UCW in the exemplar closure set, require:
face residual maxima ≤ (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}),
spin residual maxima ≤ (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}),
projection residual maxima ≤ (\varepsilon_{\Pi}),across the required κ ladder.
Additionally, exemplar acceptance defines margin requirements:
minimal margin-to-gate thresholds for certified stability, specified by corridor preset.Margins ensure the exemplar is robust rather than barely passing.
F.1.c.2 Ω* Requirements
For Ω* exemplars:
root receipts exist for each root,
six edges have promotable UCWs,
four faces have promotable commutation UCWs,
interior Ω* certificate exists and passes replay verification,
interior loop suite is adequate and includes required adversarial probes for risk class.
Ω* acceptance is strict; partial closure yields NEAR, not OK.
F.1.c.3 Stress/Regression Gates
If exemplar risk class requires stress or regression:
regression packs must pass for the specified suite,
stress packs must pass for the specified suite,
failures trigger quarantine and rollback per governance rules.
Stress/regression are promotion gates for exemplar kits intended for reuse.
F.1.c.4 Policy Compliance
Acceptance is context-indexed:
all evidence must be admissible under pinned policy,
no heuristic evidence may appear in witness roots for promotion-critical outcomes,
no corridor widening occurs without explicit corridor identity and re-verification,
no scope gaps exist; ConvBundleFP coverage is complete.
Policy compliance is verified by CI replay.
F.1.d Failure Criteria
Failure criteria classify exemplar outcomes when acceptance criteria are not met. Failures are not ambiguous; they are structured refusal outcomes.
F.1.d.1 Plateau
Plateau occurs when residual curves or enclosure widths fail to improve with κ and remain above gates, under adequate probes and admissible repairs. Plateau yields refusal with:
plateau certificate,
minimal counterexamples,
admissible next actions (new charts, probe escalation, portal escalation).
F.1.d.2 Drift
Drift occurs when replay under fixed context yields inconsistent outcomes:
canonicalization instability,
ordering nondeterminism,
environment-dependent behavior not pinned,
ConvFP scope incompleteness.Drift yields quarantine and remediation plans; exemplar cannot be promoted as reference.
F.1.d.3 Policy Block
Policy block occurs when required verification steps are forbidden or budgets are insufficient. Policy block yields:
refusal with blocked actions,
policy bridge requirements or policy change requests,
boundary-limited status where applicable.
F.1.d.4 Convention Conflict
Convention conflict occurs when required operators are under mismatched ConvFP regimes without admissible bridges. Convention conflict yields:
conflict certificate or refusal,
bridge requirements or portal classification.
F.1.d.5 Non-Identifiability
Non-identifiability occurs when probe adequacy is insufficient or multiple candidates are indistinguishable under corridor. It yields:
probe inadequacy refusal,
required probe expansions and adversarial suite requirements.
F.1.d.6 Portal Necessity
Portal necessity occurs when closure cannot be achieved in current schema/carrier. Portal necessity yields:
portal report,
required semantic extension obligations,
quarantines preventing misuse of incomplete closure as truth.
F.2 Encode
F.2.a Route Words
Worked exemplars are encoded as WordNF bundles.
F.2.a.1 WordNF Bundle Structure
Each exemplar includes:
baseline evaluation WordNFs per root,
transport WordNFs for required edges,
face WordNFs for commutation comparisons,
interior loop suite WordNFs for Ω* exemplars,
Snap/tunnel/bridge segments when repair or acceleration is involved.
All route words include:
segment streams with explicit gate runs, refinement steps, tunnel events, splice records,
context hashes and method/bound/rounding ids,
chain hashes for tamper evidence where required.
F.2.a.2 Segment Streams
Segment streams must encode:
evaluation segments with certified receipt references,
projection segments with projector id and residual hooks,
probe generation segments with ProbeFP commitments,
UCW computation segments with gate stack id,
tunnel and splice segments with pre/post hashes and witness hooks.
Segment streams are mandatory for replay; omitted segment streams yield non-replayable exemplars.
F.2.b Witness Packs
Witness packs are merkle-rooted collections of:
UCWs: all UCWs required by closure targets.
Evaluation receipts: baseline and accel certified evaluation receipts, including bound provenance ids.
Ω certificates:* for RQF exemplars, including interior loop suite and boundary witness commitments.
Adequacy packs: probe regime definitions and adequacy metadata.
Stress packs: adversarial test results when required.
Regression packs: periodic rerun results and drift detection evidence.
Witness packs include expansion indices for deterministic replay.
F.2.c Receipt Packs
Receipt packs provide minimal replay bundles.
Pins roots: all context-defining objects (ConvFP/ConvBundleFP, corridor, policy, schemas, verifier catalog, environment/toolchain pins).
Witness roots: witness packs described above.
Schema/verifier catalogs: minimal catalogs required for decoding all objects.
Environment snapshots: required backend/toolchain pins for determinism.
Receipt packs are exportable under visibility constraints and are the unit of CI verification.
F.2.d Performance Packs
Performance packs store non-semantic performance metadata.
latency models for evaluation routes vs κ,
κ cost models and truncation depth schedules,
speedup metrics for accel charts relative to baseline,
verification overhead metrics (probe costs, UCW costs),
rollback plans: fallback charts and routes, quarantine triggers.
Performance packs are used by scheduler and dashboards, never by acceptance predicates.
F.3 Prove
F.3.a Regression Correctness
Regression correctness ensures that exemplar outcomes remain stable over time.
Re-run invariance. Rerunning the exemplar under pinned contexts and environments yields identical accept/refuse outcomes and identical canonical evidence outputs.
Drift detection. If any UCW or evaluation receipt changes outcome under fixed context, drift is detected and the exemplar is quarantined; drift logs are emitted with localization.
Quarantine correctness. Quarantine actions propagate correctly: dependent artifacts are prevented from promotion-critical routing until remediation.
Bisect readiness. Regression results include lineage pointers enabling deterministic bisection to isolate changes: schema migrations, method upgrades, corridor changes, policy changes, activation events.
Regression correctness is proven by regression packs and CI replay.
F.3.b Bisect Strategy
Bisect strategy is the deterministic algorithm for localizing failures.
Lineage bisect. Bisect across object identity lineage: which migration edge introduced difference.
Migration bisect. Bisect across schema/verifier migrations using migration receipts and translator artifacts.
Activation bisect. Bisect across chart activation and bridge activation events: isolate which activation caused failure.
Corridor/policy bisect. Bisect across corridor/policy pointer changes and compat receipts.
Bisect strategy outputs minimal failing changesets and is itself replayable.
F.3.c Stress Strategy
Stress strategy defines adversarial testing required to claim robustness.
Adversarial probes. Probes near branch cuts, singularities, ordering ties, and worst-case commutation points.
Boundary tests. Sweeps across stability region boundaries with deterministic step plans.
Perturbations. Small perturbation envelopes applied to parameters and probes; stability must hold.
Worst-case commutation tests. Probes designed to maximize face/spin/projection residuals.
Stress failures produce refusal packs with counterexamples and quarantine outcomes; stress is a promotion gate for high-risk exemplars.
F.3.d Upgrade Strategy
Upgrade strategy governs safe evolution of exemplar artifacts.
Safe upgrades. Upgrades to methods/bounds/projectors must be accompanied by migration receipts and re-verification; exemplar ledger version increments.
Safe deprecations. Deprecations of charts/bridges must preserve fallback and must not break exemplar replay; deprecation triggers regression.
Migration receipts. All upgrades produce migration receipts linking old and new ids, with re-verification evidence.
Backward witness preservation. Old exemplars remain replay-verifiable via archived verifiers or verified translators; exemplar ledgers preserve backward proof packs.
Upgrade strategy prevents loss of trust during evolution.
F.4 Extract
F.4.a Quickstart Kits
Quickstart kits are minimal bundles that reproduce exemplar results deterministically.
Each kit includes:
minimal receipt pack (pins and witness roots with indices),
required WordNF bundle,
replay script specification (verifier invocation plan),
required contexts (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP),
budgets and expected outcomes.
Quickstart kits avoid optional candidate logs and include only promotable evidence and required audit metadata.
F.4.b Reproducible Bundles
Reproducible bundles are export formats.
Export manifests: list object ids, expansion indices, schema/verifier catalogs.
Integrity proofs: bundle roots, merkle roots, chain hashes.
Boundary-limited bundles: explicit disclosure restrictions and non-assertion of external verifiability.
Exchange packs: ICIB-ready bundles including compat receipts and quarantine instructions.
Bundles are hash-addressed and support cross-site replay under pinned contexts.
F.4.c Dashboards
Dashboards are extraction artifacts for monitoring.
Exemplar health: pass/fail status for each exemplar under each supported context.
Regression health: regression suite pass rates, drift alarms, bisect readiness.
Stress health: stress suite pass rates, counterexamples, quarantines.
Drift alarms: drift incidents and scope impact.
Risk badges: risk class distribution and missing suite coverage.
Dashboards are generated deterministically from ledger and CI logs.
F.4.d Release Checklists
Release checklists define promotion and deployment gates.
promotion gates: tri-gate pass, adequacy pass, Ω* pass (if required), stress/regression pass.
CI pass: deterministic replay CI passes for canonical bundles.
security checks: integrity proofs verified; no corruption; no policy laundering; no scope gaps.
export compliance: visibility modes honored; boundary-limited bundles labeled; disclosure boundaries respected.
archival snapshot: archive proof packs, schema/verifier catalogs, environment snapshots; record archive indices.
Release checklists are deterministic and auditable; failure yields refusal and remediation plans.
Appendix F — Worked Ledgers and Regression Suites
Appendix G — Metro Data Model, Query Algebra, and Indexing
G.1 Spec
G.1.a Metro Primitives
The metro is a typed, hash-addressed, witness-bearing multigraph whose primitives are schema objects. The metro data model separates semantic objects (nodes/edges/atlases) from non-semantic indices (names, lookup tables, dashboards) while ensuring every semantic claim is backed by receipts and witnesses.
G.1.a.1 Node Types
Nodes are immutable NodeSpec objects, each with a node id derived from canonical encoding. Node types include:
Seed node: primitive generator candidate or promoted primitive; requires atlas obligations for promotion as hub.
Derived node: constructed from other nodes via derivation edges and receipts.
Candidate node: scheduled or discovered candidate pending verification and RQF.
Alias node: naming/index object referencing multiple node ids without asserting equivalence.
Portal node: semantic extension node; requires portal reports and new schema obligations.
Bridge node: reusable conversion capability packaged as BridgeSeed/BridgeRegistry entries.
Bundle node: export pack or proof slice node; auxiliary packaging.
NodeSpec includes domain tags, carrier type, weight/depth (when applicable), invariant schema keys, ConvBundleFP scope binding, and atlas pointer fields.
G.1.a.2 Edge Taxonomy
Edges are immutable EdgeSpec objects. Edge types include:
baseline: certified baseline evaluator association (chart→node).
accel: accelerated evaluator association requiring transport binding to baseline.
transport: meaning-preserving mapping, witnessed by UCWs.
reduce: basis expression with coefficient proofs and certified residual checks.
collapse: closed-form mapping into subalgebra; coefficient and representability proofs required.
derive: construction mapping for derived nodes with full receipts.
bridge: convention/policy/lens bridges with BridgeSeed/MagicCertPack and UCWs.
migrate: schema/convention/policy evolution edges with migration receipts.
dep: proof dependency edge with membership proofs when set-based.
refuse: refusal linkage to refusal artifacts and counterexamples.
quarantine/deprecate: governance edges recording lifecycle changes (non-semantic to meaning, but semantically enforced as routing constraints).
Every semantic edge type requires witness pointers and receipts; non-semantic edges must be explicitly marked as such and never treated as evidence.
G.1.a.3 Atlas Objects
An atlas is an AtlasSpec object linked to a node id. AtlasSpec defines:
root chart sets (S,F,C,R) and their certified evaluation receipts,
edge witness pointers (UCWs for root pair transports),
face witness pointers (UCWs for commutation over root triples),
interior witness pointer (Ω* certificate) when required,
chart activation states and governance pointers,
closure obligations and their satisfied status.
AtlasSpec is immutable; updates create new atlas versions linked by migration edges and governance logs.
G.1.a.4 Chart Objects
Charts are ChartSpec objects. ChartSpec includes:
root assignment, method id, bound id(s), rounding id, projector id,
parameter schema and admissibility region,
stability region and scope coverage,
required witness hooks for baseline↔accel binding,
certified evaluation receipts indexed by context.
Charts can be baseline or accel; accel charts require transport UCWs linking to baseline for activation.
G.1.a.5 Context Coverage Maps
Coverage maps are deterministic records mapping contexts to evidence availability.
For each node/edge/chart, maintain a map:[(\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},\mathrm{Policy},\mathrm{Corridor},\mathrm{Verifier}) \mapsto {\text{receipt ids}}.]
Coverage maps are indices, not semantic claims. They report the existence of receipts; they do not assert validity beyond replay.
Coverage maps drive routing and scheduling and must respect quarantine and deprecation constraints.
Coverage maps are rebuilt deterministically from receipts; they are invalidated on schema/verifier migrations and drift alarms.
G.1.b Query Algebra
The query algebra defines typed queries and deterministic query planning. Queries do not compute truth; they retrieve and assemble proof-carrying artifacts, enforcing admissibility and governance constraints.
G.1.b.1 Typed Query Classes
Canonical query classes include:
Identity queries.
GetById(type, id) retrieves canonical bytes and decoded object.
Name queries (non-authoritative).
FindByName(name, domain_tag, constraints) returns candidates with disambiguation keys and alias class references.
Structural queries.
FindByWeightDepth(w,d,domain_tag) returns nodes with matching grading and admissibility constraints.
Context queries.
FindCertified(target, context) returns receipts/UCWs that certify a target under the context.
Route planning queries.
PlanRoute(src, dst, context, constraints) returns admissible routes with required witness coverage.
Witness queries.
GetWitnesses(target, context) returns UCWs, evaluation receipts, stress/regression packs required for replay.
Coverage queries.
Coverage(target, context_predicate) returns coverage map entries and gaps.
Failure and refusal queries.
Failures(target, scope) returns refusal artifacts, plateau evidence, drift alarms, and remediation plans.
All query results are context-indexed and must respect visibility modes.
G.1.b.2 Query Constraints
Queries accept explicit constraints:
policy_hash or policy predicate,
corridor_hash or corridor family,
conv_bundle_hash or scope requirements,
risk_class and required suites,
visibility_mode and export constraints,
status filter (promoted/certified/candidate/quarantined/deprecated).
Constraints are enforced deterministically; missing constraints default to safest behavior (exclude quarantined and require promoted evidence for promotion-critical queries).
G.1.b.3 Route Query Semantics
Route planning is governed:
routes must be well-typed and context-compatible,
routes must not cross convention/policy boundaries without explicit bridges,
route candidates are ranked by non-semantic cost metadata only after legality filters,
missing evidence yields NEAR outputs with explicit witness obligations rather than unsafe guesses.
Route planning output includes:
selected path or candidate path set,
required receipts/UCWs,
blockers and missing bridges,
minimal replay bundle pointers when possible.
G.1.b.4 Witness Query Semantics
Witness queries return minimal witness sets sufficient for replay.
for each target class, compute minimal witness closure via receipt roots,
expand witness roots via indices,
include pinned context records required to interpret witnesses,
respect visibility mode: withheld pins yield boundary-limited bundles.
Witness queries are deterministic and auditable.
G.1.c Index Invariants
Indices accelerate retrieval and planning. Indices are non-semantic: they never assert equivalence or truth; they only help locate proof-carrying artifacts.
G.1.c.1 Deterministic Indices
All indices must be:
deterministically computable from object store content,
stable under canonical encoding rules,
rebuildable after compaction/migration,
invalidated on schema/verifier updates that affect interpretation.
Indices include:
name indices,
weight/depth indices,
scope indices,
context coverage indices,
witness indices,
failure indices.
G.1.c.2 Non-Semantic Metadata Separation
Index records may contain:
names, tags, synonyms,
performance metadata,
counts and timestamps (non-semantic),
selection pointers.
Index records must not contain:
un-witnessed equivalence assertions,
unverified claims,
implicit policy/corridor widening.
Any semantic statement belongs in EdgeSpec + receipts/UCWs, not in indices.
G.1.c.3 Alias Rules
Alias nodes and alias indices are permitted to group items by name or conceptual similarity, but:
alias membership is not evidence,
alias preferences are context-indexed and auditable,
alias nodes may reference equivalence edges, but equivalence edges carry the proof.
Aliases are enforced as non-semantic by schema: they cannot be consumed by verifiers as proof inputs.
G.1.c.4 Disambiguation Constraints
Disambiguation constraints are deterministic:
use domain tag, carrier type, weight/depth, ConvBundleFP prefix, policy/corridor class, id prefix,
disambiguation keys are stable and cannot include mutable fields.
Disambiguation prevents accidental merging.
G.1.d Extraction Contracts
Extraction contracts define how to produce proof slices, minimal replay bundles, and audit packets from the metro.
G.1.d.1 Proof Slicing Contract
Given a target receipt id (r), the slice exporter must produce a bundle containing:
the receipt record,
full expansion of pins_root and wits_root via expansion indices,
all referenced schema records and verifier catalog records,
any required environment/toolchain snapshots.
Slice must be minimal: removing any included object must make replay impossible.
G.1.d.2 Minimal Replay Bundle Contract
A minimal replay bundle must:
be hash-committed by a bundle manifest,
include integrity proofs (merkle roots, chain hashes),
be executable by a verifier under pinned environment.
If visibility mode prevents full export, the bundle must be marked boundary-limited and include explicit disclosure restrictions.
G.1.d.3 Audit Packet Generation Contract
Audit packets are standardized bundles:
promotion packets (for promoted nodes/edges),
refusal packets (for failures),
drift packets,
conflict packets,
migration packets,
import clearance packets.
Audit packets include replay scripts and minimal counterexamples for failures.
G.1.d.4 Visibility Boundaries
Extraction must enforce:
visibility modes and disclosure boundaries,
redaction markers and boundary-limited status,
refusal when export would leak restricted pins.
Visibility boundaries are policy-enforced and audited.
G.2 Encode
G.2.a Graph Schema
Graph schema defines canonical records for NodeSpec, EdgeSpec, AtlasSpec, and governance edges.
G.2.a.1 NodeSpec Encoding
NodeSpec includes:
node id, node type, domain tag, carrier type, weight/depth/level,
invariant schema keys and admissibility region ids,
ConvBundleFP binding and scope,
atlas pointer, closure obligations pointer,
context coverage map references (indices),
lifecycle state (as governance metadata, not identity),
optional refusal pointers and portal pointers.
Identity-critical fields are included in hash identity; mutable metadata fields are separated into governance records.
G.2.a.2 EdgeSpec Encoding
EdgeSpec includes:
edge id, edge type, src/dst ids,
context hashes (policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP/verifier),
WordNF pointers for semantic edges,
witness pointers (UCWs, receipts, stress/regression packs),
admissibility fields and required gates,
lifecycle governance pointers (quarantine/deprecate).
EdgeSpec is immutable; updates create new edges linked by migration.
G.2.a.3 AtlasSpec Encoding
AtlasSpec includes:
root charts per root and their evaluation receipts,
edge UCWs and face UCWs pointers,
Ω* pointer when present,
chart activation states and pointers,
closure obligations and satisfied status,
regression/stress suite requirements and results pointers.
AtlasSpec must commit to exact witness ids used; otherwise Ω* is not replay-verifiable.
G.2.a.4 Link-Edge and Migration Encodings
Links are encoded as typed records:
DEP, REF, EQUIV, BRIDGE, MIGRATE, QUARANTINE, DEPRECATE.
Only EQUIV links are semantic and require receipts. MIGRATE links require migration receipts. Governance links are enforceable but non-semantic.
G.2.b Index Records
Indices are separate schema objects.
G.2.b.1 Name Indices
map name → candidate ids,
include disambiguation keys and alias class references,
never assert equivalence.
G.2.b.2 Weight/Depth Indices
map (domain_tag, weight, depth) → node ids,
optionally include basis membership pointers and relation pointers,
index is deterministic and rebuildable.
G.2.b.3 Context Coverage Indices
map (target id, context hash) → receipt ids,
include status (promoted/certified/candidate/quarantined),
include missing obligations list pointers when not fully covered.
Coverage indices are built from receipts and governance logs.
G.2.b.4 Witness Indices
map (word_hash, context) → UCW ids,
map (node/chart, context) → evaluation receipt ids,
map (bridge endpoints, context) → BridgeSeed ids,
map (atlas, context) → Ω* ids.
Witness indices enable rapid retrieval for route planning and audit.
G.2.b.5 Failure Indices
map (target id, context) → refusal ids,
classify by refusal class and dominance signatures,
store minimal counterexamples pointers,
store remediation plan pointers.
Failure indices are used for scheduler prioritization and negatify maps.
G.2.c Query Plan Encoding
Queries compile to deterministic query plans represented as query ASTs.
G.2.c.1 Query AST Schema
Query AST includes:
query class id,
typed inputs and constraints,
deterministic execution plan: index lookups, filters, expansions,
required context validations and visibility checks,
expected output schema.
G.2.c.2 Deterministic Planner
Planner determinism requires:
canonical ordering of candidate retrieval,
deterministic tie-breakers,
fixed filter ordering (admissibility then risk then cost),
explicit refusal behavior on scope/policy violations.
Planner outputs a query plan record with a plan id; executing the plan yields deterministic outputs given fixed store contents.
G.2.c.3 Cost Metadata
Cost metadata is used only after legality and admissibility filtering. Cost metadata includes:
predicted evaluation cost,
witness recomputation cost,
risk overhead (stress/regression requirements).
Cost metadata is non-semantic and never used to accept claims.
G.2.c.4 Audit Binding of Query Decisions
Query execution produces audit logs:
plan id, constraints, context hashes,
indices consulted and their versions,
objects selected and filtered out, with reasons,
refusals due to visibility or scope gaps.
Audit logs are hash-addressed and support replay of query outcomes.
G.2.d Storage Coupling
Storage coupling defines how MG (graph store) references OS (object store) and how caches interact.
MG stores only ids and small indices; all payload bytes live in OS.
OS is content-addressed; mutating bytes changes ids and requires migration edges.
caches store expansion results and query results keyed by memo keys; caches are non-authoritative and invalidated on drift/migration.
invalidation dependencies include schema ids, verifier ids, corridor/policy ids, ConvBundleFP ids, and governance log versions.
Storage coupling is designed for replay integrity and compaction safety.
G.3 Prove
G.3.a Index Correctness
Index correctness ensures indices reflect store content without introducing semantic claims.
Reference integrity. Every id referenced by an index must exist in OS or be explicitly declared external.
Stable ordering. Index entries must be ordered deterministically; any nondeterminism yields invalid index state and triggers rebuild.
No semantic claims. Indices must not assert equivalence; only point to semantic edges (EQUIV) that carry receipts.
Deterministic recomputation. Indices must be rebuildable deterministically from OS and governance logs; rebuild produces identical canonical bytes and ids under pinned canon_hash.
Index correctness is audited by CI.
G.3.b Query Soundness
Query soundness ensures queries return only admissible artifacts under a context.
Context enforcement. Queries that request certified/promoted outputs must filter to receipts whose context hashes match the requested context or are bridged by explicit bridge receipts.
Blocked edges excluded. Quarantined or policy-blocked edges are excluded from certified route plans unless the query explicitly requests candidate evidence.
Visibility enforcement. Restricted/private objects are returned only if the query mode permits; otherwise results are boundary-limited or refused.
Refusal correctness. If no admissible result exists, query must return refusal with blocker set (missing bridges, missing roots, policy blocks) rather than returning unsafe approximations.
Query soundness ensures planning and extraction do not create fake validity.
G.3.c Slice Minimality
Slice minimality ensures proof slices are neither bloated nor missing dependencies.
Minimality condition. A slice is minimal iff removing any object breaks replay verification.
Merkle expansion correctness. Slice exporters must expand pins/wits roots via indices and verify merkle roots match.
Replay sufficiency. Slice contains all schema/verifier catalogs and environment pins required for deterministic replay.
Boundary-limited behavior. If visibility constraints prevent minimal slice completeness, exporter must produce boundary-limited slice with explicit limitations.
Slice minimality is required for exchange packs and audit packs.
G.3.d Alias Safety
Alias safety ensures aliasing does not collapse meaning.
Alias nodes and name indices are non-semantic; they cannot be consumed as proof.
Equivalence is asserted only by EQUIV edges with UCWs and receipts.
Conflict preservation: aliasing does not delete conflicts; conflicts remain in conflict ledgers and quarantines.
Planner safety: route planning may use alias nodes to suggest candidates but must validate by receipts and UCWs before use.
Alias safety is enforced by schema and verified by CI property tests.
G.4 Extract
G.4.a Query Cookbook
The query cookbook is a library of deterministic query templates.
Common lookup recipes: by id; by name with disambiguation; by weight/depth; by domain tag.
Route planning recipes: cheapest certified path; fastest certified evaluator; hub routing; fallback path; risk-aware route.
Coverage audit recipes: missing roots; missing UCWs; missing Ω*; policy block reports; corridor coverage gaps.
Diff recipes: atlas diffs; corridor diffs; bridge diffs; migration diffs; import diffs.
Cookbook entries include required constraints and expected outputs; each is addressable as a QueryPlan template.
G.4.b Slice Exporters
Slice exporters are deterministic tools.
slice-by-goal: minimal bundle for a demanded claim (eval/equiv/promote/bridge/import).
slice-by-node: all evidence for a node: atlas, charts, receipts, UCWs, refusals.
slice-by-edge: all evidence for an edge: WordNF, UCWs, receipts, repair/tunnel logs.
slice-by-policy: artifacts admissible under a policy; includes policy blocks and bridge needs.
slice-by-scope: artifacts covered by a scope; identifies scope gaps and required ConvFP expansions.
Each exporter produces a bundle manifest, integrity proofs, and visibility compliance declarations.
G.4.c Dashboards
Dashboards are extraction products built from indices and receipts.
Coverage dashboards: root coverage per node; UCW coverage per edge; Ω* coverage; context coverage maps.
Bottleneck dashboards: missing bridges; policy blocks; plateau regions; probe inadequacy regions; portal candidates.
Failure maps: negatify maps; refusal class distributions; counterexample hotspots; regression/stress failure hotspots.
Risk overlays: risk badges; missing stress/regression coverage; quarantined active artifacts.
Dashboards are deterministic and audit-linked to underlying receipts/logs.
G.4.d Metro Station Codes
Station codes are deterministic human-readable identifiers for navigation.
Station code format.<domain_tag>::<class>::<key>::<id_prefix>where class ∈ {node,edge,atlas,chart,bridge,wit,rcpt} and key includes weight/depth if present.
Hub codes. Special station codes for hubs:
primary hubs: Fourier/Derivative/Black–Scholes transfer hubs (if used in the domain),
metro hubs: nodes with high witness coverage and high routing centrality.
Line codes. Functional line labels:
Eval line, UCW line, RQF line, AAS line, SPP line, ICIB line, CI line.
Interchange codes. Interchanges are points where multiple lines meet: major bridge nodes, major atlas hubs, major policy/corridor hubs.
Station codes are indices only; they do not assert equivalence. They are used for extraction, navigation, and memory mapping.
Appendix G — Metro Data Model, Query Algebra, and Indexing
Appendix H — Gate Stack Catalog and Legality Predicate Library
H.1 Spec
H.1.a Predicate Taxonomy
Legality is decided by a deterministic library of predicates evaluated in a fixed order (“gate stacks”). Predicates are typed, scope-aware, and context-indexed. A predicate consumes pinned records and evidence and returns a structured outcome with required artifacts and counterexamples on failure.
H.1.a.1 Predicate Classes
The canonical predicate classes are:
Typing predicates (TY).Validate schema decodability, lens typing, carrier typing, admissibility membership, parameter typing.
Scope predicates (SC).Validate ConvBundleFP coverage of operator families invoked; validate schema scope coverage; validate absence of scope gaps.
Policy predicates (PO).Validate policy admissibility: allowed operator families, allowed methods/bounds/rounding, budget ceilings, disclosure boundaries.
Corridor predicates (CO).Validate corridor applicability: gate definitions, κ ladder binding, probe regime binding, residual functional identifiers.
Preservation predicates (PR).Validate invariant preservation obligations declared by lens schemas or transport signatures: predicate preservation, observable coherence.
Representability predicates (RP).Validate projection/canonicalization stability under (\Pi_h) and relevant projection residual gates; validate projector scope and commutation requirements.
Commutation predicates (CM).Validate face commutation: UCW face residual gate satisfaction for route pairings and face paths; enforce adequate probes.
Holonomy predicates (HO).Validate loop closure: UCW spin residual gates, loop map signatures, monodromy/boundary probe requirements.
Replay predicates (RE).Validate replay sufficiency: pins and witness roots expandable, WordNF decodable, environment/toolchain pins satisfied, deterministic recomputation possible.
Budget predicates (BU).Validate budget compliance and termination: caps respected, no unbounded loops, exhaustion evidence when failing.
Predicates may be hard (context/typing/scope/policy) or soft (gates on residuals). Hard failures are refused immediately; soft failures yield NEAR only when repair is admissible under policy and budgets.
H.1.a.2 Predicate Outcomes
Every predicate returns a structured outcome:
PASS(evidence_refs),
FAIL(refusal_id, counterexample_id, evidence_refs),
NEAR(next_actions, evidence_refs) (only for soft predicates under strict constraints).
NEAR is never promotable evidence and must include explicit next actions that are admissible and bounded.
H.1.a.3 Context Indexing
Predicates are evaluated under pinned context hashes:[X := (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V,\Sigma).]Predicate outcomes are meaningful only under (X). Any attempt to reuse a predicate outcome under a different context requires explicit bridges and re-verification.
H.1.b Gate Stacks
A gate stack is a deterministic evaluation order of predicates, tailored by task class and risk class. Gate stacks are schema objects referenced by receipts and verifiers.
H.1.b.1 Standard Stack Order
The canonical baseline stack order is:
TY — typing and schema validity,
SC — scope coverage,
PO — policy admissibility,
CO — corridor applicability,
PR — invariant preservation,
RP — representability stability,
CM — commutation gates (if applicable),
HO — holonomy gates (if applicable),
RE — replay sufficiency,
BU — budgets and termination.
This order is mandatory for promotion-critical claims. Alternative orders are permitted only if they are proven equivalent under verifier invariance; otherwise they are forbidden.
H.1.b.2 Per-Target-Class Stacks
Target class stacks specialize the standard stack:
EVAL stack: TY → SC → PO → CO → RP → RE → BU
CM/HO appear only if evaluation participates in UCW or closure tests.
UCW stack: TY → SC → PO → CO → RP → CM → HO → RE → BU
Both CM and HO are mandatory; RP cannot be skipped.
PROMOTE stack (RQF/Ω):* TY → SC → PO → CO → RP → CM → HO → RE → BU plus auxiliary gates (stress/regression) as required by risk.
BRIDGE stack: TY → SC → PO → CO → RP → CM → HO → RE → BU plus stress/regression required by bridge kind.
IMPORT clearance stack (ICIB): TY → SC → PO → CO → RE → BU plus compat predicates and quarantine checks; CM/HO required when equivalence claims are imported.
REFUSAL validation stack: TY → SC → PO → CO → RE → BU plus refusal correctness checks.
Each stack declares its required inputs and required witness types.
H.1.b.3 Per-Risk-Class Stacks
Risk classes add auxiliary gates:
Low risk: base stack only; regression optional unless policy mandates.
Medium risk: regression mandatory for promotion; perturbation checks optional.
High risk: regression mandatory; stress mandatory; perturbation robustness mandatory; adversarial boundary probes mandatory when scope is boundary-sensitive.
Risk-class augmentation is deterministic and encoded in stack specs; it is not a runtime choice.
H.1.b.4 Auxiliary Gates
Auxiliary gates include:
STRESS_PASS: required stress packs and adversarial probe outcomes,
REGRESSION_PASS: required regression packs,
PERTURB_PASS: perturbation robustness checks,
DRIFT_CLEAR: drift alarm absence and determinism audit pass.
Auxiliary gates are treated as predicates with explicit inputs and outputs; they are never implicit.
H.1.c Residual Functionals
Residual functionals define how discrepancies are measured. Residual functionals are typed and pinned by corridor.
H.1.c.1 Face Residuals
Face residual functionals (\rho_{\text{face}}) compare outputs of two routes.
scalar: interval difference normalized by declared scale,
vector: norm of difference in declared norm,
function: supremum over probe set of pointwise residuals,
operator: operator norm or spectral residual.
Face residual is computed as κ-indexed maxima over probes.
H.1.c.2 Spin Residuals
Spin residual functionals (\rho_{\text{spin}}) measure loop closure defect.
defined on loop map (L_{\square}) acting on probe carriers,
computed as κ-indexed maxima over probes,
boundary probes required for monodromy-sensitive scopes.
Spin residual failure under stable face indicates holonomy obstruction; classification depends on dominance logic.
H.1.c.3 Projection Residuals
Projection residual functionals (\rho_{\Pi}) measure representability stability under projector (\Pi_h).
projection residual computed on probes and κ grid,
commutation with transports may be required by schema; failures classified separately.
Projection residual is mandatory for promotion-critical claims; skipping representability is forbidden.
H.1.c.4 Auxiliary Residuals
Aux residuals may include:
perturbation sensitivity residuals,
regression delta residuals,
stress adversary maxima,
cost residuals (non-semantic).
Aux residuals are admissible only if:
corridor declares them,
policy requires them,
schema defines their normalization.
H.1.c.5 Normalization Rules
Residual normalization rules are pinned:
scaling factors,
norm selection,
reference value selection,
unit conventions.
Normalization is convention-sensitive when it affects meaning; otherwise it is corridor/policy sensitive. Any mismatch yields refusal.
H.1.d Refusal Classes
Refusal classes are the standardized failure outputs of predicates and stacks. Each refusal is tied to minimal failing predicate mapping and counterexample requirements.
H.1.d.1 Minimal Failing Predicate Mapping
Refusal generation follows:
if any hard predicate fails (TY/SC/PO/CO), refusal class maps directly:
TY → TYPE_FAIL or CTX_RESOLVE_FAIL,
SC → SCOPE_GAP or CONV_CONFLICT,
PO → POLICY_BLOCK,
CO → CORRIDOR_FAIL.
if hard predicates pass, identify dominant soft gate failure:
CM fails → KERNEL_MISMATCH unless HO dominates,
HO fails with CM passing → HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION,
RP fails → REPRESENTABILITY_FAIL (or DRIFT if unstable),
adequacy fails → PROBE_INADEQUATE,
plateau signatures → PLATEAU,
replay failure → REPLAY_FAIL or BINDING_MISMATCH,
budget exhaustion → BUDGET_EXHAUSTED.
Dominance rules are deterministic and must be included in predicate specs.
H.1.d.2 Refusal Payload Requirements
A refusal payload must include:
refusal class token,
minimal failing predicate id,
minimal counterexample object id,
context hashes,
budgets consumed and remaining,
best-achieved residual curves and margins,
admissible next actions (finite, deterministic, policy-respecting),
quarantine semantics when needed.
H.1.d.3 Counterexample Object Requirements
Counterexample objects must include:
minimal κ where failure occurs,
minimal probe(s) realizing maximal residual,
minimal subword or segment subset when applicable,
evidence pointers (WordNF hashes, curve values),
recommended remediation steps tied to admissible actions.
Counterexamples are replay-verifiable and part of refusal correctness.
H.2 Encode
H.2.a Predicate Specs
Each predicate has a canonical PredicateSpec record.
Fields include:
pred_id and schema version,
required inputs (object ids and context requirements),
required pins (which records must be present),
required witnesses (UCWs, eval receipts, packs) if predicate consumes prior evidence,
outputs: PASS/FAIL/NEAR with required artifacts,
determinism constraints (no randomness; pinned ordering; environment pins),
refusal mapping rules and counterexample generation method id,
budget fields (if predicate has internal bounded search).
PredicateSpec is hash-addressed and referenced by gate stack specs.
H.2.b Gate Stack Specs
GateStackSpec is a canonical record.
Fields include:
stack_id and schema version,
ordered list of pred_ids,
κ ladder binding: κ ladder id and explicit κ list or reference,
probe regime binding: ProbeFP id, adequacy pack requirements,
thresholds binding: gate ids and numeric thresholds (or corridor reference),
context binding requirements (policy classes allowed, corridor classes allowed),
required auxiliary suites by risk class,
deterministic short-circuit rules: early-exit conditions and refusal precedence.
GateStackSpec must be sufficient for verifiers to reproduce evaluation order and outcomes.
H.2.c Counterexample Schema
Counterexample objects are schema-typed.
Fields include:
cex_id, pred_id, refusal_class,
context_hashes and word_hash references,
minimal κ and probe references,
minimal subword/segment subset reference when applicable,
evidence pointers: residual values, enclosure diffs, scope gaps, policy forbiddances,
admissible next actions with preconditions,
minimization trace: deterministic method used to obtain minimality.
Counterexample schema is essential for reproducible debugging.
H.2.d Predicate Logs
Predicate logs record evaluation traces.
Legality traces: ordered list of predicate outcomes with evidence pointers.
Decision logs: for NEAR, record action selection rationale and expected defect decrease.
Replay logs: record recomputation checks and hash validations performed during replay.
Bisect hooks: record lineage pointers and candidate causes for failures.
Predicate logs are immutable and referenced by receipts and audit packs.
H.3 Prove
H.3.a Soundness of Legality
Legality soundness ensures that passing a stack implies all required obligations hold under the pinned context, and failing yields honest refusal.
Hard-fail ordering. Typing/scope/policy/corridor failures are detected before any semantic gate evaluation; no semantic gate is evaluated under invalid contexts.
No hidden semantics. Scope predicate ensures ConvBundleFP coverage and forbids implicit operator semantics.
No policy laundering. Policy predicate blocks the use of evidence under incompatible policies unless explicit policy bridges exist and are verified.
Refusal correctness. Refusal payloads contain minimal counterexamples and exhaustive evidence of why acceptance was impossible under constraints.
Soundness is maintained by verifier replay and CI.
H.3.b Compositional Legality
Compositional legality governs route composition and witness chaining.
composition is permitted only when intermediate types unify and contexts match or are bridged explicitly,
additional boundary obligations are enumerated: new commutation squares and new loops introduced by composition must be witnessed,
loop closure inheritance: composing certified edges may introduce cycles that require holonomy witnesses; inheritance rules are explicit,
receipts for composed claims bind the union of pins and witness roots plus new required witnesses.
Compositional legality prevents building “large proofs” from incompatible fragments.
H.3.c Partial Legality (NEAR)
NEAR is a formal intermediate result state.
Meaning. NEAR indicates hard predicates pass but one or more soft predicates fail by gate margin, and admissible repairs remain.
Non-promotability. NEAR cannot be used as evidence for semantic claims; it may be used only for scheduling and remediation.
Admissible next actions. NEAR must include a finite list of admissible repair actions (κ escalation, probe expansion, method swap, Snap, tunnel) each with preconditions and bounded budgets.
Termination rules. NEAR must terminate: repeated NEAR outcomes must progress via defect decrease or end in FAIL with refusal on budget exhaustion.
NEAR semantics are enforced by Snap and scheduler governance.
H.3.d Drift vs Plateau
Classification correctness requires distinguishing drift from plateau.
Cross-κ consistency. Plateau is stable non-improvement across κ; drift is inconsistency under fixed context and expected monotonic tightening.
Determinism audits. Drift triggers include ordering instability, canonicalization non-idempotence, environment mismatch not pinned.
Classification correctness. If probes are inadequate, classification is PROBE_INADEQUATE, not plateau. If probes adequate and residual stable above gates, plateau is valid; if residual flips or changes non-monotonically, drift is indicated.
Remediation correctness. Plateau yields method/probe/carry escalation plans; drift yields ConvFP/method/env pin remediation and quarantines.
Drift vs plateau is a core safety boundary in the proof system.
H.4 Extract
H.4.a Gate Stack Reference
Gate stack reference tables are extraction artifacts.
stacks per task class (EVAL/UCW/PROMOTE/BRIDGE/IMPORT),
stacks per risk class (low/medium/high) with auxiliary gates,
required probes and adequacy packs per stack,
required κ ladders and thresholds per stack,
thresholds table listing (\varepsilon_{\text{face}},\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\varepsilon_{\Pi}) and any aux gates.
Gate stack references are versioned and included in verifier catalogs.
H.4.b Refusal Handbook
Refusal handbook maps refusal classes to typical causes and remediation templates.
for each refusal class, list:
minimal failing predicate,
typical counterexample patterns,
admissible next actions,
required bridges or portal triggers.
include policy-block remediation: policy bridge templates and governance workflows,
include scope-gap remediation: ConvFP expansions and convention bridges,
include plateau remediation: AAS template calls, probe expansions, carrier escalations, T0-OBSTR triggers,
include drift remediation: environment pin updates, method migrations, determinism fixes.
Handbook entries are deterministic and used for scheduler remediation planning.
H.4.c Audit Macros
Audit macros are standardized trace templates.
Legality trace macro: evaluate stack; record PASS/FAIL per predicate; output minimal failing predicate.
Commutation audit macro: compute UCW for route pair; produce residual curves; output why-equivalent/why-failed report.
Loop audit macro: compute loop UCW; compute loop map signature; output holonomy summary and obstruction triggers.
Replay audit macro: expand pins/wits roots; recompute evidence; verify hashes; output integrity proofs.
Audit macros are used by CI and audit packet generators.
H.4.d Predicate Coverage Reports
Coverage reports summarize which predicates and gate stacks are supported per domain.
list supported predicate ids and their schema versions,
list unsupported predicates and whether they require portal semantics or new carrier support,
list missing predicate alerts: where a domain attempts to use a predicate but no implementation exists,
list required upgrades and timelines: new residual functionals, new probe regimes, new projector types.
Coverage reports drive roadmap and prevent silent skipping of required legality checks.
Appendix H — Gate Stack Catalog and Legality Predicate Library
Appendix I — Tri-Lock, Holonomy Suites, and Loop Witness Engineering
I.1 Spec
I.1.a Tri-Lock Definition
Tri-Lock is the canonical verification discipline that binds semantic equivalence to three orthogonal constraints: commutation, holonomy, and representability. Tri-Lock is enforced by UCW theory and by gate stacks; it is the minimal guardrail preventing false equivalences, path-dependent semantics, and representation artifacts.
I.1.a.1 The Three Locks
Let (X := (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP},P,C,V,\Sigma)) be the pinned context and let (\mathcal K) be the corridor κ ladder with probe regime (\mathcal P(\kappa)). Tri-Lock requires:
Face-lock (commutation).For each required commutation square (route pairing) ((W_A,W_B)), the κ-indexed face residual maxima satisfy:[\forall \kappa\in\mathcal K:\quad r_{\square}(\kappa)\le \varepsilon_{\text{face}}.]Face-lock ensures that alternative routes agree on an adequate probe family.
Spin-lock (holonomy).For each required loop word (\ell) inducing loop map (L_\ell), the κ-indexed spin residual maxima satisfy:[\forall \kappa\in\mathcal K:\quad s_{\ell}(\kappa)\le \varepsilon_{\text{spin}}.]Spin-lock ensures that route compositions close as intended and that meaning is not path-dependent.
Projection-lock (representability).For projector (\Pi_h) required by schema and context, the κ-indexed projection residual maxima satisfy:[\forall \kappa\in\mathcal K:\quad r_{\Pi}(\kappa)\le \varepsilon_{\Pi}.]Projection-lock ensures that computed results belong to stable canonical meaning classes and are not artifacts of representation.
I.1.a.2 Gate Interactions
Tri-Lock is evaluated as a coupled system; failures are classified by dominance.
Face passes, spin fails. Holonomy-dominant signature. Indicates structural loop obstruction (Tier-0 candidate or portal boundary) if stable under refinement and invariant under admissible chart substitutions.
Face fails, spin passes. Route disagreement signature. Indicates missing transport evidence, incorrect equivalence claim, insufficient chart binding, or inadequate commutation witnesses.
Projection fails. Representability failure signature. Indicates projector instability, canonicalization drift, or scope/convention mismatch affecting normalization; must be resolved before commutation and holonomy are interpreted as semantic.
Mixed failures. Multiple locks fail with no clear dominance; requires localization to identify whether failure arises from scope/policy mismatch, probe inadequacy, or method instability.
Tri-Lock never permits “pass two locks, ignore third.” Projection-lock is mandatory for promotion-critical claims; skipping it is forbidden.
I.1.a.3 Context Binding Invariants
Tri-Lock is meaningful only under fixed context hashes. Invariants:
all locks are evaluated under the same ConvBundleFP scope and policy/corridor regime,
probe regime is pinned and adequate for the scope,
projector id and rounding ids are pinned and stable,
any convention changes require explicit convention bridges; any policy changes require explicit policy bridges,
any corridor changes require explicit corridor identity changes and re-verification.
Any unbridged context mismatch invalidates Tri-Lock claims and yields refusal.
I.1.b Loop Suite Families
A loop suite is a finite set of loop words whose closure properties diagnose structural consistency. Loop suites are domain-dependent and risk-sensitive.
I.1.b.1 Normalization Loops
Normalization loops test whether canonicalization commutes with evaluation and transport.
apply evaluation → projection → inverse evaluation route (when defined) vs projection-first route,
test idempotence and commutation of (\Pi_h) with key transforms,
detect canonicalization drift and ordering nondeterminism.
Normalization loops are required for any domain with nontrivial canonical forms.
I.1.b.2 Transform Inversion Loops
Transform inversion loops test whether forward/inverse transforms commute under declared conventions and bounds.
Fourier/Laplace/Mellin forward→inverse loops,
normalization and scaling convention sensitivity detection,
tail-bound correctness detection through loop residual behavior.
Transform inversion loops are high-risk and require boundary probes if transforms involve branch-sensitive operators.
I.1.b.3 Branch/Monodromy Loops
Branch/monodromy loops test analytic continuation and branch-cut semantics.
loop around singularities in parameter space,
check monodromy consistency with declared continuation policies,
detect convention mismatch vs intrinsic monodromy obstructions.
Branch loops are intrinsically high-risk and require adversarial boundary probes and perturbation suites.
I.1.b.4 Basis-Change Loops
Basis-change loops test whether basis selection and ordering yields stable meaning.
basis change (B\to B') then return (B'\to B),
verify coefficients remain stable under projection and ordering rules,
detect tie-breaker nondeterminism and basis drift.
Basis-change loops are central in SPP relation search and in reduction proofs.
I.1.b.5 Corridor Loops
Corridor loops test that tightening corridors strengthens evidence without changing semantics.
re-run UCWs under tighter corridors and compare outcomes,
verify no silent widening or policy laundering,
detect whether previously accepted claims are fragile or robust under stronger gates.
Corridor loops are governance loops and are used by regression suites.
I.1.c Loop Map Semantics
Loop maps formalize the semantic effect of traversing a loop word in the metro. A loop map is not an abstract function; it is an executable, pinned program determined by WordNF segment stream, projectors, and context.
I.1.c.1 Loop Map Signatures
Each loop word (\ell) defines a loop map signature:[\mathrm{LoopSig}(\ell) := (\mathrm{word_hash},;\mathrm{carrier_id},;\Pi_h,;\rho_{\text{spin}},;\mathcal K,;\mathrm{ProbeFP},;\mathrm{scope},;\mathrm{ctx}).]Loop signatures are hash-addressed and include all required schema ids.
I.1.c.2 Carrier Dependence
Loop maps are carrier-dependent.
on scalar carriers, loop maps act on scalar probe values or scalar parameters and compare via scalar residual functionals,
on function carriers, loop maps act on function evaluations at probe points,
on operator carriers, loop maps act on spectra or operator actions on probe vectors.
Carrier dependence must be explicit in loop map signatures; otherwise loop interpretation is ambiguous and must be refused.
I.1.c.3 Projector Coupling
Loop maps incorporate projection/canonicalization to enforce representability.
projectors (\Pi_h) are applied at schema-declared points in the loop word,
projector commutation with transforms must be witnessed where required,
projection residuals must be tracked; if projection fails, loop results are non-interpretable and must be refused.
Projector coupling prevents loops from “closing” only due to non-canonical representations.
I.1.c.4 Monodromy Handling Rules
When loop suites are branch/monodromy-sensitive:
continuation policy must be explicit and pinned by ConvFP scope,
probe regimes must include boundary probes near branch cuts and singularities,
loop traversal must record path class and continuation semantics; ambiguous continuation yields refusal,
conflict detection distinguishes:
convention-induced monodromy mismatch (requires convention bridge),
intrinsic monodromy (portal necessity).
Monodromy handling is enforced by policy and stress suites.
I.1.d Holonomy Dominance Criteria
Holonomy dominance is the condition under which persistent loop failures are interpreted as structural obstructions rather than route disagreement or numeric error.
I.1.d.1 Separation from Face Failures
A failure is holonomy-dominant only if:
face-lock is satisfied for the relevant transport/equivalence edges, or face failures are localized and repaired such that remaining failures are in spin-lock,
projection-lock is satisfied or projection failures are resolved; otherwise spin evidence is not meaningful.
If face or projection failures dominate, the system must first resolve transport/projection issues rather than extract obstructions.
I.1.d.2 Stability Criteria
Holonomy dominance requires stability:
spin residual curves plateau stably above gates across κ ladder,
plateau persists under admissible probe expansions that satisfy adequacy,
plateau persists under admissible chart substitutions within the same semantic regime (same ConvBundleFP and policy),
plateau persists under perturbation probes within stability regions.
Without stability, failures are classified as uncertainty or drift.
I.1.d.3 Tier-0 Triggers
Tier-0 triggers occur when:
holonomy dominance holds,
the obstruction appears scalar-compressible into a modulus interval under the domain’s obstruction mapping,
extracted candidate obstruction interval is stable and supported by a minimal loop suite,
convention and policy invariance tests pass within scope.
Tier-0 triggers schedule T0-OBSTR and then RQF forcing of the candidate modulus.
I.1.d.4 Portal Triggers
Portal triggers occur when:
holonomy dominance holds but obstruction cannot be represented in the current modulus space under current schema,
conflict proofs persist across admissible bridges, indicating intrinsic incompatibility,
monodromy evidence indicates topological obstruction requiring a new carrier or new observables,
closure fails systematically in root-quad forcing with portal classification.
Portal triggers produce portal reports and quarantine prevent misinterpretation as Tier-0 scalar constants.
I.2 Encode
I.2.a Loop Suite Records
Loop suites are schema-typed objects.
Fields include:
loop_suite_id, domain_tag, risk_class, scope_id,
ordered list of loop ids, each loop id binding a WordNF hash and context constraints,
admissibility constraints (stability regions, policy constraints),
required probes and κ ladders (ProbeFP ids and κ ladder ids),
minimal loop suite extraction rule id and resulting minimal suite ids,
expected outputs: required UCWs and refusal classes.
Loop suites are versioned; changes require migration and regression updates.
I.2.b Loop Map Records
Loop map records encode executable loop semantics.
Fields include:
loop map signature id (LoopSig hash),
required operators and operator catalog ids,
projector ids and projection insertion points,
probe regimes and adequacy requirements,
κ bindings and evaluation budgets,
deterministic execution fields (no randomness; environment pins where required).
Loop maps are replay-addressable via WordNF and segment streams.
I.2.c Spin Residual Packs
Spin residual packs store evidence for holonomy.
Fields include:
loop id and loop map signature id,
κ-indexed spin residual curve and margins to (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}),
boundary probe outcomes (when required),
perturbation outcomes (when required),
supporting face and projection residual summaries to establish holonomy dominance,
classification tag and stability metrics.
Spin residual packs are witness objects used by T0-OBSTR and RQF.
I.2.d Holonomy Refusal Packs
Holonomy refusal packs are refusal objects specialized for holonomy failures.
Fields include:
refusal class (HOLONOMY_OBSTRUCTION or UNCERTAINTY_PLATEAU or CONVENTION_CONFLICT),
minimal failing loop(s) and minimal counterexample probes,
stability evidence and invariance evidence (or failure thereof),
next actions:
run T0-OBSTR,
run RQF with candidate modulus,
escalate probes,
attempt tunnel to alternative charts,
portal report creation.
Holonomy refusal packs block unsafe promotion and guide deterministic escalation.
I.3 Prove
I.3.a Loop Closure Theorems
Loop closure theorems characterize correctness of loops under Tri-Lock.
I.3.a.1 Closure Under Tri-Gate
If a loop UCW passes tri-gate under corridor (C), then:
commutation constraints and representability constraints are satisfied under pinned context,
loop traversal does not accumulate semantic discrepancy beyond (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}) across κ ladder.
This yields safe use of loop closure in atlas coherence and bridge chaining.
I.3.a.2 Stability Under Refinement
If loop closure holds at κ ladder and remains stable under κ strengthening, then closure is robust within the corridor regime. If closure fails at stronger κ, then prior closure was fragile; classification is either non-identifiability (probe inadequacy) or drift.
I.3.a.3 Projection Commutation Obligations
For loops involving projection insertion points, projection must commute with transports and evaluation where required by schema. This commutation is witnessed by UCWs. Failure of projection commutation invalidates loop closure interpretations and yields representability refusal.
I.3.b Monodromy Safety
Monodromy safety ensures correct interpretation of branch-sensitive loops.
boundary probes are required and adequacy must include boundary coverage,
continuation policy is pinned by ConvFP; any mismatch yields convention conflict,
conflict detection distinguishes:
mismatch due to conventions (bridgeable),
intrinsic monodromy (portal required).
Monodromy safety prevents mislabeling convention drift as mathematical obstruction.
I.3.c Holonomy Compositionality
Holonomy compositionality governs how loop witnesses compose across graph cycles.
composing loop witnesses across adjacent cycles yields derived loop witnesses only when context hashes unify and intermediate projection and commutation constraints hold,
boundary-to-interior inheritance: in RQF, closed faces define interior loop suites; interior closure is meaningful only under established boundary closures.
Holonomy compositionality enables Ω* interior coherence certificates and prevents path-dependent equivalence classes.
I.3.d Obstruction Validity
Obstruction validity is the proof obligation that extracted obstructions are not artifacts.
Non-artifact criteria: stability across κ, probes, method substitutions; invariance under fixed conventions and policies; absence of drift.
Cross-chart persistence: obstruction signature persists across independent charts and roots where available, linked by transport UCWs.
Adversarial checks: boundary probes and perturbation suites do not eliminate the obstruction; if they do, obstruction is artifact and rejected.
Obstruction validity is the foundation for Tier-0 candidate proposal and subsequent RQF forcing.
I.4 Extract
I.4.a Loop Suite Catalog
Loop suite catalog is an extraction index of available suites by domain.
For each suite:
suite id, domain tag, risk class,
required probe regimes and κ ladders,
required operator scopes and ConvFP scope coverage,
admissibility regions and boundary requirements,
recommended corridors and policies.
Catalog entries are used by scheduler and CI.
I.4.b Holonomy Dashboards
Holonomy dashboards summarize holonomy evidence and hotspots.
loops with spin residual failures above gates,
holonomy dominance classification confidence,
stable obstruction candidates and their support loops,
portal candidates and conflict evidence,
blocked loops due to policy/scope constraints and required bridges.
Dashboards drive prioritization of T0-OBSTR, RQF, and portal work.
I.4.c Loop Audit Scripts
Loop audit scripts are minimal replay procedures.
expand pins and witness roots,
reconstruct loop WordNF and loop map signature,
regenerate probes and recompute spin residual curves across κ,
validate tri-gate acceptance or refusal classification,
emit integrity proofs and counterexample objects on failure.
Additionally, for Ω*:
interior loop suite scripts derived from atlas boundary closure,
replay scripts to verify Ω* certificate.
Loop audit scripts are used by CI and audit packets.
I.4.d Repair Templates
Repair templates are deterministic remediation macros focused on holonomy failures.
Holonomy-focused Snap macros: attempt projection stabilization, complete missing commutation edges, expand boundary probes, then re-evaluate spin residuals.
Tunnel triggers: if holonomy is suspected to be representation artifact, attempt admissible tunnels to alternate charts that are transport-bound to baseline; record tunnel events and require loop closure re-verification.
Escalation decision tree:
if face or projection fails: repair those first,
if holonomy-dominant and stable: trigger T0-OBSTR,
if T0 candidates fail RQF: portal report,
if policy blocks: policy bridge demand.
Repair templates output explicit job plans and refusal outcomes when blocked.
Appendix I — Tri-Lock, Holonomy Suites, and Loop Witness Engineering
Appendix J — Bridge Algebra, CorrEq Certificates, and Migration Semantics
J.1 Spec
J.1.a Bridge Algebra
Bridge algebra is the formal system governing bridges as typed morphisms between contexts, lenses, charts, policies, and corridors. A bridge is a reusable capability represented as a BridgeSeed with replay-verifiable evidence (Ch17). Bridge algebra defines composition, legality, identity bridges, and optional inverse/adjoint notions under explicit conditions.
J.1.a.1 Bridge Kinds and Endpoint Types
Bridges are typed by kind and endpoints. Canonical bridge kinds:
LENS_BRIDGE: (L_i \to L_j), representation change between lenses.
CHART_BRIDGE: (\mathsf{Chart}_a \leftrightarrow \mathsf{Chart}_b) with transport binding.
CONV_BRIDGE: (\mathrm{ConvFP}_1 \to \mathrm{ConvFP}_2) on declared scope.
POLICY_BRIDGE: (P_1 \to P_2) for evidence admissibility translation.
CORRIDOR_BRIDGE: (C_1 \to C_2) compatibility mapping when permissible.
PORTAL_BRIDGE: (S_1 \to S_2) across semantic regimes requiring portal semantics.
META_BRIDGE: composite bridges (chains treated as a single capability).
Endpoints are typed references: lens ids, chart ids, ConvFP ids, policy ids, corridor ids, schema regimes. Endpoints must unify for composition.
J.1.a.2 Typed Composition
Given bridges (B_1: A \to B) and (B_2: B \to C), typed composition (B_2 \circ B_1: A \to C) is defined only when:
endpoint types unify exactly,
scope constraints are compatible (operator-family scope and carrier scope),
context constraints unify or are bridged explicitly.
Composition yields a chain object and may be promoted as META_BRIDGE only when compositional evidence is complete.
J.1.a.3 Chain Legality
A chain is legal iff:
each component bridge is promotable under the chain context or is itself bridged to the chain context by explicit policy/convention bridges,
intermediate representations satisfy representability obligations; projection stability is maintained across boundaries,
additional commutation witnesses required at chain boundaries are present and pass tri-gate under chain corridor,
new cycles introduced by chaining are holonomy-closed under spin gates.
Chain legality is a verifier predicate and results in a chain receipt; absent chain receipt, chaining remains candidate-only.
J.1.a.4 Identity Bridges
Identity bridges represent “no-op” transformations that are nevertheless explicit and typed.
identity on a lens: (\mathrm{Id}_L: L \to L),
identity on a convention regime: (\mathrm{Id}_{\mathrm{ConvFP}}: \mathrm{ConvFP}\to\mathrm{ConvFP}),
identity on policy and corridor records: (\mathrm{Id}{P}), (\mathrm{Id}{C}).
Identity bridges are used to satisfy interface uniformity and to allow chains to include explicit boundary markers. Identity bridges must still bind contexts and be replay-verifiable; they cannot be used to bypass scope or policy checks.
J.1.a.5 Inverse and Adjoint Notions
Inverses are optional and exist only under explicit conditions.
Inverse bridge. A bridge (B:A\to B) has an inverse (B^{-1}:B\to A) only if:
both directions are admissible and promotable,
round-trip loops close under spin gates,
representability is stable in both directions.
Adjoint bridge. An adjoint is defined only in domains where an adjoint structure exists (inner products, dual spaces). Adjoint bridges require additional schema declarations and proof obligations; they are not assumed.
When inverses/adjoints are not provable, the algebra uses explicit reverse bridges (distinct objects) rather than assuming invertibility.
J.1.b CorrEq Certificates
CorrEq certificates formalize corridor equivalence and compatibility. Corridors define acceptance regimes; transferring evidence across corridors is nontrivial and is governed by CorrEq.
J.1.b.1 Corridor Equivalence vs Compatibility
Equivalence (CorrEq=IDENTICAL). Corridors are equivalent on a scope if:
same gate stack ids and residual functional ids,
same thresholds,
same κ ladder semantics,
same probe regime requirements,
same admissibility constraints.
Compatibility (CorrEq=TIGHTENING_COMPATIBLE). One corridor may be a tightening of another such that:
thresholds are tighter or equal,
κ ladder is stronger or equal,
probe regime is stronger or equal,
residual functionals identical.
Reverification-required. If corridors differ in gates/κ/probes, evidence must be recomputed; no transfer.
Incompatible. Corridors differ in residual functionals or normalization rules; transfer is forbidden without rebuilding evidence.
CorrEq is scope-indexed: equivalence in one scope does not imply equivalence globally.
J.1.b.2 What Transfers
Evidence transfer across corridors is permitted only under explicit CorrEq outcomes:
if CorrEq=IDENTICAL, UCWs and evaluation receipts transfer directly.
