if CorrEq=TIGHTENING_COMPATIBLE, prior evidence may be reused only as starting evidence; promotion under tighter corridor requires explicit verification that gates are satisfied under the tighter corridor. Direct transfer without recheck is forbidden unless the prior evidence includes all tighter κ points and stronger probe regimes by construction.
if CorrEq=REVERIFY_REQUIRED, no transfer; recompute evidence under target corridor.
J.1.b.3 Safe Tightening vs Widening
Safe tightening. Tightening is allowed by creating a new corridor identity; it strengthens obligations. CorrEq records may declare tightening-compatibility but still require rechecks for promotion.
Widening prohibitions. Widening is never used to validate previously failing claims. Widening creates a different acceptance regime; any reuse requires new receipts under widened corridor and explicit governance. CorrEq records must emit widening warnings and block silent widening in repair paths.
J.1.b.4 CorrEq as Proof Object
CorrEq outcomes are recorded as CorrEq certificates that:
bind both corridor hashes,
bind scope and carrier type,
record compatibility classification,
specify transfer permissions and required re-verification obligations,
include refusal conditions for disallowed transfers.
CorrEq certificates are required for any attempt to reuse evidence across corridor regimes.
J.1.c Migration Semantics
Migration semantics govern safe evolution of schemas, conventions, policies, atlases, and implementation versions while preserving replay and integrity.
J.1.c.1 Schema Migrations
Schema migrations map old schema versions to new schema versions.
schema migration introduces migration receipts linking old/new object ids,
migrations are no-loss rewrites: all meaning-critical fields preserved or explicitly transformed,
migrations require re-verification of promotion-critical proofs; old proofs remain replayable under old verifiers or via verified translators.
J.1.c.2 ConvFP Migrations
ConvFP migrations handle convention evolution.
changing semantic conventions changes ConvFP; equivalence across ConvFP requires convention bridges,
migration records deprecations, current pointers, and bridge promotion requirements,
backward witness preservation is required: old artifacts remain interpretable; new artifacts cannot silently reuse old ConvFP.
J.1.c.3 Policy Migrations
Policy migrations evolve governance constraints.
policy changes create new policy ids,
reusing evidence across policies requires policy bridges and policy-bridge receipts,
policy migration triggers regression and may quarantine artifacts that are no longer admissible.
J.1.c.4 Atlas Migrations
Atlas migrations update chart sets, activation states, closure witnesses, and Ω* certificates.
atlas updates create new AtlasSpec ids,
activation/deprecation changes are governance logs; they do not alter node identities,
RQF closure and Ω* must be re-verified when closure evidence changes.
J.1.c.5 Backward Witness Preservation
Backward witness preservation ensures old receipts remain replayable.
preserve old schema decoders and verifier catalogs or provide verified translators,
preserve old pinned context records (corridor, policy, ConvFP) referenced by old receipts,
include legacy artifacts in regression suites to ensure long-term replay.
Backward witness preservation is a migration gate, not optional.
J.1.d Conflict Semantics
Conflicts are first-class evidence objects; they are preserved and governed rather than “merged away.”
J.1.d.1 Incompatibility Proofs
An incompatibility proof establishes that two artifacts cannot be reconciled under the current semantic regime.
conflict may be semantic (ConvFP mismatch without bridge), governance (policy block), corridor (residual functional mismatch), or intrinsic (portal necessity),
conflict proofs include minimal counterexamples and context alignment evidence.
J.1.d.2 Portal Boundary
Portal boundary is crossed when incompatibility cannot be resolved by admissible bridges.
repeated stable holonomy failure not compressible into scalar modulus under schema,
monodromy evidence requiring richer carriers or new observables,
incompatible convention regimes with no admissible bridges,
closure impossibility under RQF with a portal report.
Portal boundary yields portal candidates and prohibits forced equivalence.
J.1.d.3 Quarantine Rules
Conflicts trigger quarantine:
conflicting artifacts are quarantined from certified routing,
dependent promotions may be suspended,
quarantine clearance requires bridge promotion or portal creation, both verified.
Quarantine preserves integrity.
J.1.d.4 Resolution Pathways
Resolution pathways are deterministic:
attempt explicit bridges if compatible (convention bridge, policy bridge),
if corridor mismatch, re-verify under target corridor or refuse transfer,
if conflict persists under adequate probes, produce portal report,
keep conflict ledger entries and schedule remediation jobs.
J.2 Encode
J.2.a BridgeSeed Records
BridgeSeed is a schema object with:
bs_id, bridge_kind, schema version,
src_ref, dst_ref typed endpoints,
scope: operator-family scope id, carrier scope id, admissibility region ids,
context constraints: allowed policy/corridor classes and ConvFP compat constraints,
WordNF template: segment skeleton with parameter slots and required operator ids,
witness roots: merkle-rooted set of required UCWs and auxiliary certificates,
guards: representability guards, drift guards, refusal semantics,
lifecycle states: candidate/certified/promoted/active/deprecated/quarantined with context coverage maps.
BridgeSeed records are immutable; updates occur via migration edges.
J.2.b CorrEq Records
CorrEq records encode corridor equivalence/compatibility.
Fields include:
correq_id, schema version,
corr_a_hash, corr_b_hash,
scope id and carrier type id,
compatibility classification (IDENTICAL, TIGHTENING_COMPATIBLE, REVERIFY_REQUIRED, INCOMPATIBLE),
transfer permissions: which witness types may transfer and under what conditions,
required re-verification obligations and gate stacks,
widening prohibitions and warnings,
corridor diffs: explicit gate and κ differences.
CorrEq records are used by ICIB and internal reuse logic.
J.2.c Migration Receipts
Migration receipts are the proof objects that certify safe migrations.
Fields include:
migrate_id, schema version,
old_id, new_id and type tags,
translator id or rewrite procedure id,
re-verification packs proving preserved outcomes (UCWs and receipts replay pass),
rollback plan id,
version catalog pointers for old/new schemas and verifiers,
affected scope lists and required regression suites.
Migration receipts are promotion gates; migrations without receipts cannot affect promoted routing.
J.2.d Conflict Certificates
Conflict certificates are canonical records.
Fields include:
conflict_id, conflict class,
involved artifact ids and aligned contexts,
minimal counterexample objects (κ/probe/subword) and evidence pointers,
affected scopes and carrier types,
remediation obligations: bridge candidates, corridor re-verification tasks, portal candidates,
quarantine actions and clearance requirements.
Conflict certificates are replay-verifiable and used for governance.
J.3 Prove
J.3.a Bridge Composition Soundness
Bridge composition is sound only when the composed chain is itself verified.
Compositional receipts. A composed chain must have a receipt binding the union of pins and witness roots for all components and any boundary witnesses.
Additional UCWs at boundaries. Composition introduces boundary commutation obligations; these require UCWs demonstrating that bridging followed by transport equals transport followed by bridging where required.
Loop closure for chains. Chaining can introduce new cycles; spin-lock must hold for those cycles. Failure yields holonomy refusal and blocks chain promotion.
Replay stability. Composed chain must be replay-stable under pinned contexts and environment pins; otherwise it is quarantined.
Bridge composition soundness ensures that macro bridges are safe and not merely concatenations.
J.3.b Corridor Transfer Correctness
Corridor transfer correctness determines when UCWs and receipts can be reused under different corridors.
if CorrEq=IDENTICAL, transfer is correct.
if CorrEq=TIGHTENING_COMPATIBLE, transfer is correct only when evidence includes all stricter κ points and probe requirements; otherwise re-verification is required and must be performed.
if CorrEq=REVERIFY_REQUIRED, no transfer; recompute under target corridor.
if CorrEq=INCOMPATIBLE, transfer forbidden; treat as distinct regimes requiring separate evidence.
Corridor transfer correctness prevents corridor laundering and ensures corridor-indexed equivalence is honored.
J.3.c Migration Correctness
Migration correctness requires no-loss rewrite and preserved replay.
No-loss rewrite. All meaning-critical fields are preserved or explicitly transformed under verified translator; omitted fields are forbidden.
Link integrity. All references remain valid; any broken reference yields migration refusal.
Backward verification. Old receipts remain replayable under old verifier or via verified translation.
Regression obligations. Migration triggers regression suites for affected promoted artifacts; failures quarantine migrated artifacts and require rollback.
Migration correctness maintains long-term integrity.
J.3.d Conflict Preservation
Conflict preservation is mandatory.
No merge ⇒ meaning. Merging stores does not assert equivalence; conflicts are preserved as conflict certificates.
Quarantine correctness. Conflicting artifacts are quarantined from certified routing until resolved by bridges or portals.
Portal necessity proofs. When bridges cannot reconcile, portal reports are generated and recorded; conflicts remain as evidence of boundary.
Conflict preservation ensures the system remains honest and robust under growth.
J.4 Extract
J.4.a Bridge Maps
Bridge maps are extraction artifacts showing available conversions.
chain catalogs by endpoint types and scope,
fastest chains (cost metadata) subject to certified admissibility,
safest chains (risk and suite coverage) for high-risk operations,
blocked chains and missing bridge requirements,
context coverage maps: which policies/corridors/ConvFP regimes are supported.
Bridge maps are non-semantic navigation aids; all semantic claims still require receipts and UCWs.
J.4.b Migration Playbooks
Migration playbooks define standard upgrade procedures.
schema migration steps: translator selection, rehashing, migration receipts, regression runs,
ConvFP migration: bridge promotion plan, compatibility maps, deprecations, current pointers,
policy migration: policy bridge creation, evidence re-verification, quarantine updates,
atlas migration: chart activation/deprecation, closure re-verification (edges/faces/Ω*),
rollback scripts for failed migrations.
Playbooks are deterministic, versioned, and audited.
J.4.c Corridor Compat Reports
Corridor compat reports are derived from CorrEq records.
CorrEq classification summary and scope,
explicit gate and κ diffs,
transfer eligibility for UCWs and evaluation receipts,
widening warnings and prohibitions,
required re-verification job plans when needed.
Compat reports are used by ICIB and internal reuse.
J.4.d Conflict Ledgers
Conflict ledgers list unresolved conflicts and their statuses.
conflict certificates and minimal counterexamples,
affected scopes and dependencies,
attempted bridges and failure outcomes,
portal candidates and required semantic extensions,
scheduled remediation jobs and priorities.
Conflict ledgers preserve integrity and guide long-term resolution.
Appendix J — Bridge Algebra, CorrEq Certificates, and Migration Semantics
Appendix K — Acceleration Template Library (Apéry, Hypergeometric, Transform, Kernel)
K.1 Spec
K.1.a Template Families
Acceleration templates are parameterized chart families used by AAS and tunneling to synthesize certified evaluators with superior convergence. Templates are strictly separated into search and verification phases: template families define candidate generator spaces; promotion depends only on certified verification.
Each template family ( \mathcal{T} ) is defined by:
a chart semantics skeleton (series/integral/recurrence/transform/kernel),
a parameter schema,
admissibility and stability regions,
required bound families for certification,
required transport binding obligations to baseline charts.
The canonical template families are:
K.1.a.1 Apéry / Binomial Suppression Family
Templates exploiting binomial coefficients and combinatorial suppression to accelerate convergence, typified by Apéry-like constructions.
Characteristics:
alternating or signed term structure,
binomial suppression factors ( \binom{2n}{n}^{-1} ) or related combinatorial weights,
harmonic-polynomial payloads (P(H_n,H_n^{(2)},\dots)) or nested sums,
explicit remainder bound families derived from ratio envelopes and combinatorial asymptotics.
Applicability:
odd ζ ladder and related constants,
variants for Dirichlet beta and character sums.
K.1.a.2 Hypergeometric Family
Templates expressed via generalized hypergeometric series, Pochhammer symbols, and rational parameter sets.
Characteristics:
coefficient ratios expressible as rational functions of (n),
analytic continuation regimes and potential branch sensitivities,
tail bounds derived from ratio test envelopes and asymptotic expansions.
Applicability:
constants with hypergeometric representations,
transform-linked invariants.
K.1.a.3 Euler–Maclaurin Family
Templates accelerating partial sums and integrals via Euler–Maclaurin corrections.
Characteristics:
base sum or integral plus correction terms using Bernoulli numbers,
remainder bounds tied to derivatives and smoothness assumptions,
stability region constraints for derivative growth.
Applicability:
slow converging sums or integrals,
special function evaluations.
K.1.a.4 Continued Fraction Family
Templates representing invariants as continued fractions or J-fractions.
Characteristics:
recurrence relations defining convergents,
error envelopes based on contraction or continued fraction bounds,
sensitivity to rounding and stability constraints.
Applicability:
constants with known continued fraction expansions,
ratio-type invariants.
K.1.a.5 Kernel Semigroup Family
Templates using semigroup kernels and resolvent identities.
Characteristics:
kernel (K_t) or operator semigroup approximations,
error envelopes from truncation in spectral or time parameter,
stability and admissibility tied to semigroup contractivity.
Applicability:
invariants defined by integral transforms, heat kernels, diffusion analogs,
operator-based definitions and portals into richer carriers.
K.1.a.6 Modular / Character Sum Family
Templates using modular forms, Dirichlet characters, and parity selection.
Characteristics:
character-weighted sums (e.g., (\chi_4(n))),
modular acceleration via q-series style suppression,
tail bounds derived from geometric series envelopes in q or from modular growth bounds.
Applicability:
Dirichlet beta values, L-values, modular-derived constants,
χ-modulated ladders.
Each family includes portal boundary conditions: when template semantics require analytic continuation or topological/monodromy carriers beyond current schema, templates are classified as portal-sensitive and require portal workflows.
K.1.b Parameter Spaces
A template family defines a parameter space ( \Theta_{\mathcal{T}} ) that must be finite-enumerable under bounded caps and deterministic ordering.
K.1.b.1 Monomial Bases
Many templates use a basis of features ( {f_i} ) (harmonic numbers, polylog terms, derivative terms, modular terms). Parameter space includes:
choice of feature set (dimension (m)),
feature degrees and truncations,
basis ordering pinned by ConvFP and schema.
Feature sets are enumerated deterministically with fixed expansion schedules:
start from minimal feature set,
add features in canonical order,
cap dimension by budget.
K.1.b.2 Coefficient Caps
Coefficient vectors (\alpha) used in linear combinations are bounded:
integer caps ( |\alpha|_\infty \le B ),
rational caps on numerator/denominator,
normalization rules (gcd reduction, sign normalization),
canonical ordering of candidate coefficient vectors.
Coefficient caps and escalation schedules are part of enumerator specs and are pinned in logs.
K.1.b.3 Truncation Strategies
Templates require deterministic truncation selection:
search truncation (N_{\mathrm{fit}}) for candidate generation (oracle-level),
verify truncation (N_{\mathrm{ver}}(\kappa)) for certified receipts (bound-driven),
fallback truncation rules when bound assumptions are near failure.
Truncation strategies must be monotone and budgeted.
K.1.b.4 Sign Characters and Parity Filters
Parameter space includes sign structures:
alternating signs,
Dirichlet characters (e.g., χ₄),
parity restrictions (odd terms only),
phase conventions where applicable.
Sign character semantics are convention-scoped and require ConvFP coverage.
K.1.b.5 Transform Parameters
Transform-based templates include transform parameters:
normalization constants and sign conventions (ConvFP pinned),
windowing parameters,
inversion schemes,
stability region constraints.
Transform parameters are partitioned into semantic (ConvFP) and numeric (corridor/policy) subsets.
K.1.b.6 Stability Region Constraints
Every parameter tuple must declare admissibility region ids:
interior stability,
boundary-aware stability,
regularization stability (if applicable).
Candidates outside admissibility are refused rather than heuristically tested.
K.1.c Verification Obligations
Templates become charts only through certified verification. Verification obligations are mandatory and are template-kind specific.
K.1.c.1 Tail Bounds
Each template must provide bound ids for:
series remainder or quadrature remainder,
transform truncation remainder (if applicable),
composition envelope bounds for composite pipelines.
Tail bounds require:
explicit assumption region ids,
checkable admissibility conditions,
composition rules.
K.1.c.2 Enclosure Proofs
Certified evaluation receipts must be constructible:
compute approximant (S_N),
compute bound (B(N)),
compute rounding cushion (\delta_{\mathrm{rnd}}),
output enclosure (\mathcal E) containing true value.
Enclosure operations must be deterministic and conservative.
K.1.c.3 Transport Binding UCWs
Every accel chart must be bound to baseline via UCW tri-gate.
face-lock: candidate vs baseline outputs agree within (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}),
spin-lock: any new cycles close within (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}),
projection-lock: representability stable within (\varepsilon_{\Pi}).
Binding UCWs must be computed on adequate probes and κ ladders.
K.1.c.4 Loop Closure
Templates that introduce new chart families must include loop suites that ensure no path dependence:
baseline↔accel↔baseline loops,
transform inversion loops for transform-based templates,
canonicalization loops for projection stability.
Loop closure obligations are required for activation and for bridge promotion.
K.1.c.5 Projection Stability
Templates must specify the projector regime:
rounding projectors for numeric stability,
basis canonicalizers for feature expansions,
normalization projectors for transform conventions.
Projection residual gates are mandatory for promotion-critical equivalence and for RQF contexts.
K.1.c.6 Stress Requirements
Templates are risk-tagged. High-risk templates require:
adversarial probes (boundary/singularity/ordering ties),
perturbation robustness tests,
stress suites before chart activation.
Stress requirements are policy-defined and template-kind dependent.
K.1.d Domain Ladders
Templates are organized by domain ladders: structured sequences of targets across weight/depth.
K.1.d.1 Odd ζ Ladder
Targets: (\zeta(3),\zeta(5),\zeta(7),\dots).Templates: Apéry/binomial, hypergeometric, Euler–Maclaurin.Verification: baseline receipts plus UCW binding, SPP relation search at each weight.
K.1.d.2 Even β Ladder
Targets: (\beta(2),\beta(4),\beta(6),\dots).Templates: character sum families (χ₄), q-series style suppression, binomial variants.Verification: strict ConvFP for character normalization; projection stability required.
K.1.d.3 Depth-2 Candidates
Targets: (\zeta(a,b)), MZVs, and related depth-lift invariants.Templates: kernel semigroup and transform-based families, hypergeometric nested forms.Verification: higher risk; stronger probes and robust non-reduction certificates needed.
K.1.d.4 Domain-Specific Ladders and Portal Boundaries
Domains may define additional ladders (L-values, elliptic periods, logA-like constants). Portal boundaries are declared when:
monodromy or genus transitions appear,
current carrier cannot represent required invariants,
ConvFP cannot reconcile required conventions.
Portal boundary triggers portal workflows; templates become portal-sensitive and require portal reports.
K.2 Encode
K.2.a Template Specs
A TemplateSpec is a canonical record:
template_id, family_id, schema version,
target signature: domain tag, weight/depth ranges, carrier type,
parameter schema: typed fields, admissible ranges, default rules (non-semantic only),
admissibility predicates: stability region ids, assumption region ids,
bound ids: required tail-bound and envelope ids,
method ids: evaluator method id and transform/solver ids if applicable,
rounding id and projector id requirements,
witness hooks: binding UCWs, loop suites, stress/regression requirements.
TemplateSpec is hash-addressed and migratable.
K.2.b Enumerator Specs
EnumeratorSpec defines deterministic candidate generation.
Fields include:
canonical enumeration order id,
cap escalation schedules (coefficient caps, feature dimension caps),
feature expansion order,
truncation schedule for search-phase approximants (N_{\mathrm{fit}}),
deterministic tie-breakers,
replay fields and budget caps,
seed policy: forbidden by default; if permitted, seed pinning rules.
EnumeratorSpec ensures reproducibility of discovery.
K.2.c Candidate Records
CandidateRecord encodes candidate charts proposed by search.
Fields include:
candidate_id, linked template_id and parameter tuple,
coefficient vector(s) with normalization rules,
search-phase oracle-fit logs (tagged heuristic),
PSLQ parameters,
oracle precision used,
basis values used,
relation vector found or not found,
admissibility checks outcome (pre-checks, not certified),
candidate status: rejected/pending_verification/verified/promoted/quarantined,
provenance links to enumerator state and search budgets.
Candidate records are never promotion evidence; they are candidate ledgers.
K.2.d Verification Records
VerificationRecord encodes certified verification:
certified evaluation receipts for candidate chart (interval/tail/rounding),
baseline evaluation receipts used for comparison,
residual packs demonstrating containment and gate margins,
transport UCWs binding candidate chart to baseline (tri-gate),
loop closure UCWs for cycles introduced,
projection stability evidence and projector ids used,
promotion readiness fields:
risk class,
required stress/regression suite ids,
pass/fail status for suites,
activation eligibility.
VerificationRecord is promotable evidence and is referenced by receipts.
K.3 Prove
K.3.a Verify-Only Promotion
Templates enforce verified-only promotion.
certified residual containment is required: candidate enclosure overlaps baseline enclosure within corridor normalization,
no oracle trust: PSLQ logs and oracle fits are never used to accept equivalence,
binding to baseline via UCW is required; equivalence is not inferred from numeric proximity.
Promotion of an accel chart is permitted only when:
evaluation receipts are certified,
binding UCW passes tri-gate,
required stress/regression packs pass.
K.3.b Tail Bound Correctness
Tail bound correctness is a proof obligation.
bound assumptions must be satisfied; if not checkable, verification is boundary-limited or refused under strict policy,
composition rules must hold for the template pipeline; missing composition invalidates certification,
conservativeness must be maintained under rounding; bounds must dominate true remainder plus rounding cushion,
auditability: bound ids must be replay-recomputable; failures quarantine bounds and dependent charts.
K.3.c Robustness Proofs
Robustness requires:
perturbation tests: small perturbations of parameters/probes maintain acceptance margins,
boundary probes: if stability regions approach branch cuts or singularities, boundary probes must be included and passed,
stress suites: high-risk templates must pass adversarial tests before activation,
drift detection: repeated replay under pinned contexts yields identical outcomes; mismatches quarantine charts and trigger remediation.
Robustness proofs are stored as stress packs and regression packs.
K.3.d Template Reuse Soundness
Template reuse across weights and targets is permitted only under explicit rules.
parameter reuse constraints: parameters are reused only within the template’s declared admissibility region and scope; otherwise the candidate is refused.
stability region inheritance: reused templates must inherit or explicitly declare stability regions; boundary-sensitive reuse requires boundary probes.
cross-weight generalization: template families may define a mapping from weight (w) to parameter tuples; such mapping must be deterministic and versioned.
proof reuse: prior verified charts may guide search ordering but cannot replace verification; each new weight requires certified verification and binding UCWs.
Template reuse soundness prevents “pattern hallucination” and maintains proof-carrying integrity.
K.4 Extract
K.4.a Template Chooser
The template chooser is a deterministic selection mechanism producing:
candidate template family set,
expected convergence category,
required bound ids and projector ids,
risk class and required suites,
enumerator spec and budgets.
Inputs:
target class (eval/accel/closure),
domain ladder (odd ζ, even β, depth-2),
required κ and SLA,
policy constraints and scope.
Outputs include a selection log and compat requirements (ConvFP and policy bridges if required).
K.4.b Best-Chart Tables
Best-chart tables are computed from verified coverage.
Best per weight: for each weight, list promoted accel charts ranked by certified cost metadata, subject to context.
Best per latency: for each demanded κ, list fastest certified chart and required binding UCWs.
Verified coverage: context coverage map entries showing where charts are certified/promoted/active.
Fallback charts: baseline charts and alternate certified charts per root.
Best-chart tables never include candidate-only charts; they are extracted only from verified evidence.
K.4.c Failure Atlas
Failure atlas classifies common failure modes by template family.
plateau due to loose bounds → bound upgrade recipes,
plateau due to slow convergence → template family switch or transform family insertion,
drift due to canonicalization instability → ConvFP remediation and projector stabilization,
scope gaps due to operator coverage → ConvFP scope expansion or convention bridge,
policy blocks → policy bridge templates or policy change requests,
portal boundary → portal report and new carrier/schema requirements.
Failure atlas entries include refusal evidence pointers and remediation playbooks.
K.4.d Discovery Playbooks
Discovery playbooks are deterministic procedures for AAS.
AAS run scripts: enumeration order, cap escalation schedule, oracle-fit parameters (candidate-only),
integration steps: create candidate chart specs, schedule certified verification, compute binding UCWs,
activation gates: stress/regression requirements, loop closure checks, rollback plan,
rollback steps: quarantine candidate, revert activation pointers, preserve evidence and logs.
Playbooks are used by scheduler and CI and are replay-addressable.
Appendix K — Acceleration Template Library (Apéry, Hypergeometric, Transform, Kernel)
Appendix L — Dimension Models, Basis Enumeration, and Forced Generator Tables
L.1 Spec
L.1.a Dimension Model Library
Dimension models define expected graded dimensions of invariant spaces and drive SPP deficiency detection. Dimension models are governance contracts: they induce obligations and schedules; they do not by themselves certify mathematical truth.
L.1.a.1 Model Forms
A dimension model is one of:
Weight-only model:[d_w := \dim(\mathcal V_w).]
Refined model with depth/level:[d_{w,\delta} := \dim(\mathcal V_{w,\delta}),]where (\delta) may encode depth, level, or auxiliary grading.
Vector-valued model:[\mathbf d_w := (d^{(1)}_w,\dots,d^{(k)}_w)]for multiple subspaces (e.g., parity-separated spaces, character-separated spaces).
Models must declare the grading semantics and admissibility conditions for applying the model to the metro.
L.1.a.2 Scope and Admissibility
Each model is scoped by:
domain tag and invariant schema keys,
carrier type constraints,
convention regime constraints (ConvFP/ConvBundleFP predicates),
policy class constraints (evidence standards required).
A model is admissible only within declared scope. Applying a model outside scope is illegal and yields refusal for scheduling decisions.
L.1.a.3 Model Migration
Model updates are handled by migration:
new model version creates new model id; old model remains archived,
forced generator tables and schedules are recomputed deterministically,
any promotions justified by old model remain valid only as evidence-based promotions, not as model assertions; the system records “model delta” reports indicating changed deficiency obligations,
model migration triggers regression of SPP results (basis completeness claims) and updates roadmaps.
Model migration does not retroactively invalidate promoted nodes; it changes future obligations and scheduling.
L.1.b Forced Generator Calculus
Forced generator calculus converts dimension models into required generator degree multisets and deficiency metrics.
L.1.b.1 Generating Functions
Given a multiset of generator degrees (G={g_1,\dots,g_m}), define:[F_G(x) := \prod_{g\in G}(1-x^g)^{-1} = \sum_{w\ge 0} f_w(G)x^w,]where (f_w(G)) is the number of monomials of total weight (w) generated by degrees in (G), with multiplicities.
For refined models, use a multivariate generating function:[F_G(x,y) := \prod_{(g,\delta)\in G} (1-x^g y^\delta)^{-1}.]
The library defines canonical generating function computation procedures that are deterministic and bounded for (w\le W_{\max}).
L.1.b.2 Forced Degree Multiset Construction
Forced degree multiset (G^\star) is constructed deterministically from a seed multiset (G_0) and model (d_w) up to (W_{\max}).
Algorithmic rule:
initialize (G:=G_0),
for (w=1) to (W_{\max}):
compute (f_w(G)),
compute deficiency (\mathrm{Def}(w):=\max(0,d_w-f_w(G))),
append degree (w) to (G) exactly (\mathrm{Def}(w)) times,
canonicalize (G) as an ordered multiset.
For refined models, the insertion rule is applied per ((w,\delta)) according to model structure and canonical scheduling rules.
L.1.b.3 Deficiency Criterion
Deficiency is the discrepancy between model dimension and current basis span proxy:[\mathrm{Def}(w):=\max(0,d_w-f_w(G_{\mathrm{prom}})),]where (G_{\mathrm{prom}}) is the multiset of degrees of currently promoted primitives (or promoted basis seeds) under the model scope.
Deficiency is used to schedule:
candidate generator proposals,
relation searches,
non-reduction certificates,
RQF closures for promotions.
Deficiency computation is deterministic and auditable.
L.1.b.4 Canonical Scheduling
Canonical scheduling maps forced degrees to candidate families.
each required generator slot at weight (w) produces a “must fill” demand,
the scheduler selects candidate families in canonical priority order (domain-defined): depth-1 candidates first unless model forces depth>1, then depth-lift candidates,
verification jobs are generated for each candidate: reduction attempt, non-reduction attempt, atlas forcing, stress/regression if high-risk,
scheduling is deterministic under scheduler policy version.
Canonical scheduling ensures reproducibility and prevents arbitrary basis choices.
L.1.c Candidate Families by Weight/Depth
Candidate families define the search space for required generators and relations.
L.1.c.1 Canonical Proposals
For each weight (w) and depth (\delta), canonical proposals include:
known ladder candidates (domain-specific),
Tier-0 candidates from obstruction extraction (if loop evidence suggests),
portal candidates where closure indicates semantic extension is required.
Canonical proposals are ordered by:
prior evidence availability (existing certified evaluation charts),
coherence feasibility (available root charts),
risk class and suite overhead.
L.1.c.2 Relation Families
Relation families define reduction targets and relation search spaces.
basis relations at fixed weight: candidate expressed as linear combination of current basis,
cross-depth relations: depth reduction laws (if schema declares them),
collapse relations: closed-form edges into subalgebras,
cross-convention relations: only via explicit bridges.
Relation families are bounded by coefficient complexity caps and feature selection caps; boundedness is mandatory.
L.1.c.3 Reduction Targets
Reduction targets are specific tasks:
attempt to reduce each candidate into existing basis at weight (w),
attempt to reduce all existing non-primitive nodes into basis expressions to maintain canonical basis representation,
attempt to identify collapse edges that reduce generator requirements.
Reduction targets are certified via interval residual containment and projection stability.
L.1.c.4 Non-Reduction Targets
Non-reduction targets are tasks to certify independence within bounded search:
prove that no reduction exists within coefficient caps, robustly,
ensure probe adequacy and perturbation robustness,
ensure stability under κ refinement and chart substitutions.
Non-reduction certificates are required for promoting new primitives in SPP.
L.1.d Evidence Standards
Evidence standards define what is sufficient to update basis records and promote forced generators.
L.1.d.1 Certified Reductions
A certified reduction requires:
certified evaluation receipts for candidate and basis combination,
certified coefficient proofs (bounded complexity, canonical normalization),
residual containment across κ ladder under corridor gates,
projection stability under (\Pi_h),
context indexing and policy compliance.
Certified reductions are represented by reduction edges with receipts.
L.1.d.2 Certified Non-Reductions
A certified non-reduction requires:
bounded search honesty: explicit caps and deterministic enumeration order,
failure evidence: residual curves plateau above gates across κ, robust under chart substitutions,
probe adequacy: adequacy packs and adversarial probes where required,
perturbation robustness (for high-risk candidates),
no drift evidence.
Non-reduction produces a non-reduction certificate used for promotion gating.
L.1.d.3 Robustness Requirements
Robustness requirements scale with risk class:
low risk: regression required for promoted basis elements, stress optional unless boundary-sensitive scope,
medium risk: regression required, perturbation recommended,
high risk: regression and stress mandatory; boundary probes mandatory; quarantine on failure.
Robustness requirements are policy-defined and stored in risk tags.
L.1.d.4 Promotion Gating
Promoting a forced generator requires:
evidence that deficiency requires a new primitive slot (forced generator table),
non-reduction certificate under bounded search,
RQF atlas coherence and Ω* where required for hub promotion,
stress/regression pass when required,
receipts binding pins and witness roots enabling replay.
Promotion is verified-only; model deficiency is not sufficient by itself.
L.2 Encode
L.2.a Dimension Tables
Dimension tables are canonical records.
Fields include:
model_id, schema version,
scope fields (domain tag, invariant schema keys, ConvFP predicates),
admissibility fields and assumptions,
table values (d_w) (and (d_{w,\delta}) if refined),
provenance fields (metadata; non-semantic),
compatibility predicates and migration links.
Dimension tables are hash-addressed and used by SPP. Changes produce new model ids and migration receipts.
L.2.b Basis Records
Basis records represent the current basis sets by weight/depth.
Fields include:
basis_id, schema version,
mapping (w\mapsto B_w) where (B_w) is an ordered list of node ids,
membership proofs for each basis element (merkle root commitments for sets),
ordering rules pinned by ConvFP and schema,
context coverage maps: for each basis element, which contexts have certified evaluation receipts and required UCWs,
lifecycle and governance metadata.
Basis records are rebuilt deterministically from promotion receipts and are audited by CI.
L.2.c Relation Records
Relation records encode reductions and failures.
Reduction edges: coefficient vectors, reduction receipts, residual packs, projection stability evidence.
Coefficient proofs: coefficient normalization, bounded complexity caps, canonical encoding.
Residual packs: κ-indexed residual curves and margins, probe regime ids, baseline receipts.
Refusal packs: failed reduction attempts and non-reduction evidence; includes plateau certificates and counterexamples.
Relation records are hash-addressed and used to update basis dashboards and forced generator schedules.
L.2.d Schedule Records
Schedule records encode SPP job graphs.
Fields include:
job graph id and schema version,
target weights/depths and forced generator slots,
candidate families to evaluate,
budgets and caps for reduction and non-reduction searches,
dependency edges: reduction before non-reduction, atlas forcing before promotion,
test plan ids, regression/stress suite ids,
expected outputs: basis updates, relation updates, promotion candidates.
Schedules are deterministic and auditable.
L.3 Prove
L.3.a Deficiency Correctness
Deficiency correctness ensures forced generator tables are computed correctly.
generating function computation yields correct monomial counts (f_w(G)) for (w\le W_{\max}) under deterministic algorithm,
insertion rule is deterministic and independent of runtime ordering,
basis degree multiset (G_{\mathrm{prom}}) is derived deterministically from promoted node degrees under model scope,
deficiency invariance under ordering: reordering basis elements does not change deficiency, since deficiency depends only on degree multiset and model.
Deficiency correctness is audited by CI using fixed model fixtures.
L.3.b Reduction Soundness
Reduction soundness ensures that recorded relations are correct.
coefficient proof correctness: coefficients are canonical and validated under coefficient schema,
residual containment: candidate minus combination residual is enclosed and contains 0 across κ ladder under corridor gates,
projection stability: both sides stable under (\Pi_h); projection residual gates satisfied,
context indexing: reductions are valid only under pinned contexts; reuse requires bridges,
replay sufficiency: reduction receipts contain pins/witness roots enabling independent verification.
Failures yield refusal packs; no partial reductions are promoted.
L.3.c Non-Reduction Soundness
Non-reduction soundness ensures independence evidence is honest.
bounded search honesty: explicit coefficient caps and feature caps; deterministic enumeration order,
plateau evidence: residual curves remain above gate; failure persists across κ refinement,
probe adequacy: adequacy packs confirm probes separate candidate from span; boundary probes included when scope demands,
adversarial checks: perturbation and stress suites for high-risk candidates confirm failure is not artifact of ill-conditioning,
drift exclusion: repeated replay yields stable outcomes; drift yields quarantine rather than non-reduction.
Non-reduction certificates are scoped and must not be overstated as absolute independence.
L.3.d Promotion Obligations
Promotion obligations ensure forced generators become valid primitives safely.
RQF closure and Ω* required when promotion intends hub-level reuse across roots,
policy-safe promotion: evidence admissible under policy; stress/regression required by risk class must pass,
bridge correctness: any cross-convention or cross-policy use requires explicit bridges and receipts,
regression/stress gating: promotions are included in ongoing suites; failures trigger deactivation/quarantine.
Promotion obligations are verified and stored as receipts.
L.4 Extract
L.4.a Forced Targets List
Forced targets list is computed deterministically from model and current basis coverage.
Outputs include:
next weights with positive deficiency,
required generator count per weight (and depth, if refined),
canonical candidate families to attempt per slot,
required job plans: reduction, non-reduction, RQF, stress/regression,
policy/corridor contexts under which the targets are intended to be promoted.
Forced targets list is used by scheduler and roadmaps.
L.4.b Basis Dashboards
Basis dashboards summarize:
current basis per weight/depth and promotion status,
deficiency status vs model,
certified coverage per basis element (root charts, UCWs, Ω* where required),
unresolved regions and pending candidates,
risk overlays: which basis elements are high-risk and require additional suites.
Dashboards are deterministic and audit-linked.
L.4.c Relation Dashboards
Relation dashboards summarize:
known reductions and collapse edges per weight,
coefficient proof coverage and context scopes,
conflicting relations and convention dependencies requiring bridges,
failed reduction attempts and non-reduction certificates,
portal candidates indicated by repeated closure failures.
Relation dashboards drive SPP scheduling and conflict resolution.
L.4.d Roadmap Generator
Roadmap generator produces an automatic roadmap to weight (N).
Outputs include:
forced generator schedule up to (N),
expected compute budgets per weight (reduction caps, κ ladders, probe counts),
predicted bottlenecks: missing charts, missing bounds, policy blocks, probe inadequacy, portal boundaries,
remediation tasks: AAS template expansions, probe regime upgrades, bridge promotions, portal work,
CI and suite requirements for safe scaling.
The roadmap is deterministic given model id, policy, corridor families, and current metro state.
Appendix L — Dimension Models, Basis Enumeration, and Forced Generator Tables
Appendix M — Governance, Risk, Negatify, and Scheduler Policy Library
M.1 Spec
M.1.a Governance Objects
Governance is the formal regime controlling admissibility, disclosure, promotion, quarantine, and evolution. Governance objects are hash-addressed records; governance decisions are replayable and audited. Governance never replaces verification; it constrains and sequences verification.
M.1.a.1 Policies
A policy (P) is a record defining:
admissible operator families and disallowed operations,
admissible method families, bound families, rounding models,
budget ceilings and escalation permissions,
allowed bridge kinds and tunnel permissions,
visibility mode constraints and disclosure boundaries,
required suites (stress/regression) per risk class.
Policies are immutable by id; changes create new policy ids and require policy bridges for evidence transfer.
M.1.a.2 Corridors
Corridors (C) define acceptance regimes:
gate thresholds and residual functional ids,
κ ladders and probe regimes,
admissible methods and stability regions,
budgets and termination constraints.
Corridors are governed objects; widening/tightening is explicit and audited. Corridors cannot be silently modified.
M.1.a.3 Risk Policies
Risk policies define:
risk classification rules for artifacts and scopes,
required stress/regression suites per risk class,
quarantine triggers and clearance requirements,
downgrade and rollback rules,
escalation permissions for portal-sensitive regions.
Risk policies are pinned and versioned; changes require governance migration and regression validation.
M.1.a.4 Visibility Modes
Visibility modes are enforceable constraints:
public: exportable replay bundles,
restricted: partial disclosure; boundary-limited external verification,
private: local-only verification; no export of sensitive pins,
boundary_limited: explicit limitation on what is verifiable externally,
quarantined_export: candidate-only exports with explicit non-assertion of truth.
Visibility modes are applied to receipts, packs, and export manifests. Violations yield refusal.
M.1.a.5 Quarantine Semantics
Quarantine is the governance state that prevents unsafe reuse.
Quarantine triggers: drift alarms, regression failures, stress failures, policy mismatch, import incompatibility, missing pins/witnesses.
Quarantine scope: quarantine may apply to specific artifacts, scopes, or contexts; quarantine propagation follows dependency edges.
Quarantine clearance: clearance requires explicit verification tasks, bridge promotions, migrations, or portal resolutions; clearance is recorded in quarantine clearance receipts.
Quarantine enforcement: certified routing excludes quarantined artifacts unless a query explicitly requests candidate evidence.
M.1.a.6 Approval Gates
Approval gates are governance-controlled prerequisites for high-impact actions:
corridor widening requests,
activation of high-risk accel charts,
promotion of portal bridges,
policy changes or policy bridge promotions,
cross-metro import clearance into promoted routing.
Approval gates are recorded as governance events linked to evidence and CI outcomes; they are not informal approvals.
M.1.b Risk Scoring
Risk scoring classifies artifacts and actions so that governance can enforce appropriate test and verification requirements. Risk scoring is deterministic under pinned risk policy.
M.1.b.1 Risk Classes
Risk classes are ordinal and scoped:
Low risk: baseline charts and transports in well-behaved regions; no branch sensitivity; strong bounds.
Medium risk: accel charts verified by AAS; moderate sensitivity; limited tunnels; typical commutation/holonomy obligations.
High risk: portal-sensitive scopes, branch/monodromy loops, tunnel-derived charts near boundaries, cross-policy bridges, cross-metro evidence, new projector regimes.
Risk class is recorded as a tag in receipts and packs and is used to select required suites.
M.1.b.2 Stress/Regression Requirements
For each risk class, risk policy declares:
regression suites required and their cadence (metadata),
stress suites required and adversarial probe families,
perturbation robustness checks required,
required margins to gates and stricter corridor selections if applicable.
Risk policy may require stronger probe adequacy for high-risk scopes.
M.1.b.3 Drift Sensitivity
Risk scoring includes drift sensitivity based on:
reliance on canonicalization and ordering rules,
reliance on numeric backend semantics,
involvement of new operator families,
cross-metro imports and compatibility bridges.
High drift sensitivity increases required regression coverage and narrows allowed environments.
M.1.b.4 Operator Scope Risk
Operator scope risk is derived from:
branch-sensitive primitives,
regularization-sensitive procedures,
ill-conditioned transforms,
semantic discontinuities and boundary constraints.
Scope risk influences required adversarial probes and portal triggers.
M.1.c Negatify Protocol
Negatify is a deterministic protocol for mapping the “failure space” of a domain, producing structured inverse maps that highlight the most dangerous regions, the most common refusal motifs, and the minimal counterexamples that define those motifs. Negatify is not an aesthetic concept; it is a governance tool for accelerating safe development.
M.1.c.1 Failure-Space Mapping
Negatify builds a failure map by aggregating refusal artifacts and counterexamples.
classify failures by refusal class and by dominant lock failure (face/spin/projection),
index failures by operator scope, carrier type, stability region, corridor, and policy,
extract minimal counterexamples (κ/probe/subword) as canonical “failure seeds.”
Failure-space mapping produces a deterministic atlas of failure hotspots.
M.1.c.2 Inverse Maps
Inverse maps represent “where the system breaks first.”
map from scope regions to minimal failing predicates,
map from parameter regimes to perturbation sensitivity failures,
map from corridor/policy combinations to policy blocks,
map from chart families to plateau signatures.
Inverse maps are used to prevent repeated futile searches and to prioritize missing infrastructure (bounds, probes, bridges, portals).
M.1.c.3 Adversarial Probe Generation
Negatify generates adversarial probe suites from failure seeds.
boundary probes near singularities and branch cuts derived from counterexample locations,
ordering tie probes derived from canonicalization counterexamples,
worst-case commutation probes derived from subword counterexamples,
perturbation probes derived from stability region sensitivity evidence.
Adversarial probe generation is deterministic: given failure seeds and risk policy, it outputs probe suite definitions pinned by ProbeFP ids.
M.1.c.4 Remediation Extraction
Negatify produces remediation tasks:
which new probes are required,
which bound families are missing or too loose,
which bridges are missing (policy or convention),
which portals are required,
which AAS templates should be expanded or pruned.
Remediation tasks are emitted as scheduler demands with explicit job graphs and budgets.
M.1.d Scheduler Policy
Scheduler policy defines how demands become jobs, how jobs are ordered, and how failures escalate. Scheduler policy is deterministic and audited.
M.1.d.1 Urgency Metric
Scheduler policy pins an urgency function (U) over demand records and telemetry snapshots, incorporating:
required κ,
demand frequency and dependency impact,
deficiency impact (SPP),
risk overhead and suite requirements.
Urgency is deterministic under pinned scheduler policy version and telemetry snapshot.
M.1.d.2 Fairness and Starvation Avoidance
Scheduler policy enforces:
deterministic aging for pending demands,
class-based fairness windows,
rejection of repeated futile jobs unless new admissible actions appear,
conversion of blocked tasks into policy bridge demands or portal demands.
Fairness is auditable and does not alter semantic acceptance.
M.1.d.3 Escalation Decision Trees
Escalation is deterministic:
hard predicate failures → bridge demands or policy changes,
soft gate failures → Snap repair within budgets,
repeated failures → tunnel/AAS if admissible,
holonomy-dominant stability → T0-OBSTR,
RQF failure with portal signature → portal report and portal demands,
policy blocks → policy bridge demands.
Escalation never widens corridors silently and never promotes heuristic evidence.
M.1.d.4 Audit Binding
Scheduler decisions are logged:
demand id and context,
urgency components,
chosen job graph and dependencies,
blocked actions and reasons,
expected outputs and acceptance predicates.
Audit binding ensures scheduling is replayable.
M.2 Encode
M.2.a Policy Records
PolicyRecord is a canonical schema object with:
policy_id and schema version,
allowed operator families and disallowed operations,
allowed method families and forbidden method ids,
budget ceilings by job class,
bridge allowances (which bridge kinds admissible),
tunnel allowances (AUTO_TUNNEL allowed or forbidden; deterministic enumeration requirements),
disclosure boundaries and visibility mode rules,
required stress/regression suites per risk class,
refusal rules for policy violations.
PolicyRecord is referenced by receipts and is enforced by verifiers and schedulers.
M.2.b Risk Policy Records
RiskPolicyRecord is a canonical schema object with:
risk_policy_id and schema version,
risk classification rules: mapping from artifact types and scopes to risk classes,
suite requirements: stress/regression suite ids and required margins per risk class,
quarantine triggers: drift/stress/regression conditions and propagation rules,
downgrade rules: how to downgrade promotions safely,
portal escalation thresholds: when risk becomes portal requirement.
RiskPolicyRecord is referenced by governance logs and scheduler policy.
M.2.c Failure Maps
FailureMapRecord encodes negatify outputs.
Fields include:
failure class taxonomy and mapping rules,
failure seeds: minimal counterexample objects and their scope/κ/probe locations,
blocked regions: scope regions where admissible actions repeatedly fail,
remediation tasks: explicit job graph templates and priorities,
priority overlays: urgency weights derived from dependency impact and risk.
Failure maps are deterministic and versioned; updates require migration and regression.
M.2.d Scheduler Records
Scheduler records include:
DemandRecord: target class, contexts, SLA, risk tags.
JobGraph: job nodes and dependencies; budgets; expected outputs.
RunLog: executed job outputs; refusals; evidence packs; budget consumption.
DecisionLog: action selection and escalation reasons.
ScheduleReplayRecord: deterministic reconstruction evidence for schedule decisions.
Scheduler records are hash-addressed and auditable.
M.3 Prove
M.3.a Safety Invariants
Governance enforces non-negotiable invariants.
No heuristic promotion. Search-phase evidence cannot enter witness roots for promotion-critical receipts.
No silent widening. Corridor widening is explicit governance event; requires new corridor identity and re-verification.
No policy laundering. Evidence certified under one policy cannot certify under another without explicit policy bridges and re-verification obligations.
No convention laundering. Evidence under one ConvFP cannot be treated as evidence under another without explicit convention bridges.
These invariants are verified by CI property tests and enforced by verifiers.
M.3.b Risk Monotonicity
Risk monotonicity states:
adding capabilities (new operator scope, new tunnels, new bridges) cannot reduce required safety gates unless accompanied by evidence and governance updates,
activating accel charts cannot reduce baseline requirements; baseline remains the anchor for truth,
portal-sensitive expansions require stronger suites and cannot be treated as lower risk by default.
Risk policy updates are versioned and require regression validation.
M.3.c Refusal Correctness
Refusal correctness ensures that refusals are honest and useful.
refusals include minimal failing predicate and counterexamples,
refusals include budgets consumed and blocked actions,
refusals include admissible next actions and remediation plans,
refusals are replayable and verifiable under pinned contexts,
refusals contain quarantine semantics where required to prevent contamination.
Refusal correctness is enforced by refusal checklists and CI.
M.3.d Governance Compliance
Governance compliance is enforced by CI and verifier rules.
CI checks policy compliance: no forbidden operator families used in promoted proofs,
CI checks corridor compliance: no silent widening and correct gate usage,
CI checks bridge requirements: any cross-policy or cross-convention reuse must include bridge receipts,
CI checks quarantine correctness: quarantined artifacts not used in certified routing,
compliance failures yield quarantines and remediation demands.
Governance compliance guarantees long-term integrity under evolution and import.
M.4 Extract
M.4.a Governance Dashboards
Governance dashboards are extraction products:
policy blocks and required policy bridge demands,
corridor changes and lineage graphs (tightening/widening events),
approval gate status for high-impact actions,
quarantine status and clearance requirements,
compliance summaries for CI runs.
Dashboards are context-indexed and audit-linked.
M.4.b Risk Dashboards
Risk dashboards summarize:
risk class distribution across nodes/edges/charts/bridges,
missing stress/regression suite coverage,
high-risk active artifacts and mitigation queue,
drift alarms and quarantines affecting risk posture,
portal candidate counts and statuses.
Risk dashboards drive governance prioritization.
M.4.c Negatify Atlases
Negatify atlases are structured failure-space maps:
worst-case regions by operator scope and stability region,
adversarial probe suites generated from counterexamples,
common failure motifs and their minimal seeds,
fast remediation recipes: which probes/bounds/bridges to add,
blocked regions and pruning recommendations for search.
Negatify atlases are versioned and used to guide safe acceleration and basis growth.
M.4.d Scheduler Reports
Scheduler reports summarize:
demand backlog and urgency ranking,
SLA status and missed SLAs with blockers,
escalation statistics: how often Snap/tunnel/T0-OBSTR/portal triggered,
roadmap updates derived from deficiency and failure maps,
compute queue projections and budget consumption summaries.
Scheduler reports are deterministic and replayable.
Appendix M — Governance, Risk, Negatify, and Scheduler Policy Library
Appendix N — ICIB Exchange Formats and Cross-Metro Compatibility
N.1 Spec
N.1.a Import Object Taxonomy
ICIB exchange operates over a finite set of schema-typed object classes. Imported objects are treated as foreign evidence until locally verified or bridged under local policies and conventions.
N.1.a.1 Primary Importable Objects
Nodes (NodeSpec).Invariant nodes, candidate nodes, portal nodes, bridge nodes, bundle nodes. Node identity is preserved; importing does not merge identities.
Charts (ChartSpec) and Atlases (AtlasSpec).Baseline and accel charts, atlas configurations, chart activation pointers, closure obligations.
Words (WordNF) and Segments.Route identities, segment streams (GateRun, TunnelEvent, BridgeEvent, SpliceRecord, SnapEvent), chain hashes.
Witnesses (UCW, Ω).*Commutation/holonomy/projection UCWs and Ω* interior coherence certificates; probe regimes and loop map signatures required to interpret them.
Receipts (Rcpt and specialized receipts).Receipts binding pins/witness roots; compat receipts; policy-bridge receipts; migration receipts; compaction receipts.
Bridges (BridgeSeed, MagicCertPack).Reusable bridge tissue; application traces; chaining traces; lifecycle states.
Refusals and Conflict Evidence.Refusal packs, plateau certificates, drift logs, conflict certificates, portal reports.
N.1.a.2 Reference-Only Objects
Policies and corridors may be imported as references but are not automatically adopted.
PolicyRecord (reference-only).Imported policies define source admissibility; local policy primacy remains.
CorridorRecord (reference-only).Imported corridors define source gates; local corridor selection governs local claims.
ConvFP and ConvBundleFP (semantic references).Imported convention records are interpretable only if schema compatible and scope-compatible; otherwise require convention bridges or remain quarantined.
Reference-only objects are used for compatibility analysis and translation planning.
N.1.b Compatibility Semantics
Compatibility is a typed, conservative relation: it determines whether imported artifacts can be interpreted and reused under local semantics and governance.
N.1.b.1 Semantic Compatibility
Semantic compatibility concerns meaning.
ConvBundleFP compatibility. Imported operator scopes must be covered by a locally recognized ConvBundleFP or by an explicit convention bridge chain.
Lens and schema compatibility. Imported LensSpec and invariant schemas must be decodable and semantically compatible; if schema semantics differ, portal or migration required.
Projector compatibility. Projection/canonicalization regimes must match or be bridged with explicit commutation evidence; otherwise representability claims are not transferable.
Semantic compatibility outcomes:
SEM_IDENTICAL,
SEM_BRIDGEABLE,
SEM_INCOMPATIBLE (portal boundary or schema mismatch).
N.1.b.2 Governance Compatibility
Governance compatibility concerns admissibility.
Policy compatibility. Imported evidence is admissible under local policy only if:
local policy permits required methods/operations, or
policy bridge upgrades evidence by re-verifying under local admissibility constraints.
Corridor compatibility. Corridor-indexed claims require:
identical corridors, or
explicit CorrEq mapping indicating transfer eligibility, or
local re-verification under local corridor.
Governance outcomes:
GOV_IDENTICAL,
GOV_BRIDGE_REQUIRED,
GOV_REVERIFY_REQUIRED,
GOV_BLOCKED.
N.1.b.3 Schema/Verifier Compatibility
Schema/verifier compatibility concerns interpretability and replay.
Schema decoding. Local schema registry must decode imported object schemas; otherwise importer must refuse or require migration receipts and translators.
Verifier compatibility. Local verifier must support imported verifier versions or have verified translators that preserve replay outcomes.
Outcomes:
SV_SUPPORTED,
SV_TRANSLATE_REQUIRED,
SV_UNSUPPORTED.
N.1.b.4 Translation Rules
Translation rules define conservative mapping of foreign evidence to local statuses.
No confidence inflation. Foreign promoted cannot become local promoted without local verification and governance gates.
Context-indexed mapping. Evidence is valid only under context hashes; differing policy/corridor/ConvFP contexts require bridging or re-verification.
Boundary-limited handling. If visibility restrictions prevent expansion of required pins/witnesses, local status is boundary-limited or quarantined.
Translation results are recorded as compat receipts and quarantine decisions.
N.1.c Quarantine Discipline
Quarantine is the default state for imported artifacts. Quarantine prevents unsafe reuse and preserves conflict evidence.
N.1.c.1 Default Quarantine
Upon import, each object is assigned:
a quarantine status: quarantined unless proven reusable,
a quarantine reason: schema unsupported, missing pins, policy mismatch, corridor mismatch, convention mismatch, unknown provenance.
Quarantined objects may be stored, indexed, and used for candidate exploration but not for certified routing or promotion-critical evidence.
N.1.c.2 Clearance Requirements
Clearance requires one of:
Local verification. Recompute receipts/UCWs under local policy and corridor; bind new local receipts.
Verified bridges. Apply convention/policy bridges with bridge receipts and required re-verification.
Verified translators. Use migration receipts for schema/verifier translation and re-run replay checks.
Clearance always produces:
clearance receipts,
updated coverage maps,
regression/stress requirements for activation if high-risk.
N.1.c.3 Propagation Rules
Quarantine propagates along dependencies:
any receipt that depends on quarantined pins/witnesses is quarantined for promotion-critical use,
any bridge chain involving quarantined components is quarantined,
any atlas closure depending on quarantined UCWs is NEAR or FAIL under local closure checks.
Propagation is deterministic and recorded in governance logs.
N.1.c.4 Rollback Rules
Rollback handles unsafe imports after partial clearance:
deactivate imported charts/bridges that cause regressions or drift alarms,
preserve all imported evidence and logs; rollback changes only activation pointers and routing eligibility,
quarantine affected artifacts and schedule remediation jobs.
Rollback must preserve baseline functionality and is audited.
N.1.d Sync Governance
Periodic sync supports long-term cross-metro collaboration while maintaining integrity.
N.1.d.1 Periodic Sync
Sync is a governed operation:
sync schedule is explicit and pinned,
sync imports new manifests and diffs from remote metro,
sync does not auto-promote; quarantine-by-default remains.
N.1.d.2 Diff Semantics
Diffs are computed over hash identities and schema objects.
added objects, removed objects, updated objects (via migration edges),
changed coverage maps and activation pointers,
changed conflict ledgers and refusal evidence.
Diffs are deterministic and replayable.
N.1.d.3 Conflict Evolution
Conflicts may evolve:
new bridges may resolve old conflicts,
new evidence may strengthen or invalidate compat outcomes,
drift alarms may introduce new conflicts.
Conflict evolution is logged; conflicts are preserved rather than overwritten.
N.1.d.4 Long-Term Governance Constraints
Long-term constraints include:
allowable remote sources and trust boundaries,
maximum acceptable risk for imported active artifacts,
required regression/stress coverage before activation,
compaction and archival policies for imported bundles.
N.2 Encode
N.2.a Import Manifests
ImportManifest is the canonical exchange object and the sole authoritative input to import.
N.2.a.1 Manifest Schema
Fields include:
manifest_id, schema version,
source_metro_id and source provenance fields (metadata),
list of object ids with type tags,
bundle roots: pins_root, wits_root expansions when provided,
schema/verifier catalog pointers,
environment/toolchain snapshot pointers when required for replay,
visibility mode declarations and disclosure boundaries,
integrity proofs: bundle root proofs, merkle indices, chain hash proofs where applicable.
Manifests must be self-consistent; inconsistencies produce refusal.
N.2.a.2 Pins/Wits Expansions
If export mode allows, manifest includes:
expansion indices for pins_root and wits_root,
the merkle algorithm id used,
ordered list reconstruction rules.
If export mode restricts expansions, manifest must declare boundary-limited status; local importer records which elements are missing and enforces quarantine.
N.2.a.3 Visibility Modes
Manifest must declare per-object visibility:
public exportable,
restricted export with withheld pins,
private objects omitted or replaced with redaction markers.
Visibility declarations are audited; violating declarations yields refusal and quarantine.
N.2.a.4 Integrity Proofs
Manifest includes:
object hash proofs,
merkle root proofs and expansion indices,
chain hash proofs for WordNF streams where committed.
Integrity proofs enable tamper-evidence and safe storage.
N.2.b Mapping Tables
Mapping tables record foreign→local references without asserting equivalence.
N.2.b.1 Foreign→Local References
A MappingTable includes:
foreign object id → local stored copy id (often identical hash bytes if canonical encoding matches),
type tags and schema ids,
provenance pointers (manifest id, source id).
Mapping never asserts that foreign id equals an existing local node id in meaning; it only records references.
N.2.b.2 Alias Pointers
Mapping tables may create alias nodes:
alias class links foreign names to local candidates,
alias membership is non-semantic,
equivalence requires explicit UCW edges and receipts.
N.2.b.3 Hub Alignment Proposals
Mapping may include candidate hub alignments:
proposed mapping of foreign hub nodes to local hubs,
required compat receipts and bridges,
status: candidate-only until verified.
Hub alignment accelerates routing but is never treated as proof.
N.2.b.4 Migration Links
If foreign artifacts use schema versions not supported locally, mapping includes:
required translator ids,
migration receipt requirements,
quarantine flags until migration completes.
N.2.c Compat Receipts
CompatReceipt records compatibility decisions for imported artifacts.
Fields include:
imported artifact id and local context targets,
semantic compatibility outcome and required convention bridges,
governance compatibility outcome and required policy bridges or re-verification requirements,
schema/verifier compatibility outcome and required translators,
refusal reasons when blocked,
clearance job plans (job graph references) when bridgeable.
CompatReceipt is replay-verifiable: it references explicit predicate results and required evidence.
N.2.d Exchange Packs
Exchange packs are standardized bundles assembled from manifests and clearance outcomes.
Minimal replay bundles. Contain receipts, pins/witness expansions, schema/verifier catalogs, and environment snapshots required for replay.
Boundary-limited exports. Contain partial bundles plus explicit declarations of what cannot be verified externally.
Policy-bridge requirements. Include policy-bridge templates and required re-verification tasks for target policy.
Quarantine instructions. Include quarantine propagation and clearance requirements for importers.
Exchange packs are hash-addressed and used in cross-metro exchange workflows.
N.3 Prove
N.3.a Safe Import Theorem
If an imported artifact contributes to a locally promoted claim, then:
local receipts bind local pins and local witness roots,
compat receipts prove semantic and governance compatibility or provide explicit bridges,
policy bridges and convention bridges are verified where needed,
local verifier replay reproduces acceptance outcomes.
Thus, local truth is derived only from locally verifiable evidence or explicitly verified bridges; imports cannot bypass local governance or verification.
N.3.b Confidence Translation Correctness
Confidence translation is conservative.
foreign promoted → local quarantined until verified; never automatically promoted,
foreign certified → local candidate until certified under local corridor/policy or bridged,
foreign heuristic remains heuristic; cannot enter witness roots for promotion,
foreign refused remains refused evidence; may guide remediation but cannot be used as positive proof.
No translation may overstate confidence; violations yield refusal and audit incident.
N.3.c Conflict Correctness
Conflict correctness ensures conflicts are detected and preserved.
conflicts are asserted only when contexts are aligned (same or bridged policy/corridor/ConvFP),
conflicts include minimal counterexamples demonstrating divergence (κ/probe/subword),
conflicts classify portal necessity when incompatibility is intrinsic and persists under adequate probes and admissible bridges,
conflicts trigger quarantine and remediation queues; conflicts are not erased.
Conflict correctness prevents false merges and supports portal boundary detection.
N.3.d Sync Determinism
Sync determinism ensures periodic sync is auditable and reproducible.
diffs computed deterministically from manifests and prior sync states,
mapping decisions deterministic under mapping policies and disambiguation rules,
quarantine actions deterministic under compat outcomes and governance rules,
sync logs and governance logs provide replay of sync decisions.
Sync determinism is required for long-term multi-metro integrity.
N.4 Extract
N.4.a Import Reports
Import reports are generated deterministically.
They include:
what was imported (object lists by type),
what was quarantined and why,
what was verified locally and by which receipts,
what was promoted/activated under local governance and why,
what was refused and required next actions.
Import reports are audit-linked to manifests and compat receipts.
N.4.b Compat Dashboards
Compat dashboards summarize:
semantic compat outcomes and missing convention bridges,
governance compat outcomes and required policy bridges,
schema/verifier compat outcomes and required translators,
blocked regions and remediation plans.
Dashboards drive scheduling of clearance jobs.
N.4.c Conflict Ledgers
Conflict ledgers list:
conflict certificates and minimal counterexamples,
affected scopes and dependencies,
attempted bridges and their outcomes,
portal candidates and required semantic extensions,
resolution status and next actions.
Conflict ledgers preserve integrity and guide portal work.
N.4.d Sync Dashboards
Sync dashboards summarize:
sync health and last sync status,
drift alerts and regression impacts due to imports,
compaction and archival status of imported bundles,
governance summaries: quarantines, clearances, activations, rollbacks.
Sync dashboards support long-term cross-metro governance.
Appendix N — ICIB Exchange Formats and Cross-Metro Compatibility
Appendix O — Security, Integrity Proofs, and CI Manifests
O.1 Spec
O.1.a Threat Model
Security is defined by explicit adversarial capabilities and failure modes. The system assumes attackers or accidental faults may affect storage, execution, and exchange. The threat model is scoped to the proof-carrying architecture and its operational environment.
O.1.a.1 Corruption
Corruption includes:
modification of canonical bytes of stored objects,
partial truncation or bit-flips in object store,
corrupted expansion indices for merkle roots,
corrupted bundle manifests,
corrupted logs or missing logs.
Corruption threatens replay invariance and must be detected deterministically.
O.1.a.2 Substitution
Substitution includes:
swapping one object id for another in receipts or witness references,
swapping context hashes (policy/corridor/ConvFP) to launder evidence,
swapping schema/verifier versions to change interpretation,
swapping expansion indices to hide missing dependencies.
Substitution is prevented by hash bindings and merkle root commitments.
O.1.a.3 Nondeterminism Exploitation
Nondeterminism exploitation includes:
leveraging uncontrolled randomness to produce opportunistic passing outcomes,
exploiting runtime-dependent iteration ordering,
exploiting numeric backend differences to vary rounding behavior,
exploiting time/locale-dependent behavior in serialization.
Promotion-critical verification must be deterministic; nondeterminism is confined to candidate search and is never trusted.
O.1.a.4 Drift
Drift includes:
semantic drift from evolving conventions without migration,
canonicalization drift from unstable ordering or tie-breakers,
implementation drift from code changes affecting evaluation/witness computation,
environment drift from backend updates or toolchain changes not pinned.
Drift threatens long-term integrity; it is detected by regression and replay invariance tests and results in quarantines.
O.1.a.5 Disclosure Boundary Violations
Disclosure violations include:
exporting restricted pins or witnesses,
producing boundary-limited proofs labeled as fully verifiable,
leaking sensitive data through logs or serialized records.
Visibility modes and export constraints must be enforced; violations yield refusal and incident logs.
O.1.a.6 Denial of Verification
Denial includes:
triggering unbounded verification costs,
constructing adversarial routes that cause explosive witness obligations,
forcing infinite refinement loops.
Budgets and termination guarantees are mandatory; verification must refuse rather than run unbounded.
O.1.b Integrity Guarantees
Integrity guarantees are the enforceable properties that the system maintains under the threat model.
O.1.b.1 Tamper-Evidence
Tamper-evidence is ensured by:
hash identities over canonical bytes with domain separation,
merkle roots committing pin and witness sets,
chain hashes committing WordNF segment ordering,
bundle manifests committing object sets and indices.
Any tampering changes hash commitments and is detected.
O.1.b.2 Replay Invariance
Replay invariance guarantees:
promoted receipts and witnesses are replay-verifiable under pinned contexts and pinned environments,
recomputed evidence matches stored commitments,
acceptance outcomes are deterministic.
Replay invariance is enforced by CI and regression suites; failures trigger quarantine.
O.1.b.3 Schema/Verifier Invariance
Schema/verifier invariance guarantees:
objects are interpreted only under supported schemas and verifiers,
schema migrations and verifier upgrades are performed only via migration receipts and verified translators,
backward witness preservation is maintained.
Unsupported schema/verifier combinations yield quarantine or refusal.
O.1.b.4 Quarantine Correctness
Quarantine correctness guarantees:
unsafe artifacts are prevented from certified routing,
quarantine triggers are evidence-based and auditable,
clearance requires explicit verification tasks and receipts,
rollback preserves baseline functionality and audit trails.
O.1.c CI Governance
CI governance defines continuous enforcement of integrity and determinism.
O.1.c.1 Manifests
CI manifests specify:
which bundles must replay-verify,
required verifier versions and schema catalogs,
required environment/toolchain pins,
acceptance expectations (pass criteria and expected root hashes).
Manifests are themselves hash-addressed and versioned; changes require governance and regression validation.
O.1.c.2 Regression/Stress Suites
CI governance requires:
regression suites for promoted artifacts and high-dependency hubs,
stress suites for high-risk scopes, tunnels, bridges, and portal-sensitive components.
Suites are pinned and versioned; suite changes require migration and CI updates.
O.1.c.3 Failure Handling
Failure handling is deterministic:
classify failure as corruption, drift, environment mismatch, policy mismatch, or scope gap,
quarantine affected artifacts,
generate failure diff packs and counterexamples,
schedule remediation jobs,
optionally rollback activation pointers and revert to last known good state.
Failures are not resolved by widening corridors or weakening gates.
O.1.c.4 Rollback Requirements
Rollback requirements ensure safe recovery:
rollback deactivates unsafe charts/bridges without deleting evidence,
rollback preserves fallback routes and baseline availability,
rollback produces rollback logs and governance receipts,
rollback triggers regression to confirm stability after rollback.
O.1.d Compliance Boundaries
Compliance defines what may be asserted, what may be exported, and how boundaries are labeled.
O.1.d.1 Visibility Modes
Visibility modes constrain exportability:
public: fully exportable replay bundles,
restricted: partial export with boundary-limited verification,
private: local-only verification,
boundary_limited: explicit limitation to prevent misuse,
quarantined_export: candidate-only exports.
Visibility mode is pinned in receipts and manifests; exports must respect it.
O.1.d.2 Export Constraints
Export constraints require:
explicit declaration of withheld pins/witnesses,
explicit statement of whether independent verification is possible,
avoidance of revealing restricted content through logs.
Violation yields refusal and security incident evidence.
O.1.d.3 Boundary-Limited Proofs
Boundary-limited proofs are:
replay-verifiable only within a declared boundary (e.g., local access to withheld pins),
labeled as boundary-limited in receipts and exchange packs,
forbidden from being consumed as fully verifiable evidence in foreign metros without clearance.
O.1.d.4 Disclosure Refusal Rules
If an export request would violate disclosure boundaries, export tooling must:
refuse with a disclosure refusal class,
produce a redacted manifest if permitted,
provide admissible alternatives (share only hashes, share boundary-limited pack, share proof of existence without contents).
O.2 Encode
O.2.a CI Manifests
CI manifests are canonical records.
Fields include:
ci_manifest_id, schema version,
list of bundle ids to verify,
expected bundle root hashes and merkle roots,
required verifier catalog id and schema catalog id,
required environment/toolchain snapshot ids,
acceptance expectations:
which receipts must pass,
which UCWs must pass,
which Ω* certificates must pass,
which bridges must pass,
failure classification policy id and quarantine action policy id.
CI manifests are referenced by CI logs and governance dashboards.
O.2.b Integrity Proofs
Integrity proofs are schema objects enabling tamper-evident verification.
Bundle root proofs: manifest hash, included object ids, and proof that object bytes hash to ids.
Merkle expansion proofs: pins_root and wits_root, expansion indices, recomputed roots.
Chain-hash proofs: WordNF segment list, segment ids, recomputed chain hash.
Signatures (optional): if cryptographic signing is used, signatures are additional integrity evidence; they do not replace hash identities and replay.
Integrity proofs are included in audit packets and exchange packs when allowed.
O.2.c Security Logs
Security logs are immutable records of integrity events.
Drift logs: identify mismatched outcomes under fixed contexts; include evidence diffs and suspected causes.
