Corruption logs: hash mismatches, missing objects, merkle mismatches, chain hash mismatches.
Policy laundering logs: context mismatch evidence, unbridged policy usage, forbidden method usage.
Quarantine logs: quarantine triggers, affected scopes, clearance requirements, propagation evidence.
Remediation logs: remediation job graphs, migration receipts, rollback actions, clearance outcomes.
Security logs are indexed and included in governance dashboards.
O.2.d Adversarial Suites
Adversarial suites are stress suite records.
Fields include:
stress_suite_id, schema version,
scope and risk class,
adversarial probe definitions and adequacy metadata,
pass criteria and gate thresholds (by corridor/policy),
required environment pins,
counterexample schema for failures and minimization rules.
Adversarial suites are required for high-risk promotions and for portal-sensitive regions.
O.3 Prove
O.3.a Replay Invariance
Replay invariance is enforced by CI and verifiers.
Receipts replay. Pins and witnesses expand to complete sets; verifier reproduces acceptance outcomes deterministically.
UCWs recompute. Residual curves recompute exactly under pinned contexts; outcomes match stored commitments.
Ω recompute.* Interior loop suites and boundary commitments reproduce Ω* acceptance; mismatches yield quarantine.
Bridge replay. Bridge application traces reproduce UCW evidence and gate outcomes; chain hashes and witness roots validate.
Archival replay. Archived bundles remain replayable using preserved schema/verifier catalogs or verified translators.
Replay invariance failures are classified as drift, corruption, environment mismatch, or schema mismatch and handled by quarantine and remediation.
O.3.b Corruption Detection
Corruption detection is deterministic.
Hash mismatch: object bytes do not hash to recorded id.
Merkle mismatch: expanded leaf ids do not recompute merkle roots.
Replay mismatch: recomputed evidence differs under pinned environments; may indicate drift or hidden environment dependence; classify separately.
Schema mismatch: cannot decode schema id or schema hashes inconsistent.
Environment mismatch classification: pinned environment conditions not satisfied; classify as environment mismatch rather than semantic failure.
Corruption detection produces corruption logs, quarantines, and rollback actions when necessary.
O.3.c False-Positive Control
False-positive control ensures CI failures are meaningful.
Deterministic comparisons: compare canonical numeric strings and canonical bytes, not raw floats.
Rounding id rules: all numeric outputs used for verification are produced via pinned rounding ids; formatting differences are excluded by canonical encoding.
Environment pin sufficiency: CI runs under pinned environments; if multiple environments are allowed, comparators require environment pins sufficient to ensure identical outcomes.
Tolerance governance: corridors define tolerances; CI does not tighten tolerances ad hoc. Corridor migrations are required to change tolerances.
False positives are mitigated by strict canonicalization and pinned environments.
O.3.d Quarantine Correctness
Quarantine correctness ensures quarantines are justified and safe.
Trigger validity: quarantines are triggered only by verifiable evidence (drift logs, corruption logs, stress/regression failures, policy laundering logs).
Clearance requirements: each quarantine includes explicit clearance tasks and required receipts.
Safe downgrades: quarantined artifacts are excluded from certified routing; fallback routes must exist or service-level degradation is recorded.
Rollback safety: rollback actions preserve baseline availability, preserve evidence, and are replay-auditable.
Quarantine correctness is enforced by CI and governance playbooks.
O.4 Extract
O.4.a Security Reports
Security reports are deterministic summaries built from logs and CI results.
incident list: corruption/drift/policy laundering/disclosure violations,
integrity proof summaries: which bundles verified, which roots matched,
mitigations: quarantines, rollbacks, migrations, bridges,
compliance summaries: visibility enforcement, export compliance, policy compliance,
open remediation tasks and their priorities.
Security reports are audit-ready and exportable according to visibility mode.
O.4.b CI Dashboards
CI dashboards summarize CI status.
pass/fail by manifest and by bundle type (receipts/UCWs/Ω*/bridges),
drift alarms and affected scopes,
quarantines and clearance progress,
remediation backlog and scheduling status,
bisect readiness indicators and pointers to failure diff packs.
Dashboards are deterministic and linked to evidence.
O.4.c Failure Diff Packs
Failure diff packs provide minimal evidence for debugging.
residual curve diffs: κ-indexed values and margins,
enclosure diffs: interval endpoints and rounding cushion differences,
context diffs: policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP/verifier/schema hash mismatches,
minimal counterexamples and subword localizations,
suspected cause classification and next actions.
Diff packs are replayable and used by bisect tools.
O.4.d Rollback Playbooks
Rollback playbooks are deterministic operational procedures.
Activation rollback: deactivate charts/bridges; revert to baseline pointers; rerun regression.
Migration rollback: revert schema/verifier upgrades; restore old catalogs; re-verify canonical bundles.
Corridor pointer rollback: restore previous corridor pointers; quarantine artifacts certified only under withdrawn corridors.
Policy pointer rollback: restore previous policy pointers; quarantine artifacts that depended on changed policies; schedule policy bridges if needed.
Rollback playbooks preserve evidence and enforce safe recovery.
Appendix O — Security, Integrity Proofs, and CI Manifests
Appendix P — Reference Implementation, Replay Harness, and Tooling
P.1 Spec
P.1.a Reference Code Boundaries
The reference implementation is partitioned to enforce determinism, auditability, and separation between trusted verification and untrusted discovery. Boundaries are normative: crossing boundaries requires typed interfaces and schema-validated objects.
P.1.a.1 Kernel vs Plugins
Kernel (trusted). Implements:
canonical encoding and hashing,
schema validation and decoding scaffolding,
content-addressed storage primitives and merkle expansion primitives,
deterministic arithmetic interface sufficient for verifier recomputation,
enclosure primitives (interval kernels and conservative rounding application),
verifier execution framework (predicate evaluation, gate stacks, replay enforcement),
budget enforcement primitives.
The kernel contains no domain-specific evaluator families, no heuristic search, and no external oracles as authority.
Plugins (untrusted by default). Implement:
domain evaluator charts (series/integrals/transforms),
AAS candidate generation procedures,
SPP relation search candidate generation,
portal-specific transforms and carriers,
optional performance models and scheduling heuristics.
Plugin outputs become trusted only when converted into certified receipts and UCWs verified by kernel verifiers.
P.1.a.2 Trusted vs Untrusted Components
Trusted: verifier, schema registry, canonical encoder, hash engine, interval kernel, rounding application, receipt expansion and integrity proofs.
Untrusted: candidate generators (PSLQ, high-precision numeric oracles), heuristic pruning, template exploration, external import sources.
Untrusted components may produce candidate logs and proposed artifacts; they cannot produce promotable truth without kernel verification.
P.1.a.3 Deterministic Interfaces
All interfaces between components are deterministic and schema-bound.
inputs and outputs are canonical schema objects,
decisions affecting outputs are logged as deterministic decision records,
any nondeterminism is forbidden for promotion-critical flows; if allowed for candidate search, it is seed-pinned and segregated.
Determinism is enforced by CI replay.
P.1.a.4 Forbidden Shortcuts
The implementation forbids:
promoting based on oracle numerics or PSLQ fits without certified verification,
widening corridors or relaxing policies implicitly to make a proof pass,
substituting charts or methods without transport UCWs and activation governance records,
using names or indices as semantic evidence,
relying on runtime iteration order or locale/time-dependent behavior in canonicalization,
consuming imported promoted evidence as local truth without ICIB clearance.
Violations yield refusal artifacts and quarantine, not silent behavior.
P.1.b Replay Harness
Replay harness is the executable mechanism that verifies artifacts from bundles and receipts under pinned contexts and environments.
P.1.b.1 Bundle Replay
Bundle replay consumes a bundle manifest and:
validates integrity proofs (hash and merkle proofs),
expands pins_root and wits_root using expansion indices,
validates schema and verifier catalogs are present and supported,
executes the relevant replay scripts (receipt replay, UCW replay, Ω* replay, bridge replay, import clearance replay),
emits replay logs and pass/fail outcomes.
Bundle replay is deterministic given bundle bytes and pinned environment.
P.1.b.2 Receipt Verification
Receipt replay procedure:
decode receipt and plan spec,
expand pins_root and wits_root; verify merkle roots,
validate context hashes match pinned records,
recompute required witness obligations (UCWs, evaluation receipts) where the verifier requires recomputation,
apply the gate stack acceptance predicate and auxiliary suite gates,
emit accept/refuse with evidence and produce verifier logs.
Receipts without replay sufficiency are boundary-limited or refused.
P.1.b.3 UCW Verification
UCW replay procedure:
resolve WordNF route word(s),
resolve corridor, probe regimes, residual functional ids, projector ids,
regenerate probes deterministically,
recompute κ-indexed residual curves,
apply tri-gate and auxiliary gates; emit class and promotability decision,
emit counterexamples on failure.
UCW replay is mandatory for any equivalence claim; stored residuals are non-authoritative.
P.1.b.4 Ω* Verification
Ω* replay procedure:
resolve AtlasSpec and its boundary commitments,
verify root receipts and chart admissibility,
verify six edge UCWs and four face UCWs,
reconstruct interior loop suite WordNFs and loop map signatures,
recompute interior loop UCWs and apply spin gates,
emit Ω* accept/refuse with localization to failing boundary cells.
Ω* verification ensures SFCR interior coherence and prevents global inconsistency.
P.1.b.5 Bridge Verification
Bridge replay procedure:
resolve BridgeSeed and its word template,
validate admissibility under policy/corridor/ConvBundleFP,
instantiate bridge application with pinned parameters,
recompute required UCWs and loop closures introduced by the bridge,
validate stress/regression packs if required for bridge kind,
emit accept/refuse and application traces.
Bridge verification is required before activating bridge chains in routing.
P.1.b.6 Import Verification
Import replay procedure (ICIB clearance):
validate import manifest integrity and schema/verifier compatibility,
build mapping tables and quarantine-by-default status,
execute compat receipts and determine required bridges or re-verification,
perform local verification and produce local receipts for clearance,
enforce confidence translation and prevent overstated confidence,
emit import clearance report and quarantine logs.
Import verification prevents foreign evidence from bypassing local governance.
P.1.c CI Harness
CI harness is the continuous enforcement system running replay, regression, stress, and security checks.
P.1.c.1 Regression Pipelines
Regression pipeline executes:
fixed regression suites for evaluation receipts and UCWs,
RQF Ω* regression for promoted hubs,
bridge regression for active bridges and policy bridges,
ICIB regression for import clearance rules and compat receipts.
Regression suite definitions are versioned and pinned by CI manifests.
P.1.c.2 Stress Pipelines
Stress pipeline executes:
adversarial probe suites for high-risk scopes,
boundary and perturbation tests,
worst-case commutation and loop closure tests.
Stress suites gate promotion and activation; failures trigger quarantines and rollback plans.
P.1.c.3 Manifests
CI manifests specify:
bundles to verify and expected root hashes,
required verifier catalogs and schema catalogs,
environment/toolchain snapshot ids,
acceptance expectations and failure classification policies.
Manifests are hash-addressed and audited.
P.1.c.4 Environment/Toolchain Snapshots
CI runs under pinned environments:
numeric backend versions,
library versions,
toolchain/build versions,
verifier implementations.
Environment mismatch yields classification as environment failure, not semantic failure.
P.1.c.5 Deterministic Runners
CI runners must:
enforce canonical encoding and stable ordering,
disable nondeterministic parallel reductions in promotion-critical tasks unless deterministic scheduling is provably preserved,
enforce fixed seeds only for candidate search tasks and segregate them.
Deterministic runners are required for trust.
P.1.d Tooling Contract
Tooling contract defines required tools, their inputs/outputs, and their safety rules.
P.1.d.1 Compaction Tools
Compaction tools:
deduplicate objects by identity,
compress candidate logs into ledgers without losing refusal exemplars,
produce compaction receipts proving replay equivalence for promoted artifacts,
preserve indices required for merkle root expansion and replay.
Compaction cannot remove evidence required for audit or clearance.
P.1.d.2 Migration Tools
Migration tools:
translate schema versions and verifier versions via verified translators,
produce migration receipts and rollback plans,
rerun regression suites on migrated bundles,
preserve backward witness verification by archiving old catalogs or translators.
Migration tools are governance-critical.
P.1.d.3 Export Tools
Export tools:
assemble minimal replay bundles and exchange packs,
enforce visibility modes and disclosure boundaries,
produce integrity proofs and boundary-limited declarations where applicable,
refuse exports that would violate disclosure boundaries.
Export tools are security-critical.
P.1.d.4 Audit Tools
Audit tools:
generate audit packets (promotion/refusal/drift/conflict/migration),
run replay scripts and integrity checks,
generate failure diff packs and bisect suggestions,
produce governance summaries and compliance reports.
Audit tools never assert truth; they verify and report evidence.
P.1.d.5 Scheduler Tools
Scheduler tools:
ingest demands and compute urgency under pinned scheduler policy,
build job graphs deterministically,
schedule and execute jobs under budgets,
record run logs, decision logs, and escalation logs,
trigger quarantines and rollbacks based on CI outcomes.
Scheduler tools are constrained by governance invariants.
P.2 Encode
P.2.a Module Layout
The reference implementation is arranged by module boundaries. The layout is conceptual and maps to filesystem organization.
kernel/
canon (canonical encoding)
hash (domain-separated hashing, merkle, chain hashes)
store (content-addressed store interface)
intervals (enclosure primitives, rounding application)
verify (predicate engine, gate stacks, replay executor)
budget (budget counters and termination guards)
schema/
schema registry, validators, decoders
migration translators and version catalogs
metro/
NodeSpec/EdgeSpec/AtlasSpec definitions
graph indices and query planner
alias classes and disambiguation services
eval/
evaluator library and chart registry
bound library and projector library
method selection and fallback ladders
transport/
transport signatures and operator catalogs
WordNF compiler and normalization engine
legality predicate hooks
repair/
Snap engine and defect computation
trace generation and MagicCertPack extraction
tunnel/
tunnel enumerators and candidate integration
splice record engine and rollback hooks
discovery/
AAS engine
T0-OBSTR engine
RQF engine
SPP engine
governance/
policies, corridors, risk policies
approvals, quarantines, deprecations
negatify maps and remediation planning
icib/
import manifests
mapping tables and compat receipts
clearance workflows and sync schedules
ci/
CI manifests
regression/stress runners
integrity checks and dashboards
tools/
compaction tools
migration tools
export tools
audit tools
scheduler tools
This layout is referenced by tool indices and version catalogs.
P.2.b Code IDs
Code ids pin the toolchain used to produce and verify artifacts.
code_hash: source code commit hash or content hash of the implementation,
build_hash: build configuration hash (compiler flags, packaging),
toolchain_hash: versions of interpreter/compiler, libraries, and runtimes,
verifier_catalog_hash: supported schema versions, gate stacks, operator catalogs, translators.
Code ids are part of environment/toolchain snapshots referenced by receipts and CI manifests when required.
P.2.c Replay Bundles
Replay bundles are canonical bundles enabling independent verification.
Bundle manifest schema: list of object ids, type tags, schema ids, context hashes, and visibility mode.
Expansion indices: merkle expansions for pins_root and wits_root; index objects are included in bundle.
Integrity proofs: hash proofs for each object, merkle proofs, chain hash proofs for WordNFs.
Boundary constraints: if export is restricted, bundle includes boundary-limited declarations and withheld pins list.
Replay bundles are hash-addressed and archived.
P.2.d Tool Outputs
Tools produce standardized outputs.
Standard logs: run logs, decision logs, predicate logs, replay logs.
Refusal packs: refusal objects with counterexamples and next actions.
Remediation artifacts: job graphs for remediation, migration plans, bridge plans.
Dashboards: coverage, risk, failure, drift, CI health, sync status.
All tool outputs are schema objects with hash identities and are audit-linked.
P.3 Prove
P.3.a Determinism Proof Obligations
Determinism is proven operationally via CI and replay invariance checks.
canonical encoding idempotence for all schema types,
stable ordering for all sets and lists used in proofs (pins/wits, probes, bases),
pinned environments: numeric backends and toolchains sufficient to reproduce outcomes,
seeded search isolation: randomness permitted only for candidate search; promoted evidence is deterministic and replay-verifiable.
Any determinism violation yields drift logs and quarantines.
P.3.b Soundness Obligations
Soundness obligations are enforced by verifiers and CI.
verify-only promotion: no oracle trust; no heuristic evidence in witness roots,
UCW recomputation: residual curves recomputed, not trusted from stored caches,
enclosure correctness: intervals/tails/rounding must be conservative and auditable,
no laundering: no policy laundering, no corridor laundering, no convention laundering; bridges required and must be verified.
Soundness is a core invariant; violations are security incidents.
P.3.c Migration Obligations
Migration obligations ensure safe evolution.
migration receipts required for schema/verifier upgrades,
backward verification preserved by archived catalogs or verified translators,
translator correctness proven by replay equivalence on canonical bundles,
regression after migration: rerun regression suites and quarantine on failure,
rollback plans required for all migrations.
Migration cannot be informal; it is proof-carrying.
P.3.d Compaction Obligations
Compaction obligations ensure storage reduction does not destroy replay.
compaction receipts prove replay equivalence of promoted bundles,
refusal exemplars and conflict evidence preserved for remediation and negatify maps,
merkle expansions and indices preserved,
integrity dashboards updated and CI manifests remain satisfiable.
Compaction must not change semantic outcomes; it only changes storage representation.
P.4 Extract
P.4.a Verifier Catalogs
Verifier catalogs are extraction artifacts listing:
supported schema versions and decoders,
supported operator catalogs and gate stacks,
supported corridor/policy compatibility predicates,
supported translators and migration receipts,
known deprecations and tombstones.
Verifier catalogs are pinned in CI manifests and in replay bundles.
P.4.b API Stubs
API stubs are extraction-ready interface documents and schema references.
Storage: put/get/exists, merkle expansion, bundle operations.
Schema: validate/encode/decode/migrate.
Evaluation: eval receipts, bound evaluation, projector application.
Transport: compile WordNF, normalize, apply transport, legality checks.
Witness: compute/verify UCW, compute/verify Ω*, witness pack assembly.
Repair: Snap invocation, defect computation, trace generation.
Tunnel: tunnel enumeration, candidate integration, splice records, rollback hooks.
Discovery: AAS, T0-OBSTR, RQF, SPP interfaces.
Scheduler: demand ingestion, job graph planning, run orchestration, escalation, logs.
ICIB: import manifests, compat checks, quarantine and clearance, sync.
API stubs include input/output schemas and refusal classes.
P.4.c Ops Kits
Ops kits are operational playbooks.
deploy checklists: schema registry and verifier catalogs, environment pins, storage initialization,
CI checklists: manifests, regression/stress suites, deterministic runner verification,
upgrade playbooks: schema/verifier migration, translator validation, rollback procedures,
maintenance playbooks: compaction, archival snapshotting, cache invalidation, quarantine clearance workflows,
incident response: corruption detection, drift response, disclosure breach response.
Ops kits are deterministic sequences with evidence outputs and audit logs.
P.4.d Roadmap Tooling
Roadmap tooling produces actionable development tasks and schedules.
Roadmap generator: SPP deficiency-driven roadmap to weight (N); AAS template expansion needs; RQF closure targets.
Bottleneck extractor: identifies missing roots, missing bridges, probe inadequacy, policy blocks, portal boundaries.
Forced-target extractor: next forced generator slots and required verification tasks.
Risk mitigator: generates required stress/regression suites and prioritizes risk mitigation tasks.
All roadmap tools output job graphs and governance artifacts; they are auditable and deterministic.
Appendix P — Reference Implementation, Replay Harness, and Tooling
APPENDIX METRO MAP (A–P)
Spine Hubs
A — Identity & Addressing
Canonical serialization; hashing; ordering; address grammar; environment/toolchain pins; determinism audits.
B — Proof Objects
UCW; receipts; refusals; MagicCertPack; BridgeSeed; counterexamples; minimal replay bundle semantics; exchange bundle semantics.
C — Governance Regimes
Corridors; policies; κ schedules; budgets; compatibility predicates; policy-bridge templates; widening/tightening rules; rollback rules.
D — Certified Numerics
Intervals/enclosures; tails/bounds; projectors (\Pi_h); error envelopes; bound audits; certified evaluation receipts.
G — Metro Data Model
NodeSpec/EdgeSpec/AtlasSpec; chart registries; link-edge types; query algebra; deterministic indices; proof slicing; visibility enforcement.
P — Reference Implementation
Kernel vs plugins; verifier catalogs; replay harness; CI harness; tooling contract (compaction/migration/export/audit/scheduler).
Functional Lines
1) Identity → Proof → Replay (always-on)
A → B → PPurpose: turn any claim into a replay-verifiable, hash-bound artifact pipeline.
2) Certified Evaluation Line
D → B → G → PPurpose: produce certified evaluation receipts and wire them into the metro with replay bundles and indices.
3) Legality & Gate Stack Line
A → H → B → PPurpose: enforce legality predicates and gate stacks; ensure refusal minimality and deterministic traceability.
4) Holonomy & Tier-0 Line
I → (B, H) → (E) → PPurpose: detect loop obstructions; produce holonomy witnesses; extract Tier-0 candidates; route into algorithmic passes and replay.
5) Acceleration Line
K → D → E → B → PPurpose: synthesize accel charts (AAS), verify with certified numerics, log tunnel/splice events, bind via UCW, and deploy safely.
6) Basis Growth Line
L → E → B → G → PPurpose: compute deficiency/forced targets, run SPP pipelines, certify reductions/non-reductions, update basis records and indices.
7) Governance & Risk Line
M → C → O → PPurpose: apply risk scoring and Negatify maps; enforce corridors/policies; gate promotions via CI/security; produce compliance evidence.
8) Cross-Metro Exchange Line
N → J → B → O → PPurpose: import/export via ICIB; reconcile corridors/policies via CorrEq and bridges; quarantine-by-default; promote only after verification.
9) Security & CI Line
O → (B, P, A) → CI manifestsPurpose: determinism/replay enforcement; integrity proofs; regression/stress; drift/corruption detection; release gates.
Common Extraction Routes
Certify a new evaluator chart
D → K → E → B → PEnclosures/tails/projectors → template family → verified algorithm pass → receipts/UCWs → replay harness + tool outputs.
Add a safe accel chart and activate
K → D → B → J → O → PDiscover candidate → certify numerics → bind to baseline via UCW → register bridge/activation logic → stress/regression gates → deploy.
Diagnose commutation failure
H → B → I → (E) → PPredicate localization → counterexample extraction → holonomy/face separation → repair/tunnel/escalation planning → replay evidence.
Extract Tier-0 candidate and force atlas
I → (H, B) → E → (G) → PHolonomy stability → legality classification → obstruction record → RQF/Ω* closure → atlas updates + replay bundles.
Publish an exchange-ready bridge
J → B → O → N → PBridge algebra + CorrEq → BridgeSeed/MagicCertPack → CI/security gates → exchange pack manifests → replay harness.
Generate minimal replay bundle
B → A → G → PWitness/receipt roots → canonical encoding + merkle expansion → slice exporter/indexer → runnable replay bundle.
Transfer Hubs
H — Gate Stack / Predicate Catalog Hub
Centralizes legality predicates, gate stacks, residual functionals, refusal mappings, and audit macros; feeds every pass and every receipt.
I — Loop/Holonomy Hub
Centralizes loop suites, loop map signatures, spin residual packs, obstruction triggers, and portal boundary detection.
J — Bridge Algebra Hub
Centralizes bridge kinds, chaining legality, CorrEq corridor transfer rules, migration semantics, and conflict resolution pathways.
O — Security/CI Hub
Centralizes determinism audits, integrity proofs, CI manifests, regression/stress suites, quarantine correctness, rollback playbooks.

Closure Skeleton (SFCR)
Vertices: (\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R).Edges: (SF,SC,SR,FC,FR,CR).Faces: (SFC,SFR,SCR,FCR).Interior: (SFCR) with (\Omega^*) certificate.Cell witnesses: edge UCWs → face UCWs → interior loop suite UCWs → (\Omega^*).
Ω* Lawful Transport Calculus
A Proof-Carrying Metro Graph for Certified Invariants, Deterministic Replay, and Obstruction-Driven Discovery
Abstract
This treatise specifies a single executable framework that completes ordinary mathematical practice by making representation change, verification, reuse, and discovery first-class, machine-checkable objects. The framework is a Lawful Transport Calculus for invariants—constants, identities, reductions, and emergent “missing moduli”—implemented as a hash-addressed metro graph whose nodes are invariant objects and whose edges are typed transports, evaluators, reductions, promotions, and bridges. The central thesis is that an invariant is not defined by a numeral string but by a portable closure object: a value equipped with a 4-root atlas of certified evaluators, a complete set of commutation/holonomy/representability witnesses, and a governance record preventing silent drift in conventions, policies, probes, or tolerances. Every accepted claim is replayable and locally verifiable: no proof by narrative, no trust in floating arithmetic, and no untracked convention changes.
The framework is organized as a 4^4 crystal. Each chapter is a deterministic extraction block with quadrants ({\mathsf S,\mathsf F,\mathsf C,\mathsf R}) (Square/Flower/Cloud/Fractal), facets ({1,2,3,4}) (Spec/Encode/Prove/Extract), and atoms ({a,b,c,d}) (Definition/Construction/Witness/Hook). This produces a memory-web whose addresses are stable, whose claims are typed, whose evidence is merkle-committed, and whose verification is enforced by deterministic verifiers and CI.
1. Core Ontology: Lenses, Context, and Legality
A lens formalizes a representation as a triple[L := (D,\mathcal A,\mathcal I),]where (D) is a carrier domain, (\mathcal A) is an address algebra (canonical indexing, ordering, serialization, hashing, and slicing), and (\mathcal I) is an invariant schema (equivalence relations, admissibility predicates, observables, and preservation laws defining “same meaning” inside the lens). Every lens and every meaning-affecting choice is pinned by a convention fingerprint (\mathrm{ConvFP}) and bundled into a scope-indexed (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP}) that covers all operator families invoked by any route. The system separates four governance substrates:
Policy (P): admissible operations, budgets, risk gates, disclosure boundaries, and automation permissions (e.g., whether tunneling or search is allowed; whether randomness is permitted in candidate search; whether stress/regression is mandatory).
Corridor (C): tolerance contracts and refinement schedules specifying when approximation is acceptable and how refinement is staged; in the scalar specialization, corridor includes gates ((\varepsilon_{\text{face}},\varepsilon_{\text{spin}},\varepsilon_{\Pi})) and a κ ladder (\mathcal K), and generalizes to vector/operator carriers with typed residual functionals and adequacy requirements.
Verifier (V): deterministic protocol that resolves pins, validates bindings, recomputes evidence, applies gate stacks, emits accept/refuse decisions, and generates counterexamples.
Schema catalog (\Sigma): typed record layouts and canonical encoding rules ensuring idempotence and hash stability.
A transport is a typed morphism between lenses,[T: L_i \to L_j,]equipped with explicit legality predicates: type correctness; scope coverage under (\mathrm{ConvBundleFP}); policy admissibility; corridor admissibility; invariant preservation (exact or corridor-bounded); representability stability under projectors; and deterministic replay constraints. Mathematics—definitions, computations, approximations, simplifications, reductions, accelerations—is treated as finite programs over a minimal kernel, accepted only when accompanied by explicit witnesses.
2. WordNF: Route Identity as a Mathematical Object
Every route through the metro is recorded as a canonical normal form WordNF. WordNF is not documentation; it is route identity, a deterministic, hashable description of a computation path including:
the segment stream of gate runs, refinement steps, projection steps, bridge events, and—when present—tunnel events and splices,
bound parameters, method/bound/rounding/projector ids, κ schedule ids, probe regime ids,
pre/post corridor hashes for any corridor changes (tightening only inside repair; widening only as explicit governance events),
chain hashes committing segment order, and
context hashes binding ConvBundleFP, policy, corridor, and verifier.
WordNF removes ambiguity from “routes.” Two computations are the same route iff their WordNF hashes match. Any rewrite of routes must be justified by witnessed commutations and is recorded as a rewrite/splice object; no silent re-association, no silent substitution, no silent normalization changes.
3. UCW and Tri-Lock: The Atom of Truth
The central witness object is the Unified Commutation Witness (UCW), which enforces Tri-Lock:
Face-lock (commutation): κ-indexed route disagreement residual curve (r_{\square}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\text{face}}) over an adequate probe regime.
Spin-lock (holonomy): κ-indexed loop closure residual curve (s_{\square}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\text{spin}}), where the loop map is derived explicitly from route encode/decode and projector insertions.
Projection-lock (representability): κ-indexed projector residual curve (r_{\Pi}(\kappa)) is within (\varepsilon_{\Pi}), ensuring stability under the declared projector (\Pi_h) (canonicalization/quantization/basis normalization).
UCW binds the route pairing (WordNF), the corridor/policy/ConvBundleFP context, the κ ladder, the probe regime and adequacy metadata, residual functional ids, and the verifier protocol. UCW classifies failures into semantic categories (kernel mismatch, holonomy obstruction, uncertainty plateau, convention conflict, policy block, drift, mixed), and produces counterexample objects consisting of minimal failing predicates, minimal κ/probe witnesses, and minimal failing subwords. UCW forbids “agreement by coincidence” because every acceptance is tied to corridor gates, adequacy, projection stability, and replay.
4. Certified Evaluation: Intervals, Tails, and Projectors
Certified evaluation produces enclosures, not point estimates. For each evaluator chart, certified receipts bind:
a conservative enclosure (interval/rectangle/ball/operator enclosure) with outward rounding under a pinned rounding id,
tail bounds and truncation envelopes under pinned bound ids with explicit assumption regions and composition laws,
projector stability under (\Pi_h) where required, including projection residual obligations,
deterministic truncation selection (N(\kappa)) or mesh refinement rules, and
replay sufficiency via pins and witness roots.
Certified evaluation is the anchor that makes routes auditable and makes transport witnesses meaningful. No floating numerics are accepted as proof; floats may appear only inside the certified enclosure calculus and never as decisive equality.
5. Receipt Algebra: Dependency, Merge, and Equivalence Are Distinct
The framework separates three relations that standard practice conflates:
Dependency: proof consumption with explicit membership proofs and typed dependency edges.
Merge / co-verification: index union with no semantic claim; conflicts preserved; no merge implies meaning.
Equivalence / transport: meaning preservation requiring UCW-grade witnesses and receipts binding pins and witness roots.
Receipts bind a PlanSpec, pins_root, wits_root, and context hashes. Cross-policy reuse requires policy-bridge receipts; cross-convention reuse requires convention bridges. The framework thereby prevents consensus drift, hidden assumptions, and semantic laundering.
6. Repair and Acceleration: Snap, Tunneling, and “Magic” Stabilization
A Snap operator is the lawful repair mechanism: it performs corridor-disciplined repair under strict constraints:
actions must strictly decrease a defect functional derived from gate violations,
no silent widening is permitted,
every action is logged and replayable, and
if repair cannot succeed within budgets, Snap emits refusal artifacts rather than guesses.
Tunneling is the lawful acceleration/repair mechanism for changing representation. A tunnel is a first-class event recorded in WordNF with pre/post hashes, defect measures, candidate logs, verification hooks, and replay fields. Tunneling cannot hide heuristics: any oracle-guided discovery remains candidate-only until verified by certified evaluation and UCW binding. Successful repair/acceleration episodes are stabilized into reusable “brain tissue”:
MagicCertPack: proof-carrying record of defect reduction with witnesses and replay bindings.
BridgeSeed: reusable typed bridge derived from MagicCertPacks and witness sets; compositional and replay-stable.
Thus the system supports “magic” only when it is stabilized into typed objects with deterministic replay.
7. Metro Graph: The Living Memory Web
All knowledge is stored as a typed metro graph:
Nodes: invariants (seed, derived, candidate, portal), atlases, bundles.
Edges: baseline evaluators, accel evaluators, transports, reductions, collapses, promotions, bridges, migrations, refusals.
Payloads: chart specs, parameter schemas, bound derivations, projector definitions, probe regimes.
Witnesses: UCWs, Ω* certificates, stress/regression packs, refusal packs, conflict certs.
Determinism: canonical serialization and stable hashing; merkle roots for pins/wits; chain hashes for WordNF.
The metro is simultaneously a knowledge base, a compiler target for verification, a routing substrate for evaluation, and a provenance/audit log.
8. Discovery as Failure Interpretation: Tier-0 Obstructions and Portals
Discovery is formalized as failure interpretation. When closure repeatedly fails under lawful repair:
Holonomy-dominant stable failure triggers the Tier-0 Obstruction Compiler (T0-OBSTR). If spin residuals stabilize to κ-independent intervals supported by adequate probes and invariant under admissible chart substitutions, the failure is interpreted as a missing modulus required for loop closure.
Any proposed modulus becomes a candidate seed and is forced through Root-Quad Forcing (RQF): it must admit a 4-root atlas and satisfy SFCR boundary closure and interior closure with an Ω* certificate.
If closure cannot be achieved without extending the semantic alphabet (new carrier, new observables, monodromy class), the system emits a portal report rather than inventing a false constant. Portals are governed expansions, not ad hoc fixes.
9. Basis Growth: Dimension Models and Forced Generators
Growth is guided by a dimension model (d_w) (and refined (d_{w,\delta})) and a forced generator calculus:
compute deficiency via generating function monomial counts,
schedule forced generator slots and relation searches,
install certified collapse edges (e.g., even-zeta collapses into (\mathbb Q\cdot\pi^{2n}) under declared conventions),
attempt certified reductions into the current basis,
when reductions fail robustly under bounded search and adequate probes, produce non-reduction certificates and promote new primitives under RQF and governance gates.
The Seed Promotion Pipeline (SPP) makes basis expansion systematic, auditable, and purpose-driven.
10. Acceleration Synthesis: Apéry and Beyond
Atlas Acceleration Synthesis (AAS) provides a disciplined engine for finding faster evaluators:
discovery may use high-precision oracle computations only to propose candidate charts,
acceptance requires certified interval verification against baseline receipts with deterministic tail bounds,
promotion requires UCW binding and loop closure so accel charts are additional routes, not additional truths,
activation requires risk-gated stress/regression and rollback readiness.
The acceleration library is template-driven (Apéry/binomial, hypergeometric, Euler–Maclaurin, continued fractions, kernel semigroups, modular/character sums) with deterministic enumeration order and replayable candidate ledgers.
11. Demand-Driven Mathematics: Purpose Loop and Governance
A UseCase Demand Scheduler ranks invariants and tasks by urgency (digits × call volume × latency, plus dependency and deficiency impacts). It triggers baseline evaluation, UCW closure, acceleration synthesis, and—when needed—kernel growth via obstruction compilation. This makes the development of constants, identities, and reductions purposeful and schedulable, while preserving proof-carrying integrity through governance: policies, corridors, risk scoring, negatify failure-space maps, quarantine rules, and approval gates.
12. Cross-Metro Exchange: ICIB Imports, Compatibility, and Policy Bridges
ICIB formalizes imports as compatibility and clearance, not as merge. Imports are quarantined by default and become local truth only via:
compat receipts proving semantic/governance/schema compatibility,
explicit convention/policy bridges and CorrEq corridor transfer rules,
local re-verification and RQF closure where promotion is intended,
conservative confidence translation (no overstated confidence), and
conflict preservation with counterexamples and portal boundary detection.
13. Deterministic Replay, CI, and Security
The framework enforces as machine predicates:
Replay invariance: promoted artifacts replay-verify from pins/wits roots, WordNF, and pinned environments.
No silent drift: any change in conventions, policies, probes, tolerances changes fingerprints; promotion invalid unless bridged explicitly.
Local verifiability: any claim can be independently re-verified without global trust.
Compositionality: receipts and UCWs compose; dependency, merge, and equivalence are never conflated.
Budgeted honesty: failures yield refusal artifacts with classification and counterexamples, not guesses.
Tamper-evidence: hashing, merkle proofs, chain hashes; corruption and substitution are detected; quarantine and rollback are enforced.
Continuous verification CI runs replay bundles, regression suites, stress suites, and integrity checks under pinned toolchains and environments.
14. Crystal Addressing Scheme (4^4 Extraction Spine)
Every item in the treatise is addressed as:[\mathbf{ChXX}.\mathbf{Q}.\mathbf{F}.\mathbf{a},]with:
( \mathrm{ChXX}\in{01..21}): chapter,
( \mathrm{Q}\in{A,B,C,D}): quadrant lens (Square/Flower/Cloud/Fractal),
( \mathrm{F}\in{1,2,3,4}): facet (Spec/Encode/Prove/Extract),
( a\in{a,b,c,d}): atom (Definition/Construction/Witness/Hook).
Appendices are addressed as ( \mathbf{AppX}.\mathbf{Y}.\mathbf{y} ) with (X\in{A..P}), (Y\in{1..4}), (y\in{a..d}). This addressing makes the manuscript itself a deterministic memory-web: every claim has stable coordinates for extraction, cross-linking, and replay.
